Dha Nafr replied, "How can a man have a solution when he is a king's prisoner and is expecting to be killed at any time? I have no advice to give you, except to say that Unays, the elephant keeper, is a friend of mine. I will send him a message strongly commending you and ask h i to seek permission for you to address the king. Speak to h i as you see fit, and Unays will intercede on your behalf as well as he can." 'Abd al-Muttalib agreed and Dhii Nafr sent Unays the following message: "'Abd al-MutMb is lord of Quraysh and custodian of the well of Mecca; he feeds both men in the plains and wild animals on the mountains. The king seized 200 of his camels. So seek permission for him to see the king and intercede for him as best you can." Unays responded that he would. Unays then spoke to Abraha, saying, "0 king, here at your door seeking audience is the lord of Quraysh and keeper of the well of Mecca; he feeds both men in the plains and the wild beasts in the mountains. Allow him in to see you to discuss a matter with you." Abraha let him in. 'Abd al-Mutr;llib was the most dignified, handsome, and impressive of men. When Abraha saw him, he wanted to honour him by not making him sit below himself. But he did not want the Abyssinians to see him sitting next to himself on the throne. So Abraha descended, sat down on a carpet, and had 'Abd al-Muttalib take his place beside him. He then told his interpreter to ask why he had come and he did so. 'Abd al-Muffalib replied, "What I want is for the king to return the 200 camels he took from me as compensation." Hearing this, the king told his translator to reply as follows: "You impressed me when I saw you, but you displeased me when you spoke. You want to talk to me about 200 camels I took from you in compensation, but not about the building which is your religion and your ancestors' religion that I have come to destroy?" 'Abd al-Muttalib replied, "I am the owner of the camels; the building has its own master who will protect it." Abraha replied, "He won't protect it from me." "Then it's between you and Hi," 'Abd d-Muffdib said. And so Abraha returned his camels to 'Abd al-MuyMib. Ibn Ishaq stated that when he went in to see Abraha, 'Abd al-Munalib was accompanied by Y a h u r b. Nafatha b. 'Adi b. al-Dil b. Bakr b. 'Abd Manat h. Kinana, leader of Bano Bakr tribe, and Khuwaylid b. Wa'ila, leader of Hudhayl. These men offered Abraha one-third of the produce of Tih%na if he would withdraw and not destroy the building. But Ahraha refused their offer. And God alone knows whether or not that happened.
lessons: When facing overwhelming adversity, recognizing the limits of your own power is not weakness — it is wisdom and clarity. Abd al-Muttalib knew he could not fight Abraha, and he was honest about it.
True trust in God means releasing the outcome to Him even when you cannot see how things will resolve. Abd al-Muttalib said plainly, 'The building has its own Master,' and meant it.
Dignity and composure in the face of a powerful oppressor can itself be a form of strength. Abd al-Muttalib's bearing impressed even a conquering king.
practical_advice: When you face a situation beyond your control, identify clearly what is within your power to address and focus your energy there, as Abd al-Muttalib did by seeking his camels rather than making futile demands.
Practice saying aloud, 'This matter is between this and God,' for circumstances you cannot change. This is not resignation but a genuine act of tawakkul (trust in God) that reduces anxiety.

Seek out people who have access or influence when you are facing a difficult situation — Abd al-Muttalib built a chain of advocates (Dhu Nafr to Unays to Abraha) rather than giving up when direct access was blocked.

title: Abd al-Muttalib Prays at the Door of the Ka'ba Before Abraha's Advance
summary: Before Abraha's army entered Mecca, Abd al-Muttalib held the door knocker of the Ka'ba and prayed earnestly to God for protection, then led the Quraysh to the mountain peaks to await the outcome.
story: When they left Abraha, 'Abd al-Mu<r;llib went to report to Quraysh and told them to retreat from Mecca to defensive positions in the mountains. Then 'Abd al-Mudib took hold of the metal door knocker of the kacba and stood there with a group of men from Quraysh praying to God and asking His help against Abraha and his troops. As he stood holding the kacba door knocker, 'Abd al-Mugalib recited the verses: "0 God, Your worshippers protect their homes, so protect Your building, Let not their cross and their power vanquish Yours tomorrow If You should leave them free with our qibla, then that is what You will." Ibn Hishm said that these were what he found to be the authentic verses of this poem. Ibn Ishaq reported that 'Abd al-Mugalib then released the kacba door-knocker and went off, along with his men of Quraysh, to the mountain peaks, taking up defensive positions and waiting for whatever Abraha might do.
lessons: When human effort has reached its limit, turning to God in sincere prayer is not passivity but the most powerful action available. Abd al-Muttalib did everything he could, then turned to the only One who could do what he could not.
Honest prayer acknowledges God's sovereignty completely — Abd al-Muttalib's prayer did not demand an outcome but submitted to God's will while expressing his heartfelt need.
Responsible leadership means both acting practically (telling people to retreat to safety) and spiritually (turning to God). Both dimensions of care matter in a crisis.
practical_advice: When you have done everything within your power and still feel helpless, try the practice of physically holding or touching something meaningful — a door, a prayer mat, a place of worship — as you make du'a. Embodied prayer can deepen sincerity and calm the nervous system.
Write out an honest prayer in your own words, as Abd al-Muttalib did in verse. Do not perform it for anyone; let it be a raw, real conversation with God about what you fear and what you need.
After praying, take a practical step forward as Abd al-Muttalib did — he prayed, then moved his people to safety. Faith and action work together; neither replaces the other.

title: The Elephant Refuses to March on Mecca and the Army of Abraha Is Destroyed
summary: On the morning Abraha prepared to enter Mecca, his elephant Mahmud refused to advance toward the city and knelt down. God then sent flocks of birds carrying stones that killed the soldiers, and Abraha himself died a miserable death on his return to Yemen.
story: Next morning Abraha prepared to enter Mecca, readying his elephant and equipping his troops. The elephant's name was Mahmad. When they directed the elephant towards Mecca, Nufayl b. Hahib came close to it, took its ear and told it, "Kneel down, then go hack to where you came from. Here you are in God's holy land." Then he released the elephant's ear and it knelt. According to al-Suhayli this means that the elephant fell to the ground, since it is not in the nature of elephants to kneel; although it has been said that some elephants do kneel like camels. God knows best. Nufayl b. eabib hurried off into the mountains. Abraha's troops beat the elephant to make it stand up, struck its head with axes, and stuck hooks into its hide until it bled. But it refused to stand. Then they turned it to face towards Yemen, and it got up and moved in a hurry. When they directed it towards Syria and then towards the east it did the same. But when they turned it towards Mecca it knelt again. Then God sent against them birds from the sea like swifts and crows, each one of which carried three stones, one in its beak and one in each claw. The stones were like chickpeas and lentils. Every soldier hit died, but not all were struck. So they retreated in haste along the road by which they had come, calling out for Nufayl b. Habib to lead them on the way back to Yemen. About this situation Nufayl spoke these verses: "Greetings to you from us, 0 Rudayna. How much we have gladdened our eyes this morning! Rudayna, had but you seen, but may you see not, what we saw at Mt. Mulpa~sab. Then you would have forgiven and praised me, and not felt ill will at what passed between us. I praised God to see the birds, and feared that a stone be cast upon me. While all called out for Nufayl, as though I owed the Abyssinians some debt." Ibn Ishaq continued, "So down they scrambled by any path, dying randomly where they went. Abraha was struck on his body and they carried him away as his lingers dropped off one by one. As they fell pus and blood emerged. When they got him to S a n s he was like a baby bird. He finally died when his heart burst from his chest. Or so they say." Ihn Ishaq went on to relate that Yacqiibb. Wtha told him that it was reported that measles and smallpox were seen for the first time in Arab lands that year. Also that that was the first year bitter plants were observed there: the African rue, the colocynth, and the Asclepras grgantea. Ibn Ishaq commented that when God sent Muhammad (SAAS) one of the actions he enumerated as God's grace and bounties upon Quraysh was His having repelled the Abyssinians and saved Quraysh for posterity. And so God spoke in the Qur'an, "Have you not seen how God dealt with those who had elephants? Did he not defeat their scheme, sending flocks of birds which cast upon them hard stones of baked clay, rendering them like digested chaff?" (siirat al-Fil, CV, v.1-5). Ibn Abi Hatim said that he was told, by Aba Zur'a, Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah b. Aba Shayba, and Abn Mu%wiya, from al-Acmash, Abii Sufym, and Wbayd b. 'Umayr, that when God wished to destroy those with the elephant He sent upon them birds like swifts that were raised up from the sea. Each bird carried three stones, two in its claws and one in its beak. He said the hirds came, lined up over their heads, screeched, and dropped what was in their claws and beaks. Each stone that fell on a man's head exited through his behind and each stone that fell on one side of a man exited from the other. God also sent a fierce gale which struck the stones and increased their velocity. And so they were all killed. But, as Ibn Ishaq has stated above, not all were struck by the stones. Some did return to Yemen to tell.
lessons: God's protection of what is sacred does not always come through the means people expect. The Quraysh had no army capable of stopping Abraha, yet the Ka'ba was protected. This teaches that outcomes are ultimately in God's hands, not in the balance of worldly power.

Arrogance and the belief that human might is invincible has historically been the downfall of those who possessed it. Abraha's complete confidence in his army and his elephant became the very context of his destruction.
The Qur'an preserved this event as a reminder of God's bounty to Quraysh and to humanity. Recognising past instances of divine care and protection is a foundation for present hope and gratitude.
practical_advice: When you feel surrounded by forces far larger than yourself — whether a difficult institution, an unjust authority, or an overwhelming situation — recall the story of the Elephant and remind yourself that God has intervened in impossible situations before and is not limited by worldly power.

Gratitude is a practice, not just a feeling. Make a list of three times in your own life when something resolved in a way you could not have engineered yourself. Reflect on these as your personal equivalent of Surat al-Fil.

Avoid placing ultimate trust in any worldly resource — your status, your connections, your wealth, or your plans. Hold these as tools while anchoring your confidence in God, because it is God's decree that ultimately prevails.

title: The Army of the Elephant: Divine Destruction of Abraha's Forces
summary: Abraha's massive army, which had marched on Mecca with an elephant to destroy the Kaaba, was decimated by a flock of birds sent by God carrying stones, with survivors returning to Yemen in ruin and Abraha himself dying in a gruesome manner.
story: carried three stones, two in its claws and one in its beak. He said the birds came, lined up over their heads, screeched, and dropped what was in their claws and beaks. Each stone that fell on a man's head exited through his behind and each stone that fell on one side of a man exited from the other. God also sent a fierce gale which struck the stones and increased their velocity. And so they were all killed. But, as Ibn Ishaq has stated above, not all were struck by the stones. Some did return to Yemen to tell their people of the disaster that befell them. And it was also said that Abraha, God curse him, went back, his fingers dropping off one by one, and that when he reached Yemen his chest burst open and he died. Ibn Ishaq related that Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr told him, on the authority of Amra, that Aisha had said, 'I saw the elephant's keeper and guide in Mecca, both blind and crippled, begging for food.' As mentioned above, the keeper's name was Unays, though no name was given for its guide. But God knows best. Al-Naqqash stated in his Quran exegesis that a torrent bore away their bodies into the sea. Al-Suhayli said that the events of the elephant occurred on the first day of Muharram, year 886 of the era of Dhu al-Qarnayn (Alexander the Great). And I add that it was the same year that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was born, as is generally known. It is also said, however, that these events preceded his birth by some years, as we will report, God willing, and in Him we trust.
lessons: Divine protection can manifest in unexpected and miraculous ways, reminding us that worldly power is never absolute.
Those who seek to harm what is sacred may face consequences that far exceed any harm they intended to inflict.
The survival of a sacred trust — whether a place, a community, or a value — can be a sign of a greater divine plan unfolding.
practical_advice: When you feel powerless against forces much larger than yourself, reflect on historical moments where the vulnerable were protected by means beyond human calculation, and let this renew your trust in God.
In times of fear or persecution, remind yourself that outcomes are not solely determined by the strength of the aggressor — stay steadfast and seek refuge in prayer.
If you witness injustice seemingly 'winning,' resist despair; history repeatedly shows that oppressive power contains the seeds of its own undoing.

title: The Poets of Quraysh Commemorate the Defeat of the Elephant Army
summary: Arab poets composed verses celebrating God's miraculous defeat of Abraha's elephant army and the divine protection granted to the Kaaba, reflecting on the sacred nature of Mecca.
story: Here Ibn Ishaq mentions the poetry recited by the Arabs on this great occasion on which God rendered His sacred house victorious, wishing it to be honoured, respected, purified, and dignified through the mission of Muhammad (SAAS) and through the true religion He legislated to him. One of the elements of this religion, indeed one of its pillars, is prayer, the direction of which would be towards this sanctified Kaaba. What God did then to the elephant's army was not a victory for Quraysh over the Abyssinian Christians; the Abyssinians were at that time closer to it (i.e. to victory from God) than the polytheist Quraysh. The victory belonged to the sacred house and served to set the foundation and pave the way for the mission of Muhammad (SAAS). Included in this poetry are the verses of Abd Allah b. al-Zibaara al-Sahmi: 'They fled in terror from Mecca's interior, its sanctuary undisturbed for ages past; Sirius had created no nights inviolate since not even one of the mightiest of men could ever aspire to attack it. Ask the Abyssinian prince what he saw of it; he who has knowledge of it shall inform the ignorant. Sixty thousand men did not return to their land, even their sick did not live after their homecoming. Ad and Jurhum were there before them, but God held it high above all men.' Abu Qays b. al-Aslat al-Ansari al-Madani also spoke verses on the subject: 'God's work it was the day of the Abyssinians' elephant; many times did they urge it on, but it didn't budge; Their flanks were under its sides and they slashed its trunk until it tore. They used a knife instead of a whip; when they aimed it at its back it was badly wounded. So it turned and ran away, and those there did fail for their evil. God sent a gale down upon them, overwhelming them as if they were dwarves. Their priests urged fortitude but they screamed like bleating sheep.' Abu al-Salt Rabi'a b. Abi Rabia Wahb b. Allaj al-Thaqafi spoke the following verses, though Ibn Hisham attributed them to Umayya b. Abi al-Salt: 'Our Lord's signs are manifestly clear, only disbelievers doubting them. He created night and day and all clear, its reckoning determined. Then a merciful Lord makes clear the day with a sun of spreading rays. He kept the elephant at al-Mughammis until it crawled as though hamstrung. Kneeling down like a camel, and still, as if it were carved out of a rock from the mountain. Around it heroes from among the kings of Kinda, eagle-like lords in wars. They left it, then all fled in fright, each with a broken leg. All religions except Abraham's al-Hanifa are null in God's sight on Judgement Day.' Abu Qays b. al-Aslat said also: 'Arise and pray to your God, cleansing yourself at the pillars of His house between the rugged mountains. From it you had a certain calamity on the day of Abu Yaksum [Abraha], leader of the phalanges: His elite walks on the plain, his troops on their camels at the mountain heights. When you received victory from the Owner of the Throne, hosts of angels repelled them, casting soil and stones. Quickly they turned in flight; and of the Abyssinians all who returned were injured.' Also among such verses is the one of Ubayd Allah b. Qays al-Ruqayyat extolling the greatness of the Kaaba and its protection through the destruction of those wishing it harm: 'Split-face (Abraha) attacked it, coming with his elephant, but his army turned back, defeated. Birds rained stones down upon them until he was like a man being stoned. Whoever attacks that place returns with his armies defeated, humiliated.'
lessons: Gratitude expressed through art, poetry, and remembrance is a powerful way to preserve communal memory of God's blessings.
Recognizing that a victory belongs to God rather than to one's own tribe or group is a mark of spiritual maturity and humility.
The Kaaba's sanctity transcended tribal allegiances, pointing toward a universal truth that would later be embodied in the message of Islam.
practical_advice: Find meaningful ways to express your gratitude — through journaling, poetry, or storytelling — especially after experiencing relief or rescue from a difficult situation.
When something good happens to your community, take time to reflect collectively on the source of that blessing rather than attributing it solely to human effort.
Study the sacred spaces and traditions of your faith as anchors for your identity during times of confusion or spiritual drift.

title: The End of Abyssinian Rule in Yemen: The Successors of Abraha
summary: After Abraha's death, his sons Yaksum and Masruq successively ruled Yemen under Abyssinian domination, leaving the Yemeni people in prolonged suffering under foreign occupation.
story: Ibn Ishaq and others related that upon the demise of Abraha, his son Yaksam assumed power, followed by the latter's brother Masruq b. Abraha. He was the last of their kings and it was from him that Sayf b. Dha Yazan of Himyar wrested the kingship with the aid of troops he brought from Chosroe Anushirwan, as we shall relate. The events with the elephant occurred in the month of Muharram, in the year 886 in the era of Dhu al-Qarnayn, Alexander son of Phillip, the Macedonian, after whom the Greeks count their calendar. Abraha and his two sons having died and Abyssinian rule over Yemen having ended, al-Qullays, the temple Abraha had built, in his ignorance and stupidity, as a substitute for the Arab pilgrimage, was abandoned and left unattended. He had constructed over it two wooden idols, Ku'ayb and his wife, each one 60 cubits high. These idols were invested by spirits, and consequently anyone chancing to take anything from the temple building or its furnishings would come to harm. This continued to be the case until the time of al-Saffah, the first of the Abbasid caliphs. When he was told about it and all its marble and furnishings that Abraha had brought from the Balqis castle in Yemen, he sent people to disassemble it stone by stone and to take away all its contents. That is how al-Suhayli related it; God knows best.
lessons: Institutions built on arrogance and the desire to replace what is sacred will ultimately crumble and be abandoned.
Suffering under oppressive rule, though prolonged, is not permanent — change and liberation eventually come.
What human beings build out of pride and rivalry with the divine cannot endure the way truth and sincere worship endure.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a prolonged situation of hardship or oppression, resist the urge to lose hope — study the patterns of history that show how even entrenched injustice eventually ends.
Reflect on the structures in your own life that may have been built on false foundations, and consider what it would mean to rebuild them on sincerity and truth.
In moments of collective grief or national suffering, communities can find solidarity by holding onto their shared sacred values and identity.

title: Sayf b. Dha Yazan Seeks Help to Liberate Yemen from Abyssinian Rule
summary: The Yemeni nobleman Sayf b. Dha Yazan, distressed by Abyssinian occupation of his homeland, traveled to the Byzantine emperor and then to the Persian king Chosroe seeking military aid to reclaim Yemen, facing initial rejection before finding an opening.
story: Ultimately, the people of Yemen having so long suffered in misery, Sayf b. Dha Yazan, the Himyarite, became rebellious. His genealogy was Sayf b. Dha Yazan b. Dhu Asba b. Malik b. Zayd b. Sahl b. Amr b. Qays b. Mu'awiya b. Jashm b. Abd Shams b. Wa'il b. al-Ghawth b. Qutran b. Arib b. Zuhayr b. Ayman b. al-Hamaysa b. al-Aranjaj, the last being Himyar b. Saba'. Sayf was also known as Abu Murra. He made his way to the Byzantine emperor and complained to him of the state of affairs in Yemen, asking him to oust the Abyssinians and appoint him governor there. He suggested the emperor send from Byzantium whatever troops he thought necessary for this purpose and so himself would become king of Yemen. The emperor declined. So Sayf went to al-Nu'man b. al-Mundhir, who was Chosroe's vice-regent over al-Hira and the neighbouring territories in Iraq, and complained to him about the Abyssinians. Al-Nu'man responded that every year he sent an official delegation to Chosroe, and invited Sayf to stay with him until then. Sayf agreed. Sayf later did accompany al-Nu'man, who took him in to see Chosroe, seated in the chamber where his crown was kept. His crown was like a large grain bucket and was, so they say, set with rubies, chrysolites, pearls, gold, and silver, suspended by a gold chain from the dome of his audience chamber. His neck did not bear the crown; he was kept hidden by a cloth until he sat down there. Then he would put his head into his crown. When he was thus positioned on his throne, the cloth would be removed. All who saw him for the first time would kneel down in awe of him. When Sayf entered he bowed his head down low, and the king commented, 'It is really stupid to come in through such a tall doorway and bow one's head down so low!' When this remark was transmitted to Sayf, he responded that he had only done this out of his preoccupying distress that overwhelmed all else. He then went on to say, 'O king, crows have taken charge of our country!' 'What crows?' Chosroe asked, 'the ones from Abyssinia or from Sind?' 'From Abyssinia. I've come to ask your help; and you shall have kingship over all our land.' Chosroe replied, 'Your country is far away, and its riches meagre. I'm not going to embroil an army from Persia in Arabia. I don't need that.' He then presented Sayf with 10,000 pure-minted dirhams and a robe of honour. Having received these, Sayf went outside and began distributing the money to people. When the emperor heard of this he wondered why, and so sent for him and asked him why he was throwing away a king's gift to people. Sayf replied, 'What else am I to do with your gift? Are not the mountains of my country made of gold and silver?' He said this to arouse Chosroe's interest. So Chosroe then assembled his advisers and asked their counsel about Sayf and his mission. They reminded him of the presence in his gaols of prisoners he had condemned to death and suggested he send them with Sayf. For if they were killed that was what Chosroe had intended for them anyway, and if they triumphed his own domain would be expanded. Chosroe therefore sent off with Sayf eight hundred men from his prisons and placed them under the command of a man named Wahriz, an elder of the highest repute and ancestry. They set sail in eight ships, of which two sank; so six ships eventually arrived on the coast of Aden. Sayf gathered together all his own men he could to help Wahriz, saying, 'My leg is with yours till we all die or all triumph.' Wahriz replied that that was fair. Masruq b. Abraha then came out with his troops to meet them in battle. Wahriz sent his own son out to do combat and test their mettle. Wahriz's son was slain; this increased the anger and hatred the father had for them. When the enemy troops were positioned on the battlefield, Wahriz asked his men to indicate which was their king. They pointed to a man on an elephant with a crown.
lessons: Persistence in seeking justice, even after repeated rejection, is a virtue — one closed door does not mean the path is permanently blocked.
When advocating for your people or cause, resourcefulness and patience are often more powerful than immediate access to power.
Suffering under oppression can become the very fuel that drives courageous action; distress, when channeled rightly, can lead to liberation.
practical_advice: When you face rejection while seeking help — from an employer, institution, or authority — do not interpret it as a final verdict. Regroup, seek other avenues, and remain persistent.
If you are carrying a heavy burden on behalf of others, find allies who share your vision; Sayf's story shows that unexpected partnerships can open doors that seem permanently shut.
In situations where you feel powerless, consider what unique assets or arguments you possess that you have not yet fully used — Sayf's wit in responding to Chosroe ultimately changed the king's mind.

title: Wahriz Defeats Masruq ibn Abraha: The Persian Victory Over the Abyssinians in Yemen
summary: The Persian commander Wahriz, driven by grief over his slain son, uses his legendary archery to kill the Abyssinian king Masruq ibn Abraha on the battlefield, routing the Abyssinian forces and ending their rule over Yemen. Wahriz then enters San'a triumphantly with his banner held high.
story: all his own men he could to help Wahriz, saying, "My leg is with yours till we all die or all triumph." Wahrii replied that that was fair. Masriiq b. Abraha then came out with his troops to meet them in battle. Wahriz sent his own son out to do combat and test their mettle. Wahriz's son was slain; this increased the anger and hatred the father had for them. When the enemy troops were positioned on the battlefield, Wahriz asked his men to indicate which was their king. They pointed to a man on an elephant with a crown and wearing a ruby between his eyes. He saw him and told them not to attack him. For some time nothing was done. Then Wahriz asked what the Abyssinian leader was doing now. They told him he was now on a horse. Again Wahriz told them not to attack him, and for long they waited. Eventually Wahrii asked after the king and was told he was now on a mule. "So", Wahrii commented, "on an ass's filly, is he? He has humbled himself and his domain. I'll take a shot at him. If you see his companions not moving, then stay still till I give you further orders, for I will have missed the man. But if you see his people crowding around him and not advancing, I shall have hit him. In that case, you advance at them." With that he braced his bowstring; they say his bow was so stiff only he could brace it. He then had his eyebrows tied back and let the arrow fly. He split the ruby between the king's eyes and the arrow pierced right through his head and emerged from its back. He was knocked off his mount, and the Abyssinians milled around him in confusion. The Persians charged and the Abyssinians were defeated, being killed or fleeing in all directions. Wahriz advanced to enter San'a'. When he reached its gateway he insisted that his banner could never enter in a lowered position, and that they should therefore demolish the gate. This was done and he went inside with his banner held high.
lessons: Grief and loss can be transformed into focused determination rather than reckless rage — Wahriz channeled his sorrow over his son's death into patient, strategic action.
True strength often requires waiting for the right moment; Wahriz's discipline in holding back his forces until the perfect opportunity demonstrated that patience is a form of power.
Pride in one's cause and dignity, as shown by Wahriz refusing to lower his banner, reflects that one's principles and honor should not be compromised even in the smallest symbolic acts.
practical_advice: When you are grieving a loss, allow yourself to feel the pain but then consciously redirect that energy toward purposeful action rather than impulsive decisions.
In moments of pressure or conflict, practice the discipline of waiting and observing before acting — not every challenge requires an immediate response.
Identify one area of your life where you have been compromising your values for the sake of convenience, and take a concrete step this week to restore that boundary.

title: Sayf ibn Dhi Yazan Receives Delegations and Is Given Glad Tidings of the Prophet
summary: After the Persian victory restores Yemeni sovereignty, Arab delegations including Quraysh — led by Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim — come to congratulate Sayf ibn Dhi Yazan, who in turn shares with them the prophetic tidings he knew of the coming Messenger of God.
story: Arabs from Hijaz and elsewhere came in delegations to Sayf, praising him and congratulating him that the kingship had gone to him. Quraysh sent such a delegation and its number included 'Abd al-Muttalib b. Hashim. Sayf gave him the glad tidings of the coming of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and informed him of what he knew about him. Details of this will be given hereafter in the chapter related to the predictions of his coming.
lessons: Good news and hope can be shared and multiplied — Sayf's sharing of prophetic tidings with Abd al-Muttalib shows that knowledge of God's plans is a gift meant to be passed on.
Political and worldly victories can serve as occasions to point people toward higher, spiritual truths rather than remaining focused solely on material triumphs.
Hope in the coming of goodness sustains communities through long periods of difficulty; the prophecy of the Prophet gave the people of Arabia something greater to look forward to.
practical_advice: When you experience a personal victory or blessing, reflect on how you can use that moment to uplift or give hope to someone else in your life.
Seek out knowledge about your faith's deeper meanings and prophecies — understanding the broader story of your tradition can strengthen your sense of purpose during uncertain times.
Practice sharing hopeful and uplifting information with those around you rather than focusing only on current hardships or difficulties.

title: The Poetic Celebration of Sayf ibn Dhi Yazan's Triumph
summary: Poets including Abu al-Salt al-Thaqafi and Adi ibn Zayd al-Hiri compose verses celebrating Sayf ibn Dhi Yazan's perseverance and eventual triumph, praising his long journey seeking help from Caesar and Chosroe before achieving victory through Persian forces.
story: According to Ibn Ishaq, Abu al-Salt b. Rabi'a al-Thaqafi spoke the following verses. However, Ibn Hisham attributed them to Umayya b. Abi al-Salt. "Let vengeance seekers be like Ibn Dhu Yazan, who took to the sea and for ages sought a way to his enemies, Going to Caesar when his journey seemed right, but finding there none of what he sought. Then he turned after ten years to Chosroe, disregarding his life and the cost Until he came bringing a band of free men; how quickly you sped! What a fine group of men; I never saw their like before. Persian braves, mighty warriors, archers, like lions raising their cubs in the forests, Their bows shooting arrows like howdah poles, swiftly dispatching their enemies. You sent lions against black dogs, their prey defeated and lost in the land So drink up, enjoy your crown, and settle at the top of Ghumdan's palace as your permanent home. Those were fine deeds. They were not two pails of milk mixed with water and later turning to urine!" It is said that Ghumdan mentioned here was a palace in Yemen built by Ya'rub b. Qahtan; it was owned after him by Wa'ila b. Himyar b. Saba'. It was reputed to be 20 storeys high. God knows best. Ibn Ishaq relates that 'Adi b. Zayd al-Hiri, of the Banu Tamim, spoke these verses, "What after San'a'? There lived governors of abundant gifts. Its builders raised it high to the scattering clouds, its chambers musk-scented. Guarded by the mountains against the enemy hordes, its heights unscalable. The hooting of owls pleasingly answered at night by the flute players. Fate led there the army of free men, their knights in procession, Crossing the desert on mules bearing death, accompanied by their foals, Until the princes saw them from the castle heights, their divisions armour-clad, The day they shouted at the barbarians and al-Yaksum, 'Damn all who flee!' A day long remembered, when a prosperous way of life ended which had been secure, A day when the one was replaced by the many, so times change, many the marvels, After the Tubba kings came noblemen whose satraps held quiet sway there."
lessons: Perseverance through repeated rejection and failure — as Sayf experienced when Caesar turned him away — is honored and remembered long after the struggle is over.
The poetic tradition of commemorating real human struggle reminds us that our hardships are worth documenting and reflecting upon, as they carry lessons for generations to come.
What seems like an impossibly long journey toward justice or restoration can ultimately be achieved when one refuses to abandon hope and keeps seeking new paths forward.
practical_advice: Reflect on a time in your past when you were rejected or turned away from something you sought — write down how that experience ultimately shaped your growth or redirected you toward something better.
Consider expressing your own journey through a creative outlet such as writing, poetry, or journaling, as giving narrative form to your struggles can bring clarity and emotional release.
When you face repeated doors closing, take Sayf's example and ask yourself whether you have truly exhausted every avenue, or whether there is still one more path worth trying.

title: The End of Abyssinian Rule in Yemen: 72 Years of Dominion Concluded
summary: The text documents the conclusion of 72 years of Abyssinian rule over Yemen, which passed through four rulers — Aryat, Abraha, Yaksum ibn Abraha, and Masruq ibn Abraha — before being ended by the Persian forces under Wahriz.
story: The rule of the Abyssinians in Yemen, lasting between Aryat's arrival and the death of Masruq b. Abraha at the hands of the Persians and the removal of the Abyssinians, was 72 years. Their dominion was passed through four rulers, Aryat, then Abraha, then Yaksum b. Abraha, and finally Masruq b. Abraha.
lessons: All worldly dominion is temporary — even 72 years of established power came to an end, reminding us that no unjust authority endures forever.
Historical cycles of rise and fall teach us that placing ultimate trust in any political power or worldly institution is a form of misplaced reliance.
The oppressed communities who endured Abyssinian rule for decades teach us that patience through prolonged hardship can eventually yield relief, even if it takes generations.
practical_advice: When you feel trapped in a difficult circumstance that seems permanent, remind yourself of historical examples where long-standing difficulties did eventually change — this can restore a sense of hope.
Avoid placing your sense of security entirely in external institutions, leaders, or circumstances; invest instead in strengthening your inner faith and values which remain stable through change.
Take time this week to reflect on a difficulty in your own life that has lasted a long time — write down any signs, however small, that indicate change is possible.

title: Persian Rule in Yemen Ends: The Prophecy Fulfilled Through Chosroe's Death
summary: Chosroe orders his governor Badhan in Yemen to bring him the Prophet Muhammad's head, but the Prophet prophesies the exact night of Chosroe's death at his own sons' hands — and when it comes true, Badhan and the Persians in Yemen accept Islam.
story: Ibn Hisham's account states that eventually Wahriz died and Chosroe appointed his son al-Marzuban b. Wahriz over Yemen; when he died, Chosroe appointed the latter's son al-Taynujan. Later Chosroe exiled al-Taynujan from Yemen and appointed Badhan to the rule; it was during his reign that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was appointed to his mission. Ibn Hisham also relates that he was informed, on the authority of al-Zuhri, that the latter said that Chosroe wrote the following to Badhan, "I am told that a man from Quraysh has appeared in Mecca claiming to be a prophet. Travel to him and seek his repentance. If he repents, well and good. If he does not, send me his head!" Badhan sent Chosroe's message to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who replied, "God has promised me that Chosroe will be killed on such and such a day and month." When this response was brought to Badhan he came to a halt and waited, saying, "If indeed he be a Prophet, it will occur as he said." And God did kill Chosroe on the day foreseen by the Messenger of God (SAAS). According to Ibn Hisham he died at the hands of his son Shirawayh. Others state that his sons joined forces to kill him. This Chosroe was by name Abrawiz b. Hurmuz b. Anushirwan b. Qabbadh. It was he who defeated the Byzantines, as referred to in the Almighty's words in the Qur'an, "A.L.M. The Romans have been defeated in the closest land" (surat al-Rum, XXX, v. 1-3). This will be explained later. According to al-Suhayli his death occurred the night of Tuesday, the tenth of Jumada al-Ula, of the year 9 AH. What happened, it is thought, though God alone knows, is that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote to Chosroe inviting him to accept Islam, he became enraged, tore up the letter and then wrote his own instructions to his governor in Yemen. Some accounts report that the Messenger of God (SAAS) replied to Badhan's emissary with the words, "This night my Lord has killed your lord." And it was as he said, Chosroe being killed that very same night by his sons as a result of his having changed from justice to tyranny. Having deposed him they appointed his son Shirawayh in his place. But he only lived on for six months or less after he had murdered his father.
lessons: When powerful forces threaten those who speak truth, divine protection and the fulfillment of prophecy demonstrate that ultimate authority belongs to God alone, not to kings or emperors.
Badhan's response — pausing to wait and see whether the Prophet's words proved true — is a model of open-minded, evidence-based reflection rather than blind allegiance to worldly power.
Tyranny destroys itself from within: Chosroe's deviation from justice led to his being killed by his own sons, teaching us that injustice ultimately undermines even the most powerful.
practical_advice: When you face threats or intimidation from those in positions of power over you, ground yourself in your principles and trust that standing firm in truth carries its own protection.
Practice the discipline of Badhan — when you encounter a claim or challenge that unsettles you, pause rather than react immediately, and give yourself time to observe and reflect before deciding.

Reflect on any area of your life where you may have drifted from fairness or justice in your dealings with others; make one concrete correction this week before small injustices compound into larger consequences.

title: Badhan and the Persians of Yemen Accept Islam
summary: After the fulfillment of the Prophet's prophecy about Chosroe's death, the Persian governor Badhan sends word of his and the Persians' acceptance of Islam, and the Prophet honors them by calling them 'of us and to us, the people of the House,' later affirmed through the saying about Salman al-Farisi.
story: Al-Zuhri added that when news of Chosroe's death reached Badhan, he sent word to the Messenger of God (SAAS) of the acceptance of Islam by himself and the Persians along with him. His Persian envoys asked, "To whom do we belong, Messenger of God?" He replied, "You are from us and to us, the people of the House." According to al-Zuhri, that was why the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke the words, "Salman is of us, the people of the House." It seems that this was after the emigration of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Medina. He therefore sent his commanders to Yemen to inform people of what was good and to call upon them to believe in God, the Almighty and Glorious. First he dispatched Khalid b. al-Walid and 'Ali b. Abi Talib; later Abu Musa al-Ash'ari and Mu'adh b. Jabal followed them, and Yemen and its people accepted Islam. Badhan died and his son Shahr b. Badhan ruled after him. It was he whom al-Aswad al-'Ansi killed after al-Aswad had pretended prophecy and taken Shahr's wife, as we will report, and expelled from Yemen the deputies of the Messenger of God (SAAS). When al-Aswad was killed the authority of Islam returned.
lessons: The Prophet's embrace of Badhan and the Persian converts with the words 'you are from us and to us' demonstrates that Islam transcends ethnic and national boundaries — belonging in faith is not determined by birth.
The feeling of belonging and being welcomed into a community is a profound human need; the Prophet's intentional words of inclusion were a powerful act of emotional and spiritual care.

Sending teachers and guides — like Khalid, Ali, Abu Musa, and Mu'adh — to newly converted communities shows that accepting faith is only the beginning; nurturing, education, and community support are essential to spiritual growth.
practical_advice: If you are new to a community, a faith, or a group and feel like an outsider, reflect on the Prophet's words to the Persian converts — genuine belonging is built on shared values and sincere commitment, not on origin or background.
Make a deliberate effort this week to make someone who is new or different feel genuinely included and welcomed, using specific words of belonging rather than merely tolerating their presence.

If you have recently made a significant change in your life — a new belief, a new commitment, a new direction — seek out a teacher, mentor, or community that can help you grow and sustain that change over time.

title: The Ancient Stone Inscription of Yemen Prophesying the Succession of Rulers
summary: An ancient stone inscription found in Yemen, reportedly from the Book of Psalms, prophesied in sequence the rule of Himyar, the Abyssinians, the Persians, and finally Quraysh the merchants over the land of Dhamar — all of which came to pass.
story: Ibn Ishaq states also that there was, so they claim, a stone inscription in Yemen, from the Book of Psalms, dating from ancient times which read, "Who rules Dhamar? Himyar the Good. Who rules Dhamar? The evil Abyssinians. Who rules Dhamar? The free Persians. Who rules Dhamar? Quraysh the merchants." A poet's words on this subject are recorded by al-Mas'udi as follows, "When Dhamar was named, it was asked, 'To whom do you belong?' 'To Himyar the good', came the reply. Asked to whom next, the reply came, 'To the Abyssinians most vile.' 'To whom next?' it was asked, and 'To the free Persians' came the reply. 'To whom next?' it was asked, and 'To the merchants of Quraysh,' came the reply." It is said that these verses quoted by Muhammad b. Ishaq were found inscribed at the grave of Hud, upon whom be peace, when the wind exposed his tomb in Yemen. That was shortly before the time of Balqis, in the days of Malik b. Dhu al-Manar, brother of 'Amr Dhu al-Adhsar b. Dhu.
lessons: Divine knowledge encompasses all of history — ancient prophecies recorded long before events occurred point to a Creator who is outside of time and guides history according to a plan beyond human comprehension.

History has a direction and a purpose; understanding that the rise of Quraysh and ultimately the Prophet was foreseen from ancient times can strengthen one's sense that their own life, too, has deeper meaning and purpose.
Even in eras of oppression — the 'evil Abyssinians' — the inscription reminds those who suffer that their condition is not the final word; it is one chapter among many in a larger unfolding story.
practical_advice: When life feels chaotic or purposeless, seek out the broader narrative of your faith tradition — understanding that history moves according to a divine plan can restore a sense of meaning during confusing times.
Reflect on your own life story as a series of chapters rather than a fixed state — just as Yemen passed through multiple eras of rule, your current difficult chapter is not your permanent reality.
Explore the prophetic and wisdom literature of your tradition this week; sometimes encountering ancient guidance can provide fresh perspective on modern challenges.

title: The Prophecy Inscribed on Hud's Tomb
summary: A mysterious prophecy was said to be inscribed on the tomb of Prophet Hud, listing the succession of peoples who would inherit power, ending with the merchants of Quraysh. The inscription was reportedly discovered when wind exposed his tomb in Yemen.
story: came the reply. Asked to whom next, the reply came, 'To the Abyssinians most vile.' 'To whom next?' it was asked, and 'To the free Persians' came the reply. 'To whom next?' it was asked, and 'To the merchants of Quraysh,' came the reply." It is said that these verses quoted by Muhammad b. Ishāq were found inscribed at the grave of Hūd, upon whom be peace, when the wind exposed his tomb in Yemen. That was shortly before the time of Balqis, in the days of Malik b. Dho al-Manar, brother of 'Amr Dhū al-Adh'ār b. Dhū al-Manar. It is said also that it was inscribed on Hūd's tomb and that those were his own words. It is al-Suhayli who related this; God knows best.
lessons: Divine plans unfold across generations, and no worldly power is permanent — only God's plan endures.
Prophecy and scripture remind us that God's knowledge encompasses past, present, and future, which should inspire trust in His wisdom.
Recognition that power eventually transfers to those who carry truth is a source of comfort for believers who feel overlooked or marginalized.
practical_advice: When feeling powerless or insignificant, reflect on how God's plan has always elevated those who hold to truth, even after long periods of obscurity.
Study the historical succession of civilizations to gain perspective that current hardships are temporary phases in a larger divine narrative.
Seek out authenticated historical and prophetic accounts that provide meaning and context to present struggles.

title: Al-Sātirūn, King of al-Hadr, and the Fall of His Fortress
summary: Al-Sātirūn, king of the magnificent fortress of al-Hadr, was betrayed by his own daughter al-Nadira, who opened the fortress gates to the besieging Persian king Sābur in hopes of marriage. Sābur, after marrying her, swiftly killed her when he realized she had betrayed her own father.
story: His story was related at this point by 'Abd al-Mālik b. Hishām because of what certain scholars of genealogy had stated in connection with al-Nu'māin b. al-Mundhir, mentioned above. They related that when Sayf b. Dhū Yazan went to al-Nu'māin and asked him for help in regaining control over Yemen, it was stated that al-Nu'man had descended from al-Sātirūn, king of al-Hadr. We earlier stated from Ibn Ishāq that al-Nu'man b. al-Mundhir was of the line of Rabī'a b. Naqr, and that it was reported from Jubayr b. Mut'im that he was a descendant of Qanw b. Ma'ad b. 'Adnān. These are, then, three statements on his genealogy, and Ibn Hishām went on to tell about the king of al-Hadr. Al-Hadr was a great fortress built by that king, whose name was al-Sātirūn, on the banks of the Euphrates. It was positioned high up and built tall and very spacious, its accommodations being like those of a large town. It was extremely well fortified and decorated in the utmost luxury, splendour, and good taste; it received taxes from all the surrounding areas. The genealogy of al-Sātirūn, as given by Ibn al-Kalbi, was al-Dayzin b. Mu'āwiya b. 'Ubayd b. Ajram, from the tribe of Sulayh b. Hulwan b. al-Hāf b. Qudā'a. Others said that he was of the al-Jarāmiqa, one of the Tawā'if kings whom he used to lead when they gathered to make war against one of their enemies. His fortress was between the Tigris and the Euphrates. Ibn Hishām went on to state that Chosroe Sābur Dhū al-Aktāf attacked al-Sātirūn, king of al-Hadr. Someone other than Ibn Hishām stated that the one who attacked the king of al-Hadr was Sābur b. Ardashir b. Bābik, the first of the Sassanian kings, who humiliated the Tawā'if kings and returned control to the Chosroes. Sābur Dhū al-Aktāf, this authority claims, came a long time afterwards. God knows best. That information comes from al-Suhayli. Ibn Hishām's account continues, 'And Sābur besieged al-Hadr for two years.' Others say four years. His reason for doing this was that al-Sātirūn had attacked Sābur's territory during the latter's absence in Iraq. The daughter of al-Sātirūn, whose name was al-Nadira, looked down and saw Sābur dressed in silk garments and wearing a gold crown studded with topaz, emeralds and pearls; and he was handsome. So she secretly sent him a message asking if he would marry her if she opened the fortress gate for him. He replied that he would. That evening al-Sātirūn drank wine till he was drunk; he was always drunk when he went to bed. Then she took the fortress keys from beneath his head and gave them to a servant of hers who opened the gates. Another account states that she directed Sābur's people to a wide water channel through which they entered al-Hadr. Yet another version has her telling them of a spell over the fortress; they knew it would not be conquered until a grey pigeon was taken, its feet dyed with the menstrual fluid of a blue-eyed virgin, and then released. If it dropped on the walls of the fortress the spell would be removed and the gates would open. Sābur did this, and the gates did come open. So Sābur went inside, killed al-Sātirūn, confiscated al-Hadr and then destroyed it. Then he took al-Nadira away with him and married her. While sleeping at night in bed, she began tossing and turning and could not sleep. Sābur had candles brought and they searched her bed, in which they discovered one myrtle leaf. Sābur asked her if that was what had kept her awake, and she replied that it was. Sābur asked her how her father had treated her. She answered, 'He furnished me with silk brocade, dressed me in silk, gave me the choicest food to eat and wine to drink.' Sābur commented, 'And then you treated him the way you did! You'd do the same to me quicker!' Then he tied her tresses to the tail of a horse and it galloped away until she was killed.
lessons: Betraying those who love and care for you, even for worldly gain or attraction, leads to destruction — loyalty and gratitude are sacred obligations.
Worldly beauty, wealth, and power are fleeting temptations that can lead a person to commit grave moral betrayals.
The story of al-Nadira is a timeless warning that those who are willing to betray their own kin for personal gain reveal a character flaw that will ultimately destroy them as well.
practical_advice: When facing temptation that requires compromising your loyalty to family or community, pause and reflect on the long-term consequences rather than short-term gain.
Cultivate gratitude for those who have sacrificed for you — ingratitude and betrayal corrode the soul and damage relationships irreparably.
If you have made a choice that harmed someone who trusted you, acknowledge it honestly, seek forgiveness, and commit to rebuilding integrity in your relationships.

title: The Poets Reflect on the Fall of al-Hadr — Verses of A'sha and 'Adi ibn Zayd
summary: Pre-Islamic poets A'sha Bani Qays and 'Adi ibn Zayd composed verses lamenting the destruction of al-Hadr and meditating on the impermanence of worldly power, wealth, and glory, using the fates of great kings as a reminder of mortality.
story: A'sha Bani Qays b. Tha'laha composed these verses about this: 'Consider al-Hadr and its people's ease; but do blessings last forever? Sābur kept his troops there two years, striking it with their adzes. And when he gave prayer to his Lord, and repented, He took no revenge. Did his Lord provide him extra power, and was there ever the like of his castle? He had appealed to his people, "Come to your task; it is set; Die nobly with your swords; I see death is ordained for those so fated."' Also 'Adi b. Zayd spoke the following verses in this regard, 'Al-Hadr was afflicted from above by a dreadful, terrible, huge calamity Due to a maiden who did not protect her father when he was delirious and off his guard; She gave him much evening wine, undiluted, which he drank abundantly. And wine deludes, its imbiber never quenched. That night she surrendered her people, believing the (enemy's) chief would marry her, But next morning the bride's reward was blood running in streams, Al-Hadr was ruined and confiscated; her chamber was fired and its contents burned.' 'Adi b. Zayd also recited the verses, 'O malicious blamer of fate, are you guilt-free and perfect? Or has time promised you a solid pledge to be ever safe? Indeed you are stupid and conceited. Or whom did you see fate made immortal, and who had a watchful guard lest he be harmed? Where now is Caesar Anoshirwan, the king of kings, or where Sābur before him? The noble Banū Aqfar, kings of Byzantium, not one of them remains remembered. And al-Hadr's lord, built by him, with both the Tigris and the al-Khabūr rivers made to flow to it, Of marble built and plaster adorned, with birds nesting in its heights, Unsuspecting of misfortune, yet all state now gone, its gates deserted. Remember the lord of al-Khawarnaq, who set forth one day, though right guidance requires thought. His wealth and properties delighted him, the sea was his command, and his palace al-Sadr, Yet his heart was converted and he said, "What joy for the living to death progressing, Then becoming like leaves dried and tossed by the wind and breezes?"'
lessons: The greatest civilizations and most powerful rulers are subject to decay and disappearance — only God's kingdom is eternal.
Poetry and reflection on history are powerful tools for cultivating humility and awareness of one's mortality.
Blaming fate for one's misfortunes while ignoring personal responsibility and the transient nature of worldly blessings is a form of self-deception.
practical_advice: When feeling attached to material success, status, or comfort, reflect on how even the mightiest kingdoms crumbled, and redirect your energy toward what is lasting and meaningful.
Use literature, history, and poetry as mirrors for self-reflection — they can reveal truths about human nature that help you grow emotionally and spiritually.
When grieving a loss — of wealth, status, or relationship — remember that impermanence is a universal condition, and that grief can become a gateway to deeper wisdom and gratitude.

title: The King of al-Khawarnaq Repents and Abandons His Kingdom
summary: A powerful king who owned the palaces of al-Khawarnaq and al-Sadr was warned by a wise man about the fate of kings before him. Deeply affected, he repented, abdicated his throne, dressed as a beggar, and retreated into the wilderness in devotion to God.
story: The man referred to in the verses above as the 'lord of al-Khawarnaq' was a king of by-gone days who had been warned by a learned man of his time about his conduct and practices. The king had exceeded his bounds, become arrogant and self-willed, and followed his own instincts unbridled. The learned man warned him by reference to the kings and states that had preceded him, how they had gone without a trace, and that nothing he took from others would not be transmitted from him in turn to those following him. This advice had impact and influence on him, and he was converted, giving thought to both his present and past and to the constraints of the grave. So he repented, returned to the faith and changed his ways. He abdicated, dressed himself as a mendicant, went off into the wilderness and the desert, and enjoyed solitude, shunning that pursuit of the senses and disobedience to the Lord of the Heavens followed by most people. His story was told in extensive detail by the Sheikh and Imam Muwaffaq b. Qudama al-Maqdisi, may the Almighty have mercy on him, in his book Al-Tawwābīn (The Repentants). Similarly, the hāfiz Abū al-Qāsim al-Suhayli reported it, thoroughly documented, in his book Al-Rawd al-Unuf (The Virgin Meadows) which is clearly written and well organized.
lessons: True wisdom is recognizing the impermanence of worldly power before it is too late — repentance and return to God are always possible, no matter how far one has strayed.
A single sincere piece of counsel from a wise person can transform an entire life — the company we keep and the advisors we listen to profoundly shape our destiny.
Letting go of status, comfort, and ego in pursuit of spiritual truth is one of the most courageous acts a human being can perform.
practical_advice: If you find yourself caught up in arrogance, materialism, or the relentless pursuit of status, pause and seek the counsel of someone wiser and more grounded than yourself.
Reflect regularly on what you would leave behind if your life ended today — this practice of mortality awareness can help realign your priorities toward what truly matters.

Do not let fear of losing comfort or status prevent you from making a profound change in your life; the king of al-Khawarnaq gave up everything and found peace — sometimes the deepest contentment comes from letting go.

title: The Rise and Fall of the Tawā'if Kings and the Restoration of Persian Empire under Ardashir
summary: After Alexander the Great defeated Darius of Persia, he fragmented the Persian empire by appointing separate kings over each region, creating the era of the Tawā'if petty kings. This fragmented rule lasted about 500 years until Ardashir ibn Bābik reunited the kingdoms and ended the Tawā'if era entirely.
story: An Account of the Tawā'if kings. Regarding the ruler of al-Hadr, Sātirūn, he was, as previously explained, considered a precursor to the rest of the Tawā'if ('factions') kings. He lived at the time of Alexander, son of Phillip of Macedonia, the Greek. This came about when Alexander defeated the king of the Persians, Darius son of Darius. Alexander subjugated Darius's kingdom, destroyed his land, arrested the best of his people, expropriated his produce, scattered the Persians to the winds, and determined that they should never thereafter join together and unite their forces. So he began appointing a separate king over each group of people in any given area, Arabs and non-Arabs alike. Thereafter each of these kings would protect his own area, preserve his own allotment, and exploit his own region. When one king died, he would be succeeded by his son or by some other one of his people. This system prevailed for about 500 years. Eventually, in the time of Ardashir b. Bābik of Banā Sāsān b. Bahman b. Asfandiyar b. Yashtasib b. Lahrāsib, their kingdom returned to its former condition. Ardashir took over, rejoined the petty kingdoms altogether in one, and ended the rule of the Tawā'if kings; so not a trace of them remained. Ardashir delayed besieging the oldest-established, greatest, and most powerful of these kinglets, the ruler of al-Hadr, since he was their leader and precursor. When Ardashir died, his son Sābur turned his attention to al-Hadr and besieged it until he conquered it, as told above. And God, all Glorious and Almighty, knows best.
lessons: Political fragmentation and division are tools of conquest — unity under a shared purpose and identity is a prerequisite for the survival of any community.
The rise and fall of empires follows patterns that, when studied, provide deep lessons about the role of divine will in human history.
No matter how firmly established a system of power appears, it is always temporary — God alone is the eternal sovereign.
practical_advice: When experiencing division or conflict within your family, community, or organization, study how disunity has historically led to collapse, and actively work toward reconciliation and common ground.
Study history not merely as a series of facts but as a moral narrative — ask yourself what lessons the rise and fall of powers hold for your own life choices.
When you feel that your community or efforts are fragmented and ineffective, remember that cohesion and unified purpose have historically been the turning point for great reversals of fortune.

title: Ishmael and Hajar Settle in the Valley of Mecca — The Well of Zamzam
summary: Prophet Abraham left his wife Hajar and infant son Ishmael in the barren valley of Mecca by divine command with only dates and water. When their provisions ran out, God caused the well of Zamzam to flow for them, and later the tribe of Jurhum settled nearby, bringing comfort to Hajar.
story: An Account of the sons of Ishmael who were the Arabs of Hijāz, and of events of the jāhiliyya period up to the time of the Mission. We have already, in a previous work, given an account of Ishmael, upon whom be peace, along with the Prophets. It tells of how his father Abraham, upon whom be blessings and peace, bore him away with his wife Hajar and made them to dwell in the Mecca valley between the mountains of Fārān, an unpleasant and desolate place. Ishmael was still unweaned at the time. Then Abraham went away and left them there, God having commanded him to do so. Ishmael's mother had nothing except a leather bag containing dates and a skin-bottle with water in it. When that was exhausted, God made the well of zamzam flow for Hajar, it being a tasty sustenance for the hungry and a cure for the sick, as is related in the long hadith of Ibn 'Abbās recorded by al-Bukhāri, God have mercy on him. Later, Jurhum, who were a group of the original Arabs who were descended from the ancient Arab tribes, settled near Hajar in Mecca, with no right to the water except for their own drinking and use. Hajar felt comfortable and secure with them. Abraham, peace be upon him, kept an eye on them all the time; it is said he would ride his horse, al-Buraq, on his journeys to and from.
lessons: Complete trust in God — tawakkul — means acting within one's means and then surrendering the outcome to Him, as Hajar did when she ran between the hills searching for water.
Divine provision often comes precisely at the moment of greatest need and apparent hopelessness, reminding us that despair in God's mercy is never justified.
Obedience to God's command, even when it requires painful sacrifice and separation from loved ones, is the mark of true faith — and God never abandons those who trust Him.
practical_advice: When you feel abandoned, alone, or without resources, remember that Hajar was left in a barren desert with an infant and God provided for her — bring your fears to God in sincere prayer before concluding that there is no way forward.

Practice tawakkul by distinguishing between what is in your control (your effort, your prayer, your preparation) and what is in God's hands (the outcome), and consciously release your grip on the latter.

When facing a profound test of faith that requires leaving behind security or comfort, seek the company of righteous people — as Jurhum's arrival brought comfort to Hajar, surrounding yourself with a supportive community can transform isolation into belonging.

title: Jurhum Settles Near Zamzam and Hajar's Comfort with Them
summary: After the miraculous appearance of zamzam water, the tribe of Jurhum settled near Hajar and Ishmael in Mecca, bringing comfort to Hajar while Abraham continued to visit periodically from Jerusalem.
story: Later, Jurhum, who were a group of the original Arabs who were descended from the ancient Arab tribes, settled near Hajar in Mecca, with no right to the water except for their own drinking and use. Hajar felt comfortable and secure with them. Abraham, peace be upon him, kept an eye on them all the time; it is said he would ride his horse, al-Buraq, on his journeys to and from Jerusalem.
lessons: Finding community in unfamiliar places is a blessing from God that brings comfort during hardship.
Even when physically separated from loved ones, maintaining connection through regular visits reflects true care and devotion.
God provides not only material sustenance but also human companionship to ease loneliness.
practical_advice: When feeling lonely or displaced, seek out a community of people who share your values and can offer genuine companionship.
Even if you cannot be present with those you love at all times, establish a consistent rhythm of checking in and showing care.
Recognize moments of unexpected comfort and community as gifts from God, and receive them with gratitude rather than suspicion.

title: Ishmael Grows Up, Marries, and Establishes His Lineage
summary: Ishmael grew to manhood among the Jurhumites, married twice, and fathered twelve sons and a daughter, becoming the ancestor of the Arab tribes of Hijaz.
story: Later, when the boy became a youth and he would busy himself with his father, the issue of making sacrifice arose, the sacrifice being in reality Ishmael. When Ishmael grew up, he married a woman from Jurhum, then left her and married another, the daughter of Mudad b. 'Amr al-Jurhumi. She bore him 12 sons whose names have been mentioned before. These were: Nabit, Qaydhar, Adhhul, Misha, Mismac, Mashi, Dimma, Adhar, Yatiir, Nabsh, Tima, and Qaydhuma. These are as given by Muhammad b. Ishaq and others using written sources. He also had one daughter whose name was Nasma; it was she he married to his nephew al-'isii b. Ishaq b. Abraham. From her were born to him al-Riim and Fads, and also al-Ashban, in one of the two accounts. The Arabs of Hijaz, with all their different tribes, are traced in their genealogy to Ishmael's two sons Nabit and Qaydhar.
lessons: The fruits of patience and perseverance in youth become the foundation of enduring legacy.
Family bonds and righteous lineage are among the greatest gifts one can leave for future generations.
God's plan often unfolds across generations, and our personal trials are part of a much larger divine story.
practical_advice: Reflect on the legacy you are building through your choices today, knowing that your actions affect those who come after you.
When facing transitions in life such as changes in relationships or family structure, trust that God's plan encompasses more than you can currently see.
Honor your roots and family connections as a source of strength and identity, especially during times of uncertainty.

title: Nabit and Then Jurhum Take Custodianship of the Ka'ba After Ishmael
summary: After Ishmael's death, his son Nabit held custodianship of the Ka'ba briefly before the Jurhumites seized control, governing Mecca for a long period while Ishmael's descendants refrained from contesting them.
story: After Ishmael, the governor and ruler-in-chief of Mecca and the custodian of the temple and of the well zamzam was Nabit, cousin through his mother to the Jurhumites. Later the Jurhumites gained control over al-bayt (the ka'ba), being jealous of their relatives, and ruled in Mecca and the areas around it for a long time in place of Ishmael's line. The first to gain control after Nabit was Mudad b. 'Amr b. Sa'd al-Raqib b. 'Aybar b. Nabt b. Jurhum. Jurhum was the son of Qahen. His line was also given as Jurhum b. Yaqtun b. 'Ayhar b. Shalikh b. Arafkhshud b. Sam b. Nuh al-Jurhumi. The Jurhumites settled in the heights of Mecca, at al-Qu'ayqi'an. Al-Samaydac, leader of Qatura', had settled with his people in the lower part of Mecca. The Jurhumites and Qatura', charged a tax on all who passed by them on their ways to Mecca. Ultimately warfare broke out between the tribes of Jurhum and Qatura'. Al-Samaydac was killed, and full power over Mecca and al-bayt thus were gained by Mudad. The descendants of Ishmael did not contend with him despite their large numbers, respected position, and the diversity of their locations throughout Mecca and its surroundings; this was due to their family ties to them and to the majesty of the sacred bayt.
lessons: Restraint in the face of injustice, when motivated by reverence for what is sacred, is a form of nobility not weakness.
Power gained through envy and conflict is inherently unstable and carries within it the seeds of its own downfall.
Respect for sacred things can transcend tribal rivalry and serve as a unifying force even among competing groups.
practical_advice: When you feel displaced or overlooked in a situation you have rightful claim to, ask yourself whether restraint might serve a higher purpose than confrontation.
Identify the sacred values and principles in your own life that you would not compromise even under pressure from others.
Channel feelings of anger or injustice into patient endurance and trust in God's ultimate justice rather than reactive conflict.

title: Jurhum's Corruption in Mecca and the Story of Isaf and Na'ila
summary: The Jurhumites fell into moral corruption in the sacred territory of Mecca, culminating in the desecration of the Ka'ba by two individuals who were transformed into stones as a divine punishment, which later became objects of idolatrous worship.
story: After Mudad, power went to his son al-Harith and then to his son, 'Amr. Later Jurhum acted wrongfully in Mecca and corruption there spread. People came to disrespect the sacred mosque. It is even said that one of their men, named Isaf b. Baghi, and a woman named Na'ila, daughter of Wa'il, met inside the ka'ba and that he committed fornication with her. And so God turned them into two rocks which the people set up near the temple as a warning to others. After a great deal of time had passed, these two rocks became the object of worship aside from God, during the period of Khuza'a. This will be further explained later. These became two idols set on high and named Isaf and Na'ila.
lessons: Disrespect for what is sacred invites consequences that extend far beyond the individuals involved.
What begins as a cautionary reminder can, over time, become a source of error if people lose sight of its original meaning.
Moral corruption in positions of power or trust is especially destructive and affects entire communities.
practical_advice: Cultivate a deep sense of reverence for sacred spaces, times, and boundaries in your own life as a guard against moral drift.
Regularly revisit the original meaning and purpose behind your rituals and practices to prevent them from becoming hollow or distorted.
When you notice moral corruption spreading in your environment, consider what role of witness or restraint you can play rather than going along with the tide.

title: Khuza'a Defeats Jurhum and Takes Control of the Ka'ba
summary: After Jurhum's long corruption in Mecca, the tribe of Khuza'a rose against them in battle, defeated them, and seized control of the Ka'ba, with Ishmael's descendants remaining neutral throughout.
story: When the misdeeds of Jurhum in the sacred land grew worse, Khuza'a, who had settled near the sanctuary, arose against them. They, Khuza'a, were of the line of 'Amr b. 'Amir who had left Yemen because of what he expected would occur with the 'Arim torrent, as we have related above. It is also said that Khuza'a traced their descent to Ishmael's sons. God knows best. Anyway, Khuza'a did join together to fight Jurhum and they engaged in battle; the descendants of Ishmael sided with neither party. Khuza'a, being the tribe of Bakr b. 'Abd Manat and Ghubshan, gained victory and ousted them from the ka'ba.
lessons: Persistent wrongdoing eventually invites a reckoning, regardless of how long a people remain in power.
Neutrality in the face of conflict can sometimes be an expression of wisdom and respect for what is greater than any tribal loyalty.
God's sacred trust passes to those who honor it; those who corrupt it will eventually lose it.
practical_advice: Reflect honestly on whether you are honoring the trusts and responsibilities placed in your care, before circumstances force a reckoning.
When caught between competing factions or disputes, consider whether remaining neutral might be the most principled stance.
Do not let the apparent stability of a corrupt situation lull you into complacency; work steadily toward what is right.

title: 'Amr b. al-Harith Buries the Ka'ba's Treasures and Jurhum Departs Mecca
summary: As Jurhum was expelled from Mecca by Khuza'a, their leader 'Amr b. al-Harith buried the Ka'ba's sacred treasures in the well of zamzam and led his people back to Yemen, composing mournful poetry about their loss.
story: 'Amr b. al-Harith b. Mudad al-Jurhumi, their leader, made off with the ka'ba's two gazelles, made of gold, the cornerstone, the 'black stone' that is, as well as decorated swords and other items, and buried them all in the well zamzam, which he filled with earth. Then he and his people moved out, back to Yemen. On this subject 'Amr b. al-Harith b. Mudad composed the following: "Her tears flowing and her eyes red and sore, in grief she said 'It's as though between al-Hajun and al-Safa there has never been a friend, and no evening pleasure in Mecca.' So I told her, my heart made to flutter as though by a bird between my ribs: 'Yes indeed; we were its people but changing times and evil misfortunes destroyed us, We were custodians of the ka'ba after Nabit, circling it around, our fine state clear. We took charge of the temple after Nabit with dignity, the wealthy gaining no favour with us. We had power and prestige, so be proud of our reign, no tribe there more proud than us. Did you not marry a woman to Ishmael, the finest man I knew, so his descendants are from us, and we are his relatives.' So what if the world turn against us? Life has its turns and tribulations It was the king, the almighty ruler, who used his power to oust us; thus, O people, do the fates decree. If the carefree sleep and I not rest, then I say, 'O enthroned one, why did Suhayt and 'Amir die?' In exchange for them I got faces I dislike, tribes including Yimyar and Yuhabir. We were despised, after having been in delight, passing years thus biting us. Tears flowed from eyes weeping for a land with a secure and sacred place and shrines, Weeping for a place whose pigeons are left unharmed, living safe there, and sparrows too. In it wild animals are safe and may leave it without fear - they would never be attacked."
lessons: Even in the bitterest of losses, acknowledging God's sovereignty over the decrees of fate is the beginning of inner peace.
The grief of losing a cherished home or status is a profoundly human experience that poetry and expression can help process and dignify.
What we are stewards of in this life is never truly ours; holding it with open hands prepares us for the moment it is taken.
practical_advice: When experiencing a significant loss of status, home, or belonging, allow yourself to grieve honestly rather than suppressing the emotion.
Find a creative or expressive outlet such as writing, poetry, or journaling to process feelings of displacement and sorrow.
Remind yourself regularly that your worth and identity are not defined by your position or possessions, which can be taken away, but by your character and relationship with God.

title: 'Amr b. al-Harith's Farewell Verses Warning the New Rulers of Mecca
summary: As Jurhum departed Mecca, their leader addressed the new rulers Banu Bakr and Ghubshan with solemn verses warning them that fate treats all peoples alike and they too would one day fall as Jurhum had fallen.
story: According to Ibn Ishaq, 'Amr b. al-Harith b. Mudad also spoke as follows addressing Banu Bakr and Ghubshan who came to power after themselves in Mecca: "O people, move along; your end is that one day you will not be able to move. Urge on your mounts, release their reins before your death, and do with us what you will do. We were people as you were; fate changed us; you will become as we became." Ibn Hisham stated that these verses were the ones he found to be genuine. And a certain authority on poetry told me that these verses are the first poems spoken by the Arabs and that they were discovered inscribed on rocks in Yemen, their author's name not being given.
lessons: The rise and fall of peoples and powers is a universal pattern that should inspire humility in those currently holding authority.
Warning others from your own painful experience is an act of wisdom and generosity, even when they may not be ready to hear it.
Awareness of life's impermanence should motivate us to act with integrity while we have the opportunity, not to despair.
practical_advice: In moments of power or success, actively reflect on the stories of those who came before you to maintain humility and perspective.
Use your own experiences of loss or failure as a source of wisdom you can share with others, transforming pain into purpose.
Practice regularly reminding yourself that your current circumstances, whether good or difficult, are temporary, which can reduce both arrogance in ease and despair in hardship.

title: Khuza'a Rules the Ka'ba for Centuries and the Origin of Their Name
summary: Khuza'a held custodianship of the Ka'ba for approximately three to five hundred years after defeating Jurhum, with their name deriving from their separation from the party of 'Amr b. 'Amir when migrating from Yemen.
story: So Khuza'a took control of the ka'ba, inheriting that rule by primogeniture until the last of them, Hulayl b. Hubshiyya b. Salal b. Ka'b b. 'Amr b. Rabi'a al-Khuza'i. It was his daughter Hubba who got married to Qusayy b. Kilab; to him were born his four sons 'Abd al-Dar, 'Abd Manaf, 'Abd al-'Uzza, and 'Abd. Eventually he gained control over the ka'ba, as I will later explain in detail if God Almighty wills it, and in Him is all trust. Khuza'a continued controlling the temple some 300 years; some say 500, and God knows best. Their period of rule was damned, because it was during their period that idols were first worshipped in Hijaz. Khuza'a were so-named because they takhazza'u, separated, from the men of 'Amr b.
