'Amir when they came from Yemen on their way to Syria. Khuza'a stopped in Marr al-Dhahran and settled there.
lessons: Long periods of apparent stability can mask slow spiritual and moral decline that only becomes visible in hindsight.
The origins of a people's name and story carry wisdom about who they are and how they came to be, worth remembering.
Even custodians of sacred trust can corrupt their stewardship; the outward position of honor does not guarantee inner righteousness.
practical_advice: Periodically examine whether the traditions and practices you uphold are still aligned with their original sacred purpose.
Seek to understand the history and roots behind the communities and institutions you belong to, as this context shapes present realities.
Do not mistake duration or stability for righteousness; actively and consciously tend to your spiritual and moral wellbeing rather than assuming it will maintain itself.

title: 'Amr b. Luhayy Introduces Idol Worship to Hijaz
summary: 'Amr b. Luhayy, the wealthy and influential Khuza'i leader, traveled to Syria where he encountered idol worship, brought the idol Hubal back to Mecca, and commanded the people to worship it, thus introducing idolatry to the Hijaz.
story: Their period of rule was damned, because it was during their period that idols were first worshipped in Hijaz. This came about because of their leader 'Amr b. Luhayy, God curse him; it was he who first influenced them towards idol worship. He was a man of enormous wealth. They say he gouged out the eyes of 20 camels to show that he owned 20,000 of them. It was a custom among the Arabs that anyone who came to own 1,000 camels should gouge out the eyes of one of them. By doing this he would avert the evil eye of malicious envy from them. Al-Azraqi was one of those who related this practice. According to al-Suhayli, in the time of al-hajj, the pilgrimage, he probably sacrificed 10,000 animals and awarded 10,000 sacerdotal garments per year. He would feed the Arabs, preparing for them food of dates mixed with butter and honey, and give them barley wine to drink. They say that his word and deed were like law among them, due to his generosity, and to the high regard and deep respect they held for him. Ibn Hisham states that a learned man told him that 'Amr b. Luhayy once left Mecca for Syria on business and reached Ma'ab in the Balqa' region. There at that time lived the 'Amaliq, the sons of 'Imlaq or, as some say, 'Imliq b. Lawadh b. Sam b. Nuh. 'Amr witnessed them worshipping idols, so he asked them why. They replied that if they asked the idols for rain it came, or for victory they won it. 'Amr then asked them to give him an idol he could take to Arab lands where it could be worshipped, and they gave him one named Hubal. This he brought to Mecca and set on a pedestal and ordered the people to worship and venerate it.
lessons: The corruption of religious belief often begins not with malice but with curiosity, admiration, and imitation of others without discernment.
Wealth, generosity, and social influence are not guarantees of spiritual wisdom; a person can be respected and deeply misguided simultaneously.
The leaders people follow shape the spiritual destiny of entire communities, making the choice of whom to trust and emulate of profound consequence.
practical_advice: Be discerning about whose influence you allow to shape your beliefs and practices, especially those who combine wealth and charisma with spiritual leadership.
When exposed to new ideas or practices from other cultures, pause and evaluate them critically against your own values and sacred knowledge before adopting them.
Reflect on how social pressure and the desire to please or imitate respected figures can gradually lead you away from what you know to be true.

title: The Origin of Idol Worship Among Ishmael's Descendants Through Attachment to Sacred Stones
summary: According to Ibn Ishaq, idol worship among the descendants of Ishmael began gradually when travelers would carry stones from the Meccan sanctuary with them and circumambulate them wherever they settled, eventually leading to the worship of any stone they happened to admire.
story: According to Ibn Ishaq, people claimed that the first to worship idols were of the tribe of Ishmael. They did so because as they started to travel outside Mecca, having felt constrained and overcrowded there and seeking living-space elsewhere, they would always carry with them a stone from the sanctuary to venerate the shrine. Wherever they settled they would put down the stone and circumambulate it as they would the ka'ba. Eventually they
lessons: Spiritual deviation rarely happens suddenly; it most often begins with small, seemingly innocent distortions of authentic practice.
Attachment to the outward form of worship, divorced from its original meaning and intention, can gradually become a substitute for true faith.
The human need for the sacred is so powerful that if not properly directed, it will find expression in other objects, even incorrect ones.
practical_advice: Regularly reconnect with the original meaning and intention behind your acts of worship to prevent them from becoming empty routine or distorted habit.
Be aware of how convenience and habit can slowly reshape your understanding of what is sacred, and periodically check yourself against authentic sources of knowledge.
When you feel spiritually displaced or far from your roots, seek to reconnect with genuine sources of guidance rather than improvising substitutes that may lead you astray.

title: The Introduction of Idol Worship by 'Amr b. Luhayy
summary: The story of how 'Amr b. Luhayy al-Khuza'i introduced idol worship to the Arabs, bringing the idol Hubal to Mecca and corrupting the pure religion of Abraham and Ishmael.
story: worshipped, and they gave him one named Hubal. This he brought to Mecca and set on a pedestal and ordered the people to worship and venerate it. According to Ibn Ishsq, people claimed that the first to worship idols were of the tribe of Ishmael. They did so because as they started to travel outside Mecca, having felt constrained and overcrowded there and seeking living-space elsewhere, they would always carry with them a stone from the sanctuary to venerate the shrine. Wherever they settled they would put down the stone and circumambulate it as they would the ka'ba. Eventually they took to worshipping any stone that pleased or inspired them, thus reverting to pagan ways and neglecting the religion they had followed. In the ~ahihcollection we find a statement from Abu Raja' al-'U@ridi as follows: "In the pre-Islamic period, if we found no rock then we would gather up a pile of dirt, bring a goat, milk it on to the pile, and then circumambulate it." Ibn Ishaq's narration goes on: "And they substituted another religion for that of Abraham and Ishmael, upon both of whom be peace, worshipping idols and following the false ways of the people before them."
lessons: Spiritual drift rarely happens overnight — it begins with small compromises and substitutions that gradually replace the original truth.
Following the customs of one's ancestors without reflection or critical examination can lead an entire community away from righteousness.
A single influential figure who introduces corruption can misdirect generations; the responsibility of those in leadership positions is immense.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually lost or confused about your faith, return to its original sources rather than following cultural habits that may have drifted from the truth.
Regularly examine your practices and ask whether they are rooted in sincere conviction or merely inherited habit, and seek knowledge to distinguish between the two.
Be mindful of the leaders and influencers you follow; evaluate their guidance against established principles rather than accepting it uncritically.

title: The Arabs Preserving Some Abrahamic Practices Alongside Idolatry
summary: Despite falling into idol worship, the pre-Islamic Arabs retained certain rituals from the time of Abraham — including pilgrimage, circumambulation, and sacrifice — but corrupted them by mixing in polytheistic innovations.
story: Nevertheless they did maintain some of the practices of the era of Abraham, upon whom be peace. These included venerating the ka'ba, circumambulating it, going there for pilgrimage and visitation, mounting the hills of 'Arafat and Muzdalifa, making sacrifice and invoking God's name at both pilgrimage and visitation and at the same time introducing innovations. The tribes of Kinma and Quraysh would make the cry, "Labbayka, All&humma,labbayka! At your service, 0 God, at your service! At your service; you have no partner except one who is yours. You rule him, he rules not." They would affirm His unity in their invocation and then include reference to their idols as well while asserting that He had power over them. God Almighty stated to Muhammad (SAAS), "Most of them do not believe in God without associating others" @rat YfisuLf, XII, v.106). This meant, "they do not state My unity out of knowledge of My right unless they also ascribe to me some associate of My own creation." Al-Suhayli and others state that the first to utter this prayer was 'Amr b. Luhayy and that Satan appeared before him as an old man and taught him that. 'Amr would listen as he recited it, then say as he had. And the Arabs followed his lead in so reciting. And it is established in al-Bukhzri's jahih collection that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would say "Qadz. Qad?', when he heard people recite the words, "At your service, 0 God, you have no partner." That meant: "Enough right there!"
lessons: Mixing truth with falsehood does not preserve the truth — it corrupts it; partial adherence to righteousness while compromising on its core is still a form of deviation.
The human heart has a natural inclination toward the divine, which is why even the idolaters retained some recognition of God, yet without purifying that recognition it leads to spiritual confusion.
Satan's strategy is often subtle: he does not ask people to abandon all good entirely, but to mix corruption into it gradually, making the deviation feel natural and even pious.
practical_advice: Audit the beliefs and practices in your life to ensure they are not a mixture of genuine conviction and inherited cultural habits that contradict your core values.
When you notice yourself justifying a compromise by pointing to the good you are still doing, take that as a signal to reflect more deeply on whether the compromise itself is acceptable.
Seek clarity in your spiritual life by studying original, authentic sources rather than relying on traditions passed down without examination.

title: The Prophet's Vision of 'Amr b. Luhayy in Hell
summary: The Prophet Muhammad reported seeing 'Amr b. Luhayy dragging his intestines in hell-fire, identifying him as the first person to introduce idol worship and set loose the sa'iba camels — a warning about the eternal consequences of introducing corruption into religion.
story: Al-Bukhei stated that Abti al-Yam= told him that Shu'ayh told him from a(-Zuhri that the latter heard al-Musayyab say, "A-bahira is the camel that is not milked by anyone, since its milk is left for the idols; and al-sz'zba is the camel that does not carry anything since it is set aside for their gods." Aha Hurayra stated that the Prophet (SAAS) said, "I saw 'Amr b. 'Amir al-Khuzzci dragging his insides in hell-fire; he was the first person who set loose the al-sz'zba camels." Al-Bukhai also relates it thus, and Muslim too, from a tradition from Salih b. Kisan, from al-Zuhri, from Sacidand back to Aha Hurayra. Muhammad b. Ishaq said that Muhammad b. Ibr2him b. al-Harith al-Taymi related to him that Abii Salih al-Samman told him that he heard Aba Hurayra say that he heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say to Aktham b. al-Jawn al-Kbuza'i: "0 Aktham, I saw 'Amr b. Luhayy b. Qama'a b. Khindif dragging his insides in hell-fire and I never saw two men more alike than you and he." Aktham replied, "Maybe this resemblance to him is harmful to me, 0 Messenger of God?" He answered, "No; you are a believer while he is a disbeliever. It was he who first changed the religion of Ishmael, who set up idols, who set aside al-bahira camels' milk for the idols, established certain animals as free from burden-bearing, disallowed sacrifice of the male of twin goats, or considered certain stallion camels free from work." Al-Bukheri said that Muhammad b. Abii Ya'quh Abii 'Abd All& al-Kirmmi related to him that Hassan b. Ibr%him told him, quoting Ytinus from al-Zuhri, from Wrwa that 'A'isha said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I saw hell with its denizens beating one another and I saw 'Amr dragging along his insides; it was he who first set loose the al-sd'zba camels."
lessons: One's physical appearance or worldly resemblance to another person does not determine one's spiritual fate — what matters is the state of one's heart and the sincerity of one's belief.

Introducing innovations that corrupt religion carries a uniquely grave accountability; those who mislead others bear not only their own burden but a share of the burden of all who followed them astray.
The distinction between a believer and a disbeliever is not superficial — it is the most fundamental distinction in how a person's life and choices are ultimately judged.
practical_advice: Do not allow surface-level comparisons — in appearance, background, or social standing — to cause you anxiety about your own worth or destiny; focus instead on the sincerity of your own faith and actions.
Reflect on whether any of your habits, traditions, or practices may be introducing harm into your life or the lives of others, and have the courage to abandon them even if they are widely accepted.

When you feel uncertain about your standing before God, return to the foundational question: do you believe sincerely, and do your actions reflect that belief? That is the ground on which your fate rests, not external comparisons.

title: The Quranic Condemnation of Pre-Islamic Pagan Innovations
summary: God Almighty condemned in multiple Quranic verses the pagan Arab practices introduced by 'Amr b. Luhayy — including dedicating animals to idols, killing children, and inventing false religious laws — as lies falsely attributed to God.
story: What is meant here is that 'Amr b. Luhayy, God curse him, introduced certain innovations into the religion by which he changed the religion of Abraham. The Arabs imitated him in adopting these and thus strayed far off, in ways that were deplorable and atrocious. Almighty God had expressed disapproval of these things in various verses of His unassailable Qur'm. The Almighty stated: "And do not speak lies with what your tongues describe, saying: 'This is lawful', and, 'this is unlawful', thus falsely attributing lies to God" (siirat al-Nahl, XVI, v.116). The Almighty also said, "God has not ordained the bahira, the sz'iba, the wa$ila, or the hami. But it is those who disbelieve who falsely attribute lies to God. Yet most of them do not understand" (swat al-Ma'rda, V, v.103). The Almighty also said, "And they put aside for what they know not a portion of what We have provided for them. By God, you will surely be questioned about what you have been falsely attributing" (siirat al-Nahl, XVI, v.56). The Almighty stated as well, "And they set aside for God a portion of the crops and livestock He bestowed on them and said, 'This is for God!' by their assertion, and, 'This is for our associate gods!' So that which is for their associates does not reach God, while what was God's does reach their associates! Evil is what they judge. Likewise, these associates have deemed appropriate for many of the polytheists the killing of their children; this is to destroy them and distort their religion. Had God not wished, they would not have done so. So ignore them and what they invent. They said: 'These are animals and produce that are sacrosanct. None but whom we say shall taste them.' This they claim, and, 'These are animals whose backs are sacrosanct.' And, 'These are animals over whom God's name shall not be invoked.' These are falsehoods attributed to God and He will punish them for their inventions. And they said, 'What is within the wombs of these animals is specifically for our menfolk and forbidden for our wives', whereas if it be stillborn then they are all partners in it. God will punish them for their characterizations. He is wise, all-knowing. Lost are those who killed their children foolishly and without knowledge, and who proscribed what God has endowed them with, attributing falsehood to God. They had gone astray, and they were not rightly guided" (swat al-An'zm, VI, v.136--40). In his ~ahih collection, al-Bukhari stated, in his chapter entitled: "The Arabs before Islam" that Abti al-Nu'mm and Abn 'Awma both related to him on the authority of Abo Bishr, from Sa'id h. Juhayr, from Ibn (Abbas who said: "If it please you to know the ignorance of the Arabs before Islam, then read those verses coming after verse 130 in siirat al-An'am: 'Lost are those who killed their children foolishly and without knowledge and who proscribed what God has endowed them with, attributing falsehood to God. They had gone astray, and they were not rightly guided'" (VI, v.140). We have given explanation for this verse and how they used to innovate with foolish and immoral laws, which their leader 'Amr b. Luhayy, God damn him, had thought gave some benefit and kindness to animals. In this he was a liar and a cheat. Yet despite this folly and error, those silly and ignorant people did follow his lead. In fact they followed him into errors even more grievous and serious than these, namely in worshipping idols along with God the Almighty and Glorious. They thus brought changes into the true religion and straight path God had sent down to Abraham, His true companion, which is worship of God alone without associate, and proscription of polytheism. They changed the ceremonies of the pilgrimage and the tenets of the religion without knowledge or proof or directives either strong or weak. In this they behaved similarly to those of the polytheist peoples who had preceded.
lessons: Fabricating religious justifications for cultural practices — declaring things lawful or unlawful without divine authority — is among the gravest of wrongs, for it distorts the relationship between people and God.

Communities that follow charismatic but misguided leaders without applying independent moral reasoning risk committing even greater injustices over time, as each generation normalizes what the previous one introduced.
The Quran's direct address of historical wrongs is a mercy — it names the errors of the past so that those who come after can recognize and avoid the same patterns in their own lives.
practical_advice: When someone presents a rule, norm, or expectation as though it has divine or moral authority, ask yourself whether it genuinely comes from a trustworthy source or whether it is a human invention dressed in sacred language.

If you find yourself in a community or environment where harmful practices are normalized and widely followed, recognize that popularity does not equal righteousness, and have the courage to seek a truer path.
Use scripture and authentic religious knowledge as a mirror to examine your own assumptions about what is right and wrong, rather than relying solely on what you were taught by culture or tradition.

title: The Arabs' Deviation from the Religion of Abraham: Idol Worship and Its Origins
summary: An account of how the Arab peoples, following the lead of Amr ibn Luhayy, gradually deviated from the pure monotheism of Abraham and Ishmael by introducing idol worship, describing the origins of idolatry traced back to the people of Noah and the proliferation of idols across Arabia.
story: ignorant people did follow his lead. In fact they followed him into errors even more grievous and serious than these, namely in worshipping idols along with God the Almighty and Glorious. They thus brought changes into the true religion and straight path God had sent down to Abraham, His true companion, which is worship of God alone without associate, and proscription of polytheism. They changed the ceremonies of the pilgrimage and the tenets of the religion without knowledge or proof or directives either strong or weak. In this they behaved similarly to those of the polytheist peoples who had preceded them. They were like the people of Noah. These were those who first associated others with God and who worshipped idols. For this reason God sent Noah to them. He was the first messenger sent to turn people from worship of idols, as we learn from Noah's story in the Qur'm: "And they said: 'Do not abandon your gods; do not forsake Wadd, Suwac, Yaghiith, Yacnq, and Nasr'; many did they lead astray. Give evil-doers increase of nothing but their error!" (siirat Niih, LXXI, v.234). Ibn 'Ahbas said that these were persons of virtue from among Noah's people; when they died people would attend their graves and eventually came to worship them.
lessons: Deviation from truth rarely happens all at once; it occurs gradually through small compromises and blind following of influential figures, reminding us to critically examine the beliefs and habits we inherit.

Even practices that begin with honoring the righteous — such as venerating graves — can drift into something spiritually harmful when knowledge and guidance are abandoned, showing how intention alone does not guarantee correctness.
God responds to spiritual corruption by sending guidance and messengers, demonstrating that no community is ever left without the possibility of return to the truth.
practical_advice: When you find yourself drifting from your core values or spiritual foundation, pause and trace back when the drift began rather than simply continuing forward out of habit or social pressure.
Regularly return to primary sources of religious knowledge rather than relying solely on cultural tradition or the practices of others, no matter how respected they may be.
If you feel spiritually confused or lost, recognize this as a signal to seek clarity — speak to a knowledgeable and trustworthy guide rather than dismissing the unease.

title: The Idols of the Arab Tribes: Wadd, Suwa, Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr
summary: A detailed account of the various idols worshipped by the Arab tribes across the Arabian Peninsula, tracing each idol to a specific tribe and location, illustrating the widespread nature of pre-Islamic polytheism.
story: According to lbn Ishaq and others, having made these changes to the religion of Ishmael, the Arabs continued having idols. Banti Kalb b. Wabra b. Taghlih b. Hulwan b. ' I m m b. al-Haf b. Qudag had an idol named Wadd. It was set up in Diimat al-Jandal. At a place called Ruhq there was SUW~:idol of the Banti Hudhayl b. Ilyas b. Mudrika h. Madar. Yaghnth was the idol for Banii An'am from Tayyi', and for the people of Jurash from Madhhij. It was set up at Jurash. Also there was Ya'nq in the land of Hamdm in Yemen, used by Bann Khaywitn, a tribe of Hamdm. In the land of Himyar was Nay, belonging to a tribe called Dhii al-Kula'.
lessons: The fragmentation of spiritual truth into countless local traditions and idols reflects what happens when communities lose a unified, divinely-guided center — a reminder of the importance of universal and consistent principles over tribal or cultural variation.
The proliferation of idols across Arabia shows that humans naturally seek something to worship and revere; the question is whether that object of devotion is worthy and true.

History demonstrates that falsehood, however widespread and socially normalized, does not become truth through popularity — a lesson in holding to principle even when surrounded by widespread deviation.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether the things you invest your greatest trust and hope in — whether status, wealth, relationships, or habits — are truly worthy of that trust, or whether they are modern equivalents of idols that cannot truly help you.

When you feel pulled in different directions by conflicting cultural or social expectations, anchor yourself to clear and consistent principles rather than drifting with whatever group you happen to be part of.
Study the root causes of practices you have inherited rather than accepting them unexamined, so that your choices are truly your own and grounded in something meaningful.

title: Khawlan's Division of Crops Between God and Their Idol Amm Anas
summary: The tribe of Khawlan divided their crops and livestock between God and their idol Amm Anas, consistently favoring the idol — a practice condemned in the Quran — illustrating the spiritual corruption of pre-Islamic Arab religious practice.
story: Ibn Ishaq goes on to report that Khawlan had in their land an idol called 'Amm Anas. They would, it is said, set aside part of their crops and livestock for division between it and God. Whatever of God's portion came into the share of 'Amm Anas they would leave for the latter, while if any set aside for 'Amm Anas went into the portion set aside for God, they would retrieve it for 'Amm Anas. It was about them that God revealed, "And they set aside for God a portion of the crops and livestock which He had bestowed upon them" (siirat al-An'zrn, VI, v.136).
lessons: When people prioritize their own constructed desires and traditions over their obligations to God, they reveal what they truly worship — a sobering mirror for self-examination about our own daily priorities.
The Quran's direct response to this specific practice shows that God is attentive to the details of human behavior and holds people accountable even for subtle forms of ingratitude and misdirection.

Gratitude must be expressed with consistency and sincerity; giving God the lesser portion of our time, attention, and resources while reserving the best for worldly pursuits mirrors the error of Khawlan.
practical_advice: Honestly assess whether you are giving God your best or your leftovers — your most alert moments or only your exhausted ones, your genuine charity or only what you can easily spare.
When you notice yourself consistently making excuses to avoid spiritual obligations while readily fulfilling worldly ones, treat that pattern as a warning sign worth addressing directly.
Practice gratitude daily by consciously acknowledging God's blessings before you allocate your time, money, and energy, rather than treating worship as an afterthought.

title: The Man Who Cursed the Idol Sa'd After His Camels Fled
summary: A man of the Banu Milkan tribe brings his camels to the idol Sa'd seeking blessing, but when the camels flee from the blood-stained rock, he becomes enraged, throws a stone at it, and composes verses declaring it worthless — an inadvertent testimony to the futility of idol worship.
story: Banii Milkan b. Kinana b. Khuzayma b. Mudrika had an idol named Sacd, a tall rock in open desert in their territory. One of their men took a herd of his camels to stand near by there to receive the idol's blessing, so they say. But when the camels, which were for meat and not riding, saw the idol stained with the blood of sacrifices, they shied away and scattered in all directions. Their owner became enraged and picked up a rock and threw it at the idol, saying, "May God not bless you; you caused my camels to flee from me!" Then he went off to find them and round them up. When he had done so, he spoke the verses: "We came to Sa'd for him to put us all together, but Sa'd scattered us; so we are not of Sa'd. Is Sa'd anything but a stone in the desert offering no prayer for anyone, whether misled or rightly guided."
lessons: Even those deep within systems of falsehood can arrive at moments of clarity when reality fails to match the promises made by false sources of hope — these moments of disillusionment, though painful, can be openings toward truth.
The idol could not fulfill what was expected of it, just as any substitute for God ultimately fails to deliver — whether that substitute is wealth, prestige, another person, or any worldly security.
Anger and disappointment, when directed honestly, can lead to insight; this man's rage produced a verse of clarity that exposed the emptiness of what he had trusted.
practical_advice: When something you have placed great hope in disappoints you repeatedly, take that as an invitation to examine whether your trust was well-placed rather than simply doubling down on the same source of hope.
In moments of frustration and loss, try to step back and ask what the experience is revealing about where your ultimate reliance lies, and whether that foundation is truly secure.
Use setbacks as opportunities to honestly reassess your dependencies, redirecting trust toward what is truly reliable and lasting rather than toward what merely feels familiar or culturally expected.

title: Hubal: The Great Idol of the Kaaba, and the Story of Isaf and Na'ila
summary: An account of the idol Hubal placed over a well inside the Kaaba by Amr ibn Luhayy, and the story of Isaf and Na'ila, two people said to have been transformed into stones after committing an act of desecration inside the Kaaba, later venerated as idols near Zamzam.
story: Moreover Quraysh had emplaced an idol over a well inside the kacba whose name was Hubal. As previously stated, Ibn Hisban reported it to have been the first idol set up by (Amr b. Luhayy, God curse him. Ibn Ishaq goes on to state that they adopted Isaf and Na'ila as gods, emplaced near zamzam, where they would make sacrifice to them. He reports that they were a man and a woman who had sexual intercourse in the ka'ba, and so God transformed them into two rocks. Ibn Ishaq went on to state that 'Abd AUah b. Abn Bakr b. Muhammad b. 'Amr b. Hazm reported that 'Amra quoted '.&'isha as saying, "We always heard that Isaf and Nx'ila were a man and a woman of Jurhum who fornicated in the kacba, so God, Almighty and Glorious, turned them both into stone." God knows best. And it has also been said that God did not postpone their punishment until they had fornicated there, but that He transformed them before the deed and that thereafter they were set up on the mountains of al-Safa and al-Marwa. When it was the time of 'Amr b. Luhayy, he placed them both at zamzam and people began circumambulating them. On this subject Ahn Talib spoke the verse: "Where the Ash'ariin kneel their mounts, where the streams flow from Issf and Na'il." Al-Waqidi reported that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered the destruction of Nx'ila, the day he conquered Mecca, there emerged from it a black woman with grey hair tearing at her face, lamenting and wailing loudly.
lessons: Sacred spaces demand reverence; when sanctity is violated, whether literally or spiritually, the consequences can be profound and lasting — a reminder that our inner sacred spaces also require protection and care.
Human beings have a tendency to venerate the wrong things, even transforming cautionary signs into objects of worship, which is why continuous, grounded religious education is so essential.
The destruction of false idols upon the conquest of Mecca demonstrates that no falsehood, however deeply entrenched, is permanent when truth arrives with authority and conviction.
practical_advice: Identify any 'sacred spaces' in your own life — your prayer time, your relationships, your integrity — and take deliberate steps to protect them from carelessness or violation.
When you encounter something presented as spiritually significant by your community, examine its origins carefully rather than venerating it simply because it has always been there.
Reflect on whether there are beliefs or habits in your life that began as cautionary lessons but have since become normalized — and commit to honest reassessment of them.

title: The Reaction of Quraysh to the Prophet's Message of Divine Unity
summary: When Prophet Muhammad proclaimed the oneness of God, the Quraysh reacted with astonishment and mockery, saying 'He has made the gods into one god! How very strange!' — capturing the initial shock and rejection his message provoked among the polytheists of Mecca.
story: The people of each house had an idol they would worship there and that if a man were to journey he would touch the idol before mounting. This was also what he would begin by doing upon his return before he went inside to his family. When God sent Mnlpmmad (SAAS) with the message of the unity of God, Quraysh said, "He has made the gods into one god ! How very strange!"
lessons: Truth often appears strange and unwelcome to those deeply conditioned by falsehood; the strangeness of a message to its audience says nothing about the message's validity.
The Prophet faced public mockery and tribal rejection yet continued his mission, demonstrating that the fear of social disapproval must not override the commitment to truth.
The Quraysh's reaction — shock and ridicule rather than engagement — illustrates how ego and tradition can become barriers to receiving life-changing guidance.
practical_advice: When a truth you share is met with mockery or dismissal, resist the urge to measure its worth by the reaction it receives; hold to what you know to be right with patience and humility.

If you find yourself reacting to new or challenging ideas with immediate rejection, pause and ask yourself whether your reaction is based on genuine reasoning or on the discomfort of having familiar assumptions questioned.

Practice the courage of conviction by identifying one truth you believe in deeply but have hesitated to express or act on because of fear of others' reactions, and take a small, honest step toward it.

title: The Tawaghit: Sacred Houses Venerated Alongside the Kaaba
summary: A description of the tawaghit — sacred houses and temples venerated by Arab tribes alongside the Kaaba — with their own custodians, priests, offerings, and rituals of circumambulation, reflecting the complex religious landscape of pre-Islamic Arabia.
story: Ibn Is@¶ went on to relate that along with the ka'ba the Arabs venerated tawZgbit. These were buildings they honoured in much the m e way as they did the ka'ba. They had custodians and priests, and offerings would be made to them just as to the ka'ba, along with the rituals of circumambulation and blood sacrifice. But they still recognized the precedence of the kacba over them because it was the building and mosque of Abraham, God's true companion, peace be upon him. Quraysh and the Banii Kinana had an idol named al-Wzza at Nakhla and Bana S h a y b a of Sulaym were its custodians and priests; they were allies of Bana Hashim. Khilid b. al-Walid destroyed it when he conquered them, as we shall relate. Al-Lat was the idol for Thaqif at al-Tilif and its custodians and priests were Banii Mu'attih from Thaqif. Abo Sufym and al-Mughira h. Shu'ba destroyed it after the people of al-Tilif accepted Islam, as we shall relate later. Manit was the idol of the tribes of al-Aws and d-Khazraj and the people of Medina who shared their religion in the coastal region, towards al-Mushdld in Qudayd. Abn Sufyan destroyed it also. Some say it was 'Ali b. Aba Tilib who did it, as we will relate. D h a al-Khalasa was an idol of Daws, Khath'am, and Bajila and the Arabs in their territory of Tabila. It was called "the southern (Yemeni) ka'ba", while the one in Mecca was known as the "northern (Syria, Shimi) ka'ba". Jarir b. 'Abd All* al-Bajli destroyed it, as we will explain. Qals was an idol of Tayyil and those two nearby mountains, meaning Ajn and Sulma, famous mountains referred to above. R i ' h was a temple of Uimyar and the people of Yemen, as above related in the story of Tubba', one of the kings of Himyar, and the story of the two rabbis when they destroyed it and killed a black dog that came from it. Rudal was a temple of Banii Rabi'a b. Ka'b h. Sa'd Ibn Zayd Manat b. Tamim.
lessons: The fact that even idol-worshippers acknowledged the Kaaba's precedence as the house of Abraham shows that an awareness of original truth persists even within corrupted traditions, and that no soul is entirely without some access to the light of guidance.

The systematic destruction of these temples upon the coming of Islam demonstrates that when truth is established with wisdom and authority, it removes the structures of falsehood — not through hatred, but through purposeful and just action.
Every corrupted system has its custodians and beneficiaries who maintain it; meaningful change requires addressing not just the symptoms but the structures and interests that sustain false systems.
practical_advice: Identify any structures in your life — habits, relationships, or thought patterns — that you maintain out of routine or vested interest rather than genuine benefit, and commit to evaluating them honestly.
Recognize that acknowledging a greater truth while still holding on to lesser falsehoods is a form of spiritual inconsistency; work toward aligning your actions fully with what you know to be true.
When working toward positive change in your community or environment, be patient and strategic, understanding that entrenched systems require consistent, principled effort to dismantle.

title: The Poet Al-Mustawghir and His Verses on the Length of Life
summary: The pre-Islamic poet al-Mustawghir, said to have lived 330 years, composed verses reflecting on the wearying length of life, the passing of centuries, and the inexorable movement of time — a meditation on mortality and the transience of worldly existence.
story: It is said that this al-Mustawghir lived for 330 years, he being the longest-lived of all Mudar. It was he who spoke the lines: "I have wearied of life and its length; I have lived for years in their hundreds; One century was followed by two more, adding up the years from all the many months. Is what is left anything but like what we spent? Days and nights pass and urge us on." According to Ibn Hishzm these lines should be attributed to Zuhayr b. Janab b. Hubal. Al-Suhayli stated that the persons who lived longer than two or three hundred years include this Zuhayr, Wbayd b. Shariyya, Daghfal b. Hanzala al-Nassaba, d-Rabic b. Dabac al-Fuzari, D h a &Isba' d-'Adwani and Nasr b. Dihman b. Ashjac b. Rayth b. Ghatfan. T h e hair of this last-mentioned blackened after it had turned white, and his back became straight after having been crooked.
lessons: Even an extraordinarily long life, stripped of its divine purpose and direction, can feel wearisome and empty — a reminder that the quality and orientation of life matters far more than its length.

The poet's reflection that what remains is like what has already passed captures the human experience of time: each phase of life feels significant in the moment but recedes into the same stream of passing days.
Awareness of mortality and the passing of time, rather than being a source of despair, can be a powerful motivator to invest the present moment in what is truly meaningful and lasting.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the relentless passage of time or the weight of accumulated years, refocus not on how much time has passed but on what you can do meaningfully with the time that remains.
If life feels wearying or repetitive, explore whether you have a clear sense of purpose that gives your days direction; often fatigue is less about effort and more about the absence of meaning.

Practice reflecting briefly at the end of each day on whether you used your time in ways that align with your deepest values, adjusting course gently rather than waiting for a larger crisis to prompt change.

title: The Palace of al-Khawarnaq and the Fate of Its Architect Sinammar
summary: The architect Sinammar spent twenty years building the magnificent palace of al-Khawarnaq for al-Nu'man, only to be thrown from its heights and killed by the king who feared he might build something equally grand for others — a story of betrayal and injustice that became proverbial.
story: According to al-Suhayli, al-Khawarnaq was a palace built by al-Nucmzn the Elder for Sabiir so that he could have his children there in it with him. It was designed by a man named Sinammar over 20 years. No finer building than it had ever been seen. Al-Nu'man feared that Sinammar might build a similar one for others, so he cast him down from its heights and killed him. A poet therefore spoke the following lines about him: "He recompensed me, may God give him His worst punishment, as Sinnamar was recompensed, and he was guiltless. Except his striving on the building for twenty years with the best bricks, saffron and ceramics. And when the building was complete and it towered like a mountain or a difficult height He threw down S i n a m on to the top of his head; that being, by God, one of the most ignominious acts ever done."
lessons: Sinammar's story is a timeless warning about placing absolute trust in those with unchecked power over you; his twenty years of devoted service were met with treachery, reminding us that ultimate security cannot come from human patrons alone.

The poet's outrage at this injustice reflects the universal human recognition that betrayal of trust and loyalty is among the most morally reprehensible acts — and that such wrongs are remembered and condemned across generations.

No worldly achievement, however magnificent, protects the one who created it from the ingratitude and cruelty of those who benefit from it; true security lies in a relationship with God, not with human benefactors.
practical_advice: If you find yourself giving everything to a relationship, job, or cause while receiving little recognition or security in return, honestly assess whether the arrangement is just and sustainable, and ensure you are not making yourself entirely vulnerable to another's whims.

When you experience betrayal after genuine effort and loyalty, allow yourself to grieve the injustice while also recognizing that your worth is not defined by the response of those who failed to honor your contribution.

Channel the energy of righteous indignation at injustice — your own or others' — into constructive action and advocacy rather than bitterness, remembering that God does not overlook oppression even when human courts do.

title: The Destruction of the Idols and Sacred Houses Upon the Coming of Islam
summary: Upon the spread of Islam, the Prophet Muhammad dispatched companions to destroy the various idol temples and sacred houses across Arabia, bringing to an end centuries of organized polytheism and fulfilling the restoration of Abraham's pure monotheism.
story: What is implied here is that all such houses were destroyed when Islam came. The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched troops of his men to each of these palaces to
lessons: The systematic dismantling of idols by the companions of the Prophet represents the culmination of a long spiritual struggle, demonstrating that patient, principled effort eventually transforms even the most deeply entrenched systems of falsehood.

The restoration of pure monotheism was not achieved overnight but through decades of sacrifice, preaching, migration, and perseverance — a reminder that meaningful change in ourselves and our world takes sustained commitment.
Every false idol — whether of stone, habit, desire, or belief — can ultimately be removed when we are guided by truth and willing to take decisive, grounded action.
practical_advice: Identify the metaphorical idols in your own life — those things you have habitually relied upon for security, identity, or comfort in place of God — and commit to a gradual, intentional process of replacing them with genuine trust in God.

Celebrate the small victories in your own spiritual journey the way history celebrates the conquest of Mecca: each time you overcome a harmful habit, a destructive thought pattern, or a misplaced attachment, recognize it as a genuine triumph worth acknowledging.

Draw courage from the historical reality that what seemed impossible — the transformation of an entire civilization from polytheism to monotheism — was achieved through faith, patience, and community; your own transformation, however difficult, is similarly possible.

title: Destruction of Pre-Islamic Palaces and Idols at the Conquest of Makkah
summary: The Prophet dispatched troops to destroy the pre-Islamic palaces and idols that rivaled the Ka'ba, until nothing remained to compete with it and God alone was worshipped.
story: What is implied here is that all such houses were destroyed when Islam came. The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched troops of his men to each of these palaces to destroy them, and he sent to those idols men who broke them to pieces. Ultimately nothing remained to rival the ka'ba, and God was worshipped alone and without associate.
lessons: The removal of falsehood, however grand or entrenched it may appear, is ultimately inevitable when truth prevails.
True victory is not merely military but spiritual — the establishment of worship of God alone is the highest goal.
Patience through long years of struggle is vindicated when God's decree finally arrives.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the dominance of falsehood around you, remind yourself that no structure built on falsehood can stand forever.
Channel feelings of anger at injustice into constructive action rather than despair, trusting that change comes in God's time.
Reflect regularly on moments when truth prevailed in your own life to strengthen your faith during current struggles.

title: The Lineage of 'Adnan and the Scholarly Debate on Arab Genealogy
summary: Islamic scholars debated the exact number of generations between 'Adnan and Ishmael, with opinions ranging from four to forty forebears, and leading figures like Malik and Ibn Mas'ud cautioned against tracing genealogy beyond what is reliably known.
story: There is no question of 'Adrian being of the line of Ishmael, son of Abraham, upon both of whom be peace. What dispute there is relates to the number of forebears there were from 'Adrian to Ishmael according to the various sources. At one end of the spectrum, there is the extreme view that considers there to have been forty; this is the view of Christians and Jews who adopted it from the writings of Rakhiya, the clerk of Armiya (Jeremy) b. Halqiya, as we will relate. Some authorities maintain there were thirty, others twenty, yet more fifteen, ten, nine, or seven. It has been said that the lowest estimate given is for four, according to the account given by Masa h. Ya'qtib, on the authority of 'Abd Allah b. Wahb b. Zumca al-Zumaci from his aunt, and then from Umm Salama who stated that the Prophet (SAAS) said that the line was: 'Ma'ad b. 'Adnm b. Adad b. Zand b. al-Yara b. ACraq al-Thara'. According to Umm Salama this Zanad was al-Hamaysac, al-Yara was Nabit, while Acraq al-Thara was Ishmael. This was implied because he was Abraham's son; for Abraham was not consumed by hell-fire, since fire does not consume moist earth, the meaning of al-thar~. Al-Daqatni stated that he knew of no 'Zand' except the one in this tradition, and Zand h. al-Jawn, who was Aba Dalana the poet. Abo al-@sim al-Suhayli and other Imams stated that the time lapse between 'Adnm and Ishmael was too great for there to have been only four, ten, or even twenty generations between them. That, they said, was because the age of Ma'ad son of 'Adnzn was twelve at the time of Bukhtunassar (Nebuchadnezzar).
lessons: Intellectual humility is a virtue — acknowledging the limits of one's knowledge is more honorable than speculation presented as fact.
Scholarly disagreement, when conducted respectfully, reflects the richness of the Islamic intellectual tradition rather than weakness.
Not every question requires a definitive answer; learning to be comfortable with uncertainty is a sign of mature faith.
practical_advice: When faced with confusing or conflicting information, seek out the most reliable sources and be honest about what remains unknown rather than forcing a false certainty.
Practice saying 'I do not know' as the scholars did — it is a statement of integrity, not ignorance.
When you feel lost in complexity, anchor yourself to what is firmly established and leave peripheral disputes aside.

title: Imam Malik's Caution Against Tracing Genealogy Beyond 'Adnan
summary: Imam Malik and other leading scholars cautioned against tracing Arab genealogy beyond 'Adnan to Ishmael or Adam, citing the unreliability of such claims and emphasizing intellectual honesty.
story: And this is why Malik, God bless him, did not enthuse over the attempt at tracing genealogy back to before 'Adrian. Al-Suhayli commented further, 'We have merely discussed tracing back these lines to accord with the school of thought of those scholars who favour and do not disapprove of it, men such as Ibn Ishitq, al-Bukhm, al-Zubayr b. Bakkw, al-Tabari, and others.' As for Mxlik, God have mercy on him, he expressed disapproval when asked about someone tracing his descent back to Adam and commented: 'Whence comes to him knowledge of that?' When he was asked about tracing back to Ishmael, he expressed similar disapproval, asking, 'Who could provide such information?' Mdik also disliked tracing the genealogy of the prophets, such as saying, 'Abraham son of so-and-so'. Al-Mu'ayp stated this in his book. Al-Suhayll commented also that Mali's viewpoint was analogous to what was related of Wrwa b. al-Zubayr who is reported to have said, 'We have found no one who knows the line between 'Adnm and Ishmael.' It is reported that Ibn 'Abh% said, 'Between 'Adrian and Ishmael there were 30 ancestors who are unknown.' Ibn 'Abbas is also reputed to have said when he traced back lines of descent as far as 'Adnm: 'The genealogists have lied. Twice or thrice.' And that (scepticism) is even more characteristic of Ibn Mascnd, whose (attitude) was like that of Ibn 'Abhas. Wmar b. al-Kha@b stated, 'We carry back the genealogy only as far as 'Adrian.'
lessons: The greatest scholars demonstrated their greatness precisely by knowing the boundaries of their knowledge.
Saying 'I do not know' when one genuinely does not know is an act of courage and piety, not weakness.
Overconfidence in unverifiable claims can lead to larger errors in belief and practice — humility guards against this.
practical_advice: When you feel pressure to have an answer for everything, take courage from the example of Imam Malik who freely said 'I do not know' even on matters related to sacred history.
In stressful debates or disputes, focus on what is firmly established rather than defending uncertain positions.
Practice distinguishing between what you know with certainty, what you believe with probability, and what is simply unknown — this clarity reduces anxiety.

title: The Poem of Ibn al-Nashi' Praising the Prophet's Miracles and Noble Lineage
summary: The scholar Ibn al-Nashi' composed a celebrated poem enumerating the Prophet Muhammad's miracles — including the splitting of the moon, water springing from his fingers, and the inimitable Quran — and tracing his noble lineage.
story: Nothing could be finer than the verses stating the Prophet's genealogy by Imm Abii al-'Abbxs 'Abd All& b. Muhammad al-Nsshi' in the famous poem ascribed to him as follows: 'I praised the Messenger of God, seeking by praising him a plenitude of the best of rewards for myself; I praised a man beyond praise, unique in his qualities, far beyond others far or near, A Prophet whose light shone high in places east, his gifts apparent to the people of places west. The prophets brought him to us before his coming, news of him spreading on all sides. The foretelling divines began calling his name, using it to feud off the impact of misleading ideas. Idols were given voice declaring to God their innocence of those who told the untruths. They spoke in clear words to the unbelievers, 'A prophet has come to you from Lu'ayy b. Ghdib.' And evil spirits wanted to eavesdrop but shooting stars scattered them from their seats. He led us to where we would never have found our way, so lengthy was our blindness to clear philosophies. He brought evidences showing that they were signs from one Almighty in reward and punishment One of which was the splitting of the moon in two, mountain tops then being covered as if by a turban by the moonbeams. And another was the rising of water springs between his fingertips while no wells or pools were near. By it he quenched the thirst of a large crowd; and the water flowed into rivulets in all directions, down to the plain land. And a well brimmed over at his arrow's touch, one before too dry for a single drinker's palate. And the udder flowed to his palm's rub, which before when squeezed for liquid had no teat to respond to a milkman's touch. And there was eloquent utterance from the hand of a fortuneteller about plans of an enemy bent on attack. And his being told of matters before their being, and of the after-effects to come when these did arise. And from those signs there was revelation he brought, one that is expedient, to come with a multitude of wonders. Thought could not conceive of it, and so the like of which no eloquent man could articulate, nor did it occur to the mind of any preacher. It encompassed all knowledge, embraced all wisdom, eluding the aim of the scheming and deceitful. He brought it to us, not through a trickster's tales, a scribe's pages, or an author's description. Revelation comes to him sometimes to answer a questioner, to respond to a solution seeker, or in the exhortation of someone who is conversing; For the bringing of proofs, the imposition of laws, the telling of narration, or the interpretation of purpose; For the quotation of proverbs, the proving of a case, the revealing of some disbeliever, or the suspension of a liar; In the meeting of some assembly, in the thick of some battle, or as difficult, puzzling problems occur. So, it came down in different ways, with straight-out meaning and flowing varieties. Its verses confirm each other, as if their meanings were scrutinized by a watchful eye. And the inability of man to achieve such as we have described is well known by the many who attempted (to imitate it). For father he had 'Abd All&, the most noble of fathers, inheriting from him the most noble of qualities. And from Shayba, be he praised, in whom Quraysh had pride above all others of honour and high station; He who by whose face clouds were sought to bring rain, and whose views were'
lessons: Reflecting on the miracles and noble qualities of the Prophet deepens love for him and strengthens one's connection to the faith.
The Quran's inimitability — its inability to be replicated despite centuries of challenge — is itself a living proof of divine origin.
Expressing gratitude and love through art, poetry, and remembrance is a spiritually enriching practice cherished across Islamic history.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually low or disconnected, spend time reading or listening to accounts of the Prophet's miracles and character to reignite your faith.
Cultivate gratitude by regularly reflecting on the specific blessings the Prophet's guidance has brought into your life.
Consider expressing your faith creatively — through writing, poetry, or reflection — as a way of deepening your personal connection to what you believe.

title: Poetic Ode on the Noble Lineage of the Prophet Muhammad
summary: An elaborate poetic ode by Ibn Sharshir traces the noble ancestral lineage of Prophet Muhammad through generations of distinguished forebears, celebrating each ancestor's unique virtues and qualities.
story: different ways, with straight-out meaning and flowing varieties. Its verses confirm each other, as if their meanings were scrutinized by a watchful eye. And the inability of man to achieve such as we have described is well known by the many who attempted (to imitate it). For father he had 'Abd All&, the most noble of fathers, inheriting from him the most noble of qualities. And from Shayba, be he praised, in whom Quraysh had pride above all others of honour and high station; H e who by whose face clouds were sought to bring rain, and whose views were followed in troubled times. And from Hiishim, who built his lofty, honourable repute through brave exploits and his gracious generosity. And 'Abd M a d , who taught his people not to exceed the bounds of their ambitions, and how to control their desires. And Qu~ayy,whose nobility of line is at such a level as to be beyond anyone's reach; Through him God assembled the tribes after they had been split apart by the greed of thieving hands. And Kilsb attained a fortress in the peaks of glory, beyond the reach of all, near and far alike. And Murra, whose firmness of resolve was not dissolved by the disgrace of any fool or the evil of any sinner. And Ka'b, whose glory was too high for any seeker of fame to foUow his footsteps; and who reached the highest rank with the least of efforts. And Lu'ayy who wiped out his enemies; thus he subdued the supercilious, the haughty, and the most victorious. And in G h l i b was such courage that he refused for his people to be overcome by any mighty one, and repelled from them every violent enemy. And Fihr had in Quraysh an oratory to rely upon when conflicts erupted. And Malik was always the best of maliks (lords), the most honourable of companions, the most honourable of masters. And al-Nadr had such elevation as to outstrip all, right on up to meet the gleaming light of the stars. By my life, Kinma displayed even before him qualities too fine for any conqueror to attain. And before him Khuzayma established in his honour an ancient heritage from honoured kin. And Mudrika, no man ever yudrik (achieved) the like of what he had. H e was most vimous and most high, beyond mere base pursuits. And Ilyas, for him al-ya's (despair) was dictated for his enemies even before the squadrons met in battle. And for Mu& the total glory had always been his whenever mounted warriors had met in battle. And Nu% achieved among his people a leadership position, so lofty as to outstrip the gaze of anyone staring hard to see. And Ma'ad was an 'udda (instrument) for his allies when they feared the plots of their warring foe. And 'Adnm is still, if his virtue be assessed, unique and above all peer or companion. And for Udd, virtue ta'adda (was discharged) emanating from his self-resolve and an inheritance passed down from grand, wise chieftains. And in Udad there was calm judgement adorned with sagacity, for calm judgement gains from a frowning of eyebrows. And Hamaysac continued to reach ever-new heights, and follow after the desired far-reaching aspirations. And Nabt who came from the family tree of glory had built up his lofty refuge on the towering mountains. And Qaydhir was accorded the generosity of Hztim, the wisdom of L u q m and the determination of H%jib. These were progeny of Ishmael, true to his vows, a man whose level of greatness could never be exceeded by others. And God's 'companion' (Abraham) who was the most noble of men ever to walk or ride upon earth. And Tzrih, whose lasting reputation for generosity had made apparent his laudable deeds. And N&iir, the nahhar (butcher) of his enemies had many memorable accomplishments those accounting cannot enumerate. And Ashra', a forest lion in battle, cutting a person to shreds with his sharp weapons. And Arghii, a deputy to be trusted in warfare, tenacious against a mean-spirited antagonist. And Faligh is not left behind his people in virtue, nor does he pass beneath them in rank. And ShiQii and Arfakhshidh and Sm, had qualities so great as to protect them from any detractor or accuser. And Nuh (Noah) still retains high regard with God, who counts h i among good men, well-chosen. And Lamk, his father, stood out among the finest, brave against the mailclad warrior. And before Ldmk there was yet Mutawashlikh, who drove away his enemies wi!h tough spears. And Idris the prophet was one with status with God beyond compare with that of any ambition. And Yarid was a man great among his people's leaders, scornful of the base, clear and precise in his purpose. And Mihlayil had understanding of virtues from which all unpleasant defects had been refined away. And Qayniin of aid acquired the glory of his people, gaining the summit of prestige by the stride of his riding camels. And Anash devoted himself to glory and deemed it far above b s e desires. And Shith stuck to principle, a man of virtue and nobility, free of mean faults. All of these gained from Adam's light and from his stem gathered the fruits of goodness. And the Messenger of God was the most noble ever horn; he emerged from the line of all these fine, distinguished individuals. IIis male forebears matched his female ancestors, all clear of any taint of disgrace. May there be upon him the peace of God on every day that dawn gives us light and at every sunset too.
lessons: The Prophet's lineage demonstrates that nobility of character is cultivated across generations — each individual's virtue contributes to a larger legacy of goodness.
Recognizing the chain of goodness that preceded you can instill a sense of purpose and belonging; you are part of something much greater than yourself.
True honour is measured not by worldly power alone but by qualities like generosity, wisdom, firmness of resolve, and service to one's community.
practical_advice: When feeling lost or without direction, reflect on the positive qualities of those who came before you — family, community elders, or spiritual figures — and consider what legacy you wish to contribute to.
Practice gratitude by acknowledging the sacrifices and virtues of your ancestors or mentors; this perspective can ground you during times of anxiety or confusion.
Identify one quality from those you admire most — generosity, resolve, or calm judgement — and make a conscious effort to embody it in your daily interactions.

title: Mudar's Fall and the Origin of Camel Chanting
summary: Mudar, known for his fine voice, fell from his mount and dislocated his arm; his pained cry of 'Oh, my hands!' captivated the camels and inadvertently gave rise to the Arab tradition of chanting to guide camels.
story: They say that Mudar was the first to use chants with camels. That is because he had a fine voice and because one day he fell off his mount and dislocated his arm. In pain he shouted, "Wiiyadayiih! Wayadayah!" ("Oh, my hands, my hands!") and at that the camels stretched out their necks listening.
lessons: Moments of personal pain and vulnerability can unexpectedly give birth to something meaningful and lasting — suffering is not always without purpose.
God can bring benefit out of hardship in ways we could never anticipate; a moment of injury became the origin of an enduring cultural tradition.
Our natural expressions of distress are heard — even the animals responded to Mudar's cry — reminding us that we are never as alone in our pain as we may feel.
practical_advice: When you are in pain, allow yourself to express it honestly rather than suppressing it; authentic expression can be the beginning of healing and sometimes even something creative or constructive.
Reflect on past hardships in your life and ask yourself: what unexpected good emerged from that difficulty? This exercise builds resilience and trust in God's wisdom.
Use difficult moments as opportunities for self-discovery — Mudar's pain revealed a gift (his voice) he may not have known would matter so much.

title: The Naming of Mudrika and Tabikha
summary: Two brothers, 'Amir and 'Amr, went hunting and when their camels ran off, 'Amir chased and caught them while 'Amr stayed to cook; their father named them Mudrika (the catcher) and Tabikha (the cook) based on their actions.
story: According to Ibn Ishaq, Mudrika's real name was 'Amir, and that of Tabikha was 'Amr. One day they went hunting and while they were cooking their catch, their camels ran off. 'Amir chased after them and caught them, while his brother sat cooking. When they returned to their father and related this to him, he commented to 'Amir, "You are Mudrika" (the catcher), while to 'Amr he said, "You are Tiibikha" (the cook).
lessons: Our choices and actions in everyday moments define who we are and how we are remembered — character is revealed not in grand occasions but in ordinary decisions.
Different people possess different strengths and roles; both the one who chases and the one who tends the fire are necessary, and neither should be diminished.
A wise mentor or parent can see a person's defining quality and affirm it, which can shape a person's sense of identity and confidence for life.
practical_advice: Reflect on a moment when someone recognized a strength in you that you had not fully acknowledged in yourself; allow that recognition to encourage you rather than burden you with expectation.
Avoid comparing your response to challenges with that of others — 'Amir and 'Amr both contributed differently and both were named with honour; your unique contribution has worth.

When you face a sudden disruption or crisis, notice your instinct: do you chase the problem or tend what remains? Both responses can be valid — what matters is acting with integrity and doing what you are best suited for.

title: The Prophet's Assertion of His Lineage from Banu al-Nadr b. Kinana
summary: A delegation from the Kinda tribe came to the Prophet claiming kinship with him, but he firmly and repeatedly clarified that he was from Banu al-Nadr b. Kinana, neither contradicting his mother nor refuting his father.
story:
Al-Tabarm said, "We were told by IbrZhim b. Na'ila al-Asbahani, by Ismacil b. 'Amr al-Bajali, and by al-Hasan b. Sdih from his father, from al-Jashish al-Kindi who said, 'A group of people from Kinda tribe came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, "You are from us," claiming kinship with him. But he responded, "No, we're Bano al-Nadr b. Kinana; we neither contradict our mother nor refute our father."'"

Imam Abu Wthmm Sacidb. Y*ya b. Sa'id said, "We were told by my father and by al-Kalbi on the authority of Abu Sslih from Ibn 'Abbas that the latter said, 'A man from Kinda, called al-Jashish, came to the Prophet (SAAS) and said, "O Messenger of God, we claim 'Abd Manaf as one of us." The Prophet (SAAS), turned away from him. The man came back with the same comment and again was ignored. Once more he said the same. Then the Prophet (SAAS) stated, "We are from Banu al-Nadr b. Kinana. We neither contradict our mother nor refute our father."'"

Imam Ahmad said that Bahz and 'Affan related to him as follows: "We were both told by Hammad b. Salama that 'Uqayl b. Abu Talha said ('Affan gave his name as 'Uqayl b. Talba al-Salmi) on the authority of Muslim b. al-Haysam from al-Ash'ath b. Qays, that the latter said that he came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in a delegation from Kinda. ('Affan added the words 'who did not consider me their best man') and said to him, 'O Messenger of God, we claim you to be one of us.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'We are Banu al-Nadr b. Kinana; we neither contradict our mother nor refute our father.'" Al-Ash'ath b. Qays commented, "By God, I never hear anyone denying that Quraysh is from al-Nadr b. Kinana without whipping him to the limit."
lessons: Honesty about one's identity and origins is a mark of integrity; the Prophet never allowed false claims of kinship to stand, even when they were flattering.
Standing firm in truth, even when others persistently push a false narrative, is a form of moral courage and a protection of trust.
One's lineage and roots are a trust; neither denying them out of shame nor falsely claiming what is not one's own reflects sound character.
practical_advice: When others misrepresent your identity or background for their own benefit, calmly and clearly correct the record without anger, just as the Prophet did repeatedly and with dignity.
If you feel pressure to agree with something false about yourself simply to please others or avoid conflict, remember that your integrity is worth more than their approval.
Ground yourself in knowing who you are and where you come from; clarity about your own identity gives you the confidence to respond to false claims without anxiety or defensiveness.

title: God's Selection of the Prophet Muhammad Through Noble Lineage
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) described how God chose Kinana from the sons of Ishmael, then Quraysh from Kinana, then Hashim from Quraysh, and finally himself from the descendants of Hashim, signifying a chain of divine selection.
story:
Muslim related in his sahih collection from a tradition of Abu 'Amr and al-Awza'i that Shaddad Abu 'Ammar related to him that Wathila b. al-Asqar told him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "God chose Kinana from among the sons of Ishmael, Quraysh from Kinana, Hashim from Quraysh and myself from the descendants of Hashim."

Abu 'Amr b. 'Abd al-Barr said, "Banu 'Abd al-Muttalib are known as the fa'ila, the family, of the Messenger of God (SAAS); Banu Hashim was his fakhdh, his tribal subsection; Banu 'Abd Manaf was his batn, his tribal subdivision; Quraysh was his 'imara, his mini-tribe; Banu Kinana was his qabila, his tribal confederation; and Mudar was his sha'b, his people. May God's blessings and peace be upon him always till the Day of Judgement."
lessons: Divine selection does not happen by accident; it reflects a purposeful, wise plan that unfolds across generations, reminding us that God's wisdom operates on a scale far beyond our own.
Every person's origin and family are part of a larger story that God has shaped; there is dignity in recognizing the role of lineage and community in one's identity.
Gratitude for one's blessings, including the blessing of guidance through the Prophet, is deepened when we reflect on how deliberately and mercifully God arranged the circumstances of that guidance.
practical_advice: When you feel that your life lacks purpose or direction, reflect on the truth that God's plans are deliberate and long-prepared; your place in the world is not accidental.
Take time to feel genuine gratitude for the blessing of faith and prophetic guidance by reflecting on the chain of people and events God arranged so that this guidance could reach you.
If you struggle with a sense of unworthiness, remember that God's mercy reaches people through carefully chosen means; being a recipient of that mercy is itself a gift to be honored.

title: Sama b. Lu'ayy's Death in Oman
summary: Sama b. Lu'ayy fled to Oman after a quarrel with his brother Amir, died there as a stranger after a snake killed him, and composed farewell verses with his finger on the ground.
story: Then Ibn Ishaq gives the story of Sima h. Lu'ayy and how he went to Wman and lived there. That was due to a quarrel between himself and his brother 'Amir. 'Amir made him afraid and so he fled to 'Urn=, dying there as a stranger. What happened was that while he was grazing his camel a snake bit its lip making it fall on its side. Then the snake hit and killed Sxma. It is said that he wrote these verses with his finger on the ground: "Eyes, weep for S m a b. Lu'ayy, the snake has clung to Ssma. None such as S m a b. Lu'ayy have I seen, found a dead victim of a camel. Send a messenger to 'Amir and KaB that my soul yearns for them. Though my home is in Wmm I am a Ghdihr who emigrated hut not from poverty. Perhaps the glass you have poured, Ibn Lu'ayy, fearing death, has not really been poured. You aimed to repel death, 0 Ibn Lu'ayy, but no one so intending has power over death, And many a silent, plodding camel you've left prostrate after long, intense exercise."
lessons: Conflict within families can lead to lasting separation and loneliness — reconciliation should be pursued before it is too late.
Death can come unexpectedly and in distant places; one should live in a way that leaves no regrets about strained relationships.
Even in one's final moments, the human soul reaches out for love and connection, reminding us of the importance of maintaining family bonds.
practical_advice: If you are estranged from a family member due to conflict or fear, take a step toward reconciliation today — send a message, make a call, or seek a trusted mediator.
Reflect on whether fear or pride is keeping you from mending an important relationship, and consider whether the cost of separation is truly worth it.
When feeling alone or far from loved ones, write down or express your feelings honestly, as Sama did — articulating grief can be a first step toward healing.

title: The Messenger of God Recognizes Sama's Poetic Verse
summary: A descendant of Sama b. Lu'ayy introduced himself to the Prophet Muhammad, who immediately recognized him by his ancestor's famous line of poetry about fearing death.
story: And Ibn Hishim said that he heard that a son of Sima went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and introduced himself as descended from Sama b. Lu'ayy. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, "The poet?" One of his Companions then said, "0 Messenger of God, you seem to be referring to his line of verse, 'Perhaps the glass you've poured, Ibn Lu'ayy, fearing death, has not really been poured.'" And he answered, "Yes indeed."
lessons: The Prophet's vast knowledge and memory of Arab heritage allowed him to connect with people through their ancestry and culture.
Acknowledging someone's lineage and history is a form of honor and respect that strengthens human connection.
Poetry and literature preserve the memory of those who are gone, giving their lives continued meaning across generations.
practical_advice: When meeting someone, show genuine interest in their background and family history — it builds trust and makes them feel seen.
Learn about the cultural and family heritage of those around you as a way of deepening your relationships.
Preserve and share family stories and lessons with younger generations so that meaningful memories are not lost.

title: Awf b. Lu'ayy Left Behind by His Caravan and Welcomed by Tha'laba
summary: Awf b. Lu'ayy was separated from his Qurayshi caravan in the territory of Ghatafan, where Tha'laba b. Sa'd took him in, arranged his marriage, and treated him as a brother.
story: Ibn Ishaq said, "They claim that CAwf h. Lu'ayy travelled with a caravan from Quraysh and to the territory of G h a t a f a h. Sacd h. Qays h. 'Aylan. There he was delayed so the rest of his people left him behind and went off. Then Tha'laha h. Sa'd, he being his brother by the genealogy of Bann Dhubyan, came along, detained him, arranged his marriage, and treated him like an intimate and a brother. His relationship became widely known among Bana Dhubyzn. They say it was Tha'laba who said to 'Awf the following verse when he was delayed and left behind: 'O Ibn Lu'ayy, tether your camel with me; your people have left you and you ought not to be left.'"
lessons: Generosity and brotherhood can emerge from strangers in moments of vulnerability and abandonment.
Being left behind or overlooked by one's own community can open the door to unexpected belonging elsewhere.
True kinship is defined by how one treats another in their time of need, not merely by blood or tribal connection.
practical_advice: If you feel abandoned or left out by your social circle, remain open to new connections — sometimes the most meaningful relationships come from unexpected sources.
When you see someone isolated or left behind, take the initiative to include them, as Tha'laba did for Awf.
Do not let pride prevent you from accepting help or hospitality when you genuinely need it; receiving with grace is also a virtue.

title: Umar b. al-Khattab's Admiration for Banu Murra b. Awf
summary: Umar b. al-Khattab expressed admiration for the noble lineage of Banu Murra b. Awf, descendants of Awf b. Lu'ayy, acknowledging their honor and their connection to Quraysh.
story: Ibn Isbaq continued to indicate that either Muhammad b. Ja'far b. ai-Zubayr, or Muhammad b. 'Abd di-Rahmm b. 'Abd All& b. al-uusayn told him that Wmar b. al-Khat(zb had said, "If 1 were to claim an attachinent to any Arab tribe or that they had a relationship to us, it wouid be to Bans Murra b. 'Awf. We know kindred men among them and also how they treated that man." By this he meant 'Awf b. Lu'ayy. He went on to relate that a man he did not distrust had told him that Wmar b. al-Khaftsb said to some men of Bana Murra: "If you want to trace back your genealogy to your kinfolk, then trace it to 'Awf." Ibn Ishaq added that these people were nobles of Ghataf%n,their ieaders and ilite, with good reputation locally and in ail of Ghatafm and Qays, and that they did maintain their own genealogy. If their ancestry were referred to by others, they wouid say, "We neither deny nor disown it. That is the ancestry we prefer." Then they would recite their verses recalling their relationship to Lu'ayy.
lessons: Integrity and nobility of character are recognized and honored across generations, even beyond one's own tribe.
Umar's willingness to publicly acknowledge the virtue of another tribe demonstrates that true leadership includes recognizing excellence wherever it exists.
A people who neither deny nor disown their heritage display a dignified confidence grounded in their true identity.
practical_advice: Acknowledge and express appreciation for the good qualities and contributions of those outside your immediate circle — it builds bridges and inspires others.
Be secure enough in your own identity that you neither boast about it excessively nor deny it when questioned.
Study your own family and community history so that you can speak about your origins with the same dignity and groundedness as Banu Murra.

title: The Prophet's Farewell Address on the Sacred Months
summary: In his farewell address, the Prophet Muhammad clarified the correct number and identity of the sacred months, correcting longstanding tribal disagreements and innovations.
story: It has been established in both of the jahih collections, on the authority of Abii Bakrah, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated in his khu~batal-mads', farewell address, "Time has revolved in its own fashion since the day God created the heavens and the earth, the year consisting of twelve months, four of which are sacred. Three of these are contiguous: Dhn al-Qa'da, Dhu al-Ijijja, and al-Mubarram, and there is also Mudar's month of Rajab which comes between Jumadz and ShaCDan." Thus by saying this he expressed preference for the view of Mudar and not for that of liabi'a. God the Almighty and Glorious stated in the Qur'm, "With God the months number twelve, so ordained by God, (since) the day he created the heavens and the earth; of these four are sacred" (siirat al-Tamba, IX, v.36). This constitutes a reply to Bann 'Awf b. Lu'ayy for their considering the sacred months eight in number. In so doing they added ro God's decree, introducing therein something which was not part thereof. His statement in the hadith that "three are contiguous [months]" is a disapproval of the aaitude of the al-nasz7 people vvho delayed the sacred month, making it §afar, rather than al-Muharram. Also, the phrase therein "Mudar's month of Rajab" is a refutation of Rabi'a's view.
lessons: Divine revelation provides clarity and correction when human traditions have deviated from truth, and it should be accepted with humility.
Adding to or subtracting from God's commands, even out of cultural habit or tribal pride, is a form of overstepping that the Prophet came to correct.
