The Prophet's farewell address was a moment of comprehensive guidance, showing that true leadership clarifies confusion and unifies people around established truth.
practical_advice: When you encounter conflicting opinions or long-held traditions on a religious matter, return to primary sources — the Quran and authentic Sunnah — for clarity.
Be willing to accept correction gracefully when your understanding of religious practice is shown to be mistaken, rather than clinging to familiar but inaccurate customs.
Use moments of gathering or farewell to communicate the most important truths you want others to carry forward, as the Prophet did in his farewell address.

title: Qusayy b. Kilab Regains Guardianship of the Kaaba for Quraysh
summary: Qusayy b. Kilab came to Mecca, married the daughter of the chief of Khuzaa, and through alliances reclaimed guardianship of the sacred House, reuniting the scattered Quraysh around it.
story: An Account of Quvayj1 b. Kilrtb and of his part in regaining guardianship over the ka%a for Quraysh by taking it from Khuzrtca. Nora Quraysh gathered together at the holy place which God secured for the worshippers, a$er havieg lived scattered andj5ugmented in the mountains and hollom. What happened was that when his father died, Quvayy's mother was married to Rabi'a b. Ijaram of Wdhra and he took her and her son to his own country. Eater, when he grew up, Qusayy came to Mecca and married Hubha, daughter of the chief of Khuza'a, Ijulayl b. b b s h i y y a . Regarding Khuzz'a, they say that Ijulayl entrusted guardianship over the holy House to Qusayy because of the large family Qusayy had with his daughter. Hulayl told {-rim,"You have more right to it than I do." Ibn Ishaq stated that this account was heard only from Khuzii'a. He said that others claim that he sought the assistance of his maternal brothers, their leader being Riz* b. Rabi'a, and of the nibes of =nZna and @dZ% and of some men in the Mecca region from Quraysh and others. And so he ousted them (Khuza'a) from the House of God and he himself assumed control over it.
lessons: Leadership and guardianship of sacred responsibilities go to those who are most prepared, committed, and trusted, not merely those who hold it by default.
Reuniting a scattered community around a shared purpose and sacred center is among the greatest acts of service a leader can perform.
Alliances built on trust, marriage, and mutual respect can accomplish what individual strength alone cannot.
practical_advice: If your community is fragmented or lacking direction, consider what shared values or sacred purpose could bring people together again.
Seek alliances and build relationships broadly when working toward a significant goal — no great achievement is accomplished in isolation.
When you are entrusted with a role of responsibility, take it seriously and work to be worthy of that trust, as Qusayy did with the guardianship of the Kaaba.

title: Amir b. al-Harith al-Adwani Adjudicates Inheritance with Slave-Girl's Wisdom
summary: The Arab judge Amir b. al-Harith spent a sleepless night unable to resolve a case of inheritance for a hermaphrodite, until his slave-girl Sukhayla offered him a practical ruling principle that resolved the dilemma.
story: And there was 'Amir b. al-&rib al-'Adwmi. In the case of all conflicts arising among the Arabs, they would come to h i for adjudication and accept whatever he decided. On one occasion they asked him to resolve the matter of inheritance to a hermaphrodite. He lay awake all night reflecting on how to adjudicate this and when a slave-girl of his, Sukhayla by name, who used to pasture his sheep, saw him thus, she enquired of h i , "What is wrong with you, poor thing, that you spend the night awake?" So he told her, in case she might be of help. And she replied, "Judge by where from urination comes." He commented, "Sukhayla, you've shown the way well, by God." And that was how he did decide.
lessons: Wisdom and insight can come from the most unexpected and humble sources — humility to receive guidance matters more than the status of the one offering it.
Losing sleep over a difficult decision reflects a sincere commitment to justice and responsibility.
A great leader is not one who has all the answers, but one who remains open to counsel and gives credit where it is due.
practical_advice: When facing a problem that keeps you up at night, do not be too proud to discuss it with others around you — sometimes a fresh perspective from an unlikely source holds the answer.
Practice intellectual humility by genuinely listening to people regardless of their social status or background when they offer insight.
If you are in a position of leadership or responsibility, build a culture where those around you feel safe to speak up and contribute ideas.

title: Qusayy's Arbitration and Guardianship Over the Ka'ba
summary: Qusayy b. Kilab gathered the Quraysh tribes and, after battles with Khuza'a and arbitration by Ya'mur b. 'Awf, gained rightful guardianship over the Ka'ba and control over Mecca, uniting his people and restoring their ancient heritage.
story: Qusayy was lord and master over his people, obeyed and revered. Eventually he gathered Quraysh together from their various locations in the Arabian peninsula and gained the help of the Arab tribes that obeyed him in making war on Khuza'a, in removing them from the House of God, and in handing over its guardianship to himself. They engaged in many battles with much bloodshed and then agreed upon arbitration. They appointed as arbitrator Ya'mur b. 'Awf b. Ka'b b. 'Amir b. Layth b. Bakr b. 'Abd Manat b. Kinana who decreed that Qusayy was more fit for guardianship over the House than Khuza'a was. He ruled that all losses inflicted on Qusayy by Khuza'a and Bana Bakr were an issue that should be "crushed underfoot", and that what losses Khuza'a and Banu Bakr had received from Quraysh, Kinana, and Quda'a should be compensated by bloodwit payment. His decision meant that Qusayy should be given free access to Mecca and the ka'ba. Thereat Ya'mur was nicknamed al-Shadhdhakh, "the crusher". Ibn Ishaq further related that Qusayy thus gained guardianship over the House and control over Mecca. He brought together to Mecca his people from their dwellings and acted as king over them and over all the Meccans as well; and they treated him as king. However, he confirmed the Arabs in their prior practices, considering that to be a duty incumbent upon himself that he ought not change. He thus confirmed Safwan, 'Adwan, al-nasa'a, "the intercalators", and Murra b. 'Awf in their previous practices, until Islam came and God destroyed all of that. Qusayy was the first of Banu Ka'b who took the kingship and was obeyed as such by his people. He had rights of the hijaba, the saqaya, the rifada, the nadwa, and the liwa'. Thus he controlled all of Mecca's honours. He divided up Mecca among his people into quarters and he settled each family of Quraysh into their dwellings there.
lessons: Legitimate leadership is earned through justice, perseverance, and the willingness to resolve conflict through proper arbitration rather than endless bloodshed.
True authority comes with responsibility — Qusayy used his position not for personal gain alone but to unite and serve his people.
Even in times of conflict, seeking a fair and acknowledged mediator is a sign of wisdom and moral maturity.
practical_advice: When facing a dispute that feels unresolvable, consider seeking a trusted, neutral third party whose judgment both sides can respect.
If you find yourself in a position of influence, ask yourself whether you are using it to bring people together or to serve only your own interests.
Reflect on the long-term consequences of conflict — Qusayy's willingness to negotiate ultimately brought greater stability than continued warfare could have.

title: Qusayy Establishes the Dar al-Nadwa and the Civic Institutions of Mecca
summary: Having united the Quraysh in Mecca, Qusayy constructed the Dar al-Nadwa as an assembly house for counsel and dispute resolution, and established the key civic and religious honors that governed Meccan life for generations.
story: Qusayy b. Kilab enjoyed total authority, including being guardian and keeper of the ka'ba and the awarder of battle banners. He also constructed a building to prevent violence and resolve disputes; this he named the dar al-nadwa, the assembly house. When a conflict became serious the chiefs of each of the tribes would meet, take counsel there, and decide the issue. All contracts and marriage agreements would be made there. And that was also the only place where a girl would first wear the dara', the sleeved chemise, when she reached the age to do so. The door to this building was facing the masjid al-haram, the holy mosque. Later it became owned by Hakim b. Hizam, after belonging to Banu 'Abd al-Dar. He sold it, during the reign of Mu'awiya, for 100,000 dirhams. Mu'awiya criticized him for having done so, saying, "You've sold your people's honour for 100,000." But Hakim replied, "But today nobility is measured by one's piety. I bought it, by God, before Islam for a skinful of wine and now here I've sold it for 100,000. And I swear that the money will go to charity in God's cause, so which of us had been cheated?" This was related by al-Darqutni in the Asma' Rijal al-Muwatta' (Identities of the Men in the Muwatta') (of Malik b. Anas).
lessons: Building institutions for peaceful dialogue and conflict resolution is one of the most enduring contributions a leader can make to their community.
True nobility lies in piety and righteous intention, not in the possession of material symbols of honor — as Hakim's response to Mu'awiya beautifully illustrates.
Every generation inherits structures and responsibilities from those before them; what matters is whether we honor their spirit or merely their form.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by conflict in your community or family, consider whether a structured, neutral space for conversation could help de-escalate tensions.
Ask yourself whether the things you hold onto as symbols of your identity or honor are truly serving a righteous purpose, or whether they have become attachments.
If you benefit financially from something connected to your heritage or community, reflect on whether you can direct that benefit toward a cause greater than yourself.

title: Qusayy Institutes the Rifada: Feeding the Pilgrims of Mecca
summary: Qusayy b. Kilab established the practice of feeding and providing for pilgrims during the Hajj season, calling upon the Quraysh to honor God's guests, a tradition that endured for generations after him.
story: He had the prerogative of providing drink for the pilgrims; they drank only from his cisterns. At that time, in Mecca the holy well, zamzam, was obliterated, as it had been since the time of Jurhum. People had forgotten about it because of its great antiquity and they could not locate its whereabouts. According to al-Waqidi, Qusayy was the first person to institute lighting a fire at al-Muzdalifa to guide people coming there from 'Arafat. The word al-rifada means providing food for the pilgrims during the pilgrimage season until they leave to return to their own countries. Ibn Ishaq went on to relate that Qusayy had imposed this rifada on his people, having said, "O People of Quraysh, you are God's neighbours and the inhabitants of Mecca and of the holy places. The pilgrims are God's guests and visitors to His House. They have full right to hospitality. So provide food and drink for them during the pilgrimage days, until they depart from you." And so they did. Each year they would set aside a portion of their wealth as a tribute they would pay him. He would use it to provide food for the people during the days they were at Mina. This practice was followed at his command during al-jahiliyya, the pre-Islamic period, and it has passed on thereafter up to the present. It is the food the sultan provides for people at Mina each year until the pilgrimage ends.
lessons: Generosity toward guests and travelers — especially those on a spiritual journey — is a profound act of worship and communal responsibility.
A leader who calls their community to serve others, and backs that call with personal sacrifice, plants seeds of virtue that outlast their own lifetime.
Caring for those who are far from home and in need is not merely a social obligation but a spiritual one, rooted in recognizing the dignity of every person.
practical_advice: Consider how you might extend hospitality in your own life — even small acts of feeding others or welcoming guests carry deep spiritual weight.
Reflect on whether you are contributing a portion of your wealth or resources to causes that serve the wider community, not just your immediate circle.
When you feel spiritually distant or disconnected, look for ways to serve others on their own journeys — in doing so, you often find your own path renewed.

title: Rizah b. Rabi'a Responds to Qusayy's Call and the Tribes Unite
summary: Rizah b. Rabi'a, the paternal brother of Qusayy, responded to his call for aid by rallying tribal forces and marching through the night to support Qusayy's campaign against Khuza'a, as commemorated in his famous verses.
story: Ibn Ishaq related that when Qusayy had finished his war, his brother Rizah b. Rabi'a left for his own country accompanied by his three paternal brothers, who were Hunn, Mahmud, and Julhuma by name. And Rizah spoke the following verses in responding to Qusayy: "When an envoy came from Qusayy saying, 'Respond to your friend', We arose to him leading our fine horses, leaving aside the slow and overweight. We travelled with them by night till dawn, sheltering by day to survive. They were speedy as sand-grouse to the water, as we answered Qusayy's messenger. We gathered men from al-Sira' and the two Ashmadhs, collecting tribesmen from each village. What a band of horse that night, over a thousand, running free and fast, When they passed by 'Asjar and took the quick route from Mustanakh, Skirting the edge of Wariqan and passing by al-'Arj and a tribe encamped, Overstepping the pasture, not tasting it, racing on, night-long, from Marr, Keeping the colts near their dams to minimize their neighing. When reaching Mecca, we destroyed the men tribe after tribe, Switching them with our sword-blades, rendering them witless from all sides Crushing them beneath our horses' hooves, as does the strong and mighty to the lowly. We killed Khuza'a in their own home, and Bakr group by group. We banished them from the Sovereign's land, so that they would never settle good land again, We captured them in irons and quenched our vengeance thirst from all their tribes." According to Ibn Ishaq, when Rizah returned home, God gave him increase. He also gave Hunn increase; they constitute the two tribes of Walhra to this day.
lessons: Answering the call of a righteous cause, even at great personal cost and difficulty, is a mark of loyalty and brotherly devotion that echoes through history.
The bonds of kinship and shared purpose can mobilize extraordinary effort — when a cause is just, people find the strength to travel through the night.
Even after conflict ends, what endures is not the destruction wrought but the communities rebuilt and the increase God grants to those who acted with sincerity.
practical_advice: When someone you care about calls on you for support in a difficult time, ask yourself whether you are responding with the urgency and wholehearted commitment that the situation deserves.
Reflect on the relationships in your life where loyalty has been tested — have you shown up fully when it mattered most?
When you feel exhausted by a long struggle, remind yourself that God can bring increase and blessing from even the most grueling journeys, as He did for Rizah and his brothers.

title: Qusayy's Division of Prerogatives to His Son 'Abd al-Dar
summary: When Qusayy grew old, he entrusted all leadership prerogatives of Quraysh to his eldest son 'Abd al-Dar to equalize him with his more prominent brothers, which later caused a major dispute among their descendants splitting Quraysh into two factions.
story: When Qusayy grew old he entrusted all the prerogatives he had enjoyed leadership of Quraysh and its honour of governing the provision of food and drink for the pilgrims, guardianship of the House, the issuance of banners, and the summoning of assemblies - to his son 'Abd al-Dar, who was his oldest. He only entrusted him with all these prerogatives because the rest of his brothers, 'Abd Manaf, 'Abd Shams, and 'Abd had become noblemen during their father's rule and had attained great power and prestige. Thus Qusayy wished for 'Abd al-Dar to be equal to them in prestige and so gave him alone these powers. 'Abd al-Dar's brothers did not dispute his action. But when they all had passed away their sons came into conflict about this and said, 'Qusayy only entrusted 'Abd al-Dar with all that to equalize him with his brothers; we are entitled to what our fathers were due.' 'Abd al-Dar's family replied, 'This is a matter that Qusayy decided in our favour, and we have the greater right.' And so a great dispute arose among them; and Quraysh split into two factions, one giving allegiance and alliance to the family of 'Abd al-Dar, the other to the family of 'Abd Mannf with whom they made a formal pact. Upon making the oath they placed their hands into a deep dish containing perfume. Then they went and wiped their hands on the corners of the ka'ba. This was thereafter known as the 'Treaty of the Perfumed'. On the 'Abd Mannf side there were the Quraysh tribes of Banu Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzza b. Qusayy, Banu Zuhra, Banu Taym, and Banu al-Harith b. Fihr. On the side of 'Abd al-Dar's descendants were Banu Makhzum, Banu Sahm, Banu Jumah, and Banu 'Adi. The tribes of Banu 'Amir b. Lu'ayy and Muharrib b. Fihr remained separate from the rest, not allying with either side. Ultimately they made peace, agreeing that the prerogatives of feeding and watering the pilgrims should be held by Banu 'Abd Mannf, while those of guardianship of the ka'ba, issuance of banners, and calling of assemblies were to be held by Banu 'Abd al-Dar. This arrangement became firm and permanent.
lessons: Favoring one child over others, even with good intentions, can plant seeds of lasting conflict among descendants.
Wise leadership requires anticipating how decisions will affect future generations, not only the present.
Compromise and peace-making, even after deep tribal disputes, is possible when both sides prioritize communal stability over personal pride.
practical_advice: When distributing responsibilities or inheritances in a family, communicate your reasoning clearly to prevent future misunderstandings.
If you find yourself in a family or community dispute over inheritance or roles, seek mediation and focus on what arrangement best serves everyone long-term.
Recognize that resentment left unaddressed across generations tends to grow; address grievances early and transparently.

title: The Genealogical Chain Leading to the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS)
summary: Ibn Hisham traces the noble lineage of the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) through his father 'Abd Allah and mother Amina, affirming through hadith that God chose each ancestor in his line as the most honored of their people.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that Qusayy had four sons and two daughters, the sons being 'Abd Manaf, 'Abd al-Dar, 'Abd al-'Uzza, and 'Abd, the daughters Takhmur and Barra. The mother of all these was Hubba, daughter of Hulayl b. Huhshiyya b. Salal b. Ka'b b. 'Amr, al-Khuza'i. It was from Hulayl that Qusayy b. Kilab took control over the Sacred House. According to Ibn Hisham, 'Abd Manaf b. Qusayy had four sons, Hashim, 'Abd Shams, and al-Muttalib, their mother being 'Atika daughter of Murra b. Hilal. Another son of his was Nawfal b. 'Abd Manaf, his mother being Waqida, daughter of 'Amr al-Mu'aniyya. Ibn Hisham stated as well that 'Abd Manaf also fathered Abu 'Amr, Tumadir, Qulaba, Hayya, Rayta, Umm al-Akhtham, and Umm Sufyan. Also according to Ibn Hisham, Hashim b. 'Abd Manaf had four (sic) sons and five daughters. The sons were named 'Abd al-Muttalib, Asad, and Abu Sayfi; the daughters were Nadla, al-Shaffa', Khalida, Da'ifa, Ruqayya, and Hayya. The mother of 'Abd al-Muttalib and Ruqayya was Salma, daughter of 'Amr b. Zayd b. Labid b. Khidash b. 'Amir b. Ghanm b. 'Adi b. al-Najjar from Medina. Ibn Hisham also gave the names of the mothers of the remaining children. He stated that 'Abd al-Muttalib had ten sons and six daughters. The sons were al-'Abbas, Hamza, 'Abd Allah, Abu Talib (whose name was 'Abd Manaf and not 'Umran), al-Zubayr, al-Harith, who was the first-born of his father who was therefore accorded his name, Jahl (whom some name as Hajl), who was nicknamed al-Ghaydaq, 'the liberal', for his generosity, al-Muqawwim, Du'aj, and Abu Lahab (whose name was 'Abd al-'Uzza). His daughters' names were Safiyya, Umm Hakim al-Bayda', 'Atika, Umayma, 'Arwa, and Barra. And Ibn Hisham also gave the names of their mothers. He stated that the mother of 'Abd Allah, Abu Talib, al-Zubayr and all the girls except Safiyya was Fatima, daughter of 'Amr b. 'A'idh b. 'Umran b. Makhzum b. Yaqza b. Murra b. Ka'b b. Lu'ayy b. Ghalib b. Fihr b. Malik b. al-Nadr b. Kinana b. Khuzayma b. Mudrika b. Ilyas b. Mudar b. Nizar b. Ma'ad b. 'Adnan. He said further that 'Abd Allah fathered Muhammad, the Messenger of God (SAAS), the lord of all Adam's children. His mother was Amina daughter of Wahb b. 'Abd Manaf b. Zuhra b. Kilab b. Murra b. Ka'b b. Lu'ayy. He then gave mention of all her maternal forebears. Ibn Hisham concluded that the Prophet (SAAS) was the most noble of Adam's children in worthiness and descent from both his father and his mother. Previously recounted is the following statement of al-Awza'i on the authority of Shaddad Abu 'Ammar from Wathila b. al-Asqa': 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "God chose Kinana from Ishmael's progeny and Quraysh from Kinana, Hashim from Quraysh, and myself from Hashim."' Muslim recounted this tradition.
lessons: Divine selection and honor are not arbitrary but reflect a continuous chain of virtue and nobility across generations.
Knowing one's lineage and roots can be a source of pride, identity, and connection to a larger purpose.
The Prophet's honored ancestry reminds believers that God's plans unfold gradually across time and generations.
practical_advice: Reflect on your family history and the sacrifices of your ancestors as a source of strength and gratitude in difficult times.
Recognize that your identity is shaped by those who came before you; honor their legacy through your own conduct.
When feeling insignificant or lost, remember that you are part of a larger, meaningful story that extends far beyond your individual life.

title: Khalid b. Sinan al-'Absi and the Fire of al-Harratayn
summary: Khalid b. Sinan, a man of the inter-prophet period alleged by some to have been a prophet, extinguished a supernatural fire but died after his companions broke his command not to call him by name, and he was buried having told them a sign by which they might revive him.
story: The hafiz Abu Ya'la al-Mawsili stated that al-Mu'alla b. Mahdi al-Mawsili related to him, as did Abu 'Awana, from Abu Yunis, from 'Akrama and down to Ibn 'Abbas, that a man from 'Abs called Khalid b. Sinan said to his people, 'I shall put out from you the fire of al-Harratayn!' But one of his people responded, 'O Khalid, you have only ever told us the truth; what have you to do with the fire of al-Harratayn you claim you will extinguish?' So Khalid went forth accompanied by some of his people, including 'Umara b. Ziyad, until they reached where the fire emerged from a fissure in the mountain. Khalid then drew a line at which he made them sit, saying, 'If I am delayed, do not call for me by my name.' As the fire spurted out, it looked like sorrel-red horses following one another. Khalid approached it and began beating it with his stick saying, 'Bada, bada, bada kullu huda; the son of the goatherdswoman claimed I won't come out from it bearing my clothes in my hand.' Then he went inside the fissure with the fire. When he had been gone a long time, 'Umara b. Ziyad said to Khalid's people, 'By God, if he were alive he would have come out to you by now!' Some said, 'Call out to him by name.' The account continued, indicating that others replied, 'He forbade us to call him by his name.' But some did so and he emerged carrying his head, complaining, 'Didn't I forbid you to call me by name? By God, you've killed me, so bury me! And if some donkeys pass, one of which is bobtailed, then exhume me and you'll find me alive.' They did bury him and some donkeys did pass by, one of them being bobtailed. So some wanted [to exhume him].
lessons: Disregarding the clear instructions of a trustworthy guide, even out of concern or impatience, can have grave and irreversible consequences.
Patience and trust in those with greater knowledge or authority is a virtue that protects both individuals and communities.
The story of Khalid b. Sinan illustrates how people who carry great gifts can be 'squandered' by their own community's lack of faith and discipline.
practical_advice: When someone you trust gives you a specific instruction for a difficult situation, resist the urge to act on impulse and follow the guidance you were given.
If you are waiting anxiously for news or an outcome, remind yourself that impatience and premature action often make things worse rather than better.
Reflect on whether there are areas in your life where a lack of patience or trust in guidance has led to missed opportunities or harm, and commit to doing better.

title: Hatim al-Ta'i's Generosity: Slaughtering His Horse for a Hungry Mother and Her Children
summary: Hatim al-Ta'i slaughters his only horse to feed a desperate neighbor woman and her six starving children, going without food himself despite being the most in need.
story: there was neither sound nor movement astir, the side of our tent was lifted. He called out, 'Who is there?' The person went away. At daybreak, or thereabouts, he again said, 'Who is there?' and a woman replied, 'It is your neighbour so-and-so, Abii 'Adi; I have no one to turn to but you. I'm coming to you from my children who are moaning like wolves from their hunger. 'Bring them to me quickly,' he told her." Al-Nawa went on, "I jumped up and exclaimed, 'What are you doing? Lie down! Your children are writhing from hunger and you've no means to soothe them, so what can you do for her and her children?' He responded, 'Be silent; by God, I will satisfy you, if God wills it.'" She went on, "So in she came, carrying two children and with four others walking at her side, as though she were an ostrich surrounded by her chicks. Then he went over to his horse, thrust his spear in its upper chest and struck his flint and lit a fire. Next he brought a long knife and skinned the horse after which he handed the knife to the woman saying, 'After you.' Then he said, 'Now send your children.' And she did so. Then he said (to al-Nawzr) 'You, evil woman, would you eat something before a poor man's children!' "He then went all round to each one of them until they had all got up and approached the horse. Then he wrapped himself up in his cloak and stretched out to one side watching us. And, by God, he did not taste one bite himself, even though he was the most of all in need. And by next morning there was nothing of the horse left but bones and hooves!"
lessons: True generosity means giving from what you need most, not merely from your surplus — Hatim sacrificed his most valuable possession, his horse, without hesitation.
The one who gives most is often the one who eats last: prioritizing others over oneself, especially the vulnerable, is the hallmark of noble character.
Trust in God's provision enables the generous soul to give freely, believing that what is given away will be replaced through divine means.
practical_advice: When someone in need comes to you at an inconvenient time, resist the urge to turn them away because of your own difficulties — often giving is most meaningful precisely when it costs you something.
Practice putting the needs of others before your own comfort in small, daily ways, such as serving food to guests before eating yourself, to cultivate a spirit of genuine selflessness.
When you feel you have nothing left to give, reflect on what you still have and ask yourself whether someone else's need is greater — this perspective shift can unlock unexpected generosity.

title: Hatim al-Ta'i Refuses to Dine Alone with His Wife and Feeds the People Instead
summary: When his wife arranges for them to eat alone in a private tent away from others, Hatim al-Ta'i cannot bring himself to dine without inviting the people, opening the tent curtains and sharing the food with all.
story: Al-Daqutni stated that Qadi (judge) Abii 'Abd Allah al-Muhzmili related to him, as did 'Abd Allzh b. Abii Sad, as did 'Uthaym b. Thawaba b. Hatim al-Tz'i from his father, from his grandfather, that Hztim's wife said to Hatim, "O Abii Saff%% I desire that you and I eat alone together, with no one else there." So he gave her instructions, and she moved her tent a parasang, a couple of miles, away from the rest. He ordered food and it was prepared, and the tent curtains were let down for him and for her. When the food was cooked, he took off his head-covering, then spoke these verses, "Do not you cook my pot with your curtains hiding it; for me, then, what you cook is forbidden. But at that hill light the fire with heavy wood if you do light it, not with quick kindling wood." The narrator continued, "Then he drew aside the curtains, brought out the food and invited in the people. He and they then ate. She told him, 'You haven't fulfilled what you said.' And he responded, 'I couldn't bring myself to do it. My spirit was too noble to bring blame on me for this, when before I have always been generous.'"
lessons: A person's deep character and values will assert themselves even when circumstances make compromise seem acceptable — Hatim's generosity was not a performance but a core identity.
Generosity is not simply about material giving; it reflects an inner condition of the soul that refuses to be content while others go without.
Maintaining consistency in one's values, even at personal cost to relationships or comfort, builds a trustworthy and admirable character over time.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether your generosity is conditional — do you give freely in public but hold back in private? Work toward consistency between your public and private character.
If you have a habit of sharing and giving, do not abandon it merely for the sake of personal comfort or to please others; your values are most tested in exactly those moments.
Discuss with loved ones the importance your values hold for you, so that those closest to you understand and even come to share in the beauty of a generous life.

title: Hatim al-Ta'i's Verses on Trusting God as the True Provider
summary: When Hatim al-Ta'i encounters the miserly verses of al-Mutalammis, he rebukes them and composes his own verses affirming that generosity does not exhaust wealth and that God is ever near as the true provider.
story: The judge Aba al-Faraj al-Mu%f&b. Zakariyys' al-Jariri said that al-Husayn b. al-Q%im al-Kawkabi related to him, as did Aba al-'Abb%s al-Mubarrid that al-Thawri told him, from Aba 'Ubayda, that when the following verses of al-Mutalammis reached Hztim al-Tx'i, "One of little wealth you can repair and he survive, but not the man of much corruption. And preserving wealth is better than exhausting it and wandering abroad without provisions", he said, "What's wrong with him, may God cut his tongue! Is he advising people to be miserly? Should he not have said, 'Generosity will not exhaust one's wealth before its going, nor stinginess increase the miser's wealth. So don't seek wealth through living miserly; for every day there's a blessing that comes anew. Do you not see that wealth comes and goes, and that He who gives to you is not far away.'" The judge Abn al-Faraj commented, "He (Hntim) spoke well with those words, 'He who gives to you is not far away,' and if he had been a Muslim, good would have been hoped for him in the hereafter. For God stated in the Qur'an, 'Ask of God from His bounty' (Surat al-Nisa', IV, v.32) and also, 'If my servants ask you about me (then say) that I am nigh and that I answer the call of those who pray to me' (Surat al-Baqara, II, v.186)."
lessons: Miserliness is not a protection against poverty; true security comes from trust in God as the One who provides, not from hoarding resources.
Every day brings a new blessing from God — this recognition liberates the heart from anxiety about scarcity and opens it to generosity.
Even those outside of revealed faith can arrive at profound truths about God's nearness and provision through sincere reflection on the nature of the world.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about finances or resources, consciously remind yourself of past instances where God provided for you unexpectedly — build a personal record of God's generosity toward you.
Challenge the mindset that saving and withholding is always wise; ask yourself whether fear of scarcity is driving your decisions more than genuine prudence.
Recite or reflect on Qur'anic verses about God's nearness and provision, such as Surah al-Baqarah 2:186, especially during moments of financial worry or uncertainty.

title: Hatim al-Ta'i Distributes the King's Gifts to Poor Tribesmen on the Road
summary: Returning from the court of King al-Nu'man laden with royal gifts of gold, silver, and produce, Hatim al-Ta'i immediately distributes everything to poor tribesmen he meets on the road, leaving nothing for himself.
story: And from al-Waddfi b. Ma'bad comes the account that Hztim al-Tx'i presented himself at the court of al-Nu'mbn b. al-Mundhir, who received and honoured him. Then upon his departure he gave him two camels loaded with gold and silver, as well as valued produce of his land. So off went Hntim, to be met by bedouins of the Tayyi' tribe as he approached home. They addressed him thus, "O Hntim, you've just come from the king, whereas we have come from our people in poverty!" Hntim responded, "Come and take what I have, and distribute it amongst you." They rushed forward to do so, taking and distributing the gifts of al-Nu'mbn. Then his maidservant, Tarifa, came out to Hatim and said, "Both be pious and keep some for yourself; these people won't leave you a dinar or a dirham, a sheep or a camel." Hatim responded by speaking the verses, "Tarifa said: 'You keep us no dirhams, though we are neither wasteful nor overburdened with them.' If what we have is used up, then God will provide for us from someone else and it is not we who will provide for ourselves. No sooner does the dirham get acquainted with our rags than it passes on over and leaves again. If our dirhams were once to assemble together, they'd compete with one another for ways to give charity."
lessons: Wealth that passes through the hands of the generous becomes a means of communal blessing; Hatim saw himself not as an owner but as a conduit for God's provision.
Relying on God rather than accumulated wealth as one's security frees the soul from attachment and enables radical generosity.
The generous person does not wait to give when convenient — the moment of need presented by others is itself the moment of giving.
practical_advice: Practice giving at the moment of receiving rather than always deferring charity to a later, more convenient time — this builds the reflex of generosity.
When advisors or loved ones urge you to hold back from giving, weigh their counsel with wisdom but do not let fear or social pressure extinguish a genuine impulse toward generosity.
Cultivate the belief that your provision comes from God, not solely from your own efforts or savings — this belief is the foundation that makes sustained generosity psychologically sustainable.

title: Hatim al-Ta'i Gives 100 Camels to the Orphan Who Slaughtered All His Sheep
summary: A poor orphan boy, hosting Hatim al-Ta'i, slaughters all 100 of his sheep to honor his guest; Hatim, humbled by the boy's generosity which surpassed his own, gives the orphan 100 of his finest camels in return.
story: Aba Bakr b. 'Ayyash said that Hntim was once asked whether any other Arab was more generous than he and he replied, "All the Arabs are more generous than me!" Hztim then went on to relate that once he overnighted with a young Arab, an orphan, who had 100 sheep. So he slaughtered for him one ewe and when he brought it in and offered him its brain, Hntim commented, "What delicious brain!" Thereupon the young Arab kept on bringing him more and more of it until he told him he had had enough. Next morning Hatim found out that the young Arab had killed all 100 of the sheep and had nothing at all left for himself! Hatim was asked what he did then, and he replied, "However could I thank him enough if I were to do all in the world for him? But in any case, I did give him 100 of my very best camels."
lessons: True generosity is not measured by the absolute amount given but by the proportion of one's total means — the orphan who gave everything surpassed the wealthy Hatim in the quality of his giving.

Genuine humility before others' virtue is itself a form of nobility; Hatim's willingness to say 'all Arabs are more generous than me' demonstrates that greatness includes recognizing greatness in others.
A kind word of appreciation, even unintentionally, can inspire extraordinary acts in others — Hatim's simple comment about the brain's taste triggered the boy's total sacrifice.
practical_advice: Guard your words of appreciation and praise, as they carry more weight than you may realize — they can inspire others to go far beyond what you expected.
Practice intellectual and moral humility by actively looking for and acknowledging generosity, patience, or virtue in those around you, especially those with less material wealth than yourself.

When someone gives you far more than you expected or deserved, resist the impulse to simply move on — find a meaningful way to honor and reciprocate their sacrifice, even if you cannot fully match it.

title: The Mother of Hatim al-Ta'i: Imprisoned for Generosity, Unbroken Upon Release
summary: The mother of Hatim al-Ta'i, a woman of great generosity, is imprisoned by her brothers for a year to break her habit of giving away her wealth, but upon release she immediately gives again, declaring she will never deny the hungry.
story: Muhammad b. Ja'far al-Kharz'iti stated in his book Makarim al-Akhlaq (Acts of Nobility of Character), that al-'Abbas b. al-Fadl al-Rabi' related to him, as did Ishaq b. Ibrahim, as did Hammad al-Basiyya and some elderly men of Tayyi', that it is said that 'Antara, daughter of 'Afif b. 'Amr b. Imru' al-Qays, the mother of Hatim al-Ta'i, could never hold on to anything due to her munificent generosity. Her brothers would restrain her but she ignored them. Since she was a woman of wealth they ultimately imprisoned her inside a house for a year, even feeding her there to make her change her behaviour. After a year they released her, believing she had reformed, and paid her over a portion of her money and told her to enjoy it. But a woman from Hawazin who used to visit her came and asked her for money and she responded, "Take this money, for, by God, I've so suffered hunger myself, I'll spare no effort not to deny anyone who asks of me." Then she spoke the following verses, "By my life, I have been so bitten before by hunger, that I'll spare no efforts never to deny the hungry, So say now to this man blaming me, 'Spare me, and if you don't then chew on your own fingers!' What can you tell your sister except your blame or the reproach of those who are misers. What you witness today is only my nature, and how, O brother, should I abandon my nature?"
lessons: Character rooted in deep values cannot be broken by external pressure, isolation, or punishment — the mother of Hatim emerged from a year of imprisonment more resolved than ever in her generosity.
Personal suffering, such as having experienced hunger oneself, can become the seed of empathy and generosity toward others rather than a reason for self-protection.
Those who try to suppress another's noble character through control or coercion often only strengthen the resolve of the one they seek to change.
practical_advice: When others pressure you to abandon a value or good habit that is central to who you are, use that pressure as an opportunity to examine why that value matters to you and to reaffirm your commitment to it.

If you have experienced hardship, poverty, or deprivation, allow that experience to open your heart toward others in similar circumstances rather than closing it out of a desire to protect your own security.

Do not allow the disapproval of others — even family — to make you feel ashamed of genuine virtue; discern carefully between criticism that is wise counsel and criticism that simply reflects others' smallness of spirit.

title: Hatim al-Ta'i's Three Principles: Protecting Neighbors, Keeping Trust, and Harming None
summary: Hatim al-Ta'i declares three qualities of which he is most proud: never exposing a female neighbor to suspicion, never breaking a trust, and never being the cause of harm to anyone.
story: Al-Haytham b. 'Adi stated, on the authority of Malik b. 'Araki b. 'Adi b. Hztim, on the authority of his father and his grandfather that he witnessed Hatim's excessive generosity and that Hatim had said to him, "Now, son, I pride myself on three qualities — I've never exposed a woman neighbour to suspicion, never given my trust and not fulfilled it, and no one has ever come to harm from me."
lessons: The protection of others' honor and dignity, especially the vulnerable, is a pillar of moral excellence that stands alongside material generosity.
Trustworthiness — fulfilling one's commitments — is a foundation of noble character; breaking trust is a harm that no amount of generosity can compensate for.
A life well-lived can be measured not only by what one gave but by what one refused to do: harm, betrayal, and dishonor avoided are themselves achievements.
practical_advice: Identify three core principles of conduct that define your own character at its best — write them down and use them as a compass when you face moral decisions.
Take special care to protect the reputations and dignity of those around you, particularly those who are vulnerable or whose honor depends on your discretion.
Before making a commitment or promise, reflect carefully on whether you can truly fulfil it — it is better to decline than to give your word and break it.

title: The Dead Hatim al-Ta'i Appears in a Dream to Honor a Guest Who Called on His Grave
summary: A man from the 'Abd al-Qays tribe mockingly calls on the grave of Hatim al-Ta'i for hospitality; that night Hatim appears to him in a dream with verses rebuking him for bringing guests to a grave yet still honoring the request for hospitality.
story: Abii Bakr al-Kharz'iti said, "'Ali b. Harb related to us, quoting 'Abd al-Rahmzn b. Yahya al-'Adawi, quoting Hisham b. Muhammad b. al-Sa'ib al-Kalbi, on the authority of Aba Miskin (otherwise known as Ja'far b. al-Muharrir b. al-Walid) from al-Muharrir Mawla Aba Hurayra, as follows: A party of men from the 'Abd al-Qays tribe passed the grave of Hatim al-Ta'i and came near by. One of them, Aba al-Khaybari by name, went over and began running his foot over his grave, saying, 'O Abii Jaff%4, give us hospitality!' One of his companions exclaimed, 'What are you doing, talking to a decomposed corpse!' After darkness had fallen they went to sleep, but the man who had addressed Hatim awoke in a fright and began shouting, 'Everyone, look to your mounts; Hatim came to me while I slept and spoke verses I've memorized that went: O Aba al-Khaybari, you're a man who brings disgrace and dishonour to the tribe. You brought your companions to seek hospitality at a grave whose corpse had perished. Do you wish to blame me when...'"
lessons: The reputation and legacy of the truly generous endures long after their death, becoming a moral touchstone that continues to instruct and inspire the living.

Hospitality and generosity were so deeply associated with Hatim al-Ta'i that even after his death people instinctively turned to his memory in times of need — a reminder that a life of giving creates an enduring legacy.
The story invites reflection on what kind of legacy we are building through our daily choices and whether our character will be remembered as a source of comfort and generosity to others.
practical_advice: Reflect on the legacy you are currently building — if people were to speak of your character after your death, what qualities would they associate with your name?
Invest in habits of generosity, hospitality, and care for others not for the sake of recognition but because these qualities become the truest and most lasting form of remembrance.
When you encounter accounts of those renowned for noble character — whether from Islamic history or elsewhere — use their stories as mirrors to examine and elevate your own conduct.

title: Hatim al-Tai's Generosity After Death
summary: A group of travelers seeking hospitality stop at the grave of Hatim al-Tai, where supernatural events unfold demonstrating his legendary generosity even after death.
story: of his companions exclaimed, "What are you doing, talking to a decomposed corpse!" After darkness had fallen they went to sleep, but the man who had addressed Hatim awoke in a fright and began shouting, "Everyone, look to your mounts; Hatim came to me while I slept and spoke verses I've memorized that went: '0 Ahn al-Khayhari, you're a man who brings disgrace and dishonour to the tribe. You brought your companions to seek hospitality at a grave whose corpse had perished. Do you wish to blame me when you stay here, while around you is the Tayyi' tribe and their generosity? We're ones to satisfy our guests, and delay the milking of their camels when they come to us.'" He (Abu Hurayra) went on: 'And then, to their surprise, the camel of the man who had spoken to the grave began hobbling on three legs. So they killed it and cooked and ate it, saying: "By God, Hatim hosted us both alive and dead!" ' 'Next morning they mounted their friend behind another rider and set off and were surprised to see a man waving to them as he approached on a camel, leading another behind him. "Which of you is Abu al-Khaybari?" he asked. Abu al-Khaybari identified himself and the man explained, "Hatim came to me in my sleep and told me he had hosted your companions with your camel, and so he asked me to bring you this mount. Take it!" And he gave it to him.'
lessons: True generosity is a quality of character so deep it defines a person's legacy beyond their lifetime.
Hospitality and care for guests is a noble virtue that resonates through time and community memory.
Even in desperate circumstances, the spirit of those who lived generously continues to provide for others in unexpected ways.
practical_advice: When you feel unable to give, reflect on the example of those who gave freely even from scarcity — small acts of generosity have lasting impact.
If you are in need, do not be too proud to seek help from your community; generosity is waiting to meet your need.
Honor the memory of generous role models in your life by practicing hospitality and giving, carrying their legacy forward.

title: Abd Allah ibn Jud'an: From Misery to Nobility
summary: Abd Allah ibn Jud'an, once despised and destitute, discovers a cave full of treasure and transforms himself into one of the most generous nobles of pre-Islamic Mecca.
story: An Account of some matters relating to 'Abd Allah b. Jud'an. His genealogy was 'Abd Allah, son of Jud'an, son of 'Amr, son of Ka'b, son of Sa'd, son of Taym, son of Murrah, lord of the Banu Taym. He was the nephew of the father of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, "the trusting", God bless him. He was a noble knight during the era before Islam, one of those who gave generously to the needy. At first he was poor and deprived, mean and sinful, so that his people, tribe, kinsfolk, and family all hated him, even his father too. One day he went off into the outskirts of Mecca, aimless and miserable, and noticed a fissure in a mountain. He thought there might be something there that would harm him, so he approached it, so that he might die and so be relieved from his sufferings. When he drew near it, he saw a serpent coming out towards him, darting at him. He tried to avoid it, jumping away, but it was no use. But when it was upon him, he saw it to be of gold, its eyes of sapphire. So he destroyed it, picked it up, and went into the cave. To his amazement, there he found the graves of some of the kings of Jurhum, including that of al-Harith b. Mudad, who had long ago disappeared without trace. On their heads he found plaques of gold giving the dates of their death and the periods of their reigns. All about them were large quantities of jewels, pearls, gold, and silver. Of these he took what he wanted and left, after having marked the entry to the cave. When he reached his people he made gifts to them so that they loved him and then he became their leader. He would feed the people and when he ran out of valuables he would go off to the cave, get more and return. He had a watering trough from which mounted riders would feed; the trough was so large that a boy fell in and drowned. Ibn Qutayba and others reported that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I used to shelter in the shade of 'Abd Allah b. Jud'an's watering trough during the sweltering heat of midday." In a hadith relating to the death of Abu Jahl, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to his Companions, "Look for him among the dead; you will recognize him by a scar on his knee. I was competing with him among the crowd at a feast given by Ibn Jud'an. I knocked him over and he fell against his knee which broke, the damage still being visible there on it." And they did find him just so. People report that he used to feed people with dates and barley and with milk to drink until he heard the verses of Umayya b. Abi al-Salt: "I've seen doers and their deeds, and found their noblest to be Banu al-Dayyan. Wheat mixed with honey is their food, not what Banu Jud'an entertain us with." So Ibn Jud'an sent off 2,000 camels to Syria, and they returned bearing wheat, honey and butter. Then he had a man call out each night from the ka'ba roof, "You're all to come to Ibn Jud'an's trough." Whereupon Umayya spoke the following: "He has one energetic fellow summoning (people) in Mecca, while another calls out from above its ka'ba To come to large wooden platters filled with ears of wheat mixed with honey." Yet despite all this, it is established in the Sahih tradition collection of Muslim (b. al-Hadjdjaj) that 'A'isha said: "O Messenger of God, Ibn Jud'an used to donate food to eat and was hospitable to guests; will that benefit him on Judgement Day?" He replied, "No; he never once said: 'O my Lord, forgive me my sins on Judgement Day.'"
lessons: Material generosity and social nobility, however great, are incomplete without a sincere turning of the heart toward God.
A person's circumstances can change dramatically; despair in one's lowest moment does not define one's entire story.
The Prophet Muhammad himself acknowledged the good in others even among those who did not embrace faith, reflecting a balanced and just perspective.
practical_advice: When you feel like an outcast or burden to those around you, remember that your current state is not your permanent state — persevere and seek a new path forward.
Reflect regularly not only on your outward acts of charity but on your inner spiritual connection to God, ensuring your good deeds are rooted in sincere faith.
Do not allow the praise of others for your worldly achievements to become a substitute for the deeper work of seeking God's forgiveness and mercy.

title: Imru' al-Qays: The Poet Remembered on Earth, Forgotten in the Hereafter
summary: The Prophet Muhammad describes the fate of the celebrated pre-Islamic poet Imru' al-Qays, noting that despite his earthly fame and even his verses saving lives, he will lead the poets to hellfire for neglecting the next world.
story: An Account of Imru' al-Qays b. Hujr al-Kindi, author of one of the mu'allaqat odes. His ode is the most magnificent and best known of them all, and it begins: "Halt here, both of you, and let us lament memory of a loved one and a dwelling . . ." Al-Imam Ahmad stated that Hushaym related to him, as did Abu al-Jahm al-Wasili, from al-Zuhri, from Abu Salama, from Abu Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said: "Imru' al-Qays will be the company commander of the poets on their way to hell-fire!" A large number of transmitters related this tradition from Hushaym, including Bishr b. al-Hakam, al-Hasan b. 'Arafa, 'Abd Allah b. Hasan, the Commander of the faithful al-Ma'mun, brother of al-Amin, and Yahya b. Ma'in. Ibn 'Adi considered the transmission chain to be through 'Abd al-Razzaq from al-Zuhri, but this would then be discontinuous and bad from another direction, through Abu Hurayra; and it would not be a reliable tradition except from the first chain of transmission. The hafiz Ibn 'Asakir gave the genealogy of Imru' al-Qays as having been the son of Hujr, son of al-Harith, son of 'Amr, son of Hujr, the akil al-mira'r, son of 'Amr, son of Mu'awiya, son of al-Harith, son of Ya'rub, son of Thawr, son of Murta', son of Mu'awiya b. Kinda. He was also known as Abu Yazid, Abu Wahb, and Abu al-Harith al-Kindi. He lived in the regions of Damascus and mentioned a number of these in his poetry, as for example in the lines: "Halt here, both of you, and let us lament memory of a loved one and a dwelling at the winding ridges between al-Dakhul and Hawmal, Tudah and al-Miqrat; its traces have not been erased by the interweaving winds from south and north." These are well-known places in Hawran. He then related, on a chain of authorities through Hisham b. Muhammad b. al-Sa'ib al-Kalbi that Farwa b. Sa'id b. 'Afif b. Ma'di Karib related to him, from his father, from his grandfather, that while he and others were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) along came a delegation from Yemen. They said, "O Messenger of God, God gave us life by two verses from the poetry of Imru' al-Qays." He replied, "How so?" They explained, "We were making our way to visit you but lost our way en route, spending three days unable to get water. So we split up at the base of an acacia and a mimosa so that we each could die in the shade of a tree. When we were at our last gasp, there was a man speeding on his camel. When one of our men saw him, he spoke the following verses, the rider hearing them: 'And when she (the wild ass) says that the water was her desire but that the white of her veins was bloody She made for the well at Darij, its green slime shading it, overflowing.' So the rider asked, 'Whose verses are those?' He saw our state of exhaustion. We answered, 'Imru' al-Qays b. Hujr.' Then he said, 'By God, he did not lie; this is Darij, where you are now.' So we looked, and there was the water about 50 arm's-lengths away. We slowly made our way to it on our mounts and found it just as Imru' al-Qays had said, with 'green-slime shading it'. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'That's a man who is remembered on earth but forgotten in the next world, honoured in the former but ignored in the latter. In his hands he will carry the banner of the poets, leading them to hell-fire.'" Al-Kalbi reported that Imru' al-Qays advanced with his banners flying intending to battle Banu Asad after they had killed his father and passed by Tabalah. There was the shrine of Dhu al-Khalasa, an idol at which the Arabs would seek divine support. Imru' al-Qays asked for prophecy and the arrow for negation came out. This then happened a second and a third time. At that he broke the arrows and struck them against the face of Dhu al-Khalasa, exclaiming: "You'd bite your father's penis! If your father were the man murdered, you'd not impede me!" He then launched a raid against Banu Asad and engaged them in swift battle. And al-Kalbi commented that (from then on) until the arrival of Islam, support from Dhu al-Khalasa was not sought. Some say that Imru' al-Qays gave praise to the Byzantine emperor and asked his support and assistance in certain wars but did not receive encouragement from him. Consequently Imru' al-Qays spoke verses against him, and it is said that the emperor gave him poison to drink and so killed him. Death came to him when he was beside the grave of a woman on a mountain called 'Asib and it was there he wrote the verses: "Oh neighbour, the shrine is near and I rest where a mountain cleft is established. Oh neighbour, we are strangers here and all strangers are of a common kin."
lessons: Worldly renown, talent, and even unintended benefit to others cannot substitute for conscious devotion to God and preparation for the afterlife.
The Prophet's balanced acknowledgment — recognizing Imru' al-Qays's gift while being clear about its spiritual insufficiency — teaches us to distinguish between earthly and eternal values.
A life spent in pursuit of fame and beauty without spiritual grounding may leave a powerful legacy on earth but a hollow one before God.
practical_advice: Regularly examine whether your talents and gifts are oriented only toward worldly recognition or also toward drawing you closer to God and serving a higher purpose.
When you receive praise for your skills or achievements, use that moment of gratitude to also reflect on your spiritual state and your accountability to God.
Do not let the temporary honor of being remembered and admired by people distract you from the more important work of being known and forgiven by your Creator.

title: The Seven Suspended Odes (Mu'allaqat) of Pre-Islamic Arabia
summary: An account of the seven celebrated pre-Islamic Arabic odes, known as the Mu'allaqat, which were hung on the Ka'ba in recognition of their literary excellence, with their authors and opening verses described.
story: They say that the seven mucallaqat, the select, displayed odes, were hung up on the ka'ba. That was because when one of the Arabs composed an ode he would exhibit it to Quraysh. If they approved of it they would hang it on the ka'ba in recognition of its worth. It is from this practice that the seven odes were gathered. The first of these, as previously noted, was by Imru' al-Qays b. Hujr the Kindite, as previously mentioned. It begins: "Halt here, both of you, and let us lament memory of a loved one and a dwelling at the winding ridges between al-Dakhiil and Hawmal." The second ode was by al-N2bigha al-Dhubymi, whose given name was Ziyad b. Mu'iiwiya . He is also said to have been Ziyzd b. 'Amr b. Mucawiya, who was the son of Dabbab b. Jzbir b. Yarbac b. Ghayq b. Murra b.'Awf b. Sacd b. Dhubyan b. Baghid. His ode begins: "Oh abode of Mayya at al-'Alya', the mountain top; your people have gone for so long and earlier generations dwelt there." The third ode was by Zuhayr b. Abii Sulma, also named Rabi'a b. Riyah al-Muzani. It begins: "Are there still some voiceless remnants of Umm CAwfa's dwelling at the plain of al-Darnj and al-Mutathallam?" The fourth was composed by Tarafa b. al-'Abd b. Sufyan b. Sa'd b. Malik b. Dubay'a b. Qays b. Tha'laba, the sou of W h b a h. Sa'b b.'Ali b. Bakr b. Wa'il. It opens: "On the rocky ground of Thahmad, there are traces still of Khawla, risible like the shadow of an old tattoo on the back of a hand." The fifth ode was by 'Ankara b. Shaddad b. Mugwiya b. Qurad b. Makhzum h. Rabi'a b. Mdik b. Ghdib, the son of Quwy'a, the son of 'Abs the 'Absite. It begins: "Have the poets left anything unsaid, or have you recognized the abode after using imagination?" The sixth was by 'Alqarna b.'Abda b. al-Nu'man b. Qays, a member of Banii Tamim. It opens: "Though your heart is burdened with care, there is joy in beautiful women; soon after youth comes time for greyness." The seventh ode - and there are some who do not affirm it to have been one of the mu'allaq&t, as, for example al-Asma'i and others -is by Labid, the son of Rabi'a b. Mdik b. Ja'far h. Kilab b. Rabi'a b. 'Amir b. Sa'Sa'a b. Mu$wiya b. Bakr b. Hawain b. Man~iirb. 'Ikrima b. Khasafa b. Qays b.'Aylan b. Mudar. It begins: "The place where she dwelt at Mini%;its water-troughs and its mountains have been deserted." As for the ode of unhown authorship, according to Abii Wbayda, al-Asma'i, al-Mubarrid and others, it reads: "Is there any reply for one who asks at the ruins; or has it ever been accustomed to speak?" It is a lengthy poem and has many fine lines.
lessons: Excellence and beauty in any craft can be a means of recognition and honour, reminding us that God values those who strive for quality in their work.
Poetry and art have long served as vehicles for expressing the deepest human emotions — grief, longing, and love — validating that such feelings are a natural part of the human experience.
The preservation of cultural heritage across generations teaches us that our efforts today can leave a lasting legacy for those who come after us.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by emotion, try expressing yourself through writing, poetry, or journaling — giving form to your feelings can bring relief and clarity.
Seek out beauty and excellence in the creative works of others; engaging with art, literature, or poetry can be a powerful way to process sadness or a sense of loss.
Reflect on the traditions and wisdom passed down through your own family or community; reconnecting with your heritage can provide a sense of belonging and reduce feelings of being lost.

title: Umayya b. Abu al-Salt: The Poet Who Received Signs but Turned Away
summary: The story of Umayya b. Abu al-Salt, a pre-Islamic poet who was believed to have received divine signs and possessed knowledge of true faith, yet turned away from it and went astray, with the Quranic verse about one who abandons God's signs applied to him.
story: The hiif;? Ibn 'As&ir said that his name was Umayya b. Aba al-Salt, 'Abd Alhh b. Abii Rabi'a b. 'Awf, the son of Wqda b. 'Izza b. 'Awf b. Thaqif b. Munabbii b. Bakr b. HawGn, known as Abii Wthmm and some say Abii a4-Hakam al-Thaqafi. He was a pre-Islamic poet who went to Damascus before Islam. It is said that he was a righteous man and initially a man of the faith, but he turned away from it, and that God was referring to him in the words: "Tell them of him to whom we brought our signs, but he passed them by; so Satan followed him, and he went astray" (sarat al-A%iif, VII, v.175). Al-Zubayr b. Bakk* said that Ruqayya daughter of 'Abd Shams b. 'Abd Manaf gave birth to Umayya the poet, the son of Aba al-Salt, whose name was Rabi'a b. Wahb b. 'Il%jb. Abii Salama b. Thaqif. Others said that his father was a famous poet of Ta'if, and that Umayya was their best poet. 'Abd al-Razzaq said that al-Thawfi stated that Habib b. Abii Thabit reported to him that 'Abd Allah bb.'Am said that by God's words: "Tell them of him to whom we brought our signs but he passed them by; so Satan followed him and he went astray", Umayya b. Abii al-Salt was implied. And thus did Abii Bakr b. Mardawayh, from Abii Bakr d-Shafici, from Muc%dhb. d-Muthanna, from Musaddad, from Abii 'Awma, from 'Abd al-Malik b.CUmayr, from Nafi' b. Clisim b. Mas'iid. He said that he was in a circle in which was 'Abd Allah b. CAmr. One person there quoted the verse in the Qur'an chapter al-A+iij "Tell them of him to whom we brought our signs but he passed them by." So he asked, "Do you know who he is?" Someone said: "Sayfi b. al-Wih." Someone else said, "No, he is Bal'am, an Israelite." "Incorrect," he replied. Someone asked, "Who, then?" "Umayya b. Abii al-Salt," he answered. Abii Salih and al-Kalbi said the same, and Qatada reported it from several others.
lessons: Knowledge and awareness of the truth are not sufficient on their own — one must also act upon that knowledge with sincerity and courage, or risk losing the guidance one has been given.
Allowing worldly concerns, pride, or fear to prevent us from embracing the truth we know in our hearts is among the gravest spiritual dangers a person can face.
The story of Umayya is a reminder that divine guidance is a gift that must be actively cherished and acted upon, not taken for granted or set aside out of convenience.
practical_advice: If you find yourself knowing the right path but struggling to walk it, take one small, concrete step today toward alignment between your beliefs and your actions — consistency builds conviction.
When you feel spiritually lost or guilty about past choices, remember that sincere turning back to God is always possible; do not let shame keep you paralysed.
Surround yourself with people of faith and integrity who can hold you accountable and encourage you when you feel pulled away from your values.

title: Umayya b. Abu al-Salt and Abu Sufyan's Journey to Syria: Questioning the Expected Prophet
summary: During a trading journey to Syria, Umayya b. Abu al-Salt repeatedly visited Christian scholars and became deeply troubled after learning about the expected Arab prophet, while his companion Abu Sufyan remained indifferent and skeptical.
story: Al-Tabrmi said that 'Ali b.CAbd alLCAzizrelated to him, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Shabib al-Rab'i, quoting Muhammad b. Maslama b. Hishzm al-Makhziirni, quoting Ismacil, the son of al-Turayh b. Isma'il al-Thaqafi, that his father told him on the authority of his father, from Marwm b. al-Hakam, from Mu5wiya b. Aba Sufyan from his father, saying: "I and Umayya b. Aba al-Salt went on business to Syria and whenever we stopped anywhere for the night Umayya would take out a sacred book he had and would read it to us. This went on until we stopped at a Christian village. So its people came to him, honoured him and gave him presents, and he went off with them to their houses. "Late in the morning he came back, threw off his two garments and took out two black ones he had and dressed in them. He then asked me: 'Abii Sufym, do you have access to any Christian scholar well versed in the Bible you could ask a question?' I replied, 'I've no interest in that; if such a person were to tell me something I wanted, I'd not trust him. And if he told me something I disliked, I'd certainly be very angry with him.'" He went on, "So he went away and a Christian sheikh disputed with him. Then Umayya came in to me and said: 'What prevents you from going to this sheikh?' I replied that I was not of his religion, but Umayya responded, 'So what? You'd hear and see wonderful things from hi.' Then he said: 'You're of Thaqif, aren't you?' 'No,' I replied, 'but I am of Quraysh.' 'Well,' he asked, 'so what prevents you from going to the sheikh? He likes you, I swear it, and he'd counsel you.' "Umayya then left and remained with them till he returned to us later that night; he undressed and lay down on his bed. But I swear he was restless! "Next morning he was sad and depressed, his 'evening drink dropping on his morning draught' (as the saying goes) not speaking to us, nor we to him. Eventually he said, 'Won't you ride?' I responded: 'You want to leave then?' He replied, 'Yes.' "So we rode away and travelled for two nights. Then on the third night he said: 'Wouldn't you like to talk, Aha Sufym?' I replied, 'There's something you want to tell? I swear, I never saw anything like the way you came back from your friend.' '"Well that's something you've nothing to do with; but it's to do with something that scared me about my munqalab (hereafter).' '"Do you have a mungalab then?' I asked. '"Yes, by God,' he replied, 'I'm to die then be brought back to life.' '"Want to take my wager?' I asked. '"What about?' '"That you'll not he brought back nor be called to account.' "He laughed, then said, 'Oh but yes, by God, Abii Sufysn; we certainly will be brought back, and then called to account so that one group can enter heaven and another hell-fire.' '"And which group are you in according to what your friend told you?' '"He has no knowledge of that, either in my case or his own.' "We journeyed on for two more nights, with him wondering at me and me laughing at hi, until we reached the Damascus valley. There we sold our goods, remaining there two months. "Then we again journeyed on until we reached a Christian village, where we made a stop. When they saw him they came to hi, gave him gifts and he went with them to their houses. He came back in the afternoon, put on his two garments and went to them. He returned later that night, threw off his clothes and fell on his bed. But I swear be did not sleep a wink. "Next morning he was sad and depressed, not talking to us nor we to him. Then he said, 'Wouldn't yon like to move on?' 'Why yes,' I responded. So we travelled on several nights, with him still depressed. Eventually he spoke, asking, 'Abn Sufyzn, would you like to travel on in advance of our companions?' 'Would you like that?' I responded. 'Yes,' he replied. "So off we went and travelled one hour's distance ahead of our companions. Then he said: 'Let's go to a rock.' 'What do you want,' I asked h i and he replied, 'Tell me about Wtba b. Rabi'a; does he avoid doing wrong or evil?' 'Certainly, by God.' 'Is he held in esteem by high and low, a central figure in the tribe?' 'Yes.' 'Do you know any man of Quraysh more noble than him?' 'No, by God, I know of no one.' 'Is he financially needy?' he asked. 'On the contrary, he's a man of great wealth.' 'How old is he?' 'He's over a hundred,' I replied. 'So nobility, age, and wealth have brought hi contempt.' 'Why should all that bring him contempt? No, by God, they benefit hi.' 'Just so; would you like to rest here?' he then asked. 'I would,' I replied. "So we rested till our fatigue passed. Then we travelled on till we alighted at the next rest station where we stayed. When it was night he spoke to me: 'Abn Sufyal.' 'What do you want?' I asked. 'Would you like to proceed as yesterday?' 'Would you?' I asked. 'Yes,' he replied. "So on we went on two long-necked camels until we were ahead. He said: 'Let's go to a rock and talk again about Wtba b. Rabi'a.' 'Let's talk of him again,' I replied. 'Does he avoid wrongdoing and evil and do good and order good be done?' he asked. 'Yes, by God, all that he does.' 'Is he wealthy?' 'Yes, he's wealthy.' 'Do you know of any man of Quraysh more central than him?' 'No, no one.' 'How old is he?' he asked. 'He is over a hundred.' 'So his age, nobility, and wealth have brought him contempt?' 'Certainly not, by God; if you have something to say, then do so.' 'No,' he replied, 'Just remember what I said, whatever results from it.' "Then he went on: 'What so affected me was going to that scholar and asking him some things and then saying to him, "Tell me about the prophet who is expected." '"He replied that he was an Arab; I responded that I knew that, and asked him from what group of Arabs. '"He said: "He is from the...'"
lessons: When truth confronts us — even through unexpected sources — the unease and restlessness we feel is often a sign of our conscience recognising something important; we should not suppress that feeling but investigate it honestly.
Spiritual anxiety about what comes after death is a natural and healthy form of reflection; it is an invitation to seek truth rather than a burden to be dismissed.

The example of Abu Sufyan's indifference and Umayya's troubled heart shows that proximity to truth without sincere action leads to spiritual stagnation, while even anxious seeking is a step in the right direction.
practical_advice: If you are feeling anxious about your purpose, your faith, or what happens after death, treat that anxiety as a signal to seek answers rather than avoid the questions — speak to a knowledgeable person you trust.
When difficult knowledge unsettles you, resist the urge to distract yourself or suppress the feeling; instead, sit with it, journal about it, and allow it to guide you toward clarity.

If someone in your life seems deeply troubled or preoccupied with existential questions, approach them with openness and patience rather than dismissiveness — they may be on the verge of an important realisation.

title: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt's Encounter with the Christian Scholar and the Prophecy of Muhammad
summary: Abu Sufyan recounts how Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt consulted a Christian scholar who described the coming Arab prophet with striking accuracy, unsettling both men and leading to a series of conversations about who this prophet would be.
story: he asked. "'He is over a hundred.' "'So his age, nobility, and wealth have brought him contempt?' "'Certainly not, by God; if you have something to say, then do so.' "'No,' he replied, 'Just remember what I said, whatever results from it.' "Then he went on: 'What so affected me was going to that scholar and asking him some things and then saying to him, "Tell me about the prophet who is expected." "'He replied that he was an Arab; I responded that I knew that, and asked him from what group of Arabs. "'He said: "He is from the people of a house to which the Arabs make pilgrimage." "'I said, "We have a house to which the Arabs make pilgrimage." "'He replied: "He is one of your brothers from Quraysh." "'This had an impact on me, by God, like nothing ever had before, and the success of this world and the next left my grasp. I had wanted to be him. I asked him then: "'"If so it will be, describe him to me." ""'He is a man young when he entered old age; he started out avoiding wrongdoing and evil, doing good and ordering it. He is poor, respected by high and low and has a central position in the tribe. Most of his army are of angels." ""'What is the sign of that?" I asked and he replied: ""'After the death of Jesus son of Mary, peace be upon him, Syria suffered 80 earthquakes, each causing a disaster. One overall quake is left which will bring several disasters.""' Abu Sufyan went on: "I responded: 'But, by God, that's silly; if God sent a messenger he would only take him away in age and honour.' "Umayya spoke up, 'But by him whose name you invoked, that's how it is, Abu Sufyan. He is reiterating that the Christian's statement is true. Shall we rest here?' "'Yes, I'd like that,' I responded." He went on: "So there we stayed until our fatigue left us and we travelled on till we were a two-stage or two-nights' distance from Mecca. Then a rider caught us up from behind; we questioned him and he said: 'After you left, the people of Syria suffered an earthquake that devastated them and caused them heavy losses.'" Abu Sufyan went on: "Then Umayya approached me and asked: 'So what do you think about what the Christian said, Abu Sufyan?' "I replied: 'By God, it's my considered view that what your friend told you was right.'"
lessons: Divine truth reaches people through many channels; those who seek sincerely will find signs even before they recognize them.
Worldly ambition and jealousy can blind a person to guidance that is standing right before them.
The fulfillment of prophecy is a mercy and a test simultaneously — it invites belief but also demands humility.
practical_advice: When you encounter information that challenges your assumptions, pause and reflect rather than dismissing it immediately.
Notice when jealousy or personal ambition is coloring your perception of the truth, and consciously set it aside.
Look for patterns of confirmation in your life — sometimes God sends multiple signs before we pay attention.

title: Abu Sufyan's Return to Mecca and His Encounter with the Prophet Muhammad
summary: After months in Yemen, Abu Sufyan returns to Mecca and is struck when the Prophet Muhammad — unlike every other merchant — does not ask about his goods, prompting Abu Sufyan's wife Hind to reveal that Muhammad now claims prophethood.
story: Abu Sufyan continued: "So we arrived at Mecca where I settled my current affairs, then left for Yemen on business, staying there five months before returning to Mecca. "While there at my house people come to greet me and ask me about their goods. Eventually Muhammad b. Abd Allah came to me; Hind was there with me playing with her children. He greeted me, welcomed me back and asked me about my journey and accommodations, but he did not enquire about his goods. Then he rose. I commented to Hind, 'By God, that surprises me! Every one of Quraysh who had goods with me asked me about them, but this man did not ask about his.' "Hind asked: 'Don't you know what's going on with him?' "Apprehensive, I asked her what, and she replied: 'He claims to be a messenger from God.' "She dumbfounded me. I recalled what the Christian had said, and I shivered. Hind asked me what was the matter and I came to myself and said: 'This is crazy! He is too smart to say that.' "'On the contrary,' she explained, 'he really is saying that and promulgating that. He also has followers in his religion.' "'This is crazy,' I repeated." Abu Sufyan continued: "I then went outside and while performing a ritual circumambulation of the ka'ba I met up with him. I said to him: 'Your goods totalled up to so-and-so and there were profits. Send someone to receive them; I won't be taking out the cut I charge my own people.' "But he refused that, saying: 'Then I won't accept them.' I went on: 'Then send someone to take them and I will subtract the cut I get from my people.' So he did send for his goods and received them, while I had from him what I would take from others."
lessons: The Prophet's detachment from worldly goods was itself a form of silent dawah that made even his opponents take notice.
Sometimes a single unexpected act of character speaks louder than any argument or sermon.
Denial of the truth is often driven not by lack of evidence but by pride and an unwillingness to accept disruption to one's worldview.
practical_advice: Practice detachment from material concerns in your daily interactions — people notice and remember integrity.
When something genuinely surprises you about a person's character, treat it as an invitation to reflect more deeply rather than explaining it away.
If you find yourself repeatedly dismissing something as 'crazy,' ask yourself honestly whether fear or pride might be driving that dismissal.

title: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt's Admission That He Hoped to Be the Prophet
summary: In a candid conversation with Abu Sufyan, Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt admits that his own scriptures pointed to an Arab prophet and that he had long believed it would be him, but the description clearly pointed to someone from Banu Abd Manaf.
story: Abu Sufyan went on: "So destiny struck its blow and revelation came to the Messenger of God (SAAS). And I left in a party of Quraysh on a business trip to Yemen, so I passed by Umayya and commented mockingly: 'Well Umayya, the prophet you were describing has emerged.' "'Indeed he is true, you should follow him,' he replied. "'But what prevents you from following him?' I enquired. "'Nothing but the embarrassment before the women of Thaqif. I have been telling them that I was him, then they would see me a follower of a young man of Banu Abd Manaf!' "Then Umayya continued: 'It's to me as though, Abu Sufyan, you've defied him then have been bound fast like a billy goat and taken to him for whatever judgement he might want.'"
