Al-Tabrani stated that Bakr b. Ahmad b. Nufayl related to him, quoting Abd Allah b. Shabib, quoting Ya'qub b. Muhammad al-Zuhri, quoting Mujashi' b. Amr al-Asadi, quoting Layth b. Sa'd, from Abu al-Aswad Muhammad b. Abd al-Rahman from Urwa b. al-Zubayr, from Mu'awiya b. Abi Sufyan, from Abi Sufyan b. Harb, who said that: "Umayya b. Abi al-Salt had been (with me) at Ghazza or Ilyaz' and while we were returning home Umayya asked me: 'Abu Sufyan, would you like to go on in advance of our companions so that we can talk?' "'Yes,' I agreed. "So we did." "Then he asked: 'What about Utba b. Rabi'a?' "'Honoured by high and low.' "'And does he avoid sin and evil deeds?' "'Yes.' "'Is he aged and of noble birth?' "'Both aged and of noble birth.' "'Nobility and age have brought him contempt!' "'You lie; his increase in age brought him only increase in honour.' "'Abu Sufyan, that's a word I've never heard anyone say to me since I reached awareness; don't rush to judgement before I tell you.' "'Well then do so,' I responded. "'In my books I would find reference to a prophet who would be sent from this area of ours and I thought — indeed I had no doubt — that I would be him. But when I enquired of scholars, he was to be of Banu Abd Manaf. So I looked into Banu Abd Manaf and the only person I found worthy of such a mission was Utba b. Rabi'a. And when you told me of his age, I knew that it was not him, since he had passed the age of 40 and had not received revelation.'"
lessons: Ego and social embarrassment are among the most powerful barriers between a person and accepting truth.
Knowledge without humility can become a curse — Umayya knew the signs yet could not bring himself to submit.
Pride in one's own status is not neutral; it actively blocks growth and can lead to eternal loss.
practical_advice: Identify the specific social or personal fear that is keeping you from making a positive change, and name it clearly so it loses its power over you.
Ask yourself honestly: am I resisting this truth because of evidence, or because of what people will think of me?
Remember that the courage to change your position publicly, even at social cost, is a sign of strength, not weakness.

title: The Vision of Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt's Daughter and the Angels Who Found Him Impure
summary: Multiple accounts describe visions in which angelic beings examined Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt's heart and found him spiritually impure, symbolizing that prophethood was offered to him but he turned away from it.
story: Abd al-Razzaq said that Ma'mar related to him, from al-Kalbi, as follows: "Umayya was once lying down with two of his daughters present when one of them got scared and screamed to him. 'What's wrong?' he asked her. She replied: 'I saw two eagles rip off the roof of the house. Then one of them swooped down on you and split open your belly while the other perched on top of the house. The latter asked: "Is he aware?" "Yes," was the response. "Is he pure?" the eagle asked again. "No," came the answer.' "He then said: 'This was good expected of your father; but he did it not.'" This was also told from another source in another way. Ishaq b. Bishr said, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from al-Zuhri, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyib and Uthman b. Abd al-Rahman, from al-Zuhri, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyib that al-Fari'a, sister of Umayya b. Abi al-Salt, went to see the Messenger of God (SAAS) after the conquest of Mecca. She was a person of reason, intellect, and beauty, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) was much impressed by her. One day he asked her: "Fari'a, do you know any of your brother's poetry?" "Yes indeed; and even more remarkable than that is what I've seen." She went on: "My brother was on a journey and after he left he appeared to me, and came and lay on my bed while I was stripping hair off a piece of hide I held. Suddenly two white birds — or what seemed like two white birds appeared. One alighted at the high window while the other came in and descended on to him. The latter then made a split between his chest and his pubic region, put its hand inside him, drew forth his heart, placed it on its palm then sniffed it. The other bird then asked: 'Is he aware?' 'Yes, he's aware,' the second replied. 'Is he pure?' it asked again. 'He declined,' answered the second. Then it replaced his heart where it belonged and the wound was healed in the twinkling of an eye. Then the two birds were both gone. "When I had seen that I went over to him and stirred him, saying: 'Do you feel anything?' He answered, 'No, except for some weakness in my body.' "I had been alarmed at what I had seen and he asked, 'Why do I see you upset?' "So I told him what had happened, and he responded: 'Goodness was wanted of me but was averted from me.' He then spoke the verses: 'My cares' distresses flowed on, blinding my eyes, my tears preceding, From the certainty that had come to me, and I had been brought no disavowal conveyed by anyone who spoke (to me), Will I be one of those blazed by fire surrounding them like a tent (by its smoke and flames) Or will I dwell in the paradise promised the guiltless, its cushions piled high? The two positions are not equal there, nor are actions equal in kind, They are two groups, one who enter paradise,'"
lessons: Spiritual purity is not merely intellectual acknowledgment of truth but a wholehearted turning of the heart toward God.
A person can possess extraordinary knowledge and gifts yet still fail the most important test — submission and sincerity.
The pain of missed opportunity is real; Umayya's own poetry reflects his awareness that he had turned away from something precious.
practical_advice: Reflect regularly on whether your heart is aligned with what your mind knows to be right — knowledge and action must move together.
Do not let the moment of spiritual readiness pass unused; when you feel drawn toward goodness, act on it before hesitation hardens into habit.
If you feel you have missed an important opportunity for growth or change, do not despair — acknowledge it honestly as Umayya did, and recommit to purity of intention going forward.

title: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt's Death and Final Lamentations
summary: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt, a poet who recognized the truth of prophethood but failed to embrace it, dies in spiritual anguish, repeatedly calling out as if before divine judgment and lamenting his own wrongdoing.
story: averted from me.' H e then spoke the verses: 'My cares' distresses flowed on, blinding my eyes, my tears preceding, From the certainty that had come to me, and I had been brought no disavowal conveyed by anyone who spoke (to me), Will I be one of those blazed by h e surrounding them like a tent (by its smoke and flames) Or will I dwell in the paradise promised the guiltless, its cushions piled high? The two positions are not equal there, nor are actions equal in k i d , They are two groups, one who enter paradise, its gardens enfolding them, And another group sent to hell, its installations paining them; These hearts were long accustomed, whenever tending to good, to have harriers set up against them, And these were diverted to misfortune away from the pursuit of paradise by a world which God will erase. A slave who called for his soul and censured it, knowing that God, the acutely aware, is clearly watching, What gives the spirit a desire for life? Even though it lives a while, death overtakes it. One fleeing his fate will one day soon unexpectedly agree to it, If you don't die exultant you will die decrepit; death has a cup and man must taste it.' She went on: "Then he continued on his journey, but only a little thereafter he was borne along in his bier. When news of it came to me I went to him and found him laid out in his burial winding-sheet. When I drew near him h e emitted a deep sigh and stared; he looked up to the ceiling and raised his voice, saying: '"Labbaykuma! Labbaykum~!At your service, at your service both of you; here I am before you hoth, not with money to ransom me nor with a clan to protect me.' "He then lost consciousness, having emitted a deep sigh. So I said, 'The man has expired! ' "But he stared up to the ceiling and raised his voice, saying: 'At your service, at your service both; here am I before you both. Not innocent to seek forgiveness, nor with kinsfolk to achieve victory.' "Then he lost consciousness, but suddenly emitted a deep sigh, stared up to the ceiling and said: 'At your service, at your service hoth, here am I before you both, blessed by good fortune but destroyed by wrongdoing.' "Then he lost consciousness, but suddenly emitted a deep sigh and said, 'At your service, at your service both, here I am before you both. "'If, 0 Lord, you forgive, forgive en masse; what slave of yours has no pain.' "Then he lost consciousness but soon emitted a deep sigh, and said: 'Each life, though it may last very long, is moving on hut once till when it ceases. "'Would that I were, before what appeared to me, shepherding goats in the mountain heights.'" She went on: "And then he died. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told me: '0 F%i'a, your brother is like him to whom God brought his signs but he passed them by . . .' (to the end of the verse)" (siirat al-AViif; VII, v.175).
lessons: Knowledge of the truth without acting upon it leads to deep spiritual regret — recognizing what is right is only the beginning; one must have the courage to follow it.
No worldly wealth, status, or clan can substitute for sincere faith when one stands before God; the only real refuge is sincere repentance and surrender.
Delaying a decision of the heart, especially one tied to eternal consequence, is itself a fateful choice — time and opportunity do not wait indefinitely.
practical_advice: When you recognize a truth that demands change in your life, act on it without delay; prolonged hesitation often leads to permanent loss of the opportunity.
In moments of anxiety about your spiritual state, turn to sincere supplication and repentance rather than poetry or regret alone — God's mercy is available to those who sincerely seek it.
Reflect regularly on your own accountability before God while you still have time to correct your course, rather than waiting until circumstances force the reflection upon you.

title: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt Meets the Prophet and Witnesses the Recitation of Surah Ya-Sin
summary: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt arranges a meeting with the Prophet Muhammad, listens to the recitation of Surah Ya-Sin, privately acknowledges the Prophet's truth, but ultimately departs without accepting Islam.
story: Al-Zuhri went on: "Then Umayya b. Abti &Salt left for Bahrain and the Messenger of God (SAAS) claimed prophecy. Umayya resided eight years in Bahrain then went to Tz'if, where he asked people, 'What is Muhammad son of 'Abd Allxh saying?' "They replied: 'He claims that he is a prophet; he is the one yon were wishing for.' " He continued: "So Umayya left for Mecca where he met him and asked: '0 son of 'Ahd al-MutMib, what is this yon are saying?' He replied: 'I say that I am the Messenger of God, and that there is no God but Him.' Umayya said, 'I would like to speak with yon; give me an appointment tomorrow.' 'Certainly, you have an appointment tomorrow,' came the reply. Umayya then asked: 'Would you like me to come alone or with a group of my friends?' 'Whichever you prefer,' responded the Messenger of God (SAAS). 'Then I will come with a group, and so you too come with a group,' concluded Umayya. "So early morning Umayya appeared with a group from Quraysh while the Messenger of God (SAAS) came with a number of his Companions. They all sat down in the shade of the kacba. Umayya began by making an address, then spoke some rhyming prose, and recited some poetry. When he had finished the poetry, he said: 'Answer me then, son of 'Abd al-MutMib.' At that the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke the words, 'In the name of God the beneficent, the merciful, Ys Sin. By the Qur% full of wisdom.' (sarat Yii Sin, XXXVI, v.1-2). When he had finished the chapter, Umayya arose suddenly and walked off, dragging his feet. The Quraysh men followed him, asking, LWell,what do you have to say, Umayya?' 'I bear witness', replied Umayya, 'that he is right.' 'Will you follow him?' they asked. 'Until I look into his case,' he replied."
lessons: Intellectual acknowledgment of truth is not the same as spiritual submission to it; the gap between 'he is right' and 'I follow him' can become the defining tragedy of a person's life.
Social pressure and the opinion of one's peers can paralyze a person into inaction even when their own conscience has already confirmed the right path.
The Quran's power to move hearts is evident even in those who ultimately resist it — its truth resonates even when pride or hesitation prevents full surrender.
practical_advice: When your heart recognizes something as true and right, do not let the phrase 'until I look into it further' become an indefinite postponement — set a real deadline for your decision.
Be aware of how the presence of peers and social groups can influence major personal decisions; seek moments of solitude to make your most important choices.

If you find yourself caught between what you know to be right and what your social circle expects, name that tension explicitly and address it directly rather than allowing it to quietly paralyze you.

title: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt at the Battlefield of Badr — Grief and Final Rejection of Islam
summary: After the Battle of Badr, Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt visits the burial pit of fallen Quraysh leaders, mourns them in verse, and ultimately turns away from Islam for good.
story: He continued: "Umayya went off to Syria and the Messenger of God (SAAS) left for Medina. Then, following the deaths of the people at the battle of Badr, Umayya returned from Syria and stayed at Badr. He travelled on to see the Messenger of God (SAAS). Someone asked Umayya: '0 Abn al-Salt, what do you want?' 'I want Muhammad,' he replied. 'What will you do?' he was asked. H e replied, 'I believe in him and I will throw him the keys of this affair.' Someone asked him: 'Do you know who is in the burial pit?' 'No,' he answered. He was told: 'In it are Wtba b. Rabica and Shayba b. Rabi'a, your maternal uncle's sons, along with his mother Rabi'a, daughter of 'Abd Shams.' He went on: "So Umayya mutilated the ears of his riding camel and cut off its tai1, then stood over the pit and spoke the verses: 'What is in Badr - Mt. 'Aqanqai but chiefs and noble lords ...' (up to the end of the ode, which we will give in full in relating the history of the battle of Badr, if God wills it.) "He then returned to Mecca and Tx'if and left Islam."
lessons: Grief for worldly losses can become a barrier to spiritual truth if it is allowed to override what the heart already knows to be right.
Tribal loyalty and personal attachment, when placed above truth, can undo a person at the very moment they were closest to accepting guidance.
The nearness to accepting truth makes its rejection all the more consequential — the greater the opportunity, the greater the loss when it is turned away.
practical_advice: In moments of grief and personal loss, be careful not to make permanent spiritual decisions based on temporary emotional pain — allow yourself time to grieve before making life-defining choices.
Recognize when loyalty to people or a community is serving as a substitute for loyalty to truth, and ask yourself honestly which deserves priority.
If you have been postponing an important spiritual or moral decision, reflect on whether unresolved grief or anger toward others is the real obstacle, and address that first.

title: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt and the Origin of 'Bismika Allahumma'
summary: On a journey, Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt and companions from Quraysh are terrorized by a spirit woman after killing a snake, and Umayya is taught by an old spirit to say 'In your name, O God,' which repels her.
story: Al-Suhayli mentioned in his book al-TaCrif ma al-Iclam (Identzhing and Infommng) that Umayya b. Abn al-Salt was the h s t who said, "bismika Allahumma", i.e. "in your name, 0 God". And concerning that he told a strange tale, as follows. He says that they were among a group of Qnraysh who went away on a journey. They included Harh b. Umayya, the father of Abii Sufym. On their way they came across a snake and killed it. But that evening a spirit woman came to them and berated them for killing that snake. With her she had a staff which she struck on the ground so hard that it completely stampeded their camels and they scattered in all directions. So off they went and chased them till they had them back. When they were all gathered, she again appeared, struck her staff on the ground and stampeded the camels. So off they went to search for them but having failed they asked Umayya, "By God, do YOU have any way out of our problem?" He replied, "No, by God, hut I'll look into it." SOthey travelled on into that region hoping to find someone whom they could ask for some solution to their trouble. Eventually they saw a light ahead in the distance. When they arrived there they found an old man lighting a fire at the door of his tent. But he was in fact an evil spirit who was extremely tiny and ugly. They greeted him and he asked them what they wanted. (When they told him) he said: "When she comes to you, say: 'In your name, 0 God' and she will flee." So when the travellers had assembled their camels and she came for the third or fourth time, Umayya looked her in the face and said: "In your name, 0 God." She promptly fled in disarray. But the evil spirits attacked Harb b. Umayya and killed him with that snake. His companions buried him there far from home or friend. On that subject the spirits spoke the verse: "The grave of Harb is in a wasteland and there is no grave near that of Harb."
lessons: Divine protection is invoked through sincere acknowledgment of God's name — even partial faith and turning to God in moments of crisis can bring real relief.
Seeking counsel and guidance when facing a problem beyond one's own ability is a sign of wisdom, not weakness.
The death of Harb b. Umayya far from home illustrates that no worldly precaution can override one's appointed fate, making reliance on God essential rather than optional.
practical_advice: When overwhelmed by fear or a problem that feels beyond your control, begin by invoking God's name and presence — this simple act of acknowledgment can reorient your heart and bring calm.
Do not face serious difficulties alone out of pride; seek counsel from those wiser or more experienced, as Umayya's group did when they were helpless.
Reflect on the fragility of life and the reality that we are far from permanent safety in this world — let this motivate consistent spiritual preparation rather than panic.

title: Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt and His Understanding of the Languages of Animals
summary: Several accounts describe Umayya ibn Abi al-Salt apparently understanding the speech of animals, including a ewe warning her lamb, a mule complaining of a needle, and a crow predicting both his and its own death.
story: Some people say that Umayya sometimes understood the languages of the animals. When passing some bird in his travel he would tell his companions, "This one says so-and-so." But they would say they did not know whether he spoke the truth. But once they passed by a flock of sheep from which an ewe and her kid had been separated. She turned to it and bleated, as though urging it to hurry. Umayya asked his companions whether they knew what it said and they replied that they did not. He explained: "She's saying, 'Let's hurry along so the wolf won't come and eat you up like one did your brother last year.'" The group moved quickly on and asked the shepherd whether a wolf had eaten a sheep in that area the previous year. He replied in the affirmative! One day, moreover, Umayya passed by a mule carrying a woman and raising its head to her and grumbling. Umayya said: "The mule is telling the woman that there's a needle in the saddle which she mounted on him." So they took the woman off, untied the saddle, and discovered there was a needle as he had said. Ibn alSikkit recounted that Umayya b. Abii al-Salt was drinking when a crow cawed. Umayya said, "May you have dust twice in your mouth!" His companions asked Umayya what it was saying and he replied that it had said: "You'll drink that cup in your hand then die." At that point the crow cawed again and Umayya reported: "It says: 'The proof is that I'm going to fly down to this garbage pile, eat some and a hone is going to stick in my throat so I will die.'" At that the crow swooped down to the garbage pile, ate something, a bone lodged in its throat and it died! Umayya said, "Well, he spoke the truth in this about himself but I'm going to see whether he was right about me or not." He then drank that cup.
lessons: Extraordinary gifts of perception or knowledge do not in themselves lead a person to salvation — what matters is whether one acts rightly upon what they know.
Signs and warnings, whether from creation or from revelation, are only beneficial when they move a person to action rather than mere curiosity or passive observation.
The story of the crow's self-fulfilling prophecy is a reminder that death is certain and that one should not treat warnings about it as matters to simply 'wait and see.'
practical_advice: When you receive a warning — from a friend, your own conscience, or reflection on life — resist the urge to treat it as something to test or verify later; act on it while you still can.
Gifts and abilities are a trust; ask yourself regularly whether the talents and perceptions you have been given are being used in service of what is truly good.
Use reminders of mortality not as sources of dread but as gentle recalibrations that help you focus on what genuinely matters in your daily life.

title: Umayya b. Abi al-Salt and the Crow's Death Omen
summary: Umayya b. Abi al-Salt witnessed a crow predict its own death and then his own, after which he drank a cup and died, fulfilling the omen. The story illustrates how he recognized prophetic truth yet refused to submit to Islam.
story: then die." At that point the crow cawed again and Umayya reported: "It says: 'The proof is that I'm going to fly down to this garbage pile, eat some and a hone is going to stick in my throat so I will die.'" At that the crow swooped down to the garbage pile, ate something, a bone lodged in its throat and it died! Umayya said, "Well, he spoke the truth in this about himself but I'm going to see whether he was right about me or not." He then drank that cup in his hand, keeled over, and died!
lessons: Recognizing truth intellectually is not the same as submitting to it with one's heart — true faith requires both acknowledgment and surrender.
Death is a certainty that no one can escape; the wise person prepares for it rather than testing fate.
Pride and hesitation can prevent a person from taking the step that would transform their life, even when the truth stands clearly before them.
practical_advice: When you recognize something to be true deep down, do not delay acting on it out of pride or habit — act before it is too late.
Reflect on your own mortality regularly so that you are not caught unprepared; use this awareness to motivate positive change now.
If you find yourself saying 'I'll see about that later' regarding important spiritual or personal growth, treat that delay as a warning sign and address it today.

title: The Prophet's Appreciation of Umayya b. Abi al-Salt's Poetry
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) listened to one hundred verses of Umayya b. Abi al-Salt's poetry while riding and commented that Umayya almost became a Muslim, noting that his poetry believed but his heart disbelieved.
story: The Imam Ahmad stated that Rawh related to him, quoting Zakariya' b. Ishaq, from Ibrahim b. Maysara, that he heard 'Amr b. al-Sharid say that al-Sharid stated, "I was riding on the same camel as the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he asked me, 'Do you know any of the verses of Umayya b. Abi al-Salt?' 'Why yes,' I replied. 'So recite them,' he asked. I then did recite a line and he went on asking for more, with me reciting till I had spoken a hundred lines for him. At that point the Prophet (SAAS) remained silent as did I." This anecdote is similarly told by Muslim from a hadith of Sufyan b. 'Uyayna from Abi Tamim b. Maysara. In another line we have it from 'Amr b. al-Sharid from his father al-Sharid b. Suwayd al-Thaqafi from the Prophet (SAAS). In some accounts the Messenger of God's words were, 'If he had almost become a Muslim.' Yahya b. Muhammad b. Sa'id stated that Ibrahim b. Sa'id al-Jawhari related to him, quoting Abu Usama, quoting Hatim b. Abi Sufra, from Simak b. Harb, from 'Amr b. Nafi', from al-Sharid al-Hamdani (whose uncles were of Thaqif) that he said, "We went off with the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the 'farewell pilgrimage'. While I was walking along one day I sensed the tread of a camel behind me and it was the Messenger of God (SAAS). He said: 'Al-Sharid?' I replied, 'Yes.' Then he asked, 'Can't I give you a ride?' 'Why certainly,' I accepted. I was not tired but wanted the baraka, the blessing, of riding with the Messenger of God (SAAS). So he made his camel kneel and carried me, asking: 'Do you know any verses of Umayya b. Abi al-Salt?' I replied in the affirmative and he asked me to recite them, which I did. 'I think he spoke a hundred verses,' he commented. Then he added, 'God has knowledge about Umayya b. Abi al-Salt.'" Ibn Sa'id then stated, "This is a strange hadith; and in what is related is that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said of Umayya: 'His poetry believed but his heart disbelieved.' God alone knows."
lessons: Talent and insight are gifts from God, but they do not automatically lead a person to salvation — the heart must also choose to submit.
The Prophet's appreciation of beauty and wisdom in poetry shows that Islam values intellectual and artistic excellence when it points toward truth.
A person can be very close to the right path and still miss it; this is a reminder never to delay one's spiritual commitments.
practical_advice: Do not assume that knowing the right thing or even articulating it beautifully is sufficient — examine whether your heart and actions are aligned with what you know.
Seek the company of righteous people even briefly, as al-Sharid sought the blessing of riding with the Prophet, because such moments carry profound benefit.
When you admire someone's wisdom or eloquence, use it as a stepping stone to deeper reflection on truth rather than letting admiration stop at the surface.

title: The Prophet's Declaration That the Truest Poetic Word Is Labid's Verse
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) declared that the truest word ever spoken by a poet was Labid's verse: 'Is not all vain except God,' affirming that ultimate reality belongs only to the Divine.
story: The following anecdote is established in the sahih collection in a hadith of Ibn Mahdi from al-Thawri from 'Abd al-Malik b. 'Umayr, from Aba Salama, from Abu Hurayra who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said: "The truest word spoken by a poet is the verse of Labid: 'Is not all vain except God.' And Umayya b. Abi al-Salt almost became a Muslim."
lessons: Everything in this world is transient and ultimately without lasting substance except God — anchoring one's sense of meaning in the Divine brings true stability.
The Prophet's engagement with poetry shows that wisdom can be found in unexpected places and should be recognized and appreciated wherever it appears.
A single line of truth, sincerely held, outweighs volumes of eloquence that do not reach the heart.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the vanity or emptiness of worldly pursuits, return to the foundational truth that only God endures — let this reorient your priorities.
Train yourself to extract wisdom and benefit even from sources that are imperfect or incomplete, as the Prophet did with Umayya's poetry.
Memorize or keep close to you a short phrase or verse that reconnects you to what truly matters, and return to it in moments of confusion or distress.

title: Umayya b. Abi al-Salt's Verses on the Sun and Angels
summary: Umayya b. Abi al-Salt composed verses describing the sun's reluctant rising and the angels urging it upward, which the Prophet (SAAS) confirmed as true, supplementing with a hadith about seventy thousand angels urging the sun each morning.
story: Ibn 'Abd stated: "'Abd Allah b. Muhammad — who was Abu Bakr b. Abi Shayba — related to us, quoting 'Abda b. Sulayman, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from Ya'qub b. 'Utba, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) declared Umayya to be truthful in a piece of his poetry which says: 'As a man with a bull beneath his right foot, and an eagle for the other, and a lion in waiting; And the sun appears at the end of each night, its colour red in the morning and turning rosy; It is reluctant to arise for us, in its gentleness, unless tortured or else flogged.'" The Messenger of God (SAAS) said (of this verse): "He spoke the truth." In an account of Abu Bakr al-Hudhali, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, he (the Prophet) said, "The sun never rises until it is urged up by 70,000 angels saying: 'Come on up! Come on up!' And it replies: 'I will not rise above any people who worship me to the exclusion of God.' When just about to rise a devil comes to the sun intending to divert it, but it rises between the devil's horns and burns him. And when the sun gathers itself for its setting it directs itself to God, the Almighty and Glorious. So a devil comes to it wishing to divert it from its obeisance but it sets between his horns and burns him."
lessons: The natural world is in constant worship and submission to God, even when human beings are heedless — reflecting on this can restore a sense of humility and awe.
Truth can sometimes be glimpsed by those who have not fully submitted to faith, but only revelation confirms and completes that glimpse.
The consistency of creation's obedience to God stands as a reminder and an encouragement for human beings to align themselves with the same devotion.
practical_advice: When you wake each morning, pause to recognize that the very rising of the sun is an act of cosmic worship — let this orient your own day toward gratitude and purpose.
When doubt arises, look at the order and consistency of the natural world as evidence of a Creator who sustains all things with purpose.
Reflect on how inanimate creation submits to God effortlessly, and use this as motivation to willingly choose submission rather than resisting what you know to be right.

title: Umayya b. Abi al-Salt's Verses on the Throne-Bearers and the Angels
summary: Umayya b. Abi al-Salt composed verses describing the awesome burden of the angels who bear God's throne, trembling beneath its weight out of profound fear and reverence.
story: In his poem on the throne-bearers, Umayya recited: "And there's many a bearer of one of the supports of his throne, who, were it not for the God of creation, would be fatigued and slow-moving, Standing on their feet, weary beneath it, their jugular veins trembling from the severity of their fear." These verses were transmitted by Ibn 'Asakir. It is reported from al-Asma'i that he would recite the following verses of Umayya: "Glorify God, for He is worthy of glory; our Lord in heaven has been mighty. There in his great edifice that existed prior to man, above the heavens, where he laid down his throne. Longer it is than sight can see, before it you see al-mala'ik (the angels) bow down."
lessons: Even the mightiest of God's creation — the angels bearing His throne — are overwhelmed by His majesty, which puts human pride and self-sufficiency in perspective.
Glorifying God is not a burden but a natural response to recognizing His greatness; those who perceive His majesty are moved to praise.
Awe of God is not paralyzing but orienting — it reminds every created being of their proper place and relationship to the Creator.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by your own burdens, reflect that even the greatest of God's angels carry their immense weight only by His sustaining power — ask God to sustain you in the same way.
Incorporate regular moments of glorification (tasbih) into your day, even briefly, as a way of recentering yourself on what is truly great and worthy.
Use reflection on the vastness of God's creation and the unseen world to shrink your worries to their proper size and restore a sense of calm trust.

title: The Story of 'Abd Allah b. Jud'an and the Prophet's Response About His Good Deeds
summary: 'A'isha asked the Prophet (SAAS) whether the famous generosity and nobility of 'Abd Allah b. Jud'an would benefit him in the afterlife, and the Prophet replied that it would not because he never once asked God for forgiveness on the Day of Judgement.
story: The following verses praising 'Abd Allah b. Jud'an of Taym are also from the poetry of Umayya b. Abi al-Salt: "Should I make mention of my need or is your sensitivity enough for me; your nature is goodness And your knowledge of rights; you are of lofty status; refinement, high esteem, and majesty are yours. You are a generous man whose fine character no morning or evening time can change. You compete with the wind by your nobility and generosity when cold winter nights prevent the dog from staying outside (the tent). Your land is one of all nobility built by Banu Taym, and you are its sky. Any man who one day praises you has in offering praise in itself reward enough." Umayya offered him other verses of praise in his poetry. This man 'Abd Allah b. Jud'an was a much praised, very noble, and famous man. He had a platter from which a rider could eat while on his mount, since it was so large and piled with food. He would fill it with the finest wheat mixed with butter and honey. He used to pay to free slaves and help the unfortunate. 'A'isha asked the Prophet (SAAS) whether all that would ultimately benefit him. But he replied: "He never once said the words: 'O Lord, forgive me my sins on Judgement Day!'"
lessons: Good deeds and generosity are praiseworthy, but without a relationship with God and acknowledgment of one's need for His forgiveness, they do not fulfill the deepest purpose of human existence.
No amount of worldly nobility or charity can substitute for the humility of turning to God and recognizing one's dependence on His mercy.
This story is a call to self-examination: it is not enough to do good in the world; one must also cultivate a conscious relationship with God.
practical_advice: Do not let your good deeds give you a false sense of spiritual security — regularly turn to God in humility and ask for His forgiveness and mercy.
If you are in the habit of helping others, which is beautiful and important, make sure your inner life is equally cultivated through prayer, reflection, and connection to God.
Use the reminder of accountability on the Day of Judgement not as a source of paralyzing fear but as a gentle motivator to ensure your outward generosity is matched by inward sincerity.

title: Bahira the Monk Recognizes the Signs of Prophethood in the Young Muhammad
summary: The Christian monk Bahira perceived the signs of prophethood in the young Muhammad (SAAS), then aged twelve, when he traveled to Syria with his uncle Abu Talib, noticing a cloud that shaded him alone from the rest of the caravan.
story: BAHfRA THE MONK. It was he who perceived prophethood in the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he was with his uncle Abu Talib and went to Syria in a company of merchants of Mecca. He was at that time 12 years of age. (Bahira) saw a cloud shading him apart from the rest, so he made food for them and invited them to be his guests, as we will relate in the biography. Al-Tirmidhi related an account which we discussed in detail therein. The Ibn 'Asakir recounted various testimonies and evidences relating to the biography of Bahira, but did not recount what al-Tirmidhi told; and that is strange. Ibn 'Asakir reported that Bahira lived in a village called al-Kafr situated some six miles from Basra. That is the place known as "Bahira's monastery". And it is also said that he lived in a village called Manfa'a in the plain of Balqa', beyond Zayra. But God knows best.
lessons: Divine providence was evident in the life of the Prophet from his earliest years, with signs appearing even to those outside the Muslim community.
The recognition of truth by a sincere seeker — even one from a different faith tradition — is a testimony to the universality of God's guidance.
God protects and honours those He has chosen for a great mission, often in ways that are visible to others before the person themselves fully understands their role.
practical_advice: Reflect on how even in your own early life there may have been signs, moments of grace, or protection that you can now recognize as God's care over you.
Remain open to recognizing truth and goodness wherever it appears, even in unexpected people or places, as Bahira did.
When you feel uncertain about your path or purpose, trust that if God has a role for you, He will prepare the way — your task is to remain sincere and attentive.

title: Quss b. Sa'ida al-Iyadi's Speech at the 'Ukaz Festival
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) recalled the eloquent preacher Quss b. Sa'ida al-Iyadi, who spoke at the 'Ukaz festival on mortality, the passing of generations, and the certainty of death, declaring that God has a religion more pleasing than what his people followed.
story: The hafiz Abu Bakr Muhammad b. Ja'far b. Sahl al-Khara'iti said in his book Hawatif al-Jann (The Calls of the Spirits) that Dawud al-Qanyari related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Salih and Abu 'Abd Allah al-Mashriqi, from Abu al-Harith al-Warraq, from Thawr b. Yazid, from Muriq al-'Ijli, from 'Ubada b. al-Samit that the latter said that when the delegation from the tribe of Iyad came to the Prophet (SAAS) he asked: "O members of the Iyad delegation, what happened to Quss b. Sa'ida al-Iyadi?" They replied, "He died, O Messenger of God." He commented, "Well, I once witnessed him at the 'Ukaz festival mounted on a red camel speaking words of strange and wonderful eloquence that I find I don't recall." A bedouin from the far back of the group came up to him and said: "I remember it, O Messenger of God," and the Prophet (SAAS) was pleased at this. "Yes", the man went on, "he was at the 'Ukaz festival on a red camel and saying, 'O people gather around, for all those who have passed away are gone. And things to come will come. Gloomy nights. Castle-filled skies. Seas that rage. Splendorous stars. Towering mountains. Flowing streams. In the heavens there is notice; on earth there is warning. How is it I see people pass away and do not return? Were they content to stay and did so? Or were they left and fell asleep? Quss swears by an oath beyond reproach, that God has a religion more pleasing than the one you follow.' Then he recited as follows: 'In those who first passed away in ages gone there are signs for us, Since I saw ways leading to death which had no beginnings, And I saw my people passing towards them, young and old alike, Not anyone who has passed on will come to you, nor will any of those who stay remain behind; I have become sure that I will inevitably pass on to where the people have gone.'" This chain of authorities as given is strange. Al-Tabarani related it from another line, stating in his book al-Mu'jam al-Kabir (The Great Encyclopedia) that Muhammad b. al-Sarri b. Mahran b. al-Naqid al-Baghdadi related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Hassan al-Sahmi, quoting Muhammad b. al-Hajjaj, from Mujalid, from al-Sha'bi, from Ibn 'Abbas, that he said: "The delegation from 'Abd al-Qays came to see the Prophet (SAAS) and he asked them, 'Which of you knows al-Quss b. Sa'ida al-Iyadi?' They replied, 'We all knew him, O Messenger of God.' 'Well, what's he done?' he asked. 'He died,' they told him. He went on: 'I'll never forget him at the 'Ukaz festival in the sacred month. He was on a red camel..."
lessons: The reality of death and the passage of generations is a universal truth that calls every person to reflect on what truly matters before their own time comes.
God's guidance reaches people through many channels — even before formal prophecy, sincere souls could perceive that a truer religion existed than what their community practiced.
The Prophet's fondness for and memory of Quss's speech shows that words of wisdom about mortality and meaning are precious and worth preserving.
practical_advice: When you are grieving the loss of someone or feeling the weight of life's transience, let that feeling turn you toward reflection and gratitude rather than despair.
Take time to contemplate the reality of death not as a morbid exercise but as a clarifying one — ask yourself what you would regret leaving undone and act on that today.
Pay attention to voices of wisdom around you, even from unexpected sources, because God places reminders of truth in many places for those who are willing to hear.

title: The Delegation from Abd al-Qays and the Memory of Quss b. Sa'ida
summary: A delegation from Abd al-Qays visited the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS), who asked them about Quss b. Sa'ida al-Iyadi, learning he had died, and recalled his profound speeches at the Ukaz festival about mortality, the passage of time, and the inevitability of return to God.
story: The delegation from CAbd al-Qays came to see the Prophet (SAAS) and he asked them, 'Which of you knows al-Quss b. Sa'ida al-Iyadi?' They replied, 'We all knew him, O Messenger of God.' 'Well, what's he done?' he asked. 'He died,' they told him. He went on: 'I'll never forget him at the 'Ukaz festival in the sacred month. He was on a red camel addressing the people in the words, "O people, gather, listen and take note. Those who live die. Those who die pass away. And what is to come will come. In heaven there is notice and on earth there is warning. A resting-place is put down and a roof is raised up. Stars move to and fro and seas do not empty. And Quss swore a true oath that if there be contentment in the matter, then discontent will follow! God has a religion more beloved by Him than that in which you engage. How is it I see people pass away and do not return? Were they content to stay and did so? Or were they left and fell asleep?" Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Can anyone among you recite his poetry?' One of them responded: 'In those who first passed away in ages gone there are signs for us. Since I never saw ways leading to death which had no beginnings, And I saw my people moving towards them, young and old alike, The past will never return for me, nor will any of those who stay remain behind. I have become sure I will inevitably pass on to where the people have gone.'
lessons: Reflecting on the mortality of others is a spiritual practice the Prophet himself engaged in, reminding us that contemplating death is not morbid but deeply meaningful.

Great wisdom can come from unexpected sources — Quss b. Sa'ida, a pre-Islamic Arab, arrived at truths about God and the afterlife through sincere reflection, showing that the human heart can find its way toward truth.
The grief of losing a wise person is natural and honourable; the Prophet's fond remembrance of Quss shows that mourning those who spoke truth is a sign of spiritual depth.
practical_advice: When you are grieving a loss, try to honour the person's memory by recalling the wisdom or goodness they embodied — this transforms grief into gratitude.
Spend time reflecting on the temporary nature of life not to feel despair, but to reprioritise what truly matters to you today.
Seek wisdom wherever it appears, even in unexpected people or traditions, as sincerity and reflection can lead anyone toward profound truth.

title: Al-Jarud al-Abdi Accepts Islam Before the Prophet
summary: Al-Jarud b. al-Mu'alla, a learned Christian scholar from the tribe of Abd al-Qays, came to the Prophet (SAAS) with a delegation, recited poetry in his honour, engaged him in sincere dialogue about faith, and embraced Islam along with some of his companions.
story: Al-Jaid b. al-Mu'alla b. Hanash b. Mu'alla al-'Abdi was a Christian of great learning in the interpretation and analysis of the holy texts, an expert on the history and sayings of the Persians, well versed in philosophy and medicine, a man of evident good manners and culture, fine in appearance and of great wealth. He went to see the Prophet (SAAS) as a member of a delegation of men of sound judgement, maturity, eloquence, and scholarship from the tribe of 'Abd Qays. When he approached the Prophet (SAAS) he stood before him, gestured towards him and spoke the verses: 'O Prophet of the right guidance, men have come to you through deserts and mirages, Crossing down the flat open lands towards you, not counting fatigue for you as fatigue at all. All the short-legged beasts greatly urged along in speed by our young she-camels, The fine horses racing over the land, bolting with their mailclad warriors like stars, glinting, Seeking to avert the suffering of an awful day, one of terror, pain, and great distress, A day when all mankind will be brought together, a day separating out all who strayed in disobedience, Moving towards a light from God, a proof, a favour and a grace to be attained, God distinguished you with all good, O son of Amina, through her; when (for others) good only comes and goes. So, give your blessings in abundance, O proof of God, unlike the blessings of a false (prophet).' When he finished, the Prophet (SAAS) invited him to sit down close to himself and said, 'O Jarud, both you and your people are late in coming.' Al-Jarud responded, 'By my father and my mother, anyone late coming to you is indeed unfortunate, that being the greatest of faults, the worst of sins. But I'm not one of those who saw you or heard of you, aggressed against you, and followed someone other than you. I now follow a religion of which you have knowledge. But I have come to you and here I am, abandoning it for your religion. Will that not have faults, sins, and misdeeds forgiven and please the Lord?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'I will guarantee you that! So sincerely express your belief in the unity (i.e. monotheism) and give up the religion of Christianity.' Al-Jarud replied: 'By my father and my mother, extend your hand, for I bear witness that there is no God but God alone and that He has no associate; and I testify that you are Muhammad, His slave and His Messenger.' And so he accepted Islam, as did some of his people. And the Prophet (SAAS) was pleased at their accepting Islam and treated them with such honour and generosity as to make them very happy and delighted.
lessons: It is never too late to seek truth — al-Jarud came late, yet the Prophet received him with warmth and gave him the assurance he needed, showing that God's door of mercy is always open.

Sincere questioning before making a commitment to faith is not weakness but wisdom; al-Jarud openly asked whether his past would be forgiven before he took his shahada, and the Prophet honoured that sincerity.
When someone embraces a new path with courage, honouring them generously — as the Prophet did — strengthens their conviction and makes the transition feel safe and supported.
practical_advice: If you feel it is too late to change, seek forgiveness, or begin again — remember al-Jarud's example and take the first step anyway, trusting that sincerity is what matters most.
When facing a major life decision involving your values or beliefs, it is entirely appropriate to ask the hard questions first before committing, as honest inquiry leads to genuine conviction.

When someone around you makes a courageous change — leaving behind something familiar to embrace something better — celebrate and honour them warmly, as the Prophet did, to help them feel secure in their new path.

title: Al-Jarud's Account of Quss b. Sa'ida's Life and Wisdom
summary: After accepting Islam, al-Jarud described to the Prophet (SAAS) the remarkable life of Quss b. Sa'ida — a pre-Islamic Arab monotheist who spent centuries in reflection, warned of death and resurrection, and delivered powerful orations at the Ukaz festival about God's oneness and the Day of Judgement.
story: Then the Messenger of God went over to them and asked, 'Does any one of you know Quss b. Sa'ida al-Iyadi?' Al-Jarud replied: 'By my father and mother, we all know him. And I am one who knows a lot about him. Quss, O Messenger of God, was a fine Arab knight. He lived 600 years, of which he spent five lifetimes living in the wastelands and deserts, crying out the glory of God like Jesus did. He settled nowhere, had no home to shelter him and no neighbour to appreciate him. He dressed in hair-cloth and outdid the fasting ascetics. He never tired of his monastic ways, on his journeys living off ostrich eggs and befriending lions, enjoying the darkness, observing and considering, thinking and experiencing. He therefore became one whose wisdom was legion, awe-inspiring things being discovered through him, and he attained the leadership of the disciples of Christ in reputation. He was the first Arab to acknowledge God and to accept His unity. He devoted himself firmly to God, becoming fully convinced of life after death and of judgement. He warned of the evil of refusal and enjoined action before it was too late. He reminded of death and spoke of his acceptance of fate without either anger or pleasure. He visited graves and preached of resurrection day. He spoke verses in elegy. He gave thought to the fates and informed about the heavens and about nature. He talked of the stars and discussed the water. He described the seas and informed of ancient relics. He orated while riding and exhorted with perseverance. He admonished against grief and against an excess of anger. He wrote many sermons and made mention of all things terrible. He spoke compellingly in his orations, gave explanation in his writings, expressed fear of time, cautioned against weakness, exalted God's power, avoided any blasphemy, expressed yearning for the pure faith, and called for immersion in religious life and thought. He is the person who said on the day of the 'Ukaz festival: "East and west, orphans and groups, peace and war, dry and moist, brackish water or sweet, suns and moons, winds and rains, nights and days, females and males, lands and seas, seeds and plants, fathers and mothers, united and scattered, signs followed by signs, light and dark, abundance and nothingness, a Lord and idols, mankind has gone astray, the raising of a child, the burial of a dead one, the cultivation of a harvest, rich and poor, benefactor and evil-doer, woe to the indolent, let the worker improve his labour, and let the hopeful lose his hope. Nay; on the contrary He is one God, neither given birth nor a father. He both repeats and does for the first time. He makes to die and brings to life. He created male and female, Lord of the first life and of the after life. Moreover, O tribe of Iyad, where now are Thamud and 'Ad? Where are the fathers and their forefathers? Where now are those (who were) ill and those who visited them? To each is his appointed time. Quss swears by the Lord of mankind, and the one who flattens the smooth ground, you will be gathered individually, on assembly day, when horns will be blown and drums be beaten, when the earth will shine, and the preacher exhort and the despairing withdraw and the observer gazes out; woe to him who turns away from the truth most evident, and the shining light, and the great Judgement Day, on the day of separating, and the scales of justice, when He who decrees decides, and the apostle gives testimony, and the allies are far off, and the faults are apparent, and so one group goes to paradise, and one group to hell-fire."'
lessons: A life devoted to sincere reflection and remembrance of God — even in solitude, hardship, and without formal community — can yield profound spiritual wisdom and lasting impact on others.
Quss's life teaches that contemplating mortality, visiting graves, and reflecting on past civilisations are not signs of depression but of deep spiritual awareness that leads to clarity and purpose.
Warning others of consequences, calling for action, and speaking truth — even when it is uncomfortable — is one of the most honourable things a person can do with their life.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually empty or disconnected, try spending time in quiet reflection in nature, away from screens and noise, as Quss did in the desert — solitude, when used intentionally, can restore clarity.
If you are feeling overwhelmed by the complexity of life, return to simple, foundational truths: there is a God, life is temporary, and your deeds matter — let these anchors steady you.

Channel any feelings of sadness about the passage of time or the loss of loved ones into productive reflection on how you want to live the remainder of your own life, so that loss becomes motivation rather than paralysis.

title: The Story of Quss ibn Sa'ida and His Verses on Death and Judgement
summary: The Messenger of God recounts witnessing the wise Arab preacher Quss ibn Sa'ida at the 'Ukaz festival, delivering profound speeches and poetry about death, resurrection, and the oneness of God, foretelling the coming of a prophet.
story:
be beaten, when the earth will shine, and the preacher exhort and the despairing withdraw and the observer gazes out; woe to him who turns away from the truth most evident, and the shining light, and the great Judgement Day, on the day of separating, and the scales of justice, when He who decrees decides, and the apostle gives testimony, and the allies are far off, and the faults are apparent, and so one group goes to paradise, and one group to hell-fire.

'It is he who spoke the verses: "The heart passionately recalled and reflected: nights within which there are days. And bucketsful pouring from storm clouds that burst forth with water, there being fire in their insides, Their light blinded the eye and violent thunder was cast in east and west; There are lofty castles possessed of wealth while others are empty and uninhabited. There are unshakable mountains towering, and seas whose waters abound, There are stars which flash in the dark of night which we see every day are revolved, And then there is a sun urged on by moon of night, each pursuing the other and in motion. There are the young, the hoary, and the old, all of them one day to meet above. And there is plenty beyond the sunrise of those whose mind is not perplexed. All these I mentioned are signs of God for the souls of good guidance and wisdom."

And the Messenger of God (SAAS) said: 'Whatever else I may forget, I will never forget him (Quss) at the 'Ukaz festival, standing up on a red camel and addressing the people: 'Gather around and listen, and having heard, be aware, benefit! And speak, and if you speak, tell the truth! He who lives dies, he who dies is gone, and all that is coming will come, rain and plants, the living and the dead, dark nights and skies with zodiac signs, and stars that shine, and seas that rage, and light and shade, and nights and days, and innocence and sins. There is knowledge in the heavens and lessons on earth such as to amaze even the perspicacious. There is a ground laid out, a roof raised up, stars that set, seas that do not empty, destinies drawing near, a treacherous fate, as sharp as a surgeon's knife and as accurate as a precise scale. Quss swears an oath, neither insincere nor sinning thereby, that if there be any pleasure in this matter, then there will surely be discontent.' Then he said, 'O people, God has a religion which is more pleasing to him than this religion of yours you engage in. And this is its time, its epoch.' Then he said, 'How is it I see people go but not return? Were they content to stay and did so? Or were they left and fell asleep?'

The Messenger of God (SAAS) then turned to some of his Companions and said, 'Which one of you can recite his verses to us?' Abu Bakr, the truthful, replied, 'By my father and my mother, I witnessed him on that day when he spoke the verses: "In those who went first in ages past are clear proofs for us. Since I saw ways leading to death which had no beginnings. And I saw my people moving towards them, young and old alike. The past will never come back to me, nor will any of those who stay remain behind; I have become sure that I will inevitably pass on to where the people have gone."'
lessons: Reflection on death and the transient nature of life is a sign of wisdom and spiritual awareness, as Quss ibn Sa'ida demonstrated through his profound words long before Islam.
The natural world — the stars, the seas, the mountains — are signs of God meant to guide the sincere soul toward truth, and observing them with a reflective heart is itself an act of worship.

Truth resonates across time; those who sincerely seek God's guidance, even before formal revelation reaches them, are honoured by God and remembered with mercy, as the Prophet said of Quss: 'God have mercy on Quss!'
practical_advice: When feeling lost or overwhelmed by life's uncertainty, pause and reflect on the natural world around you — the rising sun, the turning of seasons — as reminders that there is an order and a Creator behind all things.
Memorise or recite poetry and prose that reminds you of your mortality; contemplating death is not morbid but clarifying, helping you prioritise what truly matters.
When you encounter wisdom, even from unexpected sources, take note of it and share it with others, as the Prophet shared Quss's words with his Companions to draw lessons from them.

title: Abu Bakr Recites the Verses of Quss ibn Sa'ida from Memory
summary: When the Prophet asked his Companions if any of them could recite the verses of Quss ibn Sa'ida, Abu Bakr stepped forward and recited what he had personally witnessed and memorised from Quss's famous sermon.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then turned to some of his Companions and said, 'Which one of you can recite his verses to us?' Abu Bakr, the truthful, replied, 'By my father and my mother, I witnessed him on that day when he spoke the verses: "In those who went first in ages past are clear proofs for us. Since I saw ways leading to death which had no beginnings. And I saw my people moving towards them, young and old alike. The past will never come back to me, nor will any of those who stay remain behind; I have become sure that I will inevitably pass on to where the people have gone."'
lessons: Abu Bakr's readiness to respond to the Prophet's call demonstrates the virtue of being attentive, prepared, and eager to contribute knowledge to the community.
Witnessing and retaining wisdom — even wisdom from a pre-Islamic sage — and being willing to share it is a mark of intellectual and spiritual generosity.
The verses Abu Bakr recited remind us that acknowledging the inevitability of death is not despair but clarity; it is the foundation of purposeful living.
practical_advice: Cultivate the habit of memorising meaningful words, verses, or reflections that move your heart — they will serve you and others in moments when wisdom is needed.
When you feel stuck or directionless, remember that those who came before you also faced mortality and uncertainty; their example can be a source of grounding and perspective.
Practice being present and attentive in gatherings of knowledge so that, like Abu Bakr, you are ready to contribute meaningfully when called upon.

title: The Man of Abd Qays Recounts Witnessing Quss ibn Sa'ida in the Desert
summary: A tall, venerable elder from the tribe of Abd Qays narrates to the Prophet how in his youth he followed his lost camels into the desert and stumbled upon Quss ibn Sa'ida, who was living alone between two graves, disciplining lions at a watering hole and composing poetry about death and companionship.
story:
Then a tall, broad-shouldered, venerable old sheikh of 'Abd Qays approached the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'By my father and my mother, I saw something remarkable from Quss.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'What was it you saw, O brother from 'Abd Qays?' He explained: 'In my youth some camels I owned wandered off, so I hurried and followed their trail into rocky desert land where there were thorny twigs, wastelands of dead tree roots and palm trunks, waterlily ponds, and ostrich tracks and nests. While I passed along the trails in these high and low wastes, I came to a hilly area whose upper slopes were thick with thorn trees, their branches hanging low, their first fruits like seeds of pepper and heads of chamomile. Suddenly there was a bubbling spring, a green meadow, and a lofty tree. And there too was Quss b. Sa'ida at the base of that tree, standing with a stick in his hand. I approached him and said, "Good morning to you!" "Good morning to you likewise" he replied. There were many lions going down to drink at the water-hole. Whenever one tried to drink from the hole before another he would strike it with his stick and say: "Be patient! Wait till the one before you drinks!" I was absolutely amazed at that. He looked across at me and said, "Don't be afraid." Then I saw two graves with a mosque between them. I asked him, "What are these graves?" He replied, "They're the graves of two brothers who used to pray to God, Almighty and Glorious is He, at this spot. I remain here between their two graves, praying to God until I join them." I asked him, "But wouldn't it be better for you to join your people and enjoy their well-being and explain to them what it is they do that is evil?" "May your death make your mother grieve! Don't you know how Ishmael's sons abandoned the faith of their father and followed rival gods and venerated idols?"

Then he approached the graves and spoke the following verses: "O my two friends, arise; long have you lain. I urge you not to prolong your slumber; I see sleep between your skin and your bones, as though he who gives a drug to drink has given it to you. Is it from your long sleep that you do not respond to him who calls you, as though he who gives a drug to drink has given it to you? Do you not know that I am in Najran alone, with no friend there but for you two? Living at your graves, not leaving night after night until your response is heard. I weep for you throughout my life, and what had the heartbroken gained that we wept for you both? If a soul were to be a ransom for the soul of another, I would strive to have my soul become ransom for you both. As if death were the closest goal for my soul when death had come to you in your two graves."

Al-Hasan went on: "So the Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'God have mercy on Quss! He will be resurrected as a whole nation by himself on Judgement Day.'"
lessons: Quss ibn Sa'ida's solitary life between the graves of his companions illustrates how grief, when channelled into devotion and prayer, can become a path of spiritual elevation rather than destruction.
His refusal to return to a people who had abandoned the faith of their forefather Ishmael shows the courage required to stand apart from cultural norms when they conflict with sincere belief in God.

The Prophet's prayer for Quss — that he be resurrected as a whole nation — affirms that God does not allow sincere devotion and honest seeking to go unrecognised, even in the most isolated circumstances.
practical_advice: When you experience profound loneliness or loss, consider turning that energy into increased devotion and remembrance of God, as Quss turned his grief for his companions into a life of prayer.
If you feel that you are one of the few people around you who holds certain values or beliefs, take comfort in the example of Quss — sincerity before God matters more than the approval of the crowd.

When you are anxious and encounter something unexpected or frightening, recall Quss's calm reassurance — 'Don't be afraid' — and trust that God places you in situations that carry lessons worth receiving.

title: The Man Lost in the Dark Desert Hears a Voice Announcing the Coming of Muhammad
summary: A man searching alone at night for his lost camels in a frightening desert hears a disembodied voice in the darkness reciting verses that announce the prophethood of Muhammad, describing him by his noble attributes.
story:
The man recited, "So I stayed the night in a valley where I was scared for my life, having only my sword to rely on, watching the stars, peering into the blackness, until the night had gone its term and morning was almost breathing; I then heard someone speak aloud the verses: 'O you lying down in the pitch black of night, God has sent a prophet in the sacred sanctuary, From Hashim, a people of trust and honour, illuminating all most gloomy, dark and impenetrable.'"

The man continued, "I turned but saw no one and heard nothing else. So I then spoke the following: 'O you who speaks in darkest night, welcome indeed to you, a phantom who visited us! Explain, may God give you guidance, in tone of words, what it is you urge that can be gained?'

I then heard a clearing of the throat and a voice saying, 'The light has appeared; and falsehood has been defeated. For God has sent Muhammad with righteousness, the master of the red mount, and the crown and the helmet, he of the shining visage, and the full eyebrows, and the eyes of marked contrast between the white of the cornea and the black of the iris, the man responsible for the words of the "shahada", or testimony of the faith: "la ilaha illa Allah", there is no God but God. That person is Muhammad, the one sent to the black and the white, to the people of the village and the people of the deserts.' Then he recited the words: 'Praise be to God who did not create nature in vain; He did not once leave us forsaken after Jesus, nor was He uncaring. He sent among us Muhammad, the best Prophet ever dispatched, whenever parties of men hurried (to seek audience with) him.'"
lessons: Even in the deepest darkness and fear, God's guidance can reach a sincere heart in the most unexpected ways, reminding us that the divine never truly abandons the seeker.

The announcement of the Prophet Muhammad's coming — described through his physical and moral attributes — affirms that the truth is specific, detailed, and recognisable to those who open their hearts to it.
Darkness, whether literal or metaphorical, is a precursor to light; the man's terrifying night in the desert ended with a herald of the greatest dawn in human history.
practical_advice: In moments of fear and isolation, try to remain alert and open rather than closing down — some of the most significant realisations come when we are stripped of our usual comforts and distractions.
When you feel that falsehood surrounds you and truth seems absent, hold on to the certainty that divine support is present even when unseen, as this man heard truth proclaimed in an empty desert.

Use the darkness of difficult seasons in your life as a time for watchfulness and reflection rather than despair — morning, as the text says, was already breathing even while the man peered into the blackness.

title: The Prophet Declares That Quss ibn Sa'ida Believed in Judgement Day
summary: After multiple accounts of Quss ibn Sa'ida's wisdom and poetry about resurrection are gathered, the Prophet Muhammad declares by oath that Quss truly believed in Judgement Day, honouring his sincere faith despite his pre-Islamic context.
story:
So the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "By Him who sent me with the truth, Quss did believe in Judgement Day!"

The origins of this tradition are well known; these other lines of transmission despite their weakness, are helpful in affirming the origin of the anecdote. Abu Muhammad b. Darstawayh commented upon the strangeness of what occurred in this hadith; most of it is obvious, if God Almighty wishes it so.

The same source attributes the following verses to Quss b. Sa'ida: "O announcer of death and of the grave, in the tomb there are men in rags which are remnants of their clothing. Leave them, for one day they will be called; and they, if they do waken from their sleep, will stay awake. Until they revert to some state other than theirs, to a new creation similar to what they were before. Some will be naked, some in their clothes, some of which will be new, some quickly frayed and tattered."
lessons: The Prophet's recognition of Quss's sincere belief teaches us that God's mercy is not confined to those who lived after revelation arrived; sincere hearts that followed their fitrah (natural disposition) toward God are honoured.
Belief in accountability — in a Day of Judgement — is the anchor of ethical living; Quss's entire wisdom rested on this foundation, demonstrating its universal human resonance.
The resurrection described in Quss's verses — where the dead are called and awaken to a new creation — is a reminder that this life is not the final word, and that every soul's account is being kept.
practical_advice: When you are grieving or feel hopeless about the state of the world, anchor yourself in the belief that there is ultimate justice — a Day when all accounts will be settled and all wrongs addressed.
Reflect regularly on the afterlife not as a source of fear but as a source of motivation: the awareness that this life is temporary can free you from anxiety about worldly losses.

Seek out wisdom wherever it appears — in poetry, in the words of those who came before, in nature — because sincere seekers of truth, like Quss, leave behind insights that continue to guide long after they are gone.

title: Quss b. Sa'ida's Speech at the 'Ukaz Festival
summary: A delegation from Iyad recalls to the Prophet the famous speech of Quss b. Sa'ida at the 'Ukaz festival, in which Quss reflected on mortality, the passing of generations, and the existence of a religion more pleasing to God.
story: A delegation from Iyad went to the Prophet (SAAS) and he asked: 'What did Quss b. Sa'ida do?' They replied: 'He died.' He commented, 'Well, I heard some words from him I no longer recall.' Some of those present told him they remembered them, so he asked them to tell them. One of them responded, 'He stood up at the 'Ukaz festival and said, "O people, listen, hear, and take note. All those who live die. And all those who die pass away. And all that is to come will come. Dark nights. Skies with zodiac signs. Stars that shine. Seas that rage. Mountains tower above. Rivers flow. In heaven there is notice and on earth there is warning. I see people die and not return. Were they content to stay and did so? Or were they left and fell asleep? Quss swears an oath by God, not sinning thereby, that God has a religion which is more pleasing to Him than that you engage in." Then he recited the verses: "In those who went first in ages past are clear proofs for us. Since I saw ways leading to death for people which had no beginnings, And I saw my people moving towards them, young and old alike. I have become sure that I will inevitably pass on to where the people have gone."'
lessons: Reflection on mortality is a timeless spiritual practice that can awaken the soul to deeper truths.
Even before formal revelation, sincere hearts were guided toward recognizing a higher truth beyond idol worship.
The memory of wisdom passed down through generations carries immense spiritual value.
practical_advice: When feeling lost or anxious about life's purpose, pause to reflect on the inevitability of death as a motivator to seek what truly matters.
Seek wisdom in the words of those who came before you — history and biography carry lessons that can ground you in difficult times.
If you sense that the path others are on is wrong, have the courage like Quss to speak truth even in a crowd.

title: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl Rejects Idolatry and Seeks the True Religion
summary: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl, a noble Qurayshi, abandoned idol worship and the religions of his people before Islam, dedicating himself solely to the worship of the one God and the religion of Abraham, earning persecution from his own kinsman al-Khattab.
story: Zayd b. 'Amr gave up the worship of idols and abandoned their religion; he would only eat what had been slaughtered in the name of God alone. Hisham b. 'Urwa related to me, from his father, that Asma', daughter of Abu Bakr, said: 'I saw Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl leaning his back against the ka'ba saying, "O tribe of Quraysh, by Him in whose hand is Zayd's soul, not one of you apart from myself follows the religion of Abraham." Moreover, he would say, "O God, if only I knew the way most favoured by you, I would worship you by it, but I do not know." Also he would bow in prayer while on his mount.' Abu Usama too related this, from Hisham, and added, 'He would pray towards the ka'ba and say, "My God is the God of Abraham, and my religion is that of Abraham." He would give life back to girls about to be killed at birth, telling a man wishing to kill his daughter, "Don't kill her. Give her to me to look after. When she grows up, you can take her back or if you wish give her up."' There was a group of Quraysh who consisted of Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl, Waraqa b. Nawfal b. Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzza, 'Uthman b. al-Huwayrith b. Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzza, and 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh b. Ri'ab b. Ya'mur b. Sabra b. Murra b. Kabir b. Ghanm b. Dudan b. Asad b. Khuzayma. These men were present among Quraysh when they were before an idol of theirs to which they would make sacrifice at one of their feast days. As they all gathered, this group of men met apart and agreed that they would make a secret pact of friendship with one another. One of them said, 'By God, you know that your people are doing nothing; they have strayed from the religion of Abraham and gone against it. Why worship some idol incapable of doing harm or good? Seek out for yourselves!' So they left, seeking and searching throughout the land to find the hanifiyya, the religion of Abraham. None of this group was a man more honourable in deed nor more dedicated than Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl. He gave up the idols and abandoned all the religions, of the Jews, the Christians, and all the sects except the hanifyya, the religion of Abraham. He spoke of the unity of God alone and cast aside the gods beneath Him. He ate none of the meat his people slaughtered, and warned them of the judgement they would suffer from the practices they followed. Al-Khattab so persecuted him that eventually Zayd left him by going to the heights of Mecca. So al-Khattab assigned a group of young Quraysh thugs to watch him and told them not to let him enter Mecca. He never did so except without their knowledge, and when they found out they harmed him and evicted him violently, hating that he should spoil their religion for them or get others to join him in his own practice.
lessons: Standing alone for the truth, even when persecuted by one's own community, is a mark of profound spiritual courage.
Sincerity in seeking God — even without complete knowledge — is itself a form of worship that God honors.
Protecting the vulnerable, as Zayd did with infant girls, is a natural expression of a conscience aligned with divine guidance.
practical_advice: When you feel rejected by those around you for your values, remember that standing firm in truth is more important than social acceptance.
If you are uncertain about the right path, follow the example of Zayd and call out to God honestly: 'I do not know, but I seek You.' Sincerity in uncertainty is itself a spiritual act.
Use your position and resources to protect those who cannot protect themselves, as this is a sign of a heart that has not been corrupted by its environment.

title: Zayd b. 'Amr's Journey Through Syria and His Death in Lakhm Territory
summary: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl traveled across Syria and Mesopotamia seeking the true religion of Abraham, was told by a monk that a prophet was near, and was killed on his return journey before he could witness the prophethood of Muhammad.
story: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl had determined to leave Mecca and travel across the land seeking the hanafiyya, the religion of Abraham. His wife Safiyya, daughter of al-Hadrami, whenever she saw him preparing to make the departure he wished, would warn al-Khattab b. Nufayl. Zayd did leave for Syria, enquiring and seeking the religion of Abraham among the people of the first holy book. They say he went on doing this until he covered al-Mawsil and all Mesopotamia, finally reaching Syria and travelling throughout it. Eventually he came upon a monk in a church in the region of al-Balqa' who had, as they claim, much learning in Christianity. Zayd asked him about the hanifiyya, the religion of Abraham, and the monk replied, 'You are asking about a religion you'll not find anyone today to instruct you in. Those who had knowledge and familiarity with it have faded away and gone. But the emergence of a prophet draws near, his time having come.' Now Zayd had examined Judaism and Christianity, but had not been content with anything in them, and so he left quickly for Mecca after what the monk had told him. But when he was in Lakhm territory he was attacked and killed. Waraqa spoke the following verses eulogizing him: 'You followed the proper course and did right, O son of 'Amr; and indeed you have avoided the ovens of hell, By your worshipping a Lord unlike any other and by your abandoning the idols of tyrants as they are, Man may indeed be reached by God's mercy, even though he be seventy valleys beneath the earth.'
lessons: A sincere seeker of truth who dies before reaching the full light may still be saved by God's mercy, as the Prophet confirmed that Zayd would be 'a nation by himself on Judgement Day.'
Divine providence arranges that truth-seekers are guided even through imperfect means — the monk's hint about a coming prophet pointed Zayd toward hope.
God's mercy transcends the formal boundaries of religion and reaches those whose hearts were genuinely oriented toward Him.
practical_advice: When you feel you have searched everywhere and still haven't found peace or clarity, do not despair — sometimes the answer is closer than you think, and God honors the journey itself.
If you feel you have missed out on something important in life, trust that God judges by intention and sincere effort, not only by outcomes.
When mourning someone who sought goodness but did not live to see its fulfillment, take comfort in the certainty that God's mercy is wider than our understanding.

title: Zayd b. 'Amr Approaches a Jew and a Christian, Then Commits to the Religion of Abraham
summary: Zayd b. 'Amr approaches a Jewish scholar and then a Christian seeking to join their religion, but both rebuff him; a Christian ultimately directs him to the religion of Abraham, and Zayd swears to God to follow it, upon which the Prophet later declared he would be a nation unto himself on Judgement Day.
story: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl worshipped God before the coming of Islam. He once came to a Jew and told him he wanted him to induct him into his religion. The Jew replied, 'I will not accept you in my religion until you experience your share of God's anger.' Zayd replied, 'It is from the anger of God that I am fleeing.' Zayd went on further till he came to a Christian, telling him he would like to engage with him in his religion.
