The Christian replied, 'I'll not accept you in my religion until you experience your share of false guidance.' Zayd replied, 'It is from false guidance that I flee.' The Christian then told him: 'I will show you the way to a religion in which, if you follow it, you will be rightly guided.' 'What religion is that?' asked Zayd. 'The religion of Abraham,' he replied. So Zayd said, 'O God, I swear to you that I will follow the religion of Abraham; in it I shall live and die.' When Zayd's story was referred to the Prophet (SAAS) he stated, 'He will be as a nation all by himself on Judgement Day.'
lessons: True spiritual seeking requires persistence — Zayd was rejected by two communities yet did not give up his search for truth.
Making a solemn commitment to God, even in uncertainty, is a powerful act of faith that does not go unrecognized.
The Prophet's confirmation that Zayd would be a nation unto himself shows that God's reward is proportional to one's sincere dedication to truth.
practical_advice: When you feel rejected or unwelcome — by institutions, communities, or people — remember that rejection is sometimes God's way of redirecting you to something better.
If you are struggling to find your spiritual footing, make a direct, personal commitment to God in your own words, as Zayd did. Sincerity matters more than formality.
Do not measure your worth by how others receive you. God's recognition of Zayd as 'a nation by himself' shows that one sincere soul outweighs the judgment of crowds.

title: Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl's Search for the True Religion
summary: Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl, dissatisfied with idolatry, Judaism, and Christianity, travels through Syria seeking the true religion of Abraham and is told by a monk that a prophet will soon emerge from his own land in Mecca.
story: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl said, "I examined Judaism and Christianity and disliked them both. I travelled in Syria and the neighbouring lands and eventually came to a monk in a cell. I told him of my alienation from my own people and of my dislike for the worship of idols, Judaism, and Christianity. He told me, 'I see you desire the religion of Abraham, 0 brother from the people of Mecca. You are searching for a religion that today no one adheres to, the religion of your father Abraham. He was a hanif; neither a Jew nor a Christian. He prayed and prostrated himself in the direction of that house which is in your land. So you must go to your own country. God will send from your people in your land one who will bring the religion of Abraham, the hanifiyya; he is the most noble of creation in God's sight.'"
lessons: Sincere spiritual seeking, even in confusion and alienation, can lead one toward truth — the journey itself is an act of worship.
God prepares guidance for those who turn away from falsehood with an honest heart, even before they find the full truth.
One person's steadfast refusal to follow the crowd in worshipping falsehood is a powerful act of faith that God does not leave unrewarded.
practical_advice: If you feel spiritually lost or alienated from the values around you, treat that discomfort as a sign of a healthy conscience — let it motivate reflection rather than despair.
Seek knowledge from multiple sources and trust your instincts when something feels spiritually wrong, even if others around you accept it.
When you feel alone in your values, remember that sincerity of heart matters more than social approval — God sees the one who searches honestly.

title: Zayd ibn Amr's Prayer and Submission at the Kaaba
summary: Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl stands at the Kaaba declaring his submission and worship to God alone, performing prayer and pilgrimage in the tradition of Abraham before Islam was revealed.
story: Zayd used to say when he entered the kacba, "Here I am before you, really and truly, in worship, verity and submission. I turn in submission quoting Abraham, facing the kacba." He would stand as he would say, "0 my God, I am submissive to you, fearful, and humble. Whatever painful task you impose, I will perform it. It is faith I desire, not vanity. He who journeys in the heat is not as one who naps in the afternoon." ... He was observing the sun; when it set he approached the kacba and performed the prayer of two prostrations. Then he said, "This is the qibla, the prayer site, to which Abraham and Ishmael directed themselves. I do not worship a stone, nor pray to it, nor eat what was sacrificed to it, nor will I seek the judgement of divining arrows. Yet I will pray towards this house till I die." He would also perform the pilgrimage and station himself on Mt. 'Arafat. He would also make the prayer call saying, "Labbayk! At Your service; there is no partner to You, nor any peer." Then he would leave 'Arafat, walking and praying, saying, "Labbayk! At Your service, in worship and submission."
lessons: True worship requires inner sincerity — Zayd sought faith, not social recognition or ritual habit, and this sincerity distinguished his devotion.
One can maintain spiritual discipline and connection to God even without a complete community or established religious framework around them.
The acts of submission, humility, and prayer are timeless expressions of the human soul's natural orientation toward its Creator.
practical_advice: Develop a personal practice of remembrance and prayer that is driven by genuine desire to connect with God, not merely habit or social expectation.
When you feel spiritually isolated, anchor yourself in the fundamentals — gratitude, humility, and honest communication with God in your own words.
Reflect on what you are truly seeking from worship: is it faith and nearness to God, or something else? Let sincerity guide your practice.

title: Zayd ibn Amr and Waraqa ibn Nawfal Seek Religion Together
summary: Zayd ibn Amr and Waraqa ibn Nawfal travel together seeking the true religion; a monk tells Zayd to return home as the prophet will soon appear, while Waraqa adopts Christianity but Zayd is not accepted into it.
story: Zayd b. 'Amr and Waraqa b. Nawfal went off seeking religion and finally met a monk at al-Mawsil. The monk asked Zayd, 'Where have you travelled from, 0 camel master?' 'From Abraham's building,' he replied. 'What are you seeking?' then asked the monk. 'I am seeking the religion,' replied Zayd. The monk responded, 'Go back; it is soon to appear in your land.' Zayd went on: 'Now Waraqa did adopt Christianity; but in my case, although I intended to become Christian he (the monk) would not accept me.' So Zayd returned home and spoke the words: 'Here I am before You, in truth, in worship and in submission. It is faith I desire, not vanity. Is he who journeys in the heat as one who naps in the afternoon? I believe in what Abraham believed in.' He also said, '0 my God, I am submissive to You, fearful, and humble. Whatever painful task You impose, I will perform it.' Then he would bow down and prostrate himself in prayer.
lessons: Sometimes a door that closes — like Zayd being refused entry into Christianity — is God's way of preserving you for something greater.
Perseverance in worship even without a clear community or label is a sign of profound spiritual maturity and trust in God.
Two people can begin the same journey and reach very different destinations; one's individual sincerity ultimately determines where they arrive spiritually.
practical_advice: When a path you hoped for does not open to you, resist the urge to force it — consider that you may be being guided toward something more fitting.
Continue your spiritual practices even in periods of uncertainty and incompleteness; consistency in worship sustains the soul during searching times.
Do not measure your spiritual progress by comparison with others who may choose different paths; focus on your own sincerity and relationship with God.

title: Zayd ibn Amr Refuses Food Sacrificed to Idols
summary: Before revelation, the Prophet Muhammad and Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl meet in the lowlands of Baldah, and Zayd refuses to eat food slaughtered on sacrificial stones dedicated to idols, critiquing Quraysh's practices.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) met Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl in the lowlands of Baldah, before revelation descended upon the Prophet. A meal was set out for the Prophet (SAAS) but he, Zayd, refused to eat of it. Then Zayd said, "I'm not going to eat from what you slaughter on your sacrificial stones; and I will only eat what has had the name of God spoken over it." Zayd b. 'Amr would criticize Quraysh for the animal sacrifices they made, saying, "Sheep are created by God; He sends rain down from the skies and makes things grow for them from the earth. But then you slaughter them in the name of gods other than Him, denying all of that and venerating them!"
lessons: Moral courage means maintaining principles even in social settings where conformity is expected and refusal may seem awkward or offensive.
Those who hold firm to truth even before it becomes established around them are pioneers whose example endures.
Acknowledging God's gifts — rain, growth, sustenance — while directing gratitude to Him alone is the essence of monotheistic consciousness.
practical_advice: Identify practices in your daily life that may be inconsistent with your values and have the courage to quietly decline participation, even when it is socially uncomfortable.
Use the gifts and blessings you observe around you — nature, sustenance, health — as regular reminders to direct your gratitude to God rather than to secondary causes.
When others around you accept something harmful or wrong without question, do not feel obligated to go along — principled dissent, done respectfully, is a form of integrity.

title: Zayd ibn Amr Consults Jewish and Christian Scholars and Declares Faith in Abraham's Religion
summary: Zayd ibn Amr approaches Jewish and Christian scholars seeking the religion of Abraham; both warn him away from their faiths and describe the Abrahamic way, leading Zayd to raise his hands and declare publicly his commitment to Abraham's religion.
story: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl left for Syria enquiring after the religion he would follow and met a learned Jew whom he asked about his religion. Zayd said, "I'm inclined towards adopting your religion, so tell me of it." The man replied, "You will not be in our religion until you receive your share of God's anger." Zayd replied, "But it's only God's anger from which I flee; I bear none of God's anger, nor could I. Can you then direct me to something else?" He replied, "I don't know of anything - except for you to be a hanif." "What", asked Zayd, "is a hanif?" "The religion of Abraham, peace be upon him; he was neither a Jew nor a Christian and worshipped none but God." So Zayd left and met a Christian scholar to whom he spoke as before. But the Christian replied, "You will not be of our religion until you take your share of the curses of God." Zayd replied, "But it's only from the curses of God that I flee. I do not at all ever bear the curses of God or His anger, nor could I. So would you direct me to something else?" "I don't know of anything, except for you to be a hanif," the Christian answered. "What is a hanif?" Zayd asked. "It is the religion of Abraham," the Christian answered; "he was neither a Jew nor a Christian and worshipped none but God." Having considered their statement about Abraham, Zayd went outside and when he was in open country, he raised his hands and said, "0 God, I testify that I follow the religion of Abraham."
lessons: Truth can sometimes be confirmed through unexpected sources — even those who do not fully hold it can point toward it, as the Jewish and Christian scholars inadvertently guided Zayd.
The sincere heart that flees from sin and divine displeasure, rather than running toward worldly benefit, is on the right track spiritually.
Public declaration of one's commitment to God, even alone in open country, is a meaningful act of faith that transforms inner conviction into lived reality.
practical_advice: When searching for truth, do not be discouraged if the sources you consult are imperfect — pieces of truth can come from many directions, and your heart can help you assemble them.
Let your spiritual choices be driven by what you wish to move toward — closeness to God — rather than purely by fear, but recognize that avoiding what displeases God is itself a noble motivation.
Find moments in your day or week to make a conscious, personal declaration of your values and faith — spoken or written affirmations of your commitments can strengthen resolve and clarity.

title: Zayd ibn Amr Stands at the Kaaba Warning Against Adultery and Saving Infant Girls
summary: Zayd ibn Amr publicly warns the Quraysh against adultery at the Kaaba and intervenes to save infant girls from being buried alive, taking them in and caring for them himself.
story: Asma' said: "I saw Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl standing leaning his back against the kacba, saying, '0 tribe of Quraysh, by God, none of you is in Abraham's religion except me.' And he used to give life back to a girl about to be killed at birth, saying to the man who wished to kill his daughter, 'Don't kill her; I will take care of providing for her.' Then he would take her away, and when she grew up he would tell her father, 'If you like, I'll either return her to you or provide for her.'" ... 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi al-Zinad said that on a line of authority from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, Asma' said: "I heard Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl say, as he was resting his back against the kacba: '0 tribe of Quraysh, beware of adultery; it bequeaths poverty.'"
lessons: A single person committed to justice and mercy can make a tangible, life-saving difference even within a society deeply entrenched in wrong.
Speaking truth to one's own community, from a place of genuine concern rather than superiority, is one of the most courageous acts of moral leadership.
Protecting the vulnerable — especially those with no voice of their own — is a fundamental expression of the values of Abraham's religion and all sincere faith.
practical_advice: Do not wait for society to change before you act justly — identify one concrete way you can protect or advocate for someone vulnerable in your own circle and take action.
If you witness a harmful norm being practiced around you, consider whether you have an opportunity to speak against it respectfully, as Zayd did at the Kaaba, without waiting for others to lead.
Reflect on how your personal moral conduct — in private matters like honesty and sexual ethics — ripples outward into your community's wellbeing, as Zayd taught that adultery bequeaths poverty.

title: Zayd ibn Amr Predicts the Coming of the Prophet and Sends His Greetings
summary: Zayd ibn Amr describes the coming prophet in detail to 'Amir ibn Rabi'a, expresses his faith in him, and asks that his greetings be passed on; after 'Amir becomes Muslim, the Prophet returns Zayd's greeting and prays for mercy on him.
story: 'Ali b. 'Isa al-Hakami related to me, from his father, from 'Amir b. Rabi'a as follows, 'I heard Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl say, "I await the coming of a prophet from the progeny of Ishmael, then from the tribe of 'Abd al-Muttalib. I do not see myself living to his time. But I have faith in him, assert his truthfulness, and testify that he is a prophet. If you live on a long time and see him, then tell him of my greetings to him. I will inform you of his qualities so that he will not be unknown to you." I replied, "Do so then." He continued, "He will be a man neither tall nor short with neither much nor little hair. Redness is never absent from his eye. He bears the mark of prophethood between his shoulders. His name is to be Ahmad and this country will be the place of his birth and his mission. Then his people will expel him from it and will disapprove of his message until he will emigrate to Yathrib; and so his authority will appear. Take care that you are not deceived about him, for I have travelled all over seeking the religion of Abraham. And those of the Jews, Christians, and Magians to whom I made enquiry answered, 'That religion is behind you.' And they would describe him as I have described him to you and say, 'There is no prophet but him.'" 'Amir b. Rabi'a stated, 'And when I became a Muslim I informed the Messenger of God (SAAS) of the words of Zayd b. 'Amr and did pass on his greetings to him. He returned such greeting to him and prayed for mercy for him, saying, "I have seen him in paradise in long, flowing robes."'
lessons: Faith in what one cannot yet see, expressed through study, testimony, and sincere commitment, is one of the highest forms of spiritual devotion.
God honors those who sincerely seek truth even if they do not live to witness its full arrival — Zayd's rank in paradise testifies to this divine justice.
The Prophet's mercy extended even to those before his time who sought truth sincerely, reminding us that God's justice encompasses all of human history.
practical_advice: If you are going through a period where you cannot yet see the fruit of your spiritual efforts or good deeds, take comfort from Zayd's story — sincere effort is recognized by God regardless of timing.
Pass on good words, blessings, and greetings between people whenever you can; the simple act of conveying Zayd's salaam was honored by the Prophet himself.
Study the qualities and character of the Prophet in detail so that your love and recognition of him is grounded in knowledge, as Zayd's anticipation was grounded in careful description.

title: Said ibn Zayd Asks the Prophet to Seek Forgiveness for His Father
summary: Said ibn Zayd, one of the ten promised paradise, asks the Prophet to pray for forgiveness for his father Zayd ibn Amr, and the Prophet responds that Zayd will be raised on Judgement Day as a nation by himself.
story: His son, by whom I mean Sa'id b. Zayd, one of the 'ashara, the ten, God bless him, said, '0 Messenger of God, my father was as you have seen and heard; seek forgiveness for him.' He replied, 'Yes; he will be raised up on Judgement Day as a nation, by himself.'
lessons: Children can honor their parents most deeply by advocating for them spiritually and seeking mercy on their behalf from God and those close to God.
God's mercy is vast enough to honor an individual whose sincere faith preceded the formal revelation — justice and mercy are the foundations of divine judgment.
The Prophet's response — measured, compassionate, and hopeful — models how to acknowledge the goodness in people who lived and died before or outside the full reach of revelation.
practical_advice: Pray regularly for your parents, living or deceased, and advocate for them in whatever ways are open to you — this is one of the most enduring forms of love and gratitude.
Do not assume that those who died without the full light of revelation are necessarily lost — trust in God's perfect knowledge and justice regarding every sincere soul.
When someone you love has passed, channel your grief into prayers, good deeds in their memory, and trust in God's mercy rather than into despair.

title: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl: The Monotheist Before Islam
summary: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl lived and died before the coming of Islam, yet worshipped God alone following the religion of Abraham. The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) said he would be raised on Judgement Day as a nation all by himself between him and Jesus son of Mary.
story: Here in his account Ibn 'Asakii gave several very strange anecdotes, some of which are extremely implausible. Then he related from a variety of limes back to the Messenger of God (SAAS) as having said: "He will he raised up on Judgement Day as a nation all by himself." These accounts include the one by M u w d b. Wthmsn b. Ahii Shayba in which he indicated that Yiisuf b. Ya'qiib al-Safb and Y&y% b. Sacid al-Umawi related to him, from Mujdid, from al-Sha'bi, from Jabir, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was asked about Zayd h. 'Amr b. Nufayl having approached the qrbla before the coming of Islam and saying, "My God is the God of Abraham, and my religion that of Abraham" and then making the prayer prostrations. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "That man will be gathered (on Judgement Day) as a nation all by himself between me and Jesus son of Mary." The chain of authorities for this is very good. Al-Waqidi stated that Miisa b. Shayha related to him, from Khzrija b. 'Abd Allah b. Ka'b. h. M a l i , who said, "I heard Sa'id h. al-Musayyab make reference to Zayd h.'Amr b. Nufayl with the words, 'He died while Quraysh were building the ka'ba, five years before the divine inspiration descended upon the Messenger of God (SAAS). And death came to him while he spoke the words, "I follow the religion of Abraham."' His son Sacid h. Zayd became a Muslim and a follower of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Once Wmar b. al-Khac@b and Sa'id b. Zayd approached the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him about Zayd b.'Amr b. Nufayl and he responded, 'May God give him forgiveness and grant him mercy. He died following the religion of Abraham.' And after that day Muslims never failed to pray for forgiveness and mercy for him whenever they mentioned his name. And then Sa'id h. al-Musayyab himself spoke the words, 'May God grant him mercy and forgiveness.' "
lessons: Sincerity of heart and genuine search for truth can lead a person to God even without formal revelation, showing that the human soul naturally inclines toward the divine.
One can be spiritually distinguished and beloved to God even when living in a society that has turned away from truth — isolation from falsehood is itself an act of worship.
The Prophet's mercy and compassion extended even to those who passed before Islam; he prayed for Zayd and honored his spiritual journey, teaching us to recognize goodness wherever it exists.
practical_advice: If you feel spiritually isolated — like no one around you shares your values — take comfort in Zayd's example. Your sincerity to God is recognized even when no community surrounds you.
When you feel lost searching for truth or meaning, remember that the genuine search itself is honored by God. Keep seeking, keep questioning, and do not give up on the journey.
Honor and remember those who walked a righteous path before you. Regularly making du'a for those who inspired your own faith connects you to a long chain of spiritual seekers.

title: The Poetry of Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl: Testifying to the Oneness of God
summary: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl composed powerful poetry rejecting idol worship and affirming submission to God alone, recorded by multiple scholars as evidence of his pure monotheistic faith before the coming of Islam.
story: Among the verses of Zayd h. 'Amr h. Nufayl, God have mercy on him, are the lines: "To God I give my praise and homage, expressing a lasting joy that time will not fade. To the Sovereign Lord all high, above whom there is none, a God to whom no other Lord can draw close." It is also said that these lines are of Umayya b. Abii &Salt. But God knows best. Among his verses affirming the oneness of God are the lines related by Muhammad b. Ishaq and al-Zubayr b. Bakkar, among others: "I turn my face in submission to Him to whom the earth bearing heavy rocks submits; He laid it down and when it settled He made it firm and level, then raised up the mountains above it. I turn my face in submission to Him to whom the clouds bearing sweet cool water submit, When they are drawn along to a place, they obey, and pour water down copiously upon it. I turn my face in submission to Him to whom the wind, changing direction hither and yon, submits." Muhammad h. Ishaq said, "I heard from Hisham b.Wrwa that, according to his father, Zayd b.'Amr spoke the verses: 'Is it one Lord or one thousand I'm to worship according to your calculations? I have abandoned al-Lat and al-Wzza together; thus do all those who are firmand determined. It is not al-Wzz~I worship, nor her daughters, nor do I visit the two idols of the 'Amr tribe. Nor do I worship Ghanm, though he was our Lord when I had little wisdom. I was perplexed -and at night many things are strange that a sensible man understands well by day That God erased many men who lived in wickedness, Yet gave long life to others through the faith of a people so that from them a young child wuld multiply, And though a man may falter, one day he may be cured, as a green tree branch will leaf again, But I worship the All-merciful, my lord, that the compassionate Lord may forgive my sins. So preserve your fear of God your Lord; while you preserve it you will not be lost. You will see that the abode of the innocent is paradise, and that there is a burning hell-fire for disbelievers, Shame while alive and when they die they will suffer temble torture.'"
lessons: Expressing faith through creative means — poetry, writing, or art — is a powerful way to affirm and strengthen one's own convictions, as Zayd did when no community supported him.
Standing alone against the dominant culture requires firm determination; Zayd's refrain 'thus do all those who are firm and determined' reminds us that clarity of purpose empowers resistance.
The image of a faltering man who is eventually cured, like a green branch that leafs again, is a profound metaphor for spiritual recovery — no stumble is permanent for one who keeps turning to God.
practical_advice: When you feel confused or spiritually lost, write down what you do believe — even a few lines. Articulating your faith, like Zayd did through poetry, can bring clarity and calm.

If you feel pressured by your environment to conform to things that conflict with your values, remember Zayd's example: identify clearly what you are turning away from and why, and let that clarity be your anchor.
When you feel you have faltered or fallen short, recall the image of the green branch that leaves again. Turn back to God with sincerity, and trust that recovery and renewal are always possible.

title: Zayd b. 'Amr, Waraqa b. Nawfal, and the Companions Who Left Makkah Seeking Truth
summary: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl, Waraqa b. Nawfal, 'Uthman b. al-Huwayrith, and 'Ubayd Allah b. Jahsh all left for Syria in search of the true religion. While the others became Christians, Zayd alone refused to adopt any religion and remained upon the pure worship of God, following the way of Abraham.
story: It has been stated earlier that Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl left for Syria along with Waraqa b. Nawfal, Wthmzn b. al-Huwayrith, and Whayd Allah b. Jahsh. All became Christians except Zayd, who adopted no religion but remained in his natural state of the worship of God alone and without partner, following as best he could the religion of Abraham, as we have mentioned. As for Waraqa b. Nawfal, his story will he told in discussion of the beginnings of the divine mission. As for Wthman b. al-Hnwayrith, he remained in Syria until he died there while with Caesar. His story is very strange and was related by at-Umawi. In brief it is as follows. When he approached Caesar and complained to him of the treament he had received from his own people, Caesar wrote for him to Ibn Jafna, the king of the Arabs of Syria, to the effect that he should provide an army to go with h i to make war against Quraysh. H e did decide to do so, but the bedouins wrote to him advising him against that action because of what they saw to be the greatness of Mecca, and also bow God had treated the army that had come with the elephants. So Ibn Jafna invested h i with a tunic dyed with poison and he was killed by it. Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl eulogized h i in poetry recorded by al-Umawi which we have left out for brevity's sake. His death occurred some three years before the mission of Islam. But God, All-glorious and Almighty is He, knows best.
lessons: The search for truth is one of the most noble human endeavors, even when the path is unclear and the seeker must travel far from home and community.

Not everyone who embarks on a sincere journey reaches the same destination; Zayd's example shows that remaining in a state of pure search — refusing falsehood even without having found the full truth — is itself a form of integrity.

The fate of 'Uthman b. al-Huwayrith, who sought worldly power through an alliance with Caesar and was killed for it, stands as a reminder that pursuing truth through worldly means and political power can lead one astray.
practical_advice: If you are in a period of spiritual searching — unsure of what you believe — do not feel you must rush to adopt a label or community before you are ready. Honest seeking, like Zayd's, has its own honor.

When those around you, even close companions, make choices that do not feel right to you, trust your own conscience. Zayd's companions all became Christians but he followed his own heart's conviction.
Beware of mixing the search for truth with the desire for status, influence, or revenge against those who wronged you. Keep your spiritual journey separate from worldly grievances.

title: The Building of the Ka'ba and Its History Through the Ages
summary: The text recounts the successive rebuildings of the Ka'ba — attributed to Abraham, then the giants, then Jurhum, and finally Quraysh — with the most credible account identifying Abraham as its original builder, occurring approximately five to fifteen years before the coming of Islam.
story: An Account of some of the events that occurred in the time of the fat~-a, including the building of the ka'ba. It has been said that it was Adam who first built it. Such a statement comes on the authority of 'Abd All~h h. 'Amr; Ibn Lahi% is one of its chain of authorities and he is an authority considered d a c g weak. The most credible of statements is that Abraham, al-Khalil, "the true friend", peace be upon him, was the first who built it, as reported above. Sim%kb. Harb so related, from Khdid b. 'Ar'ara back to 'Ali b. Abn Tdib who said, "Then it collapsed, was rebuilt by al-'amaliqa, 'the giants', fell down and was built again by Jurhum; thereafter it collapsed and was rebuilt by Quraysh." Here I will recount its being built by Quraysh; this occurred some five, or as some say fifteen, years before the coming of Islam. Al-Zuhri stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) had by then attained puberty." Material related to all that will come in its proper place, if God wills it, and in Him there is trust.
lessons: The Ka'ba's repeated destruction and rebuilding across generations shows that sacred things endure not because of physical permanence but because communities continually renew their commitment to what is holy.
Every generation inherits sacred trusts from those before them; the responsibility to preserve, restore, and honor these trusts falls on the living, just as Quraysh rebuilt what had fallen.
God's house endured across centuries and civilizations — this continuity is a source of comfort, reminding believers that God's presence and guidance persist through all of history's upheavals.
practical_advice: When something important in your life has broken down — a relationship, a practice, a sense of purpose — remember that collapse is not the end. The Ka'ba was rebuilt multiple times. You too can begin again.
Take time to reflect on the sacred trusts in your life — your faith, your family ties, your community commitments — and ask yourself what needs to be renewed or restored right now.
Find comfort in the long arc of history. When the world feels unstable, anchor yourself in the knowledge that God's guidance has persisted through every age of human civilization.

title: Ka'b b. Lu'ayy's Prophecy of the Coming of the Prophet Muhammad
summary: Ka'b b. Lu'ayy, an ancestor of the Prophet, would gather his people on Fridays and address them with wisdom and a striking prophecy that a noble prophet would emerge from the sacred sanctuary, foretelling the coming of Muhammad centuries before his birth.
story: An Account of KaCb b. Lu'ayy. Abii Nu* related in a line from Muhammad b. al-Hasan b. Zabala, from Muhammad b. Talha al-Taymi, from Muhammad b. Ibrzhim b. al-Hztith back to Abn Salama who said, "Ka'b b. Lu'ayy would gather his people on Friday, a day Quraysh used to name al-'aaba, and he would address them as follows: 'And moreover you must listen, learn, understand, and know. Tranquil night, sunlit day; the earth is flat, the skies erected, the mountains bulwarks, the stars markers. The first are as the last, the female and the male, the couple, and whatever excites, all heads for decay. So respect your ties of relationship and preserve your kinship, and make your wealth multiply. For did you ever see return someone who expired, or a dead person revived? The (eternal) abode is before you and thought is not what you express. Embellish and venerate your sanctuary and preserve it well; for to it glorious tidings will come. And from it will emerge a noble prophet.' "Then he would say, 'Daytime and night, every day has a happening; the same to us whether by day time or night. They both bring events when they arrive and their coverings over us bring many blessings. The Prophet Muhammad will come unexpectedly, and bring such news; believable indeed is their bearer.' "Then he would say, 'By God, were I hut part of them, by hearing and sight, by hand and foot, I would vigorously rise up like a camel, and charge into it like a stallion.'"
lessons: Divine signs and prophecies of the Prophet's coming were embedded throughout human history, showing that God prepares the world for His messengers long before they arrive.

Ka'b b. Lu'ayy's passionate wish to have lived to see and support the Prophet — 'were I but part of them' — teaches us to cherish the blessing of being from the Ummah of Muhammad and to never take that gift lightly.

His call to 'respect your ties of relationship, preserve your kinship, and make your wealth multiply' through generosity reminds us that ethical social conduct and family bonds are timeless values foundational to all true religion.
practical_advice: When you feel disconnected from your faith, reflect on how long God prepared for the coming of Islam — through prophets, seekers, and even ancestors of the Prophet who foretold his arrival. You are part of a magnificent, divinely orchestrated story.

Ka'b's urgency — 'you must listen, learn, understand, and know' — is a call to intellectual and spiritual engagement. Set aside regular time each week, as Ka'b did on Fridays, to reflect, learn, and reconnect with your values.

Honor your family ties and community relationships today. Ka'b's wisdom links the sacred and the social: venerating the sanctuary and preserving kinship are mentioned together, reminding us that worship and human connection are inseparable.

title: Ka'b b. Lu'ayy's Prophecy of the Coming Prophet
summary: Ka'b b. Lu'ayy prophesied the coming of a noble prophet, expressing his deep longing to witness the mission of the future Messenger of God.
story: tidings will come. And from it will emerge a noble prophet.' "Then he would say, 'Daytime and night, every day has a happening; the same to us whether by day time or night. They both bring events when they arrive and their coverings over us bring many blessings. The Prophet Muhammad will come unexpectedly, and bring such news; believable indeed is their bearer.' "Then he would say, 'By God, were I but part of them, by hearing and sight, by hand and foot, I would vigorously rise up like a camel, and charge into it like a stallion.' "Then he would say, 'Would that I could witness the impact of his mission, when the tribe seeks the truth without vigour.'" Between the death of Ka'b b. Lu'ayy and the mission of the Messenger of God (SAAS) there was a period of 560 years.
lessons: Hope in divine promise can sustain a person even when fulfillment is far away — Ka'b longed for a prophet he would never see, yet his faith never wavered.
Expressing longing for goodness and truth is itself an act of spiritual devotion, even when circumstances feel distant from that ideal.
The arrival of guidance may be delayed by centuries, yet divine promises are never broken — patience across generations is part of God's wisdom.
practical_advice: When you feel disconnected from hope or meaning, reflect on how people before you held onto faith through long periods of uncertainty — their example can anchor you.
Write down what kind of future you deeply hope for, even if it feels far away; articulating hope is a first step toward orienting your life toward it.
When waiting feels unbearable, remind yourself that longing for goodness is itself a sign of a living, searching heart — do not mistake the waiting for abandonment.

title: The Vision of 'Abd al-Muttalib and the Rediscovery of Zamzam
summary: 'Abd al-Muttalib received repeated visions commanding him to dig zamzam, which had been buried and lost since the time of Jurhum, and after persistent effort he rediscovered the sacred well.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, "'Abd al-Muttalib was sleeping in the sacred enclosure when he received a vision ordering him to dig up zamzam. "'Abd al-Muttalib was the first to begin digging it, according to what was related to me by Yazid b. Abii Habib al-Misri from Marthad b. 'Abd Allah al-Yazani, from 'Abd Allah b. Dhurayr al-Ghzliqi, namely, that the last mentioned heard 'Ali b. Abii Talib, God be pleased with him, telling how 'Abd al-Muttalib had been ordered to dig zamzam. 'Ali reported 'Abd al-Muttalib as saying: 'I was sleeping in the sanctuary when a vision came to me and said, "Dig tiba!" "But what's tiba?" I asked. But it vanished. "Next day when I again went to my bed and slept, he came again and said, "Dig barra!" "But what's barra?" I asked. But it vanished. "Next day when I again went to my bed and slept, he came again and said, "Dig al-madnuna!" "But what's al-madnuna?" I asked. But it vanished. "Next day when I again went to my bed and slept, he came again and said, "Dig zamzam!" "But what's zamzam?" I asked. "It will never fail or dry up; it will water the grand pilgrim. It lies between the dung and the blood, near the nest of the crow with the white leg and the ants' nest."' "The matter having been clarified and he having been directed to its location, and knowing that he had been spoken to in truth, he took up his pick-axe next day and went off in the company of his son al-Harith b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, his only son at that time. So he dug down for it and when the coping stone appeared to 'Abd al-Muttalib he let out a cry of praise for God.
lessons: Divine guidance is sometimes revealed gradually — each repeated vision brought 'Abd al-Muttalib closer to clarity, teaching us that confusion is often a prelude to understanding.
Acting on incomplete guidance with trust and courage, rather than waiting for absolute certainty, is a mark of true faith.
Persistence in seeking what God has directed us toward, even when the path is unclear at first, always leads to uncovering buried blessings.
practical_advice: When you feel confused about a direction in life, do not be discouraged if clarity comes in stages — return to reflection and prayer repeatedly until the path becomes clearer.
Take the first step toward something you feel called to, even if you don't have all the answers; action combined with trust often reveals what waiting alone cannot.
Keep a record of moments when confusion eventually resolved into clarity — reviewing these can build confidence that your current uncertainty will also pass.

title: Quraysh's Dispute Over Zamzam and the Miracle of Water in the Desert
summary: When 'Abd al-Muttalib rediscovered zamzam, Quraysh disputed his right to it, and on the journey to seek arbitration, a miraculous spring saved the dying party from death by thirst, settling the dispute in 'Abd al-Muttalib's favour.
story: Quraysh therefore knew that he had achieved his purpose so they came and told him, 'O 'Abd al-Muttalib, that is the well of our father Ishmael; we have a right to it, so make us partners in it with yourself.' 'I will do no such thing; I was specifically assigned to this without you; I was given it from among you all,' he replied. They asked again, 'Treat us fairly; we'll not leave you alone till we share with you in it.' 'Abd al-Muttalib replied, 'Then appoint someone of your choice for me to ask to adjudicate the matter with you.' 'The woman diviner of the Banu Sa'd b. Hudhaym,' they suggested. 'Abd al-Muttalib agreed; the woman lived in the uplands of Syria. "So 'Abd al-Muttalib mounted up with a company of men of the Banu Ummaya, along with members of each tribe of Quraysh and they all left. The earth at that time was waterless and having travelled some distance 'Abd al-Muttalib and his friends had used up their water. They were so thirsty that they became convinced they were doomed. They therefore asked the other party for water, but they refused, saying, 'We're in a waterless desert and we fear for ourselves a similar plight that afflicts you.' 'Abd al-Muttalib then said, 'I think each man should dig a grave for himself with the strength he has remaining, so that when anyone dies his friends can put him in the hole and cover him up, and so on till there is only one man left. Better the loss (without burial) of one than that of a whole company.' 'What you instruct is good,' they agreed. "So each man dug himself a hole, and they sat down to await death from thirst. But then 'Abd al-Muttalib told his companions, 'By God, our casting ourselves down by our own hands like this for death without searching the land and exerting ourselves is truly weakness; God may well provide us water somewhere in this land. Mount up!' So they did so. But when 'Abd al-Muttalib urged his mount up, a spring of sweet water burst up from beneath its hoof! 'Abd al-Muttalib made exclamations of God's greatness, as did his companions; he and they dismounted, drank, and filled their water flasks. Then 'Abd al-Muttalib called the Quraysh tribesmen who had been watching all this, saying, 'Come on over to the spring. God has given us water.' So they came and drank, all filling their flasks. They then said, 'Judgement has been made, by God, in your favour against us! We won't ever dispute with you over zamzam. He who gave you this water in this desert is also He who gave you zamzam. So return to your drawing of water, it being your right.' "So he and they all went back without consulting the diviner. And Quraysh gave 'Abd al-Muttalib free access to zamzam."
lessons: When facing what seems like certain ruin, choosing to act rather than surrender passively can open doors that despair would permanently close.
Divine support often arrives precisely at the moment of greatest helplessness — the miracle of the spring came only after 'Abd al-Muttalib refused to accept defeat.
Those who dispute against truth may need only to witness one undeniable sign before recognising what their hearts already knew — not all conflict requires prolonged struggle.
practical_advice: When you feel completely stuck or hopeless, resist the urge to give up before you have truly exhausted your effort — stand up and try one more thing before accepting failure.
In the midst of a conflict or injustice against you, maintain your trust in God and continue acting with integrity; resolution may come from an unexpected direction entirely.
Reflect on past moments when you were in a desperate situation that somehow resolved — let those memories become proof to yourself that the present crisis is not the end of your story.

title: The Dispute Over the Treasures Found in Zamzam and the Use of Divination Arrows
summary: When 'Abd al-Muttalib found two golden gazelles, swords, and armour buried within zamzam, Quraysh demanded a share, and he proposed settling the matter through divination arrows, which awarded the treasures between the Ka'ba and himself.
story: Persisting in the digging, he found there two golden gazelles that Jurhum had buried, along with some white metal swords and some breast-plates. "Quraysh then addressed him, 'O 'Abd al-Muttalib, we have a right to share in this.' 'No,' he replied, 'but let us decide what is equitable between us. Let us use divinatory arrows over it.' 'What shall we do then?' they asked. He replied, 'I will make two arrows for the ka'ba, two for myself, and two for you. Whoever has his arrows come out for something, that he shall have. Whoever has his arrows left behind gets nothing.' They agreed that this was fair. So he made two yellow arrows for the ka'ba, two black ones for himself, and two white ones for them. Then he gave the arrows to the diviner at the site of the idol Hubal, who was to cast them. And Hubal was the largest of their idols; that was why Abu Sufyan said at the battle of Uhud, 'Arise, O Hubal!', referring to that idol. So 'Abd al-Muttalib said a prayer to God." Yunus b. Bukayr recounted, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, that 'Abd al-Muttalib began saying: "O God, You are the much-praised King; my Lord; You are the one who initiates and repeats, The one who is fully holding the rocky mountain; from You comes all that is new or that is old. If You wish, You give inspiration as You want, to find the place of the ornaments and the steel. So make clear today what You wish; I have made a vow to Him who decides Give it, O my Lord, to me, and I'll not go back." "The diviner cast the arrows and the two yellow ones went towards the gazelles, giving them to the ka'ba, the two black ones came out for the swords and armour, making them for 'Abd al-Muttalib, but the two arrows for Quraysh remained behind. So 'Abd al-Muttalib hammered down the two swords into a door for the ka'ba into which he incorporated the two golden gazelles. This was, so they say, the first decoration of gold the ka'ba had."
lessons: Even in a pre-Islamic context, 'Abd al-Muttalib turned to God in prayer before any decision — recognising the divine as the ultimate arbiter is a timeless impulse of the sincere heart.
Dedicating a portion of one's bounty to the house of God, as 'Abd al-Muttalib did with the gazelles, reflects a spirit of gratitude that transforms personal gain into communal blessing.
When disputes arise over shared blessings, seeking a fair and agreed-upon process of resolution preserves relationships better than unilateral claims.
practical_advice: Before making an important decision, pause and pray — even a few sincere words asking God for clarity and the right outcome can shift your inner state from anxiety to trust.
When you receive an unexpected blessing, consider how a portion of it might serve others or a greater good; generosity in moments of abundance builds lasting peace of heart.
When in conflict over something valuable, propose a process that both parties recognise as fair rather than simply asserting your position — fairness disarms opposition more effectively than force.

title: The Superiority of Zamzam and Its Virtues as Described by the Prophet
summary: Following the rediscovery of zamzam by 'Abd al-Muttalib, the well became the most prized water source in Mecca, and the Prophet Muhammad later attested to its supreme taste and healing properties.
story: Thereafter 'Abd al-Muttalib took charge of providing water from zamzam to the pilgrims. And Ibn Ishaq and others relate that Mecca had many wells in it prior to the appearance of zamzam at the time of 'Abd al-Muttalib. Ibn Ishaq, moreover, enumerated and named them, and told their whereabouts in and around Mecca as well as those who dug them. His account of this concludes, "Zamzam overshadowed all the other wells, their customers preferring to go to it, because of its proximity to the holy mosque, the superiority of its water over the rest, and its having been the well of Ishmael, son of Abraham. The 'Abd Manaf tribe expressed because of it a superiority over all Quraysh as well as all the other Arabs." It is established in the sahih collection of Muslim in the material relating to the adoption of Islam by Abu Dharr, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said the following about zamzam: "It is the taste supreme. It is the cure of disease." The Imam Ahmad (Ibn Hanbal) said, "'Abd Allah b. al-Walid related to us, on the authority of 'Abd Allah b. al-Mu'mmal, from Abu al-Zubayr, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The water of zamzam is for what is drunk from it.'"
lessons: What God blesses becomes a source of benefit that far exceeds all worldly alternatives — zamzam surpassed every other well not by human engineering but by divine distinction.
Prophetic guidance affirms that spiritual and physical healing often come from the same sacred source — drinking zamzam with intention and prayer is an act of worship as much as nourishment.
The blessings God places in the world are often recovered through the faithful effort of individuals — 'Abd al-Muttalib's obedience restored a gift that had been buried for generations.
practical_advice: When you drink zamzam water, do so with conscious intention and supplication — the Prophet taught that it serves the purpose for which it is drunk, so bring your deepest needs to that moment.
Reflect on the sacred things in your life that may have been 'buried' by neglect or distraction — rediscovering them, as 'Abd al-Muttalib rediscovered zamzam, can renew your spiritual life.

When something appears to be lost beyond recovery, consider that God may restore it through a path you have not yet imagined — perseverance and obedience are the pick-axe that uncovers buried blessings.

title: The Virtues and Rules of Zamzam Water
summary: Scholarly accounts and hadiths about the sacred Zamzam well, including its healing properties, etiquette of drinking, and the ruling of Abd al-Muttalib that it should not be used for washing.
story: established in the ~ahzhcollection of Muslim in the material relating to the adoption of Islam by Abii Dharr, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said the following about zamzam: "It is the taste supreme. It is the cure of disease." The I m b Ahmad (Ihn Hanbal) said, "'Abd Allah b. al-Walid related to us, on the authority of CAbd All& b. al-Mucmil, from Abii al-Zubayr, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The water of zamzam is for what is drunk from it.'" Ibn Maja related this also, from a hadith of 'Abd All& b. al-Mu'mil. They spoke it and gave its wording as: "The water of zamzam is for what is drunk to it." Suwayd b. Sa'id related it from 'Abd All& b. al-Mubarak, from 'Abd al-Rahmm b. Abii al-Mawnlr, from Muhammad b. al-Munkadir, from Jnhir, from the Prophet (SAAS) who said, "The water of zamzam is for what is drunk to it." However, Suwayd b. Sa'id is a weak link. The tradition as preserved from Ibn al-Mubnrak from 'Abd Allnh b. al-Mu'mil is as given above. Al-H&im related it from Ibn 'Abbas in the form "The water of zamzam is for what is drunk to it." And this is questionable. But God knows best. Similarly, Ibn Mzja and al-H&im relate that Ihn 'Abbs said to a man, "When you drink from zamzam face the ka'ba and repeat the name of God. Then breathe deeply three times and drink from it until you are fdl. When finished, give praise to God. For the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The evidence of the differences between us and the hypocrites is that they do not drink to the fill from the water of zamzam.'" And it is related from 'Abd al-Mucdib that he said, "0God, I do not allow a person to wash with it; it is for people to drink hillun, 'freely available', ma, 'and', ballun, 'allowed'." Some learned scholars report this from al-'AbbS b. 'Abd al-Mucglib but in fact it came from 'Abd al-Muc~libhimself, for it was he who restored zamzam as stated above. But God knows best. Al-Umawi wrote in his Maghazi work as follows: "Aba Whayd related to us, Y+ya b. Sa'id informed us, from 'Ahd al-R&man h. Harmala, that the last mentioned said that he heard Sacid b. al-Musayyab relate that 'Abd al-Mu~alib b. Hnshim remarked when he dug zamzam, 'I do not allow a person to wash with it; it is freely available and allowed for a person to drink it.' T o that purpose he had two basins for it, one for drinking, the other for the prayer ablution. At that point he said, 'I do not allow a person to wash with it, deeming it inappropriate for the mosque to he washed in.'" According to Abn Whayd, al-Asma'i stated that his saying ma balluniis an i t b ~ ' , "corroboration of the preceding word", hdun. But Abii Whayd objected that an itba' cannot occur with the maw of conjunction (ma meaning "and") but it is used because, as Muctamir b. Sulaymm had indicated, ballun in the language of Himyar means mubah, i.e. "allowed". Aba Wbayd then said, "Aba Bakr b. 'Ayyash related to us, from ' h i m b. Aba al-Nujnd, that he heard Zirr (say) that he heard al-'AbhS state, 'I do not allow a person to wash in it.'" And 'Abd al-Ra$m%n b. Mahdi related to us, as did Sufya, from 'Abd a,-U m s n h. 'Alqama, that the last mentioned heard Ibn 'Abhs say the same. And this tradition relating hack to both of them is true; it has them so saying in their own era with the purpose of informing and establishing what 'Abd al-Mugalib had specified when he dug iq thus it does not contradict the preceding account. But God knows best.
lessons: Sacred things carry specific etiquette and rules that reflect reverence — approaching what is holy with intentionality brings spiritual benefit.
Scholars throughout history have carefully preserved and debated sacred knowledge, showing that seeking truth with rigor is itself an act of worship.
The Prophet's teaching that Zamzam is 'for what is drunk to it' reminds us that sincere intention shapes the outcome of our actions — we receive according to what we sincerely seek.
practical_advice: When you approach something sacred or meaningful in your life, pause to set a clear, sincere intention — mindful engagement transforms ordinary acts into moments of growth.
If you are confused by conflicting advice or information, follow the example of scholars who traced matters back to their source rather than accepting the first opinion heard.
Practice gratitude for the blessings in your life by using them purposefully and respectfully rather than carelessly, just as Zamzam was protected for drinking and not wasted on washing.

title: Custodianship of Zamzam Passes from Abu Talib to Al-Abbas
summary: After Abd al-Muttalib's death the right to provide water for pilgrims passed to his son Abu Talib, who fell into poverty and debt and was ultimately forced to relinquish control of the Zamzam well to his brother al-Abbas.
story: The rights to providing water for the pilgrims remained with (Abd al-Mudih as long as he lived, thereafter passing to his son AbB Tdib for a period. Then it so happened that he became impoverished for a while and went into debt to his brother al-rAhhas for 10,000 from one year to the next. Aha Tslib spent the money on matters relating to watering the pilgrims, and when the next year arrived he had nothing left. So he asked his brother for a loan of 14,000 to the following year, at which time, he promised, he would repay the total debt. But al-cAbhns agreed only on condition that if Aha Tdib did not repay it, control of the well would transfer to himself. T o this Aha Titlib agreed. When the next year arrived Aha Tdih had nothing to give to al-'Abhss and so he relinquished control of the well to him. Thereafter it went to 'Abd Allith, al-'Abbss' son, then to 'Ali b. 'Abd Allsh h. (Abbns, to Ditwad h. 'Ali, to Sulayma b. 'Ali and to Cisa b. 'Ali. Then 4-Mansnr took it over and entrusted its control to his mawla', his freed slave, Aha Ruzayn. Al-Umawi gave this account.
lessons: Financial hardship can cause us to lose things we hold dear, but this does not mean we have failed — circumstances beyond our control are part of life's trials.
Responsibilities and honors that we carry are held in trust; sometimes they pass on to others, and accepting that gracefully is its own form of strength.
Debt and financial pressure can compound over time — managing our obligations honestly, even when the outcome is painful, preserves our integrity.
practical_advice: When facing financial stress, make a clear accounting of your obligations and seek honest conversations with those you owe, rather than letting debt accumulate silently.
If you must give up something meaningful due to difficult circumstances, grieve the loss but also recognize that the value of what you contributed during your stewardship remains real.
Reflect on the responsibilities you currently carry — are you tending to them with the care they deserve, or are pressures causing them to slip away unnoticed?

title: Abd al-Muttalib's Vow to Sacrifice One of His Sons
summary: When Abd al-Muttalib faced opposition while digging Zamzam, he vowed that if ten sons grew up to protect him he would sacrifice one of them at the Ka'ba. When the vow came due, the arrow fell upon his beloved youngest son Abd Allah.
story: A n Account of 'Abd al-Mul~alib'svow to sacrifice one of his sons. Ihn Ishaq stated, "It is claimed that when 'Abd al-Mugalib received such opposition from Quraysh over the digging of zamzam, he vowed that if ten sons were born to him who grew up and protected him, he would sacrifice one of them for God at the kacba. "Eventually he had ten sons grown up whom he knew would give him protection. Their names were al-H%ith, al-Zuhayr, Hajl, Dir%, al-Muqawwim, Abii Lahab, alLCAhb%s, Hamza, Aha Taib, and 'Abd All*. He assembled them and told them of his vow and asked them to honour his pledge to God, Almighty and All-glorious is He. They obeyed, and asked h i what he wanted them to do. He asked each of them to take an arrow, write his name on it and return to him. "They did so and he went with them inside the kacba to the site of their god Huhal where there was the well in which offerings to the ka'ba would he placed. There, near Huhal, were seven arrows which they would use for divining a judgement over some matter of consequence, a question of blood-money, kmship, or the like. They would come to Hubal to seek a resolution, accepting whatever they were ordered to do or to refrain from."
lessons: Vows made in moments of desperation carry weight and must be honored, reminding us to be thoughtful about the promises we make under pressure.
The willingness of Abd al-Muttalib's sons to submit to their father's vow without complaint illustrates how family bonds and shared commitments can hold us together through terrifying moments.
Even in pre-Islamic tradition, people felt the pull toward devotion and sacrifice — this deep human instinct toward commitment to something greater than oneself is part of our nature.
practical_advice: Before making a vow or serious promise under stress, pause and consider whether you are making it from a place of clarity or panic — promises made in crisis can bind us in ways we did not fully envision.
When a feared outcome arrives — as the arrow landed on Abd Allah — resist the impulse to act immediately. Seek counsel, reflect, and look for a wise path forward.

Talk openly with your family about the heavy commitments you carry. Abd al-Muttalib gathered his sons and explained everything — transparency with loved ones reduces the burden of facing difficult moments alone.

title: The Community Intervenes to Save Abd Allah from Sacrifice
summary: When Abd al-Muttalib prepared to sacrifice his beloved son Abd Allah in fulfillment of his vow, the Quraysh and Abd Allah's brothers intervened and urged him to seek another way, ultimately sending him to a diviner for guidance.
story: The outcome was that when 'Abd al-Mutdib came to seek judgement with the arrows from Hubal, the one with the name of his son 'Abd All* came forth. He was his youngest boy and the one he loved most, hut 'Abd d-Mutmlib took his son 'Abd Allah by the hand, drew out his knife, and went up to Iszf and N~'ilato sacrifice him. At this Quraysh left their meeting-places and asked h i what he intended to do. When he replied that be was going to sacrifice 'Abd All*, they, along with 'Abd Allah's brothers, said, "By God, do not sacrifice h i without seeking forgiveness for him; if yon do this men will keep bringing their sons to sacrifice and how could that go on?" Yiinus b. Bukayr related from Ibn Ishaq that al-'Abbas was the one who drew 'Abd Allah out from beneath his father's foot when he had placed it on him to sacrifice him. And it is said that he scarred his face so badly that it remained visible there till he died. Thereafter Quraysh advised that 'Abd d-Mutmlib should go to the Hij% where there was a woman diviner who had an attendant spirit, and that he should consult her. "That", they said, "is the best you can do. If she then orders you to sacrifice him, do so; but if she tells you to do something that provides you a way out, then accept it." So they left for Medina, where they found that the diviner whose name was Sajah, as Yunus b. Bukayr reported from Ibn Ishaq, was at Khaybar. They rode off again and went to her and sought her advice, 'Abd al-Muffalib tellmg her of the whole problem regarding h i and his son.
lessons: Community intervention at moments of crisis can prevent irreversible harm — speaking up when someone is about to do something destructive is an act of mercy, not interference.
Even a father's love and religious obligation can coexist in painful tension; seeking counsel before acting on an overwhelming impulse reflects wisdom, not weakness.
Al-Abbas's act of physically protecting his brother teaches us that protecting those we love sometimes requires stepping directly into the path of danger.
practical_advice: If you find yourself about to make an irreversible decision while in an overwhelmed or fearful state, listen to the voices around you urging you to slow down and seek guidance first.
When someone you care about is in danger of making a harmful choice, speak up clearly and offer an alternative path — your intervention, like that of the Quraysh, may change everything.
Seek wise counsel before acting on heavy commitments, especially when the stakes involve those you love most. Traveling far to find good advice, as Abd al-Muttalib did, is never wasted effort.

title: The Diviner's Solution: One Hundred Camels Ransom Abd Allah
summary: The diviner advised Abd al-Muttalib to cast lots between Abd Allah and increasing numbers of camels until the arrow fell on the camels. After ten rounds reaching one hundred camels, the arrow finally indicated the camels, which were slaughtered as ransom for Abd Allah.
story: She told them: "Leave me today, until my attendant spirit comes and I can ask him." They left her and 'Abd al-Muttalib prayed to God. Next day they went back to her and she informed them that she had had a message. "How much is the blood-money you prescribe?" she asked. "Ten camels," they told her, that being then the case. "Then go back to your land and present your man as an offering and do the same with ten camels. Then cast arrows to decide between him and them. If the divining arrow points to him then add to the number of camels until your god is satisfied; if it points to the camels, then sacrifice them in his place. That way you will please your god and save your man." So they went back to Mecca and, when they had agreed to do as she had said, 'Abd al-Mutdib said prayers to God. Then they offered up 'Abd All* and the ten camels as sacrifice and cast the arrow. It came out against 'Abd All* so they added ten more camels and tried once more. Again it came out against him and they added ten more, then kept on doing so until the camels reached one hundred in number. When they next cast the arrow it came out for the camels. At that point the men of Quraysh told 'Abd d-Mugalib, who was standing near Hubal praying to God, "It's all over! Your God is pleased, 0 'Abd al-Mumlib." It is claimed that he then replied, "No, not until I cast the arrows three times." So they did cast three times, the arrow always indicating the camels, which were slaughtered and left there for anyone to take without hindrance. And Ibn Hish2m reported "And it is said, for any wild beast to take as well." It is otherwise related that when the number of camels reached 100 the arrow was still against 'Abd All&, so they added another 100, making 200, and the same thing happened. Likewise it did with 300. It was then that the arrow came out against the camels, whereupon 'Abd a1 Mutralib slaughtered them. But the first account is the true one. Though God knows best. Ibn Jarir related, from Yiinus h. 'Abd al-A'la, from Ibn Wahb, from Yiinus b. Yazid, from al-Zubri from Qabisa b. Dbu'ayb that Ibn 'Abb% was asked for advice by a woman who had vowed to sacrifice her son at the ka'ba. He told her to sacrifice 100 camels instead and related to her the above account about 'Abd al-Muccalib. She then asked 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar but he gave her no legal decision, simply refraining from doing so. The problem then reached Marwan b. d-Hakam, who was the governor of Medina. He said, "Neither of them gave a correct decision." He then ordered the woman to perform whatever acts of charity she could, forbidding her from sacrificing her son and not ordering her to slaughter any camels. And the people accepted Marww's judgement in the matter. But God knows best.
lessons: Patience and perseverance in the face of repeated setbacks — as the arrow kept falling against Abd Allah — teaches us that relief may come only after we have exhausted every effort and remained steadfast.

Abd al-Muttalib's insistence on casting the arrows three more times after the camels were indicated shows that thoroughness and certainty matter when the stakes are highest — do not accept an outcome prematurely if doubt remains.

The story of the woman who had vowed to sacrifice her son shows that religious rulings evolve through careful scholarship, and that mercy and charity often provide the wisest path out of impossible-seeming obligations.
practical_advice: When you face a situation where every attempt seems to fail, keep going with patience rather than forcing a premature resolution — the breakthrough may be one more effort away.

Before acting on a frightening or irreversible decision that feels religiously or morally obligatory, consult multiple wise people and weigh their counsel carefully, as different scholars offered different views in the woman's case.

When the outcome you feared finally resolves in relief, take a moment — as Abd al-Muttalib did by insisting on three more casts — to confirm it before celebrating, so your peace is built on solid ground.

title: Abd al-Muttalib's Marriage of His Son Abd Allah to Amina bint Wahb
summary: Abd al-Muttalib takes his son Abd Allah to marry Amina bint Wahb of Banu Zuhra. Along the way, a woman named Umm Qattal tries to proposition Abd Allah, recognizing the light of prophethood on his face, but he refuses out of obedience to his father.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated: "'Abd al-Muttalib then left, holding his son 'Abd Allah by the hand. They passed, it is claimed, a woman of Banu Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzza b. Qusayy who was named Umm Qattal; she was the sister of Waraqa b. Nawfal b. Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzza b. Qusayy and had been there at the ka'ba. She stared into the face of 'Abd Allah and asked him where he was going. 'With my father,' he replied. 'If you will have me now,' she said, 'you can have the like number of camels that were sacrificed for you!' 'But I am with my father, and I may not oppose him or leave him,' he answered." 'Abd al-Muttalib took him further on till they reached Wahb b. 'Abd Manaf b. Zuhra b. Kilab b. Murra b. Ka'b b. Lu'ayy b. Ghalib b. Fihr who was at that time leader of Banu Zuhra in both age and honour. He agreed to marry to 'Abd Allah his daughter Amina, who was then the woman most highly regarded among his people. It is claimed that 'Abd Allah consummated the marriage then and there, and that she conceived the Messenger of God (SAAS). Afterwards he left her and passed again by the woman who had made a proposition to him. He asked her, "How come you're not making me the offer today you did yesterday?" She replied, "The light you bore yesterday has left you, and I need you no more." She had heard from her brother Waraqa b. Nawfal who had become a Christian and studied the Scriptures, that there was to be a prophet come to that nation, and she wanted him to be born to her. So God made him of the finest and most noble lineage, as the Almighty put it, in the Qur'an, "And God knows best where to place His prophethood" (surat al-An'am, VI, v. 124).
lessons: Obedience to one's parents, even when tempting alternatives present themselves, is a mark of noble character.
Divine will shapes the circumstances of great events; no human desire can redirect what God has decreed.
The light of righteousness and prophetic lineage is recognized even by those outside the faith community.
practical_advice: When faced with temptation that conflicts with your responsibilities to family or God, remember that honouring your commitments is itself an act of worship.
Trust that God places blessings and opportunities in the right vessels at the right time; avoid envy when something good passes you by.
Reflect on how your choices today may carry spiritual weight beyond what you can see — act with integrity in every moment.

title: Fatima bint Murr Recognizes the Light of Prophethood on Abd Allah
summary: A woman diviner named Fatima bint Murr al-Khath'amiyya sees the light of prophethood on Abd Allah's face and offers him camels to be with her, but he refuses in verse; later she explains that she sought only the light she perceived in him, which God placed where He willed.
story: Abu Bakr Muhammad b. Sahl al-Khara'iti said that 'Ali b. Harb related to him, as did Muhammad b. 'Uthman al-Qurashi, as did Muslim b. Khalid al-Zanji, as did Ibn Jurayj from 'Ata b. Abi Rabi', from Ibn 'Abbas that the latter said, "When 'Abd al-Muttalib went off with his son 'Abd Allah to arrange his marriage, he passed by a woman diviner of the Tibaa people who had adopted the Jewish faith and read the Scriptures, her name being Fatima daughter of Murr al-Khath'amiyya. She saw the light of prophethood on 'Abd Allah's face and said, 'Young man, would you like to have me now and let me give you 100 camels?' 'Abd Allah replied with the verse, 'I'd rather die than do the forbidden, and staying here is not allowed, I clearly see. So how could we do what you desire?'" Then 'Abd Allah passed on with his father who married him to Amina daughter of Wahb b. 'Abd Manaf b. Zuhra; and he stayed three nights with her. Then he had an urge to accept the woman's offer so he went to her. "Well, what did you do after (seeing) me?" she asked. And he told her. She then said: "By God, I'm not a woman of doubtful morals; but I did see a light in your face, and I wanted it to be in me. But God refused to put it anywhere other than He wished." Then Fatima spoke the following verses: "I saw a sign shine forth, glistening like raindrops in dark storm clouds, Which appeared to me as a light illuminating all around it, as the full moon shines forth. And I hoped to attain it, a source of pride I could capture, but not everyone who strikes his flint lights fire, For God is what a Zuhriyya woman took, though your two garments are what she seized; but she did not know that." She also spoke the verses: "O Banu Hashim, it came off from your brother to Umayna (Amina) as they were passionately consummating their marriage, As the lamp when it dies down leaves behind its wicks, stained with oil, Not all the wealth a man holds is achieved by resoluteness, nor is what escapes him always from lassitude. So be moderate in pursuing what you want; good luck and misfortune contending together should suffice you, Suffice you either a hand tight closed or a hand laid open, fingers stretched. When Umayna (Amina) got from him what she had, she received from him an honour, never to come again."
lessons: God places His greatest gifts and honours precisely where He wills, and no human effort can override that divine placement.
Rejection of something we greatly desire can itself be a sign of God's wisdom working in ways we cannot see.
Moral integrity — refusing the forbidden even when it is outwardly attractive — is a quality associated with those of noble spiritual standing.
practical_advice: When you do not receive something you hoped and strived for, consider that God may have reserved that blessing for another vessel or another time.
Acknowledge your desires honestly, as Fatima did, without letting them lead you into dishonourable actions.
Recite or reflect on the Quranic principle that God knows best where to place His blessings, especially in moments of disappointment.

title: The Jewish Scholar's Prophecy to Abd al-Muttalib in Yemen
summary: During a winter journey to Yemen, Abd al-Muttalib stays with a Jewish high priest who, after examining him, prophesies that prophethood will come through his lineage from Banu Zuhra, and advises him to marry from that tribe.
story: Imam Abu Nu'aym, the hafiz, in his book Dala'il al-Nubuwwa (Signs of the Prophethood), related on a chain from Ya'qub b. Muhammad al-Zuhri, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. 'Umar, from 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far, from Ibn 'Awn, from al-Miswar b. Makhrama, from Ibn 'Abbas as follows: "'Abd al-Muttalib went to Yemen on a winter's journey and stayed with a Jewish high priest. 'Abd al-Muttalib said, 'And one of the monks — by that he meant a scholar of the Scriptures — said to me, "O 'Abd al-Muttalib, would you allow me to look at a certain part of you?" "Yes, if it be nothing shameful," I replied. Then the man opened up one of my nostrils and looked inside it, and did the same with the other one, saying, "I swear that in one of your hands you will have power and in the other prophethood. But we find that to be in the Banu Zuhra, so how can that be?" I replied that I did not know. The man then asked, "Do you have a shi'a?" I asked him what he meant by this word shi'a and he replied, a wife. I replied that I did not at that time and he said, "Well, when you go back marry someone from them (i.e. Banu Zuhra)."' "So when 'Abd al-Muttalib did return home he married Hala, daughter of Wahb b. 'Abd Manaf b. Zuhra, and she gave birth to Hamza and Safiyya. Then 'Abd Allah son of 'Abd al-Muttalib married Amina daughter of Wahb and she gave birth to the Messenger of God (SAAS). "When 'Abd Allah married Amina, the men of Quraysh commented, 'He won out', i.e. 'Abd Allah beat out or defeated his father 'Abd al-Muttalib."
lessons: Signs of divine truth can appear even through those outside the immediate community of believers, reminding us that guidance belongs to God alone.
Acting on sound counsel — even when it comes from unexpected sources — can align us with God's greater plan.
God's decree unfolds across generations; what seems like ordinary family decisions may carry extraordinary spiritual significance.
practical_advice: Be open to wisdom and counsel even when it comes from unexpected people or circumstances — weigh it against what is right and act accordingly.
Reflect on how your family choices and relationships may have long-term spiritual consequences beyond what you can currently perceive.
When you receive guidance that seems puzzling, move forward in trust and obedience; clarity often comes only in hindsight.

title: The Noble Lineage and Names of the Prophet Muhammad
summary: A detailed account of the Prophet Muhammad's noble ancestry, his many names given in Islamic tradition, and the Quranic affirmation that God knows best where to place His prophethood.
story: God Almighty's words were: "God knows best where to place His prophethood" (surat al-An'am, VI, v. 124). When Heraclius, the Roman emperor, asked Abu Sufyan questions relating to the qualities of the Prophet (SAAS), he wanted to know how good his lineage was in his people. Abu Sufyan replied, "He does indeed have good lineage in us." Heraclius commented, "Thus are the messengers given their missions within the ancestry of their own people." He meant from among peoples with the highest reputation and the most numerous tribal connections. May God's blessing be upon them all. He was the best of Adam's sons, their pride on earth and the hereafter. He was known as the father of al-Qasim and of Ibrahim, as Muhammad, as Ahmad, as al-Mahi, "the Abolisher", the one by whom disbelief was abolished, as al-'Aqib, "the Ultimate" after whom no prophet would follow, as al-Hashir, "the Gatherer", i.e. he at whose feet the people would gather, as al-Muqaffi, "the Tracker", as nabi al-Rahma, "the Prophet of Mercy", as nabi al-Tawba, "the Prophet of Repentance", as nabi al-Malhama, "the Prophet of the Fierce Battle", as khatim al-nabiyyin, "the Seal of the Prophets", as al-Fatih, "the Conqueror", as Taha, as Yasin, and as 'Abd Allah, "God's Servant". According to al-Bayhaqi some authorities added more names to these. He said, "In the Qur'an, God named him rasul, 'messenger', nabi, 'prophet', ummi, 'unlettered', shahid, 'witness-giver', mubashshir, 'bringer of good tidings', nadhir, 'admonisher', da'i ila Allah bi idhnihi, 'he who calls to God with His permission', siraj munir, 'illuminating lamp', ra'uf rahim, 'kind and compassionate', and mudhakkir, 'reminder'. And God made him mercy, a blessing, and a guide." He was the son of 'Abd Allah who was in turn the youngest son of his father 'Abd al-Muttalib; 'Abd Allah was the man who lay down to be sacrificed but was ransomed by 100 camels, as previously explained. Al-Zuhri said, "And 'Abd Allah was the most handsome man in Quraysh. He was brother to al-Harith, al-Zubayr, Hamza, Dirar, Abu Talib, whose own given name was 'Abd Manaf, Abu Lahab, whose given name was 'Abd al-'Uzza, al-Muqawwim, whose given name was 'Abd al-Ka'ba, though some say these were two men, Hajl, whose given name was al-Mughira, al-Ghaydaq, a man of great generosity whose given name was Nawfal, though some say that was Hajl. These, then, were the paternal uncles of the Prophet (SAAS). His aunts numbered six; they were Arwa, Barra, Umayma, Safiyya, 'Atika, and Umm Hakim, she being al-Bayda', 'the white woman'."
lessons: The Prophet's many names each reflect a different dimension of mercy, guidance, and divine purpose — meditating on them deepens love and connection.
God selects His messengers from lineages of honour and integrity, showing that character and virtue are cultivated across generations.
Recognizing the greatness of the Prophet's mission and character is itself an act of gratitude to God for sending such mercy to humanity.
practical_advice: Learn and reflect on the names of the Prophet and what each one means — choosing one to contemplate each day can bring peace and spiritual focus.
Consider the legacy you are building for those who come after you; your character and choices today shape the environment future generations inherit.
When feeling lost or spiritually distant, return to the biography of the Prophet to reconnect with the mercy and guidance he embodied.

title: The Lineage of Prophet Muhammad: Paternal Uncles and Aunts of Abd al-Muttalib
summary: A detailed account of the paternal uncles and aunts of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), listing the children of Abd al-Muttalib and their names.
story: was Nawfal, though some say that was Bajl. These, then, were the paternal uncles of the Prophet (SAAS). "His aunts numbered six; they were Arwa, Barra, Umayma, Safiyya, 'Atika, and Umm Hakim, she being al-Bayda', 'the white woman'. We will talk of all of these later, if God wills it.
lessons: Knowing one's lineage and family history is a form of gratitude to those who came before us.
