Family ties, even when complex, form the foundation of community and identity.
Recording and preserving family history is a trust passed between generations.
practical_advice: Take time to learn about your family history and the sacrifices of those who came before you.
Reach out to elderly relatives who can share stories of your family's past before those memories are lost.
Write down or record family stories so they can be preserved for future generations.

title: The Origin of Abd al-Muttalib's Name: From Shayba to Abd al-Muttalib
summary: The story of how Abd al-Muttalib, grandfather of the Prophet, was born in Medina, raised by his maternal uncles, and was secretly taken to Mecca by his uncle al-Muttalib, where he was mistakenly called 'Abd al-Muttalib' meaning 'servant of al-Muttalib', a name that stuck with him for life.
story: "All of these were children of 'Abd al-Muṭlib, whose own name was Shayba, 'grey', so known because of his grey hair, alternatively his name Shayba, meant 'the one praised for his generosity'. However, he was known as 'Ahd al-Muffalih because when his father Hashim had passed through Medina on a tradingmission to Syria, he had stayed at the house of 'Amr h. Zayd b. Labid b. H a r m b. Khidnsh b. Khindaf b. 'Adi b. al-Najjar al-Khazraji al-Najjzi who was the leader of his tribe. H%hiwas much taken with 'Amr's daughter Salma and asked her father to engage her in marriage. He did so but on condition that she should remain there with him. Some say that the condition was that she should only give hirth there with h i in Medina. When Hsshim returned from Syria he consummated marriage with her and took her with him to Mecca. When he next went away on business she was pregnant and he took her with him, leaving her in Medina. Hashim then went off to Syria but died at Ghazza . When SdmZ gave hirth to her child she named h i Shayba. He remained there with his maternal uncles of the Bano 'Adi b. al-Najja for seven years. "Then his uncle al-Mugahb b. 'Abd Manzf came and secretly took him away from his mother and returned with him to Mecca. When people there saw them both on his mount they asked him who the boy was and he replied, LMy'Abd, my servant!' Then they all came up and welcomed the boy, addressing him consequently as 'Abd d-Muṣalib, i.e. al-Mu$plib's servant, and the name stuck to hi."
lessons: Names and identities can be shaped by circumstance, yet a person's true worth comes from their character and deeds.
Being separated from one's origins does not diminish one's greatness or destiny.
Even confusion and mistaken identity can become part of a noble legacy.
practical_advice: When you feel your identity is misunderstood or mislabeled by others, focus on building your character rather than correcting every misconception.
If you have been separated from your roots, seek to reconnect with your heritage at whatever stage of life you are in.
Reflect on the unexpected ways that events in your life, even confusing ones, may be shaping a meaningful future.

title: Abd al-Muttalib's Rise to Leadership and the Rediscovery of Zamzam
summary: Abd al-Muttalib rose to become the most honored chief of Quraysh, took on the rights of providing water and food to pilgrims, and rediscovered the well of Zamzam which had been buried since the era of Jurhum. He also became the first to decorate the Ka'ba with gold.
story: "He rose to a great position of leadership among Quraysh, becoming their most honoured chief. They united behind him and in him rested the rights of al-saqzya and al-r~$&da;~ as had been the case with al-Muwib. It was al-Muttalib who dug out the well zamzam, which had been covered up since the Jurhum era. He was the first to decorate the ka'ba with gold, overlaying its doors with those two golden gazelles he found in zamzam along with the two whitemetal swords."
lessons: True leadership is built on serving others, not on titles or wealth.
Hidden blessings, like the buried well of Zamzam, may be waiting to be rediscovered through perseverance and effort.
A person who unites their community and serves them selflessly leaves an enduring legacy.
practical_advice: Look for ways to serve your community without expecting recognition or reward in return.
Do not give up when blessings or opportunities seem hidden or buried; persist in seeking them.
Invest your resources and talents in things that will benefit others long after you are gone.

title: Hashim: The Crusher of Bread, Founder of the Two Trade Caravans
summary: Hashim, great-grandfather of the Prophet, earned his name by crushing bread with meat and broth to feed the people of Mecca during years of famine, and was the first to institute the two famous trade caravans of winter and summer.
story: According to Ibn Hisham, 'Abd al-Mu@b was brother to Asad, Nadla, Aba Sayfi, Uayya, Khalida, Ruqayya, al-Shaffa' and Da'ifa. All these were children of Hiishim. His real name was 'Amr, but he was called Hiishim, i.e. "the crusher", because he would crush bread with meat and broth for his people during years of famine, as Mapad b. Ka'b al-Khuza'i said in his ode; the poem is alternatively attributed to al-Zaba'rii Wdid 'Abd All&. The verses are: '"Amr is he who crushed the bread and m a t for his people, the people of Mecca suffering drought and hunger. He received charge of both expeditions, the winter caravan and that of the summers." This related to his having been the first to institute the two expeditions, of winter and of summer; he was the oldest of his father's children.
lessons: Generosity during times of hardship is one of the highest forms of nobility.
A single act of consistent, selfless giving can define a person's legacy for centuries.
Taking initiative to solve community problems, as Hashim did with the trade caravans, is a mark of true leadership.
practical_advice: When those around you are struggling, look for practical and immediate ways to help, even if it is something as simple as sharing a meal.
Do not wait for others to solve community problems; step forward and organize solutions where you see a need.
Let your generosity be remembered not for its grandeur but for its consistency and sincerity.

title: The Four Famous Brothers of Abd Manaf and Their Safe-Conduct Agreements
summary: The four famous brothers, Hashim, Abd Shams, Nawfal, and al-Muttalib, sons of Abd Manaf, each negotiated safe passage for Quraysh's trade caravans with different ruling powers across the known world, securing the prosperity and safety of their people.
story: Ibn Jarir related that he was the twin brother of 'Abd Shams, and that Hsshim came forth with his leg attached to his twin brother's head, which was only separated after blood had flowed between them. People said that that signified wars between their progeny; and so there occurred the confrontation between Bana al-'Ahbits and Banti Umayya b. 'Abd Shams in the year 133 AH. Their third brother, al-Mut@lib, was his father's youngest boy; their mother was 'Atika, daughter of Murra, son of Hild. The fourth of the siblings was Nawfal, born to another mother named Waqida daughter of 'Amr of the tribe of Mzin. These brothers ruled their people after their father; they were known as al-mujiriirz, "the protectors". That is because they negohated safe conduct for their people of Quraysh with the kings of the neighbouring lands so that they could enter them for trading. Hitshim won safe passage from the rulers of Syria, Byzantium, and Ghasszn; 'Abd Shams won the same from the Great Negus, the emperor of Abyssinia; Nawfal gained it for them from the Chosroes, while al-Muttalib received it from the h i g s of Himyar. It was of them that a poet composed the lines, "0 you, man, changing your luggage, have you not dismounted at the family of 'Abd Manaf?"
lessons: True protection of one's community requires wisdom, diplomacy, and strategic thinking.
Brothers who work together with unity and complementary strengths can achieve what none could accomplish alone.
Even signs of future conflict, as in the birth of Hashim and Abd Shams, do not negate the good that individuals can accomplish in their own lifetimes.
practical_advice: When facing challenges that require help from others, approach negotiations with respect and diplomacy rather than confrontation.
Work alongside your family or community members by dividing responsibilities according to each person's strengths.
Do not let the fear of future conflict prevent you from building alliances and relationships that benefit your community today.

title: Qusayy: The Gatherer of Quraysh and Guardian of the Ka'ba
summary: Qusayy, an ancestor of the Prophet, united the scattered tribes of Quraysh, removed the power of Khuza'a from the Ka'ba, established the systems of feeding and watering pilgrims, and became absolute ruler over Quraysh, earning the title 'the gatherer together'.
story: All were descendants of Qusayy, whose given name was Zayd. He was known as Qusayy because his mother had married Rabi'a b. war- b. Wdhra after his father; Rabi'a travelled with her to his own land while her son was still young, and Qugayy was so named because of this, i.e. "little stranger". He later, when fully grown, returned to Mecca and united Quraysh into one, having gathered them together from all parts of the land. He removed Khuza'a's power from the House (the ka'ba) and expelled them from Mecca, thus restoring what was right. He became absolute ruler over Quraysh and controlled the feeding and watering of the pilgrims, systems he established, along with the sadzna, the hijzba, and a l - l i ~ a . And his house was where public meetings were held, all of which is explained above. It is due to this that a poet spoke the line: "Quṣayy, by my life, was known as a 'gatherer together'; through him God gathered up all the tribes of Fihr."
lessons: Feeling like a stranger or outsider in your early years does not prevent you from becoming a unifying force for your people.
Restoring what is right, even when it requires difficult action, is the duty of those given authority.
A person who brings people together from division leaves a legacy far greater than one who rules by force alone.
practical_advice: If you have felt like an outsider or displaced in life, know that your experiences may be shaping you for a greater role in your community.
When you see division around you, ask yourself what practical steps you can take to bring people together.
Serve others through hospitality and generosity, as Qusayy did through the systems he established for pilgrims, as a way of earning lasting respect.

title: Ka'b Announcing the Coming of the Prophet Every Friday
summary: Ka'b, an ancestor of the Prophet in the line of his forebears, used to address his people every Friday and announce to them the glad tidings of the coming of the Messenger of God, reciting poetry about it.
story: He was the brother of Zuhra, both being sons of Kihb, who was the brother of Taym and of Yaqza Abo Makhzm. All three were sons of Murra, who was the brother of 'Adi and H u~ayy. All of these were progeny of Ka'b. It was he who used to address his people each Friday and announce to them the glad tidings of the coming of the Messenger of God (SAAS), reciting poetry to that effect as we have given above.
lessons: The coming of the Prophet was foretold and anticipated by the righteous among his ancestors, affirming that divine guidance follows a profound and deliberate plan.
Hope in God's promise sustains communities across generations, even before the fulfillment of that promise arrives.
Consistently speaking truth and sharing glad tidings is a form of worship and communal service.
practical_advice: Make it a habit to share words of hope, encouragement, and good news with your community on a regular basis.
Hold firm to your belief in God's promises even when their fulfillment seems far away.
Use gatherings with family or community to remind one another of higher purposes and shared values.

title: The Universal Lineage of the Prophet Through All Arab Tribes
summary: Ibn Abbas and scholars affirmed that every subtribe of Quraysh and all the Adnani Arab tribes shared a linear connection with the Messenger of God, making his lineage a point of unity for all Arabs.
story: That is why Ibn 'Abb% and others comment on the Qur'anic verse, "Say, I shall not ask of you all any other reward than that of love for those related" (su'rat al-Shhra, XLII, v.23) by implying that there was not a single subtribe of Quraysh devoid of linear connection with the Messenger of God (SAAS). Ibn 'Abbas, God be pleased with him, was correct and conservative in saying as he did. Namely, that all the 'Adrian Arab tribes relate to him through their fathers' lines and many through their mothers' lines as well, as Muhammad b. I s h~q and others showed through their tracing of all his mothers and of those of the rest back through their maternal l i e s at great length.
lessons: The Prophet's connection to all people was not only spiritual but also familial, reflecting God's wisdom in choosing the most universally connected person as His final Messenger.
Love for those who are connected to us by blood or community is a natural human bond that Islam honors and elevates.
Universal connection and belonging are values that transcend tribal divisions and unite humanity.
practical_advice: Reflect on the bonds of family and community that connect you to others, and use those bonds as a source of warmth and belonging.
Do not underestimate the significance of your lineage and heritage as a source of identity and purpose.
Practice expanding your sense of connection beyond your immediate circle to include the broader human family.

title: The Prophet's Address on His Noble Lineage
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) delivers an address affirming his noble lineage, stating he was born from true marriage across all generations and was placed by God in the best of peoples, tribes, and clans.
story:
"It reached the Prophet (SAAS) that some men of Kinda were claiming relationship to him, so he stated, 'Al-'Abbas and Abu Sufyan b. Harb would say that only to make us look bad; we will never deny our forefathers. We are Banu al-Nadr b. Kinana.'"

The account states that the Prophet gave an address as follows: "I am Muhammad son of 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Muttalib b. Hashim b. 'Abd Manaf b. Qusayy b. Kilab b. Murra b. Ka'b b. Lu'ayy b. Ghalib b. Fihr b. Malik b. al-Nadr b. Kinana b. Khuzayma b. Mudrika b. Ilyas b. Mudar b. Nizar. And whenever people divided off into two groups God placed me in the better. I was born from my two parents and was tainted by none of the debauchery of the era before Islam. I was the product of true marriage, not fornication, right down from Adam to my father and my mother. I am the best of you in spirit and the best of you in parentage."
lessons: Your identity and worth are defined by your integrity and character, not by how others choose to label or demean you.
A person of noble character does not need to deny their origins or fabricate connections — truth in one's lineage reflects truth in one's heart.
When faced with attempts to diminish your standing, responding with calm clarity and factual truth is more powerful than anger or defensiveness.
practical_advice: When others attempt to distort your identity or background, respond with composed confidence grounded in what you know to be true about yourself.
Reflect on your own lineage and family history as a source of grounded identity, not pride — knowing where you come from can provide stability in difficult moments.
If you feel your reputation or character is being attacked unfairly, seek counsel from trusted people and address the matter directly rather than allowing the falsehood to spread unchallenged.

title: The Prophet's Statement: Born of True Marriage, Not Fornication
summary: Multiple transmitted accounts affirm the Prophet Muhammad's (SAAS) repeated statement that he was born of true marriage across every generation from Adam to his parents, untainted by the moral corruption of the pre-Islamic era.
story:
'Abd al-Razzaq stated that Ibn 'Uyayna informed him, from Ja'far b. Muhammad, that his father Abu Ja'far al-Baqir making reference to the Qur'anic verse, "A messenger has come to you from yourselves" (surat al-Tawba, IX, v.128) said that, "He was not tainted at all by his birth during the jahiliyya, the pre-Islamic era"; and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I was the product of true marriage, not fornication."

The same was related by al-Bayhaqi from al-Hakim, from al-Asamm, from Muhammad b. Ishaq al-Sarraj, from Yahya b. Abu Bukayr, from 'Abd al-Ghaffar b. al-Qasim, from Ja'far b. Muhammad, from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "God produced me from a true marriage, not from fornication."

Ibn 'Adi related it with a complete transmission chain as follows: "Ahmad b. Hafs related to us, as did Muhammad b. Abu 'Amr al-'Adani al-Makki, as did Muhammad b. Ja'far b. Muhammad b. 'Ali b. al-Husayn, saying, 'My father testified to me, on the authority of his father and his grandfather, as having heard from 'Ali b. Abu Talib that the Prophet (SAAS) said, "I was the product of true marriage, not fornication, from Adam right on up to when my father and mother had me. And I was not at all tainted by the fornication of the jahiliyya."'"

Hushaym said, "Al-Madini related to me, from Abu al-Huwayrith, from Ibn 'Abbas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Nothing of the fornication of the jahiliyya was born in me; I was born from nothing but a true marriage, just like the true marriage of Islam.'"

Muhammad b. Sa'd said, "Muhammad b. 'Umar informed us, quoting Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah b. Muslim, from his uncle al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'I was born from a true marriage, not fornication.'"
lessons: Moral integrity can be traced through generations — the character and choices of those who came before us shape the legacy we inherit and carry forward.
A pure origin does not make one exempt from trials, but it does provide a foundation of dignity that cannot be taken away by others.
The Islamic emphasis on lawful marriage reflects a deeper wisdom: that the sanctity of life's beginnings matters to both individual dignity and communal health.
practical_advice: Take time to reflect on the positive values and moral commitments passed down through your family, even imperfectly — these are gifts worth honoring and continuing.
When your character or origins are questioned unfairly, remember that your worth is not determined by others' accusations but by what God knows of your heart and deeds.
If you feel shame about your background or origins, speak to a trusted mentor or counselor who can help you reframe your story through a lens of dignity rather than stigma.

title: God's Selection of the Prophet Through the Best of Generations
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) describes how God progressively selected him through the best of peoples, tribes, and clans across all of human history, from the children of Adam down to Banu Hashim.
story:
It is established in the sahih collection of al-Bukhari from a hadith of 'Amr b. Abu 'Amr from Sa'id al-Maqburi, from Abu Hurayra who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'I was sent on through the best of generations of humankind, age after age, until I received my mission in the century in which I lived.'"

In the Sahih of Muslim, from a tradition of al-Awza'i from Shaddad Abu 'Amma, from Wathila b. al-Asqa', comes the statement of the Messenger of God (SAAS): "God chose Ishmael from the children of Abraham, Banu Kinana from the tribe of Ishmael, Quraysh from the tribe of Kinana, Banu Hashim from Quraysh, and myself from Banu Hashim."

The Imam Ahmad (Ibn Hanbal) stated that Abu Nu'aym related to him, from Sufyan, from Yazid b. Abu Ziyad, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Harith b. Nawfal, from al-Muttalib b. Abu Wada'a, who said that al-'Abbas stated, "Certain things the people were saying reached the Prophet (SAAS) so he mounted the pulpit and asked, 'Who am I?' They replied, 'You are the Messenger of God.' He replied, 'I am Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Muttalib; God devised creation and made me part of his best creatures. He made them all into two groups, placing me in the better of them. He created the tribes and placed me in the best one, subdivided them into buyut (clans) and placed me in the best one. And so I am the best of you both in clan and in spirit.'"
lessons: Every person has a divinely ordained place in creation — understanding this can replace feelings of insignificance with a sense of sacred purpose.
God's selection operates not through worldly power or wealth but through purity of lineage and character, reminding us that true nobility is spiritual.
Knowing that the Prophet (SAAS) was chosen through the best of human history should inspire us to aspire to goodness in our own lineages and communities.
practical_advice: When you feel lost or purposeless, reflect on the Islamic teaching that every soul was placed in this world intentionally by God — your existence is not accidental.
Seek to understand the history of your own community and family so that you may build upon what is good and correct what needs improvement, contributing positively to those who come after you.
Cultivate gratitude for the tradition and community into which you were born, while recognizing that ultimate nobility before God comes from personal piety and good character.

title: Al-Abbas Complains of Quraysh's Cold Treatment Toward Banu Hashim
summary: Al-Abbas tells the Prophet (SAAS) that Quraysh treat Banu Hashim coldly and compared the Prophet to a date palm in a hole in the ground, prompting the Prophet's angry but measured response affirming his divine selection.
story:
Ya'qub b. Sufyan said that 'Ubayd Allah b. Musa related to him, from Isma'il b. Abu Khalid, from Yazid b. Abu Ziyad, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Harith b. Nawfal, from al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, who said, "I once said, 'O Messenger of God, when Quraysh meet one another they do so joyfully, but when they meet us it is as if we don't know them.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) became extremely angry when he heard that and said, 'By Him in whose hand is the soul of Muhammad, faith will never enter the heart of any man until he loves you all for the sake of God and his Messenger.'

"I commented, 'O Messenger of God, Quraysh sat recalling their lines of genealogy and you they compared to a date palm in a hole in the ground.'

"The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'On the day when God created man he placed me among the best of them. Then when He divided them into tribes He placed me in the best one. And when He made the buyut, clans, he placed me in the best one. And so I am the best of them in spirit and the best in clan.'"
lessons: Social rejection and contempt from one's own community is deeply painful, but one's worth is never determined by the acceptance or rejection of others.
True faith requires that we love those whom God loves, not merely those who are socially powerful or popular in our community.
When we are mocked or diminished, responding with grounded truth rather than retaliation preserves both dignity and the relationship.
practical_advice: If you experience social exclusion or cold treatment from people whose acceptance you naturally desire, remind yourself that belonging to God's chosen path is a greater honor than belonging to any social circle.

When a trusted person in your life shares their pain of rejection with you, respond with empathy first, as the Prophet responded with righteous anger on behalf of his uncle's hurt — validate the feeling before offering perspective.

Practice examining moments when you yourself may have treated others coldly or dismissively, and make a conscious effort to greet everyone — especially those of lesser social standing — with warmth and openness.

title: Abu Sufyan Insults Banu Hashim Before the Prophet's Daughter
summary: Abu Sufyan insults the Prophet's family by comparing his presence among Banu Hashim to a sweet basil plant among rotten garbage, prompting the Prophet (SAAS) to respond with visible anger and a clear statement of God's selection of his lineage.
story:
Al-Hakim and al-Bayhaqi related from a hadith from Muhammad b. Dhakwan, the uncle of the son of Hammad b. Zayd, from 'Amr b. Dinar, from Ibn 'Umar who said, "We were sitting in the courtyard of the Prophet (SAAS) when a woman passed by. Someone said, 'That's the daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS)!' Abu Sufyan then commented, 'Muhammad's presence in Banu Hashim is like a sweet basil plant amidst rotten garbage!' The woman hurried off and informed the Prophet (SAAS).

"Soon the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived, his face showing his anger. He said, 'What's the meaning of certain statements reaching me about people? God created all seven heavens and chose the highest for whomsoever of his creatures he wished to dwell there. Then he created all living things and chose from them mankind. From mankind he chose the Arabs and from them Mudar. From Mudar he chose Quraysh and from them the Banu Hashim. From Banu Hashim he chose me. I am the choicest of the chosen; so whoever loves the Arabs, it is through loving me that he loves them. Whoever hates the Arabs, it is through hating me that he hates them.'"
lessons: Hateful speech directed at a person's family strikes at the heart — the Prophet's daughter's instinct to immediately inform him reflects the importance of not silently absorbing insults alone.
God's response to the humiliation of His Prophet was not silence but clarity — divine truth is the most powerful answer to contemptuous mockery.
Even those who mock the righteous may later change, as Abu Sufyan himself eventually embraced Islam — judgment of a person's final state belongs to God alone.
practical_advice: When someone insults you or your family, do not bottle up the pain — share it with someone you trust who can respond with wisdom and support.
If you witness someone being mocked or demeaned for their background or family, speak up or report it rather than remaining passive — the Prophet's daughter's action was itself an act of courage.
When anger arises from genuine injustice, channel it as the Prophet did — not into personal retaliation, but into a clear and principled statement of truth.

title: Gabriel's Testimony on the Superiority of Prophet Muhammad and Banu Hashim
summary: Gabriel searched the entire earth and testified that no man was superior to Muhammad and no tribe superior to Banu Hashim, a tradition recorded by al-Bayhaqi and substantiated by multiple narrations.
story: Al-Aim and al-Bayhaqi recounted from a hadith of Mnsa b. CUbayda that 'Amr b. CAbd All& b. Nawfal related to him from al-Zuhri, from Abii Wsama or Abii Salama, from CA'isha, God bless her, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "Gabriel said to me, 'I searched the earth from east to west but found no man superior to Muhammad; and I searched the earth from east to west but found no tribe superior to the Banii Hashi.'" The shaykh al-Bayhaqi said, "These traditions, even though the transmitters of some are unreliable, do substantiate one another. And the message of all does relate back to the hadith of Wathila b. al-Asqac. But God knows best."
lessons: Divine recognition of one's worth does not require human validation; God's assessment of our value transcends worldly judgments.
Being part of a righteous community or family is a blessing, but individual character and proximity to God define true nobility.
Humility remains essential even when granted high status, as the Prophet himself said such things 'without boasting'.
practical_advice: When you feel undervalued or overlooked by others, remind yourself that God sees your worth completely and accurately.
Reflect on the good qualities of your community and family as sources of strength, without letting pride in lineage lead to arrogance.
Journal about moments where you felt recognized or affirmed, and connect those feelings to gratitude toward God rather than self-congratulation.

title: Abu Talib's Poem Praising the Prophet and the Honor of His Lineage
summary: Abu Talib composed verses celebrating the Prophet's noble lineage through Abd Manaf and Hashim, affirming that despite Quraysh's plots against them, their family stood firm in protecting justice and the sanctuary.
story: On this subject Abu Talib spoke the following verses in praise of the Prophet (SAAS): "Were Quraysh to gather one day to boast, CAbd Manaf would be their heart and core, And if the chiefs of 'Abd Manaf were assessed, their most noble and ancient of line would be from Hashim, Were they to boast one day, then Muhammad would be the one chosen from their inmost, and their pride. Quraysh, all and sundry of them, plotted against us, but they did not succeed and were confounded; Since ancient days we reject injustice and if others turned away in contempt we set them straight. Each day we protect their sanctuary from evil, repelling from their refuges all seeking them; By us the dried-out branches are revived, their roots becoming moist and growing under our protection."
lessons: Standing firm against injustice, even when the entire community plots against you, is a mark of true nobility of character.
Family bonds rooted in shared values and righteous principles provide resilience against external hostility and persecution.
Those who protect others and revive what is withered are themselves a source of life and strength for their communities.
practical_advice: When facing collective opposition or social pressure, identify the values and principles you refuse to compromise on, and let those anchor your resolve.
Lean on family or close community members who share your values during times of external opposition rather than facing hardship alone.
Look for ways you can be a source of support and revival for others around you, as this gives purpose and strength during difficult times.

title: Al-Abbas Recites Verses of Praise for the Prophet at Tabuk
summary: At the camp of Tabuk, al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib sought permission to recite verses praising the Prophet, who encouraged him warmly, and al-Abbas delivered a poem tracing the Prophet's honored journey from the loins of Adam through Noah and Abraham to his birth.
story: Abu al-Sakn Zakariyya b. Yahya al-Tai said in the famous anecdote ascribed to him: "Umar b. Abu Zuhr b. Hasin said, on the authority of his grandfather Hamid b. Munhib, that his grandfather Khuraym b. Aws stated, 'I went away to join the Messenger of God (SAAS) and came to him at his camp at Tabuk. I had become a Muslim. I heard al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib say, "Oh Messenger of God, I would like to speak verses in your praise." The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Speak on and may God not block your tongue." So he recited, "Before this you were pleasantly in the shade (of paradise) in a place where leaves clustered above. Then you came down to this land; no human you, no embryo, no clot. A droplet, rather, riding (Noah's) vessel, the deep having bridled an eagle and his family. Being carried from loins to womb, one world passing, another surface appearing, Until your supreme house encompassed Khindaf so lofty (in honour), beneath her all other beings. When you were born the earth glowed, the horizon shining with your light. So we are in that illumination and that light, making our way upon the paths of righteousness."'"
lessons: Expressing gratitude and love for those who have guided us toward righteousness is a spiritually uplifting act.
The Prophet's welcoming response to heartfelt praise teaches us to receive sincere appreciation with grace and warmth.
Recognizing the divine light that illuminates the path of righteousness helps us stay oriented toward goodness even in dark times.
practical_advice: Find meaningful ways to express appreciation and gratitude for those who have positively shaped your spiritual or personal journey.
When someone offers you sincere praise or encouragement, receive it humbly and use it as motivation to continue doing good.
In moments of confusion or darkness, recall figures or teachings that have been a light in your life and let that memory guide your next step.

title: Ibn Abbas Questions the Prophet About His Existence Before Adam
summary: Ibn Abbas asked the Prophet where he was when Adam was still in paradise, and the Prophet smiled broadly and described his blessed journey through the loins of prophets and chaste wombs, never through fornication, until his birth.
story: Ibn Abbas who said, "I posed the following question to the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Where were you, may my parents be your ransom, when Adam was still in paradise?' He replied, smiling so broadly all his teeth could be seen, 'I was in his loins. Then the vessel carried me into the loins of my father Noah, who cast me out into the loins of my father Abraham. My parents never once engaged in fornication. God went on transporting me from respectable loins to chaste wombs, pure and unsullied, and whenever a line split I was always in the better half. God has covenanted prophethood in me and made Islam my pact. He made mention of me in the Torah and in the Bible. All the prophets made clear my qualities; the earth brightens with my light and the dark clouds with my face. He taught me His Book and gave me honour in His heaven. He cut off for me one of His own names; the enthroned One is Mahmud, while I am Muhammad and Ahmad. He promised me that He would reward me with a garden and with al-kawthar, the river of paradise, that He would make me the first intercessor and the first for whom there was intercession. He drew me forth, moreover, out of the best marriage for my people, and they are those who give much praise, enjoin good deeds and prohibit sin.'"
lessons: Every person's existence is part of a divine plan that predates their birth; God's knowledge and care for us is not limited to our visible life.
Purity of character and lineage, rooted in obedience to God, is something to be consciously cultivated and passed forward.
God's promises are certain; the rewards He has prepared for the righteous, including intercession and paradise, are real and worth striving toward.
practical_advice: When feeling uncertain about your place or purpose in the world, reflect on the idea that your existence is not accidental but part of a larger divine plan.
Consider the legacy of goodness you are passing on to those who come after you, whether children, students, or those you mentor.
When feeling hopeless about the future, remind yourself of the divine promises of reward, intercession, and paradise, and let that hope motivate present action.

title: The Name Muhammad: Divinely Preserved and Prophesied Before His Birth
summary: God uniquely preserved the name Muhammad so that no one bore it before the Prophet's time except a small group of Arabs who named their sons so after hearing prophecies of a coming prophet, yet none of those individuals ever claimed prophethood.
story: Similarly with "Muhammad" as well; none of the Arabs or any other people was named by it until shortly before his birth word was spread that a prophet would be sent whose name would be "Muhammad". And so a small Arab group did give their sons that name hoping that one of them would be he. But God knows best where to place His message. These were "Muhammad" b. Uhayha b. al-Julah al-Awsi, "Muhammad" b. Maslama al-Ansari, "Muhammad" b. Barat al-Bakri, "Muhammad" b. Sufyan b. Mujashi', "Muhammad" b. Humrum al-Ju'fi, and "Muhammad" b. Khoza'i al-Sulmi; no seventh person was so named. It is said that the first person named "Muhammad" was Muhammad b. Sufyan b. Mujashi'; in Yemen they say, however, that it was "Muhammad" b. al-Yakhmud of Azd. Moreover, God protected those so named from claiming the prophethood or others from claiming it for them. Not one of them showed any manifestation that would cause anyone to suspect of such, until the two signs were realized in him (SAAS) "signs that could not be denied", that being His expression.
lessons: God's plans unfold with precision; even the name of His final messenger was divinely protected and reserved for the right time.
Human anticipation and searching for guidance is natural, but true divine appointment cannot be counterfeited or assumed.
Trusting that God places His trust where it truly belongs teaches us patience when we feel overlooked despite our aspirations.
practical_advice: When you feel passed over for a role or recognition you hoped for, reflect on the truth that divine appointment comes at the right time to the right person, not by human calculation.
Avoid the temptation to force yourself into a role God has not designated for you; seek sincerely and trust the outcome to Him.
Use the example of divine precision in prophecy to build your confidence that God is aware of every detail of your life and has a specific plan for you.

title: The Prophet Was Born on a Monday: A Day of Divine Significance
summary: Multiple authentic narrations confirm that the Prophet Muhammad was born on a Monday, the same day he received prophethood, emigrated to Madinah, arrived there, and died, making Monday a day of profound spiritual significance in his life.
story: He was born on a Monday. This is from what is related by Muslim in his sahih collection, from a hadith told by Ghaylan b. Jarir from 'Abd Allah b. Ma'bad al-Zimmani, from Abu Qatada to the effect that a bedouin said, "O Messenger of God, what do you say about fasting on Mondays?" He replied, "That is the day I was born, and the day I was first sent revelation." The Imam Ahmad said, "Musa b. Dawud related to us, as did Ibn Lahi'a, from Khalid b. Abu Umran, from Hanash al-San'ani, from Ibn Abbas who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born on a Monday. He received the prophethood on a Monday, left Mecca for Medina as an emigrant on a Monday, arrived at Medina on a Monday, died on a Monday, and raised up the stone on a Monday.'" Only Ahmad gives the tradition thus, but 'Amr b. Bukayr related it from Ibn Lahi'a with an addition of the words, "The Qur'anic Surat al-Ma'ida (Ch. V) was revealed on a Monday, with the words, 'This day have I completed for you your religion' (V, v.3)." 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr recounted, from Kurayb, from Ibn Abbas who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born and died on a Monday." Similar reports from different lines of transmission also have Ibn Abbas saying that he was born on a Monday. This, then, is a matter without dispute, namely that he was born on a Monday.
lessons: Significant events in a believer's life can carry divine patterns and meaning that only become clear in retrospect.
The Prophet himself used the day of his birth as a reason for gratitude and devotion, showing us how to honor blessings through worship rather than mere celebration.
Consistency and alignment in a life of purpose, from birth to death, is a mark of a life fully surrendered to God.
practical_advice: Establish a weekly spiritual practice or moment of reflection, such as on Mondays, to connect with gratitude for your own birth and purpose.
Look back at significant dates or patterns in your life with the intention of seeing God's hand in your personal story, which can build trust and reduce anxiety.
Emulate the Prophet's practice of voluntary fasting or increased worship on meaningful days as a way of expressing gratitude for God's blessings in your life.

title: The Disputed Date of the Prophet's Birth in Rabi al-Awwal
summary: Scholars differ on the exact date of the Prophet's birth within the month of Rabi al-Awwal, with opinions ranging from the 2nd to the 12th of the month, though all agree it was in the Year of the Elephant.
story: Most people believe that the birth occurred in the month of Rabi al-Awwal, and, it is said, on the second day thereof. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr stated this in his exhaustive study and al-Waqidi related it from Abu Ma'shar Nujayh b. 'Abd al-Rahman al-Madani. Some say that it occurred on the 8th of the month. Al-Humaydi related it so from Ibn Hazm. Malik, Uqayl, and Yunus Ibn Yazid, and others related this from al-Zuhri, from Muhammad b. Jubayr b. Mut'im. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr recounted that the historians had verified this, and the great Muhammad b. Musa al-Khawarizmi so determined it. The hafiz Abu al-Khamb b. Dihya considered it likely in his book: al-Tanwir fi Mawlid al-Bashir al-Nadhir (Enlightenment on the Birth of the Warner and the Bearer of Glad Tidings). And it is said, in an account transmitted by Ibn Dihya in his book, that it occurred on the 10th of the month; Ibn 'Asakir related this from Abu Ja'far al-Baqir as did Mujahid from al-Sha'bi, as above. The birth is also said to have come on the 12th of the month. Ibn Ishaq states this. Ibn Abu Shayba relates this too in his compilation, on the authority of 'Affan, from Sa'id b. Mina, from Jabir and from Ibn 'Abbas, the latter two having stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born in the 'year of the elephant', on Monday the 12th of Rabi al-Awwal. On that same day he received his mission, was carried up to heaven, emigrated from Mecca."
lessons: Scholarly disagreement on details does not undermine the certainty of the core truths of faith; the Prophet's birth and mission are beyond dispute even when dates differ.
Approaching uncertainty with humility and continued inquiry, rather than false certainty or dismissiveness, is the mark of sound scholarship and sound character.
The blessing of the Prophet's birth is to be celebrated in the heart through love and gratitude, not reduced to debates about a calendar date.
practical_advice: When faced with uncertainty or conflicting information in your personal or spiritual life, focus on what is certain and well-established rather than becoming paralyzed by what is disputed.
Practice intellectual humility by acknowledging when you do not know something for certain, and model that honesty for those around you.
Channel your love and appreciation for the Prophet into consistent daily practice of his Sunnah rather than focusing energy on peripheral debates.

title: Scholarly Debate on the Exact Date of the Prophet's Birth
summary: Islamic scholars and transmitters of hadith report numerous differing opinions about the exact date, month, and year of the Prophet Muhammad's birth, ranging from the 8th to the 17th of Rabi' al-Awwal, with some even suggesting Ramadan or dates ranging from 15 years before to 40 years after the Year of the Elephant.
story: 10th of the month; Ibn 'Asakiu related this from Abti Ja'far al-B~qiras did Mujdid from alSha'bi, as above. The birth is also said to have come on the 12th of the month. Ibn Ishxq states this. Ibn Aba Shayha relates this too in his compilation, on the authority of 'Affa, from Sacid b. Minx', from Jabir and from Ibn 'Abbas, the latter two having stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born in the 'year of the elephant', on Monday the 12th of Rabical-Awwal. On that same day he received his mission, was carried up to heaven, emigrated from Mecca, and died. And this is what is widely known to most people; but God knows best." He is also said to have been born on the 17th, as Ibn Dihya reported from some of the Shi'a. But also on the 8th, Ibn Dihya so stating from the writing of son of the hi$? Aba Muhammad the mazir, the government minister, Abii Me, b. Hazm, from his father. What is correct about the account of Ibn Hazm, the first, that it occurred on the 8th, is as al-Humaydi recounted from him; this is best established. There is a second account, namely that he was born in Ramadan. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr transmitted this from al-Zubayr b. Bakkar. This is a very strange statement. Its basis is the fact that he doubtless received revelation in Ramadan, at the beginning of the 40th year of his life. And so his birth should be in Ramadan. However, this is debatable. But God knows best. Khaytham b. Sulayman, the ha$&, related from Khalaf b. Muhammad Kirdaws al-Wasip, from al-Mu'alla b. 'Abd al-R&ma, from 'Abd al-@amid b. Ja'far, from al-Znhri, from Wbayd Allah b. 'Abd All&, from Ibn 'Abb% who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born on a Monday in Rub8 al-Awwal and revelation of his prophethood came down to him on Monday, the first of Rub8 al-Awwafi also the Qur'a chapter al-Baqara (The Cow; 11) was revealed on a Monday in Rabical-Awwal." This is very strange. It is Ibn 'Asakir who related it. Al-Zubayr b. Bakks stated, "His mother became pregnant with him during uyyam al-tashriq, the celebratory feast in the vale of AbB Talib, near al-jumrat al-wusp, the central stoning pile at Mina. He was born in Mecca in the house known as that of Muhammad b. Ynsuf, brother of al-Hajjaj b. Ytisuf, on the 12th of Ramadan." The ha$& Ibn 'Asair recounted this from a line through Muhammad b. W t h m a b. Wqba b. Makram, from al-Musayyab b. Sharik, from Shu'ayb h. Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was collceived on the day of 'Ashiirt?, the 9th or 10th day of Muhmam, and he was born on Monday, the 12th of Rarnadzn in the 23rd year after the attack on Mecca by the troops with elephants." Other sources relate that al-Khayzuran, the mother of H a a n al-Rashid, when she performed the pilgrimage, ordered the conversion of this house into a mosque. It is known by her name to this day. Al-Suhayli gave the date of his birth as the 20th of Nisan, April. This was the most appropriate time and season; and that was in the year 882 of the era of Alexander, according to those who use astronomical almanacs. And they claim that the ascendant was at twenty degrees from Capricorn, and that Jupiter and Saturn were joined together at a position within three degrees of Scorpio, a position in the middle of the heavens. This was in correspondence with the stages of Aries. The birth occurred as the moon first arose at the beginning of the night. Ibn Dihya transmitted all this; but God knows best. According to Ibn Ishaq, his birth occurred in the year of the elephant. And this is what is well known to the people at large. Ibrahim b. al-Mundhir al-Hizm said, "And this is something about which our scholars have no doubt, the fact that he was born in the year of the elephant; and that he received his mission as a prophet at the beginning of the 40th year after the attack with elephants." Al-Bayhaqi related this as well from a tradition of Abii Ishaq al-Sabici, from Sacid h. Jubayr from Ibn Abbas who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born in the year of the elephant attack." Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, "Al-Mugalib h. 'Abd All& b. Qays b. Makhrama related, from his father, from his grandfather Qays b. Makhrama, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SPLAS) and I were born in the year of the elephant attach; our births were contemporaneous.'" Wthman, may God be pleased with him, asked Q u b ~ t hb. Ashyam, brother of Bana Yacmur b. Layth, "Were you the greater (in age) or was the Messenger of God (SAAS)?" He replied, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was 'greater' than me, but I was earlier in birth. I saw the elephants' droppings when still green from a and changing (in colour)." Al-Timidhi related this, as did al-H&ii from a hadith of Muhammad b. Ishaq. Ihn Ishaq stated: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was 20 in the year of Cukii?." He also said, "A1-fiiiri9 took place 20 years after the elephant attack. The building of the kacba was done 15 years after al-$jar, and the divine mission came about 5 years after its building." Muhammad b. Jubayr b. Mutcim stated, "Ukaz occurred 15 years after the 'elephant' battle, the building of the kacba 10 years thereafter, and the mission 15 years after that." The ha$? al-Bayhaqi related from a tradition of 'Abd al-<Azizb. Abii Thabit d-Madini as follows: "Al-Zubayr b. Mnsa related to us on the authority of Abo al-Huwayrith, saying, 'I heard CAbd al-Mdik h. Marwan say to Qubath b. Ashyam d-Kinwi al-Laythi, "O Qubath, which of you, you or the Messenger of God (SAAS) was akbar?" He replied, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was akbar (i.e. greater) than me, but I was asann, older. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was horn during the year of the elephant attack, but I was aware enough to recall my mother standing with me upon the elephant's dung while it was still changing (in colour). And the Messenger of God (SAAS) became a Prophet at the beginning of 40 years thereafter."'" Yacqiib b. Sufyxn said, "Yahya b. 'Abd Allah b. Bukayr related to us, Nucaym, meaning Ibn Maysara, related to us, from certain others, from Suwayd b. Ghafla, that the last-mentioned stated, 'I am contemporary in birth with the Messenger of God (SAAS); I was horn in the year of the elephant attack.'" Al-Bayhaqi stated, "It is related from Suwayd b. Ghatla that he said, 'I am younger than the Messenger of God (SAAS) by two years.'" Ya'qiib said: "And Ibrzhim b. al-Mundhir related to us, as did 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Aba Thabit, (and) 'Ahd All& b. Wthman b. Aba Sulayman al-Nawfali related to me, from his father, from Muhammad b. Jubayr b. Mutcim, saying, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born in the year of the elephant attack, and the Ukaz incident occurred 15 years later. The kacha was built early in the 25th year after the elephant attack, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) became a Prophet at the beginning of the 40th year after that attack.'" The outcome is that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was, according to majority opinion, born in the year of the elephant attack. Some say the birth occurred one month after that event, others say 40 days, yet others 50 days, which is the most favoured date. And according to Abii Jacfar al-Baqir, the anival of the elephants was halfway through the month of al-Muharram, while the birth of the Messenger of God (SAAS) occurred 55 days thereafter. But others say that, on the contrary, the elephant attack occurred ten years before the birth of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Ibn Abza stated this. The attack is also said to have occurred 23 years before his birth; this is what Shu'ayb b. Shu'ayb related from his father from his grandfather, as stated above. Alternatively, the birth is said to have come 30 years after the attack; Mnsa b. Wqba stated this, from al-Zuhri, God be pleased with him. And Miisa b. Wqba himself, God be pleased with him also, chose this date. Abii Zakariyya' al-'Ijlani stated that the birth came 40 years after the attack; Ibn 'Asdcir recounted this, something very strange. Even stranger is the statement of Khalifa b. Khayyar who said, "Shu'ayb b. Hibban related to me, from 'Abd al-Wabd son of Abii 'Amr, from al-Kalbi, from Abii Salih, from Ibn CAbbas as follows: 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born 15 years before the elephant attack.'" This statement is unique, objectionable, and considered weak as well. Khalifa b. Khayyq stated, "The consensus is that he was born, peace be upon him, in the year of the elephants' attack."
lessons: Scholarly disagreement and uncertainty about historical details does not diminish the significance of a great person or event; what matters most is the legacy and impact left behind.
The phrase 'but God knows best' (wallahu a'lam), repeated by scholars across centuries, teaches humility in the face of incomplete knowledge — a virtue applicable to all areas of life.
When facing confusion or contradictory information, seeking consensus and weighing evidence carefully is the path of wisdom, rather than insisting on one's own preferred view.
practical_advice: When you feel confused or overwhelmed by conflicting information in your own life, pause and remind yourself that uncertainty is a natural part of human experience — not knowing everything is okay.
Practice intellectual humility by acknowledging when you don't have all the answers, and use the phrase or spirit of 'God knows best' to release the anxiety of needing to control every outcome.
When researching an important decision, gather multiple perspectives as the scholars did, look for the consensus view, and make peace with the fact that some questions may never be fully resolved.

title: The Light Between 'Abd Allah's Eyes and the Noble Birth of the Prophet
summary: Before consummating his marriage to Aminah, 'Abd Allah ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib was seen carrying a sign of divine light between his eyes — a light that passed to Aminah upon their union, signifying the imminent arrival of the Prophet Muhammad. A woman named Umm Qattal had sought that light for herself after hearing prophecies of Muhammad's coming, but it was divinely ordained for Aminah.
story: A DESCRIPTION OF HIS NOBLE BIRTH ($AAS). It has been told above how CAbd al-Mugalib sacrificed the 100 camels, God having saved him from fulfilment of his vow to sacrifice his son 'Abd All&. This was in accord with what He had decreed of old, that the nahi al-ummi, the unlettered Prophet (SAAS) the "seal of the prophets", and the lord of all the progeny of Adam would appear. Thereafter, as explained, He arranged the marriage of 'Abd Allah to the most noble of the women of Quraysh, Amina, daughter of Wahb b. 'Abd Manaf b. Zuhra al-Zuhriyya. And when the groom took her as his wife and consummated the marriage, she became pregnant with the Messenger of God (SAAS). Umm Qattal, Raqiqa, daughter of Nawfal, sister of Waraqa h. Nawfal, saw the sign of light appear between 'Abd All&'s eyes before he united with Amina. She wished for it to be transmitted to herself, because of all the tidigs she had heard from her brother foretelling the coming of Muhammad (SAAS) and that his time was near; she therefore offered herself to 'Abd Allah. Some authorities add "To have him marry her". That does seem most likely. But God knows best. But 'Ahd AUnh resisted her and when that dazzling light had gone to Amina after he consummated marriage with her, it seems that he regretted not having accepted what Umm Qattal had offered. And so he approached her to have her repeat her offer. But she now said she had no need of him and was sad at what she had missed. She therefore recited on that subject the fine and eloquent poetry we quoted above. The chaste abstinence of 'Abd Allah was not to his own credit hut to that of the Messenger of God (SAAS); it reflected the words of God Almighty, "God knows best wherein to place His mission" @rat al-An'am, VI, v.124). The tradition, from a good transmission line, has already been given that the Prophet (SAAS) stated, "I was horn of marriage, not fornication." What is intended here is (to say) that after his mother had conceived him his father died and he remained a foetus in his mother's womb, as it is generally understood.
lessons: Divine providence arranges the circumstances of great events in ways that surpass human planning — what is meant for us cannot be taken by another, and what is not meant for us will not come to us no matter how we pursue it.

The Quranic principle that 'God knows best wherein to place His mission' reminds us that blessings and responsibilities are entrusted to those whom God has prepared, not necessarily those who seek them most eagerly.
Even the grief of missing a great opportunity, as Umm Qattal experienced, can be expressed with dignity and beauty — our feelings of loss are valid, but they need not define us.
practical_advice: When you feel you have missed an important opportunity in life, remind yourself that what is divinely decreed for you is already written — your portion will reach you, and another's blessing was never truly yours to have.

If you find yourself experiencing regret over a path not taken, try expressing those feelings creatively — through writing, poetry, or journaling — as Umm Qattal did, as a way to process and release them.

Reflect on moments in your life where things seemed to 'fall into place' without your forcing them, and use those memories to build trust that a greater plan is at work even when current circumstances feel uncertain.

title: The Death of Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib (Father of the Prophet)
summary: Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, father of the Prophet Muhammad, fell ill during a trading journey and died in Medina, leaving the Prophet as an orphan while still a foetus in his mother's womb.
story: Muhammad h. Sa'd stated that Muhammad h. CUrnar, he being al-Waqidi, related to h i , as did Masa b. 'Ubayda al-Yazidi, as did Sa'id b. Aba Zayd, from Ayyiib h. 'Abd al-RahmZin b. Aba Sa'sak, as follows: "'Ahd All& b. 'Ahd al-Mnt@lih went off to Ghazza in a Quraysh trading caravan. When they finished their business they left there and passed through Media. By then 'Ahd Allah was sick and he asked that he be left there in the company of Banii 'Adi b. al-Najjar to whom he was related. He stayed there with them sick for one month while his friends continued back to Mecca. There 'Ahd al-Mutr;llib asked them about his son 'Ahd All& and they told h i of how he was sick and they had left h i with his relatives of Banii 'Adi h. al-Najjar. 'Abd al-Munalib sent to h i his oldest son al-Harith, but the latter found that 'Ahd Allnh had died and been buried in the house of al-Nahigha. And so al-Hzrith returned to his father and told him this. 'Abd al-Musalib and his brothers and sisters all grieved terribly at his loss. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was at that time still a foetus; Ahd AUnh, son of 'Abd al-Munalih, was 25 when he died." Al-Wnqidi asserted, "This is the most firmly based of the accounts of the death of 'Abd Allah and of his age that we have." Al-Waqidi also said, "Ma'rnar related to me from al-Zuhri that 'Ahd al-Mugalib sent 'Ahd Allah to Medina to purchase dates for them but he died." Muhammad b. Saki said, "Hishm b. M~amad b. al-SS'ib al-Kalbi told us from his father as well as from 'Awma b. al-Hakam that they had both said that 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Mut@lib died after the Messenger of God (SAAS) had reached 28 mouths; and it is also said he was 7 mouths old." Muhammad b. Sa'd stated, "The first account is better established, namely that when he died the Messenger of God (SAAS) was still a foetus." Al-Zubayr b. Baliksr said, "Mulynunad b. Hasan related to me, from 'Abd al-Salam, from Ibn Kharbudh, as follows, 'Abd Allah died in Medina when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was 2 months old. His mother died when he was 4 years of age, and his grandfather when he was 8. And so he was entrusted to his uncle, Abii Taib." What al-Waqidi considered more probable and the ha& Muhammad b. Sacd wrote, was that when his father died the Messenger of God (SAAS) was still a foetus in his mother's womb. And this is the epitome and highest rank of being an orphan.
lessons: The Prophet Muhammad experienced the loss of his father before even being born, reminding us that hardship can come at any stage of life and that one's worth and destiny are not diminished by difficult beginnings.
God's plan encompasses even the most painful losses; the Prophet's orphanhood was part of a divine wisdom that shaped his character of profound empathy for the vulnerable.
Grief shared within a family and community, as Abd al-Muttalib and his children demonstrated, is a natural and honoured response to loss in the Islamic tradition.
practical_advice: When facing loss before you even had the chance to fully know someone, allow yourself to grieve that absence without guilt — mourning what could have been is a legitimate form of grief.
Remind yourself that your circumstances of birth or early loss do not define your potential; the Prophet born an orphan became the most honoured of creation.
Lean on your family or community in times of bereavement, as Abd al-Muttalib's household did together, rather than bearing grief in isolation.

title: The Vision of Amina and the Birth of the Prophet Muhammad
summary: Amina bint Wahb, mother of the Prophet Muhammad, experienced visions and signs during her pregnancy and at the moment of his birth, including a great light that illuminated the castles of Syria.
story: In the hadith literature occur the words, "and the visions of my mother who saw, as she bore me, a light came forth from her that lit up the castles of Syria." Muhammad b. Ishaq stated that Amina, daughter of Wahb and mother of the Messenger of God (SAAS), used to say that when she became pregnant with the Messenger of God (SAAS), a voice told her: "You are pregnant with the lord of this nation; when he drops to the ground (in birth) say the words, 'I invoke for h i the protection of the One God, from the evil of all who envy, from everyone committed to his oath and every prowling slave; may a champion protect me, for he is with the All-praised and All-glorious One, until I see him come into view.' And the proof of that is a light accompanying him that will fill the castles of Busm in Syria. And at his birth, then call him Muhammad, for in the Torah his name is 'Ahmad' and the hosts of earth and heaven give h i hamd, praise. In the Gospels his name is 'Ahmad', the hosts of heaven and earth giving h i hamd, praise. And his name in the Qur'an is 'Muhammad', 'the praised one.'" Muhammad b. Sa'd stated that Muhammad b. 'Umar, he being al-Waqidi, informed him that Muhammad b. 'Abd All& b. Muslim related to him, from al-Zuhri, what follows. Al-Waqidi also stated that M~Sb. 'Abda related to him the following, from his brother and Muhammad b. Ka'b al-Qurqi; as did 'Abd All& b. Ja'far al-Zuhri, from his aunt Umm Bakr, daughter of al-Miswar, from her father; as did 'Abd al-RahW b. Ibrzhim al-Mazani and Ziyad b. Hashraj, from Abii Wajza; as did Ma'mar from Abii Nujayh, from Mujzhid; as did Talha b. 'Amr from 'At? from Ibn Abbas. The accounts of some of these correlate with those of others. These accounts state that Amina daughter of Wahb said, "I became pregnant with hi (meaning the Messenger of God (SAAS)) and he caused me no difficulties up to when I gave birth to him. When he was separated from me a light emerged along with him that lit all between east and west. Then he dropped to the ground, resting upon his hands, and took up a handful of earth which he gripped tight, raising his head towards heaven." Some sources word this tradition, "he dropped down resting upon his knees and there emerged with him a light by which were illuminated the castles and market-places of Syria, even so that the necks of the camels at Busr could be seen, while he raised his head towards heaven." The hzfi* Abii Bakr al-Bayhaqi said that Muhammad b. 'Abd Allzh, the hzjiz, informed hi, quoting Muhammad b. Isma'il, quoting Muhammad b. Ishzq, and Yiinus b. Mubashshir b. al-Hasan, quoting Ya'qiib b. Muhammad al-Zuhri, quoting 'Abd al-'Aziz b. %ran, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Wthman b. Abn Sulaymm b. Jubayr b. Mut'im, from his father, from Ibn Abti Suwayd al-Thaqafi, from Wthmm b. Abii al-'AS as follows: "My mother told me that she witnessed the giving of the birth by Amina daughter of Wahb to the Messenger of God (SAAS) the night the birth occurred." She said, "All I could see in the house was light. As I looked I saw the stars coming so close that I said that they were going to fall on me!" The qadi (judge) 'Iyad recounted, from al-Shaffz, mother of 'Abd al-Rahmm b. 'Awf, that she was his midwife and that she said that when he dropped into her hands and first made a sound, she heard a voice say, "May God have mercy on you!" And (she said) that a light shone from him by which the castles of Byzantium could be seen.
lessons: The Prophet's birth was accompanied by extraordinary signs, reminding believers that God announces and honours His chosen ones in ways beyond ordinary human expectation.
Even in the midst of loss — Amina was pregnant while her husband had died — God surrounded her with signs of blessing and comfort, showing that divine mercy accompanies grief.

The fact that the Prophet's name meaning 'the praised one' was recorded across the Torah, Gospels, and Qur'an teaches that truth is consistent and that God's plan unfolds across time with perfect coherence.
practical_advice: When you are going through a difficult period such as pregnancy, illness, or grief, look for the small signs of grace and mercy around you — they are often present even in our hardest moments.

Reflect on the names and meanings associated with your faith to draw strength; the name Ahmad and Muhammad both point to gratitude and praise, reminding us to orient ourselves toward thankfulness even in hardship.
Trust that God prepares and equips those He has chosen for their roles; just as Amina was given reassurance before the birth, seek reassurance in prayer and reflection when facing the unknown.

title: Abd al-Muttalib Receives the Newborn Prophet and Praises God
summary: Upon being informed of the Prophet's birth, his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib took the newborn into the Kaba, offered prayers of thanks to God, and recited verses praising the child.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq said that when she gave birth to h i she sent her slave girl to 'Abd al-Munalib, his father having died while she was pregnant (it is also said that 'Abd AU& died when the Prophet (SAAS) was 28 months old, and God knows best which account is true) to say to him, "A boy has been born to you, so look to him." When 'Abd al-Munalib came to her she related to him what she had seen when she bore him, what she had been told about hi, and what she had been ordered to name him. So 'Abd al-Mut$lib took him in to Hubal; inside the ka'ba, and stood and prayed, thanking God the Almighty and Glorious, and speaking the verses, "Praise be to God who gave me this fine, wonderful boy, Who already in the cradle leads all boys; I entrust his safety to the pillared House (kacba), Until he becomes the epitome of young men, until I see him fully grown. I seek his protection from all who hate, and from the envious and the unstable, From those with ambition but eyeless, until I see him highly eloquent; You are he who was named in the Qur'a, in books whose meanings are well established, (Your name being) 'Abd, written upon the tongue."
lessons: Abd al-Muttalib's first response to the news of the Prophet's birth was to go to the House of God and offer thanks, demonstrating that gratitude to God is the proper response to every blessing.

Even before the Prophet could speak or act, his grandfather entrusted his protection to God alone, modelling the practice of placing those we love in God's care rather than relying solely on our own abilities.
The act of publicly declaring gratitude through poetry and prayer shows that expressing joy and thankfulness is not only permissible but noble in the Islamic tradition.
practical_advice: When you receive good news or a blessing, make your first act one of gratitude — whether through prayer, prostration, or simply saying 'Alhamdulillah' — before getting caught up in the excitement.
When you are anxious about the safety of someone you love, follow Abd al-Muttalib's example and consciously entrust them to God's protection through sincere supplication.
Find a meaningful way to mark moments of joy in your life, whether through a gathering, written words, or prayer, so that blessings are acknowledged and remembered with gratitude.

title: The Prophet Born Circumcised — Signs of Divine Honour at Birth
summary: Multiple narrations report that the Prophet Muhammad was born already circumcised and with his umbilical cord naturally severed, which his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib took as a sign of his great importance.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated, "Abn 'Abd Allah the ha$& informed us, quoting Abii Bakr Muhammad b. A h a d b. Hatim al-Darabardi, of Merv, Abii 'Ahd Allxh al-Bnshanji related to us, quoting Abn Ayyiib Sulaymzn b. Salama al-Khaba'iri, quoting Yiinus b. 'At%' h. 'Uthmm b. Rabica b. Ziyad b. al-Hzrith al-Saddaci, of Egypt, quoting al-Hakam b. Abn, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn CAbbas, from his father al-'Abb@ b. 'Abd al-Mu~alib, God bless h i , as follows. 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born circumcised and with his umbilical cord severed. His grandfather 'Abd al-Mu@b was amazed at this and found favour in him, saying, This son of mine is sure to be important. And so he was.'" There is dispute over the veracity of this. The ha& Ibn 'Asakir told it, from an account of Sufyan b. Muhammad d-Massisi, from Hushaym, from Ymus b. Wbayd, from al-Hasan, from Anas, as follows: The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "One way God honoured me was in my being born already circumcised, so that no one saw my private parts." He (Ibn 'As&) then related it on a line of transmission from al-Hasan h. 'Arafa, on the authority of Husbaym. He thereafter related it from Muhammad b. Muhammad b. Sulayman, he being al-B~ghandi, who said that 'Abd al-Ramm b. Ayyiib al-Him$ related to h i, quoting Miisa h. Abii Mnsa al-Maqdisi, that Khzlid b. Salama related to him, from Nafi', from Ibn Wmar, that the latter said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born circumcised and with his umbilical cord detached." Abii Nncaym stated that Abn Ahmad Muhammad b. A h a d al-Ghitrifi related to him, quoting al-Husayn h. Ahmad b. 'Abd Allah al-Maliki, quoting Sulaymm h. Salama al-Khabaciri, quoting Yiinus h. 'AtH', quoting al-Hakam b. Aban, quoting CIkrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, from his father al-'Abhas, as follows, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born circumcised and with his umbilical cord detached. This amazed his grandfather 'Abd d-Muw b and he found favour in him, saying, This son of mine is sure to be important. And so he was." Some authorities claim authenticity for this tradition for the lines of transmission it has; some even claim for it the status of mutawatir. However, this is debatable. It is also said that it was his grandfather 'Abd al-Mugalib who circumcised him and held a celebration for h i to which he gathered all Quraysh.
lessons: God honoured the Prophet from the very moment of his birth with signs of distinction, reminding us that those whom God elevates are often marked by qualities beyond ordinary human effort or achievement.

The Prophet himself understood and acknowledged these signs as gifts from God, not as personal pride, teaching us to receive our own unique qualities with humility and gratitude rather than arrogance.
The scholarly tradition of carefully scrutinising narrations for their chains of transmission demonstrates that intellectual honesty and rigour are themselves forms of worship in Islamic scholarship.
practical_advice: Reflect on the unique qualities and circumstances of your own birth and upbringing not as accidents but as part of a divine design, and consider how they might point toward your own purpose.
When others notice something remarkable about you, respond as the Prophet did — by attributing it to God's honour rather than personal merit.

Approach information, especially religious knowledge, with careful discernment as the scholars did, distinguishing between what is well-established and what is uncertain rather than accepting everything uncritically.

title: The Naming of the Prophet Muhammad and His Grandfather's Vision
summary: When the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) was born, his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib named him Muhammad, hoping God would praise him in heaven and creatures would praise him on earth. Strange signs surrounded his birth, including a pottery vessel splitting away from him and him being found gazing up to heaven.
story: Al-Bayhaqi said that Aba 'Abd Allāh the hāfis informed us, quoting Muhammad b. Āmil al-Qāḍī - verbally - that Muhammad b. Ismāʻīl- meaning al-Salmī - related to him that Abā Ṣāliḥ [Abd Allāh b. Ṣāliḥ] related to him that Muʻāwiya b. Ṣāliḥ told him, from Abā al-Ḥakam al-Tanūkhī as follows: "When a child was born to Quraysh they would entrust it to some Quraysh women till next morning. They would place an upside-down pottery vessel over it. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was born, 'Abd al-Muṭṭālib entrusted him to the women and they did put over him an upside-down pottery vessel. When they came to him next morning they discovered that the vessel had split into two pieces and fallen away from him. They found him there with his eyes wide open gazing up to heaven! When 'Abd al-Muṭṭālib arrived they told him, 'We never saw a newborn child like him! We discovered that the vessel had split in two away from him and found him open-eyed and gazing up to heaven!' He commented, 'Take care of him; I hope he will be of importance, or that he will achieve good.'" On the seventh day he made sacrifice for him and invited Quraysh to see him. When they had feasted, they asked, "O 'Abd al-Muṭṭālib, what have you thought of naming this son of yours you have honoured?" "I have named him Muhammad," he replied. "Why", they asked, "have you preferred him not to have one of the names of his kinsfolk?" "Because I wanted God to praise him in heaven and His creatures to praise him on earth," he replied. Language scholars say, "All that invites the qualities of good is named 'Muhammad'." As the poet said, To you, blameless as you are, I have urged on my mount, to him who is the glorious and noble master, the one 'Muḥammad' (i.e. "the laudable one"). Some scholars say that God, the Almighty and Glorious, encouraged them to name him Muhammad for the praiseworthy qualities he possessed; so that word and deed be joined and that the name and the one named be appropriately congruent in form and concept. As his uncle Abū Ṭālib composed - and the verse is also attributed to Ḥassān - "God took a part of His own name for him, to honour him; for He of the throne is named 'Maḥmūd' (i.e. he to whom praise is due) while he is 'Muḥammad'."
lessons: A noble name given with sincere intention carries profound spiritual significance, reflecting the hopes and prayers of those who bestow it.
Even at the moment of birth, signs of divine favour can manifest in ways that inspire awe and hope in those who witness them.
Choosing to align one's actions and identity with praiseworthy qualities is itself an act of worship and aspiration toward the good.
practical_advice: Reflect on the meaning behind your own name or the names you carry; let those meanings inspire you to embody goodness in your daily life.
When you witness something unexpectedly beautiful or uplifting, take a moment to pause in gratitude, as Abd al-Muttalib did, recognizing it as a sign of hope.
When naming or describing the people you love, focus on their best qualities and virtues, as this shapes how they see themselves and how the world sees them.

title: Al-Abbas Recalls the Infant Prophet Conversing with the Moon
summary: Al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib told the Prophet that he had witnessed him as an infant in the cradle whispering to the moon and gesturing to it, and it would move in the direction he indicated. The Prophet confirmed this, saying the moon would talk to him and distract him from crying, and that he could hear its prostration beneath God's throne.
story: The ḥāfiẓ Abū Bakr al-Bayhaqī said that Abū 'Abd Allāh the ḥāfiẓ informed him that Abū al-'Abbās Muḥammad b. Ya'qūb related to him, quoting Aḥmad b. Shayba al-Ramlī, quoting Aḥmad b. Ibrāhīm al-Ḥubalī, quoting al-Haytham b. Jamīl, quoting Zuhayr, from Muḥārib b. Dithār, from 'Amr b. Yathribī, from al-'Abbās b. 'Abd al-Muṭṭālib, as follows: "I said: 'O Messenger of God, it was a sign of your prophethood that encouraged me to join your religion. I saw you in the cradle whispering to the moon and gesturing to it with your finger. And it would move in the direction you indicated.' He replied, 'I would talk to it and it to me and it would distract me from crying. And I would hear its palpitations when it would prostrate beneath God's throne.'"
lessons: The universe itself is in constant communication with its Creator, and every created thing participates in a form of worship we may not always perceive.
Signs of divine favour may surround a person from their very beginning, waiting to be recognized by those with open hearts.
Moments of wonder from our earliest lives can become the turning points that bring us to faith and certainty, as they did for al-Abbas.
practical_advice: When you feel disconnected from the spiritual dimension of life, spend time in nature observing the sky, stars, or natural phenomena, allowing them to remind you of God's presence.
Reflect on early memories or formative moments that hinted at your purpose or values; these can serve as anchors of identity and faith during difficult times.
Share meaningful spiritual memories with those you trust, as al-Abbas did with the Prophet, because such stories strengthen communal bonds and reinforce belief.

title: Satan's Four Cries at Momentous Events in Islamic History
summary: Al-Suhayli related that Satan let out four cries of despair: when he was cursed by God, when he was cast from heaven, when the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) was born, and when Surah al-Fatiha was revealed.
story: Al-Suhayli related from the Qurʾān commentary of Baqī b. Makhlad, the ḥāfiẓ, that Satan let out four cries. One came when he was cursed (by God); another when he was sent down from heaven; a third when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was born; and the fourth when sūrat al-Fātiḥa, the opening verse of the Qurʾān, came down.
lessons: The forces of darkness despair most deeply at the moments when divine guidance and mercy are most powerfully manifest in the world.
The birth of a righteous individual and the revelation of sacred knowledge are events of cosmic significance that reorder the spiritual landscape.
When evil is diminished or put on the defensive, it is always in response to the emergence of truth, compassion, and divine light in the world.
practical_advice: When you feel as though negative forces or circumstances are pushing back hardest against you, consider that this resistance may itself be a sign that you are moving in a meaningful and righteous direction.
Recite Surah al-Fatiha with renewed awareness of its cosmic importance; let its words be a source of protection and grounding in your daily spiritual practice.
In moments of fear or spiritual heaviness, remind yourself that the forces of good have always caused despair in evil, and that light ultimately prevails over darkness.

title: The Jewish Scholar of Mecca Foretells the Birth of the Prophet
summary: On the night of the Prophet's birth, a Jewish scholar living in Mecca addressed a gathering of Quraysh and foretold that a prophet had been born that night, describing the mark between his shoulders. When he was shown the newborn Muhammad, he fainted upon seeing the mark and declared that prophecy had passed from Israel to the Arabs.
story: Muhammad b. Isḥāq said that Hishām b. 'Urwa would tell traditions on the authority of his father, that came from 'Āʾisha, who once said, "There was a Jew who resided in Mecca and did business there. On the night when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was born, the man addressed a gathering of Quraysh with the words, 'O Quraysh, did a birth occur among you this night?' They replied that they did not know. He then exclaimed, 'Well, God is very great; if that should have missed you, then no matter. Do look, and remember what I am telling you. This night a prophet has been born to this last nation; between his shoulders there is a mark consisting of successive lines of hair looking like a horse's mane. He will not suckle for two nights and this is because a spirit will have put his finger into his mouth and prevented him from suckling.' "The gathering broke up at this with all amazed at what he had told them. When they returned to their homes each one told his family. And they responded, 'By God, a child was born to 'Abd Allāh, son of 'Abd al-Muṭṭālib, a boy they named "Muḥammad".'
