The people then gathered and asked one another, 'Did you hear what the Jew said, and have you heard of the birth of the boy?' They then hurried off to the Jew and told him the news. He asked them to go with him, so that he could see the boy. They left together and took him in to Āmina, saying, 'Bring out your son for us.' She did so and they uncovered his back. There they saw the mark and the Jew fell down in a faint. When he came to, they asked him what was the matter with him. He replied, 'By God, prophecy has left Israel's tribe; are you glad with it, O Quraysh? He will so empower you that news of it will spread from east and west!'"
lessons: Truth manifests itself even through those who do not yet follow it; the Jewish scholar's knowledge of scripture allowed him to recognize the Prophet before the Arabs themselves fully understood the significance of the birth.
Divine signs are often recognized first by those with knowledge and preparation, reminding us that seeking sacred knowledge opens our eyes to what others may miss.

The transfer of a great blessing from one community to another should inspire humility and gratitude rather than pride, and serves as a reminder that divine favour is not inherited but earned through faith and action.
practical_advice: Invest time in learning about your own faith tradition deeply, because knowledge prepares you to recognize truth and meaning in unexpected moments and places.

When you encounter someone from a different background who points out something spiritually meaningful, receive it with openness rather than suspicion, as the Quraysh did when they listened to the Jewish scholar.
Reflect on moments in your own life when an outside perspective helped you see something important you had been missing; use those moments as reminders to stay humble and open.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit Recalls Hearing of the Prophet's Birth as a Child
summary: The poet Hassan ibn Thabit recalled that as a mature boy of seven or eight in Yathrib, he heard a Jewish man shouting to his people one morning that the star had risen beneath which 'Ahmad' would be born that night, marking his earliest memory connected to the Prophet's arrival.
story: Muhammad b. Isḥāq said that Ṣāliḥ b. Ibrāhīm b. 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. 'Awf related to him, from Yaḥyā b. 'Abd Allāh b. 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Sa'd b. Zurāra, as follows, "Certain trustworthy men of my tribe told me that Ḥassān b. Thābit once said, 'I was a mature lad of seven or eight, capable of understanding all I saw and heard, when one morning a Jew in Yathrib began shouting for the attention of his people. When they gathered about him - I heard all that myself - they asked him what the matter was. He replied, "The star has risen beneath which 'Aḥmad' will be born this night."'"
lessons: Significant spiritual events leave lasting impressions even on young minds, shaping a person's entire life trajectory.
God's plan for His creation is written in the cosmos itself, and those with knowledge of His signs can read in the natural world what others cannot.
A single overheard moment of truth can become the seed of a lifetime of faith and devotion.
practical_advice: Pay attention to formative moments and memories from your past that pointed you toward meaning and faith; revisiting them can renew your spiritual energy in the present.
Share stories of prophetic history with young people in your life, as such accounts leave indelible impressions that can anchor their faith for decades.
When you feel spiritually distant, return to the foundational stories of your tradition and allow their wonder to reconnect you with the awe you first felt when encountering them.

title: Jewish Scholars of Yathrib Discuss the Coming of the Prophet Ahmad
summary: Multiple accounts describe Jewish scholars of the Banu Qurayza, Banu al-Nadir, and Banu Abd al-Ashhal discussing signs of the coming prophet named Ahmad who would migrate to Yathrib, yet when he ultimately arrived, many denied him out of envy.
story: The ḥāfiẓ Abū Nuʿaym related in the book, Dalāʾil al-Nubuwwa (Signs of the Prophethood), from a tradition of Abā Bakr b. 'Abd Allāh al-'Āmirī, from Sulaymān b. Suhaym and Dhurayḥ b. 'Abd al-Raḥmān, both of their accounts coming from 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Abū Saʿīd, from his father, who said that he heard Abū Mālik b. Sinān say: "I came one day to talk with Banū 'Abd al-Ashhāl, with whom we then had a truce, and heard the Jew Yūshuʿ say, 'The time approaches for the coming of a prophet named Aḥmad who will arise from the sanctuary.' Khalīfa b. Thaʿlaba al-Ashhalī asked him, sarcastically, 'How might you describe him?' He responded, 'A man neither short nor tall, with fine, light eyes; he wears a full cloak and rides a donkey, carries his sword over his shoulder and to this land will he migrate.'" Abū Mālik went on, "When I returned to my own people and expressed at that time my surprise at what Yūshuʿ had been saying, I heard one of our own men comment, 'So is it Yūshuʿ who alone says that? All the Jews of Yathrib are saying the same!'" Abū Mālik continued, "I thereafter left and visited the Banū Qurayẓa whom I found in assembly discussing the Prophet (SAAS). Al-Zubayr b. Bāṭā said, 'The red star has risen and it only ever rises on the departure or emergence of a prophet. And the only one left is Aḥmad; this will be the place to which he will migrate.' And Abū Saʿīd said, 'When the Prophet (SAAS) eventually arrived my father told him this story, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "If al-Zubayr had adopted Islam, then his fellow Jewish leaders would also have done so; but they all followed him."'" Abū Nuʿaym said that Umar b. Muḥammad related to him, quoting Ibrāhīm b. al-Sindī, quoting al-Naḍr b. Salāma, quoting Ismāʿīl b. Qays b. Sulaymān b. Zayd b. Thābit, from Ibrāhīm b. Yaḥyā b. Thābit, who said that he heard Zayd b. Thābit say, "The Jewish rabbis of the Banū Qurayẓa and al-Naḍir would discuss the description of the Prophet (SAAS), and when the red star arose, they said that it was for a prophet after whom there would be no other, that his name was Aḥmad and that he would migrate to Yathrib. But when the Messenger of God (SAAS) ultimately arrived in the town they denied him, expressing their envy and disbelief."
lessons: Knowledge of truth is not sufficient on its own; the heart must be free from envy and pride in order to act on what the mind recognizes as right.
Envy is one of the most destructive spiritual diseases, capable of leading a person to deny what they know to be true.
A community that follows a leader into denial shares in the spiritual loss that denial brings, reminding us of the immense responsibility of those in positions of influence.
practical_advice: Examine your own heart honestly: are there truths you recognize intellectually but resist acting on because of ego, pride, or fear of what accepting them would cost you?
Be aware of how envy can distort your perception and judgment; when you find yourself dismissing something good, ask whether envy may be the real reason.
If you are in a position of influence, recognize that others may follow your lead into error just as readily as into righteousness; use that influence with great care and humility.

title: A Syrian Priest Foretells the Prophet's Coming to Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl
summary: A priest of Syria told Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl that a prophet had come in his land, that his star had risen, and urged him to return home, believe in him, and follow him.
story: Abū Nuʿaym and Muḥammad b. Ḥibbān said that Abū Bakr b. Abū ʿĀṣim related to them, quoting Wahb b. Baqiyya, quoting Khālid, from Muḥammad b. 'Amr, from Abū Salama and Yaḥyā b. 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Ḥāṭib, from Usāma b. Zayd who said that Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl stated, "A priest of Syria once told me, 'A prophet has come in your land. While he was arriving, his star arose. So go home, believe in him and follow him!'"
lessons: Divine guidance often reaches people through unexpected messengers and in unexpected places, reminding us that God's mercy is not confined to any one community or geography.
The call to return home and believe points to the deep connection between faith and belonging; true faith is meant to be lived out in one's own community, not in isolation.
Responding promptly to signs of guidance, rather than delaying or hesitating, is a quality of the spiritually alert person.
practical_advice: When you receive sincere advice or a meaningful sign that points you toward a better path, do not delay in responding; reflection is valuable but prolonged hesitation can cause you to miss transformative opportunities.
Seek knowledge of your faith from diverse and unexpected sources, knowing that truth can reach you through many channels if your heart is open.
Consider what it means to 'go home' spiritually: returning to your values, your community, and your foundational commitments when you have drifted from them.

title: Chosroe's Palace Shakes and the Fire of Persia Extinguishes on the Night of the Prophet's Birth
summary: On the night Prophet Muhammad was born, Chosroe's domed palace shook and 14 balconies fell, the sacred fire of Persia was extinguished for the first time in a thousand years, and Lake Sawa dried up — signs that alarmed the Persian king and his court.
story: When it was the night in which the Messenger of God (SAAS) was born Chosroe's domed palace shook and 14 balconies upon it fell down. Also the 'fire of Persia' went out, having not done so for a thousand years before, and Lake Sawa emptied. A Magian priest saw (in a dream) wild camels leading pure-bred horses that had crossed the Tigris river and spread into their land. This greatly alarmed Chosroe when he told him of it next morning; he put on a brave front and pretended lack of concern but then decided not to conceal it from his governors. He therefore summoned them, put on his crown, and sat upon his throne. Then he had them brought in and when they gathered around him he said, 'Do you know why I have summoned you?' They replied that all they knew was that their king wanted to tell them something. At that point a message came telling of the extinguishing of the fire, news that further upset the king. He then told them what he (the priest) had seen and what had alarmed him.
lessons: Divine signs often precede great transformations — what appears as disruption may be the announcement of a new mercy.
Even the most powerful empires are subject to the will of God; no worldly might can prevent what has been decreed.
Fear and anxiety in the face of the unknown are natural human responses, but seeking knowledge and counsel is the wise response to uncertainty.
practical_advice: When faced with unsettling news or unexpected change, resist the urge to panic or pretend indifference — instead, seek understanding and consult trusted people.
Reflect on moments of disruption in your life as potential turning points, not merely losses.
Remember that the rise and fall of circumstances is part of a larger divine plan; grounding yourself in this belief can reduce anxiety during turbulent times.

title: Chosroe Sends 'Abd al-Masih to the Dying Seer Satih for Interpretation
summary: Chosroe, disturbed by the portentous signs surrounding the Prophet's birth, dispatches a messenger to the renowned seer Satih in Syria to interpret the visions, only to find Satih on his deathbed.
story: The supreme priest explained, 'Last night I saw, may God save the realm, a vision.' He then recounted his vision of the camels and the king asked him how he interpreted it. He replied that it concerned some event occurring over in the territory of the Arabs who would understand it better than they themselves. Thereupon the king wrote a message, saying, 'From Chosroe, king of kings, to al-Nu'man b. al-Mundhir: Greetings. Send me some man learned in what I wish to question him about.' Al-Nu'man then dispatched to him 'Abd al-Masih b. 'Amr b. Hayyan b. Buqayla al-Ghassani. When he arrived Chosroe asked him, 'Do you have knowledge of what I wish to question you about?' 'Abd al-Masih replied, 'Let the king inform or question me as however he wishes; if I have knowledge of it (then good), otherwise I will inform him of someone who does know.' So Chosroe told him what he had been informed and 'Abd al-Masih replied, 'An uncle of mine named Satih who lives in the uplands of Syria will have knowledge of that.' Chosroe then told him to go to this man to ask him what he wanted to know and then to return with the explanation. 'Abd al-Masih left but Satih, by the time he reached him, was on his deathbed.
lessons: God's knowledge transcends the most learned of human beings; even powerful kings must seek wisdom beyond their own courts.
The pursuit of truth sometimes requires a long and difficult journey — the willingness to undertake that journey is itself a virtue.
Divine knowledge is revealed in its own time and through unexpected channels; patience in seeking understanding is essential.
practical_advice: When you are anxious about what the future holds, seek out those with wisdom and experience rather than trying to resolve uncertainty alone.
Do not be discouraged when the path to answers seems difficult or the person you sought is weakened — the answer may still come.
Trust that clarity will arrive when it is meant to; forcing resolution before its time often leads to greater confusion.

title: Satih on His Deathbed Prophesies the Coming of the Prophet and the End of Persian Rule
summary: The dying seer Satih, roused by the verses of 'Abd al-Masih, delivers a stunning prophecy confirming the fall of the Sassanian Empire and the coming of a righteous prophet from the Arabs.
story: 'Abd al-Masih left but Satih, by the time he reached him, was on his deathbed. 'Abd al-Masih greeted him and talked to him, but Satih made no reply. So 'Abd al-Masih then spoke the following verses: 'Has the noble lord of Yemen gone deaf or does he hear; or has he died, the ultimate reins (death) having him in tow. O he who determines the course, one that would exhaust anyone, your visitor is the local sheikh of the Sunan family. His mother is of the family of Dhi'b ('wolf') b. Hajan (crooked of staff, evil-eyed) vicious of fang, raucous to the ear. White, full of cloak and body, a messenger from the king of the Persians, travelling by night till deep slumber. Carried over the earth by a strong, large she-camel, tireless, unawed by thunder or vicissitudes of time. Lifting me with a bump and dropping me with a crash, bare of breast and lower back, Enveloped by a gale of manure dust, as though blasting from the two sides of a mountain.' When Satih heard those verses he raised his head and said, 'Why, it's 'Abd al-Masih, ridden here on a tireless mount, who's come to Satih, looking down at my death-bed; you've been sent by the king of the Sassanians, because of the shaking of his palace, the extinguishing of the fires, and the visions of the priest who saw wild camels leading pure-bred horses that had crossed the Tigris and spread into their lands. 'O 'Abd al-Masih, when many recitations have been made and the sceptre-bearer has appeared, the Samawa valley flooded, Lake Sawa dried up, and the fires of Persia gone out, then Syria is Syria no more for Satih. Of them there shall reign kings and queens to the number of their balconies; all that will be, will be.' At that Satih died, right then and there.
lessons: Even at the threshold of death, those given wisdom by God fulfill their duty to convey the truth — a reminder that purpose transcends personal circumstances.
The prophecy of the Prophet's coming was embedded in creation long before his birth, affirming that his mission was part of the eternal divine plan.
Empires and worldly power are temporary; only the truth endures beyond generations and civilizations.
practical_advice: When you feel that your own strength is nearly spent, remember that you may still have something of great value to offer — do not withhold it.
Reflect on how temporary all worldly power and status truly is; this perspective can free you from the anxiety of chasing or losing such things.
In moments of grief or ending, look for the signs of what is beginning — transitions often carry within them the seeds of something greater.

title: 'Abd al-Masih Returns to Chosroe with Satih's Prophecy and the King's Resigned Response
summary: Upon hearing Satih's prophecy that fourteen Sassanian kings would rule before the end of their empire, Chosroe resigns himself to the news while history confirms that this came to pass exactly as foretold.
story: So 'Abd al-Masih mounted his camel, saying the following verses: 'Get going, for you are decisive and expeditious; separation and change do not alarm you. If the rule of the Sassanians is ending (so be it), for man is all stages and periods of time, They may have achieved a status whose power mighty lions dread, Of whom was Bahram of the Tower and his brothers, the al-Hurmozan, and Sahur and Sahur.' People are indeed changeable; knowing that one of them has become destitute he is then despised and abandoned, And many are the people who are (erroneously) influenced by what they hear, as if distracted by playing flutes. However, should they see someone with riches and prosperity, then they are to him closely associated. This has been the secret of life, well preserved and repeatedly validated (they say), that's the province and concern of the unknown, And good and evil stick always as a pair; for good is pursued relentlessly, evil avoided.' When 'Abd al-Masih came in to Chosroe and told him what Satih had said, Chosroe replied, 'So, until 14 of us have ruled, many things can happen!' Ten of their kings did rule in a period of four years and the remainder ruled on until the caliphate of 'Uthman, God be pleased with him. The last of their kings, the one from whom rule was wrested, was Yazdajard b. Shahriya b. Abrawiz b. Hurmuz b. Anushirwan; it was the last mentioned in whose era the domed palace shook. His forebears had ruled for 3,164 years.
lessons: The fulfillment of prophecy across centuries is a powerful reminder that God's plan unfolds with perfect precision, regardless of what human beings arrange.
Worldly legacy, no matter how ancient or grand, is not permanent — humility before this truth is a mark of wisdom.
How one responds to inevitable change reveals character; resignation with dignity is far nobler than denial or despair.
practical_advice: When facing news that signals the end of something you have long relied upon, allow yourself to grieve but also look forward with faith rather than clinging to what cannot remain.
Reflect on the cycles of rise and fall in your own life — each ending has been followed by a new chapter, often one you could not have anticipated.
Do not measure your worth by positions, empires, or achievements that are by nature temporary; ground your identity in values and faith that endure.

title: Satih's Description of Himself and His Extraordinary Physical Form
summary: Historical accounts describe the seer Satih as a man of almost supernatural physical form — boneless except for his head, eyes, and hands — who nevertheless possessed extraordinary prophetic knowledge.
story: It is related of Ibn 'Abbas that he said, 'No one in all mankind was like Satih; he was like flesh on a butcher's block, devoid of bones or sinew except for his head, his eyes, and his hands. He folded up from feet to neck like a garment. And the only part of him that moved was his tongue.' Others say that when he became angry he would puff up and sit.
lessons: God bestows gifts without regard for outward form or physical capacity; wisdom and spiritual insight are not dependent on bodily strength.
We are often quick to judge based on appearance — Satih's example challenges us to look beyond the surface to recognize the gifts God places in unexpected vessels.
Hardship of the body does not preclude greatness of spirit; the most constrained circumstances can contain the most expansive knowledge.
practical_advice: When you feel limited by your physical circumstances, health, or abilities, remember that your most important contributions may come from what is within you — your insight, your words, your faith.
Practice suspending judgment about others based on how they appear; the person who seems least likely may carry wisdom you need.
If you struggle with feelings of inadequacy due to your body or perceived limitations, reflect on how God has used the most unlikely vessels throughout history to carry His truth.

title: Satih's Prophecy to the Leaders of Mecca About the Coming Prophet
summary: When Satih visited Mecca and was questioned by its leaders including 'Abd Shams and 'Abd Manaf, he prophesied the coming of a rightly guided prophet from among them who would destroy idols, guide to truth, and whose successors would include the first two caliphs.
story: Ibn 'Abbas recounted that when Satih went to Mecca he was met by a group of their leaders among whom were 'Abd Shams and 'Abd Manaf, sons of Qusayy. They questioned him on a variety of matters and he responded to them frankly. They asked him how it would be at the end of time and he replied, 'Take this from me and as God's inspiration to me, you are now, Arabs, in an age of decrepitude in terms of both your vision and that of foreigners. You have neither knowledge nor understanding. After you there will arise those with understanding. They will seek after all ranges of knowledge and they will destroy the idols, pursue the worthless, fight the non-Arabs, and go in search of the spoils of war. What will happen ultimately and for ever, will be that a right-guided prophet will surely draw you forth from this land and lead you to the truth. He will refute Yagath and the lies, be innocent of worship of the devil, and pray to one Lord alone. Then God will take him unto Himself, giving him praise and the best abode, one lost to the world but seen there in heaven. Then al-Siddiq ('the trusting', i.e. the first Caliph Abu Bakr) will follow him, when he gives judgement he will speak aright, and there will be no rush nor rupture in restoring rights. He will be followed by al-Hanif ('The Sincere', i.e. the second Caliph, 'Umar), the noble and demanding lord. He will be the 'host supreme' and best arbiter of the faith.' He then made mention of (the third Caliph) 'Uthman and his murder, and of the rule thereafter of the Umayyads and the 'Abbasids and all the disturbances and wars that followed.
lessons: The Prophet's coming was foretold across multiple traditions and peoples, a testament to the universality and truth of his mission.
Even in times of spiritual and intellectual 'decrepitude,' God prepares the ground for renewal — no era of ignorance is the final word.
The specific mention of Abu Bakr and 'Umar alongside the Prophet affirms the importance of righteous companionship and succession in carrying truth forward.
practical_advice: When you feel that the world around you is in a state of confusion or moral decline, take heart — every age of darkness in history has been followed by light.
Surround yourself with people of integrity and sound judgment, as the Prophet was followed by companions whose character helped sustain and spread his mission.
Reflect on the long arc of divine guidance in history; this perspective can provide calm and hope when current events feel chaotic or hopeless.

title: Satih's Verses on Piety, Protection of Neighbours, and Living with Integrity
summary: Among the preserved sayings of the seer Satih are verses urging sincere piety in private and public, avoidance of deceit, and the duty to protect and shelter one's neighbours in times of hardship.
story: Among the verses of Satih are the lines, 'Act in God's piety in secret and in public, and never deceitfully pretend good faith. And always be a protector and shield for those neighbouring you when fate's misfortunes befall them.' That poetry was transmitted by the hafiz Abu 'Asakir.
lessons: True piety is consistent whether observed or unobserved; the alignment of private and public conduct is the mark of genuine faith.
Deceit in matters of faith or relationship corrodes trust and community — sincerity is both a spiritual and social obligation.
Caring for those around us in their times of hardship is not optional generosity but a moral and spiritual duty.
practical_advice: Reflect honestly on whether your private conduct matches your public presentation — closing that gap is one of the most meaningful forms of spiritual growth.
Identify a neighbour, colleague, or community member who is facing hardship and consider one concrete way you can be a source of protection or support for them this week.
When you feel guilty about past inconsistencies between your values and your actions, use that feeling as a prompt for change rather than a reason for shame — guilt that leads to growth is a gift.

title: Abd al-Masih the Christian and His Encounter with Poison
summary: An account of Abd al-Masih al-Ghassani, a long-lived Christian who negotiated peace for al-Hira with Khalid b. al-Walid and who survived consuming rapid-acting poison by invoking the name of God.
story: Reprding 'Abd al-Masih b. 'Amr h. Q y s b. Hayyzn b. Buqayla al-Ghassxni, the Christian, he was very long-lived. The ha$?: Ibn 'As& gave his life story in his history, saying therein that it was he who made the peace treaty for al-Hira with Khalid b. al-Walid; he gave at length the story of their contact. Also he reported that 'Ahd al-Masih once ate by his own hand some rapid-acting poison, but that no harm came to him. This was because as he took it he spoke the words, "In the name of God; and by God, Lord of the earth and the heaven, who (ensures that) no harm will come when His name is invoked." He then ate it, swooned, and beat his hands on his chest. He poured with sweat and then, God bless him, recovered. Verses other than those previously quoted are also attributed to 'Ahd al-Masih.
lessons: Trust in God's protection can be a source of extraordinary courage, even in the face of what seems like certain harm.
Invoking God's name sincerely in a moment of danger is itself an act of faith that draws divine mercy.
A person's true spiritual state is often revealed not in comfort but in moments of extreme trial.
practical_advice: In moments of fear or danger, ground yourself by invoking God's name and placing your trust in Him rather than spiraling into panic.
Practice remembrance of God (dhikr) in ordinary moments so that it becomes natural and instinctive when you face difficulty.
Reflect on past moments when you feared an outcome that did not come to pass — use those memories to strengthen your trust in God's protection.

title: The Syrian Monk Aysa and His Foretelling of the Prophet's Birth
summary: A Syrian Christian monk named Aysa lived near Mecca for years, annually warning its people about the imminent birth of a great prophet among them, and upon the Prophet's birth confirmed to Abd Allah ibn Abd al-Muttalib that his newborn son was the one foretold.
story: Abii Nu'aym said that Mhammad h. m a d b. al-Hasan related to us, quoting Muhammad h. Wthmzn h. Aha Shayba, quoting Wqba b. Makrarn, quoting al-Musayyab h. Sharik, quoting Muhammad b. Sharik, all on the authority of Shu'ayh b. Shu'ayh, from his father and from his grandfather, as follows, "At Marr al-Dhahra there was a certain monk from Syria named 'Ay~a. He was under the protection of &'As b. Wa'il. God had given him great knowledge and placed in him many qualities for the people of Mecca, qualities of goodness, kindness, and learning. "He kept to a cell he had and came down to Mecca every year to meet the people and to say to them, 'O people of Mecca, soon there will be born among you a child to whom the Arabs will look for guidance and who will hold sway over the foreigners. This is his time. Whoever has contact with him and follows him will achieve success, while those in contact with h i who oppose him will experience failure. By God, I did not leave the land of fine wine, leavened bread, and security and come to live in the land of hunger, poverty, and fear for any reason other than to seek h i .' "No birth in Mecca ever occurred without his asking about it, but he would always say, 'He's not come yet.' When people would ask for a description of him, he would decline. He would hide that information because of what he knew the fuhlre leader would experience from his tribe, fearing for himself that it might some day result in harm, however slight, to him. "When the morning arrived of the day when the birth of the Messenger of God (SAAS) occurred, CAbd All& son of 'Abd al-Mn~alib went out to see <Aysa. He stood at the foot of the monk's cell and called out, 'Hallo there, CAysa.' The response came, 'Who is there?' 'I am 'Abd All&,' he replied. Then 'Ayva looked down at him and said, 'Be his father! The child I used to tell you of has been born, and on a Monday. He shall also receive God's mission on a Monday, and he will die on a Monday.' "Abd Allah replied, 'Well, this morning a boy was born to me.' 'And what have you named him?' the monk asked. 'Muhammad.' 'By God,' exclaimed cAy~n, 'I wanted the child to he born among you, the people of the House (the kacba), because of three factors of which we know. Those were that his star arose yesterday, that he was born today, and that his name is Muhammad. Go to him, for he whom I used to foretell is your son.' 'Abd Allah asked, 'How do you know he is my son? Perhaps some other child will he born today.' 'Your son,' the monk replied, 'has got the right name. And God has never been one to confuse scholars; i t is a proof. And a (further) sign of that is the fact that he is currently in pain. H e will suffer for three days. Then for three days he will show hunger and after that be cured. So you hold your tongue; for no one ever before has been the object of the envy (there will be) for him, no one has been the object of such hatred as he will have. If you live until his mission appears and he announces himself, then you will experience from your people what you will only endure by patience and in humility. So hold your tongue and protect h i .' 'Abd Allith then asked, 'What will his life-span be?' 'Whether his life be long or short, he will not attain seventy. H e will die a short span before that, in his sixties, at age 61 or 63, at an age average for his people.'" The account goes on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was conceived on the 10th of muhawarn and born on Monday, the 12th of Ramadzn, 23 years after the attack of those with elephants." Thus did Aba Nucaym relate it; and there is some strangeness in the account.
lessons: Sincere seekers of truth are willing to sacrifice comfort and security in their pursuit — the monk left his homeland and lived in hardship solely to witness the coming of the Prophet.
God's plan unfolds with perfect precision; signs converge in ways that cannot be dismissed as coincidence, reminding us that divine timing is always exact.
Those with knowledge of a coming trial sometimes remain silent to protect the bearer of that mission, teaching us that wisdom includes knowing when to speak and when to hold one's tongue.
practical_advice: If you are going through a period of waiting and uncertainty, remind yourself that divine appointments are never late — use the waiting time to prepare yourself spiritually.

When you feel lonely or isolated in your convictions, remember the monk who lived alone in his cell for years, sustained only by his certainty in what was to come — your conviction itself is a form of companionship.

Practice patience during trials by recalling that even the Prophet's birth was immediately followed by days of pain and hunger before healing — difficulty at the start does not mean failure at the end.

title: Umm Ayman and the Rearing of the Prophet
summary: An account of Umm Ayman (Baraka), the freed-slave woman who reared the Prophet Muhammad from infancy, whom he later emancipated and married to his freed-slave Zayd ibn Haritha.
story: An A~countof the rearing and suckling of the Prophet ( W S ) . Umm Ayman, whose given name was Baraka, reared h i . The Prophet (SAAS) had inherited her from his father. When he grew up he emancipated her and married her to his mawlz, his freed-slave, Zayd b. H&itha. She gave birth to Usana h. Zayd, God be pleased with them all.
lessons: Acts of care and nurturing, however humble, leave a lasting imprint — Umm Ayman's dedication to the infant Prophet was honored and remembered for all of history.
The Prophet's emancipation of Umm Ayman when he grew up shows that gratitude for those who cared for us should translate into real, tangible action.
Every person in a position of service or care — no matter how overlooked by the world — holds a dignity that God does not overlook.
practical_advice: Express gratitude to those who nurtured and raised you, whether parents, caregivers, or mentors — do not let their sacrifice go unacknowledged.
If you are in a caregiving role that feels thankless, draw strength from the example of Umm Ayman, whose quiet service was honored by the greatest of all people.
Look for practical ways to honor and uplift those around you who are in vulnerable or dependent positions, just as the Prophet honored those who served him.

title: Thuwayba, the Freed-Slave Who Suckled the Prophet, and Abu Lahab's Reward
summary: Thuwayba, a freed-slave of Abu Lahab, suckled the Prophet Muhammad, and Abu Lahab was shown in a dream after his death to be receiving a small relief from punishment every Monday because he had joyfully emancipated her upon hearing of the Prophet's birth.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) was suckled, as well as by his mother, by the freed-slave of his uncle Aha Lahab who was named Thuwayba, and later by Halima al-SaWiyya. Al-Bukhai and Muslim extracted in their two ~ a h i htradition collections from a hadith of al-Znhri, from Wrwa h. al-Zuhayr, from Zaynab daughter of Umm Salama, from Umm Habiba daughter of Aba Sufyan, as follows. She said, "0 Messenger of God, marry my sister, Ahu Sufyzn's daughter." According to Muslim's account the words used were, "my sister ' h a , Abo Sufym's daughter." The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Would you like that then?" She replied, "Yes; I'm not unmarried (and available) for you. And the person who shares best with me in goodness is my sister." The Prophet (SAAS) responded, "But that would not be permissible for me." She commented, "We are discussing your manying Abo Salama's daughter." Also in an account the words, "Duma, Aba Salama's daughter," are given. "You mean Umm Salama's daughter?" "Yes," I replied. "But", he explained, "even if she were not under my care in my household, she would not be permitted for me. She is my niece by suckling. Her father Abii Salama and I were both suckled by Thuwayba. So don't propose your daughters or your sisters for me." Al-Bukhsi's account adds, "Wrwa said, 'And Thuwayba was a mawlat, a freed-slave woman of Aba Lahab whom he had emancipated. So she suckled the Messenger of God (SAAS).'" And when Aba Lahab was dead, one of his family, greatly disappointed, was shown him. The man asked him, "What happened to you?" Aba Lahab replied, "I experienced nothing good after (leaving) you, except for being given to drink from this for my having emancipated Thuwayba." And he pointed to the hollow between his thumb and adjoining fingers. Al-Suhayli and others report that the one who saw him was his brother alLCAhbas. And this occurred a year after the death of Aha Lahah, after the battle of Badr. Their account relates that Aba Lahah said to al-(Abbbbas, "It (i.e. the punishment of hell) is alleviated for me on Mondays." This is explained as being because when Thnwayha told him the news of the birth of his nephew Muhammad, son of 'Abd All*, he emancipated her at once. And he was rewarded thus for that.
lessons: Even a single moment of joy expressed toward the Prophet — even from one who later became his enemy — was not lost before God, illustrating that no sincere good deed, however small, goes unrewarded.

The bonds formed through acts of nurturing and care, like suckling, are treated with profound seriousness in Islamic law, showing how deeply Islam honors the connections formed through vulnerability and dependency.
Abu Lahab's story is a sobering reminder that great knowledge of the truth, without embracing it fully, leads to eternal loss — yet even then, God's mercy responds to every flicker of goodness.
practical_advice: Do not underestimate the spiritual weight of small acts of kindness or generosity — a single moment of sincere goodness can have consequences you cannot foresee.
If you carry guilt over past failures or opposition to what you knew was right, know that it is never too late to turn toward goodness — begin with one sincere act.
Reflect on the people who nurtured you in vulnerable moments of your life, and honor those bonds with loyalty and gratitude.

title: Halima's Journey to Mecca Seeking a Baby to Suckle
summary: Halima bint Abi Dhu'ayb travels to Mecca during a year of famine with other women seeking babies to suckle, arriving exhausted on a worn-out she-ass with no milk in her breast or camel, and initially unable to find a baby as all others are offered before her.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq said, "Halima daughter of Abii Dhii'ayb was asked to suckle him. Abii Dhu'ayb's given name was CAbd Allah b. al-Hzrith b. Sbijna b. Jabir b. Rizzm b. NQira b. Fusayya b. N a ~ r ,b. Sa'd b. Bakr b. HawGin b. Mansiir b. CIkrima b. Khasafa b. Qays 'Aylan b. Mudar. "And the name of the foster father of the Messenger of God (SAAS), that is Halima's husband, was al-I;Iarith b. 'Abd al-Wzzi%b. Rifaca b. Mallm b. NQira b. Sa'd b. Bakr b. Hawain. "His foster-brother was 'Abd All& b. al-Hzrith; and Unaysa and Khidzma daughters of al-Hzrith were his foster-sisters. The last named was known as 'al-Shayma'; and it is said that she would cradle the Messenger of God (SAAS) along with her mother while he was with them." Ibn Ishaq went on to report that Jahm b. Abii Jahm (the mawla, the freedslave, of a woman of the Bana Tamim who lived with al-Harith b. Hahb, who was known as "the mawla of al-Hzrith b. Ha~ib"), told him that someone related to him from someone who heard 'Abd Allah b. Jacfarb. Abii T d i b say that it was related to him that Halima, daughter of al-Haith, had said, "I arrived at Mecca along with other women (al-Wxqidi told, with a chain of authorities, that they were ten in number from Banii Sa'd b. Bakr who acted as foster-mothers along with her) from Bana SaCd looking for babies to suckle; it was a year of famine. I arrived on a dark grey she-ass worn out from riding. With us I had a boy of our own, and an old milch camel which, I swear, wasn't giving a drop of milk. That whole night, along with that boy of ours, we did not sleep at all. There was nothing in my breast of use to h i and nothing in our milch camel to feed him. We did, however, have hopes of rain and relief. So on I went on that old ass of mine, which lagged behind the other riders because of its weakness and its thirst, much to their annoyance. "So we arrived in Mecca and, I swear, I don't know of one woman of ours who was not offered the Messenger of God (SAAS); but all refused h i when it was said that he was an orphan. We refused him saying, 'What good could his mother do for us? What we want is some help from the boy's father; what could his mother do for us?' I swear, every single one of my women companions, all except me, did take a baby to suckle."
lessons: Hardship and deprivation can be the very doorway through which divine blessing enters — Halima's desperate circumstances made her available for the greatest honor of her life.
When others close a door based on worldly calculations, remaining open-hearted can lead to outcomes beyond imagination.
Trust that God's timing is perfect; what appears as being left behind may in fact be being set apart for something greater.
practical_advice: When you feel like you are the last one chosen or consistently overlooked, resist the temptation to define your worth by that rejection — your moment of blessing may be exactly where others turned away.
In times of material scarcity and exhaustion, hold on to hope as Halima held on to 'hopes of rain and relief'; despair closes us off to solutions that are often closer than we think.
Before making decisions solely based on what seems financially or practically advantageous, pause to consider whether an act of kindness or openness might carry its own unseen reward.

title: Halima Takes the Orphan Child and Experiences Immediate Blessing
summary: Halima reluctantly decides to take the orphaned infant Muhammad rather than return home empty-handed, and immediately experiences an outpouring of milk, a full camel, and a blessed night together with her husband.
story: "Having found no one except him and being assembled to leave, I said to my husband, al-Uarith b. 'Abd al-Wzza, 'By God, I hate to go back amongst my companions without having a baby to suckle. I'll go off to that orphan and take him.' He replied, 'It's not your duty to do so; and so perhaps God will bring us some blessings through h i.' And so I went off and got h i; and the fact is, by God, I only took him because I couldn't find anyone else. "No sooner had I taken him and brought him back to my baggage than my breasts welcomed him, giving h i all the milk he wanted. He drank till he was satisfied and his foster-brother drank his fill too. My husband then went over to that old milch camel of ours and, to his surprise, she was full. He milked her and we both drank till we were satisfied. We then spent a fine night together. "Next morning my husband said, 'Halima, by God I really think you've taken a person who is blessed! You see what a b e, happy night we passed when we took him? May God Almighty go on bringing us more benefit yet!'"
lessons: An act done out of necessity rather than noble intention can still attract divine grace — God looks at the outcome of our actions and rewards genuine openness even when our motives are mixed.
Blessings often flow immediately once we make a decision to include and care for those whom others have excluded.
A spouse who encourages gratitude and recognizes divine favor is a tremendous asset — al-Harith's words of acknowledgment deepened the family's awareness of the blessing they carried.
practical_advice: When you are about to give up on something or someone because it seems disadvantageous, consider whether your reluctance is really based on wisdom or simply on following the crowd's judgment.
Practice noticing moments of unexpected abundance after a period of scarcity — these moments are invitations to recognize that something greater than coincidence may be at work in your life.
Share observations of blessing with those you love as al-Harith did; naming gratitude together strengthens bonds and opens the heart to receive even more.

title: The Journey Home and the Blessings That Followed Halima's Household
summary: On the return journey to Banu Sa'd, Halima's formerly weak she-ass outpaces all other riders, and throughout the child's stay her flocks remain full of milk while all neighboring flocks go hungry.
story: "We then left to return to our own land. And, I swear, my she-ass went off ahead of all the other riders, with not a single donkey able to catch up with her. Eventually my women companions said, 'Hey there, 0 daughter of Abii Dhu'ayh! Is that the she ass you were riding when you left with us?' I replied, 'Yes indeed, it's her.' They commented, 'Well, by God, something's really happened to her!' "Eventually we were back in Banii Sa'd territory, and I knew no other part of God's earth more barren than it is. But my goats would graze free, then leave the grazing, satisfied and full of milk and we would milk them as ever we wished. And this while no one around us, near or far, had their ewes give so much as a drop of milk, their ewes always going hungry. Finally the others said to their shepherds: 'Woe on you! Just watch where the flock of Abii Du'ayb's daughter grazes. You graze along with them.' And this they would do, theirs grazing where mine went, but their flocks remained hungry, not giving a drop of milk, while mine were satisfied and full so we could milk them as much as we wanted. "And God went on blessing us this way, and we recognized it. Then he reached two years of age. He was already growing up a very fine boy, not like the other children. I swear that by age two he was a sturdy boy, so we took him to his mother; we were very covetous of him because of having seen the blessings he had brought. When his mother saw h i I told her, 'Let us bring back this son of ours some other year. We are concerned he might catch a disease from Mecca.' We kept on at her until she agreed and sent h i off with us and we stayed with him for two or three months more."
lessons: Divine blessing can radiate outward from one righteous soul and benefit an entire community — caring for what God loves draws God's provision.
Those around us often notice the blessing in our lives before we fully articulate it ourselves; the testimony of witnesses can strengthen our own faith.
Recognizing and naming God's ongoing grace — 'and God went on blessing us this way, and we recognized it' — is itself an act of worship that deepens gratitude.
practical_advice: When you notice that a certain relationship, practice, or decision has brought consistent goodness into your life, be intentional about acknowledging it rather than taking it for granted.
If others around you are struggling and you are flourishing, consider how you can share or extend your blessing rather than hoarding it — blessings shared tend to multiply.
Keep a simple record of moments when things went better than expected; over time, this practice of recognition builds a reservoir of trust that sustains you through harder seasons.

title: The Chest-Opening of the Prophet as a Child — The Account of Halima
summary: Halima's foster-son reports that two men dressed in white came, laid him down, opened his chest, removed something from his heart, and then restored him — prompting Halima and her husband to return him to his mother out of fear.
story: "Then one time he was out behind our dwellings with one of his foster-brothers with our animals. Suddenly that foster-brother came in greatly agitated and said, 'That Quraysh brother of mine! Two men dressed in white came to him, laid him down, and split open his abdomen!' "At this hi father and I rushed outside towards him. We found him standing there, his colour pale. His foster-father hugged h i and asked, 'What's wrong, son?' He replied, 'Two men dressed in white came to me. They laid me down and opened my abdomen. Then they drew something from inside it and threw it away. After that they put my abdomen back as before.' So we took the boy back with us and his foster father said, 'Halima, I'm afraid my foster son has become afflicted; let's hurry and give him back to his family before what we fear does develop.' So we did take him back. His mother was very surprised to see him and when we gave h i over to her she said, 'So why did you bring him back, nurse? You both wanted to have him so much.' 'No, it's nothing,' we replied, 'it's just that God has relieved us of our task. We did our duty. We're afraid some harm or accident may befall him, so we are returning him to his own people.' His mother insisted, 'What's going on with you both! Tell me truthfully what's worrying you!' She would not leave us alone till we told her what had happened. She asked, 'Are you afraid for h i from Satan? Absolutely not; Satan won't get to him. By God, this son of mine has a great future before him. Should I tell you what happened with him?' 'Please do,' we asked. 'Well,' she explained, 'when I was pregnant with him, he was the lightest child I ever bore. And when carrying him I saw in my sleep a light emanating so bright it lit up the castles of Syria! And then, when he was horn, he came out in a manner that babies never do, drew himself up on his arms and lifted his head up to heaven. So leave h i, and don't worry.'"
lessons: Extraordinary events can provoke fear in those who witness them, even when the events are entirely good — our comfort with the divine is often limited by our understanding.
A mother's intuitive certainty about her child's destiny, grounded in signs she has witnessed, can be a source of calm that steadies those around her.
When we are frightened by something we do not understand, honesty — telling the truth about what we witnessed rather than concealing it — opens the door to reassurance and clarity.
practical_advice: When something unexpected and overwhelming happens, resist the urge to react in pure panic; pause, check on those involved, and gather the facts before drawing conclusions as Halima and her husband did.
If you carry anxiety about someone you love, consider sharing your worry with people who know them well — sometimes another perspective carries the peace you are missing.
When you feel fear about the future of someone dear to you, remind yourself that God's plan for them is not subject to the limits of your understanding or your ability to protect them.

title: The Prophet Describes the Chest-Opening Event in His Own Words
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) describes to his companions the night two angels came to him, cleansed his heart of a black clot, and then weighed him against his entire nation — finding that he outweighed them all.
story: Ibn Ishaq said that Thawr b. Yazid related to him, from Khalid b. Ma'dan, from the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) that the Companions once asked him to tell them about h i s e l f. He responded, "I am the one called for by my father Abraham, the glad tidings foreseen by Jesus, peace be upon them both. When she bore me my mother saw that a light came from inside her that illuminated the castles of Syria. I was suckled by the tribe of Sa(d b. Bahr and while I was among some of our animals two men dressed in white came, carrying a golden basin filled with ice. They laid me down, cut open my abdomen and took out my heart. This they split and extracted from it a black clot that they threw away. They then washed my heart and insides with that ice until clean. Having put it back as before, one of them said to his companion, 'Weigh him against ten of his nation.' He did so, but I outweighed them. He then said, 'Weigh him against a hundred of his nation.' This he did but I outweighed them. He said, 'Weigh h i against a thousand.' The other man did, but I outweighed them too. The first man then said, 'Leave him now, for even if you weighed him against his entire nation, he would outweigh them!'"
lessons: God's purification of those He chooses for great responsibility is thorough and complete — the removal of the 'black clot' symbolizes that divine mission requires a heart cleansed of what would hinder it.
Every human soul carries within it both light and shadow; recognizing this is the beginning of growth, and trusting God to purify what we cannot cleanse ourselves is an act of profound surrender.
The Prophet's willingness to share this intimate and extraordinary experience with his companions reflects the importance of transparency and trust in close relationships.
practical_advice: Reflect on what 'black clots' — resentments, habits, or fears — may be weighing down your heart, and actively seek means of purification through prayer, honest reflection, and seeking forgiveness.
When you feel small or insignificant compared to those around you, remember that divine worth is not measured by the world's standards — you may carry more than you know.
Take moments to tell those you trust about formative experiences that shaped who you are; this kind of storytelling builds deeper understanding and connection.

title: The Cloud That Shaded the Young Prophet — The Account of His Foster-Sister
summary: Halima finds the young Muhammad sitting outside in the heat with his sister, who explains that a cloud has been following and shading him wherever he moves, protecting him from the sun.
story: Al-Witqidi said that Ma5dh b. Muhammad related to him, from 'A@' b. Abo Rabb*, from Ibn 'Abbits, as follows, "Halima once went out to seek the Prophet (SAAS) and she found their animals to be sleeping in the sun. Him she found with his sister. She asked, 'Why outside in this heat?' His sister replied, 'My brother doesn't get hot; I saw a cloud shading him that stopped when he did and moved when he did, till he came to this spot.'"
lessons: Those who carry a divine purpose are not abandoned to the harshness of the world unprotected — God's care is personal, specific, and attentive to even ordinary moments of daily life.
Signs of divine favor often go unnoticed by the wider world and are witnessed only by those in close, humble proximity — the foster-sister, not a dignitary, was the witness here.
Nature itself can be an expression of divine care; cultivating awareness of the small, protective graces in our environment is a form of spiritual attentiveness.
practical_advice: Practice looking for signs of care and protection in your life that you may have normalized — the roof over your head, the person who checked on you, the near-miss you barely registered.
When someone in your life seems to move through difficulty with an unusual peace or ease, take the time to observe and learn from them rather than explaining it away.
Cultivate the habit of sitting quietly in nature and noticing how it reflects God's provision — this practice can reduce anxiety by grounding you in a larger, caring reality.

title: The Opening of the Prophet's Chest (First Account – With Foster Family)
summary: While staying with his foster family among Banu Sa'd, two angelic beings appeared to the young Prophet, opened his chest, removed black clots, cleansed his heart, and sealed it with the mark of prophethood, before weighing him favorably against his entire nation.
story: A man asked the Prophet (SAAS), "What was it happened to you first, O Messenger of God?" He replied, "My wet-nurse was from the Bana Sacd h. Bakr. One time a son of hers and I went off with our flock without taking any food with us. So I said, 'Hey brother, go hack and get us some food from our mother's home.' So my brother hurried away while I stayed with the animals. Then two white birds like eagles came along and one said to the other, 'Is that he?' 'Yes,' the other replied. Then they both swooped down, took hold of me and laid me out on my back. They then split my abdomen, extracted my heart, cut it open, and took out from it two black clots. One bird said to his companion, 'Bring me some ice water.' And with it they cleaned my insides. The first bud then said, 'Now get some cold water.' With that they washed my heart. Then the bird said, 'Bring me al-sakina (quietude).' This they sprinMed into my heart. The one said to the other, 'Sew it up.' So he did that and then placed on my heart the seal of prophethood. After that one bird said to the other, 'Place him in one balance scale and a thousand of his people in the other.' And when I looked up, there were the thousand above me, and I was concerned that some of them might tumble down on to me. Then the bird said, 'If his whole nation were balanced against him, he would stdl outweigh them.' Then both birds went away, leaving me there. I was tremendously afraid and raced off to my foster-mother and told her what had happened. She was afraid I'd lost my mind and exclaimed, 'God protect you!' Then she got an ass of hers ready for travel and put me on it. She rode behind me and we travelled till we reached my mother. She then said, 'Well, I've fulfilled my pact and agreement.' She then told my mother what had happened, but it didn't shock her. She said, 'What I saw was that a light came from inside me that lit up the castles of Syria.'"
lessons: Profound spiritual preparation often comes through experiences that are frightening and incomprehensible in the moment; trust that what feels overwhelming may be part of a greater divine design.
The removal of the 'black clots' symbolizes that even the most chosen of people undergo purification — personal growth requires releasing what weighs down the heart.
A mother's or caregiver's calm trust in God, even when faced with alarming news, models how faith can be an anchor for those around us in times of crisis.
practical_advice: When you experience something deeply unsettling or inexplicable, allow yourself to process the fear first, then seek a trusted person to confide in rather than carrying the weight alone.
Reflect regularly on what 'clots' — habits, grudges, or negative patterns — may be weighing your own heart down, and actively seek spiritual or personal means to cleanse them.
When someone you care for comes to you frightened and shaken, respond first with protection and presence — as Halima did — before trying to explain or rationalize the situation.

title: The Opening of the Prophet's Chest (Second Account – Abu Dharr's Narration)
summary: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari recounts asking the Prophet how he first knew he was a prophet, and the Prophet described two angels appearing in a valley of Mecca, weighing him against a man, then opening his chest and placing the seal of prophethood between his shoulders.
story: "O Messenger of God, how were you informed that you were a prophet and when did you become convinced that you were one?" He replied, "O Abii Dharr, two angels came to me when I was in one of the valleys of Mecca. One of them dropped down to the ground, while the other remained suspended between heaven and earth. One said to the other, 'Is that he?' 'Yes, it's he.' The one said, 'Weigh him against another man.' He did so, and I outweighed him." He then told it till the end, recounting how his chest was opened and sewn up and the seal was placed between his shoulders. He then said, "Then finally they turned away from me, as if their work was fully done."
lessons: The question of 'how did you know?' reflects a deeply human need for certainty; spiritual conviction often builds gradually through signs and experiences rather than arriving all at once.
Being 'weighed' and found of great worth is a reminder that divine assessment of a person's value differs entirely from worldly measures of status or wealth.
The seal placed between the Prophet's shoulders was invisible to most — true spiritual distinction is often carried internally and quietly, not announced loudly to the world.
practical_advice: When doubting your own worth or purpose, reflect on the fact that God's measure of a soul's value is not determined by others' opinions but by qualities of heart and character.
Seek out trusted companions, as Abu Dharr sought the Prophet, with whom you can ask the deep questions that weigh on your mind without fear of judgment.
Notice the 'signs' in your own life that may be quietly pointing you toward your purpose — they often only become clear in retrospect when reflected upon honestly.

title: The Opening of the Prophet's Chest (Third Account – Anas ibn Malik in Sahih Muslim)
summary: As established in Sahih Muslim, Gabriel appeared while the young Prophet was playing with other boys, opened his chest, removed a black clot described as 'the devil's lot,' washed his heart in a golden basin with zamzam water, then restored it, causing the other children to flee in alarm thinking he had been killed.
story: It is established in the ~ahih collection of Muslim through Hammad b. Salama, from Thabit, from Anas b. Mdik that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was visited by Gabriel, on whom be peace, while he was playing with the other boys. Gabriel took him, laid him down, split his heart open, took it out and withdrew a black clot from inside it, saying, "That's the devil's lot!" Then he washed it in a golden basin with water from (the well called) zamzam. He then mended it and put it back in its place. The other boys raced off to his mother - meaning his nurse - and told her, "Muhammad has been killed!" When they met him he was very pale. Anas stated, "And I used to see the mark of the sewing on his chest."
lessons: The Prophet's companions could see the physical mark of his purification on his chest — a reminder that our deepest transformations, though internal, often leave visible traces in how we carry ourselves.
The other children's fear and assumption that he had been 'killed' illustrates how people often misinterpret transformative experiences in others through the lens of their own limited understanding.
Even the most beloved of God underwent a process of purification — no soul is exempt from the need for inner cleansing, and this process should be embraced rather than avoided.
practical_advice: When you are going through an intense internal transformation, be patient with those around you who may misread your changed demeanor or quiet withdrawal — give them time to understand.
Consider zamzam water and purification rituals in Islamic practice as tangible reminders that spiritual cleansing is a real, embodied process that involves both the physical and the spiritual self.

Reflect on whether there is a 'devil's lot' — a pattern of thought or behavior rooted in whispers of despair or ego — that you can consciously work to remove from your heart through prayer and repentance.

title: The Christians of a Caravan Recognize the Young Prophet
summary: When Halima was returning the weaned Prophet to his mother, a caravan of Christians recognized signs of extraordinary importance in the child and sought to take him to their king, but Halima managed to evade them after great effort.
story: Ibn Ishzq stated, "When HalTma was returning him to his mother after he had been weaned, she passed with him by a caravan of Christians. They came up to him, turned him around in their hands and said, 'We must take this boy to our king; he is a person of great importance.' And Halima managed to evade them only after great effort."
lessons: Truth and spiritual greatness have a quality that transcends religious and cultural boundaries — even those outside the faith recognized something extraordinary in the Prophet before his prophethood.
Halima's protective instinct and her effort to keep the child safe illustrates how caregivers are often providentially placed to shield those entrusted to them.

The recognition of potential and greatness in a child is a reminder to look carefully and charitably at those in our care — we may be nurturing someone whose contribution to the world we cannot yet fully see.
practical_advice: When others seem to place unusual expectations or demands on you or someone you care for, trust your instincts about what is safe and appropriate, and do not feel obligated to comply out of social pressure.
Reflect on the people in your own life who recognized something in you before you recognized it in yourself — express gratitude for those guardians and mentors.
As a caregiver or mentor, take seriously your role in protecting those entrusted to you from premature exposure to pressures or environments they are not yet ready for.

title: The Young Prophet Is Lost Near Mecca and Found by Waraqa ibn Nawfal
summary: As Halima approached Mecca to return the Prophet to his mother, she lost him and could not find him; his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib organized a search and it was Waraqa ibn Nawfal who found the child and returned him, after which Abd al-Muttalib circumambulated the Kaaba in thanksgiving.
story: Ibn Ishaq reported also that when Halima was returning him fearing some harm had come to him, as she approached Mecca she lost him and could not find him. So she went to his grandfather 'Abd al-Mumlib, and he and a group of men went off to search for him. It was Waraqa b. Nawfal and another man of Quraysh who found him and brought him to his pandfather. He put him on his shoulders and circumambulated (the kacba), praying to God for the boy's protection, and then returned him to his mother Amina.
lessons: The involvement of Waraqa ibn Nawfal — who would later be the first to recognize the Prophet's prophethood — in this early rescue is a subtle reminder that God arranges the threads of a person's life with remarkable precision.

Abd al-Muttalib's immediate and energetic response to search for his lost grandchild, followed by gratitude and circumambulation of the Kaaba, models how to respond to relief after anxiety: with action and then with worship.
Being temporarily 'lost' is not always a sign of abandonment — it can be the prelude to being found by exactly the right person at the right time.
practical_advice: In moments of panic over a lost person or situation, channel your anxiety into purposeful action as Abd al-Muttalib did — organize, enlist help, and search systematically rather than being paralyzed by fear.
When something or someone you feared was lost is restored to you, mark the moment consciously with gratitude — a prayer, an act of worship, or a simple acknowledgment of God's mercy.
Reflect on the 'Waraqa figures' in your life — people who appeared at crucial moments of vulnerability — and consider how their presence may have been more than coincidental.

title: The Prophet's Journey to Yemen with His Uncle Al-Zubayr and Signs of Prophethood
summary: In his early teens the Prophet traveled to Yemen with his uncle al-Zubayr, during which journey signs of his prophethood were witnessed: a stallion camel knelt and submitted to him, and a flooded stream dried up miraculously to allow their party to cross.
story: In his early teens his uncle al-Zubayr took him to Yemen. His account states that on that journey they saw signs (of his prophethood). One of these was that a stallion camel had made its way some distance along a valley through which the party was passing. When the stallion saw the Messenger of God (SAAS), it knelt down and rubbed its chest on the ground and so he mounted it. Another sign was that they came to a stream in violent flood but God Almighty dried it up so that they could cross it. Thereafter his uncle al-Zubayr died; at that time he was fourteen and Abn Talib alone took charge of him.
lessons: Even before prophethood, creation itself recognized and submitted to the Prophet — a reminder that our deepest nature is attuned to what is true and good, even when our intellect has not yet caught up.
The drying of a flooded stream to allow passage is a metaphor for how obstacles that appear insurmountable can be removed when one is walking a path aligned with divine purpose.

The death of his uncle al-Zubayr, leaving him solely in Abu Talib's care, shows that loss and transition are woven into even the most blessed of lives — hardship does not indicate the absence of God's favor.
practical_advice: When you encounter unexpected obstacles on a journey or in a project, pause before giving up — consider whether the path is simply being cleared or redirected rather than closed.
Pay attention to moments when the natural world or unexpected circumstances seem to ease your way; cultivate gratitude for these as signs of unseen support rather than dismissing them as coincidence.
When you lose a supporter or mentor unexpectedly, allow yourself to grieve, but then look for who remains — as Abu Talib remained — and invest deeply in those continuing relationships.

title: The Blessings Bestowed on Halima's Family Through the Prophet's Stay
summary: The text reflects on how the Prophet's presence brought blessings to Halima al-Sa'diyya and her family during his infancy, and how this bond of nursing later moved him to show extraordinary mercy to the Hawazin tribe at the battle of Hunayn.
story: What is implied here is that the blessedness of the Prophet (SAAS) came down upon Halima al-Sa'diyya and her family, when he was young and then his virtues reverted to the people of Hawain when he took them prisoners after their battle; that occurred a month following the capture of Mecca. They were related to him because of his being suckled among them. He therefore released them, taking pity on them, and treating them with kindness, as will be related in full in its proper place, if God wills it.
lessons: Kindness given early in life — even to an infant who cannot yet reciprocate — is never truly lost; it is stored and returned, often in ways and at times we cannot predict.
The Prophet's mercy toward Hawazin decades after his nursing years demonstrates that bonds of care and nurturing create lasting moral obligations that the noble soul never forgets.
Blessings often flow through people before we are aware of them — Halima's household prospered not through any special merit of their own but through proximity to goodness.
practical_advice: Invest sincerely in acts of care and nurturing for others, especially the young and vulnerable, without calculating a return — the return may come in forms and timescales beyond your imagination.
Reflect on the caregivers of your own early life and consider whether there is an opportunity, even now, to honor or acknowledge the kindness they showed you.

When you are in a position of power over others who have wronged you or been your adversaries, ask yourself what bonds of shared humanity or past kindness might call you toward mercy rather than retaliation.

title: The Hawazin Delegation Pleads for Mercy After the Battle of Hunayn
summary: After the battle of Hunayn, a delegation from the Hawazin tribe — the Prophet's former nursing community — came to him at al-Ji'irrana pleading for the release of their prisoners, with their spokesman Zuhayr ibn Surad delivering moving verses appealing to the bond of nursing.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq related from 'Amr b. Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather, who said, "We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the battle of Hunayn and when he seized their properties and took them prisoners, a delegation from Hawein who had accepted Islam came to him at al-Ji~rma~~. They said, 'O Messenger of God, we are one family and tribe. You know well what misfortune has befallen us. Have mercy on us, and may God have mercy on you.' Their spokesman Zuhayr h. Surad then arose and said, 'O Messenger of God, those prisoners you have in the stockades are your aunts and your nurses who looked after you. If we had suckled Ibn Aba Shamar or al-NuCmanb. al-Mundhir and then we were to suffer from them what we have from you, we would have hoped for their help and kindness. And yet you are the most honourable of men.' He then recited the verses: 'Have pity on us, O Messenger of God, in kindness; for you are the man we plead with and implore. Have pity on a tribe ruined by fate, their whole divided, their fortune adversely changed, Which has left us to cry out in sorrow; a people in whose hearts there is gloom and tragedy, Unless you ward it off with kindnesses you spread forth, O most superior of men in wisdom when it is tested. Have pity on women you once sucked, your mouth filled with their pure milk, Have pity on women you once sucked, for then whatever happens will sustain your reoutation. Do not make us as those who scattered and died; spare us, for we are a tribe of fame. We give thanks for kindnesses even if not redeemed, and after this day we will have a store (of good will).'"
lessons: The power of appealing to shared humanity and remembered bonds — rather than arguments of power or rights — is demonstrated in Zuhayr's plea; mercy is most effectively sought by touching the heart, not by demanding it.

The Hawazin's situation illustrates how a community can fall from prosperity to devastation swiftly, and yet how even in the depths of loss there remains the possibility of appeal, restoration, and hope.

Zuhayr's poetic appeal, invoking the Prophet's nursing women and childhood bonds, reflects the Islamic understanding that human relationships of care and nurture carry sacred weight that transcends conflict.
practical_advice: When seeking forgiveness or mercy from someone you have wronged or been in conflict with, appeal first to your shared humanity and any bonds of relationship you hold in common, rather than only making legal or transactional arguments.

If you find yourself in a position of total defeat or dependence on another's mercy, do not let shame prevent you from speaking honestly and with dignity — the Hawazin's plea was humble but not self-degrading.
When someone comes to you broken and asking for mercy, pause before responding with conditions or negotiations — let their vulnerability and your shared history speak to your heart first.

title: Hawazin Delegation Pleads for Prisoners After Battle of Hunayn
summary: A leader of the Hawazin tribe recites moving verses to the Prophet, reminding him of his childhood among them, pleading for the release of 6,000 prisoners taken at the Battle of Hunayn. The Prophet releases them and gives generous gifts.
story: This story is also related through Ubayd Allah b. Rum*is al-Kalbi al-Ramli from Ziyad b. Tariq al-Jashami, from Aba Sarad Zuhayr b. Jarwal, who was the leader of his people. The last-mentioned stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) took us prisoners at the battle of Hunayn he was separating the men from the women. So I jumped up, stood before him and spoke some verses to him, reminding him of the time when he was a boy and grew up among Hawazin who suckled him: 'Have mercy on us, Messenger of God, in gentleness, for you are the man we hope for and await. Have mercy on a people ruined by fate, their whole divided, their fortune adversely changed, The fighting left us a war-cry only of sadness; a people in whose hearts there is gloom and tragedy. Unless you ward it off with kindness you spread forth, O most superior of men in wisdom when he is tested Have pity on women you used to suck, your mouth bled with their pure milk; When you were a small child you used to suck them and then whatever happens will sustain your reputation. Do not make us as those who scattered and died; spare us for we are a tribe of fame. We give thanks for kindness, even if disavowed, for after this day we will have store (of goodwill); Dress in forgiveness those you used to suck, your mothers; for acts of forgiveness become widely known. We hope for forgiveness from you; dress these people in it, if forgive you will, and then be triumphant. So forgive and may God forgive you from whatever you fear on Judgement Day when victory shall be given you.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Whatever prizes were due myself or the family of 'Abd al-Muttalib, that goes to God and to you all.' Al-Ansar then said, 'And what was to be for us we give to God and to his Messenger (SAAS).'" It will be later told how he set them free; they were 6,000, men and women combined. He gave them many camels and people. So that Abu al-Husayn b. Faris said, "The value of what he gave them that day totalled 500,000 dirhams." All this then came from his early blessings on earth: what blessings, then, will he bestow on those who follow him in the next world!
lessons: Forgiveness is a mark of true strength and nobility; the Prophet's release of 6,000 prisoners demonstrated that mercy is more powerful than victory.
Gratitude for past kindness — even kindness received in childhood — is a noble virtue worth honouring throughout life.
Generosity extended to those who were once enemies can transform hearts and build lasting goodwill between communities.
practical_advice: When you have power over someone who has wronged you, pause and ask yourself whether mercy might serve a greater good than punishment.
Reflect on the people who nurtured or helped you in your early years, and let that memory inspire you toward compassion when others are vulnerable.
Practice giving more than what is expected of you; generosity that exceeds obligation leaves a lasting spiritual and social legacy.

title: The Death of the Prophet's Mother Amina at al-Abwa'
summary: The Prophet Muhammad's mother Amina died at al-Abwa' when he was only six years old, while returning from a visit to his maternal uncles in Medina, leaving the young child an orphan.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr b. Muhammad b. 'Amr b. Hazam related to me that Amina, the mother of the Messenger of God (SAAS), died when he was six at al-Abwa', a place between Mecca and Medina. She had taken him to visit his uncles of the tribe of 'Adi b. al-Najjar and died when she was returning with him to Mecca." Al-Waqidi related, with chains of authorities, that the Prophet (SAAS) was taken by his mother to Medina, in the company of Umm Ayman, when he was six years old and that she visited his uncles. Umm Ayman said, "One day two Jews of Medina came to me and said, 'Bring out Ahmad for us to see.' They then looked at him and turned him around in their hands. Then one of them said to the other, 'This is the Prophet of this nation. And this shall be the abode of his refuge, where very many will be taken prisoner and many will be killed.' "When his mother heard this she was afraid and left. And it was at al-Abwa' that she died, on her way home."
lessons: Loss and grief are universal experiences that even prophets endure; experiencing hardship does not mean one is abandoned by God.
The earliest trials of life can shape a person's capacity for empathy and compassion toward others who suffer.
Even in moments of great loss, divine care and providence continue to work in ways that are not immediately visible.
practical_advice: If you are grieving the loss of a parent or loved one, allow yourself to feel the sadness without guilt — even the Prophet wept at his mother's grave.
When life strips away your sources of security at a young age, seek out trustworthy people in your community who can offer guidance and stability.
Remember that your personal history of loss does not define your future; the orphaned child of Amina became a mercy to all of humanity.

title: The Prophet Visits His Mother's Grave and Weeps
summary: The Prophet Muhammad visited the grave of his mother Amina, wept intensely, and explained to his companions that he had asked God's permission to seek forgiveness for her but was refused, moved by the natural emotion a son feels for his mother.
story: The Imam Ahmad said that Husayn b. Muhammad related to us, quoting Ayyub b. Jabir, from Simak, from al-Qasim b. 'Abd al-Rahman, from Ibn Burayda, from his father (Burayda) who said, "We left along with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and travelled until we reached Waddan. Then he told us to wait there until he returned. He went off and later returned looking grave. Then he said, 'I went to the grave of Muhammad's mother and asked my Lord for intercession for her. But He would not allow me that. I had forbidden you from visiting graves; do visit them. Similarly I had forbidden you from eating the flesh of sacrificed animals after three days had passed; do eat and take what you want. I had forbidden you from drinking from these vessels; do drink as you will.'" Al-Bayhaqi related this tradition through Sufyan al-Thawri, from 'Alqama b. Yazid, from Sulayman b. Burayda from his father as follows: "The Prophet (SAAS) went over to the site of a grave and the other men sat down around him. He began to move his head as though addressing someone, and then he wept. Umar went over to him and asked, 'What is making you weep, O Messenger of God?' He replied, 'This is the grave of Amina daughter of Wahb. I asked my Lord for permission to visit her grave and he gave permission. I also asked for forgiveness for her, but this He refused me.
