Sympathy for her overwhelmed me and so I wept.' And I never saw an occasion of greater weeping than that." Moreover al-Bayhaqi related from al-Hakim, from al-Asamm, from Bahr b. Nasr, from 'Abd Allah b. Wahb that Ibn Jurayj related to us, from Ayyub b. Husayn, from Masruq b. al-Ajda', from 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud as follows, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went off to look at graves and we went with him. He told us to sit down and we did. Then he walked on till he stopped at one grave. He spoke to it at length and then there arose a lamentation and weeping from the Messenger of God (SAAS). And we all wept at his crying so. "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) came over to us and was met by 'Umar b. al-Khattab who said, 'O Messenger of God, what made you weep? It made us weep and scared us.' "Then he came and sat down with us and asked, 'My weeping scared you, did it?' 'Indeed yes,' we replied. He explained, 'The grave you saw me addressing was that of Amina, daughter of Wahb. I asked my Lord's permission to visit it and he allowed me. Then I asked His permission to seek forgiveness for her, but this He did not permit me. Then there were revealed to me the verses: "It is not for the Prophet and for those who believe to ask forgiveness for idolaters, even if related to them, once it has become clear to them that those are going to hell. Abraham's seeking pardon for his father resulted only from a promise he had made him; and when it became clear to Abraham that he (his father) was an enemy of God, he (Abraham) disowned him. Abraham was certainly soft of heart, long-suffering" (surat al-Tawba, IX, v.113, 114). And I was affected by the emotion a son feels for his mother; that it was made me weep.'" Muslim related from Abu Bakr b. Abi Shayba, from Muhammad b. 'Ubayd, from Yazid b. Kaysan, from Abi Hazim, from Abu Hurayra as follows: "The Prophet (SAAS) visited his mother's grave; he wept and this made those with him weep too. Then he said, 'I asked my Lord's permission to visit my mother's grave and He allowed me. I also asked his permission to seek forgiveness for her, but he did not permit me. So do visit graves; they will remind you of death.'"
lessons: Grief for a lost parent is a profoundly human experience that does not diminish one's faith or dignity; even the Prophet wept openly at his mother's grave.
Accepting the limits of what we can do for those we love, while trusting in God's wisdom, is itself a form of profound spiritual surrender.
Visiting graves is a spiritually beneficial act that reconnects us with our mortality and reminds us to live with purpose.
practical_advice: If you carry grief or guilt about a deceased parent or loved one, know that your love itself is honourable — entrust them to God's mercy, which is greater than any intercession.
Allow yourself to grieve openly when it arises; the Prophet's tears were not a sign of weakness but of the depth of his humanity.
Consider visiting the graves of loved ones as a spiritual practice; it can help you process grief and refocus on what truly matters in life.

title: The Prophet Answers Where His Father and Ancestors Are
summary: The Prophet was asked by a man about his deceased father's fate and replied that he was in hell-fire, then added that his own father was likewise, demonstrating his submission to divine truth even at personal cost.
story: Muslim related, from Abu Bakr b. Abi Shayba, from 'Affan, from Hammad b. Salama, from Thabit, from Anas, that a man asked, "O Messenger of God, where is my father?" He replied, "In hell-fire." Then when the man turned away, he called out to him: "My father and yours are both in hell." Al-Bayhaqi related from a tradition of Abu Nu'aym al-Fadl b. Dukayn, from Ibrahim b. Sa'd, from al-Zuhri, from 'Amir b. Sa'd, from his father as follows, "A bedouin came to the Prophet (SAAS) and said, 'My father used to give to charity, and so on and so forth. So where is he now?' He replied, 'In hell-fire.' "The bedouin appeared angered at that and asked, 'Well, Messenger of God, and where's your father?' He replied, 'Whenever you pass by the grave of an unbeliever, warn him of hell-fire.' "Thereafter the bedouin accepted Islam and said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) put a heavy burden on me; when I pass the grave of an unbeliever, I never fail to warn him of hell-fire!'"
lessons: Truthfulness, even when it is painful to oneself, is a hallmark of prophetic character and genuine integrity.
The Prophet's willingness to apply the same divine standard to his own family as to others demonstrated that no one receives preferential treatment before God.
Difficult truths, delivered with compassion, can paradoxically open hearts — as evidenced by the bedouin who accepted Islam after the exchange.
practical_advice: When you face a painful truth about someone you love, seek peace not by denying it but by entrusting the matter entirely to God's infinite mercy and justice.
Resist the temptation to hold those in authority to a lower standard than others; consistency in principle is the foundation of trustworthy character.
If a spiritual or moral truth feels heavy, consider that accepting it fully — as the bedouin did — can itself become a source of devotion and purposeful action.

title: The Prophet Cautions Fatima About Visiting Graves
summary: The Prophet encountered his daughter Fatima outside her home and, upon learning she had attended a funeral, warned her about accompanying funeral processions to the grave, indicating the seriousness of the ruling for women.
story: The Imam Ahmad said, "Abu 'Abd al-Rahman related to us, as did Sa'id — he being Ibn Abi Ayyub — as did Rabi'a b. Sayf al-Ma'afiri, from 'Abd al-Rahman al-Hubli, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr as follows, 'While we were walking with the Messenger of God (SAAS) he noticed a woman he did not think he recognized. When half-way across the street he stopped until she reached him. Surprisingly, it was Fatima, the daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He said, "Fatima what brings you out of your house?" She replied, "I came to visit the family in this house, expressing my condolences to them for their dead." He commented, "Perhaps you went as far as al-kudz (the grave) with them." She replied, "God forbid that I would have gone to that with them, having heard what you have said about that." He went on, "Well, if you had gone that far with them you would not see paradise until your father's grandfather sees paradise!"'"
lessons: Loving guidance from those in authority, even when it carries a stern warning, is a form of care and not rejection.
Obedience to divine boundaries, even when social custom might pull us in a different direction, protects us spiritually.
Fatima's immediate response — that she had already taken the Prophet's previous instruction to heart — shows how internalising guidance before being tested is the mark of a sincere believer.
practical_advice: When you receive religious guidance that goes against a social norm, reflect on it carefully rather than dismissing it; understanding the reason behind a boundary helps you embrace it willingly.
If you find yourself uncertain whether an action is permissible, ask yourself what the person you most respect spiritually would counsel — and let that guide your choice.
Practice internalising principles of your faith during times of ease, so that when the moment of test comes, you act from conviction rather than confusion.

title: The Scholarly Debate on the Fate of the Prophet's Parents and Grandfather
summary: Islamic scholars debate the spiritual status of the Prophet Muhammad's parents and grandfather Abd al-Muttalib in the hereafter, examining various hadith traditions and theological positions.
story: that it was "sound". Ibn Ynnus stated in the Tzrikh Mzjr (History ofEgypt) that "there are faults in his narration." He died in approximately 120 AH. And what is meant by al-kudii is the "grave". And it is also said to mean "lamentation". What the tradition means is that 'Abd al-Mucplih died while still being a member of pre-Islamic society; this contrasts with the views of some of the Shi'a about him and Abii Talib. This will be dealt with in the material on the death of Abn Talib. Al-Bayhaqi stated, after relating these traditions in his work Dalii'rl al-Nubizwwa (Szgns of the Prophethood), "And how would the father and grandfather of the Prophet (SAAS) not be in such circumstances regarding the hereafter when they used to worship idols until they died, and they did not follow the religion of Jesus son of Mary upon whom be peace? Yet their having been unbelievers did not impair the lineage of the Prophet (SAAS), because marriages between unbelievers were valid. Did not many men adopt Islam, along with their wives, hut not have either to renew their marriage contracts or to separate from their wives, since these would similarly be allowed under Islam? And to God goes all success." Thus ended his comments. My own comment is, that the relating by the Prophet (SAAS) that his own parents and his grandfather 'Abd al-Munalib were in hell does not contradict the tradition related from him via various authorities that those who lived during the interregnum between the emergence of two prophets, children, the insane, and the deaf would all be put to the test in the assemblies on Judgement Day. This we laid out with chapter and verse in our Qur'm exegesis dealing with the Almighty's words: "and We have never punished before sending a prophet" (snrat al-Isrz', XVII, v.15). There will be some among these groups who will respond and others who will not. Those people, then, will be among those who will not respond; there is no inconsistency here, grace and praise be to God. As for the tradition related by al-Suhayli about which it was mentioned that there are persons in its lineage who are unknown, traced back to the son of Abii al-Zinad, from Wrwa from 'A'isha, God bless her, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked his Lord to bring his parents back to life and that God did so and that they expressed faith in him; this is a highly unreliable tradition. Even though all this would be possible in terms of the power of God Almighty. However, what is well established in the ~ a h i hcollections does contradict this tradition. And God knows best.
lessons: Difficult theological questions about loved ones' spiritual fate require patient scholarly inquiry rather than hasty conclusions.
God's justice is always balanced with His mercy; the principle that punishment only follows the sending of a prophet reflects divine fairness.
Even the most painful uncertainties about those we love can be held with trust in God's ultimate wisdom and perfect judgment.
practical_advice: When faced with distressing religious questions about deceased loved ones, seek grounded scholarly guidance rather than dwelling in anxiety.
Accept that some theological matters contain complexity and nuance; resist the urge to force simple answers onto profound questions.
Trust that God's justice is comprehensive and perfect, and let that trust bring peace to your heart when you cannot resolve every question yourself.

title: Abd al-Muttalib's Special Care for the Young Prophet
summary: After the death of his mother Amina, the young Prophet Muhammad came to live with his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib, who showed him extraordinary love, dignity, and care, recognizing his greatness.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) lived with his grandfather 'Abd al-Muttalib b. Hashim - that is, after the death of his mother Amina daughter of Wahb - a spread would be set out for 'Abd al-Mu~alihin the shade of the Kacba. His sons would seat themselves around his spread until he would come to it. But none of his sons would actually sit upon it, out of respect for him. The Messenger of God (SAAS), when a young boy, would come along and sit down on it. Then his uncles would take him to remove him from it, but 'Abd al-Mncralih would say, if he saw them doing that, 'Leave my son (sic) alone; he has great things ahead of him.' Then he would sit down with him on his spread, rub his hack, and it would please him to watch what he would do." Al-Waqidi said that Muhammad b. 'Abd All& related to him, from al-Zuhri, as did 'Ahd Allah h. Jacfar kom 'Abd al-WGd b. Hamza b. 'Abd All&, and Hashim b. CAsim al-Aslami from al-Mundhir h. Jahm, and Macmar from Ibn Aha Najih from Mujahid and 'Abd al-Rahan h. 'Abd al-'Aziz from Aba al-Huwayrith, and Ibn Aba Sabra from Sulayman b. S*im, from N&, from Ibn Jubayr, the narration of some interfering with that of others, as follows: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to live with his mother Amina daughter of Wahh and when she died his grandfather 'Abd al-Munalib took him to himself, treating him with a closeness and kindness he had not shown to his own son. H e would allow and encourage him to come close to him, and the boy would go in to him when he was alone or asleep. He would sit down on his spread, and 'Abd al-Mugalib would say when he saw him do this, 'Leave my son alone; he will establish a kingdom.'" A group of men from Banii Mudlij said to 'Abd al-Munalib, "Take good care of him; for we have never seen anyone with so noble a lineage as his." So 'Abd al-Mn&dih said to Aba Talib: "Listen to what these men are saying." And Abii T a i b did take care of him. 'Ahd al-Mugalih said to Umm Ayman - who had suckled him - "0 Baraka, do not neglect my son. I found him with some boys close to the lote-tree; and the People of the Book (i.e. Christians and Jews) claim that my boy will he the Prophet of this nation." 'Ahd al-Mugalib used never to eat without saying, "Bring my son." And he would he brought to him. And when death approached 'Ahd al-Mu~alihhe charged Abii Tdih with the care and safe-keeping of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Then 'Abd al-Mutwlib died and was buried at al-Hajiin.
lessons: A caring guardian who recognizes and honors the potential in a child can profoundly shape that child's sense of dignity and belonging.
Loss of a parent early in life does not mean a child is without love; God provides care through other means and people.
Those who protect and nurture others — especially the vulnerable — fulfill a deeply noble role that carries lasting impact.
practical_advice: If you are grieving the loss of a parent or caregiver, reflect on the people God has placed in your life who have shown you love and care.
When you feel overlooked or pushed aside by others, remember that your true worth is recognized by those who matter and ultimately by God Himself.
Seek to be the kind of person who sees and honors the potential in those around you, especially children who may feel alone or displaced.

title: The Death of Abd al-Muttalib and His Elegies
summary: Abd al-Muttalib, grandfather and guardian of the young Prophet, died when the Prophet was eight years old, mourned by his daughters with eloquent elegies composed in his presence before his death.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "And when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was eight, his grandfather 'Ahd d-Muffalih died." He also stated that 'Ahd al-Mutdib had summoned his daughters and told them that they should compose elegies for him; these daughters were called Ama, Umayma, Barra, Safiyya, 'Atika, and Umm Hakim al-Bayda'. Ibn Ishaq mentioned their poems and what they said in mourning their father, all while he listened, before he in fact died. Their words of mourning were extremely eloquent. He described that in great detail. Ibn Hishzm said, "I never met any scholar of poetry who knew these verses." Ibn Ishsq stated, "And when 'Abd al-Munalib b. H%hii died, control over providing water for the pilgrims and over zarnzarn went after him to d-'Abhas who was the youngest of his brothers. This control remained his until the coming of Islam, after which the Messenger of God (SAAS) confirmed it for alLCAbbas.
lessons: Preparing for death with dignity and allowing loved ones to express grief openly is a sign of wisdom and emotional maturity.
The loss of a beloved guardian is one of life's most profound sorrows, yet life and its responsibilities continue for those who remain.
Honoring the legacy of those who have passed through remembrance and heartfelt expression is a healthy and meaningful form of grief.
practical_advice: When you lose someone dear, allow yourself to grieve fully rather than suppressing your sorrow — expressing grief is not weakness but a human necessity.
Find ways to honor those you have lost, whether through words, acts of service, or carrying forward the values they instilled in you.
Understand that responsibilities and roles must be passed on after loss; accepting new duties can be part of the healing process after bereavement.

title: Abu Talib Takes the Prophet into His Care
summary: After the death of Abd al-Muttalib, Abu Talib took his nephew the young Prophet Muhammad into his home and cared for him with extraordinary love, noticing miraculous blessings in his presence.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was with his uncle Ahii Tzlih after having been with his grandfather 'Ahd al-Mnttalih. This was because the latter had placed him in trust of his uncle and because Aba Talih was the full brother of his father 'Abd Allah, the mother of both having been Fatima daughter of 'Amr h. 'k'idh h. Clmrzn b. Makhziim." Ibn Ishaq went on: "Abii Talih was the one who took charge of the Messenger of God (SAAS); he cared for him completely." Al-Waqidi stated that Macmar told him, from Ibn Najih, from Mujshid; and that Ma$dh b. Muhammad al-Ansm related to him from 'A@', from Ihn 'Abbas, as did Muhammad h. Sdih and 'Ahd Allah h. Ja'far, as did Ihr&im h. Ismacil h. Abn Hahiha, the account of each of these corresponding with one another, as follows: "When 'Abd al-Mutr;llih died Aha Talib took the Messenger of God (SAAS) to live with him. "Aba Talih was not wealthy; he loved the boy very much, more so than his children. The boy slept always by his side and went wherever he did. "Abn Talih had very great and unusual depth of affection for him. He would give the boy's food special attention. And if his own children ever ate, alone or as a group, without the boy's presence, they would remain unsatisfied, whereas when the Prophet would eat with them they would be well satisfied. When serving his family a daytime meal he would tell them to wait till the boy's arrival. When the Messenger of God would arrive and eat with them, they would get satisfied, and part of the food would remain uneaten. But if he did not eat with them they would remain unsatisfied. Abii TSib would say, 'You are clearly blessed.' "In the morning the other boys would arise bleary-eyed and dishevelled while the Messenger of God (SAAS) would get up bright-eyed and with his hair in place." Al-Hasan b. 'Arafa stated that 'Ali b. Thabit related to him from Talha b. (Amr that the latter heard 'Ap' b. Abii RabbSh report having heard Ibn (Abbzs say, "Abii Tdib's family would get up in the morning with bleary, dirty eyes, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) would get up bright-eyed and with his hair neatly groomed." Early each morning Abii T a b would bring to the boys their food platter and they would all sit down and snatch at it. The Messenger of God (SAAS), however, would keep his hand away and not grab at the food along with them. When his uncle saw this he set food aside for him alone.
lessons: God's blessings can manifest through the people He places in our lives to care for us during times of loss and transition.
The presence of a righteous and blessed person brings goodness and barakah to those around them — our company and character affect others profoundly.
Contentment, patience, and dignified conduct — even as a child — are marks of a noble character that others will recognize and honor.
practical_advice: When you are going through a period of loss or transition, look for the people God has appointed to support you and receive their care with gratitude.
Cultivate habits of patience and dignity in your daily life, even in small matters like how you eat or present yourself — these habits shape your character over time.
If you are in a position to care for someone vulnerable, do so wholeheartedly; the impact of sincere love and guardianship on a person in need is immeasurable.

title: The Fortune-Teller of Lihb Recognizes the Prophet's Greatness
summary: A fortune-teller from the tribe of Lihb, brought to Mecca to predict the futures of boys, becomes intensely interested in the young Prophet and declares that he has a great future, though Abu Talib quickly takes the boy away.
story: Ihn Ishaq said, "Y&'ya b. 'Abd All& b. al-Zubayr related to me that his father told h i that there was a certain man of Lihb who could predict the future. Whenever he came to Mecca the men of Quraysh would take their boys to him to see and predict their futures." He went on, "So Abij Talib brought the Messenger of God (SAAS) to him when he was still a boy, along with some others. The fortune-teller looked at the Messenger of God, but then something engaged his attention. When finished with that, he said, 'Bring that boy over to me!' When Abii T d i b saw how interested the man was in him, he took him away. Then the man exclaimed, 'Woe on you! Bring back to me that boy I saw earlier; by God, he has a big future ahead of him!' "But Abii T d i b humed away with him."
lessons: The signs of greatness in a person may be recognized by others long before that greatness is fully manifest — trust the process of your own unfolding.
A protective guardian who shields those in their care from potential harm or exploitation acts with true wisdom and love.
God's plan for a person cannot be thwarted by the curiosity or intentions of others; divine protection surrounds those chosen for a great purpose.
practical_advice: When others express unusual curiosity or interest in you or someone you care for, it is wise to exercise discernment about their motives before engaging further.
If you feel that your potential has not yet been recognized or that your path is unclear, trust that what God has written for you will come to pass in its appointed time.
Reflect on the people in your life who have protected you from harm — express gratitude for their watchfulness, and seek to be that kind of protector for others.

title: The Journey to Syria and the Encounter with the Monk Bahira
summary: Abu Talib travels with a trading caravan to Syria, taking the young Prophet along after the boy became distressed at the prospect of separation; near Busra, a learned Christian monk named Bahira recognizes remarkable signs in the Prophet.
story: Section: The journey of the Prophet (+WAS) with his uncle Abn Tzlib away to Syria and on what transpired with the monk Bahirz. Ibn IshZq stated that thereafter Aha Talib left with a trading caravan for Syria. As he was preparing to depart and assembling the goods, the Messenger of God (SAAS) began to miss him badly, or so they claim. So Aba Tndib took pity on him and said words to the effect that, "By God, 1'11 take him away with me and never he parted from h i , just as he will not be parted from me!" He did leave with him. Eventually the caravan made a halt at BusrZ in Syria, where there was a monk named Bahira living in a cell. He was very learned in Christianity; the cell he lived in had always been occupied by some monk who would acquire their Christian learning from a book, as they claim, which they had passed down in inheritance from one elder to the next. They stopped that year near Balgra, as they had often done previously without his paying them attention. When they halted, then, near his cell, this time he made a lot of food for them. This, they claim, was because of a vision he had seen while he was in his celi; he had seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) approaching in the caravan, with a cloud casting its shade only on him. When they had arrived they had dismounted in the shade of a tree near the monk. When he looked up at the cloud he saw it was shading the tree, the branches of which were extending out over the Messenger of God (SAAS) so that he was shaded
lessons: Genuine compassion for those we love — responding to their distress with presence rather than absence — strengthens bonds and can change the course of someone's life.
Signs of divine favor and special purpose can be visible to those with knowledge and awareness, even before the person themselves fully understands their mission.
God's protection over those He has chosen is visible and consistent — the cloud of divine care follows them through every journey.
practical_advice: When someone you love is anxious about being separated from you, consider whether presence and reassurance is more important in that moment than your own convenience.
Reflect on the moments in your life when you felt divinely guided or protected even before you understood why — these are reminders that God's care for you is real and ongoing.
When you embark on a new journey or chapter in life, trust that God's shade of protection accompanies you, just as it accompanied the Prophet even as a young child on the road to Syria.

title: Bahira the Monk Recognizes the Prophet's Signs
summary: During a trading journey to Syria, the monk Bahira recognized signs of prophethood in the young Muhammad (SAAS), including the cloud shading him and the seal between his shoulders, and urged his uncle Abu Talib to protect him.
story: then, near his cell, this time he made a lot of food for them. This, they claim, was because of a vision he had seen while he was in his celi; he had seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) approaching in the caravan, with a cloud casting its shade only on him. When they had arrived they had dismounted in the shade of a tree near the monk. When he looked up at the cloud he saw it was shading the tree, the branches of which were extending out over the Messenger of God (SAAS) so that he was shaded beneath them. When Bahira saw this he came down from his cell and, having ordered food which was then prepared, he sent word over to the travellers saying, "0 Quraysh, I have prepared food for you and would like you all to come over, great and small, and freemen or slaves." One Quraysh man commented to him, "By God, B&ir%,you've put yourself to much trouble today! You've never done this for us, even though we've passed by you here often before. Why today?" B&ira replied, "You are right; before it was as you say. But you are my guests, and I wanted to honour you and so have prepared you some food. I want all of you to eat of it." So they gathered around him. But the Messenger of God (SAAS), because of his youth, was left behind with the baggage, beneath the tree. When Ba)?ira saw them he did not see the quality he had seen and known to be the boy's. So he said, "0 Quraysh let not any one of you be left out of my feast." They replied, "0Balgra, the only one left out who ought to have come to you is a boy; he is the youngest of our party and he remained behind with the baggage." B&ira insisted. "Do not do this; call him over and let him attend this feast along with you." One of the Quraysh party exclaimed, "By al-Lat and al-Wzza it really is a shame on us that Muhammad son of 'Abd Allah son of (Abd al-Mutplib be kept back from eating the food among us." Then he went over to the boy, brought him back, and sat him down among the rest. When Bahira saw him he began to scrutinize him very intently, looking at various parts of his body and finding certain qualities in him. Finally, when all had h i s h e d dining and had dispersed, B a r %went up to the boy and said, "Boy, by al-Lat and al-'Uzzira I charge you to answer what I will ask you." Bahira only used these words because he had heard the boy's people swearing by these two gods. They claim that the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him: "Don't ask me anything by al-Lzt and al-Wzza; for, by God, I hate nothing more than them." So B*ira said, "By God, would you tell me about what I ask you?" So the boy told h i to ask whatever he liked. The monk then asked h i various questions about his sleep, habits, circumstances, and so on; and the Messenger of God (SAAS) responded. And what B&ira heard matched the description he expected. Then he looked at the boy's back and saw the seal of prophethood between his shoulders, in the very place according to the description he had. Having concluded this, he went over to the boy's uncle Abn Talib and asked, "What relation is he to you?" "He is my son," he replied. B&im objected, "He's not your son. This boy can't have a living father." "Well," Abii Tdib replied, "he's actually my nephew." "And what happened to his father?" the monk asked. "He died while the boy's mother was still pregnant with him." "Now you're telling the truth," the monk went on. "Take your brother's son back to his own country and guard him from the Jews. For, by God, if they see him and know what I know, they will do him evil. This nephew of yours has a great future before him; take him back soon to his own country." So his uncle Abti Talib left with him for Mecca quickly, as soon as he had finished his business in Syria. Ibn Ishzq stated, "They claim, according to what people said, that Zurayr, Tamm-, and Daris - all believers in the Scriptures - had also seen what Bahirira noticed in the Messenger of God (SAAS) on that nip with his uncle Aha Tiralib. They tried to get him, but Bahira kept them away from him, making reference to God and to the description of h i and mention of him they would find in the Holy Scripture, as well as the statement that they would not succeed in getting him. They recognized the reference he was making to them, and since they believed what he said they let the boy alone and went away." Ynnus b. Bukayr recalled, on the authority of Ibn Ishzq, that Abti Tdib spoke three odes on this subject. This is bow Ihn Ishzq related this sequence of events, but he gave no chain of authorities for it. A similar account also came down by way of a musnad mar@'.6s The hzfi? Abn Bakr al-Khara'iti stated that (Abbas b. Muhammad al-Dori related to him, as did Qarad Abn Nnh, as did Ynnus, from Ihn Ishaq, from Abn Bakr h. Aba Masa, from his father, as follows: "Abii Tdib went off to Syria along with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and a number of Quraysh elders. When they reached a point overlooking the monk - meaning B&ira- they went down and untied their baggage. The monk then came out to them, whereas previously when they had passed by he had not come out or even turned towards them." He went on, "He came down, then, as they were untying their baggage, and walked among them until he came and took the Prophet (SAAS) by the hand and said, 'This is the Lord of all mankind!'" In the account of al-Bayhaqi the phrase he used was longer, saying also, "This is the Messenger of the Lord of the worlds; God sent him as a mercy to all mankind." Some of the Quraysh elders then asked him, "What is it you know?" He replied, "When you looked down from the mountain road every single tree and every rock bent down in worship. And they would only prostrate themselves before a prophet. And I know h i by the mark of prophethood below the cartilage of his shoulder blades." He then went hack and prepared food for them. When he brought it, the boy was tending to the camels. The monk told them to send for him. As he approached a cloud was shading him, and as the boy came near the others the monk exclaimed, "See; there is a cloud above hi!" When the boy arrived he found that the others had preceded h i to the shade of the tree, but when he sat down the shade moved out over hi. The monk commented, "Just look how the tree's shade moved over him!" While B+ra was standing there urging them not to take him into Byzantine territory, saying that when they saw him the Romans would recognize h i by his description and would kill him, he turned around and, to his surprise, saw seven Romans who had arrived. He greeted them and asked why they had come. They replied, "We have information that there's this prophet who will come forth this month and so we have been sending men along each route; news we had of him brought us along this route of yours." "Do you", BaMra asked, "have anyone superior to you coming along behind you?" They replied, "No; it's just the information we had about him brought us along this road of yours." Ba@r% then asked them, "Have you ever known any matter God wished to bring about that anyone was able to reverse?" They said they had not, and then they pledged him allegiance and stayed with him there at B&ira's place. The account goes on, reporting that the monk then asked, "I abjure you by God to tell me, which of you is his guardian?" "Aba Talib," they replied. Bahira kept on admonishing him (Aba Tdib) until he took the boy hack (to Mecca); Abii Bakr sent Bid along with him and the monk provisioned him with cake and oil.
lessons: Divine protection accompanies those chosen by God even before they are aware of their own mission — the cloud of shade over the young Prophet signifies that true worth does not go unnoticed by the universe.
Integrity and inner conviction are visible long before a person rises to prominence; the Prophet's refusal to swear by idols even as a child shows that authentic character is consistent at every age.

Those who are entrusted with the care of others carry a profound responsibility; Abu Talib's willingness to heed Bahira's warning and turn back quickly is a model of protective guardianship over those in our charge.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or left out of important gatherings, remember that divine attention is not dependent on human inclusion — your worth is recognised by God even when others pass you by.

Practice staying true to your values even in small, seemingly insignificant moments; the Prophet's refusal to swear by idols in a casual exchange reflects how character is built through consistent choices in everyday situations.

If someone wiser or more experienced offers you protective counsel, take it seriously rather than dismissing it out of pride or inconvenience — discernment often comes to us through unexpected messengers.

title: The Journey to Syria and the Monk Bahira's Warning
summary: At age twelve, the Prophet Muhammad traveled with his uncle Abu Talib to Syria, where the monk Bahira warned Abu Talib to protect the boy from the Jews and return him to Mecca. Abu Talib heeded the warning and sent him back safely.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that Muhammad b. Salih related to him, as did 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far and Ibrahim b. Isma'il b. Abi Habiba, from Dawud b. al-Husayn, as follows: "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached the age of twelve, his uncle Abu Talib took him along to Syria in the caravan he was accompanying to engage in business there. They made a halt with the monk Bahira, who made some statements in confidence to Abu Talib. Bahira told him to look after the boy and Abu Talib sent him back to Mecca. The Messenger of God lived as a youth with Abu Talib, while God protected him and preserved him from the evils of the jahiliyya period for the honour He wished for him." Muhammad b. Sa'd stated, "Khalid b. Ma'dan told us, Mu'tamir b. Sulayman related to us, that he heard his father relate from Abu Mijlaz that 'Abd al-Muttalib or Abu Talib - Khalid was unsure which one - said, that when 'Abd Allah died he was sorry for Muhammad and never went away on any trip without taking him with him. And when he was on the way to Syria and stopped to set up camp, a monk came to him there and said, 'You have a man of goodness among you.' Then he said, 'Where is the father of this boy?' The reply was given, 'I am his guardian' (or it was said, 'he is his guardian'). The monk commented: 'Take care of this boy; do not take him to Syria. The Jews are jealous, and I fear for him from them.' He was told, 'It is not you who are saying that but God himself.' So he did take him back, and said, 'O God, I place Muhammad in your care!' Then he died." Al-Suhayli related, quoting the biographical writings of al-Zuhri, that Bahira was a Jewish high priest. My comment is, that it appears from the course of the story that he was a Christian monk. But God knows best. From al-Mas'udi comes the information that he was a man of the 'Abd al-Qays tribe, and that his name was Jirjis. In the work al-Ma'arif (Knowledge) of ibn Qutayba it states, "A voice was heard in the jahiliyya period, shortly before Islam, calling out the words, 'O yea, the finest of men on earth are three: Bahira, Ri'ab b. al-Bana' al-Shanni, and the third is still awaited.'" The third and one awaited was the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Divine protection precedes human understanding — the Prophet was guarded before he himself knew his own greatness, reminding us that God's care for us often works through ordinary people and circumstances.
Trustworthy guardians who act on sincere concern, like Abu Talib heeding Bahira's warning, are among God's greatest gifts to the vulnerable.
Recognition of goodness in a person can come from unexpected sources; remaining open to sincere counsel, regardless of its origin, is a mark of wisdom.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about a journey or major life transition, take time to place your trust explicitly in God, as Abu Talib did when he said, 'O God, I place Muhammad in your care!'
If you are responsible for someone vulnerable, take warnings about their wellbeing seriously and act on them even when it is inconvenient or costly.
Reflect on moments in your past where you were protected from harm without fully realizing it at the time — this practice builds gratitude and trust in God's ongoing care.

title: The Prophet's Character in Youth: Named Al-Amin
summary: Before prophethood, Muhammad grew up as the most honourable and trustworthy man among the Quraysh, protected by God from the vices of the pre-Islamic era, and was known by the title Al-Amin, 'the trustworthy one.'
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) became a youth under the care and protection of God who saved him from the evils of the jahiliyya because of the honour and mission He wished for him. When he grew up he was the finest man among his people, the best of them in character and repute, the best of neighbours, the most prudent, the most honest in speech and most trustworthy, and the one most removed from immorality and those characteristics that corrupt men, which he avoided because of his nobility." Consequently the name by which he was known among Quraysh was none other than al-Amin, "the trustworthy", because of the fine qualities God had joined in him. When he grew up he was consequently the most honourable man of his people, the best in character, the most noble in his relationships, the best neighbour, the most prudent, trustworthy and truthful, and the man most removed from immorality or harmfulness. He was never seen to do anyone harm or to quarrel with anyone, so that his people named him al-Amin, "the trustworthy one" because of all the fine qualities God had joined together in him. Abu Talib protected and cared for him, aided and assisted him until his death.
lessons: True trustworthiness is built through consistent, small everyday choices — the Prophet's reputation was earned through a lifetime of integrity, not a single grand act.
Character is itself a form of worship; being the best neighbour, the most honest in speech, and the most prudent are expressions of closeness to God.
The community's recognition of a person's goodness — like the title Al-Amin — reflects how authentic virtue naturally earns the trust of others without needing to be sought.
practical_advice: Identify one area of your daily life where you can strengthen your trustworthiness — whether in keeping promises, being honest in small matters, or showing up reliably for those who depend on you.
When you feel lost about your identity or purpose, consider what qualities those who know you best would use to describe you, and use that as a mirror for genuine self-reflection.
Make a conscious effort this week to be 'the best neighbour' in whatever community you belong to — at work, at home, or online — through small acts of care and consideration.

title: God Protects the Prophet from Indecency During the Building of the Ka'ba
summary: While carrying stones for a boyhood game, the young Muhammad received a painful unseen slap that commanded him to cover himself properly, an event echoed later when he was similarly stopped from walking uncovered during the rebuilding of the Ka'ba.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS), as I was told, used to relate about those (pre-Islamic) practices, matters from which God had protected him in his youth and time of ignorance. He said, "Once I happened to be amongst some Quraysh boys carrying some stones for those games boys play. Each of us was exposed, having taken up his shirt and fastened it around his neck to carry stones in. I was running along with them in this way and having fallen behind I suddenly received a very painful slap from someone who spoke the words, 'Put your shirt on properly!' So I took it and put it back on properly and began carrying the stones around my neck with my shirt on, alone among my friends." 'Abd al-Razzaq said that Ibn Jurayj informed him, quoting 'Amr b. Dinar, that he heard Jabir b. 'Abd Allah say, "When the ka'ba was being built, the Messenger of God (SAAS) went off to transport stones. Al-'Abbas said to the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Put your shirt over your shoulder for the stones.' He did so, but fell to the ground and his eyes were drawn up to heaven. Then he got up and said, 'It was my shirt.' So it was put back on him properly." Al-Bayhaqi related from a tradition of Yunus b. Bukayr, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, that Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah b. Qays b. Makhrama related to him, from al-Hasan b. Muhammad b. 'Ali b. Abi Talib, from his father and from his grandfather, 'Ali b. Abi Talib, who said that he heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say that Ibn 'Abbas related from his father: "I was with my nephew, carrying the rocks, wearing our shirts around our necks; when we came up to other people we covered up. While I was walking along with Muhammad in front of me, he fell down and stretched out flat on his face. I hurried along, threw down my rocks, meanwhile he was looking up to heaven. 'What's wrong?' I asked. He got up and took his shirt, saying, 'I was forbidden from walking uncovered.'" Ibn 'Abbas went on, "I hid this from the others for fear they would say (he) was mad."
lessons: God's protection of the Prophet from indecency before prophethood shows that divine preparation for a great mission begins long before the mission is announced — our character is being shaped even when we are unaware.
Modesty and dignity are not merely social conventions; they are forms of God-consciousness that protect the soul from corruption.
Sometimes God's guidance comes through sudden, jarring experiences that redirect us away from harm — remaining receptive to correction, even when it is unexpected, is a sign of a sound heart.
practical_advice: When you feel suddenly stopped or redirected in life — through an unexpected obstacle, a felt discomfort, or a change in circumstances — consider whether this might be a form of divine protection rather than mere inconvenience.
Practice mindfulness of your own dignity in daily life: how you present yourself, what environments you enter, and what you expose yourself to all shape your character over time.
If you witness someone gently correcting another person's behaviour, reflect on how correction delivered in the right moment — even anonymously — can redirect a life for the better.

title: The Prophet Protected from Nighttime Immorality on Two Occasions
summary: Twice in his youth, the Prophet Muhammad attempted to join other young men in their nighttime entertainment in Mecca but was put to sleep by God both times before witnessing any immorality, and he never attempted such things again.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related from a tradition of Yunus b. Bukayr, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from 'Ali b. Abi Talib, who said that he heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, "I wasn't interested in performing those things with women that people during the jahiliyya used to do; except, that is, on two nights on which God, Almighty and Glorious is He, restrained me. "One evening I and a young man of Mecca were watching some sheep belonging to some people there. I said to him, 'Would you keep an eye on my sheep so I can go into Mecca and spend an evening there like other lads?' 'Sure,' he replied. So I went into town and stopped at the first house where I heard the music of tambourines and flutes. I asked what was going on and was told that a man and woman had married. So I sat down to watch. But God struck my ears and, I swear, next thing I knew I was being awakened by the sun's touch. I went back to my friend, who asked what I had done. I replied, 'I didn't do a thing.' Then I told him what I had seen. "Then one other night I asked him to watch my sheep so I could go to a party. He agreed, so I went off to Mecca and heard the same as I had the previous occasion. Again I was told that there was a marriage and so sat down to watch. But God again struck my ears and, I swear, I was later awakened by the sun's touch. "When I returned to my friend he asked what I had done. 'Not a thing,' I replied, and then I told him what had happened. And, by God, I never tried or returned to anything like that thereafter right up to when Almighty and Glorious God honoured me with His prophethood."
lessons: God's protection does not always work by removing temptation but by neutralising our exposure to it — sometimes the most merciful intervention is one we only understand in retrospect.

The fact that the Prophet himself was drawn toward ordinary human curiosity before being divinely redirected is a source of compassion and relatability; our struggles with temptation do not make us irredeemable.

Persistent divine protection can serve as a turning point: after two such experiences, the Prophet voluntarily abandoned the pursuit entirely, showing that God's intervention is meant to cultivate lasting moral resolve, not merely short-term avoidance.
practical_advice: When you find yourself repeatedly pulled toward something that ultimately leaves you feeling empty or ashamed, treat this pattern as a signal to step back and ask what deeper need is driving it — and seek to meet that need in a healthier way.
Reflect on moments in your life when you were 'stopped' from doing something you later realised would have harmed you — let these memories build your trust in God's unseen care.

If you are struggling with temptation in a particular area, consider sharing accountability with a trusted friend, as the Prophet did with his companion who watched the sheep — community accountability is a time-honoured aid to moral growth.

title: The Prophet Refuses to Venerate Idols and Rejects Their Invocation
summary: From his youth, the Prophet Muhammad consistently refused to salute or touch the idols of the Quraysh, and when Bahira attempted to invoke al-Lat and al-Uzza, the Prophet expressed strong aversion to them.
story: Al-Bayhaqi said that others added, from Muhammad b. 'Amr, giving a chain of authorities, that Zayd said, "By Him who honoured him and revealed to him the Holy Book, he (the Prophet) never saluted an idol right up to when God Almighty honoured him as He did and gave revelation to him." The hafiz al-Bayhaqi said that Abu Sa'd al-Malini informed us, Abu Ahmad b. 'Adi, the hafiz told us, Ibrahim b. Ashat related to us, as did 'Uthman b. Abi Shayba, as did Jarir, from Sufyan al-Thawri, from Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. 'Uqayl, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, God bless him. We earlier made reference to the comment of the Messenger of God (SAAS) when Bahira put a question to him invoking al-Lat and al-'Uzza: "By God, do not question me by invoking them! I hate nothing so much as them!" There was a brass idol called 'Isaf and Na'ila which the polytheists would touch as they performed their circumambulation. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said 'Don't touch it.' Zayd continued, "Well, as we went round (again) I told myself I would touch it to see what would happen. When I did so, the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked me, 'Were you not forbidden to do that?'"
lessons: Clarity about what we refuse to bow to — whether idols, social pressures, or corrupt values — is foundational to integrity; the Prophet's lifelong aversion to idols shows that firm conviction does not waver even in environments of overwhelming conformity.

Speaking truth about what we find reprehensible, as the Prophet did when he said 'I hate nothing so much as them,' is an act of moral courage that protects both the speaker and those who witness their stance.

When someone in a position of spiritual or moral authority tells us not to do something, testing that boundary out of curiosity — as Zayd did with the idol — rarely leads to wisdom and often leads to accountability.
practical_advice: Identify the 'idols' in your own life — whether they are status, approval, money, or habits — that you give disproportionate power over your choices, and take one small step this week to reclaim your freedom from one of them.

Practice saying clearly and without apology what you find morally objectionable, especially in social situations where everyone else seems to accept it — the Prophet's example shows that this kind of clarity is possible without anger or aggression.

When you receive sincere guidance from someone you trust, resist the urge to test boundaries just to see what happens — trust is built by honouring counsel, not by treating it as a starting point for experimentation.

title: The Prophet's Aversion to Idols Before Revelation
summary: Before receiving prophethood, the Prophet Muhammad avoided participating in polytheistic ceremonies and expressed strong aversion to idols, as confirmed by multiple traditions including an account of angels discussing his avoidance of idol salutation.
story: Messenger of God (SAAS) when Bahira put a question to him invoking al-LZt and al-Wzza: "By God, do not question me by invoking them! I hate nothing so much as them!" As for the tradition given by the Abii Bakr al-Bayhaqi, Aba Sa'd al-Malini informed us, Abn m a d b. 'Adi, the h#fi& told us, Ibmhim b. Ashat related to us, as did Wthmm h. Aba Shayha, as did Jarir, from Sufyzn al-Thawri, from Muhammad b. 'Ahd Allah b. Muhammad b. Wqayl, from Jabii h. 'Abd Allah, God bless him, as follows: "The Prophet (SAAS) used to attend the ceremonies of the polytheists along with them. But once he heard two angels behind him, one saying to the other, 'Let's move up and stand right behind the Messenger of God (SAAS).' But the other objected, 'How can we stand right behind him when he is in the habit of saluting idols?'" He went on, "And thereafter he never again attended such ceremonies with the polytheists." This is a tradition several authorities deny being attributed to Wthman b. Aba Shayha. Regarding it I m m Ahmad commented: "His brother would never speak any such words." Al-Bayhaqi related from various sources that his meaning was that he witnessed those who saluted idols, and that that was before God made revelation to him. But God knows best. We previously reported a tradition from Zayd b. Hzritha to the effect that he abstained from witnessing the ceremonies of the polytheists until God honoured h i with His mission.
lessons: The Prophet's natural aversion to idolatry before revelation shows that a pure heart instinctively recoils from falsehood, reminding us to trust our moral instincts.
Divine guidance can come through subtle signs and experiences even before formal revelation or clarity, encouraging us to remain attentive to the corrections life offers us.
Distancing oneself from environments that conflict with one's values is a form of spiritual integrity, even when social pressure encourages conformity.
practical_advice: When you feel an inner discomfort around certain environments or practices, take it seriously as a signal worth reflecting on rather than dismissing.
Gradually distance yourself from habits or gatherings that conflict with your values, even if the change is incremental as it was in the Prophet's life.
When others question your choices, respond with calm conviction as the Prophet did, stating clearly what you dislike without aggression.

title: The Prophet at 'Arafat Before Prophethood
summary: Before receiving revelation, the Prophet Muhammad participated in the standing at 'Arafat with his people, an act understood by scholars as a remnant of the Abrahamic tradition and a blessing from God upon him.
story: It is established in the tradition that he did not participate in the assembly at al-Muzdalifa during the night on Mt. 'Arafat. Indeed he did not assemble with the people at 'Arafat. Similarly Yanus b. Bukayr said, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, that 'Abd Allah b. Aba Bakr related to him, from Wthman h. Aba Sulayrnm, from NZfic b. Jubayr b. Mut'im, from his father Jubayr who said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) while he was a member of his people's religion. He would station himself there on a camel of his at 'Arafxt, among his people until he raced away with them, God the Almighty and Glorious giving h i blessing thereby." Al-Bayhaqi stated, "The meaning of the words 'a member of his people's religion' refers to the remnants of the heritage of Abraham and Ishmael, on both of whom be peace. The Prophet (SAAS) never at any time associated with Allah any other god." I also comment, that from these words of (al-Bayhaqi) it is to be understood that he did attend the assembly at 'Arafat before he received revelation. And it was this that was a "blessing" to him from God the Almighty and Glorious. The Imam Al,unad related this tradition from Yacqtih, from Muhammad b. Ishaq. The words he used were: "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) before he received revelation while he was positioned on a camel of his with his people on 'Arafat so that he would move forward with them, this being a blessing from God." The Imzm A b a d said that Sufyan related to him, from 'Amr, from Muhammad b. Jubayr b. MucCim, from his father, saying, "I lost track of a camel of mine in the Wrana valley (close to 'Arafat) and went off to look for it. I found the Prophet (SAAS) in the assembly there. I said, 'He's one of the hums (a word used for Quraysh). What's he doing here?'"
lessons: God's blessings can be present in a person's life long before they are aware of their own special purpose, reminding us that divine care precedes our recognition of it.
Participation in wholesome communal traditions rooted in prophetic heritage carries its own spiritual blessing even without full conscious understanding.
A person of noble character stands out even in ordinary circumstances, as Jubayr's surprised observation of the Prophet illustrates.
practical_advice: Reflect on your own life for moments where you may have been divinely guided or protected without fully realizing it at the time, and cultivate gratitude for those unseen mercies.
Engage meaningfully in your community's positive traditions and gatherings, recognizing that communal worship and remembrance carry their own spiritual benefit.
When you feel out of place or different from those around you, take comfort in the Prophet's example that standing apart from the crowd can itself be a form of divine distinction.

title: The Fijar War — The Sacrilegious War
summary: During the Prophet's youth, a series of violent battles known as the Fijar or Sacrilegious War erupted between the tribes of Kinana and Qays 'Aylan after al-Barrad ibn Qays treacherously killed Urwa al-Rahhal during the sacred month, violating the long-standing prohibition on warfare in holy months.
story: An Account of how the Prophet ($US) witnessed the war known as al-fijar, the sacrilegious war. Ibn Ishaq said, "The 'sacrilegious' war raged at a time when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was 20. It was nanled t h e j j a r (sacrilegious) war because the two tribes Kinana and Qays 'Aylan acted as though the sacred month (when warfare was disallowed) was open for them to fighting. The leader of Quraysh and of Kinma was Harb b. Umayya b. 'Abd Shams. Early in the day of the battle, Qays were defeating Kinana, hut by midday the victory had gone to Kinana over Qays." Ibn Hisham stated, "When the Messenger of God reached 14 - or 15 - as Aba Wbayda the grammarian related to me, from Aha 'Amr b. al-'Ala', the 'sacrilegious' war broke out between Quraysh and its Kin%na supporters and Qays 'Ayl%n. "What started it was that Wrwa al-whit1 b. Wtba b. Jacfar b. Kilab b. Rabi'a b. CAmr b. Sa$ab b. Muswiya b. Bakr b. Haw%in had given a passing permission to a trading caravan of musk which belonged to al-Nucm%n b. al-Mundhir. And so al-Barrsd b. Qays, one of the tribe of Dumra b. Bakr b. 'Abd Manat b. Kinma asked, 'Are you going to apply the same against Kinana's will?' 'Yes,' was the reply, 'and against everyone.' "So Wrwa d-R&h%l set out with the trading party and d-Barrad left too, seeking to catch him unawares. When the party reached the high ground of Tayman Dha Tdd, Wrwa became careless and al-Bamitd fell upon him and killed him; this was during the sacred month. Consequently the warfare became known as 'sacrilegious'." Al-Barrad spoke the following verses about all this: "There's many a tragedy that distressed people before me that I, 0 Bano Bakr, stood fum against. Thereby I destroyed the houses of Bann Kilab and humbled the freed slaves to submission. I raised my fist against him at Dho Tila and he dropped like a felled tree." Labid b. Rabi'a b. Malik b. Jacfar b. Kilitb also composed the lines: "Tell Bano Kikh, if you chance on them, and 'Amir, that there are some who can master disasters, Tell Banii Numayr, if you chance on them, and the dead man's uncles of Bana ma, That the expected al-Rahgl now rests at Tayman Dho Tila." Ibn Hisham continued, "A man arrived and told Quraysh of it, saying, 'Al-Bmd has killed Wrwa, and during the sacred month, at 'Uk-.' They then rode off, unbeknown to Hawain. When IIawain learned of this they pursued them and caught up with them before they reached the sacred territory. They engaged in battle till nightfall but then they went inside the sacred territory and Hawain gave up on them. "They met for several days after that battle, with the people going forth under various banners with a leader from each of the tribes of Quraysh and Kinma, and a leader from each of the tribes of Qays. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was present at some of these battles, his uncles having taken him with themselves. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'I was giving arrows to my uncles.' That is, he would gather for them the arrows their enemy had shot at them." Ibn Hishan said, "The account of the 'sacrilegious' war is very long, longer than I have recounted. But I have been deterred from recounting it because it would have interrupted the telling of the biography of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Al-Suhayli said that al-jij~ris spelt with an "i" after the "P', in the like form to qml, fighting. Thejjdr wars among the Arabs totalled four, and al-Mas'iidi gave reference to all of them. He went on, "The last of them was this one involving al-Barrxd. The fighting in it consisted of four battles: the battle of Shamta, and the battle of al-'Ablx', both near Wkaz, and the battle of al-Sharb, which was the major battle. It was this one that the Messenger of God (SAAS) attended. For this battle the chieftains of Quraysh and Banii Kinana, respectively Harb b. Umayya and his brother Sufyan, made a pact not to take to flight. On that occasion Qays were defeated, except for Bann Nadr who held out. The fourth battle was at al-Harira, near Nakhla. Then they made an agreement to meet the following year at Wkaq. When they assembled to fulfil this appointment Wtba b. Rabi'a rode out on his camel and called out: '0 tribe of Mudar, what is it you are fighting for?' Hawein replied, 'What are you suggesting?' 'Peace,' he replied. 'How,' they asked. He said, 'We pay you blood-money for your dead, and will leave people hostage with you to guarantee payment; and we will forgo our blood-money you owe.' 'Who will guarantee us that?' 'I will,' he replied. 'And who are you?' they demanded. 'I am Wtba b. Rabi'a,' he told them. "On that basis peace was made, and they sent out to them 40 men, including Hakim b. Hizan. And when Banii 'Amr b. Sa'Sa'a saw the guarantee hostages in their bands they gave up their claims and the 'sacrilegious' war was at an end."
lessons: Violating sacred boundaries and covenants — even when framed as bold action — leads to prolonged communal suffering, teaching that short-term aggression rarely serves long-term peace.

Even in environments of conflict, a person of noble character like the young Prophet found a role that supported without escalating, showing that we can be present in difficult situations without becoming instruments of harm.
Peace achieved through honorable negotiation and mutual sacrifice, as Utba ibn Rabi'a demonstrated, is more dignified and lasting than victory through continued bloodshed.
practical_advice: When caught in conflicts between people or groups around you, look for the constructive supporting role rather than the destructive escalating one, as the young Prophet did by gathering arrows rather than fighting.
Recognize that breaking agreements or violating ethical boundaries — even once — can set off consequences far larger than anticipated, motivating careful integrity in your commitments.
When conflict seems intractable, consider whether you or someone trusted can step forward with a concrete peace proposal as Utba did, offering to absorb some loss for the sake of ending ongoing harm.

title: The Pact of al-Fudul — The Alliance of the Virtuous
summary: Before prophethood, the young Muhammad participated in the noble Pact of al-Fudul, a covenant made in the house of Abdullah ibn Jud'an in which the tribes of Quraysh pledged to defend the oppressed and uphold justice, a pact the Prophet later honored throughout his life.
story: CHAPTER: HOW THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS) WITNESSED THE PACT OF AL-FUDUL. The hZ&? al-Bayhaqi said that Aba Saki al-Malini told him, Abii Ahmad b. 'Adi, the hajiz, related to him, quoting Y & y b. 'Ali b. Hzshim al-Khaffxf, quoting Ismacil b. 'Aliyya, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Ishaq, from al-Zuhri, from Muhammad b. Jubayr b. Muy'im from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I was a witness, with my uncles, to the pact of al-murayyabin and I would not have wanted to renege - or some such word - for the very choicest herd." He (al-Bayhaqi) said that this tradition was related in these words also by Bishr b. al-Mufaddal, from 'Abd al-R&m2n. He also said that Abii Nasr b. Qatada related to him, quoting Abii 'Amr b. Mayar, quoting Abii Bakr b. &mad b. D2wiid al-Samnzni, quoting Mu'allii b. Mahdi, quoting Abii 'Aw%na, all from Wmar b. Abii Salama, from his father, from Abii Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said. 'LTheonly pact I witnessed Quraysh make was that of al-muyayyabin. And I'd not have abrogated it in return for the choicest herd." He said that al-mutayyabin referred to Hashim, Umayya, Zahra, and Makhzm. Al-Bayhaqi said, "This explanation is also interpolated within the tradition, but I do not know who it was who said it." Some biographers maintain that he was referring to the fudiil pact; for the Prophet (SAAS) was not yet born at the time of the mutayyabin pact. In my view, there is no disputing that. The fact is that Quraysh did conclude a treaty following the death of Qu~ayy. They disputed the fact of Qusayy having left to his son 'Abd al-Di%rthe right to control provision of food and drink to the pilgrims, raising the banner at the ka'ba, its guardianship, and the convening of the assembly. The tribe of 'Ahd Manaf disputed them these prerogatives and various of the Quraysh mhes gave alliance and help for victory to one or other side. So the partisans of Abd Manaf brought a howl filled with al-tib, nutmeg powder, into which they placed their hands and swore allegiance to one another. When they had done so, they wiped their hands over the corners of the house (the ka'ba). They were therefore named the muyayyabina, as mentioned above; this took place long ago. But what is meant by this alliance, is the treaty of al-fdiil, a word meaning "the excess" or "remnants". This was sworn in the home of 'Abd All& h. JudL;m, as al-Humaydi related it, from Sufym son of 'Uyayna, from 'Abd All&, from Muhammad and 'Abd al-&m, two sons of Abn Bakr. They stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I witnessed in the house of 'Abd Allah b. Jud% a treaty to which I would have responded"
lessons: The Prophet's lifelong pride in the Pact of al-Fudul shows that commitments to justice and the defense of the oppressed are among the most honorable a person can make, transcending religion, tribe, or era.
Standing for justice in one's community before receiving any formal religious role demonstrates that moral courage is a personal virtue, not merely an institutional obligation.
Covenants and agreements made for the protection of the vulnerable carry a sacred weight; honoring them even at personal cost reflects the highest character.
practical_advice: Seek out and participate in agreements, organizations, or community efforts dedicated to protecting those who are vulnerable or oppressed, recognizing this as among the most noble of actions.
Reflect on the commitments you have made to stand by others — friends, family, or community — and renew your resolve to honor them as the Prophet honored the Pact of al-Fudul throughout his life.
When you witness injustice in your community, ask yourself what practical covenant or collective action you could help initiate or join, rather than waiting for someone else to act.

title: The Pact of Al-Fudul (The Alliance of the Virtuous)
summary: A merchant from Zabid was wronged by a Meccan nobleman who refused to pay him for his goods. His public appeal for justice led the noble tribes of Quraysh to form the renowned Pact of Al-Fudul, pledging to always side with the oppressed against the oppressor.
story: the corners of the house (the ka'ba). They were therefore named the muyayyabina, as mentioned above; this took place long ago. But what is meant by this alliance, is the treaty of al-fdiil, a word meaning "the excess" or "remnants". This was sworn in the home of 'Abd All& h. JudL;m, as al-Humaydi related it, from Sufym son of 'Uyayna, from 'Abd All&, from Muhammad and 'Abd al-&m, two sons of Abn Bakr. They stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I witnessed in the house of 'Abd Allah b. Jud% a treaty to which I would have responded positively if, during Islam, I had been invited to participate in it. They made an agreement to return any excess to those who had owned it, and that no one should take unfair advantage over anyone." The authorities stated that the fdiil pact was concluded 20 years before the prophethood, in the eleventh month, Dha alQa'da, that date being some four months after the al-filar war. That was because the al-filar occurred in Sha'ba, the eighth month of the same year. The al-fdiil treaty was reputed the most fair and honourable pact reached among the Arabs. The first man who suggested and advocated it was al-Zubayr, son of 'Abd al-Mumlib. The cause for it was that a man from Zabid brought some goods to Mecca and these were brought from him by d-'A$ son of W$il who held back some of what was due to him. So the man from Zabid complained about him to his allies who were the tribes of 'Abd al-Diir, Makhziim, Jum*, Sahm, and 'Adi b. Ka'b, but they declined to help him against d-'AS son of Wa'il and instead they roughed him up and drove him away. Having experienced such ill-treatment, the man from Zabid approached Ahii Quhays at daybreak, while Quraysh were in their assemblies around the ka'ba, and called out at the top of his voice: "0 heirs of Fihr, there's a man being wronged of his goods, right here in the middle of Mecca, far from his own home and people. A man dishevelled and deprived, who has not completed his pilgrimage; fellow men, I'm right here at the very heart of the hgr.' The sacrosanct is for those whose nobility is complete; there is nothing sacrosanct for the robe of the immoral and treacherous." At this arose al-Zuhayr b. 'Abd al-MurtaJib and said, "This matter can't be ignored." So the men of H s h i m , Zuhra, and Taym h. Murra assembled in the home of 'Abd AIlnh b. Jud%n who prepared food for them. They established their pact in Dhn al-Qacda, a sacrosanct month, agreeing and swearing by God that they would act in unison with any wronged person against the wrongdoer until his rights were fulfilled, for so long as the sea makes wool wet and the mountains Thabir and Hirn' remain finnly set in their place and that they would comfort (people) as long as they are alive. Quraysh named that pact al-fudal, saying, 'LThnse men went into that matterfifadl, i.e. 'to excess'. Afterwards they walked over to al-'As b. W2il and took away from him the goods of the man from Zabid and returned them to him." Al-Zubayr b. 'Ahd al-Murtalib spoke the following verses about this pact: "I swore, Let's make a pact against them, though we're ail members of one tribe. We'll call it al-fdfit if we make a pact by it the stranger could overcome those under local protection, And those who go around the ka'ba will know that we reject injustice and will prevent all things shameful." Al-Zubayr also spoke the following: "Al-fufil made a pact and alliance that no evildoer shall dwell in Mecca's heart. This was a matter they firmly agreed and so the protected neighhour and the unprotected stranger are safe among them." @sim b. Thnbit related, in a curious hadith that, "A man of K h a t h h came to Mecca either as a pilgrim or to perform the 'urnra (the 'minor pilgrimage'), and brought with him a daughter of his named al-Qatiil, an exceedingly modest girl. But Nabih b. al-Hajj2j seized her and took her away from him. So the man of Khath'am said, 'Who will help me against this man?' H e was advised to invoke the pact of al-fudsl. So he stood at the ka'ba and called out, '0 you who made the pact of al-f4al!' Men flocked to him from all sides, their swords unsheathed and shouting, 'Help is here; what's the matter?' He told them how Nabih had wronged him by taking his daughter from him by force. The men went with him to Nabih's house and he came out to them. They told him, 'Bring the girl out! You know who we are and the pact on which we've agreed!' 'I'll do it,' he replied, 'But let me enjoy her tonight!' 'Certainly not, by God,' they insisted, 'and there will he no intercourse!' So he brought her out to them speaking the verses: 'My friends are gone, and I have not greeted al-Qatd; I did not say to them a pleasant farewell, For the al-fudd reiterated that they protect her; yet I still see myself not fearing the al-fudtzl. Don't think, girl, that I, that evening the riders left, did not feel disregarded by their going.'" And limes other than these were also given. It has been said that this was known as the "pact of al-fdel" merely because it was like an agreement reached by the Jurhum tribe that similarly favoured victims over oppressors. There were those of their leaders who advocated this, each one of whom bore the name "Fadl". They were al-Fadl h. Fadala, al-Fad1 b. Wada'a, and al-Fad1 h. al-Hiirith. This is what Ibn Qutayba stated. Others give the names as al-Fad1 b. Shars'a, al-Fad1 b. Bida'a and al-Fadl h. Quda'a. It was alSuhayli, God be pleased with him, who reported this. Muhammad b. Ishaq b. Y a m said, "Tribes of Quraysh suggested making a pact and assembled for that purpose in the house of 'Ahd All& b. Jud'm because of his high reputation and age. The pact made there at his house was between Banii Hashim, Banii 'Abd al-Munalib, Banti Asad b. 'Abd al-Wzzs, Zuhra b. Kil~h,and Taym b. Murra. They made a pact and agreement that they would support any man in Mecca who was wonged, whether from their own people or someone who had merely come there, against whoever had wronged him, and that they would ensure that his rights be upheld. Quraysh named that agreement the 'pact of al-fudall'." Muhammad h. Ishaq said that Muhammad b. Zayd b. al-Muhajir b. Qunfudh of Taym related to him that he heard Tabs b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Awf al-Zuhri say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "I wimessed in the house of 'Abd Allah b. Jud% a pact made that I wouldn't have exchanged for the choicest herd; and if it had been suggested after Islam, I would have responded positively to it." Ibn Ishiiq said that Yazid b. 'Abd All& b. Usama b. d-Hiidi al-Laythr related to him that Muhammad b. Ibrahim b. al-Harith of Taym told him that there was between d-Husayn b. 'Ali b. Aba Tdib and al-Walid b. Wtba b. Abii Sufym - al-Wdid being at that time governor of al-Medina, having been appointed over it by his uncle Muswiya b. Abn Sufym - a dispute over some property they owned at (the village of) Dhn al-Marwa. It seems that al-Walid was using his power to take advantage of al-Husayn. So al-Husayn told h i , "I swear by God that you will either justly recognize my rights or I will draw my sword and stand up in the mosque of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and invoke the pact of al-fdiil." Ibn Ishiiq continued, "So (Abd All& b. al-Zubayr, who was with d-Wdid when al-Husayn made this statement, said, 'And I too swear by God that if he does invoke it, I'll draw my sword and stand there with him until he gets his justice or we'll all die together.'" Ibn Ishaq stated that when al-Miswar b. Makhrama b. Nawfal al-Zuhn and 'Abd al-Rahman b. Wthman b. Wbayd All* of Taym heard of the incident, they gave similar responses. And when al-Walid b. Wtba heard what had been said, he treated al-Husayn justly and made him content.
lessons: Speaking up against injustice, even when you are alone and powerless, can ignite a movement for change — the man from Zabid's public cry led to one of the most honourable alliances in Arab history.
True nobility is measured not by lineage or wealth but by one's willingness to stand for the oppressed, even at personal cost.
Principles of justice transcend time — the Prophet (peace be upon him) praised the Pact of Al-Fudul even after Islam, showing that upholding fairness is a universal and enduring value.
practical_advice: When you witness or experience injustice, find your voice and speak up — silence only empowers wrongdoers, and your appeal may move others to act.
Seek out communities, institutions, or individuals around you who share a commitment to justice, and align yourself with them so that you are never alone when facing oppression.

When you feel powerless against someone in authority who is treating you unfairly, remember that collective moral pressure — like that of Al-Fudul — is often more powerful than individual force; seek allies and use legitimate means to assert your rights.

title: The Prophet's Marriage to Khadija and the Trading Journey to Syria
summary: Khadija, a wealthy and respected merchant woman, hired the young Muhammad (peace be upon him) to lead a trading caravan to Syria. Impressed by the miraculous signs witnessed by her servant Maysara and by his exceptional character, she proposed marriage to him.
story: Ibn Ishiiq stated, "Khadija daughter of Khuwaylid was a merchant woman of stature and wealth. She would employ men to trade her goods on a profit-sharing basis. "When she heard of the truthfulness, trustworthy nature and fine character of the Messenger of God (SAAS), she sent for him and proposed that he should take some of her goods on a trading venture to Syria; she offered him better terms than she did other merchants and suggested he be accompanied by a youth in her employment whose name was Maysara. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) accepted this offer and set off with her goods, in the company of Maysara. When they reached Syria, the Messenger of God (SAAS) made a halt beneath the shade of a tree near the cell of a certain monk. This monk looked out at Maysara and said, 'Who is that man resting beneath the tree?' Maysara responded, 'He is of Quraysh, those who guard the sanctuary.' The monk responded, 'No one but a prophet has ever stopped beneath that tree.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sold his wares, the goods that he had brought, and bought those things he wanted. Thereafter he set off in a caravan for Mecca, Maysara accompanying him. "At midday when it was fiercely hot, Maysara would see, so they say, two angels shading him from the sun as he travelled forward on his camel. "When he reached Mecca and brought the goods back to Khadija, she sold them for twice their cost or thereabouts. Maysara related to her what the monk had said, and how he had seen the angels giving him shade. "Now Khadija was a decisive, noble, and intelligent lady, as well as possessed of all the other fine qualities God had endowed her with. When Maysara told her all this she sent for the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said to him, so they say, 'Cousin, I am interested in yon because of your being related to me, because of your high reputation among your people, and because of your trustworthiness, fine character and truthfulness.' She then offered herself to him in marriage. "Khadija was the highest-born, most respected and wealthiest woman in Quraysh. All her people were covetous of all that and would have attained it if they could. "When she said this to the Messenger of God (SAAS), he reported it to his uncles. One of these, Hamza, went off with him to visit Khuwaylid b. Asad and made an engagement between them. Thereafter the Prophet (SAAS) did marry her." Ibn H i s h m stated, "He gave her 20 young female camels as a dowry. She was the first woman he married. And he manied no other woman until she died." Ibn Ishaq said, "It was she who bore all the children of the Messenger of God (SAAS) except for Ibrhim. His sons from her were al-@sim, by whose name he was known, as 'Abii al-@sim', 'father of Qsim' that is. Al-Tayyib and al-T&~I were his other sons. His daughters were named Zaynab, Ruqayya, Umm Knlthm, and FaGma."
lessons: Good character and trustworthiness open doors that wealth and status alone cannot — it was the Prophet's reputation for honesty that drew Khadija to him long before anyone knew he would become a prophet.
Khadija's confidence, intelligence, and decisiveness in proposing marriage show that taking initiative based on sound judgment and good values is a mark of wisdom, not boldness for its own sake.

Divine signs and protection accompany those chosen by God, but they work through ordinary means — a trading journey, a servant's observation, a monk's recognition — reminding us that the sacred can be found within the everyday.
practical_advice: Invest consistently in your character and reputation for honesty; people notice, and the right opportunities and relationships will find you as a result.

When considering important life decisions such as a partnership or marriage, look deeply at a person's values, track record, and how they treat others, just as Khadija did, rather than being swayed only by appearances or social status.

Reflect on the blessings God has placed in your life — meaningful work, supportive relationships, moments of protection — and recognise them as signs of His care for you, especially in times when you feel uncertain about your path.

title: The Marriage of Prophet Muhammad to Khadija and Their Children
