summary: An account of the Prophet Muhammad's marriage to Khadija at age 25, the dowry of 20 young female camels, and the names and birth order of their children including al-Qasim, Abd Allah, Zaynab, Ruqayya, Umm Kulthum, and Fatima.
story: them. Thereafter the Prophet (SAAS) did marry her." Ibn Hishām stated, "He gave her 20 young female camels as a dowry. She was the first woman he married. And he married no other woman until she died." Ibn Ishaq said, "It was she who bore all the children of the Messenger of God (SAAS) except for Ibrāhīm. His sons from her were al-Qāsim, by whose name he was known, as 'Abū al-Qāsim', 'father of Qāsim' that is. Al-Tayyib and al-Tāhir were his other sons. His daughters were named Zaynab, Ruqayya, Umm Kulthūm, and Fāṭima." Ibn Hishām stated, "The first-born of the boys was al-Qāsim, then came al-Tayyib, followed by al-Tāhir. The first-born girl was Ruqayya, followed by Zaynab, Umm Kulthūm, and Fāṭima, in that order." Al-Bayhaqi stated, from al-Ḥākim that he read, by the hand of Abū Bakr b. Abī Khaythama, that Muṣ'ab b. 'Abd Allāh al-Zubayrī related to him, saying, "The eldest of his sons was al-Qāsim. Then came Zaynab, followed by 'Abd Allāh, Umm Kulthūm, Fāṭima, and Ruqayya. The first of his children to die was al-Qāsim, followed by 'Abd Allāh. Khadija is said to have died at age 65 or at age 50, the latter being more likely." Others say that al-Qāsim attained an age when he could ride, even on light, swift camels but then he died, after the call to prophethood. Others say he died while still unweaned. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "In heaven there is a place for him where he can complete his suckling." However, it is well known that this statement applied to (his son) Ibrāhīm. Yūnus b. Bukayr stated that Ibrāhīm b. 'Uthmān related to him from al-Qāsim from Ibn 'Abbās, who said, "Khadija gave birth from the Messenger of God (SAAS) to two boys and four girls. These were al-Qāsim, 'Abd Allāh, Fāṭima, Umm Kulthūm, Zaynab, and Ruqayya." Al-Zubayr b. Bakkār said, "It was 'Abd Allāh who was known as 'al-Tayyib' (the good) and 'al-Tāhir' (the pure). He was given these appellations because he was born after the call to prophethood. His other sons died before his mission. All his daughters were born prior to the call to the prophethood, and emigrated with him (SAAS)." Ibn Hishām stated, "Ibrāhīm was born to Māria the Copt who was presented to the Prophet (SAAS) by al-Muqawqis, governor of Alexandria; she was from (the Upper Egypt town of) Kūrat Andāj." Ibn Hishām said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was 25 years of age when he married Khadija, according to the account of several scholars, including Abū 'Amr al-Madanī." Ya'qūb b. Sufyān stated, "I wrote down, from Ibrāhīm b. al-Mundhir, the following: 'Umar b. Abū Bakr al-Mu'ammalī related to me that more than one authority related to him that 'Amr b. Asad married Khadija to the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he was 25 years of age and Quraysh were building the ka'ba." Similarly, al-Bayhaqi transmitted from al-Ḥākim that the age of the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he married Khadija was 25 and that she was then 35. It is also said that she was 25.
lessons: The Prophet's faithfulness to Khadija alone during her lifetime reflects how loyalty and devotion are foundational values in meaningful relationships.

The death of children before their parents is among the greatest of trials, yet the Prophet's trust in God's mercy — expressed in the hadith about al-Qāsim completing his suckling in heaven — models how grief can be held alongside hope.
Every child, whether they live long or briefly, holds a cherished place in a parent's heart and in God's sight, a reminder that love transcends the length of a life.
practical_advice: When facing grief over lost loved ones, allow yourself to mourn fully while anchoring your heart in the belief that God's mercy encompasses those who have passed, especially children.
In your relationships, strive to embody the loyalty the Prophet showed Khadija — being fully present and committed to the people you love rather than always seeking more.
When you learn about the lives of those who came before you, use their stories as mirrors to reflect on your own values around family, fidelity, and gratitude for the time you have with loved ones.

title: The Prophet as a Shepherd Before Prophethood
summary: A hadith in which the Prophet Muhammad states that God never sent a prophet without first having him shepherd sheep, and that he himself shepherded for Meccans at al-Qarrār, illustrating the wisdom of humble preparation before a great mission.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated, in a chapter entitled: "The employment the Messenger of God had before he married Khadija" as follows: "Abū 'Abd Allāh the ḥāfiẓ narrated to us, quoting Abū Bakr b. 'Abd Allāh, quoting al-Ḥasan b. Sufyān; and Suwayd b. Sa'id related to us, quoting 'Amr b. Abu Yahya b. Sa'id al-Qurashī, from his grandfather Sa'id, from Abū Hurayra who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'God never sent anyone to be a prophet without his having been a shepherd for sheep.' His Companions asked him, 'And you too, O Messenger of God?' He replied, 'I shepherded them for Meccans at al-Qarrār.'" Al-Bukhārī related it from Aḥmad b. Muḥammad the Meccan, from 'Amr b. Yaḥyā. Thereafter al-Bayhaqi related the same tradition by way of al-Rabī' b. Badr, a weak link, from Abū al-Zubayr, from Jābir who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "My wage from Khadija for two expeditions was a young she-camel."
lessons: Humble, seemingly simple work is not beneath a person of great destiny — shepherding taught patience, care, and attentiveness that later served the Prophet in leading an entire community.
God's preparation of His servants often happens quietly, in lowly and unnoticed roles, long before the moment of their calling becomes visible to the world.
Every stage of life, including its most ordinary phases, carries lessons that shape character; do not despise the season of preparation you are currently in.
practical_advice: If you feel your current work or station in life is too small or beneath your potential, reflect on how the greatest of people were shaped through humble beginnings and use this as motivation to give your best to what is in front of you now.
When feeling lost about your purpose, consider that purpose is often built gradually through the disciplines of daily responsibility — tend well to what you have been given today.

Practice finding meaning in caregiving and service roles, whether at home or work, knowing that attentiveness to others' needs is a quality that God cultivates in those He is preparing for something greater.

title: The Story of Khadija's Marriage — The Account of Ammār ibn Yāsir
summary: Ammār ibn Yāsir narrates how the marriage of the Prophet to Khadija was arranged through her sister's suggestion, and how Khadija's father, having been drinking wine, conducted the marriage ceremony and later denied it upon sobering — only to confirm it when confronted.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related, through Ḥammād b. Salama, from 'Alī b. Zayd, from 'Ammār b. Abī 'Ammār, from Ibn 'Abbās who said, "Khadija's father married the Messenger of God (SAAS) to her at a time when he (her father) was — and I think this is what he said — drunk!" Then al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu al-Ḥusayn b. al-Faḍl al-Qaṭṭān related to him, as did 'Abd Allāh b. Ja'far, as did Ya'qūb b. Sufyān, who said that he was told by Ibrāhīm b. al-Mundhir, and by 'Umar b. Abū Bakr al-Mu'ammalī and by 'Abd Allāh b. Abī 'Ubayd b. Muḥammad b. 'Ammār b. Yāsir, from his father from Muqsim b. Abā al-Qāsim, the mawla of 'Abd Allāh b. al-Ḥārith b. Nawfal, that 'Abd Allāh b. al-Ḥārith related to him that 'Ammār b. Yāsir, whenever he heard people discussing the marriage of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Khadija and embellishing it as they did, would tell them, "I know better than anyone else about his marriage to her. I was his companion and his close friend. One day I was out with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and when we came to al-Ḥazūra we passed by Khadija's sister who was seated on a he-coloured camel she had for sale. She called to me and I went over to her, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) stood there waiting for me. She said, 'Would that friend of yours like to marry Khadija?' "So I went back to him and told him and he responded, 'Indeed I certainly would!' "I then reported to her what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said and she suggested, 'Come and visit us tomorrow.' When we did so next day we found that they had slaughtered a cow and dressed Khadija's father in ceremonial clothes; and his beard had been dyed yellow. I spoke with her brother and he with his father, who had been drinking wine. The brother told him of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and of his reputation and asked him if he would conduct his marriage. And so he did marry him to Khadija. They cooked the cow and we ate from it, after which her father went to sleep. When he woke up he was sober and he said, 'What's this I'm wearing, what's this yellow and why this food?' His daughter who had spoken to 'Ammār replied, 'It was your son-in-law Muḥammad son of 'Abd Allāh who gave you the ceremonial outfit and he who presented the cow to you, and we slaughtered it when you married him to Khadija.' "He denied having concluded this marriage with him and stormed off, shouting, to al-Ḥijr. Banū Hāshim brought out the Messenger of God (SAAS) and they all went off to talk to him. Khadija's father demanded, 'Where's this man of yours you claim I married to Khadija?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) made his presence known to him. Having looked at him, her father then said, 'If I already performed his marriage, then so be it. If I didn't before, I do so now!'" Al-Zuhrī stated in his works of biography that her father married her to him when he was drunk. His account is similar to the foregoing. Al-Suhayli (similarly) related it. Al-Mu'ammalī stated, "What is generally agreed upon is that it was her uncle, 'Amr b. Asad, who married her to him." This is what al-Suhayli preferred. He related so on the authority of Ibn 'Abbās and 'Ā'isha. The latter said, "Khuwaylid had died before al-Fijār, the 'sacrilegious' war. It was he who had fought against Tubba' when he wanted to remove the 'black stone' to Yemen. Khuwaylid and a group from Quraysh had prevented him from doing so. Then Tubba' had a nightmare about it, changed his mind, and left the 'black stone' where it was." Ibn Isḥāq related at the end of his biography that Khadija's brother, 'Amr b. Khuwaylid, was the one who married her to the Messenger of God (SAAS), but God knows best.
lessons: God's plans unfold even through imperfect and chaotic human circumstances — the marriage that would become one of the most blessed in history was arranged through a spontaneous conversation by a roadside.

Human dignity and the honour of commitment matter: even Khadija's father, upon sobering, recognised the weight of what had transpired and honoured it, showing that accountability can follow confusion.
The Prophet stood patiently while his companion attended to another person's need — a small detail that speaks volumes about his character of unhurried, gracious presence.
practical_advice: When life's important chapters begin in messy or unexpected ways, resist the urge to dismiss them as invalid — reflect on whether God may be at work even through the disorder.
If you have acted out of confusion or impairment and caused harm or embarrassment to others, have the courage to acknowledge what happened and make it right, as Khadija's father ultimately did.
Cultivate the habit of patience while waiting for companions and loved ones — small acts of unhurried presence, like the Prophet standing and waiting for Ammār, communicate deep respect.

title: Waraqa ibn Nawfal's Anticipation of the Prophet and His Poetry
summary: Waraqa ibn Nawfal, Khadija's Christian cousin who had studied the Scriptures, hears from Khadija the accounts of Maysara and the monk and eagerly anticipates the coming prophethood of Muhammad, expressing his longing and belief through verses of poetry.
story: Ibn Isḥāq stated that Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, had informed Waraqa b. Nawfal b. Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzzā b. Quṣayy, who was her cousin and a Christian who had studied the Scriptures and human knowledge, of what her young employee (Maysara) had told her of what the monk had said, and how he had seen the two angels shading him. Waraqa had then told her, "If this is true, Khadija, then Muḥammad is the Prophet of this nation. I learned that a prophet was expected for this nation, and this is his time." These were approximately the words he used. Thereafter Waraqa had impatiently awaited his coming, asking how long it would be. He spoke the following verses on the subject: "I persisted, being persistent in recalling both a worry that often inspired my tears, And a description from Khadija, following (another) description; my waiting has been long, O Khadija, In the heart of Mecca, with my hoping because of your words that I would see some solution. From what you related as the words of a priest who was a monk whom I would hate to be wrong, That Muḥammad will one day prevail and defeat whomever opposes him, Making a light appear in the land by which he will bring change to all creation. Those who oppose him will meet defeat, while those who aid him will achieve success. Would that I be there to witness, for I'll be the first of them in participating. Participating in that which Quraysh will hate, however much they bluster in their Mecca. I aspire, through him whom they all hate, to reach the Enthroned One, even if they descend, aside. Is it not folly to disbelieve in Him who chooses, He who raised the stars? If they and I survive, things will happen at which the disbelievers will be sorely discomfited. Even if I perish, so does every man meet from the fates with destruction and death." Waraqa also spoke the following lines, according to the account given of him by Yūnus b. Bukayr from Ibn Isḥāq: "Will you leave early in the morning, or late tonight, or in the evening, there being a fire alight in your heart from your concealing your sadness? At parting from a people whom I do not like to leave, as if after two days' journey away they seem very far removed. And at true information related about Muḥammad, told about him when he was elsewhere by an honest man. The man came back to you, O best of free women, whom you had sent to the Ghawr lowland and to the plateaux of the Najd ranges. To the markets of Buṣrā with the mounts that left early, bent over with their loads, slow-moving. He tells us of things good, through his knowledge; and truth has doors for which keys exist. That 'Abd Allāh's son, Aḥmad, is being sent to all people everywhere. My belief is that he will be sent to tell the truth, just as God's two servants Hūd and Ṣāliḥ were sent And Moses, and Abraham, until his glory shall be witnessed and widely spread abroad by report, very clear; And there shall follow him the tribes of Lu'ayy and Ghālib, their young and their grey leaders alike. If I survive till his era reaches the people, then I will give glad tidings of love. If"
lessons: Waraqa's patient, years-long anticipation of something he believed in but had not yet seen is a model of faith sustained by knowledge and hope — belief does not always require immediate confirmation.
Truth, when it is genuine, leaves signs that those with open hearts and trained eyes can recognise long before it is publicly proclaimed; sincere seekers are prepared for revelation.
Even a person outside the immediate community of the Prophet — a Christian scholar — could perceive the light of the coming mission, reminding us that truth transcends tribal and communal boundaries.
practical_advice: When you are waiting for something you deeply believe in but cannot yet see, channel your longing into productive reflection and expression, as Waraqa did through poetry and study, rather than letting anxiety consume you.
Invest in studying and learning so that when moments of truth and clarity arrive in your life, you have the knowledge and discernment to recognise and respond to them.

If you feel a sense of spiritual anticipation or that something significant is approaching in your life, write about it, speak about it with trusted people, and stay attentive — do not let the waiting make you numb.

title: Waraqa ibn Nawfal's Prophecy About the Coming Prophet
summary: Waraqa ibn Nawfal recites verses prophesying the coming of the Prophet Muhammad, expressing his hope to witness the era and his acceptance that he may not survive to see it, leaving a message for Khadija.
story: his knowledge; and truth has doors for which keys exist. That 'Abd Allah's son, Ahd, is being sent to all people everywhere. My belief is that he will be sent to tell the truth, just as God's two servants Had and Slih were sent And Moses, and Abraham, until his glory shall he witnessed and widely spread abroad by report, very clear; And there shall follow him the tribes of Lu'ayy and Ghdih, their young and their grey leaders alike. If I survive till his era reaches the people, then I will give glad tidings of love. If not, 0 Khadija, then you should know that I shall be travelling the wide earth, far from your land.
lessons: Those with sincere hearts may recognize the truth even before it fully arrives, as Waraqa's verses demonstrate prophetic discernment.
Accepting that we may not witness the fruit of what we believe in is a mark of sincere faith — the reward is in the sincerity, not the timing.
Hope in the coming of divine guidance can sustain a believer even in times of spiritual darkness and uncertainty.
practical_advice: When you feel like you are waiting for relief that has not yet come, remind yourself that sincerity in your belief is itself a form of arrival.
Write down or articulate your hopes and beliefs — as Waraqa did in verse — to anchor your heart to what you know to be true.
If you feel you will not see the results of your efforts or faith, take comfort that your conviction and witness still hold eternal value.

title: Al-Umawi's Additional Verses of Prophetic Poetry
summary: Al-Umawi adds further poetic verses attributed to Waraqa and others, glorifying God and warning against polytheism, while reflecting on the transience of worldly power and the permanence of God.
story: Al-Umawi added the lines: "Following the religion of Him who established all things, He who had virtue far greater than all men. And erected a secure building in Mecca, whose lamplight glitters in the dark, A meeting place for all the various tribes, a place to which the tired, fine mounts hurry, Standing tall like arrows of fine wood, the sinews winding around them suspended above their pastem-joints." Other verses of this, as given by Abii d-@sim d-Suhayli in his work al-Ramp (The Gardens) are the following: "I gave advice to the tribes, telling them 'I came to warn, so let no one deceive you.' Do not worship any God other than your creator, and if others call you, then say, 'We are in conflict with you.' Glory everlasting be to Him of the throne; both the waters and the dry land glorified Him before us. All things beneath the sky are subjugated before Him; no one should oppose His dominion. Nothing that you see has its splendour last, but God remains, though wealth and progeny perish. With his treasures, one day Hurmuz did not prevail, and the tribe of Ads sought eternity, but they did not last. Nor Solomon either, for the winds blew him away, and all spirits and men too, a waste land between them. Where are those kings whose glory was such that delegations would come to them from all parts? A great basin is up there where, without falsehood, all must gather one day, just as they did." He then said, "Aba al-Faraj similarly attributed these to Waraqa." He also commented that some of these verses are also considered to have been by Umayya b. Ahii al-Salt. I would also add that we have been told that 'Umar b. al-KhagZb, God bless him, used sometimes to quote some of these verses. But God knows best.
lessons: Reflecting on the fall of great civilizations and kings is a powerful reminder that only God is permanent, which frees the heart from attachment to worldly status.
True worship means refusing to bow to anything other than the Creator, even when social pressure demands otherwise.
Poetry and eloquent speech have long been used to convey deep theological truths and warnings — the arts can serve faith.
practical_advice: When anxiety about the future grips you, meditate on the fact that every worldly power has passed, and only God endures — your struggles too shall pass.
If you feel pressured to compromise your values to fit in, recall the verse: 'if others call you, say We are in conflict with you' — firm, dignified refusal is possible.
Use creative expression — writing, poetry, journaling — as a means of grounding yourself in gratitude and remembrance of God.

title: Quraysh Rebuild the Ka'ba and the Dispute Over the Black Stone
summary: Five years before prophethood, Quraysh rebuild the Ka'ba after it is damaged, but fall into a heated dispute over which tribe should have the honor of placing the Black Stone, resolving it by agreeing to accept the judgment of the first person to enter.
story: Section: On Quraysh having rebuilt the ka'ba five years before the coming of the prophethood. Al-Bayhaqi recounts the building of the kacba to have been before the marriage of the Prophet (SAAS), to Khadija. However, it is widely known that the building by Quraysh of the ka'ba took place some ten years following their marriage, as we have stated. Al-Bayhaqi begins by relating the ka'ba's construction as having occurred in the time of Abraham, as we have stated in our earlier account of him. He gives the hadith of Ibn 'Ahbas recorded in the sahih collection of al-BukhZri and also makes reference to some of the accounts attributed to Israeli legends regarding the construction of the ka'ba in Adam's lifetime. But that is not correct, for the plain statement in the Qur'an establishes that Abraham was the first person to begin building it, the first to establish it. Moreover its site was revered before that, honoured, and cared for throughout time. God Almighty stated, "The first 'house' established for mankind was the one in Bekka (Mecca), blessed and a guidance for the worlds. In it there are clear signs. (There is) the stone on which Abraham stood. Whoever enters it shall be safe. Men owe it to God to make pilgrimage to the House, (all) those who can afford to do so" (surat Al-clmrzn, III, v.96,97).
lessons: Disputes over honor and precedence are a natural human weakness, but divine wisdom often resolves conflict through unexpected and unifying means.
The Ka'ba's sanctity transcends any single tribe's claim — sacred spaces belong to all of humanity, a lesson in communal stewardship.
Even before his prophethood, Muhammad's character was so trusted that warring factions chose him as arbitrator — integrity speaks before words do.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a conflict about who deserves credit or recognition, ask yourself whether the outcome — not the credit — is what truly matters.
In group disputes, consider proposing a neutral, fair process of resolution rather than insisting on your position.
Reflect on how you are building your reputation through daily conduct — trustworthiness is earned through small, consistent actions over time.

title: The Prophet Places the Black Stone: A Moment of Wisdom and Unity
summary: The young Muhammad resolves a tribal dispute over placing the Black Stone by having all tribal leaders collectively lift it on a cloth while he places it with his own hands, earning widespread admiration and the title Al-Amin.
story: Abii Dawiid al-Tayalisi said that Hammad b. Salama and Qays and Sall related to him, all on the authority of Simak b. Harb, from Khsid b. 'Ar'ara, from 'Ali b. Aba Talib who said, "When the ka'ba fell down after Jurhum, Quraysh rebuilt it. And when they wanted to position the stone they fell into a dispute with one another. So they agreed that the first person to come in the door should do it. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) came in through the Bann Shayba door. He told them to bring a robe in the centre of which he placed the stone. Then he ordered each sub-tribe to take hold of an edge of the cloth. They then lifted it up and the Messenger of God (SAAS) took the stone and positioned it." YaCqnb b. Sufyan said that Asnghb. Faraj related to him, as did Ihn Wahb, from Yanus from Ibn Shihah as follows: "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached puberty a woman set alight to the kacba. A spark from her fire lit some of the cloth covering of the kacba and it burned. So they tore it down and rebuilt it up to that point in the corner where a dispute arose among Quraysh as to which of the tribes should be entrusted with raising it. "They agreed to appoint as arbitrator the first person who came up to them. It was the Messenger of God (SAAS) who did so, he being a youth, and he was wearing a leopardskin sash. They asked him to decide the issue and he told them to get the corner piece, which was placed in a cloth. Then he asked the leader of each tribe to come forward and gave each one a side of the cloth while he climbed up. They lifted it up and he put the corner piece in position. "As he increased in age he became only more pleasing, and so they gave him the sobriquet al-Amin, 'the trustworthy', even before revelation came down to him. They adopted the practice of never slaughtering a camel for sacrifice without seeking him, and he would say a prayer over it for them."
lessons: True leadership finds solutions that give everyone dignity rather than declaring a winner and a loser.
The title Al-Amin — the trustworthy — was earned through a lifetime of character, not granted by rank or wealth; trustworthiness is built, not inherited.
Creative problem-solving under pressure, rooted in fairness, can dissolve conflicts that seem intractable.
practical_advice: When mediating a conflict, look for a solution that honors all parties' legitimate needs rather than taking sides.
Commit to being the person others can turn to in moments of crisis — cultivate your reliability through small daily acts of honesty and fairness.
If you are overwhelmed by a seemingly unsolvable dispute in your life, step back and ask: what arrangement would allow everyone to walk away with their dignity intact?

title: The Sacred History of the Ka'ba: From Abraham to Quraysh
summary: The text traces the sacred history of the Ka'ba from its original construction by Abraham through multiple rebuildings by various peoples, establishing its spiritual significance as the first house of worship established for all mankind.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated that Abti 'Abd Allah, the hafifiz, informed him, as did Abn 'Ahd All& al-Saffar, Ahmad b. Mahran, Whayd Allah, and Isracil, from Abii Yahya, from Mujahid, from 'Abd Allah h. 'Amr as follows, "The house (of worship) existed 2,000 years before the earth: 'And lo! the earth was laid out' (sarat al-Inshiqaq, LXXXIV, v.3). 'It was,' he said, 'laid out beneath it.'" I consider this very strange. It is as though it comes from the two baggage camels seized by 'Abd Allah h. 'Amr at the battle of Yarmuk which were transporting Israeli legends, from which he used to relate; they told of strange and objectionable things. Then al-Bayhaqi stated that Ahn 'Abd Allah, the hzfifiz, informed him, quoting Aba Ja'far Muhammad h. Muhammad b. Muhammad h. 'Abd Allah al-Baghdadi, quoting Yahya h. Wthman b. Salih, as did Aha Salih al-Jubani, quoting Ibn Lahi'a, from Yazid h. Aba al-Khayr, from 'Ahd Allah b. 'Amr h. al-'AS that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "God sent down Gabriel to Adam and Eve, and told them: 'Build a house of worship for me.' Gabriel drew it out for them and Adam began digging while Eve transported until the water spoke to him, calling out from beneath him, 'That's enough, Adam!' When they had finished building, God revealed to him that he should circumambulate it. He was told, 'You are the first man, and this is the first house.' Thereafter the centuries succeeded one another until Noah made pilgrimage there. Further centuries went by until Abraham constructed its foundations." Al-Bayhaqi stated, "Ihn Lahi'a is the only one transmitting this tradition in this way." I consider him to be a weak source. If its transmission had stopped directly with 'Ahd Allah b. 'Amr it would have been a stronger and more authentic tradition. But God knows best. Al-Rabi' stated that al-Shaafi'i informed him, as did Sufya, from Ihn Aha Labid, from Muhammad b. Ka'b al-@razi - or someone else - that he (the Prophet, SAAS) said, "Adam performed the pilgrimage and the angels met him and told him, 'Your sacrifice made has been successful. We made pilgrimage 2,000 years before you!'" Yanus b. Bukayr stated that Ibn Ishaq said, "Buqayya - or, he said, someone trustworthy of Medina - related to me, from Wrwa b. al-Zubayr that he (the Prophet) said, 'There has never been a prophet who has not made pilgrimage to the ka'ba - except, that is, for Hnd and Sm.'" But I have elsewhere reported their pilgrimage to it; by which I meant their having made pilgrimage to its site, even though it was not yet built. But God knows best.
lessons: Sacred places carry meaning across centuries and civilizations — connecting to a long chain of worshippers before you deepens one's sense of belonging to something greater.
Scholarly integrity demands that we evaluate the chains of transmission critically, as Ibn Kathir models, rather than accepting every narration uncritically.
Every prophet was united in reverence for the same sacred house, reminding us that the core of all divine messages is one.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually disconnected or lost, reconnect to the historical depth of your faith tradition — knowing you are part of a long chain of believers can be deeply grounding.
Practice intellectual humility: not every claim that feels spiritually significant is authentic. Verify what you believe and why.
Visit or mentally envision sacred spaces as a form of spiritual renewal, following in the footsteps of prophets and angels who did the same.

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib's Account of the Ka'ba's Construction by Abraham
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib explains that the Ka'ba was the first house of worship established with divine blessings and guidance for all mankind, recounting how God commanded Abraham to build it, and how it was later rebuilt multiple times by different peoples.
story: After this al-Bayhaqi reported the hadith from Ibn 'Abbas giving in full the story of Abraham, upon whom be peace; it is included in the sahih collection of al-Bukhari. Al-Bayhaqi then related from the hadith of Simak b. Harb, on the authority of Khalid b. 'Ar'ara, as follows: "A man asked 'Ali about God's statement (in the Qur'an): 'The first house (of worship) established for mankind was the one in Bekka (Mecca), blessed and a guidance for the world' and asked whether it really was the first house built on earth. "He replied, 'No, but it was the first one in which blessings and guidance for all mankind were in place, along with the stone on which Abraham stood; and whoever enters it is safe. And if you like, we can tell you how it was constructed. "'Almighty God sent a revelation to Abraham, saying, "Build for me a house on earth!" This command disturbed him greatly. So God sent down al-sakina, which is a strong wind with a head to it. Head and wind pursued one another until finally stopping. Thereupon the gale whirled around itself at a certain spot, like the coils of a snake. So Abraham built (there) until he reached the position for the stone. He then said to his son: "Help me by finding a stone." He searched for one and brought it, but found that the 'black stone' had already been set in place. He asked his father, "Where did you get that?" "Someone came who wouldn't rely on your building. Gabriel brought it from heaven." So he finished it. "'Time passed and it fell down. So the 'amalzqa (the giants) rebuilt it and when it next collapsed it was Jurhum who reconstructed it. "'Again it collapsed and Quraysh rebuilt it, the Messenger of God (SAAS) being a young man at that time. When they wanted to lift into place the 'black stone' they fell into a dispute who should do so. They agreed they would let the first person to come along that street decide. It was the Messenger of God (SAAS) who came by first, so he decided for them to place it in a piece of cloth, so that all the tribes would lift it to its place.'"
lessons: God's commands may disturb us initially, but divine guidance follows to show the way — Abraham's distress at the command was met with the wind of al-sakina showing him where to build.
Sacred work often requires collaboration across generations — the Ka'ba was built and rebuilt by many hands across centuries, each contributing to its sanctity.
Heaven intervenes in ways we do not expect: the Black Stone arrived from Gabriel when human effort could not complete the task alone.
practical_advice: When a divine or moral obligation feels overwhelming and you do not know where to begin, trust that clarity will follow your sincere intention to obey.
In your own 'building' — whether of character, community, or craft — accept that others before you laid foundations and others after you will continue; your role is to do your part faithfully.
When human resources feel insufficient for a task you are called to, remain open to unexpected help arriving from sources beyond your planning.

title: Rebuilding of the Ka'ba and the Placement of the Black Stone
summary: When the Prophet Muhammad was 35 years old, the Quraysh decided to rebuild the Ka'ba due to flooding and structural damage. A dispute arose over who would have the honor of placing the Black Stone, which the Prophet resolved by having all tribes lift it together on a cloth.
story: corner piece, which was placed in a cloth. Then he asked the leader of each tribe to come forward and gave each one a side of the cloth while he climbed up. They lifted it up and he put the corner piece in position. "As he increased in age he became only more pleasing, and so they gave him the sobriquet al-Amin, 'the trustworthy', even before revelation came down to him. They adopted the practice of never slaughtering a camel for sacrifice without seeking him, and he would say a prayer over it for them." The context of this tradition is good, it being from the biographies written by al-Zuhri. One strange element in it is the wording "when he reached puberty". For it is well known that this occurred when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was 35. This is established by Muhammad Ibn Ishaq h. Yasw, God bless him. Mnsa b. Wqba stated that the building of the ka'ba occurred 15 years prior to the prophethood. Similarly, Mujahid agreed, as did Wrwa, Muhammad b. Jubayr b. Mut'im and others. But God knows best. Mnsa b. Wqba said, "There was a period of 15 years between the 'sacrilegious' war al-$jar and the building of the kacba." I observe that al-+jar and the pact of al-fudd came in one year, when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was 20. This fact substantiates what Muhammad h. Ishaq said. But God knows best.
lessons: The Prophet's wisdom in resolving conflict through inclusive, creative solutions shows that true leadership seeks to honor everyone rather than exclude rivals.
Being known as trustworthy and just long before any formal authority is granted reflects that character is built gradually through consistent righteous actions.
When communities unite around a shared sacred purpose and set aside personal rivalries, God's work can be accomplished in peace.
practical_advice: When facing a dispute where multiple parties feel entitled, look for solutions that allow everyone to participate rather than forcing a winner and a loser.
Work on building a reputation for trustworthiness in small, everyday interactions — it compounds over time and opens doors of influence when it matters most.
Before undertaking any significant communal project, ensure that roles and responsibilities are clearly and fairly distributed to prevent resentment from building.

title: The Motivations of Quraysh to Rebuild the Ka'ba
summary: Quraysh decided to rebuild the Ka'ba because floodwater was damaging its structure, a thief had stolen its treasure, and they wanted to raise its door for security. They pooled lawful funds and hired workers to undertake the restoration.
story: Mnsa b. Wqba said: "Quraysh were motivated towards rebuilding the kacba because water would flood in from its top, from above the earth roof with which it was covered and which was in disrepair. Consequently they were concerned that water might leak inside. "Also, a man named Mulayh had stolen the kacba's incense and they wanted to rebuild it taller and raise its door so that only those they wished could enter it. "They therefore established a fund for the costs and hired workers and then went there one morning to tear it down, despite their concern and apprehension that God might prevent them from doing what they wanted."
lessons: Maintaining and caring for sacred spaces is a communal responsibility that requires planning, coordination, and the use of lawful resources.
Anxiety about whether one's actions will be accepted by God is itself a sign of sincere intention and God-consciousness.
Practical problems — like a damaged roof or stolen treasure — are reminders that even the most sacred things in this world require human stewardship.
practical_advice: When a valued institution or relationship has been damaged, assess the full scope of what needs repair before beginning, rather than patching problems one at a time.
Before embarking on a major communal undertaking, ensure that all contributions come from clean, ethical sources so that the effort carries moral integrity.
Channel anxiety about outcomes into thorough preparation — do what is within your power, then trust that sincere effort will be accepted.

title: Al-Walid Begins the Demolition and the Community's Fear
summary: When Quraysh were ready to demolish the Ka'ba for rebuilding, no one dared to begin out of reverence and fear. Al-Walid ibn al-Mughira stepped forward first, and the community watched overnight to see whether he would be harmed before joining him.
story: "But the people were apprehensive about demolishing it and were very scared of it. So al-Walid b. al-Mughira said, 'I shall begin demolishing it.' And he took up a pick-axe and set about it, saying, 'O God, it is not to be feared. O God, good is all we want.' "He then demolished some of the side where two rukn, the sacred corners, are. That night everyone expected something bad would happen and said, 'Let's keep watch on him and if he comes to harm we'll not demolish any more of it and we'll repair it just as it was. But if nothing happens to him, then God will have been pleased by our action in destroying it.' "Early next morning al-Walid recommenced his work of demolition, and the others joined in with him. Eventually they had demolished it down to the foundations established by Abraham, peace be upon him. They uncovered some green rocks interlocking together like teeth."
lessons: Fear of doing the wrong thing, even in acts of worship and construction, is a form of reverence that keeps the heart connected to God.
Sometimes one courageous person must go first before others find the confidence to act; leadership often means bearing the risk others are unwilling to bear.
Declaring one's sincere intention to God before beginning a daunting task — as al-Walid said 'O God, good is all we want' — is a powerful way to anchor action in purpose.
practical_advice: When facing a task that feels overwhelming or risky, clearly state your intention and then take the first step — often the hardest part is simply beginning.
If you are paralyzed by fear of negative outcomes, consider setting a clear, time-limited test for yourself before deciding whether to proceed or stop.
Acknowledge fear openly rather than suppressing it; naming the fear, as the community did, allowed them to plan around it rather than be controlled by it.

title: The Serpent Guarding the Ka'ba Is Removed by a Bird
summary: A serpent that had lived in the Ka'ba's well for centuries and terrified anyone who approached was miraculously snatched away by a bird sent by God, clearing the way for Quraysh to begin rebuilding.
story: "There was a serpent that would emerge from the ka'ba's well into which they would drop every day the sacrificial offerings. It would emerge and sun itself on the ka'ba's wall. They were all afraid of it, because whenever anyone approached it, it would raise its head, move its coils audibly and open its mouth. One day as it lay on the ka'ba's wall as was its habit, God sent down a bird which snatched it up and flew away with it. "At this Quraysh said, 'Now we can hope that God is pleased at our plan. We have a local carpenter, and wood, and God has taken care of the serpent.'" Al-Suhayli recounted, on the authority of Razin, that a thief had entered the ka'ba during the Jurhum era to steal its treasure but that the well had caved in on him. Some men had come and lifted him out and retrieved what he had taken. After that, a serpent resided in the well, its head as big as that of a young goat, its belly white and its back black. It had lived there for 500 years. This was the serpent mentioned by Muhammad b. Ishaq.
lessons: When God wills a good work to proceed, He removes the obstacles that stand in its way — our role is to remain sincere in intention and patient in waiting.
Signs of divine support often come at the moment of greatest need, reinforcing the believer's trust that they are not alone in their efforts.
Obstacles that have persisted for a long time, even centuries, can be removed in an instant by God's command; no barrier is permanent.
practical_advice: When you encounter a persistent obstacle blocking a worthwhile goal, do not assume it is permanent — continue preparing and remain open to the possibility that circumstances will shift.
Treat unexpected good fortune or the removal of a difficulty as a sign to move forward with gratitude and renewed effort, not as an excuse to become complacent.
Share signs of encouragement with those working alongside you; when Quraysh saw the bird take the serpent, they spoke it aloud and it strengthened everyone's resolve.

title: Abu Wahb's Warning: Use Only Lawful Money in the Ka'ba's Construction
summary: When Quraysh began demolishing the Ka'ba, a respected elder picked up a stone which flew back to its place. He took this as a divine sign and warned the people to use only lawfully earned money — nothing from prostitution, usury, or injustice — in the rebuilding.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, "When they had agreed on demolishing and rebuilding it, Aba Wahb b. CAmr b. 'A'idh b. 'Abd b. 'Imram b. Makhzam (whose name according to Ibn Hisham was 'A'idh b. 'Imran b. Makhziim) went and removed a stone from the ka'ba but it flew straight out of his hand back to its position. So he said, 'O Quraysh, in rebuilding it, use only money that you have fairly earned. Nothing gained from prostitution, usury, or injustice against any person must enter it.'" (Some) people attribute this statement to al-Walid b. al-Mughira b. 'Abd Allah b. Wmar b. Makhziim. Ibn Ishaq, moreover, was inclined to believe that the man who said this was Aba Wahb b. 'Amr. He said that he was the maternal uncle of the father of the Prophet (SAAS), and a very highly regarded and honourable man.
lessons: The integrity of any sacred or communal project depends on the purity of the resources invested in it; corrupt wealth taints even noble endeavors.
When an unexpected sign or setback occurs, take it as an opportunity to reflect on whether your methods and intentions are truly aligned with your values.
Speaking difficult truths publicly, as Abu Wahb did, is an act of moral courage that protects the community even when it may be unwelcome.
practical_advice: Before committing resources to an important personal project — whether financial, relational, or spiritual — examine honestly whether those resources come from ethical sources.
If something keeps 'not working' despite your efforts, pause and ask whether there is a values misalignment in how you are approaching it, not just a strategic one.
When you see a group heading toward an ethical mistake, speak up clearly and early, as Abu Wahb did — your words may set the tone for the entire effort.

title: The Miraculous Foundations and Strange Discoveries Inside the Ka'ba
summary: As Quraysh dug down to the foundations of the Ka'ba, they encountered miraculous signs including a stone that caused Mecca to shake, a light that dazzled workers, and ancient inscribed documents with divine proclamations.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "A certain reciter of traditions related to me as follows, 'A man of Quraysh, who were demolishing the kacba, put his crowbar between two stones to remove one of them and when the stone moved all Mecca shook, so they stopped working on that foundation.'" Mnsa b. Wqba stated, "'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas claimed that leaders of Quraysh related that when they had assembled to extract stones to set up the (stations of) Abraham and Ishmael, upon both of whom be peace, one of their tribesmen began removing one of the stones from the original foundation. When he lifted it up, not knowing that it was from the original foundation, the onlookers saw a light emitting from beneath it that almost dazzled the man's sight. Then the stone jumped out of his hand and dropped back into its place. The man and the other builders were terrified. But when the stone again hid from them what was beneath it they went back to their building, saying, 'Don't move that stone, or any of those next to it.'" Ibn Ishaq stated, "It was related to me that Quraysh found in the corner a document with Syriac writing on it and they did not know what it was until a Jew read it to them. It said, 'I am God, Lord of Bakka (Mecca). I created it the day I created the heavens and the earth and formed the sun and the moon. I have set seven trusty angels around it. It will not cease to exist until its akhshabaha (a word Ibn Hisham interpreted to mean "its two mountains") disappear, a blessing to its people in their water and their milk.'" Ibn Ishaq stated, "It was related to me that they found in the shrine a document stating, 'Mecca is God's holy House. Its sustenance shall come from three paths; let it not be first desecrated by its own people.'" He also stated that Layth b. Aba Sulaym claimed that they had found a stone in the ka'ba 40 years before the coming of the mission of the Prophet (SAAS) — if what they said is true — on which was written, 'He who plants good shall harvest joy; he who plants evil shall harvest regret. If you do evil things, shall you be rewarded with good things? Indeed so, just as grapes are harvested from thorns!'
lessons: The sacred has boundaries that human hands must respect; not everything ancient and holy is meant to be unearthed or disturbed.
Divine wisdom can be embedded in the very earth and stones around us, waiting to be discovered by those who approach with humility and reverence.
The principle that 'you reap what you sow' is not merely a human proverb but a cosmic law inscribed in the fabric of creation itself.
practical_advice: When you encounter signs that a boundary should not be crossed — whether physical, ethical, or emotional — respect that limit even if your curiosity or ambition urges you forward.
Seek wisdom in unexpected places; important truths about your life and values can surface in moments of disruption and change, much as these inscriptions were found during demolition.
Reflect regularly on the spiritual law of cause and effect in your own life: identify patterns where your actions are producing outcomes, and adjust accordingly.

title: The Inscribed Slates Found in the Ka'ba
summary: Three inscribed slates were reportedly found within the Ka'ba, containing divine proclamations about God's creation of Mecca, the sacred nature of kinship, and blessings promised to the people of the holy house.
story: Sa'id b. Yahya al-Umawi stated that al-Mu'tamir b. Sulayman al-Raqqi related to him, from 'Abd Allah b. Bishr al-Zuhri, with a chain of authorities back to the prophet (SAAS), who said, "In the shrine three slates were found. On the first was written, 'It is I, God, Lord of Bakka, I made it when I made the sun and the moon and I have set seven trusty angels around it. And I have blessed its people in the meat and in the milk.' "On the second it said, 'I am God, Lord of Bakka. I created al-rahim, kinship, which I derived from my name, (Rahim, meaning merciful and compassionate). He who acts kindly'"
lessons: God's mercy and compassion are not abstract concepts but are woven into the very structure of human relationships through the bond of kinship.
Sacred places carry a history far older than any single generation, and connecting to that history deepens one's sense of meaning and belonging.
Divine blessings are often attached to specific duties — caring for family, maintaining sacred bonds — and withdrawing from those duties withdraws us from the blessing.
practical_advice: Reflect on the relationships in your life that are held sacred — family, community, spiritual bonds — and ask yourself honestly whether you are maintaining them with the care they deserve.
When you feel disconnected from meaning or purpose, return to foundational sources of wisdom in your tradition; they often contain answers that feel newly relevant.
Gratitude for where you live and the community around you is itself a spiritual practice — recognizing the sacred in the ordinary grounds you in the present moment.

title: The Three Slates Found in the Ka'ba Shrine
summary: Three inscribed slates were discovered inside the Ka'ba shrine, each containing divine messages about kinship, creation, and moral accountability attributed to God, the Lord of Bakka.
story: from 'Abd Allah b. Bisbr al-Zuhri, with a chain of authorities back to the prophet (SAAS), who said, "In the shrine three slates were found. On the first was written, 'It is I, God, Lord of Bakka, I made it when I made the sun and the moon and I have set seven trusty angels around it. And I have blessed its people in the meat and in the milk.' "On the second it said, 'I am God, Lord of Bakka. I created al-rahim, kinship, which I derived from my name, (Rahim, (meaning) merciful and compassionate). He who acts kindly to his kin, so will I treat; he who severs his ties of kinship, so will I treat.' "On the third was written: 'I am God, Lord of Bakka. I created goodness and evil and I predetermine. Joy shall be for those who bring good, and woe upon those who bring evil! "'
lessons: Divine messages remind us that our actions toward family and others carry eternal consequences, encouraging us to nurture relationships with care.
God's blessing is embedded in the foundations of creation; recognizing this fosters deep gratitude for life's provisions.
The principle of reciprocity — that God treats us as we treat others — is a profound motivation for moral consistency even when no one is watching.
practical_advice: Reflect daily on how you have treated your family and close relationships, and make one small gesture to strengthen those bonds.
When feeling lost or disconnected from purpose, reconnect with foundational beliefs about accountability and divine care for creation.
Write down one good action you will commit to today, grounding it in the understanding that goodness returns to its source.

title: The Tribes of Quraysh Dispute Over Placing the Black Stone
summary: The tribes of Quraysh fell into a dangerous dispute over who had the honor of placing the Black Stone during the rebuilding of the Ka'ba, nearly coming to violent conflict before agreeing to accept the judgment of the first man to enter the mosque.
story: "Then the tribes of Quraysh gathered stones to rebuild it, each one collecting them separately. "They built it up until it reached the place for the (black) stone but then fell into dispute about it. Each tribe wanted to raise it into its position, regardless of the rest. Eventually they broke up, established alliances, and readied to do battle. Banti 'Abd al-Dzr brought a bowl full of blood and they and Banti 'Adi b. Ka'b b. Lu'ayy made a pact to fight to the death, placing their hands into that bowl of blood. And so they were known as the 'blood-lickers'. "Quraysh remained in this confrontation for four or five nights, then they met together in the mosque, debated one another but divided into equal sides.
lessons: Disputes over honor and prestige can escalate rapidly; stepping back and agreeing on a fair process for resolution is far wiser than fighting to the death over symbolic gains.
Even in deeply divided communities, a shared willingness to submit to impartial judgment can prevent catastrophic conflict.
Pride is often the root of unnecessary suffering; recognizing this can help us choose peace over ego.
practical_advice: When in a conflict where everyone feels equally entitled, propose a neutral and fair process for resolution rather than pressing your own claim harder.
Before escalating a disagreement, pause and ask yourself whether the honor you are fighting for is worth the damage it may cause to your relationships and community.
Practice letting go of the need to 'win' symbolic moments, and instead invest that energy into outcomes that benefit everyone involved.

title: The Prophet Muhammad Resolves the Black Stone Dispute with Wisdom
summary: The Prophet Muhammad, before his prophethood, resolved a potentially bloody tribal dispute over placing the Black Stone by placing it on a robe and inviting all tribal leaders to lift it together, earning him the title 'al-Amin' (the trustworthy).
story: "Some scholars of the traditions claim that Abti Umayya b. al-Mughira b. 'Abd A m b. 'Umar b. Makhzm, who was at that time the oldest man in all @raysh, said, '0 Tribe of of Quraysh, resolve your dispute by agreeing that the first man who enters by the door of this mosque will decide the issue.' They agreed. "The first man to enter was the Messenger of God (SAAS) and when they saw him they said, 'This is al-Amin, "the trustworthy"; we are satisfied. This is Muhammad.' When he reached them and they told him of the problem, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said: 'Bring me a robe.' One was brought to him and he took the cornerpiece (the 'black stone') and placed it on the robe. Then he said, 'Let each tribe grasp one side of the cloth. Then all raise it up.' This they did until it was at the right spot, whereupon the Messenger of God (SAAS) himself positioned it. Then they built above it." Quraysh used to refer to the Messenger of God (SAAS) as "the trustworthy".
lessons: True leadership in a crisis is not about asserting personal authority but about finding creative solutions that give everyone a sense of dignity and participation.
A reputation for trustworthiness and fairness, built over years of consistent character, becomes a resource that benefits entire communities in moments of crisis.
Wisdom often lies not in choosing between competing parties but in reframing the problem so that all can share in the solution.
practical_advice: When mediating a conflict between others, look for solutions that distribute honor and responsibility rather than simply picking a winner.
Invest consistently in your reputation for fairness and reliability; in difficult moments, others will naturally turn to you for guidance.
When facing a seemingly impossible dilemma, ask yourself: is there a creative reframing that makes this a shared victory rather than a zero-sum contest?

title: Al-Sa'ib b. Abd Allah's Account of Idol Worship and the Black Stone Placement
summary: Al-Sa'ib b. Abd Allah recounts his participation in the rebuilding of the Ka'ba, describing his former idol worship and how the Prophet's arrival resolved the dispute over placing the Black Stone.
story: Imzn Alynad stated that (Ahd al-Samad related to hi, quoting Th%bitmeaning Aha Yazid - quoting Hi21 - meaning Ibn HabbZn - from Mujshid from his mawla, whose name was al-Sa'ib b. 'Ahd Allah, that the last-mentioned related to him that he was one of those who built the ka'ba before the coming of Islam. He stated, "I had a stone I had sculpted that I worshipped besides God. I used to bring clotted milk unfit for myself and pour it over it. Then a dog would lick it off, raise its hind leg and urinate on it. We continued building until we reached the position for the (black) stone, hut no one knew where it was. But then it was seen among our rocks looking like a man's head with a face almost distinguishable. A leader of Quraysh said, 'We'll position it.' But others said they too would do it. People suggested an arbitrator be appointed and they agreed that he should be the first man to appear from the mountain road. And it was the Messenger of God (SAAS) who arrived, and those there commented, 'Well, it's the "trustworthy" who's come.' They talked to him and he placed it on a cloak. He then summoned their head men and they lifted it up while he, (SAAS) placed it in its position."
lessons: The absurdity of misplaced devotion becomes clear in hindsight; this story invites honest reflection on what we truly worship or give our energy to in life.
Divine guidance often arrives through people of genuine character at exactly the right moment, even in societies that have strayed far from truth.
A life can be transformed when we recognize the difference between hollow rituals and actions grounded in genuine wisdom and integrity.
practical_advice: Periodically examine whether the things you invest time, energy, and resources in are genuinely worthy of that devotion, or whether they are habits inherited without reflection.
When you feel stuck in a situation with no clear resolution, remain open to an unexpected person or perspective that may offer a way through.
Use moments of clarity about past misguided behavior not for shame but as motivating evidence of your capacity for growth and change.

title: The Dimensions and Coverings of the Ka'ba Over History
summary: Ibn Ishaq describes the historical dimensions of the Ka'ba at the time of the Prophet and the succession of coverings and structural changes it underwent across generations of rulers.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "At the time of the Prophet (SAAS), the kacba measured 18 cubits and was covered by Egyptian-woven Qabqi cloth. Later it was covered by a l - b ~ r i i dcloths. ~ ~ The first to clothe it with the dibzj silk brocade was al-Hajj~j b. Yiisuf." My comment is that they left out of it the hijr area that was six or seven cubits long in the direction of Syria. They had insufficient funds for that; that is, they were not able to rebuild it on Abraham's foundations. They constructed one door for the ka'ba on the east side, placing it high up so that not everyone could get inside. This allowed them to permit inside only those they wished.
lessons: Even sacred spaces are subject to the limitations and imperfections of human circumstances; this teaches humility about our own incomplete efforts in service of higher purposes.
The preservation of legacy requires ongoing stewardship; neglect or selfish gatekeeping corrupts even the most honored institutions.
Acknowledging material or financial constraints honestly, rather than cutting corners silently, is a mark of integrity in communal projects.
practical_advice: When you cannot complete something to its ideal standard due to real constraints, document what was left undone and why, so future generations can build on your work honestly.
Reflect on whether you are acting as a fair steward of shared resources and spaces, or whether you are restricting access in ways that serve only your own interests.
Find gratitude in the layered history of the things you inherit; every imperfection carries a story of human struggle and aspiration.

title: The Prophet's Wish to Rebuild the Ka'ba on Abraham's Foundations
summary: The Prophet Muhammad told Aisha that he wished he could rebuild the Ka'ba on the foundations of Abraham, including the hijr area and two ground-level doors, but restrained himself out of concern for the recently converted Quraysh.
story: It has been established in both ~ahihcollections, on the authority of 'A'isha, God be pleased with her, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) told her: "Don't you know that your people had insufficient funds for the expenses? If it were not for the fact that your people were only recently unbelievers, I would have tom down the ka'ba and made for it one door on the east and another on the west. And I would have included the hijl. area within it."
lessons: True wisdom sometimes means holding back from what is ideally right in order to protect the fragile faith and unity of a community in its early stages.
Strategic patience — knowing when not to act — is as important as knowing what action to take.
Even the highest ideals must be balanced against the realistic capacity of people and communities to receive and sustain change.
practical_advice: When you know the ideal course of action but the people around you are not ready for it, consider what steps you can take now without destabilizing their progress.
Practice distinguishing between changes that serve a genuine higher purpose and changes that would simply satisfy your own sense of correctness at others' expense.
Share your deepest visions and hopes with trusted confidants even when you cannot act on them; speaking truth preserves integrity and plants seeds for future action.

title: Ibn al-Zubayr Rebuilds the Ka'ba on Abraham's Foundations
summary: Ibn al-Zubayr rebuilt the Ka'ba according to the Prophet's wishes, restoring Abraham's original foundations with two ground-level doors, but al-Hajjaj later reversed this after executing Ibn al-Zubayr, on orders from Caliph Abd al-Malik.
story: This was how Ibn Zubayr rebuilt it, just as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had indicated. It was done in the utmost splendour and beauty, one complete whole on the foundations of al-Khalll (Abraham). It had two doors at ground level, on the east and west sides, and people would enter through one and exit through the other. When he had executed Ibn al-Zubayr, al-Hajjaj wrote to the Caliph of the time, 'Abd al-Malik b. Marwan, concerning what Ibn Zubayr had done; and they believed that he had made the changes on his own authority. And so it was ordered that it he restored to its original state. They set to work on the "Syrian" wall, tearing it down and removing the (black) stone, laying out its stones on the kacba floor. Then its door was raised and the western door was blocked off; the eastern one was left as it was. When it was the rime of a,-Mahdi - or his son al-Mansiir - he (the ruler) sought advice from Mzlik on rebuilding it as it had been remodelled by Ibn al-Zubayr. But Malik, God be pleased with him, said, "I hate for the rulers to neat it like a plaything." And so he left it as it was; it remains the same to this day.
lessons: Political power can reverse even the most righteous and well-founded restorations; this reminds us that human institutions are always impermanent and subject to worldly pressures.
Imam Malik's wisdom in refusing to turn the sacred house into a 'plaything' of rulers teaches that some things deserve protection from the cycles of political change.
The vision of a great leader, even when only partially realized, leaves a lasting imprint on history that cannot be entirely erased.
practical_advice: When your good work is undone by circumstances or authorities beyond your control, take comfort in the fact that your intention and effort are recorded and not wasted.
Recognize when an institution or relationship has been subjected to too many reversals, and have the wisdom to call for stability rather than yet another change.
Seek counsel from people of deep principle and experience before making changes to things that hold communal or spiritual significance.

title: The Expansion of the Sacred Mosque Over Successive Rulers
summary: The text traces the successive expansions and improvements of the Masjid al-Haram in Mecca from the time of Umar ibn al-Khattab through Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan, showing how each ruler contributed to the sacred space.
story: As for the masjid al-harzm, the sacred mosque at Mecca, the first man to remove the buildings around the ka'ba was Wmar b. al-Khagzb, God be pleased with him. H e purchased them from their owners and demolished them. When Wthmzn ruled he bought other houses which he added to it. When Ibn al-Zubayr succeeded, he reinforced it, improved its walls, and increased the number of its doors. But he was not able to make any additions to increase its size. When 'Abd d - M ~ l i kb. Marwzn seized power he made the walls of the sacred mosque taller and ordered that the kacba be covered by the dibay. The one who camed out this order was al-Hajjxj b. Yiisuf.
lessons: Every generation has a responsibility to preserve and build upon what it has inherited for the benefit of those who come after.
Leadership that invests in shared sacred spaces expresses care not just for the present community but for the continuity of faith across time.
Material investment in places of worship reflects and reinforces the spiritual priorities of a community and its leaders.
practical_advice: Consider what communal or spiritual resources you have inherited and ask what you can contribute to their preservation or improvement, however modest.
When leaders in your community take genuine steps to invest in shared spaces and institutions, recognize and support those efforts rather than taking them for granted.
Reflect on the legacy you wish to leave in your community and identify one concrete step you can take today toward that long-term contribution.

title: Al-Zubayr ibn Abd al-Muttalib's Poem About the Eagle and the Serpent
summary: Al-Zubayr ibn Abd al-Muttalib composed verses commemorating the divine sign of an eagle driving away the serpent that had frightened Quraysh from rebuilding the Ka'ba, celebrating the tribes who participated in its construction.
story: Ihn Ishzq stated, "When they had finished the construction, having built it as they wished, al-Zubayr b. 'Abd al-MutGb spoke the following verses about the serpent that caused Quraysh to be afraid of rebuilding the kacba: '? was amazed at the eagle aiming straight for the serpent when it was in an excited state. It used to make a rustling sound, and sometimes it would dash out; When we began the building it increased our awe of the structure and we were scared. When we were too afraid to drive it away an eagle came, swooping straight down at it. It grasped it tight, then left the building to us; there being nothing else in its way. Together we assembled to begin the building, for which we already had the foundations and the soil; Next morning its foundations were raised, our men wearing no clothes. The Lord ennobled Bane Lu'ayy through it and there's no denying their founding it. Banii 'Adi had also assembled there, and Murra who were preceded by Kilzb. The Lord thereby established us in glory, and it is from God that reward is requested."'
lessons: Fear is a natural response to undertaking something sacred and great; divine assistance often arrives precisely when human courage reaches its limit.
Commemorating moments of communal achievement through art and poetry preserves collective memory and strengthens shared identity across generations.
Glory and honor are not self-generated; recognizing that they come from God protects communities from arrogance and grounds celebration in gratitude.
practical_advice: When fear paralyzes you before a meaningful undertaking, remember that the obstacle before you may be removed by forces greater than your own once you commit to beginning.
Mark your significant personal and communal achievements in some lasting form — whether writing, art, or storytelling — so the lessons are not lost.
When you achieve something great, resist the temptation to attribute it solely to your own effort; express gratitude to God and to those who contributed alongside you.

title: The Prophet Protected from Pre-Islamic Customs During Ka'ba Construction
summary: During the rebuilding of the Ka'ba, the young Prophet began to position his loincloth over his shoulder to cushion the stones he was carrying, but was divinely prevented from doing so and reverted to his former modest practice.
story: Wc carlier treated in a separate passage how God protected the Messenger of God (SAAS) from the bad practices of the pre-Islamic era. He and his uncle alLCAbbasused to transport stones and when he (SAAS) positioned his loincloth over his shoulder beneath the stones, he was forbidden from doing so and tberefore reverted to his former practice.
lessons: Divine protection of the Prophet's dignity and modesty even before his prophethood shows that God prepares chosen individuals long before their mission begins.
Our deepest character is often shaped and protected by forces we do not fully understand at the time; what seems like a small restraint may be a profound protection.
Returning to one's fundamental values when gently corrected, without resistance or shame, is a mark of inner nobility.
practical_advice: When you feel an inner check or discomfort about an action you are about to take, take that seriously as a signal worth heeding rather than overriding.
Reflect on moments in your life where you were 'held back' from something that, in hindsight, would have been harmful or unworthy of you — and find gratitude in that protection.
Practice returning to your core values quickly and without excessive self-criticism when you momentarily drift from them.

title: The Customs of al-Hums: Quraysh's Religious Innovations and Restrictions
summary: Ibn Ishaq describes how the Quraysh called themselves al-Hums and imposed a set of self-serving religious innovations — including forbidding pilgrims from eating their own food or wearing their own clothes during circumambulation — that contradicted the practices established by Abraham.
story: Ibn Ishziiq related how Quraysh began the practice of calling themselves al-Hums, a word implying intensity in religion, and intolerance. This is because they g&veextreme veneration to the holy places, to the extent that they required people not to leave there on the night of the procession to 'Arafiit. They would say, "We are men of the holy places, the haram, and the Quttdn, those who dwell at God's house." They would not make the halt on Mt. 'Arafiit, though they knew that was the wish of Abraham, peace be upon him, in order not to abandon the corrupt innovative practices they themselves established. They would not put away for storage sour cheese made from milk or butter, or clarify fat while they were in a state of ritual uncleanliness. While in this state, they would not enter any tent made of hair, and would seek shelter from the sun only under tents made of leather. Similarly they prevented those making the greater or smaller pilgrimage from eating any but Quraysh food while in that state, and these people could only circumambulate wearing Quraysh clothes. If one of these pilgrims could not find a gown from one of the Hums, who were Quraysh either by birth or by having joined Quraysh from Kinma and Khuza'a, they would have to circumambulate naked, even if they were women. A woman who happened to go round in this manner would place her hand over her vagina and recite: "Today all or part may appear, but visible though it may be, I do not make it available!" If anyone who had access to the garment of a Hums person were too proud to use it, then he could go round in his own clothes, but when he had finished he had to throw them aside; thereafter, they could not be used again, either by them or by anyone else, nor ever touched. The Arabs used to call such clothes al-luqa, "cast-offs". A certain poet spoke the limes: "How sad it is, my returning to it, it being l i e a proscribed thing cast offbefore the pilgrims."
lessons: Religious practices that serve the interests of an elite class at the expense of others' dignity are a corruption of faith, however intensely they are observed on the surface.
Confusing cultural privilege with spiritual superiority leads communities away from the universal and just principles of genuine religion.
The contrast between these invented restrictions and the Prophet's later inclusive and dignifying practice of Islam highlights how true religion uplifts rather than humiliates.
practical_advice: Examine the religious or cultural rules you follow and ask honestly whether they draw people closer to God and each other, or whether they create hierarchies that serve only some.
When you encounter communities or institutions that use religious authority to control or humiliate others, trust your instinct that something is wrong and seek authentic sources of guidance.
Reflect on whether any practices you have inherited are genuinely rooted in divine guidance or are human inventions that have accumulated unquestioned authority over time.

title: The Qur'an Corrects Quraysh's Deviation from the Pilgrimage of Abraham
summary: God revealed a Qur'anic verse to the Prophet commanding all pilgrims to proceed from Arafat as the broader masses of Arabs did, directly correcting the innovation of the Quraysh who refused to do so out of tribal pride.
story: Ibn Ishiiq stated, "They continued in these practices until God sent Muhammad (SAAS) and revealed the Qur'iin to him, as a retort to them and their innovations. God said, 'Hasten forth from the place where people hasten from' (sfirah al-Baqara, 11, v.199). By this is meant the masses of the Arabs from 'Arafzt. And also that same verse"
lessons: Divine revelation comes to liberate people from the tyranny of inherited customs that have displaced genuine worship and universal justice.
God's guidance ultimately vindicates the many who were humiliated by those who claimed exclusive religious authority.
The correction of long-standing communal error requires courage — but divine support makes that correction possible even against the most entrenched opposition.
practical_advice: When a long-standing rule or custom in your community is challenged by a principle of justice or universal dignity, be willing to examine that challenge honestly rather than defending tradition reflexively.
If you have been on the receiving end of rules that excluded or humiliated you in the name of religion or culture, seek out authentic, dignifying expressions of faith that affirm your full humanity.
Commit to at least one practice this week that aligns your actions with justice and inclusion, even if it requires departing from what is merely customary.

title: Pre-Islamic Arab Religious Innovations and the Quranic Response
summary: Before the advent of Islam, Arab tribes had introduced religious innovations during pilgrimage, including restrictions on food and clothing. The Quran was revealed to correct these practices and guide humanity back to the truth.
story: The Arabs used to call such clothes al-luqa, "cast-offs". A certain poet spoke the limes: "How sad it is, my returning to it, it being like a proscribed thing cast off before the pilgrims." Ibn Ishāq stated, "They continued in these practices until God sent Muhammad (SAAS) and revealed the Qur'ān to him, as a retort to them and their innovations. God said, 'Hasten forth from the place where people hasten from' (sūrah al-Baqara, II, v.199). By this is meant the masses of the Arabs from 'Arafāt. And also that same verse reads, 'and ask the forgiveness of God; surely God is forgiving, merciful.'" As we have previously shown, the Messenger of God (SAAS) would make the halt at 'Arafat before he received his prophethood, this being an award granted by God to him. God also revealed to him a response to their practice of forbidding people certain actions and foods in the words, "O mankind, attend to your dress at every prayer meeting and eat and drink, but do not be extreme. God does not love extremists. Say, 'Who made forbidden clothing (from) God that He brought forth for His worshippers, along with all good provisions?'" (sūrat al-A'rāf, VII, v.31-2). Ziyad al-Bakkāi stated from Ibn Ishāq, "But I don't know whether their making these innovations preceded or followed the battle of the elephant."
lessons: Human beings are prone to drifting from divine guidance and inventing their own religious rules, often burdening themselves unnecessarily. Divine revelation serves as a correction and a mercy.
God's response to human confusion is not punishment but guidance — He sent a Book and a Messenger to gently redirect those who had gone astray.
Extremism in religion, even when well-intentioned, is rejected by God. Balance and moderation are themselves acts of worship.
practical_advice: When you feel confused about right and wrong, return to foundational sources of guidance rather than adding burdens to yourself based on custom or assumption.
If you have been practicing a habit out of tradition rather than understanding, take time to examine whether it truly serves your wellbeing and aligns with sound values.
Release unnecessary self-imposed restrictions that have no grounding in wisdom. God's provisions — physical and spiritual — are meant to be appreciated, not shunned out of misplaced piety.

title: Prophecies of the Coming of Prophet Muhammad Across Traditions
summary: Jewish rabbis, Christian priests, and Arab soothsayers all carried foreknowledge of the coming of Prophet Muhammad, with references found in the Torah, the Bible, and through the words of Jesus. This demonstrates that his mission was a fulfillment of a divine covenant that spanned all of prophethood.
story: Muhammad b. Ishāq, God bless him, stated: "Jewish Rabbis, Christian priests and Arab soothsayers all spoke of the Messenger of God (SAAS) before his mission as the time for it approached. The rabbis and the priests found in their books descriptions of him and of his time along with inferences relating to him from their prophets." God Almighty said, "Those who follow the Messenger, the prophet, the ummi, 'the unlettered', to whom they will find written reference in the Torah and the Bible requiring them to do good and warning them of evil, allowing them the good things and forbidding them the bad, removing the burden and chains formerly upon them; so those who believe in him, honour him and aid him and follow the light that was sent down with him, those will be successful" (sūrat al-A'rāf VII, v.157). God Almighty stated, "And when Jesus son of Mary said, 'O children of Israel, I am God's messenger to you, bringing affirmation of the Torah before me and giving tidings of a prophet who will come after me whose name will be Ahmad'" (sūrat al-Saff, LXI, v.6). And God Almighty said, "Muhammad is the Messenger of God. And those with him are those who are very severe against the unbelievers, compassionate with one another. You shall see them bowing down, prostrating themselves, seeking grace from God, and approval. They have marks on their faces from their prostrations.
