That is their likeness in the Torah and in the Bible. (They are) like plants. (God) brings forth their sprouts and then strengthens them and thickens them so they stand straight on their stalks. He pleases the planters to discomfort through them the unbelievers. God gave promise of forgiveness and great reward to those who believe in him and perform righteous deeds" (sūrat al-Fath, XLVIII, v.29). And God Almighty said, "When God made the covenant with the Prophets, saying, 'I gave you the Scripture and wisdom, then there will come unto you a messenger confirming what you possess; you shall believe in him and render him help.' He said, 'Do you agree and accept my covenant as binding to you?' They replied, 'We affirm.' He said, 'So bear witness and I along with you am a witness'" (sūrat Āl-'Imrān, III, v.81). In the Ṣaḥīḥ of al-Bukhārī there is a hadith from Ibn 'Abbās that states, "God never sent down a prophet without entering into a covenant upon him (to the effect that) if Muhammad were sent down his mission when he was (already) living, then he (that prophet) would believe in him and aid him. God ordered him to take a covenant to his people that if Muhammad were given his mission during their lifetime, then they should believe in him, aid him, and follow him." This confirms that all prophets had talked about him and were commanded to follow him. Abraham, upon whom be peace, stated in the prayer he made about the people of Mecca, "O our Lord! Send among them as a messenger one of their own people, who will recite to them Your signs and teach them the scriptures and wisdom and purify them. You are the mighty one, the wise" (sūrat al-Baqara, II, v.129).
lessons: The coming of Prophet Muhammad was not a sudden or isolated event but the culmination of a divine plan announced through every major prophetic tradition. This unity of purpose across centuries is a source of deep spiritual reassurance.

When multiple streams of evidence converge on the same truth, it calls for sincere reflection rather than denial. The scholars and priests who recognized the signs but refused to follow bore a heavier burden than those who simply never heard.
Abraham's prayer for the people of Mecca being answered centuries later is a testament to the power of sincere supplication. No prayer made with true faith is ever lost or forgotten by God.
practical_advice: When you feel that your struggles have no meaning or that you are forgotten, reflect on how Abraham prayed for a people he would never meet — and God answered across centuries. Your prayers too are held.

If you are searching for truth and find consistent signs pointing in one direction, trust that inner recognition. Spiritual clarity often comes not in a single flash but through the gradual convergence of many signals.
Study the connections between your own tradition and the broader human story of faith. Understanding that truth is ancient and continuous can anchor you when you feel spiritually adrift.

title: The Prophet's Declaration: 'I Was the First of the Prophets to Be Created'
summary: Multiple hadith narrations establish that the Prophet Muhammad was the first of all prophets to be created in the divine plan, even before Adam, and was the last to be sent. When asked when he became a prophet, he replied: 'When Adam was between the spirit and the body.'
story: Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Mahdī related to him, quoting Mu'āwiyya' b. Ṣāliḥ, from Sa'id b. Suwayd al-Kalbī, from 'Abd al-A'lā b. Hilāl al-Sulamī, from al-'Irbāḍ b. Sāriyya who stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'For God I am the Seal of the prophets; Adam was well made in His likeness. And I shall tell you of the first of it: my father Abraham's prayer, the prediction of Jesus about my coming, and the visions seen by my mother, as well as by the mothers of the believers.'" Al-Layth related this from Mu'āwiyya' b. Ṣāliḥ who said, "When she delivered him, his mother saw a light from him that illuminated the castles of Syria." Imam Ahmad also said, "'Abd al-Raḥmān related to us, as did Manṣūr b. Sa'd, from Badīl b. Maysara, from 'Abd Allāh b. Shaqīq, from Maysara al-Fajr, who stated, 'I asked, O Messenger of God, when did you become a prophet?' He replied, 'When Adam was between the spirit and the body.'" Ahmad is the sole source of these. 'Umar b. Aḥmad b. Shāhīn related this in the work entitled Dalā'il al-Nubuwwa (Signs of the Prophethood) from a hadith of Abū Hurayra who stated, "'Abd Allāh b. Muḥammad b. 'Abd al-'Azīz — meaning Abū al-Qāsim al-Baghawī — related to us, as did Abū Ḥammād al-Walīd b. Muslim, from al-Awzā'ī, and I was told by Yaḥyā from Abū Salama, from Abū Hurayra, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was asked, 'When was it made your duty to become a prophet?' He replied, 'Between the making of Adam and the breathing of the spirit into him.'" It was related on another line of authorities from al-Awzā'ī who used the words 'and Adam was formed in his likeness'. It was also related from al-Baghawī, from Aḥmad b. al-Miqdām, from Baqiyya b. Sa'īd b. Bashīr, from Qatāda, from Abū Hurayra, relating to the words of Almighty God, "And when we made a covenant with the prophets, and with you, and with Noah" (sūrat al-Aḥzāb, XXXIII, v.7) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "I was the first of the prophets to be created and the last of them to be sent." And from a hadith of Abū Muzāḥim, on the authority of Qays b. al-Rabī', from Jābir, from al-Sha'bī, from Ibn 'Abbās, that someone asked, "O Messenger of God, when were you a prophet?" He replied, "While Adam was between spirit and body."
lessons: The Prophet Muhammad's existence in the divine plan preceded the creation of humanity itself. This speaks to the magnitude of his mission — not as a response to human failure, but as a foundational part of the divine design for creation.

The light that illuminated the castles of Syria at the Prophet's birth is a metaphor that resonates across time: true guidance, when it arrives, does not illuminate just one corner but reaches far and wide.

Understanding that great missions are prepared long before they appear should encourage patience in those who are waiting for their own purpose to unfold. What God prepares, He prepares thoroughly and at the right time.
practical_advice: When you feel that your life lacks direction or that your purpose has not yet emerged, remember that even the greatest of missions was prepared across vast stretches of time before it was revealed. Trust in your own unfolding.
Reflect on the consistent thread of your life — the prayers of those who came before you, the moments that shaped you — and recognize that your story too has a coherence you may not yet fully see.
When you feel overlooked or unrecognized, take comfort in the knowledge that true worth is not determined by when the world notices you, but by the role God has prepared for you.

title: The Jinn Listen to the Quran and the Screening of the Devils
summary: As the time of the Prophet's mission approached, the devils were prevented from overhearing the heavens, stars were hurled at them, and a group of jinn who heard the Quran recited accepted its truth and returned to warn their people.
story: The Arab soothsayers had been told by the devils from the jinn things overheard by them while they were still not prevented from so doing by having stars cast at them. Soothsayers, male and female, continued mentioning such matters, without the Arabs giving any credence to them, until God Almighty sent him, and there occurred those things they had talked of and so recognized. When the time of the Messenger of God and his prophethood approached, the devils were screened from overhearing and they were prevented from occupying those seats from which they used to overhear. Stars were hurled at them, and the devils knew that that was because of an order coming from God, the Almighty and Glorious. On this subject God sent down to his messenger (SAAS) the words, "Say: it has been revealed to me that a group of jinn listened and then said, 'We heard a wonderful Qur'ān which guides towards the right, and we believed in it. And we will never associate any other god with our Lord'" (sūrat al-Jinn; LXXII, v.1-2). We have given prior explanation to all that in our Tafsīr, exegesis of the Qur'an, as well as to God's words, "And when we directed (mention) towards you (of) a group of jinn who listened to the Qur'ān. And when they came to it, they said, 'Listen silently!' And when it was over they turned back to their people warning them, and saying: 'O people ours, we have heard a book sent down after Moses verifying what came before it, guiding towards the truth, and to the straight path'" (sūrat al-Aḥqāf, XLVI, v.29,30).
lessons: Even beings from an unseen realm recognized the truth of the Quran upon first hearing it and immediately embraced it without hesitation. This is a reminder that truth carries its own undeniable weight.

The removal of the devils' access to the heavens at the onset of revelation signifies that God actively protects and preserves the integrity of divine guidance. The channel between God and humanity was cleared for something pure.
Those jinn who heard the Quran did not keep the guidance to themselves — they returned to their community to share it and warn them. Receiving truth carries with it a responsibility to pass it on.
practical_advice: When you encounter wisdom that moves you deeply and feels undeniably true, do not dismiss that inner recognition. That resonance is itself meaningful and worth following.
If you feel that negative influences have clouded your thinking or spiritual life, know that God has the power to screen out what corrupts and protect what is pure. Ask for that protection sincerely.
When you receive insight or guidance that helps you, consider who in your life might also benefit from it. Sharing what has helped you is itself an act of gratitude.

title: The Arabs' Fear at the Shooting Stars Before the Prophet's Mission
summary: The tribe of Thaqif became alarmed when they witnessed stars being hurled across the sky. Their wisest elder, 'Amr b. Umayya, counseled them to distinguish between the major stars and the minor ones to understand the significance of the signs, while a soothsayer received mysterious warnings of events to come.
story: Muhammad b. Ishāq stated that Ya'qūb b. 'Utba b. al-Mughīra b. al-Akhnas related to him that he was told that the first Arabs to be afraid at (seeing) the stars being thrown was the tribe of Thaqīf. They consulted one of their men on the subject, whose name was 'Amr b. Umayya, one of Banū 'Ilāj, he being the most shrewd and astute of all Arabs. They asked him, "O 'Amr, don't you see what's going on in the heavens, all those stars being thrown about?" He replied, "I certainly do. Keep watch and if the ones being cast are most prominent stars, the ones by which men are guided on land and sea, and by the rising and setting of which the seasons of summer and winter are marked, for the good of men in their lives, then, by God, it means the end of the world, and the destruction of all creation. If it is other stars, while the prominent stars remain firm in their place, then it is occurring for some purpose God intends for mankind. So what could that be?" Ibn Ishāq said, "A certain scholar related to me that a woman of Banū Sahm known as al-Ghaylā who had been a soothsayer before the coming of Islam was visited one night by her other world contact who swooped down beneath her, then said, 'I know what I know; a day of slaying and slaughtering.' When she told Quraysh of this they asked, 'What does he mean?' When he came to her on another night, swooping down beneath her, he said, 'Ravines! What ravines? In...'"
lessons: When confronted with alarming or confusing signs, the wisest response is careful discernment rather than panic. 'Amr b. Umayya's counsel — to distinguish between the permanent and the passing — is a model of composed, analytical thinking under pressure.

Anxiety about the unknown is a deeply human experience. What matters is not the absence of fear, but what we do with it — seeking understanding, consulting the wise, and looking for meaning rather than collapsing into helplessness.

The universe itself was signaling the arrival of something momentous. God communicates through many channels; those who are attentive and reflective are better positioned to recognize divine signs in the world around them.
practical_advice: When something unexpected or frightening occurs in your life, pause before catastrophizing. Ask yourself: is this a disruption of something fundamental, or is it a signal that something is changing for a purpose?

Seek out people of wisdom and experience when you are confused or fearful. The tribe of Thaqif's instinct to consult their wisest elder rather than act on fear alone is a healthy model for navigating uncertainty.

Practice distinguishing between what is stable and enduring in your life and what is in flux. Grounding yourself in what remains constant can calm anxiety when the circumstances around you feel turbulent.

title: The Soothsayer Al-Ghafiala's Prophecy of Badr and Uhud
summary: A soothsayer of Banu Sahm received cryptic messages from her spirit contact about 'a day of slaying and slaughtering' and 'ravines,' which Quraysh could not interpret until the battles of Badr and Uhud took place.
story: A certain scholar related to me that a woman of Bann Sahm known as al-Ghafiala who had been a soothsayer before the coming of Islam was visited one night by her other world contact who swooped down beneath her, then said, 'I know what I know; a day of slaying and slaughtering.' When she told Quraysh of this they asked, 'What does he mean?' When he came to her on another night, swooping down beneath her, he said, 'Ravines! What ravines? In them, down their sides, Ka'b will fall.' When this reached Quraysh they said, 'What does this mean? This is something that will happen; look to see what it will be.' But they could not interpret it until the battles of Badr and Uhud took place in 'ravines'; then they knew what it was the spirit had told her.
lessons: Divine signs and warnings often arrive before we have the wisdom to interpret them, reminding us that God's plan unfolds on His timeline, not ours.
Uncertainty and confusion about the future are natural human experiences; trust in God's greater wisdom is the antidote.
Events of great consequence are often foretold in ways that only become clear in hindsight, teaching us humility about our own understanding.
practical_advice: When facing confusing or uncertain situations, write down what you observe and trust that clarity will come with time rather than forcing premature conclusions.
Practice surrendering the need to have all the answers immediately; remind yourself daily that not understanding something now does not mean it lacks meaning.
Reflect on past events in your life where confusion eventually gave way to clarity, and use those memories to build patience in present uncertainty.

title: The Yemeni Soothsayer of Janh Recognizes the Prophet
summary: A soothsayer from the Yemeni tribe of Janh, asked by his people to investigate the Prophet Muhammad, descended his mountain and declared that God had honored Muhammad by choosing and purifying him.
story: 'Ali b. N%fifi' al-Jurashi related to me that Janh, a tribe from Yemen, had a soothsayer before Islam came. And when news of the Messenger of God (SAAS) spread among the Arabs, Janh asked their soothsayer, 'Find out about this man for us' and they assembled (to hear him) at the foot of his mountain. When the sun rose he came down to them and stood leaning on a bow he had. Then, having gazed up for a long time into the sky, he began to leap about. Then he spoke: 'O people, God has honoured Muhammad by choosing him. He has purified his heart and his insides. But, O people, his stay among you will be short.' He then hurried back up his mountain, returning whence he had come.
lessons: Truth has a way of being recognized even by those who exist outside the faith community, demonstrating that God's light cannot be hidden.
The acknowledgment of the Prophet's honor and purity by independent witnesses affirms that sincere seekers of truth will recognize it when it arrives.
Even brief encounters with truth can be transformative; it is not the length of time but the sincerity of recognition that matters.
practical_advice: When others around you seem to recognize something good or true that you are struggling to see, pause and listen rather than dismissing their perspective.
Seek moments of stillness and reflection before making important assessments or judgments about matters of significance in your life.
Remind yourself that genuine goodness and integrity are recognizable across cultural and social boundaries, and cultivate those qualities in yourself.

title: The Ansar Accept Islam After Hearing Jewish Warnings
summary: Members of the Ansar from Medina were moved toward Islam partly because they had repeatedly heard Jewish neighbors warn them of a coming prophet, and when the Prophet arrived they hastened to believe before the Jews who had made the prophecies.
story: 'Asim b. 'Amr b. Qatada related to him, from some of his tribe, as follows: 'One of the things that brought us into Islam - along with the mercy of God Almighty and His guidance - was what we heard from a Jew. We were at that time polytheists and worshipped idols, while they, the Jews, were people with Scriptures who had knowledge we did not. There was always enmity between us and them and if ever we bested them in some way they disliked they would tell us, "The time is near for a prophet; he will now be sent, and with him we will kill you just as Ad and Iram were killed." We heard that from them often. So when God sent His messenger (SAAS) we responded positively when he invited us to God, and became aware of what their threats to us meant. We therefore hastened to him before them and believed in him, while they disbelieved in him. Concerning us and them this verse was sent down: "And when there came to them a book from God verifying what they had, though they formerly prayed for victory against those who disbelieved; and when there came to them that which they knew, they disbelieved in it. And so may God's curse be upon the disbelievers"' (surat al-Baqara, II, v.89).
lessons: God can use even the warnings and antagonism of opponents as a means of guiding sincere hearts toward the truth.
Those who are humble and open-hearted often receive guidance through unexpected channels, while pride can cause others to reject the very truth they once acknowledged.
Acting swiftly and sincerely upon recognized truth is itself a form of divine blessing and should never be delayed out of pride or hesitation.
practical_advice: Remain open to receiving meaningful lessons and guidance even from those who are not your allies or who speak from a place of hostility.
When you recognize a truth, act on it promptly rather than waiting for perfect conditions or universal agreement from others around you.
Reflect on moments when God used difficult circumstances or even adversarial words to guide you toward something better, and cultivate gratitude for those unexpected blessings.

title: The Jews of Khaybar Pray for Victory in the Name of the Prophet Then Disbelieve in Him
summary: The Jews of Khaybar would invoke the name of the awaited Prophet Muhammad in their prayers for victory over their enemies, and God granted them victory, yet when the Prophet was actually sent they refused to believe in him.
story: It was related through 'Abd al-Malik b. Hanan b. 'Anbara, from his father, from his grandfather, from Sa'id b. Juhayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'The Jews were at Khaybar battling Ghatafan, but whenever battle was joined the Jews of Khaybar were defeated. So the Jews resorted to this prayer, saying, "O God, we will ask you by Muhammad's rightness, the Prophet, the ummi, 'the unlettered', whom you promised us you would send at the end of time, if you will give us victory over them."' The report went on to say that when they met in battle and they spoke this prayer they defeated Ghatafan. Yet when the Prophet (SAAS) was sent, they disbelieved in him. And so God, Almighty and Glorious is He, revealed the words, 'they formerly prayed for victory against those who disbelieved.'
lessons: Knowing the truth intellectually and submitting to it in the heart are entirely different matters; knowledge alone does not guarantee faith or righteousness.
Pride, envy, and self-interest can cause a person to reject what they have themselves acknowledged and even relied upon, a warning against allowing ego to override conscience.
Divine blessings granted in response to sincere supplication carry with them a responsibility to act consistently with the values underpinning that supplication.
practical_advice: Examine whether your stated beliefs and your actual actions are aligned; hypocrisy between the two causes spiritual harm and erodes integrity over time.
When you find yourself resisting a truth you already know inwardly, pause to identify whether pride, envy, or fear of loss is driving that resistance.
Make it a practice to act consistently with the values you invoke when asking God for help, so that your requests are grounded in genuine commitment rather than convenience.

title: The Jewish Neighbor Who Foretold the Prophet to the People of Banu Abd al-Ashhal
summary: A Jewish neighbor visited the household of Salama b. Salam and spoke about the resurrection and the Day of Judgement, then foretold the imminent arrival of a prophet from the direction of Mecca, yet when the Prophet came, the Jew refused to believe in him out of envy.
story: Salih b. Ibrahim b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf related to him, from Mahmud b. Labid, from Salama b. Salam b. Waqsh, who was at the battle of Badr, who said, 'We had a Jewish neighbour from Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal and he came out of his house one day to us. At that time, I was the youngest of my household; I was wearing a fur gown I had and was lying in it in the courtyard of my family's house. The Jew talked of the resurrection, the Day of Judgement, the reckoning, the scales, paradise, and hell-fire. This he told to polytheists, idolaters who did not believe in any life after death. They replied, "Come on, now! You really think people can be sent, after their death, to some place where there is a paradise, and a fire where they will be punished for their deeds?" "Yes indeed," he replied, "and by Him by whom oaths are sworn, anyone going into that fire would prefer being placed in the hottest oven in the house, and having it covered over him, and he being let out of it only next day." "Good gracious! But what proof is there?" they asked. "A prophet is being sent from hereabouts in these lands," he replied, and gestured over towards Mecca and Yemen. "When will we see him?" they enquired. He looked over at me, I being the youngest one there, and said, "If this lad lives a normal lifespan, he'll live to his time."' Salama said, 'And a day and a night had scarcely passed thereafter until God sent the Messenger of God (SAAS) who lived among us. And so we believed in him. But he (the Jew) disbelieved in him out of evil and envy. So we asked him, "What now then; aren't you the one who told us certain things about him?" "Yes," he replied, "but he's not the one."'
lessons: Envy is among the most spiritually destructive forces, capable of causing a person to deny what they themselves have proclaimed to others.
Young people who absorb sincere words of truth early in life are often positioned to become witnesses of great historical moments of divine mercy.
The proximity of a prophesied blessing does not guarantee that one will receive it; receptivity of heart is required above all else.
practical_advice: Guard yourself against envy by regularly celebrating the good that God bestows on others and training yourself to see it as a reflection of His generosity rather than a threat.
Share truthful and meaningful knowledge with those around you, especially the young, knowing that seeds of wisdom planted early can bear fruit long after they are sown.
If you find yourself contradicting or walking back a truth you once proclaimed, stop and honestly examine whether envy, pride, or self-interest is behind the reversal.

title: Yusha the Jewish Man of Banu Abd al-Ashhal Predicts the Prophet
summary: A Jewish man named Yusha living among the tribe of Banu Abd al-Ashhal pointed toward the Ka'bah and predicted that a prophet would soon be sent from that direction, urging all who would live to see him to believe, yet when the Prophet came Yusha himself did not accept Islam out of envy.
story: Abu Nu'aym related in al-Dala'il (The Signs), from 'Asim b. Umar b. Qatada, from Mahmud b. Labid, from Muhammad b. Salama, saying, 'In the tribe of 'Abd al-Ashhal there was only one Jew, whose name was Yusha'. When I was still a boy, wearing a waist-wrapper, I heard him say, "The time is near for a prophet to be sent to you from somewhere over near this building." And he pointed over at God's temple, saying, "And whoever lives till then, let him believe in him." And the Messenger of God (SAAS) was sent and we accepted Islam, while he, living there among us, did not, out of envy and evil.'
lessons: It is a grave spiritual tragedy to possess knowledge of the truth and yet refuse to act upon it when the moment of fulfillment arrives.
Faith is not merely an intellectual exercise but an act of the will that requires choosing truth over ego and self-interest.
Community members who sincerely heed the guidance available to them, even from unexpected sources, are often blessed with the gift of faith.
practical_advice: Do not allow familiarity with religious knowledge to breed complacency; regularly ask yourself whether your knowledge is translating into sincere action and belief.
When you encounter a moment of truth or an opportunity for positive change, act on it immediately, knowing that delay can allow ego and habit to close the door.
Pray regularly for God to purify your heart of envy and self-interest so that your knowledge and your actions remain aligned.

title: Ibn al-Hayyiban the Syrian Jew Who Foretold the Prophet to Banu Qurayza
summary: A pious Jewish scholar from Syria named Ibn al-Hayyiban settled among Banu Qurayza, performed miraculous prayers for rain, and on his deathbed revealed he had come to await the final prophet, warning the Jews not to oppose him yet they later disbelieved.
story: A Jew from Syria called Ibn al-Hayyiban came to us some years before the arrival of Islam and lived among us. And by God, I never knew a non-Muslim (one who does not perform the five daily prayers) better than him. Well, he was living there with us at a time of drought, so we asked him, 'Please, Ibn al-Hayyiban, pray for rain for us!' But he replied, 'No, by God, I'll not do that until you pay me.' 'How much?' we asked. 'A qa'a weight of dates or two mudd weights of barley.' 'We'll pay it,' we agreed. He then went out with us to the top of our parched land and prayed for rain for us. And by God, he no sooner got up but clouds came and it rained! Yet others had done that once, twice, and thrice! Later his death came while he lived with us. When he knew he was dying, he said, 'O Jews, what do you think it was that brought me from a land of wine and bread to one of poverty and hunger?' 'You know that best,' we replied. He then said, 'I only came to this country because I expected the arrival of a prophet whose time is near. This land is where he will take refuge. And I had hoped that he would be sent so that I could follow him. His time is near for you. But don't hasten to him, O Jews, for he is sent to shed the blood and capture the women and children of those who oppose him. And that (hurrying to him) will not prevent him treating you thus.' And when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was sent, he besieged Banu Qurayza. Those grown men who had been young boys at the earlier occasion addressed their people, saying, 'O Banu Qurayza, he is, by God, the prophet foretold to you by Ibn al-Hayyiban.' They replied, 'He is not.' The men insisted, saying, 'Yes he is. He has just his description.' So they went down and accepted Islam, and by doing so saved their blood, their property, and their people.
lessons: A life of sincere seeking, even at great personal sacrifice, is honored by God; Ibn al-Hayyiban left comfort and security to be near the fulfillment of divine promise.
Warnings given from a place of genuine care and knowledge are a gift; ignoring them due to pride or group loyalty carries serious consequences.
Those who have the courage to act on recognized truth, even when their community does not, protect themselves and may find salvation when others do not.
practical_advice: Be willing to make meaningful sacrifices in pursuit of what you believe is true and right, even when it is costly or uncomfortable.
Take seriously the sincere guidance of those who are clearly motivated by knowledge and goodness, even if their message is difficult to hear.
When facing a moment where your community is moving in a direction you believe is wrong, find the courage to choose truth over belonging, knowing that integrity carries its own reward.

title: Zayd b. Sa'ya Studies the Prophet's Wisdom and Patience
summary: The Jewish scholar Zayd b. Sa'ya recounts that he recognized all the marks of prophethood in Muhammad except two he had not yet tested: that his patient wisdom preceded his ignorance and that the more ignorance was directed at him the more his wisdom increased, and so he sought closeness to the Prophet to observe these qualities.
story: 'Abd Allah b. Sallam stated that God having wished good guidance for Zayd b. Sa'ya, Zayd said, 'All marks of the prophethood were there as expected for me to recognize on the face of Muhammad (SAAS) when I looked at him, except that there were two I had not explored in him: that his patient wisdom preceded his ignorance, and that the more ignorance was directed at him, the more his patient wisdom increased.' He stated further, 'I used to be nice to him so that I could mix with him and get to know his wisdom and his foolishness.' He then told a story of his having made an advance payment to the Prophet (SAAS) for profit.
lessons: True wisdom is not simply the absence of ignorance but the capacity to respond to provocation and difficulty with even greater patience and clarity.
Genuine seekers of truth are willing to test and verify what they believe rather than accepting or rejecting it on hearsay or first impression.
The Prophet's character itself was a form of divine proof; his consistent patience under pressure was recognized as a mark of authentic prophethood even by scholars from other traditions.
practical_advice: Cultivate the practice of responding to rudeness, criticism, or ignorance with increased calm and patience rather than matching the energy directed at you.
When investigating something you believe may be true, approach it with sincere curiosity and careful observation rather than defensiveness or preconceived conclusions.
Identify someone in your life who models wisdom and patience under pressure, and make a point of spending time with them to learn from their example.

title: Zayd ibn Sa'ya Embraces Islam After Testing the Prophet's Patience
summary: A Jewish scholar named Zayd ibn Sa'ya, who had studied the signs of prophethood, deliberately provoked the Prophet by rudely demanding repayment of a loan in front of his companions, only to be met with calm gentleness and generosity, which led to his conversion to Islam.
story: He stated further, 'I used to be nice to him so that I could mix with him and get to know his wisdom and his foolishness.' "He then told a story of his having made an advance payment to the Prophet (SAAS) for profit. He went on, 'And when the time for the loan was due, I went to him and grabbed him by the closure of his cloak and gown, he being then at a funeral with some of his Companions. I also glared at him in an ugly fashion and said, "O Muhammad, aren't you going to pay me my due? By God, I never knew you of the tribe of 'Abd al-Munalib to be late payers!" "'Umar stared over at me, his eyes rolled like revolving celestial bodies. Then he said, "You enemy of God! Do you dare say to the Messenger of God (SAAS) what I am hearing and do what I am seeing? By Him who sent him with the truth, if I were not wary of his blaming me, I would cut off your head with my sword!" "'Meanwhile, the Messenger of God (SAAS) was looking at 'Umar, smiling quietly with gentleness. Then he said, "'Umar, both he and I were in need of something other than that from you; you should have told me to pay up properly, and him to behave better. Take him away, 'Umar, and pay him his due. And give him 20 sa's weight of dates as a bonus!"' "And so Zayd b. Sa'ya, may God be pleased with him, became a Muslim. He was present with the Messenger of God at all the battles, and he died in the year of Tabuk, God bless him."
lessons: True patience and wisdom are tested not in calm moments but in moments of public humiliation and provocation — the Prophet's gentle smile in such a moment reveals the highest character.
Responding to rudeness with generosity and fairness, rather than defensiveness, can be the most powerful form of da'wah (invitation to truth).
Those who sincerely seek truth, even through unusual or confrontational means, will ultimately find it — Zayd's entire test was a spiritual search in disguise.
practical_advice: When someone is rude or demanding toward you in public, resist the urge to retaliate or over-defend yourself; a calm, measured response preserves your dignity and may even change the other person.
If you are in a position of authority or have supporters who react angrily on your behalf, gently redirect their energy toward constructive action rather than allowing escalation.
Practice responding to criticism or unfair treatment by first asking yourself: 'What does this person actually need?' — often meeting the real need dissolves the conflict entirely.

title: Salman al-Farisi's Lifelong Journey Seeking Truth — Part 1: Leaving Home and the First Christian Master
summary: Salman al-Farisi, a Persian Zoroastrian deeply loved by his father, stumbles upon a Christian church and is spiritually transformed, leading him to secretly seek out the origins of Christianity in Syria despite his father's imprisonment of him.
story: Ibn Ishaq, God be pleased with him, recounted how Salman al-Farisi, "the Persian", God bless him, became a Muslim. He stated that 'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada the Ansari, the "helper", related to him, from Mahmud b. Labid, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas, who said that Salman al-Farisi told him as follows, "I was a Persian from Isfahan and dwelt in a village called Jayy. My father was the headman of the village and I was the one he most loved of all creatures; his affection for me was so strong that he kept me closeted in his house like a slave-girl. "I took pains to be a good Magian, and became the keeper of the fire, the one who lights it and never for a moment lets it go out. "My father owned a large farm and one day, when he was busy constructing a building, he said to me, 'Son, I'm too busy to leave here today for the farm, so you go and look to it.' And he told me certain things he wanted. Then he told me, 'Don't be away from me too long. If you are I'll be more concerned about you than about my farm or work, and you'll keep me from everything else.' "So I left for the farm as he had told me and on my way I passed by a church for Christians and heard their voices as they prayed there. I didn't know anything about other people, because my father had kept me shut up at home. So when I heard their voices I went in to them to find out what they were doing. When I saw them I was much impressed with their prayers and wanted to join them. I said to myself, 'This, by God, is better than the religion we have.' "So I didn't leave them until sunset and neglected to go to my father's farm. "I asked them, 'Where is the origin of this religion?' 'In Syria,' they replied. "I then went back to my father who had sent people to look for me and had been distracted from all his work. When I arrived he asked, 'Well, son, where were you? Didn't I ask you to do something?' 'O Father,' I replied, 'I went by some people praying in a church of theirs and I was much impressed by what I saw of their religion. So I stayed with them till sunset.' "'Son, there's no good in that religion. Your religion and that of your forefathers is better than it.' "'But no,' I replied, 'I swear by God theirs is better than ours.' "My father was afraid for me and set chains on my legs and imprisoned me in his house. So I sent a message to the Christians saying, 'If a caravan comes to you from Syria send word to me about them.' "A caravan did arrive from Syria and the Christians came and told me about them. I said to them, 'When they finish their business and are about to return home, then tell me.' "When they were ready to go home they let me know and I threw the shackles off my legs and left with them. When we arrived in Syria, I asked, 'Who is the most learned man in this religion?' 'The bishop in the church,' they replied. "So I went to him and said, 'I really like this religion, and I want to join you and to serve you in your church, to learn from you and to pray with you.' "'Come on in,' he invited me. "So I joined him. But he was an evil man, demanding alms from his people and whatever they collected he would store away for himself and not give to the poor. Eventually he had gathered seven jars of gold and silver. "I hated him mightily for what I saw him doing. "Then he died, and the Christians assembled to bury him. I told them, 'This was an evil man. He demanded alms from you but when you brought it to him he stored it away for himself and gave none of it to the poor.' "They asked me, 'How do you know that?' and I replied that I could take them to his treasure. 'Do so!' they told me, so I showed them where it was and they brought out seven jars filled with gold and silver. When they saw it they said, 'We'll never give him burial!' And so they crucified and stoned him."
lessons: A sincere heart that yearns for truth will not be satisfied with inherited religion simply because it is familiar — Salman's willingness to question his upbringing was a sign of spiritual courage.
Even trusted religious leaders can be corrupt; encountering hypocrisy in a guide does not invalidate the truth one is seeking, and one must separate the message from the messenger.
The pursuit of spiritual truth may require painful sacrifices, including separation from family and physical hardship, but the soul compelled by truth cannot rest until it finds it.
practical_advice: If you feel spiritually unsatisfied with your current path, give yourself permission to ask honest questions — seeking truth is not disloyalty, it is a sign of a living conscience.

When you discover that a person in authority has betrayed trust, address the wrongdoing honestly as Salman did, but do not let their failure cause you to abandon your search for what is genuinely good.

When family or community pressure discourages your spiritual growth, find small, legitimate ways to continue seeking — Salman sent a message to the Christians even while chained, showing that determination finds a way.

title: Salman al-Farisi's Journey Through Four Righteous Christian Scholars
summary: After exposing a corrupt bishop, Salman is taken under the wing of a succession of four deeply pious Christian scholars, each of whom dies and recommends him to the next, until the last one describes the coming prophet and sends Salman toward Arabia.
story: "Then they brought another man to take his place. And," said Salman, "I never saw a man who did not attend the five prayers who was better than he, nor more ascetic, nor more enthusiastic about the afterlife, nor more devoted both day and night. I loved him as I had never loved before. "So I stayed with him for a time and then his death approached. I asked him, 'Since I've been with you I've loved you as never before. But now what you know to be God's decree is approaching, so to whom do you entrust me? And what are your orders to me?' "'My son,' he replied, 'I don't know anyone who has done as I have. People have either died or changed or abandoned most of their prior practices, except for one man in Mawsil; he practises as I did. Join him.' "So when he died and was buried, I joined the leader of al-Mawsil. I told him, 'So-and-so enjoined me at his death to come to you; he told me you are as he was.' 'Stay with me,' I was invited. "I did so and found him the best of men, like his friend. But soon he was to die. And when death was near for him, I told him, 'So-and-so entrusted me to you and told me to join you. Now God's decree, as you know, is near for you, so whom do you recommend to me, and what are your orders to me?' 'My son,' he replied, 'I don't know anyone else who is as we were, except one man at Nasibin, so join him.' "When he did die and was buried, I joined the bishop at Nasibin and told him my story and what my two masters had ordered me. 'Stay with me,' he said. And so I did so, finding him extremely good, as his two colleagues had been. But, I swear by God, death soon approached him too, so I asked him, 'So-and-so recommended one man to me, who recommended me to another, who recommended me to you. Whom do you recommend for me, and what are your orders?' "'My son,' he said, 'I don't know anyone who is still as we were whom I can recommend, except for one man at 'Amariyya in Byzantium. He is as we were. If you wish, go to him.' "When he died and was buried, I joined the bishop of 'Amariyya, and related to him my story. He said, 'Stay with me.' So I did stay there, and he was the best of men, as well-directed and fine in his behaviour as his predecessors had been. "I worked hard and earned well and eventually I had some cows and a small goat. But then God's decree came for him too and I told him, 'So-and-so recommended someone to me, who recommended me to another, he to another, then he to another and so on until you were recommended me. To whom do you recommend me and what are your orders?' "'My son,' he replied, 'I don't know anyone who is as we were to whom to send you. But the time is near for a prophet being sent with Abraham's religion who will come forth in the land of the Arabs. He will move to refuge to somewhere with date-palms growing between two scorched, rocky plains. He has signs that are unmistakable. He eats things given to him as gifts but not alms. And between his shoulders there is the seal of prophethood. If you can go there, do so.' "Then he died and was buried, and I stayed in 'Amariyya for the length of time God wished."
lessons: Grief at the loss of a beloved spiritual mentor is natural, but true seekers do not stop — they carry the guidance forward and honor their teachers by continuing the journey.
God places righteous guides along the path of sincere seekers; each relationship, however brief, is a gift that moves the soul closer to its destination.
The description of the coming prophet passed from teacher to teacher like a torch — this shows that divine truth is preserved across generations for those who sincerely seek it.
practical_advice: When you lose a mentor, teacher, or spiritual guide, ask yourself: who did they trust, and what guidance did they leave me? The answer often points to your next step.
Do not measure spiritual progress by how quickly you arrive — Salman spent decades moving from teacher to teacher, and each stage was necessary for his journey.
If you feel lost after a significant loss or life transition, reflect on the last trustworthy guidance you received and take one step in that direction, even if the full path is not yet clear.

title: Salman al-Farisi Sold into Slavery and Arrives in Medina
summary: On his way to Arabia to find the promised prophet, Salman is betrayed by merchants and sold into slavery, yet arrives in Medina and immediately recognizes it as the land his last Christian master had described.
story: "Eventually a group of merchants of the Kalb tribe passed by and I asked them to take me to the land of the Arabs and offered them my cows and goat. They agreed, so I gave them to them and they took me with them as far as Wadi al-Qura. There they did me evil by selling me as a slave to a Jew. There with him I saw palm trees and hoped that might be the land my master had described. "While I was there with him he received a visit from a cousin of his of Banu Qurayza from Medina. He sold me to him, who bore me off to Medina. And I swear by God, as soon as I saw it I recognized it as my friend had described it to me. And there it was I stayed. "Then the mission came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who remained in Mecca without me hearing any report of him, busy as I was in the toil of slavery. But then he took refuge in Medina. "One day I was doing some work for my master at the top of a palm tree, with him sitting there underneath, when a cousin of his came and stopped there. 'Hey there, so-and-so,' the cousin said, 'God damn those Ansar of Banu Qayla! They are assembled at Quba in support of a man come from Mecca whom they claim to be a prophet.'" Salman continued, "When I heard this I began to tremble, so much I thought I would fall down on top of"
lessons: Betrayal and injustice on the path to truth do not mean God has abandoned the seeker — Salman was enslaved and yet arrived exactly where he needed to be.
When we are trapped in difficult circumstances beyond our control, God may be using them to position us precisely where our destiny awaits.
Recognition of truth, when it finally arrives, comes as a deep inner certainty — Salman recognized Medina instantly, confirming that sincere preparation opens the heart to recognize divine signs.
practical_advice: When you experience betrayal or exploitation while trying to do something good, resist the conclusion that your efforts were wasted — trust that God can bring good from even the most unjust circumstances.
During seasons of hardship and constraint, stay alert to signs of the destination you are seeking rather than becoming consumed by the difficulty of the present moment.

If you are in a situation that feels like a dead end, remember that Salman's slavery was the very vehicle that delivered him to Medina — sometimes the path to our purpose runs through our greatest trials.

title: Salman al-Farisi Hears of the Prophet's Arrival in Quba
summary: While working as a slave in Medina, Salman al-Farisi overhears news that a prophet from Mecca had arrived at Quba, causing him to tremble with excitement and nearly fall from a palm tree.
story: One day I was doing some work for my master at the top of a palm tree, with him sitting there underneath, when a cousin of his came and stopped there. 'Hey there, so-and-so,' the cousin said, 'God damn those ansar of Banii Qayla! They are assembled at Qbal in support of a man come from Mecca whom they claim to be a prophet.' When I heard this I began to tremble, so much I thought I would fall down on top of my master! So I climbed down the tree and began questioning his cousin. This angered my master and he slapped me hard. Then he asked, 'What's this to do with you? Get back to your work!' 'It's nothing,' I replied, 'I just wanted to clarify what he said.'
lessons: Truth and divine guidance can reach a sincere seeker even through the most constrained circumstances.
The heart that is spiritually alive will tremble at the nearness of divine guidance, no matter how long the wait has been.
Patience through hardship is never wasted when one holds steadfast to a noble purpose.
practical_advice: When you feel a sudden stirring of hope after a long period of waiting, do not dismiss it — take a measured step toward it.
Even when others mock or punish your search for truth, stay focused on your goal without retaliating or losing composure.
Use difficult circumstances as training grounds for resilience; the discipline you build in hardship prepares you for the moment your breakthrough arrives.

title: Salman Tests the Prophet with Charity and a Gift
summary: Salman al-Farisi devised two tests to verify the Prophet's authenticity: offering food as charity (which the Prophet declined to eat) and then as a gift (which the Prophet accepted), matching descriptions given by Christian monks.
story: I had put aside some food, so that evening I took it to the Messenger of God (SAAS) he being at Qiba', and went in to see him, saying, 'I heard you were a good man and that you have with you some companions who are strangers and needy. This is some food I have to give as alms, and I consider you have more right to it than anyone else.' I then handed it to him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to his companions, 'Eat it then.' He himself took none. 'This is one test passed,' I told myself. Then I left him and saved up some more, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was moving on towards Medina. I then went to him again and said, 'I noticed you didn't eat from the food I gave as alms. This is a present I wish to honour you with.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) did eat from this and told his companions to do the same, which they did. And again I said to myself: 'That makes it two!'
lessons: Careful, thoughtful verification is a sign of sincere seeking, not of disrespect or doubt.
True prophets carry signs that distinguish them, and a patient seeker will find them.
Hope sustained through a long journey of searching can be confirmed in a single, quiet moment of recognition.
practical_advice: When seeking clarity about an important life decision, take measured steps to verify your path rather than acting on impulse.
Trust the process of gradual confirmation; not every answer comes at once, and that is by design.
Keep your intentions pure when approaching a goal — Salman's sincerity in each step brought him closer to his answer.

title: Salman Recognizes the Seal of Prophethood
summary: Salman al-Farisi confirmed the Prophet's identity by observing the seal between his shoulders, fulfilling the final sign described by a Christian monk, and wept and prostrated in recognition.
story: I then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he was at Baqi' al-Gharqad, the cemetery, where he had followed the funeral procession of one of his companions. He was wearing two cloaks, sitting there among his companions. I greeted him, then came round behind him to look at his back to find out if I could see the mark my former master had described to me. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw me go behind him he knew I was trying to establish something of what had been described to me. So he threw off his cloak from his back and I looked at the sign and recognized it. I then prostrated myself before him, kissing him and weeping. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told me, 'Turn around!' So I stood up in front of him and told him my story as I have to you, O Ibn 'Abbas. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then wanted his companions to hear my story.
lessons: A lifelong search for truth, however difficult the path, can culminate in a moment of overwhelming clarity and gratitude.
Divine signs are placed in the world for those sincere enough to seek them with patience and perseverance.
Tears of joy and recognition are among the most beautiful expressions of a heart that has finally come home.
practical_advice: Reflect on moments in your own life when persistent searching finally led to clarity — let those memories strengthen your trust in the process.
Do not be embarrassed by emotional responses to profound truths; allow yourself to feel the full weight of meaningful moments.
Share your journey with others when appropriate; Salman's story inspired those around the Prophet, just as honest testimony can inspire those around you.

title: The Community Frees Salman from Slavery
summary: The Prophet and his companions collectively helped Salman al-Farisi fulfill his contract for freedom by planting palm trees, and a miraculous gold nugget paid off his remaining debt, setting him free.
story: Eventually the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to me, 'Salman, write!' So I wrote out for my master an agreement that I would plant 300 palm trees watered by a well and pay 40 ounces (of gold). The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said to his companions, 'Help your brother.' So they helped me, one man with 30 seedlings, another with 20, another with 15, and another with 10, each one participating as he could, until I had all the 300 together. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, 'Now Salman, go and dig the holes for them. When you have finished come to me so that I can plant them with my own hands.' So I dug and my companions helped me and when we had finished I went and told him. He then went out with me to them and we began handing him the seedlings which the Messenger of God (SAAS) himself planted. When we had finished, and this I swear by Him in whose hands Salman is, not one of those plants died. I had now settled up for the palm trees but was still in debt for the money. Now the Messenger of God (SAAS) was given a quantity of gold the size of a chicken's egg from a mine. He said, 'Hasn't the Persian done accounts?' I then said a prayer for him. Next, he said, 'Take this and pay off what you owe, Salman.' I asked him, 'Where will this stand relative to what I owe, O Messenger of God?' He said, 'Take it, for God will pay your debt with it.' So I took it and weighed it for them and, I swear by Him in whose hand Salman is, it was 40 ounces! So I gave my debt in full. And Salman was set free.
lessons: A community that rallies around one of its members in need demonstrates the highest expression of brotherhood and solidarity.
When human efforts reach their limit, divine provision can arrive in ways that exceed all expectation.
Freedom — spiritual, physical, and social — is worth every sacrifice made along the way to attain it.
practical_advice: When you are overwhelmed by a burden you cannot bear alone, allow others to help you; accepting community support is not weakness but wisdom.
Trust that when you have done everything within your power, what remains can be provided by sources beyond your own.
Celebrate the milestones of freedom and relief in your life as Salman did — with gratitude, presence, and commitment to giving back.

title: Salman Encounters the Last Holy Man and Is Told of the Coming Prophet
summary: On his long spiritual journey through Syria, Salman al-Farisi is directed by a dying monk to seek a man who heals the sick with prayer, who in turn tells Salman that the time of the final prophet has come and directs him toward the sanctuary.
story: The bishop of 'Amuriyya told him, 'Go this way and that in Syria, and there you will find a man who dwells between two thickets. Every year he emerges from one of these and then is accosted by people who are sick. Every one of these for whom he says a prayer is cured. So ask him about this religion you're seeking. And he will tell you about it.' Salman said, 'So I left and travelled to the place he had described and I found people gathered there with their sick waiting until the night the man would move out from one of the thickets towards the other. (When he came out) the people there overwhelmed him and every sick person for whom he prayed was cured. They beat me to him and I couldn't get near him till he entered the thicket he was heading for, when I touched his shoulder. Who is that? he asked and turned towards me. I replied, God have mercy on you! Do tell me about the hanifiyya, Abraham's religion! You're asking me about something people don't enquire about nowadays. The time is near for the coming of a prophet who will be sent down with that religion; he will be one of the people of the sanctuary. Go to him and he will take you to it. He then went into the thicket. At this the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented: If you have told me the truth, O Salman, you have met Jesus son of Mary!'
lessons: Divine guidance reaches the sincere seeker through unexpected and extraordinary means.
A soul that persists across years and continents in search of truth will not be left without direction.
The testimony of righteous people across different times and traditions can point toward a single, unified truth.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually lost, seek out people of wisdom and integrity rather than giving up the search entirely.
Do not be discouraged if the answer you seek comes slowly or through many intermediaries; each step of the journey is purposeful.
Reflect on how your own spiritual path has been shaped by people who pointed you in the right direction at the right time, and express gratitude for them.

title: Salman's Early Encounter with Hidden Christian Worshippers in Persia
summary: As a young man in Persia, Salman al-Farisi secretly visits a small group of sincere Christian worshippers hidden in a cave, who open his eyes to monotheism and the reality of the afterlife, setting him on his lifelong spiritual quest.
story: His friend also used to call upon some Christian worshippers who lived in a cave. Salman asked his friend to take him to visit them, but the reply was, 'You're just a young man, and I'm afraid that you will betray them and that my father will kill them.' And so he made him promise that he would not do anything his friend would not like. So he accompanied his friend and they (the Christians) were six or seven in number. And they looked as if, from all their praying, their souls had left them; they fasted by day and stayed up (praying) at night, and ate trees or whatever they found. He related of them that they believed in all the previous messengers from God and that Jesus was a worshipper of God, His messenger, and the son of His slave-girl and that God aided him with miracles. They told him, 'Boy, you have a Lord; and will have an afterlife. Awaiting you there is to be a heaven and a hell. And those people who worship fires are unbelievers and in error. God is not pleased with what they do, and they are not in his religion.' Thereafter Salman went to them often with his friend and eventually joined them totally. But then the ruler of that land expelled them, and he was the father of the young man who had visited them with Salman. The ruler imprisoned his son at home. Salman presented their religion to his older brother but the latter said, 'I'm personally just busy with making my living.'
lessons: The first encounter with sincere faith can permanently alter the direction of a life, even for a young person.
Those who dedicate themselves wholly to worship and truth leave a lasting impression on those who witness their devotion.
Not everyone around us will be ready to hear or accept truth — that is their journey, not a reflection of the truth's validity.
practical_advice: If you feel spiritually awakened by witnessing the sincerity of others, take that feeling seriously and let it guide your next step.
Do not wait for family or friends to join you on your path toward truth; some journeys must begin alone.
When someone you care about dismisses what you have found meaningful, respond with compassion rather than judgment, and trust that each person's journey unfolds in its own time.

title: Salman al-Farisi's Journey to Islam: The Final Stages
summary: Salman al-Farisi continues his remarkable spiritual journey, following Christian monks across lands, receiving a prophecy about the coming Prophet Muhammad, being sold into slavery, and finally meeting the Prophet and embracing Islam.
story: there is to be a heaven and a hell. And those people who worship fires are unbelievers and in error. God is not pleased with what they do, and they are not in his religion." Thereafter Salman went to them often with his friend and eventually joined them totally. But then the ruler of that land expelled them, and he was the father of the young man who had visited them with Salman. The ruler imprisoned his son at home. Salman presented their religion to his older brother but the latter said, "I'm personally just busy with making my living." So Salman went away with them, and they entered the church at al-Mawsil, whose people made them welcome. "Then", continued Salman, "they wanted to leave me there with those people, but I refused to do anything but accompany them. So then they went further until they came to a mountain valley, and the monks of that region hurried down to greet them and to be with them. The monks asked them how it had been away, and also enquired about me, and they praised me highly." Then there arrived a man much respected among them who addressed them and gave all due praise to God. He made mention of the messengers and what assistance they were given. He also talked of Jesus son of Mary and how he was the slave of God and His Messenger. He enjoined them to be good and forbade them evil. Then when his former companions wished to go further on, Salman kept with this man and followed him. He would fast by day and stay up at night (praying) from one Sunday to the next. He would go out before them and give them sermons, enjoining good and forbidding evil. This went on for a long period and then he wished to visit Jerusalem, and Salman accompanied him there. Salman went on, "As he walked he would turn towards me, draw near, preach to me, and tell me that I had a Lord, that there were heaven, hell, and an accounting before me, reminding me of approximately what he would preach to the congregation on Sundays. One time he told me, 'O Salman, God will send a messenger whose name is Ahmad. He will come up out of Tihama; he will eat a gift, but not alms. And between his shoulders there will be a seal of prophethood. This is the time now approaching when he will appear. I am an old man, and I don't think I will live until his coming. But if you do, then believe in him and follow him.' 'But what', I asked, 'if he tells me to abandon your faith and practices?' He replied, 'No matter what he orders you, he brings the truth with him, and the wish of the Most Merciful One is in what he says.'" Salman then related their arrival in Jerusalem and that his companion worshipped at various places there. Before he went to sleep, the priest told him to wake him up when the shadow arrived at a certain place. But Salman let him rest a little longer than he had requested and when he awoke he first praised God then reproached Salman for not having done as he requested. They then left Jerusalem and (Salman's account continues), "A cripple begged, 'O worshipper of God, I asked of you when you arrived, but you gave me nothing. I am now asking you again.' The priest looked but found no one else. So he took him by the hand and said, 'Stand up, in the name of God.' The man did so, and there was nothing wrong with him, no diseases at all; he was moving as if with the slightest limp. The priest then told me, 'O, worshipper of God, load up my baggage for me so I can go off to my own people and tell them the good tidings.' I got busy doing this, but when I wanted to go over to him, I could not find him. I did not know where he had gone. And whenever I asked anyone about him, they told me, 'He's out ahead of you.' "Eventually I was met by a caravan of Arabs of Banu Kalb and I made enquiry of them. When they heard my accent one man made his camel kneel and mounted me up behind him, and they brought me to their land. "There they sold me, and a Christian woman bought me and put me to work in a garden she had. "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived." Salman related after this how he went to see him (the Prophet) and gave him alms, then a gift to test what his companion had told him, and asked to see the seal. When he did see it he believed at once and informed the Messenger of God (SAAS) what he had been told. The account then tells of the Messenger of God (SAAS), having summoned Abu Bakr, "the trusting", who bought Salman from the woman and then set him free. Salman went on: "Later I asked the Messenger of God one day about the Christians' religion. He replied, 'There's no good in them.' I remembered all those I had accompanied and in particular that good man who had been in Jerusalem with me, and so his words had great impact on me. Then God sent down to the Messenger of God (SAAS) the statement: 'Certainly you will find that the people the most violent in their enmity to those who believe are the Jews along with polytheists. And you will find that the closest of people in friendship to those who believe are those who say, "We are Christians." That is because there are priests and monks among them and because they do not behave with conceit' (surat al-Ma'ida, V, v.82). "The Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned me, and I was afraid when I came. When I sat before him he recited, 'In the name of God the most compassionate, the most merciful. "That is because there are priests and monks among them, and because they do not behave with conceit." Then he said, 'O Salman, those whom you accompanied, and your friend as well, were not Christians; they were Muslims.' I replied, 'By Him who sent you with the truth, he certainly did order me to follow you.' And when I said, 'But what if he tells me to leave your religion and your practices?' he did say, 'Yes; do leave that, for the truth, and what pleases God is whatever he orders you.'"
lessons: A sincere seeker of truth will be guided through many trials and unexpected paths — Salman's journey across countries and servitude ultimately led him to the Prophet, showing that genuine spiritual longing is never wasted.

Divine guidance can come through unexpected teachers and circumstances; the monk's prophecy about the coming Prophet and his instruction to follow him regardless demonstrates that truth transcends cultural and religious boundaries.

Freedom from spiritual enslavement matters more than freedom from physical bondage — Salman endured literal slavery with patience, knowing his true liberation lay in reaching the truth he had sought for so long.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually lost or uncertain about your path, reflect on the sincere effort you are making — the act of seeking truth itself is a form of worship and will not go unrewarded.

If you are in a difficult or constraining situation (like a job, relationship, or circumstance you cannot immediately escape), focus on what you can learn and grow from within it, as Salman did during his years of servitude.

When you finally reach a goal after a long struggle, take a moment to verify and confirm it with gratitude rather than rushing past it — Salman carefully tested the signs he had been told about before committing, combining trust with discernment.

title: The Prophecy of the Monk in Jerusalem: The Cripple Healed and the Guide Disappears
summary: During Salman al-Farisi's journey to Jerusalem with a righteous monk, the monk miraculously heals a crippled beggar, then mysteriously disappears, leaving Salman alone to continue his search for the promised Prophet.
story: They then left Jerusalem and (Salman's account continues), "A cripple begged, 'O worshipper of God, I asked of you when you arrived, but you gave me nothing. I am now asking you again.' The priest looked but found no one else. So he took him by the hand and said, 'Stand up, in the name of God.' The man did so, and there was nothing wrong with him, no diseases at all; he was moving as if with the slightest limp. The priest then told me, 'O, worshipper of God, load up my baggage for me so I can go off to my own people and tell them the good tidings.' I got busy doing this, but when I wanted to go over to him, I could not find him. I did not know where he had gone. And whenever I asked anyone about him, they told me, 'He's out ahead of you.' "Eventually I was met by a caravan of Arabs of Banu Kalb and I made enquiry of them. When they heard my accent one man made his camel kneel and mounted me up behind him, and they brought me to their land. "There they sold me, and a Christian woman bought me and put me to work in a garden she had."
lessons: Sometimes the guides and mentors God places in our lives are meant for a season, not a lifetime — when they depart, we must trust that the path forward will still be provided.

Miracles witnessed along a spiritual journey serve to strengthen faith for the harder trials ahead; the healing of the cripple was a sign to fortify Salman before the difficult years of slavery to come.

Being sold into slavery after witnessing a miracle may seem like a contradiction, but God's plan often involves descents before ascents — the darkest moments can be the last steps before arriving at one's destination.
practical_advice: When a mentor, teacher, or support system suddenly disappears from your life, resist the urge to despair — ask yourself what they taught you and how you can carry that forward independently.
In moments of confusion about where to turn next, look for small acts of human kindness (like the tribesman who gave Salman a ride) as signs that help is still present around you.

If you find yourself in a situation that feels like a setback after significant spiritual or personal progress, remind yourself that your journey is not over — reframe the constraint as a preparation for what comes next.

title: Sacir ibn Sawada Encounters Hashim ibn Abd Manaf at the Pilgrimage
summary: A Syrian trader visiting Mecca during pilgrimage season, having heard prophecies about the coming unlettered Prophet, mistakes Hashim ibn Abd Manaf for the awaited messenger due to his majestic presence and noble bearing.
story: Abu Nu'aym stated in al-Dala'il that Sulayman b. Ahmad related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Zakariyya' al-Ghalabi, quoting al-'Ala' b. al-Fadl b. 'Abd al-Malik b. Abi al-Sawiyya al-Minqari, as did 'Abbad b. Kassib, from his father, from Abi 'Atwaa al-Khuza'i, from Sa'ir b. Sawada al-'Amiri, who said, "I was in love with one of the finest ladies of the tribe. For her I rode every kind of mount, difficult and submissive alike, leaving no place untried in my hopes of business and profit. I left Syria with goods and produce I wanted to sell to the crowds of pilgrims and Arabs and so entered Mecca one very dark night. I stayed until night was over and when I lifted my head I could see a pavilion over against the top of the mountain quilted out of heavy cloth from al-Ta'if. Animals were being slaughtered and others brought. People were eating and others encouraging the cooks to hurry up. There, too, was a man calling loudly from a hilltop, saying, 'O pilgrims, come on over and eat lunch!' There was also a little man over on a path calling out, 'O pilgrims, if you have eaten, pass on till supper.' I was amazed by what I saw and went over there seeking the group's leader. One man saw what I wanted and told me, 'Straight ahead!' "Then I saw an old man whose cheeks were very red and who seemed to have a star shining forth from his forehead. He had twisted around his head a black turban from beneath the folds of which his head-parting appeared, sesame seed in colour." (Some accounts give the wording as "sitting on a chair of black samsim wood with a saddle cushion beneath it.") "In his hand he lightly held a rod. All around him sheikhs were sitting, their heads held down, and not one of them was saying a word. Information had reached me in Syria that this was the time for the arrival of the ummi (unlettered) Prophet, so when I saw this man I thought it was he. So I said to him, 'Peace be upon you, O Messenger of God!' He replied in anger, 'Stop that! No! I wish I were he.' I asked, 'Who is that sheikh?' 'That's Abu Nadla, Hashim b. 'Abd Manaf.' So I turned away, saying, 'Now that is glory! Not like that of the house of Jafna!'" By this he meant the Arab kings of Syria of the Ghassan tribe, who were known as the Al (family of) Jafna. The office he was describing relative to Hashim was that of the rifada, namely the feeding of the pilgrims.
lessons: The nobility and generosity of the Prophet's ancestors, like Hashim who fed thousands of pilgrims, was itself a sign of the blessed lineage from which the final Prophet would emerge.
Sincere seekers of truth, even when they make mistakes in their search, are honoring a deep human instinct that God placed within them — the man's error did not invalidate his search.
True leadership is recognized by its service to others; Hashim's greatness was not in titles or wealth alone but in the act of feeding pilgrims and caring for those in need.
practical_advice: When you are searching for guidance or truth, do not be discouraged by false starts or cases of mistaken identity — each encounter along the way sharpens your discernment and brings you closer.
Observe how those in positions of authority and leadership treat ordinary people; genuine nobility expresses itself through generosity and service, not mere appearance or status.
If you feel drawn toward a higher purpose or calling but are uncertain where to find it, keep moving, keep asking, and keep engaging — the right door will eventually open as it did for this traveler.

title: Abd al-Muttalib's Vision of the Tree Reaching the Sky
summary: Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's grandfather and leader of Quraysh, is disturbed by a powerful vision of a great tree stretching from earth to sky, casting light brighter than the sun, and goes to a soothsayer to understand its meaning.
story: Abu Nu'aym said that 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. Ja'far related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Ahmad b. Abi Yahya, quoting Sa'id b. 'Uthman, quoting 'Ali b. Qutayba al-Khursani, quoting Khalid b. Ilyas, from Abu Bakr b. 'Abd Allah b. Abi al-Jahm, from his father from his grandfather, who said that he heard Abu Talib relate the following about 'Abd al-Muttalib. He said, "While I was sleeping in the hijr enclosure, I had visions that scared me really badly. I went to a Quraysh soothsayer and I was wearing a silk shawl and my long hair was brushing my shoulders. When she looked at me, she knew from my face that something was changed; at that time I was the leader of my tribe. She said, 'What is wrong with our master that he has come to us, his face so changed? Is he afraid of some fateful happening?' 'Indeed I am,' I replied. "Now she never first addressed anyone before they kissed her right hand. She would then place that hand on top of her head and the person would tell her what he wanted. I did not do that because I was the chief of my tribe. "So I then sat down and said, 'As I lay sleeping last night in the hijr I had a vision of a tree having grown up, its head touching the sky and its boughs touching both the east and the west. And I never saw any light shine more brightly, 70 times brighter than the sun's light. I saw the Arabs and the non-Arabs
lessons: Powerful dreams and visions have been a means by which God has communicated important truths throughout human history — taking one's inner life seriously is a mark of spiritual awareness.

Even the greatest leaders experience fear and confusion in the face of the unknown; Abd al-Muttalib's willingness to seek counsel despite being the chief of his tribe shows that humility in seeking guidance is a strength, not a weakness.

The imagery of a tree whose branches reach east and west and whose light surpasses the sun was a foretelling of the Prophet's universal message — signs of great events are often given before those events arrive, as a mercy to prepare hearts.
practical_advice: When you experience a troubling dream or a strong sense of foreboding, take it seriously by reflecting on it and, if needed, seeking counsel from a trusted and wise person rather than dismissing or suppressing it.
If you are in a leadership position or a role of responsibility and feel overwhelmed or confused, remember that seeking advice is not a sign of weakness — it is what wise leaders do.

In moments of fear about what the future holds, ground yourself in the awareness that signs and forewarnings are often given precisely so that we can prepare ourselves spiritually and emotionally for what is to come.

title: Abd al-Muttalib's Vision of the Tree and the Soothsayer's Prophecy
summary: Abd al-Muttalib recounts a vivid dream of a great tree touching the sky, prostrated to by Arabs and non-Arabs, and a soothsayer interprets it as a prophecy that a man from his offspring will rule east and west.
story: So I then sat down and said, 'As I lay sleeping last night in the hijr I had a vision of a tree having grown up, its head touching the sky and its boughs touching both the east and the west. And I never saw any light shine more brightly, 70 times brighter than the sun's light. I saw the Arabs and the non-Arabs alike prostrate before it, while it grew broader, taller, and more dazzling all the time, sometimes dimming but then gleaming again. I saw a group of men of Quraysh who had grasped its branches, while another group of them wanted to cut it down. When they came near it they were fended away by a young man more good and handsome than I had ever seen before. And he was breaking their backs and gouging their eyes. I raised my hand to have my share of the tree but the young man stopped me. 'But who is to have it, then?' I asked. He replied, 'It is for those who climbed it, and they were there before you.' At that I woke up in a fright. "I saw that the expression on the face of the soothsayer had now altered. She said, 'If your vision was true, then a man will emerge from your offspring who will rule both east and west, a man whom all will follow.'" After that he - 'Abd al-Muttalib, that is - said to Abu Talib: "Maybe you are to be that person." Abu Talib used to tell this anecdote after the birth of the Messenger of God, following his receiving his mission. Then he said, "As God knows best, the tree was Abu al-Qasim, the Trustworthy." Someone asked Abu Talib, "So don't you believe?" He replied, "Shame and disgrace!"
lessons: Divine signs and prophecies often come in forms that require reflection and interpretation, reminding us that truth unfolds according to God's plan, not our impatience.
Recognizing truth does not always translate immediately into acceptance; Abu Talib's reluctance shows how ego, social standing, and habit can delay what the heart already knows.
The Prophetic mission was foretold across time and cultures, affirming that God's guidance is universal and that no sincere seeker is left without signs.
practical_advice: When you receive clarity or insight about the right path, do not let pride or social pressure delay your response — act on what your heart confirms to be true.
Reflect on the signs in your life that point toward purpose and meaning; journaling or quiet contemplation can help you notice patterns of divine guidance you might otherwise overlook.
If you find yourself acknowledging truth yet holding back from commitment, ask yourself honestly what fear or attachment is keeping you there, and take one small step toward alignment.

title: Al-Abbas and Abu Sufyan in Yemen: The Rabbi's Testimony to the Prophet's Truth
summary: Al-Abbas and Abu Sufyan are trading in Yemen when they encounter a Jewish rabbi who, upon hearing about the Prophet Muhammad, becomes visibly shaken and declares the Prophet's truthfulness, while Abu Sufyan remains resistant.
story: Abn Nu'aym stated that Sulayman b. Ahmad related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Zakariyya' al-Ghalabi, quoting al-'Abbas b.
