Bakkar al-Dabbi, quoting Abu Bakr al-Hudhali, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas that al-'Abbas said, "I went off in a caravan to Yemen to trade. In that party was Abu Sufyan b. Harb. When we were in Yemen I used to prepare the food one day and then go off with Abu Sufyan and others. Then next day Abu Sufyan would prepare the food and do the same as I had. One day when I was to prepare it, he asked, 'Abu al-Fadl, how would you like to go to my house and send your food to me?' I agreed. "So I went with the group to his place and I sent off for the food. When everyone had finished eating, they left but he kept me there. He asked, 'Abu al-Fadl, are you aware that your brother's son claims to be the Messenger of God?' I replied, 'Which one of my brother's sons?' Abu Sufyan said, 'Would you evade me? Which of your brother's sons would it have to be saying that except one?' 'Nevertheless, which one?' I asked. 'He is Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah,' he replied. 'He's done that?' I enquired. 'He certainly has,' he insisted. "He then took out a letter to him from his son Hanzala b. Abu Sufyan which said, 'This is to tell you that Muhammad stood up in the open space and said, "I am the Messenger of God; I summon you to God, Almighty and Glorious is He!"' Al-'Abbas replied, 'O Abu Hanzala, I think he's telling the truth!' "'Easy does it, O Abu al-Fadl! By God, I don't like him saying that. (Yet) I don't fear any harm for us from his saying that, O son of 'Abd al-Muttalib. By God, Quraysh is always saying something or other is yours, and there's purpose behind each of them! Have you heard of that, Abu al-Fadl?' 'Yes, I have,' I replied. So he said, 'You're cursed with this, by God!' 'Maybe we're blessed with it,' I responded." Al-'Abbas went on: "It was only a few days thereafter that 'Abd Allah b. Hudhafa brought that information, he being a believer. That news spread in all the assemblies of Yemen. Abu Sufyan used to attend one assembly in Yemen where a rabbi would talk. And the Jew said to him, 'What's this news? I've heard that you have among you the uncle of the man who has been saying these things.' "Abu Sufyan replied, 'Yes, that's true. I am his uncle.' The Jew asked, 'His father's brother?' 'Yes,' Abu Sufyan told him. 'Well tell me about him,' he said. "'Don't ask me,' he responded. 'I am not at all pleased at him making that claim, and I don't want to find fault with him, though there are others better than him.'" "The Jew noticed that Abu Sufyan did not want to diminish his support nor to criticize him. 'He has no ill will towards the Jews, or the Torah,' the Jew commented." Al-'Abbas stated: "Having been invited by the rabbi, I went and sat in at one of their meetings the next day. Abu Sufyan b. Harb was there along with the rabbi. I said to the rabbi, 'I've been told that you asked my cousin about one of our men who claims to be the Messenger of God (SAAS) and that he told you he was his uncle. But he is not his uncle but his cousin. I'm his uncle, his father's brother.' "'You're his father's brother?' he asked and I stated I was. "He then approached Abu Sufyan and asked, 'Did he tell the truth?' 'Yes, he did,' he replied. "Then I said, 'Ask me some questions, and if I lie, he can refute me.' "He came over to me and said, 'I ask you now, has your brother's son sexual desires or immoral behaviour?' "'Certainly not, by the God of 'Abd al-Muttalib,' I replied, 'and he has never lied or cheated. Among Quraysh he is known as "the Trustworthy".' "'Does he write by his own hand?' he asked." Al-'Abbas thought it would be better for him if he did write by his own hand, and he wanted to say he did. But then he remembered Abu Sufyan's being there and how he would call him a liar and refute him. So al-'Abbas replied, "No, he doesn't write." He continued, "The rabbi jumped up, and his loose robe fell down as he exclaimed, 'Jews will be slaughtered, Jews killed!'" Al-'Abbas went on, "And when we returned home, Abu Sufyan said, 'O Abu al-Fadl, the Jews are terrified of your nephew.' I asked him, 'Well, having seen what you have, do you believe in him? If it's true, you could be there early; but if it's not, you'd only be among others like you.' "He replied: 'I'll not believe in him till I see horses high up on Mt. Kada'.' "'What do you mean?' I asked. 'Just a phrase that came to mind,' he replied. 'Except that I know that God won't allow horsemen to climb up on Kada'.'" Al-'Abbas said, "But when the Messenger of God (SAAS) conquered Mecca and we looked up at the horsemen high on Kada', I said, 'Well Abu Sufyan, remember what you said?' "'Yes,' he replied, 'I certainly do. And thanks be to God for guiding me to Islam.'"
lessons: Truth has a way of vindicating itself across time; Abu Sufyan's eventual acceptance at the Conquest of Makkah shows that no heart is permanently closed to guidance when God's signs become undeniable.
Intellectual honesty, even when it is uncomfortable, is a form of moral courage; al-Abbas's careful truthfulness with the rabbi, even under pressure, models the integrity that Islam demands.

External witnesses and cross-cultural recognition of the Prophet's character demonstrate that righteousness and trustworthiness are universally recognizable qualities that transcend tribal and religious boundaries.
practical_advice: When you sense the truth of something but social pressure or personal discomfort makes you hesitate, pause and ask yourself: what will I wish I had done when this moment has passed?
Pay attention to how people of integrity and knowledge — even those outside your own community — respond to someone's character; their recognition can be a powerful confirmation of truth.

If you have been resistant to acknowledging something you know in your heart to be right, consider what condition or 'proof' you have been waiting for, and whether that condition is genuine or simply a delay tactic your ego has constructed.

title: The Vision of 'Amr b. Murra al-Juhani and His Embrace of Islam
summary: 'Amr b. Murra al-Juhani sees a prophetic dream of shining light and hears voices proclaiming the coming of the Seal of the Prophets during a pre-Islamic pilgrimage, and upon meeting the Prophet confirms his faith and is sent to call his people to Islam.
story: Al-Tabarsi stated that 'Ali b. Ibrahim al-Khuza'i al-Ahwazi related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Dawud b. Dalhath b. Isma'il b. 'Abd Allah b. Sharih b. Yasir b. Suwayd, the Companion of the Messenger of God (SAAS), quoting his father, from his father Dalhath, from his father Isma'il, that his father 'Abd Allah related to him from his father, that his father Yasir b. Suwayd related to him from 'Amr b. Murra al-Juhani, who said, "I went on a pilgrimage before the coming of Islam. In my dream, when I was in Mecca, I saw a shining light emerging from the ka'ba, and going as far as the mountains of Yathrih and the fertile slopes of Yanbu'. I heard a voice coming from amidst the light saying, 'The shadows are dispersed, the light shines bright, and the Seal of the Prophets has been sent.' "Then another light shone, so that I could see to the castles of al-Hira and to Abyad al-Mada'in, Chosroe's palace. I heard a voice from that light calling, 'Islam has appeared! The idols are destroyed! And all is made whole!' "I awoke with a start and called out to my people, 'By God, something is certainly going to happen in this tribe of Quraysh!' And I told them what I had seen. "When we reached home, the news came that a man called Ahmad had received a mission. So I went to him and told him what I had seen. He then said to me, 'O 'Amr b. Murra, I am the Prophet sent to all mankind; I summon them to Islam, order them to shed blood and to bring reconciliation, to pray to God and reject idols, to make the pilgrimage to the ka'ba, to fast the month of Ramadan, one month out of twelve. For those who respond, there is paradise; for those who rebel, the fire. So have faith, O 'Amr, and God will protect you from the terror of hell!' "I said, 'I give testimony that there is no God but God, and that you are the Messenger of God. I have faith in your message as to what is permitted and prohibited, even though that offends many peoples.' I then recited to him verses that I had spoken when I heard of him. We had an idol whom my father worshipped, and I went and destroyed it. Then I joined the Prophet (SAAS), speaking the verses, 'I gave witness that God is truth, and that I am the first to abandon stone worship. I busied myself taking flight to you, crossing wild deserts and sand wastes. To accompany the best of men in spirit and line, the messenger of mankind's Lord above the stars.' "The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'Welcome to you indeed, O 'Amr b. Murra.' And I replied, 'O Messenger of God, send me to my people so that God may favour them through me as He did me through you.' "So he sent me to them, saying, 'Behave...'"
lessons: Divine guidance can come through dreams and inner experiences that precede our conscious understanding; being open and attentive to such experiences, and then acting on them with courage, is itself a form of faith.

Breaking with inherited practices of falsehood — even ones as personal as an idol worshipped by one's own father — is one of the most courageous acts a person can take, and is often the first real step toward spiritual liberation.

Once we receive a blessing of guidance, the natural and generous response is to desire that same guidance for others; 'Amr's immediate request to be sent to his people reflects the communal responsibility that comes with personal transformation.
practical_advice: If you have had a meaningful dream, vision, or intuition that pointed you toward truth or a better path, do not dismiss it — write it down, reflect on it, and consider what action it might be calling you toward.

Identify one inherited belief or habit in your life that you know does not serve your spiritual or moral growth, and take a concrete step this week to distance yourself from it, as 'Amr destroyed the idol he had grown up with.

When you experience personal growth or receive guidance that changes your life, think about one person in your circle who might benefit from hearing your story, and find a gentle, sincere way to share it with them.

title: The Conversion and Mission of 'Amr b. Murra al-Juhani
summary: Amr b. Murra al-Juhani accepts Islam, recites poetry affirming his faith, and is sent by the Prophet back to his people as a missionary. His tribe accepts Islam and the Prophet writes them a covenant document.
story: the Prophet (SAAS), speaking the verses, 'I gave witness that God is truth, and that I am the first to abandon stone worship. I busied myself taking flight to you, crossing wild deserts and sand wastes To accompany the best of men in spirit and line, the messenger of mankind's Lord above the stars.' "The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'Welcome to you indeed, 0 'Amr b. Murra.' And I replied, '0 Messenger of God, send me to my people so that God may favour them through me as He did me through you.' "So he qent me to them, saying, 'Behave with kindness and speak the truth. Be you not churlish, nor arrogant, nor envious.'" He told how he came to his people, and summoned them to the teachings of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and they all accepted Islam, except for one of their men. 'Amr then went with them to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who greeted and welcomed them. He wrote for them a document as follows: "In the name of God the Most Merciful and Beneficent. This document is a safe-passage from God through the words of the Messenger of God (SAAS) through a truth-telling document and a veracious spokesman, sent with 'Amr h. Murra al-Juhani to Juhayna b. Zayd. For you there shall be the lowlands and the slopes, the depths and the sides of the valleys. You shall cultivate their produce and drink their pure water, provided that you affirm that you shall forsake onefifth (of the harvest) and keep praying the five prayers. (Your rich shall pay the alms to the poor): one ewe for each forty sheep and one ewe for each five camels, or two ewes for any combination thereof. The rich shall not be eligible to receive charity. And God will hear wimess that we so agreed, and the Muslims attending wiU bear witness. (This document is) handwritten by Qays h. Shammas." He then gave some verses spoken by 'Amr b. Murra on that subject, as is depicted in the great musnad hadith collection.
lessons: Gratitude for guidance received should inspire a desire to share that guidance with others, not keep it to oneself.
The Prophet's instruction to 'Amr — to be kind, truthful, and free of arrogance and envy — outlines a timeless ethical framework for any messenger of good.
Even when not everyone accepts what is good, one should not be discouraged; 'Amr's whole tribe except one accepted Islam, and this was still a great success.
practical_advice: When you have experienced a positive transformation, look for ways to share it with your community rather than keeping it private.
Practice the Prophet's advice daily: commit to at least one act of kindness, one moment of honest speech, and one check of your ego or envy.
When facing partial rejection of your efforts, focus on the many who responded positively rather than dwelling on the few who did not.

title: The Prophet Muhammad as the Seal of the Prophets — Ordained Before Adam's Creation
summary: Multiple hadith traditions affirm that the Prophet Muhammad was designated as the Seal of the Prophets in God's knowledge even while Adam was still being formed from clay, indicating his unique and supreme station among all creation.
story: Many of our early ancestors said, "When God made his covenant with mankind the day he said, 'Am I not your Lord?', he made a special agreement with the prophets. And He affirmed that that group (consisted) of five great lawgiving prophets, the first of whom was Noah and the last Muhammad (SAAS)." The ha& Aha Nu'aym informed us in his book the Dalail al-Nubuwma (Signs of the Prophethood) through various routes from al-Walid b. Muslim, that al-Awzaci related to him, as did Yahya h. Aha Kathir, from Abn Salama, from Abn Hurayra, who said that the Prophet (SAAS) was asked, "When was the prophethood required of you?" He replied, "Between the creation of Adam and the blowing of the spirit into him." Al-Tirmidhi related it similarly through al-Walid h. Muslim. He defined it as "good, but unique as a hadith from Aba Hurayra; we know of it only from that line". Aha Nu'aym stated that Sulayman b. Ahmad related to him, quoting Ya'qub h. Ishaq h. al-Zubayi al-Halabi, quoting Aba Jacfar al-Nufayli, quoting Amr h. Waqid, from Urwa h. Ruwaym, from al-Sunabihi, saying that Umar said, "0 Messenger of God, when were you made a prophet?" He replied, "While Adam was being formed in the clay." He then related it from a hadith of Nasr b. Muzaim from Qays b. Rabic, from Jabir al-Jucfi, from al-Sha'bi, from Ibn Abbas, who said that someone asked, "0 Messenger of God, when were you (made) a prophet?" He replied, "While Adam was between soul and body." In the tradition we gave in the story of Adam when God extracted from his loins his progeny, He singled out the prophets by a light between their eyes. It is apparent - but God knows best - that it (i.e. the light) was commensurate with their levels and ranks in God's view. If that be the case, then the light of Muhammad (SAAS) was greater, stronger, and more evident than that of all the rest. And this is a great and obvious distinction and indication of his nobility and high worth. And to that effect there is the hadith spoken by Imam Ahmad who said that Abd al-Rahman b. Mahdi related to him, as did Muawiya b. Salih from Sacid b. Suwayd al-Kalbi, from Abd al-Ala b. Hilal al-Salami from al-Irbad b. Sariyya, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "In God's view I was the Seal of the Prophets while Adam was still within his clay. And I shall tell you of the first of it - the prayer of my father Abraham, the glad tidings of Jesus regarding me, the visions my mother saw, and similarly those that the mothers of the believers see." Al-Layth and Ibn Wahb related this from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Mahdi, and Abd Allah b. Salih from Muawiya h. Salih, who added, "his mother saw, when she delivered him, a light from him that illuminated the castles of Syria." Imam Ahmad stated that Abd al-Rahman related to him, as did Mansur b. Sa'id, from Badil, from Abd Allah h. Shaqiq, from Maysara al-Fajr, who stated that he said, "0 Messenger of God, when were you a prophet?" And he replied, "While Adam was between spirit and body." The line of transmission of this tradition is also excellent.
lessons: Understanding that the Prophet's station was established before creation itself deepens appreciation for his message and its divine origin.
The concept that God's wisdom and plan precede all of creation invites humility — our lives, too, are held within a greater divine plan we cannot always see.
The light that accompanied the Prophet's birth, illuminating distant lands, is a symbol that true guidance has far-reaching effects beyond what we can immediately perceive.
practical_advice: When feeling lost about your purpose in life, reflect on the idea that God's knowledge encompasses your existence even before your birth, and trust that your life has meaning within His plan.
Study the lives and qualities of the great prophets to ground yourself in a tradition of human beings who persevered through hardship with unwavering trust in God.

When you feel small or insignificant, remember that the Prophet himself described his prophethood as beginning before Adam's spirit was even breathed into him — greatness in God's sight is not measured by worldly timelines.

title: Adam's Supplication Through the Name of Muhammad
summary: A tradition recounts that when Adam sinned, he supplicated to God through the name of Muhammad, whom God told him was the most precious of all creation, and through this intercession Adam received forgiveness.
story: In his interpretation of the hadith of 'Abd al-Wman b. Zayd b. Aslam about which there is some controversy - al-Hakim related from his father, from his grandfather, from Umar b. al-Khattab, God be pleased with him, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "When Adam committed sin, he said, '0 God, I ask you for Muhammad's sake, won't you forgive me?' God replied, '0 Adam, how did you know Muhammad when I haven't created him yet?' Adam replied, '0 Lord, because when you created me with your own hand and blew life into me from your spirit, I raised my head and saw on the foundations beneath the throne the writing, "There is no God but God. Muhammad is the Messenger of God." I knew that you would never have added to your own name anyone but the most precious to you of your creation.' God replied, 'You have spoken the truth, 0 Adam; he is to me the most precious of my creation. And since you asked in his name, you have my forgiveness. Were it not for Muhammad, I would not have created you.'" Al-Bayhaqi said, "'Abd al-Rahman b. Zayd b. Aslam alone gave this tradition, and it is weak. But God knows best.
lessons: Even the first human being turned to God in repentance after sin, showing that seeking forgiveness is a fundamental human act, not a sign of weakness.
The tradition illustrates that God's mercy is vast and available to those who sincerely call upon Him, even after grave mistakes.
The deep awareness Adam had of Muhammad's nobility — inscribed at the foundations of the throne — points to the universality and timelessness of the Prophet's message.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by guilt or past mistakes, follow Adam's example: turn to God directly, acknowledge the error, and ask for forgiveness sincerely.
Meditate on the concept that you are loved and known by God before your creation; this awareness can dissolve feelings of hopelessness and isolation.
Do not let guilt paralyze you. Adam sinned, repented, and was forgiven — recovery and renewal are always possible through sincere return to God.

title: The Covenant of God with All the Prophets to Support Muhammad
summary: God made a covenant with every prophet throughout history that if Muhammad were sent during their lifetime, they would believe in him, support him, and instruct their peoples to do the same, affirming his supreme status across all faiths and eras.
story: God Almighty said, "And when God made a covenant with the prophets, saying, 'I brought you the Scripture and wisdom, then a Messenger will come unto you verifying what you have. Let you believe in him well, and aid him!' He said, 'Have you agreed, and accepted my covenant in that?' They replied, 'We have agreed.' He said, 'Then bear witness and I too along with you will bear witness. Whoever revokes this hereafter, those are sinners!'" (sarat Al-'Imran, 111, v.81-2). Ali b. Abi Talib and Abd Allah b. Abbas, God bless them, said, "God never sent any prophet without first making a covenant with him (to the effect that) if Muhammad (SAAS) was sent when that prophet was alive, then he would believe in him and aid him. And He ordered him to convey the covenant to his people, that if Muhammad was sent while they were living, they really would believe in him and aid him to make him victorious." This is a recognition and an affirmation of his honour and high stature among all the religions and upon the tongues of all the prophets. It is an announcement to them and from them of his being a Messenger to the end of time and of his being the most noble of the messengers and the Seal of the Prophets. And God has made plain all the facts about him, all information, all secrets pertaining to him. He made his splendour very clear, as well as facts of his birth and homeland through the words of Abraham the "true friend", peace be upon him, when he finished building the kacba: "0 our Lord, send among them a Messenger from among themselves who will recite to them your signs and teach them the Scripture and wisdom, and purify them. You are the Mighty and the Wise" (surat al-Baqara, 11, v.129).
lessons: The unity of all prophetic messages across history reminds us that the pursuit of truth and righteousness is not a new endeavour but part of humanity's oldest covenant with God.
Abraham's prayer for a messenger to come from among his descendants, fulfilled in the Prophet Muhammad, shows us that sincere prayers made with full trust in God are answered across generations.
Recognising the continuity between all prophets and their shared covenant can serve as a foundation for spiritual stability and a sense of belonging to something much larger than oneself.
practical_advice: When feeling spiritually lost or disconnected, reconnect with the foundational covenants of faith — the belief in one God and the guidance of His messengers — as an anchor for your identity.
Read the prayers of the prophets in scripture, particularly Abraham's prayer for guidance, and use them as templates for your own sincere supplication.
Cultivate a sense of historical and spiritual continuity in your faith life; understanding that you are part of a long chain of believers can ease feelings of isolation and purposelessness.

title: The Prayer of Abraham and the Glad Tidings of Jesus Foretelling the Prophet's Mission
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) explained that his prophethood was foretold by the prayer of Abraham when building the Ka'ba, the glad tidings of Jesus, and a light seen by his mother illuminating the castles of Syria.
story: And God has made plain all the facts about hi, all information, all secrets pertaining to hi.He made his splendour very clear, as well as facts of his birth and homeland through the words of Abraham the "true friend", peace be upon him, when he finished building the kacba: "0 our Lord, send among them a Messenger from among themselves who will recite to them your signs and teach them the Scripture and wisdom, and purify them. You are the Mighty and the Wise" (siirat al-Baqara, 11, v.129). The 6rst clear and evident sign of his status among the people of the earth came, then, on the tongue of Abraham, the "true friend", the prophet, the next most honoured by God after Muhammad (SAAS). This why Im%u Ahmad said that Abii al-Nadr related to him, quoting al-Faraj, meaning Ibn Fadda, as did Luqmm b. 'Amir, who said that he heard Abii Amma say that he once asked, "0Messenger of God, what was the beginning of your mission?" He replied, "It was the prayer of my father Abraham, the glad tidings of Jesus, and my mother's seeing a light emitting from her that illuminated the castles of Syria." Imam Ahmad alone gave this tradition, and none of the authors of the six (canonical) books (of traditions) included it. The hiifis Abii Bakr b. Abii 'Asim related in his book al-Mawlid (The Birth) through Baqiyya, from Safwm b. 'Am, from Hajar b. Hajar, from Abii Mariq, that a bedouin asked, "0Messenger of God, what event was it that began your prophethood?" He replied, "God took from me the covenant, as he took covenants from the other prophets." And the mother of the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw in her sleep that a light was emitting from between her feet that lit up the castles of Syria. Imzm Muhammad b. Ishiiq b. Yasa stated that Thawr b. Yazid related to him from Khalid b. Ma'dxn, from the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) that they, the Companions, asked, "0Messenger of God, tell us about yourself." He replied, "The prayer of my father Abraham, the glad tidings of Jesus, and (the fact that) my mother saw, when she was pregnant, that a light came from inside her that illuminated B u ~ r ain Syria." The chain of authorities of this tradition is also excellent. It contains good news for the people of our community in the lands of Busra. It is the first place in Syria to receive the light of the prophethood; and to God all praise and favour are due. That is why it was the first city of Syria to be conquered. It was taken peacefully during the caliphate of Abii Bakr, God be pleased with him.The Messenger of God (SAAS) travelled there twice in the company of his uncle Abii T d i b when he was a boy of 12. It was there the story of B&ira the monk was set, as we have related above. The second time he was with Maysara, the freed man of Khadija, while on a trading expedition on her behalf. Also, it has the spot where the camel known as the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS) had knelt. Its remains are still there, so they say. Eventually its remains were gathered, and a mosque, famous to this day, was built over it. That is the town where the necks of the camels lit up from the light of the fire that came from Hijiiz in the year 654, according to the statement made by the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said, "A fire leaves Hijzz that illuminates the necks of the camels at Busra." And God Almighty stated, "Those who follow the Prophet, the ummi (unlettered), whom they find written down with them in the Torah and the Gospel, who orders people to he good and forbids them evil, and who makes lawful for them good things and makes unlawful foul things, who removes from them their burden and the chains that were upon them. And so those who believe in him and honour him and help him and who follow the light that came down with him, those shall he successful" (surat al-A'r~hVII, v.157).
lessons: Divine plans unfold across generations — what Abraham prayed for, Jesus foretold, and the Prophet fulfilled — reminding us that God's wisdom operates on a timeline far greater than our own.
Signs of truth appear long before their fulfillment; patience and trust allow us to recognize them when they arrive.
The interconnectedness of all prophets and their messages shows that divine guidance is consistent and coherent — a source of reassurance when one feels spiritually lost.
practical_advice: When facing uncertainty about your path, reflect on how your own life's difficult early chapters may be preparing you for a purpose you cannot yet see.
Study the consistency between different religious traditions and scriptures to deepen your conviction and reduce spiritual anxiety.
When you feel your prayers go unanswered, remember Abraham's prayer for a prophet took centuries to be fulfilled — trust that sincere du'a is never lost.

title: The Bedouin Merchant Who Listened to the Prophet Between Abu Bakr and Umar
summary: A bedouin merchant who had come to Medina decided to observe the Prophet and witnessed a Jewish boy on his deathbed testify to the Prophet's prophethood after his father denied it, prompting the Prophet to pray over the boy.
story: Imzm Ahmad stated that Ismz'il related to him, from al-Jurayri, from Aha Sakhr al-Wqayli, who said that a hedouin told him that he had brought in some merchandise to Medina while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was still alive. And when he had finished selling it, he told himself that he should meet that man and listen to him. The bedouin said, "He met me when he was walking between Abii Bakr and Wmar. I followed them until they came to a Jew holding and reading the Torah, and consoling himself with it at the death of a son of his, the best and most handsome of boys. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, 'I beg of you, by Him who sent down the Torah, do you find in your Scripture any description of me and my place of origin?' The man made a gesture with his head, meaning, 'no'. But his (dead) son spoke, saying, 'But yes, by H i who sent down the Torah, we do find in our Scriptures a description of you and of your place of origin! I bear witness that there is no God hut God, and that yon are the Messenger of God.' And so he said, 'Keep away the Jew from your brother.' Then he (the Prophet) took care of winding the dead boy in his shroud and performed the prayers for the dead over him." This chain of authorities is excellent, and there are testimonies to its veracity in the ~ahihfrom Anas b. Malik.
lessons: Truth has a way of manifesting even in the most unexpected circumstances — a dying boy's testimony carried more weight than his father's denial, showing that sincere hearts recognize truth regardless of social pressure.
The Prophet's compassion extended to all people regardless of their faith; he honoured the Jewish boy with a proper burial, teaching that human dignity transcends religious difference.
Witnessing extraordinary events can awaken gratitude and strengthen faith in those who are simply open and attentive, as the bedouin merchant discovered by merely choosing to observe.
practical_advice: When those around you deny or dismiss something you sense to be true, trust the quiet conviction in your own heart rather than letting social pressure silence you.
Practise extending compassion to people outside your immediate community — acts of care for others, regardless of background, reflect the highest character.
Make time to simply observe and listen to people of wisdom; sometimes an unplanned encounter, like the bedouin's chance meeting, becomes the most transformative experience of your life.

title: The Jewish Scholar Who Privately Acknowledged the Prophet's Prophethood
summary: The Prophet questioned the most knowledgeable of the Jews, Abd Allah ibn Sariya, who privately admitted he and his people knew Muhammad was the true Messenger of God but refused to accept Islam publicly for fear of disagreeing with his community.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, from Salii, the freed-man of 'Ahd Allah b. Mupc, from Aha Hurayra who said that some Jews came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who said, "Bring out your most knowledgeable man." They replied, "He is 'Abd Allah b. Sariya." The Messenger of God (SAAS) sat with h i alone and adjured h i by his religion and by God's blessings to them, by God who had fed them with manna and quails and shaded them with clouds. "Do you know me as the Messenger of God?" the Prophet (SAAS) asked. "Yes indeed, by God," the Jew responded. "And my people know what I know. You are fully apparent in the Torah. But they are envious." "So what prevents you (from believing)?" he asked. "I just hate to disagree with my people," the Jew replied. "Maybe they will follow you and accept Islam, then I will."
lessons: Knowing the truth and acting on it are two very different things; the greatest barrier to transformation is often not ignorance but the fear of social disapproval.
Envy and tribal loyalty can blind entire communities to what their own scholars privately acknowledge — a warning against allowing group identity to override sincere conviction.
Delaying a righteous decision by waiting for others to act first is a form of spiritual procrastination that can cost a person everything.
practical_advice: Examine areas of your life where you know the right course of action but have delayed it out of fear of what others will think — make a small, concrete step today toward what you know to be right.
If you find yourself saying 'I'll change when others around me change,' recognise this as a trap; personal transformation is an individual responsibility, not a collective one.
When you feel the pull of peer pressure away from your values, remind yourself that private conviction without public courage provides no lasting peace.

title: The Prophet's Letter to the Jews of Khaybar Affirming His Prophethood in Their Scripture
summary: The Prophet Muhammad wrote a letter to the Jews of Khaybar invoking Moses and their shared scripture, urging them to acknowledge that he was described in the Torah and the Gospel and to accept Islam.
story: Salama b. al-Fad1 stated, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from Muhammad b. Aba Muhammad, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, to the effect that he used to say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote to the Jews of Khaybar as follows, "In the name of God the most merciful and beneficent, from Muhammad, the Messenger of God, the companion and brother of Moses and the corroborator of the message Moses brought. God stated to you, '0 Jews, people of the Torah, you will find in your book the fact that Muhammad is the Messenger of God; and those who are with him are violent against unbelievers, compassionate among themselves. You see them bowing down and prostrating themselves, desiring grace and approval from God. They have marks on their faces from the effects of prostration. That is how they are compared in the Torah and in the Gospel. (They are) like a plant that emits its shoot, then strengthens it so that it thickens and stands straight on its stem. He (God) gives the sowers delight so that He may enrage through them the unbelievers. God has promised forgiveness and great reward to those of them who believe and do good' (stirat al-Fath, XLVII, v.29). I adjure you by God by that which has come down to you, and by Him who fed manna and quails to your forebears and tribes, who dried up the sea for your forefathers to save you from Pharaoh and his deeds, can you not tell us whether you find in God's revelations to you (the fact) that you should believe in Muhammad? And if you do not discover that in your Scriptures, then there is no hatred for you. (But now) what is right has become clear from what is error. And I call you to God and to his Prophet (SAAS)."
lessons: Truth invites through reason and shared heritage, not coercion — the Prophet appealed to the Jews' own scripture and history, modelling that da'wah begins with finding common ground.
When we remind others of God's past blessings upon them, we appeal to the deepest part of their conscience; gratitude for past mercy is a powerful catalyst for present faith.
Clarity and directness in conveying truth, paired with absence of hatred even toward those who reject the message, reflects the highest standard of moral courage.
practical_advice: When trying to guide someone toward a better path, begin by acknowledging what you share in common with them rather than emphasising differences.
If your sincere efforts to help others are rejected, remind yourself that the Prophet himself faced rejection from entire communities and responded with patience and continued outreach.
Reflect regularly on the specific blessings God has given you throughout your life — cataloguing these moments of divine care can lift feelings of hopelessness and ingratitude.

title: The Jewish Scholar Who Acknowledged the Prophet but Raised the Question of His Nation's Size
summary: A Jewish man in conversation with the Prophet admitted that the Torah described Muhammad but expressed doubt about his nation's smallness, to which the Prophet replied that his nation far exceeded 70,000.
story: Ahn al-@sim d-Baghawi stated that CAhd al-W~hidh. Ghiysth, Aha Bahr that is, related to him, quoting 'Ahd al-CAziz h. Muslim, quoting 'Asim h. Kulayh, from his father, from d-Sallatan h. 'Asim, who related that his uncle said that he was seated with the Prophet (SAAS) when he gazed over at a certain man, a Jew wearing a shirt, trousers, and sandals. The Prophet (SAAS) began talking to him. The man asked, "0 Messenger of God . . ." The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, "Are you wimessing that I am the Messenger of God?" "No," the man said. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked h i , "Do you read the Torah?" "Yes," the man replied. He asked, "Do you read the Gospel?" "Yes, the man replied. "And the Qur'zn?" "No," the Jew replied. "But if you want, I will." The Prophet (SAAS) then asked, "In what you read of the Torah and the Gospel, do you find me to be a prophet?" The man answered, "We do find a description of you and of your place of origin. When you came, we hoped you would be one of us. But when we saw you we knew that you were not he." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "Why is that, 0 Jew?" He replied, "We find it to he written down that, 'There will enter paradise from his nation 70,000'. But with you we see only a few men." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then responded, "My nation is far greater in number than 70,000, and 70,000 more again." This is a unique hadith from such a source, and they (the scholars) have not promulgated it.
lessons: Appearances and current circumstances can be deeply misleading about a movement's ultimate scope and impact — what begins small can grow beyond all imagination.
People sometimes construct intellectual objections to hide emotional or social reluctance; the Jewish scholar's real hesitation was not the size of the community but something deeper.
The Prophet's calm and confident response to doubt — neither angry nor defensive — models how to engage with scepticism from a place of secure faith.
practical_advice: When you feel your efforts or your community are too small to matter, remember that every great movement in history began with a handful of committed individuals — do not judge a mission by its early numbers.

If someone raises an objection to something you believe in, try to understand whether it is a genuine intellectual question or a surface-level reason masking a deeper reluctance, and address both with patience.
Practise responding to challenges to your faith or values with calm confidence rather than anxiety or defensiveness — groundedness in what you know to be true is itself a form of da'wah.

title: Daniel in Prison Interprets Nebuchadnezzar's Forgotten Dream
summary: The prophet Daniel, imprisoned during Nebuchadnezzar's reign after the destruction of Jerusalem, offered to interpret the king's terrifying dream that the king himself had forgotten, refusing to prostrate before the king and citing his obligation to God alone.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq b. Yasar related in his hook al-Mubtada' (The Begznning) from Sacid b. Bashir, from Qatzda, from K a 0 al-&bar, and others related from Wahb h. Munabbih, that Bukhtunassar (Nebuchadnezzar), after he had destroyed Jerusalem and kept the Jews in subjugation for seven years, saw strange visions in a dream that terrified him. And so he assembled his soothsayers and wise men and questioned them about those visions of his. They suggested, "If the King first tells us of them, then we can interpret them." But he replied, "I've forgotten them. And if you don't tell me in three days about them, I'll kid the lot of you! " Off they went very fearful of his threat. Daniel, in gaol at the time, heard of this and told the gaoler, "Go and tell the King, 'We have a man who has knowledge of your visions and can interpret them.'" So he went to tell the King who summoned Daniel. When he entered, Daniel did not bow down to him, so the King demanded, "What prevents you from prostrating before me?" Daniel replied, "God brought me knowledge, and ordered me not to bow to anyone but Him." Bukhtunassar then said, "I like those who fulfil their duty to their masters. Tell me about my vision." Daniel said to hi,"You saw a huge idol, its feet on the ground and its head in the sky. Its upper part was of gold, its middle of silver, its lower part of brass, its legs of iron and its feet of clay. While you were looking at it, delighted by its beauty and the intricacy of its workmanship,"
lessons: True dignity cannot be imprisoned — Daniel remained spiritually free and morally upright even while physically confined, showing that inner freedom is independent of outer circumstances.

Standing firm in one's principles before the most powerful of rulers, as Daniel did when he refused to prostrate before Nebuchadnezzar, requires a faith so deep that worldly consequences become secondary.
God-given knowledge and wisdom are gifts that carry responsibility; Daniel used his gift not for personal gain or survival alone but to serve truth and help others, even at personal risk.
practical_advice: When you are in a position of powerlessness — whether in a difficult job, relationship, or circumstance — identify the one thing that cannot be taken from you: your values and your relationship with God, and anchor yourself there.

If you possess a skill or knowledge that could benefit others in a desperate situation, offer it generously even when the risk to yourself is real; such acts of courage build character and trust in God.

Reflect on whether there are areas in your life where you bow — metaphorically — to worldly pressures or people in ways that compromise your integrity, and consider how to realign with your deeper convictions.

title: Daniel's Prophecy of the Prophet Muhammad to Bukhtunassar (Nebuchadnezzar)
summary: The prophet Daniel interprets Nebuchadnezzar's dream of a great idol destroyed by a stone, explaining it as a prophecy of an Arab prophet who will come at the end of time to guide humanity and overcome all nations and religions.
story: "What prevents you from prostrating before me?" Daniel replied, "God brought me knowledge, and ordered me not to bow to anyone but Him." Bukhtunassar then said, "I like those who fulfil their duty to their masters. Tell me about my vision." Daniel said to him, "You saw a huge idol, its feet on the ground and its head in the sky. Its upper part was of gold, its middle of silver, its lower part of brass, its legs of iron and its feet of clay. While you were looking at it, delighted by its beauty and the intricacy of its workmanship, God cast down a stone from heaven which hit the top of its head and crushed it completely. Its gold, silver, brass, iron, and clay became mixed together so that it seemed to you that even if all men and all the spirits were to try to differentiate its components they would fail. When you looked at the stone that had been thrown, you saw it get bigger and bigger until it filled all the earth so that all you could see was it and the sky." Bukhtunassar said, "You are right. That is the vision I saw. What does it mean?" "Well", replied Daniel, "the idol represents various nations, early, intermediate, and late in time. The stone by which the idol was struck is a religion God will send and reveal to these nations at time's end. And God will send a prophet, an ummi, from the Arabs who will conquer with it all the nations and all the religions, as you saw the stone destroy the various components of the idol. It will overcome all the nations and religions, just as you saw the stone overwhelm the entire earth. God will purify the truth and annihilate falsehood through it. Through it He will guide those in error. He will teach the illiterate, strengthen the weak, uplift the humble, and give victory to the disadvantaged." The account ends the story by Bukhtunassar releasing the Israelites through Daniel, peace be upon him.
lessons: God's plan extends across centuries and civilizations — what appears as a dream or vision may carry divine wisdom far beyond the moment it is received.
Standing firm in one's principles before worldly power, as Daniel refused to prostrate to Nebuchadnezzar, is a mark of true faith and inner strength.
The coming of Prophet Muhammad was foretold across traditions and peoples, reminding us that truth is not confined to any one era or nation.
practical_advice: When you feel lost or uncertain about the future, reflect on the fact that God's plan is vast and purposeful — your current struggle may be part of a larger story you cannot yet see.

Draw strength from the example of Daniel: when pressured by authority or peer influence to compromise your values, remember that integrity before God is more valuable than approval from those in power.

If you feel weak, overlooked, or marginalized, take comfort in the prophecy that God's messenger came specifically to 'strengthen the weak, uplift the humble, and give victory to the disadvantaged' — you are not forgotten.

title: Al-Waqidi's Account of Al-Mughira with Al-Muqawkis, King of Alexandria
summary: Al-Waqidi relates the story of al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba visiting al-Muqawkis, the King of Alexandria, who questioned him about the qualities of the Prophet Muhammad, similar to how Heraclius questioned Abu Sufyan, and then consulted Christian bishops about the Prophet's description.
story: Al-Waqidi related, with a chain of authorities from al-Mughira b. Shu'ba, the story of the latter's going to al-Muqawkis, King of Alexandria, and of being questioned by him about the qualities of the Messenger of God (SAAS) similarly to how Heraclius asked Abu Sufyan Sakhr b. Harb. He related how he questioned the Christian bishops in the churches for a description of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and how they did so. It is a long story that is related by the hafiz Abu Nu'aym in Dala'il al-Nubuwwa (Signs of the Prophethood).
lessons: The recognition of Prophet Muhammad's truth was not limited to Muslims — even rulers and clergy of other faiths sought out his description, showing that truth has a universal resonance.
Seeking knowledge about truth, even from those outside one's own tradition, reflects intellectual honesty and sincerity of heart.
The Prophet's character was so remarkable that it prompted inquiry and admiration from leaders of great empires and religious institutions.
practical_advice: When searching for truth or guidance, do not be deterred by unfamiliar sources — truth often confirms itself across different contexts and traditions.
If you are going through a period of spiritual confusion or seeking, take heart that even great kings and scholars devoted effort to understanding the Prophet's message — you are in good company.
Reflect on what qualities of the Prophet most inspire you, and consider how embodying even one of those qualities might transform your own relationships and daily life.

title: The Prophet's Challenge to Jewish Scholars: Your Own Scriptures Describe Me
summary: The Prophet Muhammad passed by Jewish schools in Madinah and called upon the Jewish community to accept Islam, declaring by God that their own scriptures contained his description.
story: It is established in the sahih that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went past some Jewish schools and asked them, "O Jews, accept Islam. For, by Him in whose hand is my soul, you will certainly find description of me in your scriptures."
lessons: The Prophet's confidence in his mission was rooted not in arrogance but in divine certainty — he invited others with conviction even when faced with likely rejection.
Truth does not hide itself; it calls out openly and invites sincere seekers to examine their own sources of knowledge.
Being rejected when speaking truth is not a sign of failure — the Prophet himself faced rejection from those who had the means to verify his prophethood and still turned away.
practical_advice: When you are rejected after sharing something true or meaningful, remind yourself that the outcome is not always in your hands — your responsibility is sincerity, not results.

If you feel dismissed by those around you, draw strength from the Prophet's unwavering confidence: he did not lower his voice or retreat from truth simply because it was inconvenient for his audience.
Use moments of rejection as an opportunity for self-reflection: am I speaking truth with kindness and clarity? Am I leaving space for others to come to understanding in their own time?

title: Abd Allah ibn Amr Describes the Prophet's Qualities as Found in the Torah
summary: Abd Allah ibn Amr ibn al-As describes to Ata ibn Yasar the qualities of the Prophet Muhammad as recorded in the Torah, including his gentleness, his mission to guide the illiterate, and his role in opening blind eyes, deaf ears, and closed hearts.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Musa b. Dawud related to him, quoting Fulayh b. Sulayman, from Hilal b. 'Ali, from 'Ata' b. Yasar who said that he met 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. al-'As and asked him, "Describe to me the qualities of the Messenger of God (SAAS) that are in the Torah." He replied, "Certainly. He is, by God, referred to in the Torah as he is in the Qur'an. 'O Prophet, we have sent you to give witness, to bring good tidings, and to warn, and to protect the illiterate. You are my slave and my messenger. I have named you al-Mutawakkil, "the manager", a man not rough or uncouth nor one who shouts loudly in the markets. A man who does not pay back evil with evil, but one who forgives and pardons. God will not take him until they set the deviant community aright by them saying, "There is no God but God." By him He will open blind eyes, deaf ears, and closed hearts.'" Al-Bukhari related this from Muhammad b. Sinan from Fulayh. He also related it from 'Abd Allah (who was the son of Raja', or as others say the son of Salih) from 'Abd al-Aziz b. Abi Salama, from Hilal b. 'Alliya, and his text is similar to this, with additions. Ibn Jarir related it from a hadith of Fulayh, from Hilal, from 'Ata'. His account adds the words, "And 'Ata' said, 'I met Ka'b and asked him about that, and he did not disagree by so much as a letter; though he said, "among the merchants". And he gave the line of authorities as Sa'id, from Hilal, from 'Ata' from 'Abd Allah b. Salam.'"
lessons: The Prophet Muhammad was described in earlier scriptures as a man of extraordinary gentleness — someone who does not repay evil with evil, who does not shout in the markets, and who forgives — reminding us that these qualities are divinely praised.
The Prophet's mission was specifically directed toward those who were vulnerable, illiterate, or spiritually lost — his mercy was not exclusive but universal.
The consistency of the Prophet's description across different narrators and traditions strengthens our confidence in the authenticity of his prophethood and character.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by harsh treatment from others, recall the Prophetic example: responding to evil with forgiveness and pardon is not weakness — it is one of the highest divinely praised qualities.
If you feel spiritually blind, deaf, or closed off, know that the Prophet's mission was precisely to open such hearts — turn toward his example and teachings as a path back to clarity and peace.

In moments of confusion about faith or truth, study the character of the Prophet rather than only debating theology — his gentleness, consistency, and mercy are themselves compelling evidence of his prophethood.

title: Ibn Sallam's Account of the Prophet's Description in the Torah
summary: Ibn Sallam, a Jewish scholar who embraced Islam, narrates the description of Prophet Muhammad found in the Torah, affirming that he was foretold as a witness, bringer of glad tidings, and protector of the illiterate who would guide people to monotheism.
story: The hafiz Abu Bakr al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu al-Husayn b. al-Mufaddal al-Qattan related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far, quoting Ya'qub b. al-Layth, quoting Khalid b. Yazid, from Sufyan, quoting Abu Salih, quoting Sa'id b. Abi Hilal b. Usama, from 'Ata' b. Yasar, from Ibn Sallam, who used to say, "We do find a description of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the wording, 'We have sent you as a man to witness and to bring tidings, to warn and to protect the illiterate. You are My slave and My messenger. I have named him al-Mutammakil, "the manager"; he will be neither rough nor uncouth nor one who shouts loudly in the markets. He will not requite evil with the same but will pardon and make allowance. And He will not take him until he sets the deviant community aright by having them bear witness that there is no God but God. By him He will open blind eyes, deaf ears, and closed hearts.'" 'Ata' b. Yasar stated, "Al-Laythi informed me that he heard Ka'b al-Ahbar say the same as Ibn Sallam."
lessons: A person of deep learning who searches sincerely for truth will eventually recognize it — Ibn Sallam's testimony as a Jewish scholar who affirmed the Prophet's description in his own scriptures is a powerful reminder that truth rewards honest seeking.
The Prophet's mission was one of opening: opening blind eyes, deaf ears, and closed hearts — this is a beautiful metaphor for what genuine spiritual guidance does for any human soul.
Guidance is not forced — the Prophet's role was to witness, to warn, and to bring glad tidings, leaving the human being free to respond with their conscience.
practical_advice: If you are in a season of spiritual searching or doubt, be honest and thorough in your inquiry — sincere searching, like that of Ibn Sallam, leads to clarity.

When you encounter someone who has closed their heart to truth or kindness, do not despair — remember that the Prophet was promised that hearts would be opened through him, and pray for the softening of hard hearts, including your own.

Practice the Prophetic quality of 'pardoning and making allowance' in your personal relationships — when someone wrongs you, pause before reacting and ask yourself whether forgiveness is possible here.

title: Ka'b al-Ahbar's Description of the Prophet Muhammad from the Torah
summary: Umm al-Darda' asked Ka'b al-Ahbar how the Torah described the Prophet Muhammad, and he recounted a detailed description of the Prophet as one who is gentle, gives sight to the damaged, hearing to the dulled, and guides twisted tongues to the testimony of monotheism.
story: Yunus stated from Muhammad b. Ishaq that Muhammad b. Thabit b. Shurahbil related to him, from Ibn Abi Awfa, from Umm al-Darda' who said that she asked Ka'b al-Ahbar: "How do you find the description of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the Torah?" "We find him as Muhammad the Messenger of God; his name is al-Mutammakd. He is not rough nor uncouth nor one who shouts loudly in the markets. He has been given the keys. Through him, God will give sight to eyes that are damaged, and hearing to ears that are dulled. He will set right twisted tongues, through him, until they give witness that there is no God but God, one and without partner. And through him He will aid and protect the ill-treated." And this is also related from Ka'b from a different chain of authorities.
lessons: The Prophet Muhammad was given 'the keys' — a powerful image suggesting that his prophethood unlocks what has been sealed in human hearts, minds, and spiritual sight.
The protection of the ill-treated was central to the Prophet's mission — justice and advocacy for the vulnerable are not peripheral but core to his legacy.

That the same description of the Prophet was confirmed across multiple chains of narration and multiple scholars from different backgrounds speaks to the remarkable integrity of Islamic scholarship and the weight of prophetic truth.
practical_advice: If you feel that your spiritual senses have become dulled — that you cannot see beauty, cannot hear wisdom, cannot speak truthfully — turn to the Prophet's example and teachings as a means of renewal and restoration.
If you or someone you know is being treated unjustly, find strength in knowing that protecting the ill-treated was a divinely assigned mission of the Prophet — seek justice with patience and faith.
Reflect on the image of 'twisted tongues being set right': consider whether there are ways your own speech could be more honest, more kind, or more aligned with the truth you believe.

title: God's Revelation to David in the Psalms: The Coming of Ahmad and His Nation
summary: Wahb ibn Munabbih narrates that God revealed to the Prophet David in the Psalms the coming of a prophet named Ahmad and Muhammad, whose nation would be forgiven and elevated above all nations, coming to God on the Day of Judgment with the light of prophets.
story: Wahb b. Munabbih mentioned that God revealed to Dawud (David) in the Book of Psalms, "O Dawud, there will come after you a prophet named Ahmad and Muhammad, a truthful man, a lord; I will never be angry with him, and he will never make me angry with him. I have forgiven him before he disobeyed me, both his previous and his later sins. His nation is forgiven. I have made gifts to them similar to those I gave to the prophets, and imposed duties upon them that I imposed upon the prophets and the messengers, so that they will come to me on Judgement Day with their light like that of the prophets." The text continues until the words, "O Dawud, I have given preference to Muhammad and to his nation above all the nations."
lessons: The nation of Muhammad was honored by God before it even existed — a reminder that belonging to this community carries both a great privilege and a great responsibility.
God's forgiveness is vast and preemptive — He forgave the Prophet before any sin, and extended that forgiveness to his nation, which should inspire profound gratitude rather than complacency.
Being given duties similar to those of prophets is both an honor and a calling — the Muslim community is expected to carry truth, justice, and mercy to the world as prophets did.
practical_advice: When you feel unworthy of God's mercy or weighed down by past mistakes, reflect on the depth of God's forgiveness described here — forgiveness that preceded even the possibility of wrongdoing — and let it soften your self-condemnation.
Cultivate gratitude for the gift of being part of the Prophet's nation by actively living out its values: honesty, mercy, justice, and care for others.

If you feel spiritually ordinary or insignificant, remember that your community has been promised to come before God on the Day of Judgment with the light of prophets — invest in your spiritual practice as though it truly matters, because it does.

title: Quranic Verses Affirming the Prophet's Mention in Previous Scriptures
summary: The author presents a collection of Quranic verses affirming that people of previous scriptures recognized and knew the Prophet Muhammad as they knew their own children, and that scholars among them wept and prostrated when his revelation was recited to them.
story: Knowledge of the existence of reference to him in the books of the peoples of the Scriptures, a fact known to the religion, is essential. And many verses of the holy Qur'an give proof of that and we have, praise be to God, made reference to those in the proper places. One such verse is, "(As for) those to whom we gave the Scripture before it, they believe in it. And when it is recited to them, they say, 'We believe in it. It is the truth from our Lord. We were already submitting Muslims before it'" (surat al-Qasas, XXVII, v.52-3). And Almighty God said, "Those to whom we have brought the book know him as they know their sons. (Yet) a group of them certainly conceal the truth, though they know it" (surat al-Baqara, II, v.146). Almighty God also said, "Those who were given the knowledge of it prostrate themselves on their faces when it is recited to them and they say, 'Glory be to our Lord; the promise of our Lord is fulfilled'" (surat al-Isra', XVII, v.107-8). This means that if it were the promise of our Lord that Muhammad (SAAS) exists and be sent, then that is an indisputable fact. And all praise to Him capable of whatever he wishes; there is nothing he is incapable of doing. And the Almighty said, making reference to the priests and monks, "And when they hear what was revealed to the Messenger, you see their eyes overflow with tears for the truth they recognize. They say, 'O God, we believe, so write us down among those bearing witness'" (surat al-Ma'ida, V, v.83).
lessons: Truth has a quality that the sincere heart recognizes — even scholars and priests of other traditions wept and prostrated when they heard the Quran, because truth resonates with truth.
Knowing the truth and concealing it is a serious spiritual failure — the Quran distinguishes between those who recognized the Prophet and accepted him versus those who recognized him and hid it.

God's promises are fulfilled with certainty — what was promised about the Prophet's coming across centuries of prophetic tradition was made manifest, which should strengthen our trust in all of God's promises.
practical_advice: When you encounter a truth that moves you emotionally — perhaps in prayer, in reading, or in a moment of clarity — do not suppress or rationalize it away; allow it to guide you toward action and commitment.

If you find yourself knowing what is right but avoiding it for social or personal reasons, reflect on the Quranic warning about those who concealed truth they knew — honesty with yourself and God is the beginning of integrity.

When your faith feels uncertain or abstract, return to the concrete: people across cultures, centuries, and traditions recognized the Prophet's truth. Let the weight of that historical and spiritual consensus be a source of reassurance.

title: Jesus Son of Mary Announces the Coming of Ahmad
summary: The Quran records that the Prophet Jesus stood among his people and announced the coming of a messenger after him named Ahmad, thereby completing the chain of prophecies foretelling the prophethood of Muhammad.
story: Almighty God has related of the last of the prophets of the Israelites, Jesus, son of Mary, that he stood up among his people and addressed them as follows, "I am the Messenger of God to you, verifying what is in the Torah that came before me and announcing the coming of a messenger who will come to you after me and whose name is Ahmad" (surat al-Saff, LXI, v.6). And in the Gospel there are the tidings of al-Farqalit, "the Paraclete", by whom Muhammad (SAAS) is meant.
lessons: The Prophet Muhammad's coming was not an isolated event but the culmination of a long chain of divine prophecy — Jesus, Moses, David, and Daniel all pointed toward him.
The Paraclete mentioned in the Gospel and the Ahmad mentioned in the Quran point to a singular divine intention: that humanity would be guided through a final messenger whose message is universal.
Recognizing the continuity of prophethood helps us appreciate that Islam did not come to erase previous faiths but to complete and confirm them.
practical_advice: When you feel that your faith lacks historical depth or rootedness, study the prophetic chain from Adam through Jesus to Muhammad — you belong to a tradition thousands of years deep.

If you encounter interfaith questions or dialogue, approach them from a place of confidence and curiosity: the Quran itself honors the scriptures and prophets of other traditions while affirming its own completeness.
Reflect on the quality of 'Ahmad' — the one who praises God most — and ask yourself how you can cultivate more genuine praise and gratitude in your own daily life.

title: The Prophet's Description in the Gospel: Gentle, Forgiving, Not Returning Evil
summary: A narration from Aisha reports that the Messenger of God said it is written in the Gospel that he would be gentle, not rough or uncouth, not one who shouts in the markets, and one who pardons and forgives rather than repaying evil with evil.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related from al-Hakim, from al-Asamm, from Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, from Yunus b. Bukayr, from Yunus b. 'Amr, from al-'izz b. Harb, from 'A'isha, God be pleased with her, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "It is written in the Gospel (that he will be) not rough, nor uncouth, not one who shouts loudly in the markets, and does not repay evil with the like, but rather who pardons and forgives."
lessons: The Prophet Muhammad himself knew of and acknowledged his description in previous scriptures, showing his deep awareness of the continuity of divine guidance.
The qualities described — gentleness, restraint, forgiveness — are not passive but actively chosen responses to a world that can be harsh and unjust.
Pardoning those who wrong us is not only a prophetic quality but a divinely recorded one, affirming that forgiveness is among the highest human virtues.
practical_advice: The next time someone treats you unkindly or unfairly, pause and consciously choose the Prophetic response: pardon and forgiveness rather than retaliation.
Practice the art of being present without being loud — the Prophet did not need to shout in the marketplace to be heard. Cultivate a presence that is calm, dignified, and attentive.

When you feel the impulse to respond to harshness with harshness, remember that the Prophet's gentleness was not accidental or situational — it was who he was. Ask God to help you build that quality into your own character.

title: Description of Prophet Muhammad in the Gospel
summary: A hadith narrated by Aisha describes how the Prophet Muhammad was mentioned in the Gospel as gentle, forgiving, and not rough or loud, highlighting his foretold character.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related from al-Hiikim, from al-Asamm, fmm Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jahhzr, from Ymus b. Bukayr, from Yiinus b. CAmr, from al-'izx h. Harb, from 'A'isha, God be pleased with her, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "It is written in the Gospel (that he will be) not rough, nor uncouth, not one who shouts loudly in the markets, and does not repay evil with the like, but rather who pardons and forgives."
lessons: Forgiveness and gentleness are marks of true spiritual greatness, foretold even before one's own time.
The character of a person—how they treat others—is more enduring than any worldly achievement.
Prophetic qualities transcend culture and era; goodness is universally recognizable.
practical_advice: When you feel the urge to repay harshness with harshness, pause and ask whether forgiveness would serve a higher purpose.
Reflect on how your daily speech and demeanor affect those around you, and strive for gentleness as a conscious practice.
Study the character traits praised across spiritual traditions as a guide for your own self-development.

title: God's Revelation to Jesus Describing the Coming Arab Prophet
summary: A narration attributed to Ya'qub b. Sufyan describes a divine revelation to Jesus detailing the physical and moral characteristics of the coming Arab unlettered Prophet, Muhammad.
story: Yacqiib b. Sufyzn stated that Fayd al-Bajali related to him, quoting Salzm b. Miskin, from Muqiitil b. Hayyzn who said that the Almighty and Glorious God revealed to Jesus, son of Mary, as follows: "Serve me well and listen and obey, 0 Son of the pure, chaste, virgin. We have created you without a physical father and have made you a sign for the worlds, so worship me. And explain to the pecple of S u r a , in Syraic, and tell those around you that I am the existent truth that will not pass away. Believe in the Arab ummi (unlettered) Prophet; he who has the camel, and wears a midra'a (outer garment), and a turban that is his crown, and sandals, and has a cane that is his staff. His hair is curly, his brow is broad, his eyebrows are joined, his eyes are large, his lashes are h e , his eyes are black, his nose is curved, his cheeks are distinct, and his beard is full. The sweat of his face is like pearls; from him the scent of musk f3nanateS. His neck is like a silver vessel, and along his collarbone gold seems to flow. He has hairs that grow long and soft from his throat down to his navel, his stomach bearing no other hair. He is broad of fingers and toes. He overshadows all when he is with others. When he walks he seems to emerge from the rock and flow from a stream. He has few progeny, as if he wanted males as his offspring."
lessons: Divine wisdom operates across ages and nations; the truth of a righteous person is recognized even by those who came before them.
Physical and moral beauty together form a complete picture of prophetic character, reminding us that outer conduct and inner virtue are interconnected.
Trust that God's plan is revealed in its own time and through surprising channels.
practical_advice: Contemplate the description of the Prophet's character as a mirror for your own moral aspirations—which qualities can you cultivate today?
When feeling spiritually disconnected, reflect on the continuity of divine guidance across prophets as a source of reassurance.
Use descriptions of noble character from your tradition as a practical checklist for how you wish to be remembered by others.

title: The Inherited Document Foretelling the Muslim Nation
summary: Uthman b. al-Hakam narrates how his older relatives possessed a pre-Islamic document inherited across generations that foretold the coming of a nation that would pray powerfully and pursue enemies across seas, astonishing the Prophet when it was read to him.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related that CUthmm b. al-Hakam b. R%fiC h. Sinsn said, "Some of my older male relatives told me that they had in their possession a document they had inherited before Islam and still had when God brought Islam. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Medina they told him of it and brought it to him. In it was written, 'In the name of God, His words are truth while those of evil-doers shall perish. This statement is for a nation that will come at the end of time who will wash their extremities and wear a loincloth around their waists. They will plunge into the seas after their enemies. They will perform prayers that would have saved Noah's people from the flood if spoken then, and 'Ad's people would not have been destroyed by the wind, and Thamnd's would not have been destroyed by the shouting. In the name of God, His words are truth, while those of evil-doers shall perish.' The document then recounted another story. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was astonished at what was read to h i from it."
lessons: Divine truth is preserved across generations in unexpected ways, reassuring us that God's plan does not go unwitnessed.
The power of sincere prayer and worship is immeasurable—it could have averted the greatest historical catastrophes.
Astonishment in the face of divine signs is a mark of an open heart, not weakness.
practical_advice: When you encounter something that astonishes you spiritually, treat it as an invitation to deepen your reflection rather than dismiss it.
Commit to your prayers with the understanding that they carry a weight and power far beyond what you can see in the moment.
Seek out the spiritual heritage preserved in your family and community—wisdom passed down across generations can offer profound guidance.

title: Heraclius Views Pictures of the Prophets and Weeps Over His Own Choice
summary: When Abu Bakr sent Hisham b. al-As al-Umawi to Heraclius calling him to Islam, Heraclius displayed ancient pictures of the prophets from Adam to Muhammad and expressed his longing to leave his kingdom, but ultimately did not embrace Islam, causing Abu Bakr to weep for him.
story: We recounted, regarding the verse in swat al-A+af; "whom they fmd written down along with them in the Torah and the Gospel" (VII, v.157) the story of Hishiim h. alLCA$al-Umawi when (Aba Bakr) al-Siddiq sent him with a group of men to Heraclius, calling upon him to worship God, Almighty and Glorious is He. He related how Heraclius brought out for them pictures of the prophets in a piece of cloth, from Adam to Muhammad (SAAS). It showed how they had all looked. Then he related how, when he brought out the picture of Muhammad (SAAS) Heraclius stood up to show his respect for him. He then sat down again and gazed at it, contemplating it closely. The group then asked h i where he had obtained the picture and he responded, "Adam asked his Lord to show him all the prophets he had produced. And God did reveal their pictures to hi. They were kept in the treasury of Adam, peace be upon him, where the sun sets. And Dha al-Qprnayn (Alexander) took them out and gave them to Daniel." The account continues, "Heraclius then stated, '0 how I wish I could leave my kingdom and were a slave to the worst of your kings till I die.' He then dismissed us, giving us generous gifts, and we left." When the group returned to Aba Bakr and related to him what they had seen, how he had rewarded them, and what he had said, he wept and said, "Poor man! If God had wished good for him, He would have done so." Then he said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) related to us that they and the Jews would find among them the description of Muhammad (SAAS)."
lessons: Knowing the truth is not the same as accepting it; the hardest barrier to faith can be worldly attachment rather than intellectual doubt.
Compassion for those who recognize the truth but cannot act on it reflects true spiritual maturity, as shown by Abu Bakr's tears.
Divine guidance is accessible to all, but it requires a willingness to sacrifice power and prestige to follow it.
practical_advice: Reflect honestly on whether any worldly comfort or status is preventing you from making a change you know in your heart is right.
When you feel grief for someone who is spiritually lost, channel that grief into sincere prayer for them rather than judgment.
Consider what you would be willing to give up to follow your deepest convictions, and take one small step in that direction today.

title: Slaves of the Negus Recognize the Prophet Without Being Told
summary: Amr b. Umayya recounts how slaves gifted to him by the Negus recognized the Prophet Muhammad by sight alone, having known his description from writings, without anyone pointing him out.
story: Al-Umawi stated that 'Abd AllZh b. Ziyiid related to him from Ibn Ishaq who said that Ya'qab b. 'Ahd Alliih h. Jacfar h. 'Amr h. Umayya related to h i , from his father, from his grandfather 'Amr h. Umayya, who said, "I brought in some slaves from the Negus who had given them to me. They said, '0 CAmr, if we saw the Messenger of God, we would recognize him without you telling us.' Abti Bakr passed by, so I asked them, 'Is that he?' 'No,' they replied. Wmar went by and I asked, 'Is that he?' 'No,' they responded. We went inside the house and the Messenger of God (SAAS) went by and then they called out to me, '0 CAmr that is the Messenger of God (SAAS).' When I looked I saw it was he, yet no one had told them so. They recognized him from the writings they had."
lessons: Truth carries a light that is recognizable even to those who encounter it for the first time, because it resonates with something deep within the human soul.
The Prophet's description was preserved across cultures and peoples, showing that divine guidance transcends borders.
Sometimes recognition of what is true comes not from argument or proof but from an immediate, inner certainty.
practical_advice: Trust your deeper spiritual instincts when they point toward something good and true, even before you can fully explain why.
When you feel uncertain about a person or path, study their character and actions carefully—truth tends to announce itself.
Seek knowledge of your own tradition's descriptions of virtue so that you can recognize it when you encounter it in the world.

title: The Story of Sayf b. Dhu Yazan and His Foretelling of the Coming Prophet
summary: Two years after the birth of the Prophet Muhammad, a delegation of Arab leaders including Abd al-Muttalib visited the Himyarite king Sayf b. Dhu Yazan, who privately revealed to Abd al-Muttalib a prophecy from ancient hidden books foretelling the birth of a boy in Tihama named Muhammad who would bring leadership and primacy to his people until the Day of Judgement.
story: The ha&? Abii Bakr Muhammad b. Ja'far b. Sahl al-Khara'iti stated in his book Hawatifal-38nn (Calls of the Jinn) that 'All b. Harb related to him, quoting Ahmad b. W t h m a b. Hddm, quoting 'Amr b. Bakr - who was the son of Bakhr d-Qacnabr - from Ahmad h. al-@sim, from M4ammad b. al-SPib al-Kalbi, from Abii Sdih, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas who said that when when Sayfb. Dhii Yazan came to power over Abyssinia (Ibn al-Mundhir said that his name was al-Nu'msn b. Qays), that being two years after the birth of the Messenger of God (SAAS), a delegation of Arabs, including poets, went to congratulate and praise h i and to recognize his achievements. Among those who went to him was one delegation from Quraysh that included 'Abd al-Muftalib b. Hashim, Umayya b. 'Abd Shams, 'Abd All& b. Jud$n, and Khuwaylid b. Asad, along with other Quraysh leaders. They went to S a n 9 to see him, but he was in Ra's Ghumdzn to which Umayya b. Aba al-Salt made reference in the line of poetry: "Drink up, may you enjoy it, with your crown raised upon your head at Ra's Ghumdm, a home for you to stay in." The chamberlain went in to him and told the King where they were and he gave them permission to attend him. When 'Abd al-Mutr;llib arrived he ashed to speak with the King, who replied, "If you are a man who spe&s before hings, then I give you permission to do so now." 'Abd al-Muftalib responded, "God has placed you, 0 King, in a high, impregnable, lofty, and splendid position, having given you growth in a spot where your roots have developed strong and well, firmly estab lished, and eminently branched, in the most noble of lands, the best of origins. You are, may you be immune to curses, the King of the Arabs, their springtime that their land brings to growth, the head of the Arabs to which you give leadership, their pier upon which they depend, their refuge to which people have recourse. Your forebears were the best ever, and from them you came to us as the best successor. None with such forebears could fail, none perish with you as their successor. 0 King, we are the people of God's sanctuary, the custodians of His house. He who delighted you by removing the cares that oppressed us has brought us up to you, and so we are a delegation of congratulation, not of complaint." The King asked, "And who are you who speak?" He replied, "I am 'Abd al-Mu~alibh. HZshim." "Our sister's son?" "Yes." "Approach then." When he had done so the King addressed him and the delegation, saying, "Welcome indeed to you; may you enjoy your stay and find us generous hosts. The King has heard your speeches and of your kinship and accepted your petition. You are welcome at all times. You will he honoured as long as you stay, and awarded gifts when yon leave." The Arab delegation then went off to the guest quarters where they stayed a month having no access to the King but not being given permission to leave. He then again turned his attention fully to them and sent for 'Abd al-Mutralib with whom he sat alone, close by himself.
