He then said, "0 'Ahd al-Mut~alib,I am going to reveal to you a secret I know that I would not reveal to anyone else. But I believe you worthy of it and will tell you of it. Let you keep it to yourself until God makes His purpose known. For God will achieve His goal. I have found in the hidden book and the treasured knowledge we preserve and protect for ourselves alone certain information of vital and critical importance that affects the honour and virtue of mankind in general, your community in particular, and you in person." 'Abd al-Mu~alibasked, "0King, the like of you brings joy and good! What is it, O he for whom company after company of bedouins would sacrifice themselves?" He replied, "When a youth is born in T i h m a who has a sign upon him, a birthmark between his shoulders, he shall have leadership and through him you shall have primacy tiu Judgement Day." 'Abd al-Mu~alihexclaimed, "May you be immune to curses, I have been better rewarded than any visitor before! And were it not for the great dignity, honour and glory of the King, I would ask h i whose coming it is he is announcing such as to bring such joy to me." Ihn Dhii Yazan responded, "This is the time for his birth, or he may have already been born. His name is to be Muhammad. His father and his mother
lessons: Divine prophecy about the Prophet was preserved even among distant kings and in hidden ancient books, showing the universality of his mission.
The eloquence and dignity with which Abd al-Muttalib addressed the king demonstrates that representing one's community with honor opens doors that might otherwise remain closed.
Patience in waiting—as the delegation waited a full month—is often the price of a meaningful encounter with truth.
practical_advice: When you carry something precious within your family or community, nurture it quietly and with patience, trusting that its time will come.
Develop the skill of speaking with dignity and eloquence in difficult or high-stakes situations—preparation and character make the difference.
When you receive news that fills you with joy or hope, sit with it privately before sharing it widely, allowing it to settle and strengthen you.

title: Umar ibn al-Khattab Hears the Voice from the Calf's Belly Before Islam
summary: Before his conversion to Islam, Umar ibn al-Khattab was present near an idol when a voice emerged from a sacrificed calf's belly announcing the arrival of a Prophet who would declare there is no God but God.
story:
At that, Wmar said, addressing the people around him, 'I swear, I was once near an idol, before Islam, along with a group of Quraysh. An Arab had sacrificed a calf and we were waiting for h i to divide it up so we would have our share. Then I heard a voice louder than I had ever heard before coming out from the calfs belly. This was a month or so before Islam. The voice said, "0 Dharih, success is won! A man is crying out and saying, 'There is no God but God.'"' Ibn Hisham commented, 'Another version has the words as, "A man is crying out, his language eloquent, and saying, 'There is no God but God.'"' He went on, 'A scholar learned in poetry recited to me the lines, "I was surprised at the spirits and their grief and their gripping their cloth-blankets on their mounts / Heading for Mecca, seeking guidance, for the believing spirits are not like the impure ones."'

Wmar then said, 'Once we were in a quarter of Quraysh known by their name of the Dharih family. They had sacrificed a calf and the butcher was cutting it up when we heard a voice emitting from its stomach. We could not see anything (there), however. It said, "0 family of Dharih, success is won. A voice is crying out, its tongue eloquent, giving wimess that there is no God but God."' This account is abbreviated, as that of al-Bukm shows. They do substantiate that the one hearing the voice from the calf was Wmar b. d-Khanzb.
lessons: God prepares hearts for faith long before the moment of conversion; signs and warnings may arrive years before a person understands their meaning, teaching us to remain reflective about experiences we cannot immediately explain.
Even those who would later become great champions of truth were once immersed in falsehood and confusion — this is a reminder that no one's present state defines their ultimate destination.
The universe itself bore witness to the truth of the Prophet's mission; when even the unseen world announces divine guidance, it affirms that truth is not a human invention but a cosmic reality.
practical_advice: Reflect on moments from your past that seemed strange or inexplicable at the time — sometimes earlier experiences were preparing you for a clarity or conviction that only came later in life.
Do not judge your spiritual journey by how early or smoothly you arrived at faith; some of the greatest believers had the most turbulent and delayed awakenings.

When you encounter something that unsettles your worldview or points toward a deeper truth, resist the urge to dismiss it quickly — sit with it, as Umar's memory of the voice stayed with him until it found its full meaning.

title: Sawwad ibn Qarib's Conversion: The Soothsayer Who Followed His Channel's Call
summary: Sawwad ibn Qarib, a soothsayer, is visited three nights in a row by his channel (jinn) urging him to travel to Mecca and follow the Prophet Muhammad. He converts to Islam and later recounts his story to the assembled Muslims, who are overjoyed.
story: I was a soothsayer before Islam. One night when I was asleep, my channel came to me, kicked me and said, '0 Sawwad, listen to what I will tell you.' 'Go on,' I said. It then recited, 'I was surprised at the spirits and their concern, and how they gripped their blankets on their mounts Heading for Mecca seeking guidance, for those of them that believe are not like those that are evil. So travel to the best of Hiishim and raise your eyes to their head.' "But I went to sleep, not caring at all what he had said. Then he came back the second night, kicked me and said, 'Up you get, Sawwad b. @rib and listen to what I say.' 'Well let's hear it,' I said. He then recited, 'I was surprised at the spirits and their intentions and their gripping their saddlebags on their mounts Heading for Mecca,seeking guidance, for the honest spirits are not l i e the lying ones. So head for the linest man of Hashim, for their foremost men are not like their hindmost.' "His words did intrigue me, somewhat, hut I went to sleep. The third night he came again, kicked me and said, '0 Sawwad b. -rib, are you going to comprehend or not?' "'How?' I asked. He explained, 'A prophet has come forth in Mecca calling for worship of his Lord. Go and join him. Listen to what I tell you.' 'Let's hear it,' I said. Then he recited, 'I was surprised at the spirits and their fright, and how they gripped their cloth blankets to their mounts Heading for Mecca, seeking guidance, for the spirits who believe are unlike those who disbelieve. So travel to the best of Hshim, between their hills and rocks.' "So I realized that God wished me well. I went and got a striped garment I had, unsewed it, put it on and placed my feet in the saddle stirrups of my camel and rode off till I reached the Prophet (SAAS). He then suggested to me that I become a Muslim, and I did so. I told him what had happened, and he said, 'When the Muslims are all gathered, tell them this.' So when all were assembled, I got up and recited the following verses, 'My channel came to me after rest and sleep, and I never knew him to be untruthful; Three nights, and each time he would say, "A messenger has come to you out of Lu'ayy h. Ghalih." So I tucked up my waist-wrapper for travel, and my strong and swift she-camel carried me across the deserts, And I know that there is nothing besides God, and that you are safe from any conqueror And that you are the Messenger with closest access of all to God, 0 you born of the finest and best of men. So order us to do whatever you are told, 0 best messenger, even if what comes is such as to make the hair grey.' "The Muslims," he concluded, "were delighted with this."
lessons: Guidance can come through unexpected and repeated means; when a call to truth is persistent, it is worth heeding rather than dismissing.
Acting on divine guidance, even when one does not fully understand it, opens the door to profound transformation and spiritual fulfilment.
Sharing one's personal journey of faith with the community strengthens collective bonds and inspires others who may be on the same path.
practical_advice: When you receive a recurring sign, feeling, or inner nudge toward something beneficial, do not keep dismissing it — pause and reflect on what it may be pointing you toward.
If you feel called to make a significant life change, take the first courageous step, even if the full picture is not yet clear to you.
Once you have experienced a meaningful turning point in your life, consider sharing it with people you trust; your story may be exactly what someone else needs to hear.

title: Sawwad ibn Qarib and Umar: Confronting a Painful Past with Dignity
summary: When Umar ibn al-Khattab greets Sawwad ibn Qarib by referencing his soothsaying past, Sawwad reacts with dignified anger. Umar acknowledges his own darker past and asks Sawwad to recount his conversion story.
story: Muhammad b. d-Sa'ih d-Kalhi related this anecdote from his father, from Wmar b. Hafs. H e said that when Sawwad b. Qtrib came to 'Umar, the latter asked, "0 Sawwad b. Qtrib, what remains from your being a soothsayer?" He got angry and retorted, "0 Commander of the Faithful. I don't think you greeted any Arab like that before." When Wmar saw the anger in his face, he said, "Look Sawwad, the polytheism we were previously involved in was worse." Then he went on, "Tell me, Sawwad, that story I've so wanted to hear from you." "Certainly. Once I was tending some camels of mine at d-Sarat. One night when I was asleep a channel I had came to me, kicked me and said, 'Up you get, Sawwad b. @rib! A prophet has appeared in Tihsma who calls people to the truth and to the straight path.'" H e then related the story as given above, adding one last line to the poetry. It read: "And intercede for me on a day when no relative than you will he of any use for Sawwad b. Q~h." T h e Messenger of God (SAAS) told him: "Go to your people and speak these verses to them."
lessons: Even those in positions of authority can unknowingly cause pain through careless words; true leadership includes the humility to acknowledge this.
Every person carries a past they may not be proud of; compassion requires recognising that others' pasts do not define their present worth.
Responding to an insensitive remark with measured dignity, rather than silence or rage, preserves one's self-respect and invites genuine understanding.
practical_advice: If someone reduces you to a past identity you have grown beyond, calmly but firmly correct the framing — your transformation deserves to be acknowledged.
When you find yourself making assumptions about someone based on who they used to be, pause and ask yourself whether you are seeing them as they are now.
Practice reminding yourself that your past, however painful or shameful it feels, is part of a larger story of growth that does not have to define your future.

title: Mazin ibn al-Adab: The Idol Custodian Who Heard Truth from the Idol Itself
summary: Mazin ibn al-Adab, the custodian of an idol in Oman, hears a voice emanating from the idol twice, warning him of a new prophet and urging him to abandon idol worship. He breaks the idol and travels to accept Islam, then prays for relief from personal struggles.
story: Abii Nu'ayrn stated in his hook Dalz'il al-Nabumma (Signs of the Prophethood) that 'Ahd Allah h. Muhammad b. Jacfar related to him, quoting 'Abd al-R&man b. al-Hasan, quoting 'Ali b. Harb, quoting Abii al-Mundhir Hishzm b. Muhammad b. al-Salib, from his father, from 'Abd Allah al-Wmani, who said that they had a man called Mazin b. al-LCAdab who was custodian of an idol at a village called Samayz, in Wmm. Bana al-Samit revered this idol, along with Bana Hupna and Muhra, who were related to Main through his uncles on his mother's side, her name being Zaynab d. 'Abd All* b. RabFa b. Khuwayg, of Bana Namran. Main said, "One day we brought a ewe to the idol for slaughter, it being our sacrifice, and I heard a voice emanating from the idol saying, '0 Main, hear and rejoice. Goodness has emerged and evil been submerged. A prophet has been sent from Mudar with God's greatest religion. Abandon something carved from stone and you will be saved from scorching fire.' "I was extremely scared to hear this. "Then, a few days later, we sacriliced another ewe, and beard a voice from the idol say, 'Come to me, come close and hear what you cannot ignore. This is a prophet who has been sent; he has brought truth revealed. Believe in him so that you will avoid the heat of a fire that will flame, a fire set alight by the stone.'" Main continued, "This is amazing, I thought. And this is good intended for me. Then a man from Hijaz arrived, and I asked him what news there was back home. He replied, 'A man called Ahmad has emerged who teUs everyone who comes to him to respond to God's Messenger.'" "This news relates to what I heard, I thought. So I rushed to the idol and broke it into small pieces. I then mounted up and rode off to the Messenger of God (SAAS). God then opened my heart to Islam, which I accepted. I spoke the verses, 'I broke Yajnr into pieces, there now being a Lord through whom we would see false guidance and delusion, Through the Hshimite, who led us out of our error, his religion having been unknown to me. 0 rider, tell 'Amr and its brothers that I detest those who said, "My Lord is Y2jir,"' "I said, '0 Messenger of God, I'm a man who is crazy for pleasure, for women and wine. But now the years are pressing hard, the money is gone, the concubines have thinned out, and I have no son. Please pray to God to alleviate my suffering, to make us modest, and to grant me a son.' "The Prophet (SAAS) then spoke a prayer: '0 God, exchange his pleasureseeking with Qur'an readings, his immorality with morality, his sin and corruption with chastity, make him modest, and grant him a son.'" Main went on: "And so God alleviated my suffering and Wmam became fertile. I married four noble women, learned by heart a portion of the Qur'an, and God granted me my son Hayym."
lessons: Truth can reach a sincere heart through the most unexpected channels; when the heart is ready, no barrier — not even a stone idol — can prevent guidance from arriving.
Honest acknowledgment of one's own weaknesses and sins before God, without pretence, is the starting point of genuine transformation.
When one decisively abandons what is false and turns wholly toward truth, God replaces what was lost with something far greater and more enduring.
practical_advice: If you find yourself clinging to habits, beliefs, or objects that you know deep down are not serving you, consider what it would feel like to let them go entirely and take one concrete step in that direction today.
When approaching God or a trusted person for help, be honest about your specific struggles rather than presenting a polished version of yourself — honesty accelerates healing.
If you are going through a period of loss — money, relationships, health — reflect on whether something more meaningful might be waiting on the other side of this difficulty.

title: Mazin ibn al-Adab Faces His People's Rejection After Embracing Islam
summary: After returning to his people as a Muslim, Mazin ibn al-Adab is cursed, attacked by their poets, and marginalised, but he responds with patience and wisdom rather than retaliation, eventually witnessing his entire community embrace Islam.
story: He went on, "So when I came back to my people, they censured me and cursed me, and set one of their poets to attack me. And I realized that if I responded in like kind, I would only harm myself. "So I moved away from them. Then a great delegation of them came to me; I had been formerly in charge of their affairs. They told me, '0Cousin, we hate what has happened. If you can put it all aside, then return and be in charge of our affairs. And we'll not bother you or whatever is in your religion.' "So I did return with them and spoke the lines, 'Your hatred for us is bitter in taste, whereas our dislike for you, 0 my people, tasted like buttermilk; Time (fate) would not notice if your faults were laid out, but all of you when our faults are extolled are keenly aware; Our poet does not answer you back, while your poet is eloquent and flamboyant in cursing us. Towards you, you should know, there is in (our) hearts only irritation, while in yours there is hatred and loathing.'" Main ended: "Then God led them all to Islam."
lessons: Restraint in the face of social hostility is not weakness; it is a strategy that preserves dignity and keeps the door open for reconciliation.
When those who rejected you later seek to re-engage, responding with measured honesty rather than bitterness can become the foundation of genuine community healing.
Patience with those who oppose your convictions is often rewarded in ways that surpass what any argument or retaliation could achieve.
practical_advice: When someone attacks you verbally or socially, resist the urge to retaliate in kind; ask yourself what outcome you actually want from the situation and act in alignment with that.
If you have been ostracised for a belief or life change, give people time — hostility is often rooted in fear or confusion, not permanent hatred.
When you feel the impulse to defend yourself aggressively, try expressing your hurt through honest, calm words instead; it tends to disarm others more effectively than matching their energy.

title: The Woman of Madinah and Her Channel: First News of the Prophet Reaches Madinah
summary: A woman in Madinah who had a jinn companion in the form of a white bird learns from it that a Prophet has been sent in Mecca who has prohibited fornication, marking one of the earliest ways news of the Prophet's mission reached Madinah.
story: The hafiZ Abii NuWqayl, from Jzbir b. 'Abd Allxh, who said, "The first news of the mission of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina was as follows. A certain woman of Medina had a channel who would come to her in the form of a white bird. It alighted on a wall they had and she asked it, 'Why don't you come on down so that we can talk together and exchange news?' It replied, 'A Prophet has been sent in Mecca who has prohibited fornication. And so our pleasure is prevented.'" Al-W~qidi stated that 'Abd al-&m b. 'Abd al-'Aziz related to him, from al-Zuhri, from 'Ali b. al-Husayn, as follows, "The first news that arrived in Medina about the Messenger of God (SAAS) was that a woman called Fxcima had a channel (a male jinn) that came to her one day but stood on a wall. She asked it, 'Aren't you coming down?' 'No, a Messenger has been sent who has prohibited fornication,' he replied."
lessons: The arrival of truth disrupts comfortable arrangements that were built on what is forbidden; this disruption, though painful, is ultimately a mercy.
Divine guidance reaches communities through many channels, often before people are even aware they are being prepared to receive it.
A message that challenges one's way of life is not always an enemy — sometimes it is the beginning of a deeper and more meaningful existence.
practical_advice: When you encounter an idea, teaching, or experience that challenges a comfortable but harmful pattern in your life, try to sit with it rather than immediately dismissing it.
Consider that sometimes the disruption of something familiar is actually the arrival of something better — ask yourself what you might gain rather than only what you might lose.
Reflect on the sources from which you receive news, guidance, or influence in your life, and ask whether they are pointing you toward what is truly good for you.

title: The Jinn of Fatima Warns Against Fornication
summary: A female jinn companion refuses to visit a woman named Fatima, citing that a new Messenger has forbidden fornication. This account was among the first signs in Medina that the Prophet had received his mission.
story: The first news that arrived in Medina about the Messenger of God (SAAS) was that a woman called Fatima had a channel (a male jinn) that came to her one day but stood on a wall. She asked it, 'Aren't you coming down?' 'No, a Messenger has been sent who has prohibited fornication,' he replied. One of the early Muslims related the same account also. He mentioned that the woman's channel was called Ibn Ladhm, and stated that he had been absent from her a long time, and then when he did come she reproached him and he replied, 'I went to the Messenger and heard him forbidding fornication; so good-bye to you!'
lessons: The Prophet's mission brought immediate and far-reaching change — even unseen realms responded to his message.
Moral boundaries are not arbitrary restrictions but divine protections for human dignity and society.
Sometimes transformation begins with external signs before the heart fully understands — remain open to recognizing truth from unexpected sources.
practical_advice: When you encounter a boundary or restriction that feels difficult, pause and reflect on the deeper wisdom it may carry for your wellbeing.
If you are struggling with a habit that feels hard to leave, know that change is possible — even those distant from revelation responded to it.
Seek out people and environments that reinforce your values, just as the jinn distanced itself from what was now forbidden.

title: The Soothsayer of Syria Foretells the Prophet's Mission
summary: Uthman ibn Affan recounts how a female soothsayer in Syria was visited by her spirit companion who announced Ahmad's coming and declared that an unendurable situation had arisen, before Uthman returned to Mecca to find the Prophet had announced his mission.
story: Muhammad b. Salih related to him, from 'Asim b. Umar b. Qatada who said that Uthman b. Affan stated, 'We left in a camel caravan for Syria, before the Messenger of God (SAAS) received his mission. When we were on the outskirts of Syria where a female soothsayer lived, she stood in our way and said that her channel came to her and stopped at her door. So she asked him to come in, but he replied, "There's no way for that; Ahmad has come forth and an unendurable situation has arisen."' Uthman continued, 'Then I left and, upon returning to Mecca discovered that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had announced his mission in Mecca and was calling people to God the Almighty and Glorious.'
lessons: Divine truths announce themselves in ways that transcend ordinary understanding — the wise pay attention to signs around them.
The Prophet's coming was not a local event but one that reverberated across the known and unseen worlds.
Returning home after a journey may bring you face to face with a truth you were being prepared for.
practical_advice: When unexpected signs or events leave you unsettled, rather than dismissing them, reflect on whether they are pointing you toward a deeper truth.
Be open to the possibility that guidance can reach you through circumstances you did not anticipate.
If you feel a season of your life is ending and something new is beginning, trust the process — the 'unendurable situation' often precedes a profound transformation.

title: The Spirit of Sacira the Banu Asad Woman Is Driven Out by Islam
summary: A woman of Banu Asad named Sacira had a spirit companion who, upon learning that inspired voices were being banned with the coming of Islam, entered her chest in distress, causing her to lose her mind while crying out that Ahmad had forbidden fornication.
story: There was a woman of Banu Asad named Sacira who had a spirit companion. When it learned that inspired voices were not being allowed, he came to her and entered her chest where he made such an uproar that she lost her mind. From inside her chest he began shouting, 'Embracing has been banned. Companionship has been abolished! And an unendurable situation has arisen! Ahmad has forbidden fornication.'
lessons: The arrival of divine truth disrupts what was false or harmful, even when that disruption appears chaotic.
Not all distress signals an absence of God — sometimes upheaval accompanies the arrival of something far greater.
The proclamation of moral boundaries, though unwelcome to some forces, is ultimately a mercy for humanity.
practical_advice: When your life feels suddenly disrupted by a call toward better values, recognize that discomfort can be the beginning of spiritual growth.
If you are experiencing inner turmoil, consider whether part of you is resisting a truth you already know but have not yet accepted.
Trust that establishing healthy moral limits in your life, though it may feel like loss, creates space for something more genuine and lasting.

title: The Strange Boy of Tribe of Daws and the Jinn Who Foresaw the Prophet
summary: Mirdas b. Qays al-Sadusi recounts to the Prophet the story of a half-jinn boy born among the tribe of Daws who gave prophecies — sometimes accurate, sometimes not — and who ultimately burst into flame after declaring that heaven had been protected and the best of prophets had come forth in Mecca.
story: I was present with the Prophet (SAAS) when the subject of soothsaying came up and the changes that had occurred in it as a result of his coming forth. I said to him, 'O Messenger of God, some of that used to go on among us. I can tell you about a slave-girl we had called al-Khulasa about whom nothing but good was known until she came to us and said, "O tribe of Daws something very strange has happened to me; did you ever know anything except good (about me)?" We asked, "What's wrong?" She replied, "I was out with my sheep when a cloud overshadowed me, and I had some strange experience, some sense of a man with a woman. I was afraid I might be pregnant." Eventually came the time for her to give birth, and she did produce a boy who had lop-ears like those of a dog. He remained with us till the age when he would play with other boys. Then one day he jumped into the air, threw off his waist wrapper and began screaming out at the top of his voice, saying, "Ya wayla, ya wayla, ya awla ya awla, woe on the sheep, woe on Fahm who is the fire brand, there are horsemen, by God, behind the mountain path, and among them are youths who are handsome, noble."' "So we mounted up, our weapons ready, and asked, 'O you, woe to you, what do you see?' He asked, 'Is there some menstruating girl among you?' 'Who can find one for us?' we asked. An old sheikh of ours said, 'Yes, I have a daughter of a chaste mother.' We told him to bring her at once. So the sheikh brought the girl and (he) went up the mountain, telling her: 'Throw off your clothes and go out in front of them.' He then told our men, 'Follow on after her.' Then he said to one of our men called Ahmad b. Habis, 'O Ahmad b. Habis you get the first rider!' So Ahmad charged and struck the first rider, felling him. And they were defeated and we plundered them. Then we built a house over them which we named 'Dhu al-Khulqa'. And this boy never told us anything that did not come out as he said. "Until your mission, that is, O Messenger of God. One day he told us, 'O tribe of Daws, Banu al-Harith b. Ka'b has encamped.' So we rode off, with him telling us, 'Urge your horses on at speed, and stuff the enemy into their graves! Sweep them away in the morning and drink wine in the evening!' "We met them in battle, but they utterly defeated us. So when we returned to him we demanded, 'What's wrong with you? What have you done to us?' When we looked at him we saw his eyes all red and his ears standing straight up. He was absolutely furious, almost exploding with anger. Then he left. "We rode away and eventually forgave him, and nothing happened for a while. Then he summoned us and said, 'How would you like to go on a raid that will give you glory, and provide you fine booty so that you'll have a real treasure?' 'We need that more than anyone,' we replied. So he said, 'Then mount up.' We did so and asked, 'What next?' He replied: 'Attack Banu al-Harith b. Maslama!' Then he said, 'Stop.' So we did, and he said, 'Attack Fahm!' Then he went on, 'But you've no bad blood with them; attack Mudar; they have horses and cattle.' Then he said, 'No; attack Durayd b. al-Simma. They're few in number and under protection.' Then he said, 'No; instead go after (the tribe of) Ka'b b. Rabi'a, first, lodge them at the estate of 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a, then do battle with them!' "So we engaged them in battle but they defeated us, indeed humiliated us. So when we returned we asked him, 'Woe on you! What are you doing to us?' He replied, 'I just don't know; he who was telling me the truth is now lying to me. Imprison me in my house for three days, then come back.' "We did that. Then, after three days, we returned to him and let him out. And to our surprise he was like a stone of fire! He said, 'O Daws, heaven is protected! And the best of prophets has come forth!' 'Where?' we asked. 'In Mecca; and I am dying, so bury me on a mountain top, for I shall burst into flames. If you leave me (unburied) I will be a source of your shame. When you see me catch fire and flame, then throw three stones at me and with each one say, "In your name, O God." Then I will die down and go out.' "And he did die and burst into flame! We did what he had told us, throwing three stones and saying after each one 'O God, in your name!' And he did die down and go out. "Things went on as before until the pilgrims came and told us of your mission, O Messenger of God."
lessons: The sources of worldly guidance that people relied on before the Prophet became unreliable precisely because a greater and purer guidance had arrived.
False or mixed sources of knowledge cannot endure alongside divine revelation — truth ultimately displaces what is corrupt.
Even among those far from the Prophet, his mission sent tremors through all created things, preparing hearts to receive the message.
practical_advice: When the sources of direction you have relied upon begin to fail you, do not simply seek another worldly substitute — consider whether you are being called toward a deeper and truer guidance.
If you have experienced a series of setbacks where your plans and strategies have not worked, pause and reassess whether the foundation of your decision-making needs to change.
Trust that confusion and disruption in your life can be the prelude to recognizing a truth that has been seeking your attention.

title: The Heavenly Rider Between al-Zarqa and Mu'ta Announces the Prophet
summary: A caravan travelling to Syria reports seeing a mysterious horseman suspended between earth and sky who called out that Ahmad had come forth and put all spirits to flight, and upon returning home they found their people already discussing the Prophet's emergence in Mecca.
story: Al-Waqidi related to us, from his father, from the son of Abu Dhi'b, from Muslim b. Jandab, from al-Nadr b. Sufyan al-Hudhali, from his father, who said, 'We left for Syria on a camel caravan and when we were between al-Zarqa' and Mu'ta, having stopped there to rest for the night, along came a horseman, seemingly somehow between earth and sky, who called out, "Get up, all you who sleep! This is no time to rest! Ahmad has come forth and put all the spirits to flight!" We were terrified, though brave men we were, all having heard this. So we travelled back to our people and when we arrived we found them discussing a conflict in Mecca among Quraysh about a Prophet who had come forth from among them, from the Abd al-Muttalib family, whose name was Ahmad.'
lessons: Great events in the world of the spirit precede their unfolding in the material world — the awakening of the soul often comes before the facts are known.
Even brave and self-reliant people are humbled before the signs of God; courage does not protect one from awe.
The call to stop resting and wake up is a recurring spiritual summons — complacency is the enemy of readiness.
practical_advice: If you have been spiritually sleepy or going through life on autopilot, treat this as your call to wake up and pay attention to what truly matters.
Do not wait until the signs are undeniable before you begin reflecting on where you are heading spiritually.
When something shakes you out of your comfort — even something inexplicable — do not suppress that feeling; follow it toward deeper inquiry.

title: The Idol That Kept Falling on the Night of the Prophet's Birth
summary: A group of Qurayshi nobles including Waraqa ibn Nawfal gathered at an idol on the night of the Prophet's birth and found it repeatedly falling on its face; a great voice then rang out from within it proclaiming that a light had been born that made all idols fall and all Persian fires go out.
story: A group from Quraysh, including Waraqa b. Nawfal b. Asad b. Abd al-Uzza b. Qusayy, Zayd b. Amr b. Nufayl, Abd Allah b. Jahsh b. Ri'ah and Uthman b. al-Huwayrith, were once at a place where they had an idol around which they would assemble. That particular day they had set aside as a holiday each year. They would worship it and make sacrifice of camels to it and then feast, drink wine, and say prayers to it. When they went into it that night, they saw it to be overturned onto its face. They disliked that, so they took it and set it back up as before. Soon, however, it tumbled over violently, but they set it back straight as before. Again, for a third time, it fell over. Seeing this they were both amazed and very worried. Uthman b. al-Huwayrith said, 'Why is it turning upside down so much? This must be because something has happened.' This was occurring during the night when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was born. Uthman then spoke the following verses, 'O festival idol, around whom assemble the chiefs of delegations come from far and near, You turn upside down if defeated; tell us why. Is this unimportant, or do you turn in anger? If this is for some sin we have committed, we will acknowledge it and give up this sin, But if you have been defeated and turned over in abasement, then among idols you are no Lord, no master.' They then set the idol back in its position. When they did so, a great voice rang out from inside the idol, saying, 'It fell over because of a newly born, light from whom illuminated all the mountain sides of all the earth, in east and west, To which all the idols, without exception, fell down and at which the hearts of all the kings of the earth, without exception, trembled in fear. And the fires of all the Persians died down and went out, the king of the Persians himself spending a night of great anxiety. The soothsayers of the unknown having been abandoned by their spirits, from them no information coming, true or false. O Qusayy, come back from your error, come over to Islam and to the wide-open house.'
lessons: The birth of the Prophet was a cosmic event whose significance was felt across all realms — the world itself bore witness to a new era.
False objects of worship cannot maintain themselves in the presence of divine truth — what is hollow will eventually fall.
The response of the Qurayshi nobles shows that honest questioning — 'what has happened?' — is always the right instinct when confronted with signs.
practical_advice: When the things you have placed your trust in begin to fail you repeatedly, take it as an invitation to ask deeper questions about what you are truly relying upon.
Rather than propping up what keeps falling, consider whether the universe is redirecting you toward something truer and more stable.
The voice that came from an unexpected place carried guidance. Train yourself to listen for wisdom in unexpected forms and places.

title: Waraqa ibn Nawfal and His Companions Seek the True Religion of Abraham
summary: After witnessing the falling idol and hearing its voice on the night of the Prophet's birth, Waraqa ibn Nawfal and his companions concluded that their people had no religion and had abandoned the way of Abraham, prompting them to travel in search of the true Abrahamic faith.
story: When they heard this they conferred together in private and some of them said, 'Let us be truthful to one another and keep our secret among one another.' All of them said, 'Yes indeed!' Waraqa b. Nawfal told them, 'By God, you all know that your people have no religion. They have made an enormous mistake and abandoned Abraham's religion. What purpose is there in a stone you circumambulate that does not hear, nor see, nor can do any good or harm? O my people, adopt a religion!' The account proceeds, 'And so thereupon they left, travelling all over asking about the hanifiyya, the religion of Abraham, God bless him.' As for Waraqa b. Nawfal, he became a Christian, studied the Scriptures and so became very learned. Uthman b. al-Huwayrith travelled to the emperor, became a Christian and lived there in high esteem.
lessons: Honest self-examination — acknowledging that one's inherited practices may be wrong — is a mark of sincere courage and intellectual integrity.
The search for truth sometimes requires leaving behind the familiar and travelling into the unknown.
Even before the Prophet's formal call, God was preparing certain hearts to be ready for the message through their genuine longing for truth.
practical_advice: If you sense that the beliefs or habits you inherited no longer hold up under honest examination, do not be afraid to honestly question them — that discomfort is the beginning of clarity.
Surround yourself with companions who encourage truthfulness, especially when the truth is inconvenient, as Waraqa and his group committed to honesty with one another.
The search for meaning and true direction is noble — do not give up on it even if the path is long and uncertain.

title: Zayd b. Amr b. Nufayl and the Monk's Prophecy
summary: Zayd b. Amr b. Nufayl, after being imprisoned and then escaping, travelled to al-Raqqa where a monk told him a prophet was soon to come from his land — but Zayd was killed on his way home and never witnessed the prophethood.
story: Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl tried to leave but was gaoled. Eventually, however, he did leave, travelling far and wide till he reached al-Raqqa, in the Arabian peninsula. There he met a learned monk whom he told what he wanted to know. Then the monk said, 'You are seeking a religion to which you won't find anyone to lead you. But the time is near for a prophet who will come forth from your land and who will be sent with the hanifiyya religion.' When he was told this Zayd headed back for Mecca but the Lakhmites attacked and killed him.
lessons: Some people seek the truth with their whole lives but do not live to see its fulfilment — their effort is not wasted
God's plan operates on a timeline beyond human understanding, and sincere seekers are honoured even if they do not reach the destination
The tragedy of dying before witnessing what one hoped for reminds us to act with urgency and not delay in embracing truth when it arrives
practical_advice: Do not be discouraged when your search for meaning is interrupted by obstacles — persevere through them
When you receive hopeful signs or guidance, act on them promptly rather than postponing
Reflect on the lives of those who sought truth before you as a source of inspiration during your own difficult seasons

title: Al-Abbas b. Mirdas: The Idol Speaks Against Itself
summary: Al-Abbas b. Mirdas recounts how a supernatural vision and a voice from his idol Dimar proclaimed the advent of the Prophet Muhammad and renounced itself, leading al-Abbas and 300 men to travel to Madinah and accept Islam.
story: Al-Khara'iti stated that Ahmad b. Ishaq b. Salih Abu Bakr al-Warraq related to him, quoting 'Amr b. Uthman, quoting his father, quoting 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-'Aziz, quoting Muhammad b. 'Abd al-'Aziz, from al-Zuhri, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Anas al-Sulami, from al-'Abbas b. Mirdas who said he was one day busy impregnating a milch-camel at midday when a white dove appeared before him with a rider on its back all dressed in clothing as white as milk. The rider said to him, "O 'Abbas b. Mirdas, don't you see that the heavens have assembled their guards, that war has choked on its own breath, that the mounts have their riding blankets saddled, and that there (has come) he who brought piety and devotion on Monday, the night of the third day, the master of the furthest-riding camel?" Al-'Abbas then said, "So I returned in a fright, very scared by what I had seen and heard. Then I went to an idol we had called al-Dimar that we used to worship; it would address us from inside its belly. I swept up all around it, anointed it and kissed it, and then a voice sounded from inside it saying, 'Tell all the tribes of Sulaym that al-Dimar is gone, and that the people of the mosque have triumphed. Al-Dimar is dead, though once he was worshipped, before prayer with the Prophet Muhammad. He who inherited the prophethood and the right path following the son of Mary is from Quraysh, rightly guided.'" He continued, "So off I went until I reached my people and told them what had happened. Then I left, along with 300 of my men of Banu Haritha, to join the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was at Medina. We entered the mosque and when the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw me he said, 'O 'Abbas, how did your acceptance of Islam come about?' I then told him the story. He was delighted, and thereupon I and my men all adopted Islam."
lessons: God can use unexpected means — even the symbols of falsehood — to guide people toward truth
When clarity arrives after confusion, acting on it decisively can transform not just one individual but an entire community
The willingness to abandon deeply held practices when truth becomes clear is a mark of courageous faith
practical_advice: When you experience a moment of spiritual clarity or awakening, do not dismiss it — take time to reflect on its meaning
Be willing to let go of beliefs or habits that you recognise as false, even if they have been part of your life for a long time
Sharing your personal journey toward faith with others can inspire collective transformation

title: Al-Abbas b. Mirdas: Second Account of His Conversion
summary: In a second narration, al-Abbas b. Mirdas describes hearing a voice from his idol Dimar after the Battle of the Trench, then receiving a vision of a rider announcing the Prophet, after which he rode to the Prophet, pledged allegiance, and burned the idol.
story: Abu Nu'aym related this in the al-Dala'il (The Signs) from an account of Abu Bakr b. Abi 'Asim, from 'Amr b. Uthman. He then recounted it again through al-Asma'i, who said, that al-Wassafi related to him, from Mansur b. al-Mu'tamir, from Qabisat b. 'Amr b. Ishaq al-Khuza'i, from al-'Abbas b. Mirdas al-Sulami, who said, "My adoption of Islam first came about this way. My father Mirdas, when near death, recommended to me an idol named Dimar he had. So I placed it in a building and established a visitation time, once each day. Now when the Prophet (SAAS) came forth, I heard a voice calling out through the night that scared me. So I hurried to Dimar seeking its help and heard a voice coming from its belly that said, 'Tell to the tribe of all Sulaym that al-Anis (the close friend) is gone and that the people of the mosque live on. Dimar has perished, and he was once worshipped, before the Scripture (was sent) to the Prophet Muhammad. He who has inherited the prophethood and the right path, after the son of Mary, is from Quraysh, rightly guided.'" He went on, "I kept this from my people. After they had returned from the battle (of the siege of Medina) I was out with my herd on the side of the ravine at Dhat 'Irq, asleep, when I heard a voice. Then I saw a man riding on a dove's wing and saying, 'The light appeared on the night before Tuesday along with (the birth of) the master of the she-camel named al-'Adba' at the house of Banu al-'Anqa'.' And another voice, from the left, replied, 'Tell the disheartened spirits that the mount has put on its saddle blankets, that heaven has been protected by its guards.' I jumped up in fright at this and knew that Muhammad was a messenger. So I rode my mare as fast as I could till I reached him and pledged him my allegiance. Then I went off, burned Dimar in fire, returned again to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and recited to him the following verses: 'By your life, by my making Dimar as partner with the Lord of the worlds And by my leaving the Messenger of God with the Aws tribe around him, those being his helpers; I am like someone who left the easy ground and sought out the difficult one, to find his path into the most difficult of matters, So I believed in God, whose servant I am, and opposed those who have begun seeking destruction. And I turned my face towards Mecca, seeking to declare allegiance to the blessed Prophet of the most noble men, A Prophet who came to us after Jesus, with a message of truth in which there is the conclusive word. Entrusted with the Qur'an, the first Intercessor and the first Messenger to respond to the angels, He restored the bonds of Islam after they had been broken, making them firm and establishing the rituals. I meant you, O best man of all the world; you have been situated at the top of all heights and possessed all eminence and honour. You are the purified one of Quraysh; when they fade away you will remain blessed for the ages. When the two tribes Ka'b and Malik trace their lineage we find you of pure descent, and your fertile women too.'"
lessons: Fear can be a doorway to awakening — what frightens us may be guiding us toward truth
Burning one's idol — literally or metaphorically — is a powerful act of commitment to a new path
Expressing gratitude and devotion through poetry and words reinforces the transformation of the heart
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually unsettled or frightened, consider whether that discomfort might be prompting you toward a truth you have been avoiding
Mark important turning points in your life with a decisive act that symbolises your commitment to change
Express your gratitude for guidance — whether through prayer, journaling, or spoken words — to solidify your inner transformation

title: The Men of Khath'am: The Idol's Voice Announces the Prophet
summary: Men of the Khath'am tribe heard a voice emerge from their idol announcing the coming of the Prophet Muhammad with Islam and commanding them to abandon idol worship, which led them to seek out the Prophet and accept Islam.
story: Al-Khara'iti stated that 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad al-Balawi, in Egypt, related to him, quoting 'Umara b. Zayd, quoting Ishaq b. Bishr and Salama b. al-Fadl, from Muhammad b. Ishaq who said that it was related to him by a sheikh from among the Companions whose name was 'Abd Allah b. Mahmud, from the family of Muhammad b. Maslama, that he said that he was informed that men of Khath'am used to say, "This is what drew us to Islam. We used to be a people who worshipped idols. One day we were at an idol of ours when a group came to seek the idol's advice in settling a dispute among them. Suddenly a voice called out to them: 'O people possessed of bodies, old men and young alike, what are you with your perplexed minds to rely on idols for judgements? Are all of you in confusion, sleeping, or do you not see what I see: a shining light, clearing up the darkness, has emerged to view from Tihama? That is a prophet, Lord of mankind, who has brought Islam after disbelief. The All-Merciful has honoured him by making him a leader, and a messenger whose words are truthful. The most just of all judges, he commands prayer and fasting And piety and reconciliation and drives people away from evil. And from immorality, idol worship, and sin; he is from Hashim among the elite of leaders Announcing (his mission) in the sacred land.' And when we heard this, we broke away from the idol, came to the Prophet (SAAS) and accepted Islam."
lessons: Truth can penetrate even the symbols of falsehood to reach those who have sincere hearts
When a whole community hears the same call to truth, collective action toward goodness becomes possible
The qualities attributed to the Prophet in the announcement — justice, piety, reconciliation — are timeless guides for personal conduct
practical_advice: When you encounter a message that convicts your heart, do not rationalise it away — allow it to move you toward change
Look for communal moments of spiritual awareness as opportunities to encourage one another toward what is right
Reflect on the qualities of justice, truthfulness, and reconciliation as personal goals to cultivate in your daily life

title: Rafi' b. Umayr al-Ta'i: The Jinn Protect the Prophet's Camel
summary: Rafi' b. Umayr, a renowned tracker of Banu Tamim, recounts a series of disturbing dreams in which a young jinn threatened his camel, only to be restrained by an elder jinn, leading Rafi' to recognise the prophethood of Muhammad.
story: Al-Khara'iti stated that 'Abd Allah al-Balawi related to him, quoting 'Umara, quoting 'Ubayd Allah b. al-'Ala', quoting Muhammad b. Akhar, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, that there was a man from the Banu Tamim called Rafi' b. Umayr, a highly skilled tracker, the best of all men in travelling at night, and the most intrepid in danger. The Arabs therefore used to call him "Du'mus of the Arabs", a name implying his skill in following a trail and his bravery. He recounted how he first adopted Islam, as follows, "One night I was travelling over deep sand when I felt very sleepy. So I dismounted, tied my camel down, rested my head on its leg and went to sleep. Before falling asleep I said a prayer: 'I seek protection from the master (spirit) of this valley against being harmed or disturbed by evil spirits.' In my sleep I saw a young man creeping up on my camel, a spearhead in his hand with which he was going to stab her in the throat. At that I awoke in alarm and, though I looked in all directions, I could see nothing. I told myself it was just a dream. So I went back into a light sleep and saw the same as I had before. Again I awoke, walked all around my camel but saw nothing. Once more I slept, saw the same and awoke to find my camel trembling. Again I slept, saw the vision once more and awoke to find my camel in a disturbed state. I turned around and there was a young man just like I had seen in my dream, carrying a spearhead in his hand which was being restrained by an old man who was reciting: 'O Malik b. Muhalhil b. Ditha, easy does it, my mantle and waist-wrapper be your ransom, Leave the human's camel alone, don't harm it and choose instead whatever of my bulls you want. You're showing me a side of yourself I never knew, not to honour my relationship to you and my honour. Going up to him with a poisoned spearhead! Woe to you for doing so, O Abu al-Ghaffar. If it were not for shame and your family being neighbours, I'd tell you things of me I've not disclosed.' After he had spoken thus, the young man responded: 'Did you want to raise and drop our reputation for no contemptible action, O Abu al-Sara? They never in the past had a lord among them; it is Banu al-Akhyar who are best. So go your way, O Mu'akbar; however, the protector was Muhalhil b. Ditha.' While they were thus disputing three bulls emerged from the wilderness and the old man said to the young one, 'Go on now, nephew, just take any one of these you want in compensation for the camel of my human friend here.'"
lessons: The unseen world operates according to its own order, and humans are not without protection even in vulnerability
Recurring signs or dreams that disturb us may carry a message worth taking seriously
The courage to sit with fear and seek its meaning, rather than fleeing from it, is a form of spiritual maturity
practical_advice: When you experience repeated disturbing thoughts or dreams, pause to reflect on whether they are drawing your attention to something important in your life
Seeking protection through prayer before resting or facing uncertainty is a deeply human and spiritually grounding practice
Do not dismiss strange or unsettling experiences immediately — give yourself space to process what they might mean for your path

title: The Man Who Sought Refuge in God: The Story of the Traveler and the Jinn
summary: A traveler encounters a dispute between an old man and a young man over a camel, is advised by the old man to seek refuge in God the Lord of Muhammad when afraid, and upon reaching Medina, the Prophet tells him his story before he can speak, leading him to accept Islam.
story: not disclosed.' "After he had spoken thus, the young man responded: 'Did you want to raise and drop our reputation for no contemptible action, 0 Abii al-%a? They never in the past had a lord among them; it is Bann al-Akhy2r who are best. So go your way, 0 Mu'akbar; however, the protector was Muhalhil b. Ditha.' "While they were thus disputing three hulls emerged from the wilderness and the old man said to the young one, 'Go on now, nephew, just take any one of these you want in compensation for the camel of my human friend here.' So the young man did take one bull and left with it. Then the old man turned to me and said, 'When you go down into some valley or other and are afraid, then say, "I take refuge in God, the Lord of Muhammad, from the terror of this valley." Don't seek the protection of any spirit because they're all finished.' I asked him, 'Who is this Muhammad?' He replied, 'He is an Arab prophet, neither of the east nor of the west, who was sent down on a Monday.' 'Where does he live?' I asked. 'Yathrih, where there are palm trees', he replied. So I rode off on my mount as soon as dawn broke and rode on and on till I entered Medina (i.e. Yathrib). The Messenger of God (SAAS) saw me and he told me my story before I told him anything about it! He invited me into Islam, and I did accept Islam." Sa'id b. Jubayr commented, "We used to think that it was about him that God revealed the words, 'There were human men who used to seek protection from spirit men, and so made them more malicious'" (siirat al-Jim, LXXII, v.6).
lessons: True refuge and protection come from God alone, not from created beings or spirits. The old man's advice to invoke God's name illustrates that divine protection supersedes all earthly or supernatural powers.

When one is guided to truth through unexpected encounters, that guidance should not be dismissed. The traveler's chance meeting led him ultimately to the Prophet and to Islam, showing that God's guidance can arrive through any means.
The Prophet's miraculous knowledge of the traveler's story before being told demonstrates the divine support granted to him, reinforcing that trust in the Prophet's message is well-founded.
practical_advice: When you feel fearful or overwhelmed in an unfamiliar situation, pause and turn to God in supplication. Saying a prayer or dhikr (remembrance of God) can ground you and remind you that ultimate protection belongs to Him alone.
Remain open to guidance that comes from unexpected sources or moments in life. Sometimes a chance conversation or encounter carries the very lesson or direction your heart has been searching for.

If you are confused about your spiritual path, seek out knowledge actively, as the traveler did by asking 'Who is this Muhammad?' Asking questions and following through on the answers is itself a form of courage and growth.

title: Ali's Invocation of Daniel Against the Jinn in the Well
summary: The Prophet sent Ali to fetch water from a well, but jinn tried to prevent him by cutting the bucket; Ali descended to confront them, invoking protection through the name of Daniel.
story: Al-Balawi recounted, from W m a a b. Zayd, from Ibrahim b. Sa%, from Muhammad b. Ishaq who said that Yahya b. 'Abd All& b. al-H%ith related to him, from his father, from Ibn (Ahbas, the story of 'Ali's killing the jznn in the well which had a flag that was at al-Juhfa. This happened when the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him to get water; they (thejmn) wanted to prevent him and so they cut up the bucket. He then went down to them; this is a long and very objectionable story. But God knows best.
lessons: Obstacles placed in the path of righteous service should not cause retreat. Ali's willingness to descend into the well despite the danger exemplifies that those who serve God's cause press forward even when opposed.
Relying on the names and blessings of the righteous — as taught by one's tradition — is a permissible means of seeking God's protection during moments of danger.
Even extraordinary challenges in carrying out simple acts of service are recorded by God. No effort made sincerely for the sake of God goes unnoticed or unrewarded.
practical_advice: When a task you are trying to accomplish is repeatedly blocked, do not immediately assume it is a sign to quit. Reflect on whether perseverance and a renewed call upon God might be what is needed.
In moments of sudden fear or danger, remembering the names and examples of the righteous — prophets, companions, those known for their piety — can serve as a spiritual anchor that steadies the heart.
Seek knowledge of your tradition's prayers and protective supplications so that in difficult moments they are already memorized and ready to use, rather than having to search for them under stress.

title: The Companions Discuss the Virtues of the Quran in the Assembly of Umar
summary: In an assembly with Umar ibn al-Khattab, the Companions discuss which Quranic verses are most virtuous, with Ali championing Ayat al-Kursi for its seventy words each carrying a blessing.
story: Al-Kharz'iti stated that it was related to him by Aba al-IJaith Muhammad b. Muv'ab the Damascene and others, and also by S u l a w n , son of the daughter of Sharahbil the Damascene, and by 'Abd al-Quddiis b. al-Hajjaj, as by Khaid b. Sa'id, from al-Sha'bi, from a man who said that he was once attending an assembly with Wmar b. al-Kha~zb. There also present were a group of the Companions of the Prophet (SAAS), who were discussing the virtues of the Qur'a. One of them referred to the final verses of slirat aJ-NahJ (XVI). Another suggested slirat Ya Sin (XXXVI). 'Ali said, "How about the Ayat al-Kursi? ('The Throne Verse', slirat al-Baqara, 11, v.255). It has 70 words, each one of which has a blessing." One of those present was 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib who was never lost for a comment. He asked, "Where do you stand regarding the invocation, 'In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent?'"
lessons: Gathering with other believers to reflect on and discuss the Quran elevates the soul and draws one closer to God. The Companions modeled how intellectual and spiritual conversation can be a form of worship.

Every portion of the Quran carries distinct blessings and wisdom. Familiarity with different passages and their virtues allows a believer to draw upon the right spiritual remedy for each situation in life.

The question raised about the Basmala — 'In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent' — reminds us that even the briefest utterance of God's name carries immense spiritual weight and should never be taken lightly.
practical_advice: Set aside time regularly to sit with others — family, friends, or community members — specifically to reflect on Quranic verses and what they mean in your daily life. This practice builds both knowledge and emotional connection to your faith.

Memorize Ayat al-Kursi and make it a daily habit, reciting it after each prayer and before sleep, as the tradition recommends. Its seventy words of blessing can serve as a powerful source of calm and security.

Begin every meaningful action — a conversation, a task, a journey — with the Basmala. This small act of remembrance reorients your intention toward God and can transform ordinary moments into acts of worship.

title: Amr ibn Ma'di Karib and the Sheikh of the Valley: The Power of the Basmala
summary: Before Islam, the warrior Amr ibn Ma'di Karib repeatedly attacked an old sheikh in the wilderness, only to be subdued each time by the sheikh's invocation of the Basmala; after treacherously killing the sheikh, Amr is overcome with regret and recites verses of remorse.
story: Wmar then asked, "Tell us an anecdote, 0 Abii Thawr?" (addressing 'Amr). He responded, "One time, before Islam, I felt terribly hungry, so I urged on my horse into the wilderness, but all I found were some ostrich eggs. As I went further I came across a bedouin sheikh in a tent with a girl beside him who was as beautiful as the rising sun. He also had some young goats. I addressed hi, saying, 'Surrender, may your mother be bereft of you!' He raised his head to me and replied, 'Now lad, if you need hospitality, then dismount. If you want help, I'll help you.' But I replied, 'Surrender!' He then recited the following verses, 'We offered you hospitality out of kindness on our part, but you were not converted, in ignorance, acnng a s the unlucky do. You came and insulted us and were rude. And your throat would he slashed before you get what you hoped to get from my daughter.'" He went on: "And then all of a sudden he leapt at me, saying, 'In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Beneficent.' Suddenly I felt as if I were beneath him in submission. He then asked, 'Well, shall I kill yon or let you go?' 'Let me go,' I said. Then he stood back from me. But at that point my spirit persuaded me to try again, so I said, 'Surrender! May your mother he bereft of you!' He responded with the verses: 'In the name of God and the All-Merciful, we were victorious there, and by the Most Beneficent it was we conquered. But the determination of a haughty man will do no good when once we go forth to battle.' "He then leapt at me, while I was there under him in suhmission. 'Well,' he said, 'should I kill you or let you go?' 'Let me go,' I replied. He stood hack from me and I hurried some small distance away. But then I said to myself, '0 'Amr, can this old sheikh really defeat you? By God, better you he dead than alive.' So I went back to h i and said again, 'Surrender! May your mother be bereft of you!' Again he leapt at me, saying, 'In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent,' and again I suddenly found myself there in submission beneath him. Again he asked, 'Should I kill you or let you go?' Again I replied, 'Let me go.' 'Impossible! Now girl, bring me the sharp dagger.' She brought it to him and he lopped off my forelock! When the Arabs defeated a man and lopped off his forelock they would he placing him into servitude. So I stayed with him for a time as his servant. He then said to me, '0 'Amr, I want you to ride out into the desert with me. I have fear of you yet am fully confident in the words, "In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent."' "So off we went till we came to a valley that was overgrown, awesome and spirit-ridden. He cried out at the top of his voice, 'In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent!' Immediately every bird there left its cover and flew off. He repeated the same cry and every single lion there fled its den. Again he made the cry and all of a sudden there before us was an Abyssinian who had come out at us from the valley, standing there like a tall palm tree. The sheikh said to me, '0 'Amr, when you see us both go off alone together, then say, "May my master overcome him through the words 'In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent."" But when I saw them alone together I said, 'May my master overcome him through al-Lat and al-'Uzza.' So the sheikh did nothing. He then came hack to me and said, 'You realize you disobeyed me?' 'Yes,' I replied, 'hut I won't do it again.' He then said, 'When you see us alone together, then say, "May my master overcome him through the words 'In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent."' 'All right,' I agreed. So when I saw them go off alone together I did say, 'May my master overcome him through (the words) "In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent."' The sheikh then threw himself upon the Abyssinian, sliced at him with his sword, slit his stomach open and drew out from his insides something in the form of a black candle. He then said, '0 'Amr, this is his deceit and his rancour.' He then asked me, 'Do you know who this girl is?' 'No,' I answered. 'This', he replied, 'is al-F%ica, daughter of al-Salil al-Jurhumi, a leader of the demon spirits. Those are all her people, her relatives. Every year one man of them makes an attack on me, but God helps me defeat him through the words, "In the name of God, the most Merciful, themost Beneficent."' H e then said, 'You saw how I treated that Abyssinian. Now I'm really hungry, so get me something to eat.' I rode my mount off into the desert but only found some ostrich eggs. These I brought back for him hut found him asleep. Beneath his head there was something that looked like a piece of wood. When I drew it out it was a sword, its size being one hand-span by seven. I struck his legs off with one blow which severed them along with his feet. H e sat up on his haunches and cried out, 'May God destroy you! How treacherous you are, traitor!'" Wmar then asked, "What did you do then?" "Well," 'Amr replied, "I just kept hitting him till I had chopped him up into little hits." H e remained silent in sorrow a while, then went on, reciting the verses: "Through deceit and from close by you took Islam's brother; I never heard the like in Arabs of former times. (Even) the non-Arabs would be shamed in their honour to do as you did; may you perish for what you did to this able Lord. Yet I am amazed that you were able to murder him; and that you did not care how (well) he treated you when you sinned. He was a chief who had forgiven you often and whose hands clung to you at his place of death. Yet if I had been acting during Islam as they have done to polytheists and those of the cross, Then you would have attained by my justice a terrible fate such as to make its sufferer call out in grief and woe." "And what happened to the slave girl?" he asked. 'Amr replied, "I then went to her. When she saw me, she said, 'What happened to the sheikh?' 'The Abyssinian killed him,' I replied. 'You lie!' she cried. 'You killed him, by betraying him!' Then she recited the verses: '0eyes, weep well for the brave warrior, then weep copious droplets more; Do not tire of weeping, for fate has deceived you (in taking) a trustworthy man who had truth and was patient. A man who was pious, stately and wise, a man worthy of glory on Judgement Day. How sad I am that you let 'Amr live, for time has given you up to your fate. By my life, if you had not sought him out in betrayal, you would have faced a lion as sharp as a sword.' "What she said greatly angered me so I drew my sword and entered the tent to kill her. But in the tent I found no one. So I herded up the cattle and came home." This is a curious anecdote. It is obvious that the sheikh was a demon spirit, one of those that adopted Islam and learned the Q u r ' a , one thing he used being the invocation "In the name of God the most Merciful, the most Beneficent." He would seek protection by speaking it.
lessons: The Basmala — 'In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Beneficent' — carries a spiritual power that no force of arrogance or worldly strength can overcome. The sheikh's repeated victories illustrate that sincere reliance on God's name is the greatest protection.

Betrayal of those who have shown us mercy and forgiveness is among the gravest of moral failures. Amr's own verses of regret after the act show that the conscience ultimately bears witness against us even when no one else holds us accountable.

Pride and the refusal to accept that one has been humbled can drive a person to repeat the same destructive behavior multiple times. Amr attacked the sheikh three times despite being subdued each time, showing how ego can override reason and gratitude.
practical_advice: When you feel your pride pushing you toward a harmful or aggressive action — particularly in a conflict where you have already been shown mercy — pause and recognize that pattern. Ask yourself: is this ego or genuine need driving me?

Make the Basmala not merely a ritual phrase but a genuine orientation of the heart before any action. Saying it with awareness and sincerity can shift your internal state from reactivity or fear to calm trust in God.

If you have wronged someone who showed you kindness, do not let guilt paralyze you indefinitely. Acknowledge the wrong honestly, as Amr did through his verses of remorse, and let that acknowledgment become the foundation for changed behavior going forward.

title: The Demon Spirit Sheikh in the Tent
summary: A man encounters a mysterious sheikh in a tent who recites Quranic invocations and disappears, later understood to be a demon spirit that had adopted Islam.
story: a sword.' "What she said greatly angered me so I drew my sword and entered the tent to kill her. But in the tent I found no one. So I herded up the cattle and came home." This is a curious anecdote. It is obvious that the sheikh was a demon spirit, one of those that adopted Islam and learned the Qur'a, one thing he used being the invocation "In the name of God the most Merciful, the most Beneficent." He would seek protection by speaking it.
lessons: Even in moments of confusion and fear, rash action can lead to regret; patience and discernment are virtues.
Not everything that appears righteous on the surface is trustworthy; wisdom requires looking beyond appearances.
Reliance on God's name and remembrance offers spiritual protection against deception and harm.
practical_advice: When feeling confused or deceived by a situation, pause before reacting in anger and seek clarity first.
Develop discernment by grounding yourself in knowledge and seeking counsel from trustworthy people.
Make remembrance of God (dhikr) a daily practice to build spiritual resilience against confusion and fear.

title: Zayd ibn Amr and Waraqa ibn Nawfal Tell the Negus of Signs at the Prophet's Birth
summary: Zayd ibn Amr and Waraqa ibn Nawfal visit the Negus of Abyssinia and recount miraculous signs they witnessed at the time of the Prophet's birth, including voices from idols, a winged figure of light, and a vision experienced by the Negus himself.
story: Al-Khara'iti stated that 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad al-Balawi related to him, quoting 'Umara b. Zayd, saying that 'Abd Allah b. al-'Ala' related to him, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from his grandmother Asma', daughter of Abu Bakr, who said, "Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl and Waraqa b. Nawfal used to tell how they went to the Negus after the retreat of Abraha from Mecca. They would say, 'When we went in to him, he told us, "O you two men of Quraysh, tell me truthfully. Was there born among you a boy whose father wanted to sacrifice him and who had divining arrows cast about him, but who was saved and had many camels sacrificed for him?" "'Yes,' we replied. 'Do you have knowledge of him then, and of what became of him?' 'We replied, 'He married a woman named Amina, daughter of Wahb; he left her pregnant then went away.' 'Do you know if a child was born or not?' "Waraqa b. Nawfal replied, 'I will tell you, O King. I had spent a night at an idol of ours around which we used to circumambulate and worship. Suddenly I heard a voice emanating from its belly that said, "The Prophet is born and so the kings are abased, falsehood is averted and polytheism is dead." "'The idol thereupon tipped over on to its face.' "Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl then spoke, 'I had a similar experience, O King.' 'Let's hear it,' he was told. He related as follows, 'On just such a night as he told of, I left my people, who were talking about Amina's being pregnant, and went to Mt. Abu Qubays, wanting to be alone there because of something that was bothering me. Suddenly I saw a man descend from the skies who had two green wings. He stood atop Mt. Abu Qubays, then looked down over Mecca and said, "Satan is humiliated and the idols are cast down. The 'Trustworthy One' is born." He then spread out a cloth he had with him and floated away on it to the east and west. I saw that he had lit up all beneath the sky and a light was shining so brightly that it almost blinded me. I was awed by what I had seen. The man who had called out flapped his wings and flew down to the ka'ba, a light shining from him that illuminated all Tiba. He then spoke, "The earth is aflame and has reached its springtime." He then gestured towards the idols that were in the ka'ba and they all fell down.' "The Negus said, 'Let me tell you now what happened to me. I was asleep that same night you mentioned, alone in a pavilion, when out from the ground came a neck and a head which said, "Woe has befallen the warriors with elephants! flocks of birds cast clay stones at them. The criminal aggressor al-Ashram has perished! And the ummi prophet has been born, he from Mecca, from the sacred quarter. Whoever responds to him will be glad; whoever scorns him will be sad." He then entered the earth and was gone. I tried to scream, but couldn't say a word. I then tried to get up, but could not do so. I struck the pavilion with my hand and my family heard this and came to me. I told them, "Keep the Abyssinians away from me." They did so. And then I regained use of my tongue and feet.'"
lessons: The birth of the Prophet was accompanied by cosmic signs witnessed by people of different backgrounds, affirming that divine truth transcends borders and communities.
Those who sincerely seek truth, like Zayd ibn Amr and Waraqa, are granted insight even before receiving the formal message of Islam.
God's plan unfolds with precision; the signs pointing to the Prophet's mission were laid even before his prophethood began.
practical_advice: When feeling lost or spiritually confused, seek solitude and reflection as Zayd ibn Amr did on Mount Abu Qubays — clarity often comes in quiet moments.
Be open to recognizing signs of truth even when they come from unexpected sources or in unexpected forms.
Draw comfort in knowing that what God has decreed will come to pass; this perspective reduces anxiety about an uncertain future.

title: The Idol of Banu Wadhra Speaks and Falls: Zamal ibn Amr Accepts Islam
summary: The idol Sam of the Banu Wadhra tribe speaks out announcing the arrival of Islam and then falls on its face, prompting Zamal ibn Amr to travel to the Prophet and accept Islam.
story: The hafiz Abu al-Qasim b. 'Asakir related in his history work dealing with the life of al-Haith b. Harmi' b. al-Mudlij b. Ibn al-Miqdad b. Zamal b. 'Amr al-Wadhri, from his father, from his grandfather, from his father, from Zamal b. 'Amr al-Wadhri, who said, "The Banu Wadhra had an idol called Sam whom they venerated. And it was kept with Banu Hind b. Hiram b. Habba b. 'Abd b. Kabir b. Wadhra, its guardian being a man called Tariq. People used to make sacrifice near it. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) came forth we heard a voice that said, 'O Banu Hind b. Hiram, the truth has come forth and Sam has gone; Islam has done away with idol worship.' We were very alarmed and awed by this. Nothing more happened for several days, but then we heard a voice saying, 'O Tariq; the truth-telling Prophet has been sent with spoken inspiration. A man has emerged in the land of Tihama announcing the truth, those who support him will live secure, while those who desert him will regret it. This now is my farewell until Judgement Day.' "'At that', Zamal said, 'the idol fell forward on its face. So I bought a riding camel and rode off to the Messenger of God (SAAS) along with a group of my tribesmen. I recited to him the following verses: "To you, O Messenger of God, I directed (my mount), driving it hard over high rough ground and sandy lowlands / To give strong help to the best of men, to tie a rope of yours to my own, / And I testify that there is nothing besides God, and that I will worship Him so long as my shoes weigh down my feet." "'So I accepted Islam and pledged my allegiance to him. We told him what we had heard, and he responded, "That was demon-spirit talk."' "He then said, 'O you Arabs, I am the Messenger of God to you and to all mankind. I call upon you to worship God alone. I tell you I am his Messenger and his servant and that you must make pilgrimage to the ka'ba, fast for one month out of twelve, that being Ramadan. Those of you who respond to me shall have paradise as your abode. Whoever opposes me will have hellfire as his final destiny.' "He went on, 'So we accepted Islam and he made a pact with us. He wrote a document for us as follows: "In the name of God, the most Merciful and most Beneficent; this is from Muhammad, the Messenger of God, to Zamal b. 'Amr and to those in particular who accepted Islam with him. I am sending him to his people intentionally. Whoever accepts Islam is on the side of God and his Messenger. Whoever refuses can have a truce for two months. Witnessed by 'Ali b. Abu Talib and Muhammad b. Maslama al-Ansari."'"
lessons: When the truth becomes manifest, the sincere heart does not delay in responding; Zamal acted immediately upon witnessing the signs.
Accepting a new truth may require leaving behind deeply ingrained traditions, but the reward of aligning oneself with truth is worth the difficulty.
The Prophet's practical step of formalizing a written covenant shows that faith and organized action go hand in hand.
practical_advice: When you witness a clear sign or truth in your life, act on it promptly rather than waiting for more certainty — hesitation is often the enemy of growth.
If you are in a period of confusion about your beliefs or direction, reflect on the moments that have already shown you clarity and build on them.
Consider formalizing your commitments — writing down your intentions or goals can strengthen your resolve, as the Prophet's written covenant did for Zamal's community.

title: The Demon Spirit Mis'ar Stirs Mecca and Is Slain by the Believing Jinn Samij
summary: A demon spirit named Mis'ar incites the people of Mecca against the Prophet through poetry spoken from an idol, but is killed within three days by a believing jinn named Samij, who had accepted Islam and was renamed Abd Allah by the Prophet.
story: Sa'id b. Yahya b. Sa'id al-Umawi stated in his history of the Prophet's wars that Muhammad b. Sa'id — meaning his uncle — told him that Muhammad b. al-Munkadir said that he was told that Ibn 'Abbas said that a demon-spirit called out to Abu Qubays, saying, "O people of Fihr! May God find your views repugnant; how petty minds and intelligence are / When they disregard those slandering the religion of their fathers, those noble guardians! / He has allied with the spirits, the spirits of Busm against you, along with the men of the palm trees and the forest / You are about to see the horsemen running (right and left) swiftly killing people in sheepskin garments. / Is there any noble man among you, who has the spirit of a free man, a man of illustrious parents and relatives, / A man to strike a blow that will be a warning and a rupture from anxiety and worry?" Ibn 'Abbas stated, "This poetry became a refrain for the people of Mecca, and they would recite it to one another. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'This is a devil who speaks to people through the idols; his name is Mis'ar; and God will confound him.'
