Nothing else happened for three days and then a voice cried out on the mountain: 'We killed Mis'ar in three days when he disgraced the spirits and advocated evil. I slashed at his head with a sharp sword drawn because he reviled our Prophet made pure.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'This is a genie whose name is Samij; when he believed in me, I renamed him 'Abd Allah, he told me he spent three days searching for him (Mis'ar).' 'Ali said, 'May God reward him well, O Messenger of God.'"
lessons: Evil forces that seek to sow confusion and hostility against truth will ultimately be overcome; patience in the face of slander is rewarded.
Even among the unseen creation, there are those who embrace truth and actively defend it, reminding us that righteousness is universally recognized.
The Prophet's calm identification of the source of the evil, rather than panic or retaliation, is a model of composure under spiritual and social attack.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by hostile voices or social pressure against your values, remember that forces greater than you can see are aligned with truth.
Do not let hostile rhetoric or social pressure cause you to abandon your principles; respond with clarity and composure as the Prophet did.
Surround yourself with people who actively support truth and goodness, just as the believing jinn Samij acted on behalf of the Prophet without being asked.

title: Sa'd ibn Ubada Hears Jinn Voices on His Mission to Hadramawt
summary: Sa'd ibn Ubada, sent on a mission to Hadramawt before the migration, hears mysterious voices in the night — jinn lamenting mortality and then speaking of the Prophet's mission, the departure from Mecca, and the coming of a new era.
story: The hafiz Abu Nu'aym stated in the al-Dala'il (The Signs) that 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. Ja'far related to him, quoting Abu al-Fadl Muhammad b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. Musa b. Abu Harb al-Saffar, quoting 'Abbas b. al-Faraj al-Riyashi, quoting Sulayman b. 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Abu Thabit, from his father, from 'Abd al-Hamid b. Bahram, from Shahr b. Hawshab, from Ibn 'Abbas, from Sa'd b. Ubada, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me on a mission to Hadramawt before his migration. On the way, during the night, I heard a voice cry out, 'O Abu 'Amr, insomnia afflicted me, sleep fled and slumber was impossible / At thought of a group of men who came before and now are gone; and all mankind have their palaces disappear. / They passed away, moving to their deaths, destined (to their graves) not to a watering place. / They left, while I remained behind, alone, with no one to assist me, / Futile, unable to even deal with an issue that a child could handle (with ease). / After all, I am not remaining with a people whose tombs are like those of Thamud / And 'Ad and the centuries are all equal to death, all being mere stalks after harvest.' "Another voice then called out, 'O Khar'ab, "soft twig", has wonder taken your senses? Wonderful, wonderful things are happening between Zahra and Yathrib.' "'What's happened there, O Sha'ib?' He replied, 'The Prophet of peace has been sent with the best of words to all of humanity. And he has left the holy places for the date palms and forts.' "He asked, 'What's it all about, this Prophet with a mission, the Revealed Scripture and this "chosen" ummi?' He replied, 'He is a descendant of Lu'ayy b. Ghalib, son of Fihr b. Malik b. al-Nadr b. Kinas.' The second voice replied, 'O what a pity! I'm too old for this, my time is all gone. Al-Nadr b. Kinana and I used to aim at the same target, drink cold milk together. Early one cold morning I accompanied him out from Dawha; he arose with the sun and set with it too, with him telling what he heard and verifying what he saw. If this man is of his descent then the sword is sheathed, fear has gone, sexual immorality is over, and usury is no more.' "'Then tell me what will be?' He replied, 'Distress, misery, and hunger are over. Strength and bravery are over, except for what is to'"
lessons: Loneliness and the contemplation of mortality, though painful, can open the heart to recognizing higher truths and greater purposes.
The migration of the Prophet from Mecca to Madinah was a cosmic event recognized even by the unseen world as a turning point for all of humanity.
Times of personal isolation and night-time reflection, as experienced by Sa'd, can be among the most spiritually significant moments in a person's life.
practical_advice: When you are alone or away from your community and feeling isolated, use that solitude as an opportunity for spiritual reflection rather than despairing in the loneliness.
In moments of existential anxiety about mortality or the passage of time, anchor yourself in the conviction that your life has purpose and is part of a larger divine plan.

If you are navigating a major life transition, take comfort in knowing that even distant and unseen forces recognized the significance of the Prophet's migration — your own transitions, when aligned with truth, carry weight and meaning.

title: The Jinn's Announcement of the Prophet's Migration (Sa'd ibn 'Ubada's Account)
summary: Sa'd ibn 'Ubada, after pledging allegiance at 'Aqaba, hears a mysterious voice while traveling that announces the Prophet's migration to Medina, learning of the hijra only through this supernatural incident.
story: Aba Nu'aym then related this story from Muhammad b. Ja'far, from Ibr&im b. 'Ali, from al-Nadr b. Salama, from Hassan b. 'Ubada b. Masa, from 'Abd al-Hamid b. Babrzm, from Shahr, from Ibn 'Abbas, from Sa'd b. 'Ubada, as follows, "After we pledged our allegiance to the Messenger of God (SAAS) on 'Aqaba night, I left for Hadramawt on business. That having been concluded, I started back. Some distance along the way I fell asleep and awoke in a fright to hear a voice shouting, '0 Aba 'Amr, insomnia has afflicted me, sleep has fled and my slumber is interrupted.'" And he recounted the whole incident as given above.
lessons: Even those distant from the center of events can be touched by divine signs, reminding us that God's guidance reaches everywhere.
Pledging sincere commitment to truth, as Sa'd did at 'Aqaba, opens one's heart and perception to extraordinary moments of clarity.
The unseen world bears witness to the significance of the Prophet's mission, affirming that spiritual events leave impressions beyond the physical realm.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about being far from loved ones or important events, remember that God's signs and reassurances can reach you wherever you are.
Use moments of sudden wakefulness or unease at night as prompts for reflection and supplication rather than sources of panic.
When you return from a journey or period of separation, take time to reconnect with your community and share what you have learned or experienced.

title: Tamim al-Dari Hears the Jinn Accept Islam and Rushes to the Prophet
summary: While traveling in Syria, Tamim al-Dari seeks the protection of spirits for the night and is rebuked by an unseen voice that announces the Prophet's emergence and commands him to hasten to Muhammad and accept Islam.
story: Aba Nu'aym stated that Muhammad b. Jacfar related to him, quoting Ibr&im b. al-Sindi, quoting al-Nadr h. Salama, quoting Aba Ghaziyya Muhammad b. Miisa, from al-'Anzfb. Kh3id al-Wa~~xbi, from Khdid b. Sacid, from his father, who said that he heard Tamim al-Dari say, "I was in Syria when the Messenger of God (SAAS) received his mission. I left on some business and on the way night fell. I said, 'I claim to be under the protection of the chief spirit of this valley for this night.' When I went to bed, I heard someone I could not see calling out, 'Seek God's protection. For no spirit can give anyone protection from God.' 'By God,' I exclaimed, 'what are you saying?' The voice replied, 'The Messenger of the ummis, the Messenger of God, has come forth. We prayed behind him at al-Hajan. We accepted Islam and became his followers. The tricks of the demon-spirits are over, and they have been pelted with shooting stars. Hurry to Muhammad, the Messenger of the Lord of the worlds, and accept Islam.'" Tamim went on, "When morning came I went on to Ayyiib's monastery, where I asked to see a monk and told him what had happened. He said, 'They spoke the truth to you. He is leaving the holy place, and the place where he is migrating is holy. He is the best of prophets. Do not let anyone beat you to him.'" Tamim continued, "I took the trouble to travel hurriedly until I reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) and then accepted Islam."
lessons: Old habits of seeking protection from sources other than God can be broken in a single moment of true guidance; the key is to listen and act.
When truth becomes clear, hesitation is itself a form of loss — Tamim's immediate journey to the Prophet shows the urgency with which we should respond to divine guidance.
Both human scholars (the monk) and unseen creation testified to the truth of the Prophet's mission, reminding us that truth is confirmed through multiple channels.
practical_advice: When you feel lost or anxious and are tempted to seek relief from unreliable sources, pause and redirect your trust entirely to God.
If you receive a sign or conviction that you should make a positive change in your life, do not delay — act with the urgency Tamim showed.
Seek counsel from knowledgeable and wise people when you experience something confusing or spiritually significant; their perspective can confirm and clarify your path.

title: The Idol Suwa' Speaks Out Against Itself: The Tribe's Sheep and the Voice
summary: A man brings his mangy sheep to the idol Suwa' seeking blessing, but hears a voice from within the idol declaring the end of demonic tricks and announcing the Prophet Ahmad, leading him to abandon the idol and embrace Islam.
story: Hatim b. Isma'il stated, from 'Abd Allah b. Yazid al-Hudhali, from 'Abd All& b. Sa'ida al-Hudhali, from his father, who said, "We were at our idol named Suwa: having brought some sheep of ours to him. They consisted of 200 ewes that were afflicted with mange. We brought them close to him seeking his blessing. Then I heard a voice calling out from the belly of the idol, 'The tricks of the demon-spirits are no more; we have been pelted with shooting stars - all for a Prophet whose name is Ahmad!' "'You have misled us, by God!' I exclaimed. I turned the face of my sheep away, urging them towards my people, and I then saw a man who told me of the appearance of the Prophet (SAAS)."
lessons: False objects of worship will ultimately expose their own powerlessness; what we cling to for help cannot help us when it was never capable of doing so.
The moment of awakening from false belief can come suddenly and unexpectedly — the heart must be ready to act on it immediately.
When we realize we have been misled, the proper response is not despair but decisive redirection toward the truth.
practical_advice: Reflect honestly on what or whom you are seeking help from in your times of need, and ask yourself whether those sources are truly worthy of your reliance.
When you discover that something you trusted has let you down, resist the urge to stay out of habit or embarrassment — turn away and seek what is genuinely beneficial.
Surround yourself with people who can point you toward truth, as the man who informed this person about the Prophet served as a vital guide at a critical moment.

title: Rashid ibn 'Abd Rabbihi, the Idol Suwa', and the Name Exchange with the Prophet
summary: Rashid ibn 'Abd Rabbihi witnesses multiple idols speaking out against themselves and announcing the Prophet, eventually travels to Medina to accept Islam, and has his name exchanged with his dog's name by the Prophet in a memorable moment of humor and blessing.
story: He then stated that Wmar b. Muhammad b. Jacfar related to him, quoting Ihrzhim b. al-Sindi, quoting al-Nadr h. Salama, quoting Muhammad h. Maslama al-Makhziimi, quoting Yahyz h. Sulaymzn, from Hakim b. 'At2 al-Zafari, who is of Banii Sulaym, descended from Rashid b. 'Ahd Rahhihi, from his father, from his grandfather, from Rzshid b. 'Abd Rabbihi, who said, "The idol known as SuwaC was at al-Mi'lzt, one of a group worshipped by the tribes of Hudhayl and Banti Zafar b. Sulaym." Banti Zafar dispatched Rashid b. 'Abd Rabhihi with a gift for Suwzcfrom Sulaym. Rashid went on, "At dawn I attended an idol before the idol Suwzc. As I did so, all of a sudden a voice rang out from its belly, 'How strange! How very strange that a prophet should come forth from the family of 'Ahd a l - M u w b who would ban fornication, usury and sacrificing to idols! And the skies are being guarded and we are being pelted by shooting stars! How shmge! How very strange!' Then another idol screamed out from deep within itself, 'Al-Dim% has been abandoned, and he used to be worshipped! The Prophet Ahmad has come forth. He says the prayers, orders the payment of alms, fasting, piety, and kindness to relatives.' Then another voice shouted from the belly of another idol: 'He who has inherited the prophethood and the true religion after Mary's son is of @raysh, rightly guided A prophet come to inform of what is past, and what is today the truth, or tomorrow.'" Rashid went on: "Next dawn I attended SuwaCand there were two jackals licking all around it and eating what had been sacrificed to it. And they were directing their urine on to it." At this Rashid b. CAbd Rabbihi spoke the verse: "Is this a lord upon whose head two jackals urinate? Whoever has jackals urinate upon him is disgraced!" "This occurred at the time of the emergence of the Prophet (SAAS), his migration to Medina and his acceptance by the people. And so Rashid left and made his way to the Prophet (SAAS) in Medina, taking along a dog he had with him. Now at that time Rashid's name was Zdim, tyrant, evil-doer, and that of his dog Rzshid, rightly guided. The Prophet (SAAS) asked him what his name was. He replied 'Zalim'. 'And what is your dog's name?' 'Bshid,' he replied. 'Well,' said the Prophet (SAAS), 'Now your name is Rashid and your dog's Zdii!' And the Prophet (SAAS) laughed. "And so mshid pledged his allegiance to him and remained in Mecca with him. He then made a request to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to receive the land fees for Wahat, and he described it to him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave h i the land fees right for the heights above Wahz, along with control over shacma al-faras, the 'horse bridle', monument and over the rituals there of throwing stones at it three times. He presented him with a waterskin filled with water and spat in it. He told him, 'Empty it out at the top of the tract, and don't deny people access to its overflow.' And so he did. He therefore channelled the water into an open ditch which still flows to this day. Around it he planted palm trees. It is said that all WahZc uses it for their water supply and that the people there call it 'the water of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' The people of Wahat also wash in it. Whid's stone-casting site was attended by many travelling parties, called the 'caravans of the stone'. One morning b s h i d went to SuwaC and demolished it."
lessons: A new identity in faith is not just symbolic — the Prophet's renaming of Zalim to Rashid signifies that accepting Islam transforms who we are at our core.
The Prophet's gentle humor in exchanging the man's name with his dog's showed that guidance comes with warmth and acceptance, not condemnation of a person's past.
Small acts of blessing, like the Prophet's waterskin, can leave lasting benefits for entire communities across generations.
practical_advice: If your past identity or old habits feel like a burden or a mark of shame, know that sincere turning to God is a genuine new beginning — your past does not define your future.
Allow yourself to experience joy and lightness in your spiritual journey; the Prophet's laughter in this account reminds us that faith is not devoid of warmth and humor.
Look for ways that your personal transformation can benefit others around you, just as Rashid's blessed water source served his entire community long after him.

title: 'Amr ibn Murra al-Juhanni's Vision and Immediate Acceptance of Islam
summary: 'Amr ibn Murra sees a blinding light and hears voices during pilgrimage foretelling the Prophet's mission, and upon learning of Muhammad's prophethood rushes to him, demolishes his family's idol, and pledges allegiance with verses of poetry.
story: Abii Nu'aym stated that Sulayman b. Ahmad related to him, quoting 'Ali b. Ibrhim al-Khuzaci al-Ahwm, quoting Aba Muhammad 'Abd All& b. Dawiid b. Dalhath b. Ismacil b. Musri' b. Yasir h. Suwayd, the Companion of the Messenger of God (SAAS), from his father, from his father Dalhath from his father IsmZcil, that the father of the last-mentioned, 'Abd Allah, related to him from his father Musricb. Yasir that his father Ysir related to him, from CAmr h. Murra al-Juhanni who used to tell the following, "Once, before Islam, I went on pilgrimage with a party from my tribe. At Mecca, during my sleep, I saw a blinding light emitting from the kacba, so strong that it lit up Mt. Yathrib and the mountains overlooking the town. I heard a voice in the light that said, 'The shadows have been dispelled, the light now shines bright, and the last of the prophets has been sent.' "Then another light shone forth by which I could see the castles of al-Hira and Chosroe's palace. I also heard a voice in the light that called out, 'Islam has appeared, the idols have been demolished and reconciliation has come.' "I awoke with a start and said to my people, 'By God, in this Quraysh quarter something tremendous is going to happen!' And I told them what I had seen. When we got back home a man arrived who told us that someone named Ahmad had received a mission. I went to that man, and told him what I had seen. He told me, '0 CAmr b. Murra, I have been dispatched to all mankind to invite them into Islam, to command them not to shed blood, to bring about reconciliation, to worship God, to reject the idols, to make pilgrimage to the kacba, and to fast one month in twelve, Ramad& that is. For those who respond there will be paradise. Whoever disobeys will go to the lire. So you, CAmr b. Murra, you believe and God will spare you from the fires of hell.' "I therefore said, 'I bear witness that there is no God but God and that you are the Messenger of God. I believe in all you have brought us regarding what is lawful and what is disallowed, even though that will give offence to many people.' I the11 recited lo him some verses I had composed when I had heard of him. We used to have an idol, and my father was its guardian. I went to it and demolished it and then joined up with the Prophet (SAAS). I recited, 'I bore wimess that God is truth and that I will be the first to abandon the stone idols; I then humed to depart to you, travelling over ravines and flat lands towards you. To attend the very best of men in spirit and in ancestry, the messenger of man's sovereign above the stars.' "The Prophet (SAAS) responded, 'Welcome indeed, 0 'Amr b. Murra.' I replied, '0Messenger, may my father and mother be sacrifice for you! Send me back to my own people, so that God may give me to benefit them as he has given you to me.' "So he did send me"
lessons: Dreams and visions can serve as preparation for truth — when reality confirmed what 'Amr had seen, he did not hesitate, showing that an open and attentive heart is already halfway to guidance.
True faith sometimes requires breaking with family tradition and social pressure; 'Amr demolished his own father's idol because truth outweighed inherited loyalty.
The desire to share guidance with one's own people reflects the highest expression of gratitude — we honor a gift by wanting others to receive it too.
practical_advice: Pay attention to the dreams, moments of clarity, or inner convictions that point you toward a better path — they may be preparing you for a decision you need to make.
When accepting a positive change in your life, be willing to let go of familiar things — habits, relationships, or identities — that contradict your new direction.
After experiencing a transformation or breakthrough, channel your energy into helping others in your community find the same; service becomes the expression of gratitude.

title: Amr ibn Murra al-Juhani Accepts Islam and Returns as a Messenger to His People
summary: Amr ibn Murra al-Juhani accepts Islam and is sent by the Prophet back to his tribe with a message of guidance. Most of his people accept, but one man refuses and mocks him, only to suffer a divine punishment.
story: God is truth and that I will be the first to abandon the stone idols; I then hurried to depart to you, travelling over ravines and flat lands towards you. To attend the very best of men in spirit and in ancestry, the messenger of man's sovereign above the stars.' "The Prophet (SAAS) responded, 'Welcome indeed, 0 'Amr b. Murra.' I replied, '0 Messenger, may my father and mother be sacrifice for you! Send me back to my own people, so that God may give me to benefit them as he has given you to me.' "So he did send me to them, saying, 'Speak only what is right; do not be harsh, nor arrogant, nor envious.' "When I went to my people, I told them, '0 you, Banii Rifs'a, and you too, Bana Juhayna, I am a messenger to you from the Messenger of God. I invite you to paradise and warn you of the fire. I command you not to shed blood, to encourage reconciliation, to worship God, to reject the idols, to make pilgrimage to the ka'ba, to fast during Ramadan, one month in twelve. Those who respond will go to paradise. Those who disobey will go to the fires. 0 people of Juhayna, God, all praise be to Him, has made you better than your forebears. He made you hate, in your state of ignorance, the obscene practices which others have found acceptable. They used to unite two sisters, men would marry their fathers' wives, and they would allow warfare during the sacred month. So respond to this Prophet (SAAS) sent to you from Banii Lu'ayy b. Ghalib, and you will attain respect on earth and honour in the hereafter. Hurry! Hurry to do this, so that you will find favour with God.' "They did all respond, except for one man. He arose and said, '0 'Amr b. Murra, may God embitter your life for you! Do you order us to reject our gods and break apart our community by abandoning the religion of our forefathers in favour of what this Qurayshite from Tihama advocates? No; and you are neither welcome nor honoured here!' He then spoke the verses, 'Ibn Murra has come here and made a speech, but it's not a speech of someone wanting goodness. I consider his words and deeds will be one day, however long the wait, mere winds. Are we to deprecate our elders now gone? Whoever seeks for that will never achieve success.' "To that 'Amr b. Murra responded, 'Whoever is the liar, may God embitter his life, silence his tongue, and blind his sight!' "'Amr b. Murra went on: 'And by God, by the time he died his mouth had dropped, he had lost all taste for food, and he was blind and dumb.' "'Amr b. Murra and those others of his people who had accepted Islam then went to the Prophet (SAAS). He welcomed and honoured them and wrote for them a document, the text of which was as follows, 'In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent, this document is from God through the tongue of the Messenger of God and is a trustworthy document, a spoken truth, (sent) with 'Amr b. Murra al-Juhani to Juhayna b. Zayd: "You shall have the low-lying flat and fertile lands and the sides and backs of the valleys, and all their vegetation and their pure water to drink. Provided that you forsake one fifth (of the harvest), and perform the five daily prayers. Your rich shall pay alms (to the poor): one ewe for each forty sheep, and one ewe for each five camels, or two ewes for any combination thereof. The rich shall not be eligible to receive charity. The Muslims present here witness this document (written by) Qays b. Shammas, may God bless them all."'
lessons: When you are entrusted with a mission, approach it with gentleness and sincerity rather than harshness or arrogance, as the Prophet specifically instructed Amr not to be harsh, arrogant, or envious.
Rejection and mockery from others should not deter you from standing firm in truth; Amr faced public ridicule but remained composed and continued his mission.
Those who sincerely turn to God and bring others to guidance are honoured both in this life and the next, as the Prophet welcomed and formally honoured the delegation from Juhayna.
practical_advice: When sharing your values or beliefs with family or community and facing resistance, focus on delivering your message with kindness and move on without forcing the outcome.
If someone mocks or belittles your convictions, resist the urge to retaliate harshly; instead, state your position calmly and leave the matter to God.

Take practical steps to formalise your commitments and responsibilities, as the Prophet did by writing a covenant for the tribe, so that good intentions are translated into clear, accountable actions.

title: The Man of Juhayna Healed Through a Divine Vision and Turns to Islam
summary: A gravely ill man from the Juhayna tribe loses consciousness and receives a divine vision calling him to abandon idol worship, after which he is miraculously healed while his cousin who was foretold to take his place dies instead.
story: Abu 'Uthman Sa'id b. Yahya al-Umawi stated in his Maghazi collection that 'Abd Allah related to him, quoting Abu 'Abd Allah, quoting al-Mujalid b. Sa'id, and al-Ajlah, from al-Sha'bi, quoting a sheikh of Juhayna, who said, "One of our men became extremely sick, so ill that we dug his grave and prepared to bury him. He passed out, then opened his eyes and came to again and asked, 'Have you dug my grave?' 'Yes,' we told him. He then asked, 'What did al-Fusal do?' making reference to a cousin of his. We replied, 'Salih passed by a while ago and asked after you.' 'He's soon going to be put in my grave; a spirit came to me after I became unconscious and said, "Weep to Hubal; don't you see your grave is being made ready and that your mother is almost bereft of you? What do you think? Supposing we were to reverse it away from you and then filled the grave with stones and threw in it al-Fusal who passed by and said he would take your place, thinking he would not do so. Will you thank your Lord and pray and abandon the religion of those who worship idols and who go astray?" "Yes," I replied. The voice said, "Get up; you are made healthy."'" He went on, "So the man was cured and al-Fusal died and was put in his grave." Al-Juhayni stated, "And I saw that man of the Juhayna tribe after that; he was praying, cursing the idols and striking them."
lessons: Life and death are entirely in God's hands; when a person is brought to the brink of death and then granted recovery, it is a powerful reminder to turn back to the Creator with sincerity.
Crisis and near-death experiences can be transformative turning points that shake a person free from inherited false beliefs and open their heart to truth.
Gratitude expressed through action — such as prayer and abandoning what is wrong — is the truest form of thankfulness for any blessing received.
practical_advice: When you are going through a severe health crisis or any overwhelming hardship, use that vulnerability as an opportunity to reflect deeply on your relationship with God and what truly matters.
If you have been clinging to beliefs, habits, or influences inherited from your upbringing that you know are harmful, a moment of crisis may be the push you need to finally let them go.
Express gratitude for recovery or relief not just in words but in concrete changed behaviour, as the man demonstrated by taking up prayer and actively distancing himself from what he had abandoned.

title: Khuraym ibn Fatik Guided to Islam by a Voice in the Desert
summary: While searching for a lost she-camel in the desert, Khuraym ibn Fatik hears a mysterious voice directing him to the Prophet in Medina, leading to his conversion to Islam and the miraculous return of his camel.
story: Al-Umawi said that 'Abd Allah related to him, saying that once, when 'Umar b. al-Khattab, God be pleased with him, was in a gathering where they were discussing the spirits, Khuraym b. Fatik al-Asadi said, "Should I tell you how I came to adopt Islam?" "Yes, do," he said. "I was once searching for a young she-camel of mine, following a trail of hers that went up and down till finally I was at Abraq al-'Azzaf. There I tied my camel-mount down and spoke the words, 'I claim protection from the chief spirit of this place; I claim protection from the leader of this valley.' When I had done so, a voice called out the following verses, 'Woe to you! Seek protection with the Lord of majesty, glory, exaltation, and precedence. And then recite verses from (surat) al-Anfal, saying that God is One, and do not worry.' "I was dumbfounded, but once regaining my wits, I said, '0 voice what is it you say? Is it truth you have, or falsehood? Make plain, may God guide you, what is the way?' "It spoke again: 'This is the Messenger of God, he who does good deeds, at Yathrib, who calls to salvation, Who commands piety, prayer and restrains people from sins.' "I said, 'I'll not rest till I come to him, and believe in him.' I then set my foot in the stirrup of my mount and spoke the verses, 'Guide me, guide me on the right course, may you never hunger or lack shelter so long as you live. May you never cease being a lord of strength; don't keep the good you were granted from all the spirits as long as you live.' "He replied, 'May God accompany you and lead your mount; may He enhance your wage and protect your soul; Believe in Him, may my Lord grant you success, and give him a great victory as he will you.' "'Who are you; may God forgive you. Tell me so I can inform him when I come to him.' He replied, 'I am Malik b. Malik and am his chief over the spirits of Nasihin. Your camel is being protected until I reunite it with your people, if God wills it.'" The narrator went on, "So I travelled on till I reached Medina; it was a Friday and people were on their way to the mosque. There the Prophet (SAAS) was upon the minbar, the pulpit, looking wonderful as he addressed the congregation. I told myself I would make my camel kneel at the door of the mosque until he prayed. Then I would go in to him, accept Islam and tell him of it. When I had set my camel down, out to me came Abu Dhar who said, 'Welcome, indeed welcome! We heard of your having become a Muslim, so come on in and pray.' "I did so, and later went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and he told me of my accepting Islam. I commented, 'Praise be to God!' He said, 'By the way, your friend did keep faith with you, that being his nature. He did lead your camel back to your people.'" Al-Tabarani related this anecdote in his discussion of the life of Khuraym b. Fatik in his encyclopedia. There he stated that al-Husayn b. Ishaq al-Yasiri related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Ibrahim al-Sha'mi, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Musa al-Iskandari, quoting Muhammad b. Ishaq, from Sa'id b. Abi Sa'id al-Maqburi, from Abu Hurayra, who said that Khuraym b. Fatik said to 'Umar b. al-Khattab, "0 Commander of the Faithful, may I tell you how I came to accept Islam?" "Certainly," he replied. He then recounted it as above except for saying, "Then Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, 'the trusting', came out and said, 'Come inside. We have heard about your accepting Islam.' I replied, 'But I don't know how to cleanse myself properly.' So he taught me how and afterwards I went in to the mosque where I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) looking really happy and saying, 'There is no single Muslim who has performed the ablutions, and done so properly, who then has prayed, learned it by heart and understood it, who will not enter paradise.' "'Umar then told me, 'Bring us some proof of this (your conversion) or I will punish you severely.' So a sheikh of Quraysh, 'Uthman b. 'Affan, testified on my behalf and his testimony was accepted."
lessons: God can guide a person to truth through unexpected means and in unexpected places; what begins as a mundane search for a lost camel becomes a life-changing spiritual journey.
When you feel lost and are seeking direction in life, the guidance you need may come from sources you never anticipated, provided your heart remains open and you ask sincerely.

The Muslim community's role in welcoming newcomers with warmth and practical assistance — as Abu Bakr taught Khuraym how to perform ablution — is an essential part of helping others grow in their faith.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually lost or disconnected, do not dismiss unexpected signs or moments of clarity; instead, pause, reflect, and be willing to follow where genuine truth leads you.

If you are new to a faith practice or community and feel uncertain about rituals or belonging, do not let that uncertainty stop you from stepping forward; seek out someone willing to guide you practically, as Abu Bakr did.

When your sincerity is questioned or you are asked to prove yourself, remain calm and allow your character and the testimony of those who know you to speak on your behalf rather than becoming defensive.

title: Satih the Soothsayer and His Prophecies to the Men of Quraysh
summary: Four men of Quraysh visit the legendary soothsayer Satih al-Ghassani, who despite being a man of extraordinary physical disability possessed prophetic knowledge. He sees through their disguise and delivers detailed prophecies about the coming of a great Prophet, the early Caliphs, and future events in Islamic history.
story: Aba Nu'aym stated that Sulaymm b. Ahmad related to him, quoting Abn 'Abd al-Malik Ahmad h. Ibrahim al-Qurashi al-Dimashqi, quoting Sulayman h. 'Abd al-Raban, the son of Sbur&bil's daughter, quoting Isma'il b. Ayyash, from Y&ya b. Abn 'Amr al-Shaybani, from 'Ahd Allah b. al-Daylami, who said that a man came to Ibn 'Abbas and said, "We have heard that you, in reference to Saph, maintain that God created him. But why would He have created of mankind anyone like him?" He replied, "Yes, God did create Sauh al-Ghasszni out of flesh over strips of palm bark. He had no bones or sinews except for his skull and his two hands. He was folded over from his feet to his collarbone as a robe is folded; the only part of him that moved was his tongue. When he wanted to travel to Mecca, he was carried on a board and brought there. Four men of Quraysh went out to greet him, they being 'Abd Shams and H%him, the sons of 'Abd Manaf h. Qusayy, al-Ahwas h. Fihr, and 'Uqayl h. Abn Waqqas. They pretended different identities and told him, 'We are men of Jumah who have come to you. We heard of your arrival so thought we would come to you; our doing so is a right of yours and a duty of ours.' Wqayl presented him with an Indian platter and a straight Rudayni lance which were placed by the door of the holy ka'ba to determine whether or not Saph would see them. "Satih spoke: 'O Wqayl, give me your hand.' Wqayl did so and Satih continued, 'O Wqayl, by the hidden world, by Him who forgives sins, by the pact that is fulfilled and by the ka'ba that is built, you have presented me with a gift, the Indian platter and the straight Rudayni spear.' They replied, 'You are right, O Satih!' He then said, 'By Him who brings joy, by the rainbow, by all other joys, by the orphan lying prostrate, by the palm trees, the fresh plants, and by the dates, the crow as it passed by presented itself and made known that your group is not of Jum&, and that their ancestry is of great Quraysh.' They replied, 'You are right, O Satih! We are of those who guard the holy ka'ba; we have come to visit you because of what we have learned of your knowledge. So tell us what will happen in this time of ours and what will come hereafter; perhaps you do have knowledge of that.'" He replied, "Now you are telling the truth! Take the following from me and from what God has inspired me with. You, O Arabs, are in the age of senility; your mental perception is the same as that of the non-Arabs. You have neither knowledge nor understanding. But from your descendants there will come people of understanding who will seek out all kinds of knowledge. They will destroy the idols, reach the dam, kill foreigners, and seek after booty." They then asked, "But who will those men be, Satih?" He replied, "By the pillared house, by security, and by tranquillity, after you there will be your descendants; it is they who will destroy the idols, forbid the worship of Satan, worship the All-Merciful alone, spread the best of all religions, honour the building (the ka'ba), and ask judgements of the young heroes." "Whose progeny will they be?" they asked. "By the most noble of all, by Him who communicates with the nobles, by Him who moves the winding dunes, by Him who multiplies by doubling, thousands will arise from the line of 'Abd Shams and 'Abd Mans, and there will be disputation over this." "O Satih," they commented, "what misery there will be for them, from what you tell us you know. From what land will those men come?" He replied, "By Him who will remain everlasting, by Him who will reach eternity, there will come forth from this land a young man who will guide to what is right, who will reject the idol Yaghiith and illusion, who will be innocent of worshipping what is repugnant; he will worship one Lord alone. And then God will cause him to die, with all praise. From the earth he will be missed; in heaven he will be witnessed. After him al-jiddiq ('the trusting', i.e. Abii Bakr) will come to rule; when he judges he speaks the truth and in restoring right he is neither foolish nor intemperate. Then he will be followed to power by al-hang ('the true believer', i.e. the Caliph Wmar), he who puts to the test and is lordly, a man who disavows violent language, who gives hospitality to guests, and who is wise and prudent in his judgements. Then there will follow him a man who will be demanding and invite others to the cause, and various groups and factions will assemble about him. Then they will kill him out of envy and rage; the old man will be seized and hacked to pieces and men will speak orations over him." Then will follow al-nagr, "the protector", a man who will confuse right opinion with that of the unfriendly ones. Soldiers will rule over the earth. Then after him his son will reign, and take what he gathers yet gain little praise. He will consume his wealth and eat alone. Those after him of his progeny will expand that wealth. Following him will rule a number of kings; doubtless there will be much blood spilled over them. After them will come the beggars, who will soon be folded up like cloth. Then will come a strong and ruthless man, who destroys justice, subjugates Egypt and conquers the earth with brutality. Then will come a man short of stature, who will have a mark on his back, die violently and so good-bye! Then for a while a young man will rule who will leave the kingdom ruined. His brother will succeed him and continue the same way, concerning himself with wealth-gathering and speech-making. After him a fool will rule, a worldly, comfort-loving and spoiled man. His intimates and family will advise him, then rise up against him, depose him from rule and kill him. Then after him will rule al-sabsc, 'the seventh', who will leave the kingdom a wasteland. His sons in his rule will be like those who are deformed, all of them. After that, every destitute fellow will hunger for rule. He will be followed by the greedy one. A group of Q&y?in will favour Niziir when they meet at Damascus, in two groups between Bunyzn and Lubnxn. Yemen at that time will consist of two parties, those engaging in consultation, and those holding back. Nothing will be seen except failure and disintegration, prisoners in chains surrounded by swords and horses. Thereafter dwellings will be demolished, widows will be plundered, pregnant women will miscarry, and earthquakes will occur. Wx'il will seek the Caliphate and Niza will be furious. Slaves and evil men will be favoured, while the best of men will be in disfavour. Prices will rise, in the month of Safar, and people will kill each other. Then they will move into trenches which will have haircloth and trees to block the rivers, and he will defeat them early in the day. The best will appear, but neither sleep nor decision will avail them. (This will continue) until he enters one of the chief cities, where death and fate will overcome him. The archers will come and assemble on foot, to kill the mail-clad warriors and to capture the defenders. The evil-doers will perish; there he will be seized, at the headwaters. Then religion will die out, all things fall into disarray, the Scriptures are disbelieved, the bridges are demolished, and the only ones who escape are those on islands in the seas. Then the seeds will go bad, the bedouin will appear, there not being among them anyone to warn them against what is immoral and sinful, at a time of great difficulty, if people had decency, but alas! Wishful thinking will do no good." They asked, "What then, Satih?" He replied, "Then there will appear a man from the people of Yemen, a man like a strong, thick rope, and through him God will abolish dissension." The above material is a strange relic. We have recorded it here for its peculiarity, and for the references to the dissensions and battles it includes.
lessons: God's plan for humanity unfolds across generations; what seems distant or impossible to one era becomes reality for those who come after, reminding us to trust in a wisdom greater than our own understanding.
The coming of the Prophet was foretold across cultures and traditions, demonstrating that guidance from God is a universal mercy meant for all of humanity.

History reminds us that worldly power is transient and that justice ultimately prevails; clinging to faith during times of social upheaval and moral decline is the anchor that keeps a believer grounded.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the chaos and injustice in the world around you, remind yourself that history has always moved in cycles and that God's promise of ultimate justice is certain.

Study the lives of righteous leaders like Abu Bakr and Umar as described even in ancient prophecy — their qualities of truthfulness, hospitality, and wisdom are timeless virtues you can cultivate in your own life.

Rather than succumbing to anxiety about an uncertain future, focus on embodying the qualities that Satih praised: sincerity, justice, and devotion to God, as these are what endure when all else fades.

title: The Beginning of Revelation: The First Visit of Angel Jibreel in the Cave of Hira
summary: At the age of forty, the Prophet Muhammad received the first revelation in the Cave of Hira, when Angel Jibreel appeared and commanded him to read. Shaken and frightened, he returned home to Khadija, who comforted him with words of reassurance about his noble character.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Y&y% b. Bukayr related to him, quoting al-Layth, from Wqayl, from Ibn Shihitb, from Wrwa b. al-Zubayr, from 'A'isha, God be pleased with her, who said, "The first indication of revelation to the Messenger of God (SAAS) came in the form of true visions in his sleep. Every vision he had came like the breaking of dawn. "He then developed a liking for solitude. He would spend time alone in the cave Hirz', where he would seek religious purification through devotions. He would stay there many nights and then return to his family for more provisions to continue doing so; then he would come down to Khadija and repeat the same. "Eventually the truth came to him while he was there in that cave Hirs". "The angel came and told him, 'Read!' He replied, 'I don't read.' He then said, 'The angel then overpowered me and choked me until I could bear it no more, and then he released me. Again he said, 'Read!' Again I replied, 'I don't read.' Once more he overpowered me and choked me till I could bear it no more, then he released me and said, 'Read!' I replied, 'I don't read.' Again he overpowered me and choked me a third time until I could bear it no more. Then he released me and said, 'Read in the name of your Lord who created; He created man from a clot. Read! Your Lord is the most noble, He who taught by the pen. He taught man what he did not know' (sarat al-'Alaq, XCVI, v.1-5). "The Messenger of God (SAAS) returned home with this, his heart palpitating. He went in to Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, and said, 'Wrap me up! Wrap me up!' They did so until the terror left him. "He then spoke to Khadija, telling her what had happened, saying, 'I was afraid for myself.' "Khadija replied, 'Oh no! I swear by God He would never abuse you. You maintain family ties, you are hospitable to guests, you support the weak, provide for the poor and help out when tragedy strikes.' "Khadija then hurried off with him to Waraqa b. Nawfal b. Asad b. 'Abd al-Wzzit, who was her cousin. He had earlier become a Christian.
lessons: Even the greatest of God's messengers experienced fear and overwhelming anxiety when confronted with the unknown; feeling afraid in moments of profound change is a deeply human response that does not reflect weakness or a lack of faith.

The steadfast character built through years of honest, compassionate living — as the Prophet had demonstrated throughout his life — becomes the very foundation of reassurance when crisis strikes, as Khadija's words show.

A loving, supportive companion who speaks truth in a moment of fear is one of the greatest gifts God can place in a person's life; never underestimate the power of sincere, grounded reassurance from someone who truly knows you.
practical_advice: When you are struck by a sudden, overwhelming experience that fills you with fear and confusion, allow yourself to first seek safety and comfort — the Prophet himself asked to be wrapped up and held — before trying to process or understand what happened.

In moments of self-doubt and fear, recall the good you have done: your acts of kindness, your loyalty to family, your support of those in need. These are evidence of God's favor and protection upon you, just as Khadija reminded the Prophet.

Seek out a wise, trustworthy person in your life — like Khadija sought Waraqa — when you face something beyond your own understanding. Wisdom from others who have knowledge and experience can bring clarity and calm to even the most frightening moments.

title: The First Revelation and Khadija's Comfort
summary: The Prophet Muhammad received his first revelation from Angel Gabriel at the cave of Hira and returned home trembling with fear, whereupon his wife Khadija comforted him and took him to her cousin Waraqa ibn Nawfal for guidance.
story: to Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, and said, 'Wrap me up! Wrap me up!' They did so until the terror left him. "He then spoke to Khadija, telling her what had happened, saying, 'I was afraid for myself.' "Khadija replied, 'Oh no! I swear by God He would never abuse you. You maintain family ties, you are hospitable to guests, you support the weak, provide for the poor and help out when tragedy strikes.' "Khadija then hurried off with him to Waraqa b. Nawfal b. Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzza, who was her cousin. He had earlier become a Christian, and used to write the Hebrew script, copying out from the Bible in Hebrew whatever God inspired him to write. He was an old man by then, and he was blind. "Khadija told him, 'O cousin! Listen to your nephew!' Waraqa then addressed him, 'O nephew, what did you see?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told him what he had seen. Waraqa commented, 'This was the angel Gabriel who used to come down to Moses. How I wish I were a young man again! I hope I am still alive when your people exile you!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) exclaimed, 'Are they to exile me?' 'Yes,' he replied, 'no one has ever received what you have without being treated as an enemy. If I am alive when your time comes, I will give you every help.'"
lessons: True support from loved ones can be the first source of calm in a moment of overwhelming fear and confusion.
A person of good character — one who upholds family ties, cares for the poor, and helps others — is never truly abandoned by God.
Seeking the counsel of knowledgeable and wise people during moments of spiritual or emotional crisis is a sign of wisdom, not weakness.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed or terrified by something unexpected, allow yourself to seek comfort from those closest to you before trying to process the experience alone.
Reflect on your own character and past deeds during moments of self-doubt — a record of goodness is a source of inner stability.
Identify a trusted, knowledgeable mentor or elder you can turn to when facing experiences that feel beyond your understanding.

title: Waraqa's Prophecy of Exile and His Death
summary: Waraqa ibn Nawfal prophesied that the Prophet would be exiled by his people, then died shortly after, leaving the Prophet without this early source of support during the pause in revelation.
story: Waraqa commented, 'This was the angel Gabriel who used to come down to Moses. How I wish I were a young man again! I hope I am still alive when your people exile you!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) exclaimed, 'Are they to exile me?' 'Yes,' he replied, 'no one has ever received what you have without being treated as an enemy. If I am alive when your time comes, I will give you every help.' "Not long thereafter Waraqa died, and the revelation waned for a period, so that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was so depressed - as we have been told - that he would frequently feel like throwing himself down from the summits of high mountains.
lessons: Foreknowledge of hardship, though painful, can be a form of mercy — it prepares the soul rather than leaving it blindsided.
The loss of a spiritual guide or mentor is itself a test that calls for renewed reliance on God rather than on any individual.
Every prophet and reformer has faced opposition and exile; one's own trials of rejection are part of a noble and ancient pattern.
practical_advice: When you receive news of coming hardship, resist the urge to deny or avoid it — acknowledge it so you can prepare your heart.
If you lose a mentor or guide who gave you strength, honour their legacy by continuing on the path they helped you see.
Remind yourself during periods of isolation or loss that difficulty is not evidence of abandonment by God, but often the very context in which divine support intensifies.

title: The Pause in Revelation and the Prophet's Distress
summary: After the initial revelation, divine communication paused for a period, causing the Prophet deep depression and despair, during which Angel Gabriel repeatedly appeared to reassure him of his prophethood whenever he approached the edge of a mountain.
story: Not long thereafter Waraqa died, and the revelation waned for a period, so that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was so depressed - as we have been told - that he would frequently feel like throwing himself down from the summits of high mountains. Whenever he reached the top of a mountain, to throw himself down, Gabriel would appear and say, 'O Muhammad, you are in truth the Messenger of God!' This would relieve his distress and he would return down. And if the inspiration was again long in coming, he would feel and do the same.
lessons: Even the greatest of people can experience periods of profound despair and spiritual emptiness — such states are not evidence of failure or divine rejection.
Divine support often arrives precisely at the moment of greatest crisis, not before — the timing itself is a form of mercy and a lesson in trust.
The silence of God is not the absence of God; periods of waiting are themselves part of the prophetic and spiritual journey.
practical_advice: During periods when you feel spiritually disconnected or as though your prayers are not being heard, hold firm to what you knew to be true before the darkness came.
Seek grounding — return to the earth, to nature, to the physical world — when spiritual despair makes everything feel abstract and unbearable.
Share your darkest moments with someone you trust rather than retreating entirely into isolation; the presence of another person can interrupt a spiral before it deepens.

title: The Second Revelation: The Command to Arise and Warn
summary: The Prophet encountered the Angel Gabriel seated on a throne between the sky and earth, was overwhelmed with terror, returned home asking to be wrapped up, and received the Quranic command to arise, warn, and glorify God, after which revelation became continuous.
story: Ibn Shihab stated that Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Rahman informed him that Jabir b. 'Abd Allah al-Ansari said, in relating matters concerning the revelation period, that the Prophet (SAAS) said, "While I was walking, I heard a voice from the sky. I raised my sight and there was the angel who had come to me at Hira' seated on a throne between the sky and the earth. I was terrified and returned home, saying, 'Wrap me up! Wrap me up!' And so God revealed the verse, 'O you who are all wrapped up! Arise and give warning! And glorify your Lord! Purify your garments! And shun idolatry!' (surat al-Muddaththir, LXXIV, v.1-5). And the revelation became stronger and continued uninterrupted.
lessons: A moment of terror and retreat can be the very threshold through which a great mission begins — withdrawal is sometimes preparation, not failure.
The divine command to 'arise and warn' emerged out of a moment of vulnerability and fear, teaching us that courage is not the absence of fear but action taken despite it.
When revelation or clarity returns after a long silence, it often comes with greater force and consistency than before — trust the process of preparation.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed and need to withdraw, allow yourself that moment of stillness — but remain open to the call that will come asking you to rise again.
Identify your personal mission or purpose clearly, so that when clarity returns after a period of confusion, you know immediately what direction to move in.
Establish daily practices of worship, gratitude, and purification that anchor you regardless of whether you feel spiritually inspired or spiritually dry.

title: The Age of the Prophet at the Time of His Mission and the Role of Angel Israfil
summary: Scholarly accounts discuss the Prophet's age at the time he received his mission, the role of Angel Israfil in preparing him for three years before Gabriel's arrival, and the total span of revelation across Mecca and Medina.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Muhammad b. Abi 'Adi related to him, from Dawud b. Abi Hind, from 'Amir al-Sha'bi, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) received his mission when he was 40 years old. The angel Israfil was entrusted with his prophethood for three years, during which he would teach him words and facts; the Qur'an was not revealed then. After those three years Gabriel was entrusted with his prophethood and the Qur'an was revealed through his voice over a 20-year period, 10 in Mecca and 10 in Medina. The Prophet (SAAS) died when he was 63 years old. This chain of authorities back to al-Sha'bi is a correct one. It establishes that Israfil was entrusted with him for a 3-year period after his age of 40, and that Gabriel came thereafter. Sheikh Shihab al-Din Abu Shama, however, has stated, "'A'isha's account does not refute this. It is possible that first of all he did receive visions, and that Israfil was put in charge of him during the period he spent alone in Hira', casting words at him at speed and not actually staying with him, and teaching him gradually. This was so until Gabriel came to him and taught him, after having throttled him three times."
lessons: Great missions are rarely launched all at once — preparation, gradual learning, and stages of growth precede moments of public calling.
Confusion about the details of one's path is natural; what matters is continued movement and openness to each stage as it arrives.
Scholarly disagreement and careful inquiry are themselves signs of intellectual honesty and the importance of not oversimplifying sacred history.
practical_advice: When you feel stuck in a preparatory phase of life with no clear results yet visible, remember that preparation is not wasted time — it is the foundation of what comes next.
Trust that different teachers, mentors, and experiences arrive in sequence for a reason, each building on the last.
If you feel confused by conflicting accounts or perspectives on your own spiritual journey, focus on what is consistent and confirmed rather than what is disputed.

title: The Prophet's Seclusion at Hira and His Discomfort with Idol Worship
summary: Before his prophethood, the Prophet Muhammad would retreat monthly to the cave of Hira for solitary prayer and would feed the poor who came to him, driven by his deep discomfort with the idol worship he saw among his people.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) only preferred the seclusion and being alone from his people because of the clear error in which he saw them engaged. This was in their worshipping and prostrating before idols. His liking for being alone increased as God's revelation to him came closer (SAAS). Muhammad b. Ishaq recounted, from 'Abd al-Malik b. 'Abd Allah b. Abi Sufyan b. al-'Ala' b. Haritha - who was, he said a very attentive listener - from a certain scholar who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to go out to Hira' for one month every year and pray alone, he being one of those Quraysh men who would, in the jahiliyya, practise this prayer in seclusion. He would feed the poor who would come to him. Having left there he would not re-enter his own home without first circumambulating the ka'ba."
lessons: A soul that is sensitive to injustice and falsehood will naturally seek solitude and reflection rather than comfortable conformity with the crowd.
Caring for the poor and maintaining spiritual practice can coexist as expressions of the same inner orientation toward goodness.
The desire for seclusion before a great mission is not antisocial — it is the soul creating space to hear what it needs to hear.
practical_advice: Build regular periods of solitude and reflection into your routine, even if brief — the noise of daily life can drown out what your heart most needs to process.
When you feel alienated from the values or behaviours of those around you, channel that discomfort into personal spiritual practice rather than bitterness.
Acts of generosity toward the vulnerable, even during your own periods of inner searching and confusion, are never wasted and help ground the soul in purpose.

title: Scholarly Debate on the Meaning of Tahannuth and the Prophet's Pre-Revelation Devotion
summary: Islamic scholars debate the linguistic meaning of 'tahannuth' and discuss what religion or devotion the Prophet Muhammad followed before receiving revelation, with various opinions presented.
story: interpreted from its form, lies in what alSuhayli suggested, namely, L'enteringinto al-hinth", i.e. sin or perjury. However, a few words in the language were heard to mean "the departure from" rather than "entering into". These words have a form like the verb tahannatha, i.e. "he left the state of hinth". Examples would be tahammaba, i.e. "he abstained from crime", and tahawa~a,i.e. "he kept away from sin", and ta'aththama, i.e. "he abstained from iniquity". Similarly there is rahaja&, a word meaning to give up al-hujiid, sleep to attend prayer. Also there are tanajasa, "he left an impure state", i.e. he cleaned himself, and tagadhdhara, "he avoided foul actions". Abii Shxma cited this example. Ihn al-A'rsbi was asked about his interpreting yatahannathu as meaning "be performs his devotions", and he replied, "Well, I don't actually know that, but there is the example of yatahannafu, derived from the word al-haniJyya, signifying the religion of Abraham, peace he upon him." Ihn Hisham stated, "The Arabs say al-tahannuth and al-tahannuf (as having the same meaning), where the letter f has replaced the letter th. This is similar to the change from jaddafa to jadhdhafa, both meaning "he rowed a boat". As Ru'ba's verse goes, U. . . if my stones were with a2-ajdhcif . . .", by which he means al-aj&th (the graves). Abii 'Ubayda related to me that the bedouin sayfumma for thumma, meaning "then". I would add that some commentators say ma f~muhd when they mean ma thiimuha, when referring to tham, i.e. "garlic". The scholars differ over the devotion to prayer of the prophet (SAAS) before his mission, whether he was following some religion, and what that was. Some say that it was the religion of Noah. Others suggest that of Abraham, it being the most similar as well as the most powerful. Others say that of Moses, yet others that of Jesus. Others say that all that is established is that he did have a religion, and that he followed and observed it.
lessons: Scholarly inquiry and careful analysis of language is a valued tradition in Islam, showing that seeking understanding is a form of worship.
The Prophet's sincere devotion before revelation demonstrates that a soul inclined toward goodness will find its way even before formal guidance arrives.
Uncertainty among scholars is not a weakness but an honest acknowledgment of the limits of human knowledge, while trust in God fills what remains unknown.
practical_advice: When you encounter confusing or uncertain situations, take time to gather different perspectives before drawing conclusions, just as scholars examined multiple interpretations.
Embrace the spiritual practice of sincere devotion even when you feel unclear about the path; consistency in seeking God is itself a form of guidance.
If you feel lost in your faith journey, remember that even the greatest figures experienced periods of seeking before clarity came — keep turning toward what is good.

title: The First Revelation Comes Suddenly in the Cave of Hira
summary: The first verses of the Quran were revealed to Prophet Muhammad unexpectedly in the cave of Hira on a Monday in the month of Ramadan, marking the beginning of his prophethood.
story: The words "eventually the truth came suddenly to h i while he was in the cave Hir2" mean that it came to him abruptly and unexpectedly. As God stated, "And you had no hope that the Book would be delivered to you, hut it is a mercy from your Lord" (smut al-Qajaj, XXVIII, v.86). The first portion of this holy chapter, namely "Read in the name of your Lord who created; He created man from a clot. Read! Your Lord is the most noble, He who taught by the pen. He taught man what he did not know" (swat al-'Alaq, XCVI, v.1-5) was the first part of the Qur'm revealed, as we estahlished in the commentary and in what will follow; it came on a Monday. It has been similarly affirmed in the jahih collection of Muslim, on the authority of Abii Qatada, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was asked about fasting on Mondays. He replied, "That is a day on which I was horn, and a day on which I received revelation." Ibn 'Abbas stated, "Your prophet Muhammad (SAAS) was born on a Monday, and he was made a prophet on a Monday." Wbayd b. Wmayr, Abii Jacfar al-Baqir, and several other scholars similarly stated that he received revelation on a Monday; this is a matter undisputed among them. It is said, moreover, that that occurred during the month of Rabi' al-Awwal; it has also been previously stated from Ibn 'Abhs and Jabir that he was born on the 12th of Rabi' al-Awwal, received his mission on the same date, and also on that day he was raised to heaven. It is also widely believed that he received his mission in Ramad%, as has been stated by Wbayd b. Wmayr, Muhammad h. Ishaq and others. Ibn Ishaq stated this, using as evidence God's statement "the month of Ramadan, during which the Qur'an was revealed as a guidance to mankind" (snrat al-Bagara, 11, v.185). And it is said that this was on the 10th. Al-W~qidi attributed to Ja'far al-Baqir, to whom his chain of authorities extended, the words, "The beginning of the revelation to the Messenger of God (SAAS) occurred on Monday, the 17th of Ramadm; others, however, say it was the 24th." Imzm Alpnad stated that Abii Sa'id, a freed-man of the Banii Hashim, related to him, quoting 'Umm Abii alLcAww-, from Qamda, from Abii al-Malih, from Wzthila b. al-Asqa', that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "The tablets of Abraham were revealed on the first night of Ramadan, the Torah on the 6th of Ramadan, and the Bible on the 13th of Ramadm, while the Qur'% was revealed on the 14th of Ramadm." Ibn Mardawayh related much the same in a tradition hack to the Prophet (SAAS) that is based on the authority of Jzbir b. 'Abd All&. Therefore, a majority of the Companions and followers of the Prophet (SAAS) have concluded that the laylar al-qadar, the "night of destiny", was the 24th.
lessons: The greatest blessings in life often arrive unexpectedly and without warning, reminding us that divine mercy operates on its own timeline.
The month of Ramadan holds extraordinary significance as the time when God chose to send guidance to humanity, a reminder of the spiritual power of this season.
The beginning of something momentous can feel overwhelming and sudden, yet it is precisely in such moments that God's mercy is most present.
practical_advice: When unexpected change or challenge arrives in your life, try to pause and consider that what feels sudden may be the beginning of something meaningful and divinely guided.
Use the month of Ramadan intentionally as a time of heightened connection with God, reflection, and spiritual renewal, drawing on its historical significance.

If you are going through a period of uncertainty about your purpose, take heart that even the Prophet did not anticipate his mission — clarity and purpose can arrive at any moment when you remain sincere.

title: Gabriel Seizes and Commands the Prophet to Read
summary: The angel Gabriel appeared to Prophet Muhammad in the cave of Hira and physically seized him three times, commanding him to read, while the Prophet responded that he did not know how to read.
story: Regarding Gabriel's order "Read!", the reply was, "I don't read." The truth is that this statement, "I don't read", is a negation, that is, it implies, "I am not a man who knows how to read well." Among those who prefer this interpretation are al-Nawawi and, before him, Sheikh Abii Shiima. Those who interpret this as interrogatory are incorrect, because the prefixed 6 2 , as in ma ana biqari, i.e. "I don't read", is not added if the intent is to a h . The first interpretation is substantiated by what Aba NuCaym related from a hadith that comes down through al-Mu'tamir b. Sulaymzn from his father who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, fearful and shaking, "I have never read a document; I don't do that well. I neither read nor write." And so Gabriel seized him and choked him violently. He then released him and told him, "Read!" Muhammad (SAAS) then replied, "I don't see anything to read; and I don't read, or write." It is also read in the formfa ghanani or ma ghagani in the two phi!, collections, or gad ghat~ani.These all mean "he choked me". The phrase hatta balagha minni al-juhd, "till I could hear it no more", is read either with a damma or with afatha on the j (i.e. al-juhd or al-jahd); also with a fatha or a damma on the third letter, d, for either the nominative or the accusative case (i.e. al-juhdu or al-juhda). He, Gabriel, did this to him three times. Abii Sulaymm al-Kharghi stated, "Gabriel only did that to test his patience and to discipline him so that he would be agreeable to bear the burdens of prophethood placed upon him. Because of these burdens he would be gripped by a feverish condition and have a high temperature, that is, he would breathe hard and sweat profusely." Others say that Gabriel acted in this way for various reasons. He wanted to awaken him to the gravity of what was being placed upon him by treating him in such a distressing fashion. As God Almighty stated, "We are indeed placing upon you a heavy message" (sgrat al-Muzzammil, LXXIII, v.5). This is why, when revelation came to him, his face would turn red and he would breathe heavily and fast, like a young camel, while his forehead would stream with perspiration on an extremely cold day.
lessons: Being called to a great responsibility may be accompanied by intense difficulty and discomfort, but this is a sign of the weight and importance of what is being asked of you.
God prepares those He chooses for heavy burdens through experiences that build endurance, even when those experiences feel frightening in the moment.
Admitting what you do not know, as the Prophet honestly declared his inability to read, is a mark of integrity and opens the door to divine teaching.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed or pressured by a responsibility placed on you, remind yourself that difficulty in carrying a meaningful task does not mean you are unworthy — it means the task matters.
Practice honesty about your limitations rather than pretending to capabilities you lack; this sincerity creates space for growth and for help to arrive.
In moments of intense stress, focus on your breathing and remember that even the Prophet of God felt fear and trembling — your anxiety in difficult moments is part of being human, not a failure.

title: The Prophet Returns Home Trembling and Khadija Comforts Him
summary: After the first revelation, Prophet Muhammad returned home in a state of fear and trembling, and his wife Khadija comforted him with reassuring words, reminding him of his noble character.
story: Regarding the statement quoted above, "The Messenger (SAAS) returned home to Khadija with this, his heart palpitating", there is a variant reading of bawadir for the word fu'ad, "heart", that is. Aba Wbayda explains this word as referring to the flesh between the shoulder and the neck. Others interpret the word as referring to veins that tremble because of fear. Some readings substitute the word baadiluhu, singular bndila or bndil, for that which palpitates. This would refer to the area between the neck and the collarbone. Others interpret it as the base of the breast, or as the flesh of the breast. There are also other interpretations of the word. Regarding the phrase, "Wrap me up! Wrap me up!", when his terror had calmed he asked Khadija, "What is wrong with me? What was it appeared to me?" and then related what had happened. He then said, "I was afraid for myself." This was because he had witnessed something he had never experienced or imagined before. That was why Khadija said to him, "Be of good cheer! Oh no! I swear by God, He would never abuse you." The word for "abuse" here is from al-khrzy (disgrace); others say the word is from al-huzn, meaning sadness, that is, "He would never sadden you." This statement relates to her knowledge of the fine behaviour characteristics God had instilled in him, and that those endowed with the qualities of goodness would not be abused in this world or the hereafter. She then made reference to those exemplary traits that made his fine character. She said, "You maintain family ties, you are truthful." The Prophet (SAAS) was famous for that among those both who agreed and disagreed with hi. The words ma tahmzlu al-kalla, i.e, "you carry the burden", that is, you do so for others imply "you provide for those in need what will alleviate their burden of supporting their family". The phrase ma taksrbu al-ma'dijma, translated here as "you provide for the poor", means that you hurry to do good by giving to the poor before others do so. The poor are called by the term alma'dtim because they are lacking in their lives, their existence or "non-existence" (a meaning also of this word) being the same. As a certain poet said, "He who is relieved by death did not die; there are those who are dead though they are still living." Abn d-Hasan a l - T i h m said, as was reported from him by al-qPi, the judge, 'Iyad, in the exegesis of Muslim, "The poor can be considered dead, their clothing a threadbare winding-sheet, their shelter the grave." Al-Kha<@bistated, "The correct text is tukszb al-mucdzm, meaning 'you give generously to them' or that you provide earnings for the poor by giving them money by which they can live." Our teacher, the ha43 Abn al-Hajjnj d-Mizzi, preferred to interpret "al-ma'diim" here as referring to the money being given, that is he gave money to those without it. Some interpret this phrase to mean, "you earn by doing business with goods that are macdiim", meaning unavailable, or valuable and scarce. But this is farfetched and contentious and contrived without basis. Such interpretations are rarely found laudable and qyad and al-Nawawi and others find these interpretations weak. But God knows best! The words ma taqr<al-dayftranslated above as "you are hospitable to guests", mean that you honour them by offering them food and good accommodation. The phrase tu'inu 'alz nawzlib al-haqq (the final word here being also given as al-khayr) translated ahove as "and help them out when tragedy strikes", means that if someone is struck by misfortune, you assist them; that is, you stand by them till they find some means of livelihood.
lessons: In moments of fear and confusion, the loving support of those close to us can be one of God's greatest mercies and a source of immense comfort.
A person's character — their kindness, truthfulness, generosity, and care for others — is their greatest protection and source of reassurance in difficult times.
Khadija's response teaches us that reminding someone of their good qualities during a crisis is one of the most powerful forms of emotional support we can offer.
practical_advice: When you or someone you love is overwhelmed by fear or a shocking experience, offer calm presence and words that remind them of their own strength and goodness rather than adding to their anxiety.
Cultivate virtuous habits in times of ease — maintaining family ties, honesty, generosity, and hospitality — because these become your foundation of stability when difficulty strikes.
If you are experiencing fear or distress after an intense experience, allow yourself to be comforted by those who love you and trust that your past goodness is a shield, not something to doubt.

title: Khadija Takes the Prophet to Waraqa ibn Nawfal for Counsel
summary: After the first revelation, Khadija took Prophet Muhammad to her cousin Waraqa ibn Nawfal, an elderly Christian scholar, who could help interpret what had befallen the Prophet.
story: Regarding the words, "Khadija then hurried off with him to Waraqa h. Nawfal . . . he was an old man by then, and blind." We gave some information concerning Waraqa in material above relating to Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl, God have mercy on him. We told how he had become a Christian before the coming of Islam and how he had left and travelled to Syria accompanied by Zayd b. 'Amr, Wthmw h. al-Huwayrith and Wbayd All& b. J&sh. All of these became Christians because they found Christianity to he
lessons: When facing something beyond our understanding, seeking counsel from those with knowledge and wisdom is a sign of good judgment, not weakness.
Khadija's swift action in taking the Prophet to Waraqa demonstrates that supporting a loved one in crisis means actively seeking help, not only offering words.
Even elderly and physically limited individuals like Waraqa can possess wisdom that is invaluable in moments of great uncertainty.
practical_advice: When you face a confusing or frightening experience, do not isolate yourself — seek out a trusted, knowledgeable person who can help you make sense of what you are going through.
Be the kind of person in others' lives who actively helps them find answers and support rather than leaving them to navigate difficulty alone.
Respect and seek out the wisdom of those who are older or more experienced, as their perspective can provide clarity that is difficult to find on your own.

title: Waraqa ibn Nawfal's Background and Conversion to Christianity
summary: An account of Waraqa ibn Nawfal's spiritual journey, his adoption of Christianity before Islam, and how he had long anticipated the coming of the Prophet Muhammad through Khadija's descriptions of him.
story: them till they find some means of livelihood. Regarding the words, "Khadija then hurried off with him to Waraqa h. Nawfal . . . he was an old man by then, and blind." We gave some information concerning Waraqa in material above relating to Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl, God have mercy on him. We told how he had become a Christian before the coming of Islam and how he had left and travelled to Syria accompanied by Zayd b. 'Amr, Wthmw h. al-Huwayrith and Wbayd All& b. J&sh. All of these became Christians because they found Christianity to he at that time the religion closest to the truth. Zayd h. 'Am b. Nufayl, however, considered that there were certain innovations, changes, corruptions and interpretations that had occurred in it that prevented him from embracing it. The rabbis and monks told him, moreover, about a prophet whose time was near. He therefore returned seeking information about this, while continuing to believe in one God, as he had before. But death cut him down before the mission of Muhammad (SAAS). Waraqa b. Nufayl did adopt Christianity, and used to try to prognosticate it in the Messenger of God (SAAS) as we have said above, from the way in which Khadija would describe and characterize him. She told Waraqa of the fine, pure qualities he had, and of the signs and indications there were upon hi.
lessons: Spiritual seeking is a noble endeavor even when one has not yet found the complete truth — what matters is sincerity and a genuine desire to know God.

Sometimes a person's preparation and waiting spans an entire lifetime, as Waraqa spent years anticipating the Prophet yet died shortly after confirming him — our roles in history may be brief but still profoundly meaningful.
Even those who never formally embraced Islam but acted with sincerity and goodness are treated with mercy by God, reminding us that divine justice accounts for circumstance and intention.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually lost or uncertain about your faith, take comfort in the example of those who sincerely sought truth — focus on what you know to be right and keep moving toward it.
If you are in a period of waiting — for clarity, for a sign, for something to change — use that time to build your character and knowledge, as Waraqa did, so you are ready when the moment comes.
Speak well of those who showed goodness, even if their path was different from yours; this is both a spiritual and emotional practice that nurtures gratitude and fairness in your heart.

title: Waraqa's Recognition of the Angel Gabriel and His Prophecy of Exile
summary: When Khadija brought the Prophet to Waraqa after the first revelation, Waraqa recognized the angel as Gabriel and warned that the Prophet would be opposed and exiled by his people, expressing his wish to live long enough to support him.
story: Therefore when there occurred as ahove, she took the hand of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, "0 Cousin, listen to what happened to your nephew." And when the Messenger of God (SAAS) related to him what he had seen, Waraqa replied, "By the All-Glorious God, this was the angel Gabriel who came down to Moses!"
