He did not mention Jesus, though he came later, after Moses, because the system of religion of Muhammad (SAAS) was to be a completion and fulfilment of that sent to Moses, upon both of whom be peace. The valid opinion of the scholars, in what they say on this point, is that the shanxa complemented, and also abrogated, certain things in the system of Moses. As God said, "And so that I may make permissible to you some of what was forbidden to you" ( m a t Al'Zmrzn, 111, v.49). This comment made by Waraqa is similar to that made by the spirits, namely, "0 our people, we have heard a book revealed after Moses verifying what was before it, guiding to the truth and to the straight path" (swat al-Ahq& XLVI, v.30). Waraqa then commented, "How I wish I were a young man again!" This implies, L'How I wish I were a lad endowed with faith, useful knowledge, and good work to do." The phrase: "I hope 1am still alive when your people exile you", implies "so that I can leave with you and help you". T o this the Prophet (SAAS) responded, "Are they to exile me?" According to al-Suhayli he said this because to be parted from one's homeland is very painful. T o this Waraqa replied, "Yes, no one has ever received what you have received without being treated as an enemy. If I am alive when your time comes, I will give you every help." That is, "I will strive hard to help you always." The words, LLNotlong thereafter Waraqa died" mean that his death came shortly after this event; may God have mercy on him and bless him. For what is told of him here constitutes credence in what had occurred, faith in the revelation that had come, and good intent for the future.
lessons: Truth is often met with opposition and rejection — the Prophet himself was warned from the very beginning that his people would treat him as an enemy, yet this did not stop the mission.
The pain of being separated from one's homeland and community is a deeply human experience acknowledged even in the Seerah — it is valid to grieve such losses.
A person of integrity, like Waraqa, pledges support and sincerity even at the threshold of death — the value of what we intend and commit to is not diminished by whether we live to fulfill it.
practical_advice: When you face rejection or opposition for standing by your values, remember that even the greatest of people endured the same — you are not alone, and opposition does not mean you are wrong.
If you are grieving a separation from home, family, or community, allow yourself to acknowledge that pain as real and legitimate; the Prophet himself found it painful to contemplate exile.

Identify at least one person in your life who genuinely supports your growth and wellbeing, and invest in that relationship — Waraqa's brief but powerful support meant everything in the Prophet's most vulnerable moment.

title: The Prophetic Reports on the Fates of Waraqa, Zayd ibn Amr, Abu Talib, and Khadija
summary: The Prophet Muhammad was asked about the fate in the afterlife of Waraqa ibn Nawfal, Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl, Abu Talib, and Khadija, and he gave distinct answers about each, reflecting on their spiritual stations.
story: The ha&? Abn Ya'lx recounted, from Shurayh b. Yiinus, from Isma'il, from Mujxhid, from al-Sha'bi, from Jxbir b. 'Ahd All&, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was asked about Waraqa b. Nawfal and he replied, "I saw him; and I saw on him white clothing. I observed h i in the heart of paradise and he was wearing a silken robe." He was asked about Zayd b. CAmr b. Nufayl, and he replied, "He will be resurrected on Judgement Day as one nation by himself." He was asked about Abn mib and he replied, "I drew him forth from a deep pool in hell to a shallow shore of it." He was asked about Khadija because she died before the rules and ordinances prescribed in the Q u r ' a . He said, "I saw her beside a river in paradise in a house made of reeds where there was no noise or hardship." The chain of authorities for this is good, and there are testimonies corroborating some of it in the jahih collections. But God knows best.
lessons: God's mercy is vast and His judgment is precise — each person is assessed according to their own unique circumstances, sincerity, and the light that was available to them.
Khadija's reward — a house of peace with no noise or hardship — reflects the magnitude of her sacrifices and steadfast support, reminding us that our unseen efforts are seen by God.

The Prophet's intercession even on behalf of Abu Talib, drawing him to a shallower place, teaches us that love, loyalty, and sincere prayer for others carries weight before God even across the most difficult divides.
practical_advice: When you worry about the fate of loved ones who may not have had the same access to faith as you, trust in God's perfect and individualized justice — your role is to pray for them and leave the judgment to Him.

Let Khadija's reward — rest, peace, and the absence of hardship — inspire you: the struggles you endure for the sake of others and for truth are building something beautiful for you in ways you cannot yet see.
Do not give up on praying for those you love, even those who seem far from faith — the Prophet's concern and intercession for Abu Talib is a model of enduring, faithful love.

title: Abu Bakr and the Prophet's Visit to Waraqa After the First Revelation
summary: After the first revelation, Khadija sent Abu Bakr with the Prophet to visit Waraqa, who advised him to remain still when the voice came again, and then witnessed the revelation of Surah al-Fatiha and the declaration of faith.
story: The two hufzz al-Bayhaqi and Abn Nukym related in both their works entitled Dalz'cl al-Nubuwwa (Scgns of the Prophethood) from a hadith of Yiinus b. Bukayr, from Yiinus b. 'Amr, from his father, from 'Amr b. Shar&bil, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to Khadija, "When I was left to myself, I heard a cry and, by God, I feared that something terrible was going to happen." She replied, "God forbid! He would never do anything to you! By God, you behave with loyalty, you maintain the ties of kinship, and speak truthfully." And when Abii Bakr came in, the Messenger of God (SAAS) was no longer there so she recounted it all to him, saying, "Good old friend, go with Muhammad to Waraqa." When the Messenger of God came in, Aha Bakr took his hand and said, "Let's go at once to Waraqa." He asked, "Who told you?" "Khadija." And so they went at once to Waraqa and related it to him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, "When I am all alone I hear a voice behind me saying, 'Muhammad! 0 Muhammad!' So I rush off outside." Waraqa told him, "Don't do that. When it comes, stay right there to hear what it says to you. Then come and tell me." And when he was alone, the voice did call out again, saying, "Muhammad! 0 Muhammad! Say, 'In the name of God the most Merciful, the most Beneficent; praise be to God, Lord of the worlds . . ." And it continued right on (to the end of the sarat al-Fatcha; I) to the words, ma la al-d~lina,'nor those who go astray'. It then said, "Say: 'There is no God but God!'" So he went to Waraqa and told h i that. Waraqa replied, "Rejoice, then rejoice again! I bear witness that you are he of whom the son of Mary spoke. What you have is like what came to the angel Gabriel for Moses. You are a Prophet from God. You will be ordered into the struggle some time hereafter. And if I live that long, I will fight along with you!" When he died the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I saw the priest in heaven wearing silken garments, because he believed in me and believed what I said." He was referring to Waraqa.
lessons: When facing the unknown and the overwhelming, the counsel of a wise and knowledgeable person can provide grounding — the Prophet was advised simply to be still and listen, and that changed everything.
Khadija and Abu Bakr's immediate, practical support in a moment of the Prophet's vulnerability models what true companionship looks like: they did not minimize his fear, they took action.

Belief and sincerity, even when brief, carry eternal weight — Waraqa's few moments of faith earned him a place in paradise, reminding us never to underestimate the power of a genuine moment of trust in God.
practical_advice: When something frightening or overwhelming happens, resist the urge to immediately flee or shut down — sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is stay present, breathe, and listen for clarity.
Surround yourself with people who take your experiences seriously and help you find wise counsel rather than leaving you to spiral alone — this is the Sunnah of community support in crisis.

If you feel unworthy of God's mercy because your faith has been inconsistent or late in coming, remember Waraqa — sincerity at the right moment, with a willing heart, is enough for God to show His grace.

title: Waraqa ibn Nawfal's Poetry Anticipating the Prophet's Mission
summary: Waraqa ibn Nawfal composed verses expressing his longing to witness the Prophet's mission, his conviction in Muhammad's truth, and his hope to stand alongside him, even as he faced the possibility of dying before that day arrived.
story: We have given above some of the poetry of Waraqa that shows his concealed faith in him, his confidence in it and his dedication to it. This was when Khadija told h i what had happened when the Messenger of God (SAAS) had been with the youth Maysara, and how the cloud had been sheltering him in the extreme heat. Waraqa spoke some verses on that occasion, as we have already given above. These were: "I persisted, being determined to recall both a matter that often inspired my tears And a description from Khadija, following (another) account; my waiting has been long, 0 Khadija. In the heart of Mecca, with my hoping because of your words, that I would see some solution. From what you related as the words of a priest who was a monk, words I would hate to be wrong, That Muhammad will one day prevail and defeat whomever opposes him Making a light appear in the land by which he will bring change to all creation. Those who oppose him will meet defeat, while those who aid him will achieve success. Would that I be there to witness, for I'll be the first of them in participating. Sharing in that which Quraysh will hate, however much they bluster in their Mecca. I aspire, through him whom they all hate, to reach the Enthroned One, even if they descend, aside. Is it folly to not disbelieve in Him who chooses, H e who raised the stars? If they and I survive, things will happen at which the disbelievers will be sorely discomfited." H e also spoke the following verses i n another ode: "By news of veracity you foretold of Muhammad, telling them about him, an adviser being absent, Informing that the son of 'Abd All*, b a d , is sent to all men on earth. My thought of him is that he will be sent to speak the truth, just as the two slaves Hiid and Sd& were sent And Moses and Abraham, so that a brightness will be seen and a clear emanation of the truth. And there will follow him the two tribes of Lu'ayy b. Gh%tib,both the young men and the lordly greybeards. And if I survive until his age arrives for man, the11 I will greet him in joy and love. If not, then I will certainly, 0 Khadija please know this, be travelling somewhere above the b ~ o a dearth." Yanus b. Bukayr stated, o n the authority of I h n Ishiiq, that Waraqa also spoke t h e following: "If it be true, 0 Khadija, then know that your account to us means that Ahmad is sent. And Gabriel will bring to him, Michael being with them both, from God a revelation that relieves the heart sent down. Through him there will succeed those who have success through repentance, and through him will suffer those in distress, those deceived and led astray. Two groups of them there will be: one will go to God's gardens, and the other will be kept in pain within the enclosures of hell. Ethey call out in woe therein, iron staffs will be inserted in their heads in succession, and be put in flames. And so, glory be to Him at whose order the winds blow, and Who in all time does as He wishes, He whose throne is above a l l the heavens, and He whose decrees made for those He has created"
lessons: Waraqa's poetry reveals that a heart filled with longing for truth and goodness can be a form of worship — even when circumstances prevent full participation, sincere yearning has spiritual worth.

The pain of potentially missing something you have waited your whole life for is real and valid — Waraqa faced it with grace, saying that if he did not survive he would be 'travelling somewhere above the broad earth', trusting in God's plan.
Hope expressed honestly, even in the form of poetry or personal reflection, can be a powerful act of faith — it keeps the heart oriented toward God when circumstances feel uncertain.
practical_advice: If you are in a long season of waiting — for healing, for change, for something you deeply long for — find a creative outlet such as writing, poetry, or journaling to process and honor that longing rather than suppressing it.

When you fear you may not live to see the fruits of what you have hoped and worked for, remind yourself that your sincere intention and effort are already complete acts in God's sight — the outcome belongs to Him.

Read or recite words that affirm your trust in God's sovereignty, as Waraqa did in his verses glorifying God's power over creation — this practice of praise in uncertain times is a proven source of inner peace.

title: Rocks and Trees Greeting the Prophet Before Revelation
summary: Before the first revelation came, the Prophet Muhammad would walk into the valleys and pathways of Mecca and hear rocks and trees greeting him as the Messenger of God, though he would turn and see nothing but the natural surroundings.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that 'Abd al-Malik b. 'Abd Allah b. Abi Sufyan b. al-'Ala b. Jariyya al-Thaqafi related to him - and he had a fine ear and memory - on the authority of a scholar, that the Messenger of God (SAAS), after God wished to honour him by the beginning of the prophethood, would walk far off to do his business, out of sight of dwellings. As he went off into the pathways of Mecca and deep into its valleys, every rock and tree he passed would greet him, saying, 'Peace be upon you, 0 Messenger of God!' Hearing this, he would turn all around yet see nothing but the trees and rocks. This situation prevailed, with him seeing and hearing as long as God wished, until ultimately Gabriel brought God's honour upon him during Ramadan in Hira'.
lessons: The divine prepares its chosen ones gradually, through signs and experiences that build readiness before a greater responsibility arrives.
Unexplained and awe-inspiring experiences can precede a life-changing turning point — not every strange moment is a cause for fear.
Being chosen for a great purpose does not always come with immediate clarity; the journey toward one's mission can involve puzzling and disorienting experiences.
practical_advice: When you experience confusing or inexplicable moments in life, resist the urge to panic — reflect on whether something larger may be unfolding.
Keep a journal of unusual or meaningful experiences; patterns often become clear only in hindsight.
If you feel lost or uncertain about your direction in life, increase your time in solitude and reflection, as clarity often comes in quiet moments away from noise.

title: The Prophet's Disturbing Dreams Before Revelation and Khadija's Reassurance
summary: Before Gabriel appeared openly, the Prophet experienced disturbing visions in his sleep that he shared with Khadija, who responded with unwavering faith and reassurance that God would bring only good.
story: Musa b. 'Uqba stated, from al-Zuhri, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, as follows, 'From what we have been told, the first thing he saw - meaning the Messenger of God (SAAS) - were visions God revealed to him while he was asleep. These greatly disturbed him and he told his wife Khadija of them. God protected her from scepticism about them, opening her heart to belief. And so she said, 'Rejoice! For God has never done anything but good.' 'Thereafter he went off, but returned later to tell her how he had seen his stomach split open, and it had been washed and cleaned and all restored as before. To this she said, 'By God, I swear that this is good; so rejoice!'
lessons: Sharing your fears and disturbing experiences with a trusted, faith-filled person can transform anxiety into peace.
A believing heart that is protected from scepticism is among the greatest gifts God can give a person, both to them and to those around them.
Cleansing and restoration, even when symbolically represented in dreams, can signal a divine preparation for a new and elevated state of being.
practical_advice: When troubled by vivid or disturbing dreams, share them with someone grounded in faith and wisdom rather than letting them fester in isolation.
Practice responding to loved ones' fears with reassurance rooted in your trust in God, as Khadija did — your calm can be transformative for them.
Reflect on difficult experiences in your past that ultimately led to growth or purification; use these memories to build confidence that current hardships may also lead to good.

title: Gabriel Appears Openly, Trees and Rocks Greet the Prophet, and Khadija Consults 'Addas and Waraqa
summary: After Gabriel appeared openly to the Prophet on the heights of Mecca, confirming his mission, the Prophet returned home joyful; Khadija then sought confirmation from a Christian youth named 'Addas and from Waraqa ibn Nawfal, both of whom affirmed Gabriel's divine role.
story: 'Then Gabriel appeared before him while he was on the heights above Mecca, sitting him down on a wonderful seat of honour. The Prophet (SAAS) used to say, 'He sat me down on a rug of durnak, velvet, that was decorated with pearls and precious stones.' Gabriel then announced to him the mission from God the Almighty and Glorious, and put the Messenger of God (SAAS) at ease. Gabriel then said to him, 'Read!' He replied, 'How shall I read?' Gabriel answered, 'Read: in the name of your Lord who created. He created man from a clot. Read! your Lord is the most noble, He who taught by the pen. He taught man what he did not know.' 'And when he was returning back home, every tree and rock he passed greeted him. And so he went back to his family in good spirits, convinced that he had seen something stupendous. When he went in to see Khadija, he said, 'You remember what I told you I saw in a vision? Well, Gabriel has now appeared plainly before me. My Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, sent him to me.' He then told her what Gabriel had told him to do and what he had heard him say. She responded, 'Rejoice! For God will never bring you anything but good. And so accept the order that has come to you from God, for it is the truth. And rejoice, for you are in truth the Messenger of God.' 'She then left her home and set off to visit a youth who belonged to 'Utba b. Rabi'a b. 'Abd Shams, a Christian originally from Nineveh, who was called 'Addas. She said to him, 'I adjure you by God to tell me, do you have any knowledge about Gabriel?' He replied, 'Holy! Holy! How is it that mention is made of Gabriel in this land, all of whose people are idol worshippers?' She replied, 'Tell me what you know of him!' 'Gabriel', he replied, 'is God's trusty servant, God's intercessor between Himself and the prophets. He it was who accompanied Moses and Jesus, upon both of whom be peace.' 'Khadija then came back from visiting him and went to see Waraqa b. Nawfal.'
lessons: When a momentous truth arrives, a wise and loving person seeks multiple sources of confirmation rather than acting on impulse alone.
The natural world's response to the Prophet's presence — trees and rocks offering greetings — reminds us that all of creation exists in a state of remembrance and submission to God.
Khadija's persistent search for confirmation through 'Addas and Waraqa models the virtue of due diligence in matters of great spiritual significance.
practical_advice: When you receive what feels like an important sign or direction in life, verify it by consulting knowledgeable and trustworthy people from different backgrounds.
In moments of joy and confirmation, as when the Prophet returned home in good spirits, take time to share your gratitude and experiences with those closest to you.

Study the lives of earlier prophets and righteous people; their stories, as 'Addas' knowledge of Gabriel showed, can become the very lens through which we recognize truth when it appears in our own time.

title: Khadija Consults the Slave 'Addas About Gabriel
summary: After the first revelation, Khadija sought confirmation about the angel Gabriel from a slave named 'Addas, who described Gabriel's role with Moses and Jesus, deepening her certainty about her husband's prophethood.
story: She said to him, 'I adjure you by God to tell me, do you have any knowledge about Gabriel?' He replied, 'Holy! Holy! How is it that mention is made of Gabriel in this land, all of whose people are idol worshippers?' She replied, 'Tell me what you know of him!' 'Gabriel', he replied, 'is God's trusty servant, God's intercessor between Himself and the prophets. He it was who accompanied Moses and Jesus, upon both of whom be peace.'
lessons: Seeking knowledge and confirmation from multiple sources is a sign of wisdom and sincerity, not weakness.
The truth of prophethood transcended cultural and religious boundaries, as even a slave in a polytheistic land recognized Gabriel's significance.
Those who are spiritually aware can serve as unexpected confirmations of divine truth.
practical_advice: When facing uncertainty about something important, seek perspectives from knowledgeable people across different backgrounds.
Do not dismiss confirmations of truth simply because they come from unexpected sources.
Use the convergence of multiple testimonies to strengthen your conviction in moments of doubt.

title: Waraqa ibn Nawfal Confirms the Prophethood After Khadija's Account
summary: Khadija visited her cousin Waraqa ibn Nawfal and relayed the account of the first revelation, and Waraqa, a scholar of the scriptures, affirmed that the visitor was Gabriel and that Muhammad was the awaited prophet of the People of the Book.
story: "Khadija then came back from visiting him and went to see Waraqa b. Nawfal. She told him of what had happened to the Prophet (SAAS) and what Gabriel had commanded to him. Waraqa said to her, 'O fond daughter of my brother, I just don't know. Perhaps your companion is the prophet who is awaited by the People of the Book (i.e. Scriptures), about whom they find it written in their Torah and Bible. But I swear by God, that if this is he and he openly proclaims his mission while I am still alive, I will express my dedication to God by obedience to His Messenger and by giving him every assistance in maintaining fortitude and in achieving victory.' Thereafter Waraqa, God have mercy on him, died." Al-Zuhri stated, "Khadija was the first person to believe in God and to give credence to His Messenger (SAAS)."
lessons: Scholars grounded in scripture are often best positioned to recognize the signs of divine truth when they appear.
A sincere pledge to support truth, even one unfulfilled due to death, reflects nobility of character.
Khadija's role as the first believer demonstrates that those closest to a prophet often perceive his truthfulness most clearly.
practical_advice: Seek guidance from those with deep knowledge of scripture and history when you encounter something spiritually significant and unfamiliar.
Express your commitment to truth even when you are uncertain whether you will live to see it through — the intention itself carries value.
Support those around you who are burdened with great responsibility by seeking answers on their behalf.

title: Gabriel's First Appearance in the Cave of Hira': The Moment of the First Revelation
summary: While secluding himself in the cave of Hira', the Prophet Muhammad received the first revelation from Gabriel, who embraced him and commanded him to read, leaving the Prophet trembling with fear and uncertainty about what had befallen him.
story: "One day when he was in the cave Hira', where he would seclude himself from his people, Gabriel came down to him. As he approached, the Messenger of God (SAAS) was mightily afraid, so Gabriel placed one hand on his chest and the other on his back between his shoulders and said, 'O God, unburden him, and give him relief! Cleanse his heart! O Muhammad, rejoice! For you are the Prophet of this nation. Read!' The Prophet of God (SAAS) then replied, trembling with fear, 'I have never read any document; I don't read well. I neither write nor read.' At this Gabriel took hold of him and choked him hard, then released him and said, 'Read!' And the same happened as before. Then Gabriel seated him upon a soft rug of the durnak type of such richness and splendour as to remind him of pearls and precious stones. Gabriel then told him, 'Read: in the name of your Lord who created . . .' and so on to the end of the verses. He then said to him, 'O Muhammad, do not be afraid. You are the Messenger of God!' Then he left. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was overcome by anxiety and he asked himself, 'What am I to do? And what can I say to my people?'"
lessons: Even the greatest of God's messengers experienced fear and confusion at the onset of their divine mission, showing that such feelings do not disqualify one from God's service.
Divine assignments often begin with overwhelming experiences that exceed our current capacity, yet God equips those He chooses.
The command to 'read' despite the Prophet's illiteracy teaches that God's power operates beyond human limitation.
practical_advice: When you are overwhelmed by something you feel unequipped to handle, remember that God does not assign a burden without also providing the means to bear it.
Allow yourself to feel fear in difficult moments — what matters is not the absence of fear but your willingness to move forward despite it.
In moments of confusion after an overwhelming experience, give yourself time to process before seeking answers, as the Prophet did by returning home.

title: Trees and Rocks Greet the Prophet on His Return from Hira'
summary: As the Prophet walked home after the first revelation, every tree and rock turned to him and said 'Peace be upon you, O Messenger of God,' calming his soul and confirming in him the honour God had bestowed.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then arose afraid, but Gabriel appeared before him in all his splendour. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw a sight that filled him with wonder, and Gabriel told him, 'O Muhammad, be you not afraid! Gabriel is God's messenger. Gabriel is God's messenger to His prophets and His messengers. Be secure in God's nobility. You are the Messenger of God.' As the Messenger of God (SAAS) went home every tree and rock bowed down before him, saying, 'Peace be upon you, O Messenger of God!' And so his soul was calmed, and he was sure of God's having honoured him."
lessons: God provides comfort and reassurance through unexpected and extraordinary means when His servant is in a state of fear.
The created world bears witness to the dignity of those whom God has honoured — a reminder that divine appointment carries cosmic significance.
Inner peace after a moment of overwhelming fear is itself a sign of divine support.
practical_advice: Look for signs of reassurance in the world around you during moments of spiritual distress — God's comfort can come through the most unexpected channels.
When you have experienced something overwhelming and sacred, allow the subsequent calm to settle into your heart as confirmation rather than chasing more proof.
Trust that when God assigns you a role, the world around you will in some way align to support that purpose.

title: Khadija Consults the Monk Near Mecca and Then 'Addas About Gabriel
summary: After comforting the Prophet, Khadija went to a nearby monk and then to 'Addas, a slave of 'Utba ibn Rabi'a, both of whom described Gabriel's role alongside Moses and Jesus, further confirming to Khadija the divine nature of her husband's experience.
story: "She then went off to see the monk, who lived close by to Mecca. When she drew near and he recognized her, he said, 'What is wrong, O mistress of all the women of Quraysh?' She replied, 'I have come to you for you to tell me about Gabriel.' He replied, 'Glory be to God, our most holy Lord! How is it that Gabriel is mentioned here, in this land whose people worship idols? Gabriel is the trusted servant of God, and his envoy to His prophets and to His messengers. It is he who was the companion of Moses and Jesus.' And so she knew how much God was honouring Muhammad. Then she went to a slave of 'Utba b. Rabi'a called 'Addas, and questioned him. He told her the same as the monk had, and more besides. He said, 'Gabriel was with Moses when God drowned Pharaoh and his men. He was also there when God spoke to Moses on Mt. al-Tur. And he it was who accompanied Jesus, son of Mary, and he through whom God aided Jesus.'"
lessons: Proactive inquiry in the face of spiritual uncertainty is a mark of sincere faith and deep love.
The consistent testimony of multiple independent witnesses is one of the most reliable ways to confirm truth.
Gabriel's repeated role across prophetic missions reveals the continuity and unity of God's divine plan throughout history.
practical_advice: When seeking clarity about something spiritually significant, do not rely on a single source — seek multiple perspectives from knowledgeable people.
Approach your search for truth with persistence and humility, as Khadija did, asking question after question until the picture became clear.
Use historical and scriptural evidence to anchor your faith when it is tested by new and unfamiliar experiences.

title: Khadija Consults Waraqa ibn Nawfal and He Pledges to Follow the Prophet
summary: Khadija went to Waraqa ibn Nawfal, swore him to secrecy, and told him of the revelation. Waraqa affirmed that Gabriel only descends to prophets and pledged that if he lived to see the Prophet's mission, he would follow and support him.
story: "She then left 'Addas and went to Waraqa b. Nawfal whom she asked about Gabriel. He told her the same. He then asked her what was the matter, and she made him swear not to divulge what she would tell him. He so swore, and she told him, 'The son of 'Abd Allah related to me — and he is truthful and never, I swear it, either told a lie or was accused of lying — that Gabriel came down to him in Hira', told him he was the prophet of this nation, and made him recite some verses he had been sent with.' Waraqa was dumbfounded at this, and said, 'If Gabriel has actually placed his feet upon earth, he has done so for the best of people thereupon. And he never came down for anyone except a prophet. For he is the companion of all the prophets and messengers, the one whom God sends down to them. I believe what you tell me of him. Send for 'Abd Allah's son, so that I may question him, hear what he says and talk to him. I am afraid it may be someone other than Gabriel, for certain devils imitate him and by so doing can mislead and corrupt some men. This can result in a man becoming confused and even crazy whereas before he had been of sound mind.' Khadija arose and left him, confident that God would never do anything but good to her husband."
lessons: True scholarship includes both affirmation and discernment — Waraqa confirmed the revelation while also urging caution against deception.
Confidence in God's protection over a righteous loved one is a powerful spiritual anchor in times of uncertainty.
The willingness to pledge loyalty to truth before one has all the details is a hallmark of genuine faith.
practical_advice: When sharing something deeply personal and significant, choose your confidants wisely and establish trust before speaking.
Do not let the possibility of deception paralyze you from pursuing truth — seek to verify while maintaining openness.
Carry confidence in God's goodness toward the righteous people in your life; let that confidence be your calm in moments of fear.

title: Waraqa ibn Nawfal Meets the Prophet Directly and Bears Witness to His Prophethood
summary: The Prophet went to Waraqa ibn Nawfal at Khadija's encouragement, described Gabriel and the revelation, and Waraqa formally bore witness that it was Gabriel and that the words were from God, pledging to follow him if he lived long enough.
story: "So she returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him what Waraqa had said. God Almighty then sent down the words: 'Nun. By the pen, and what they write! You are not, by grace of God, mad!' and so on to the end of these verses (surat al-Qalam, LXIII, v.1). He then told her, 'Absolutely not, by God! It was Gabriel!' She told him, 'I'd like you to go to him and inform him, so that God might give him guidance.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) did go to Waraqa who asked him, 'Did he who came to you appear in lightness or in the dark?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) described Gabriel to him and his majesty, and what he had revealed to him. Waraqa then said, 'I bear witness that that was Gabriel, and that these were words spoken by God. He has ordered you to convey certain things to your people. It is a matter of prophethood, and if I live on into your era, I will follow you.' He then said, 'Rejoice, O son of 'Abd al-Muttalib!'"
lessons: Direct conversation with those who carry divine responsibility is a means of receiving clarity and bearing witness to truth.
The testimony of a learned elder who affirms rather than dismisses is an immense gift to someone beginning a momentous and uncertain mission.
God's revelation responded to the Prophet's inner anxiety by affirming his sanity — showing that divine support is timely and directly responsive to need.
practical_advice: When you are struggling with doubt about your purpose or calling, seek out wise and knowledgeable mentors who can help you see what you cannot see yourself.
Do not be afraid to describe your experiences honestly to those you trust — transparency invites affirmation and discernment.
When someone in your life receives guidance or reassurance, encourage them to seek further confirmation from those equipped to give it.

title: The Faltering of Revelation and the Community's Doubt
summary: After the first revelation, the coming of divine revelation paused for a period, leading people to say that God had abandoned the Prophet, until God sent down Surat al-Duha and Surat al-Inshirah in their entirety.
story: "And thereafter the revelation faltered. So people said, 'If he had come from God, he would have continued. But God is displeased with him.' And so God sent down the suras of al-Duha (XCIII) and Alam Nashrah (al-Inshirah, XCIV) in all their entirety."
lessons: Periods of silence and pause in one's spiritual journey are not signs of divine abandonment but often precede deeper confirmation and growth.
The cruelty of public doubt and mockery during a person's most vulnerable spiritual moment reveals the importance of building a close circle of unwavering support.
God's response to the pause in revelation — with two entire chapters of comfort and affirmation — shows that He addresses our deepest fears of being forgotten.
practical_advice: When you feel that God has gone silent in your life, resist the temptation to interpret that silence as rejection — it may be preparation.
Do not measure the authenticity of your spiritual journey by the comments of those who stand outside it.
Return to the words of Surat al-Duha and Surat al-Inshirah when you feel spiritually abandoned — they were revealed precisely for that feeling.

title: Khadija's Test of the Angel and the Prophet's Reassurance
summary: Khadija tested whether the visitor was an angel or devil by placing the Prophet inside her shift, causing Gabriel to depart, which settled her faith. The Prophet was further reassured by trees and rocks greeting him as the Messenger of God.
story: stated, "I told 'Abd Allāh b. Hasan this story and he commented, 'I heard my mother Fāṭima relate this account from Khadija, except that I heard her say, "She took the Messenger of God (SAAS) inside her shift, and it was then that Gabriel, upon whom he peace, went away."'" Al-Bayhaqi stated, "This was something that Khadija did to settle the matter, to preserve her faith and affirm her credence." The Prophet (SAAS) was secure in what Gabriel had said to him, in the verses he had revealed to him which we have recounted several times, and in the fact of the trees and rocks having made salutation to him. Muslim stated in his sahih collection that Abū Bakr b. Abū Shayba related to him, quoting Yahya b. Bukayr, quoting Ibrāhim b. Tahmān, and Simāk b. Harb, from Jabir b. Samura, God be pleased with him, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I know one rock in Mecca that used to greet me even before I received my mission; I still know it now." Abū Dāwūd al-Tayalisi stated that Sulaymān b. Mu'ādh related to him, from Simak b. Harb, from Jabir b. Samura, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "There is in Mecca a rock that used to greet me on the nights when I was receiving my mission; I recognize it when I pass by it." Al-Bayhaqi related, from a tradition of Ismā'il b. 'Abd al-Rahmān al-Suddi al-Kabir, from 'Abbād b. 'Abd Allāh, from 'Ali b. Abū Tālib, God be pleased with him, who said, "We were with the Messenger of God in Mecca, walking in various parts of the town, and no tree nor mountain received him without saying, 'Peace be upon you, O Messenger of God.'" And in another account he said, "When I entered the valley in his company, he would not pass by a single rock or tree without its saying, 'Peace be upon you, O Messenger of God'; I heard this."
lessons: Divine reassurance comes through multiple channels — when one form of confirmation arrives, others follow to strengthen the believer's certainty.
The faith of those closest to us, like Khadija's careful and loving verification, is itself a mercy and a source of stability during times of spiritual uncertainty.
Creation itself bears witness to truth; when we walk a righteous path, there is harmony between us and the world around us.
practical_advice: When you feel uncertain about a major decision or spiritual experience, seek grounded, trusted counsel from someone who loves you and has wisdom — as Khadija did for the Prophet.
In moments of self-doubt, recall the consistent signs and affirmations in your life that have pointed you toward your purpose.

Practice mindfulness in nature; the Prophet found reassurance even in rocks and trees. Spending time in natural surroundings can reconnect you to a sense of being held and supported by something greater.

title: The Prophet's Distress During the Intermission of Revelation
summary: After revelation paused, the Prophet experienced such intense grief and despair that he felt like throwing himself from mountaintops, but Gabriel appeared each time to reassure him of his prophethood.
story: Al-Bukhāri stated in his account given above, "then the revelation waned, so that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was so depressed, as we have been told, that he would often feel like throwing himself down from the summits of high mountains. Whenever he reached the top of a mountain to throw himself down, Gabriel would appear to him and say, 'O Muhammad, you are in truth the Messenger of God.' This would relieve his distress and he would return down. And if the revelation was again long in coming, he would feel and do the same. When he would reach the mountain summit, Gabriel would appear and speak to him as before."
lessons: Even the greatest of God's messengers experienced overwhelming grief and despair; struggling emotionally is not a sign of weakness or faithlessness.
Divine help does not always arrive before the moment of greatest need — sometimes it comes precisely at the edge, to teach us that we are never truly alone.
Periods of silence or spiritual dryness do not mean abandonment; they are often preparation for a renewed and more powerful phase of purpose.
practical_advice: When you feel that divine connection or sense of purpose has gone silent, resist catastrophic conclusions — the silence is often a pause, not an ending.
Reach out to someone trustworthy when despair feels overwhelming, just as the Prophet returned to Khadija and to the reassurance of those around him.
If you notice yourself caught in cycles of hopeless thinking, acknowledge the pattern and seek support — from a counselor, imam, or trusted friend — before reaching a breaking point.

title: The Vision of Gabriel on the Throne and the Revelation of Surah Al-Muddaththir
summary: During the intermission of revelation, the Prophet saw Gabriel seated on a throne between the heavens and the earth, was seized with trembling, and returned home to be wrapped up, after which Surah Al-Muddaththir was revealed, launching the public mission.
story: In both the ṣaḥīḥ collections there is a tradition from 'Abd al-Razzāq, from Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, who said that he heard Abū Salama 'Abd al-Rahmān relate from Jābir b. 'Abd Allāh, who said that he heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) talking about the intermission in the revelation, say, "While I walked, I heard a voice from heaven. I raised my sight towards the sky and there I saw the angel who came to me in Ḥirā', seated upon a throne amidst the sky. I knelt down before him in fear, right down to the ground, then went to my family and said, 'Wrap me up! Wrap me up!' And so God sent down, 'O you who are wrapped up! Arise and give warning! And glorify your Lord! Purify your garments! And shun idolatry!' (sūrat al-Mudaththir, LXXIV, v.1-5). He went on, 'And then the revelation became stronger and continued uninterrupted.'" The above verse was the first of the Qur'an sent down after the intermission in revelation, though not the very first of all, which was the verse, "Read: in the name of your Lord who created" (sūrat al-'Alaq, XCVI, v.1). It is established on the authority of Jābir that the first verse to be revealed was, "O you who are wrapped up." It is perhaps appropriate to interpret his words as we have suggested, for in the sequence of what he states, there is evidence for the angel having come down earlier, as he recognized him from earlier. Moreover, in Jābir's words making reference to the "intermission in the revelation", there is a proof that revelation did precede this instance. But God knows best. It is established in both ṣaḥīḥ collections, from an account of 'Ali b. al-Mubarak, as well as in the works of Muslim and al-Awzā'i, both of whom also quote Yaḥya b. Abū Kathir as having said, "I asked Abū Salama b. 'Abd al-Rahmān, 'Which part of the Qur'ān was revealed before?' He replied, 'The verse, "O you who are wrapped up."' 'I asked, "And the verse, 'Read in the name of your Lord who created'?" He replied, 'I asked Jābir b. 'Abd Allāh which verse of the Qur'ān came earliest, and he replied, "It was 'O you who are wrapped up.'" So I asked him, "What about 'Read in the name of your Lord who created'?" and he answered, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'I spent a month in seclusion in Ḥirā' and when it was over I went down into the heart of the valley. I heard a voice calling out to me and I looked ahead, behind me and to left and right, but I saw nothing. Then I looked up at the sky and there he was, on a throne in the air. I was seized by a trembling" — or he used the word "anxiety" instead of the word "a trembling" — "and went on to Khadija. She ordered them and they wrapped me up. And then God revealed the verse, 'O you who are wrapped up . . .' and so on till the verse, 'and purify your garments.'"" According to a variant account he used the words, "And there was the angel who had come to me in Ḥirā' seated on a throne between the heavens and the earth, and I knelt down before him." This is a plain reference to his prior coming to him and to his having made revelation from God to him, as we have stated. But God knows best.
lessons: Fear in the face of the overwhelming and divine is a natural human response — even the Prophet trembled before Gabriel; fear does not disqualify us from our calling.
A period of waiting and uncertainty can end suddenly with a clear divine call to action — despair during the wait is not warranted.
Home and family are sanctuaries in moments of spiritual overwhelm; seeking comfort and wrapping oneself in safety is not weakness but wisdom.
practical_advice: When anxiety or fear strikes unexpectedly, allow yourself to first seek comfort and safety — rest, warmth, and a trusted person — before trying to process what happened.
Understand that your purpose and calling may be clarified in stages; the early confusing experiences are often the foundation for a clearer mission ahead.
When feeling spiritually disconnected or uncertain, remember that the call to 'arise and give warning' — to be purposeful and active — often comes precisely after periods of stillness and confusion.

title: The Revelation of Surah Al-Duha and the Woman's Taunt
summary: When the Prophet was unwell and revelation paused for several nights, a woman mocked him saying his spirit had abandoned him, prompting God to reveal Surah Al-Duha reassuring him of divine favor.
story: This is established in both ṣaḥīḥ collections and elsewhere, as well as from the account of al-Aswad b. Qays, from Jandah b. 'Abd Allāh al-Bajali, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was indisposed and did not get up for one, two or several nights. A woman commented, 'So your spirit soon abandoned you, then!' And so God revealed the verses, 'By the dawn and by the night when it darkens, your Lord has not forsaken you, nor is He displeased.'" And it was thereby that the revelation transpired for mankind, whereas it was by the former circumstances that the prophethood came about. Some authorities state that the intermission lasted for approximately two years, or two years and a half. What is apparent — though God alone knows best — is that it was a period comparable to that related to Mā'iz; al-Sha'bi and others made this observation. This does not refute that Gabriel first brought him revelation, in the verses, "Read in the name of your Lord who created." Thereafter Gabriel became associated with him, following the revelation of "O you who are wrapped up, arise and give warning! And glorify your Lord. Purify your garments! And shun idolatry!" Thereafter the revelation intensified and continued; that is, it came uninterrupted, little by little. At this time the Messenger of God (SAAS) began devoting himself totally to the mission, with energy and determination. He summoned all to God, both near and far, slaves and free men alike. And all of intelligence, excellence, and good favour believed in him, while all who were stubborn and intransigent continued to oppose and disobey him.
lessons: Mockery and cruelty from others during our lowest moments is not a sign that God has abandoned us — divine reassurance often follows human cruelty closely.
Illness and inactivity are not spiritual failures; the Prophet himself experienced physical weakness, and God responded with words of comfort, not rebuke.
After every prolonged period of difficulty and silence, there can come a surge of clarity and purposeful action that more than makes up for the waiting.
practical_advice: When others mock or doubt you during a difficult season — especially when you're unwell or struggling — do not internalize their words as truth about your worth or your relationship with God.
Memorize or reflect on Surah Al-Duha during periods of feeling abandoned or forgotten; its words were revealed precisely for such moments.
Allow yourself recovery time during illness without guilt; restoration of the body is often the precondition for renewed spiritual and purposeful energy.

title: The First Believers: Abu Bakr, Ali, Khadija, and Zayd
summary: The first people to accept Islam were Khadija, Ali ibn Abi Talib, Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, and Zayd ibn Haritha, representing the first woman, youth, free man, and freed slave to embrace the faith.
story: At this time the Messenger of God (SAAS) began devoting himself totally to the mission, with energy and determination. He summoned all to God, both near and far, slaves and free men alike. And all of intelligence, excellence, and good favour believed in him, while all who were stubborn and intransigent continued to oppose and disobey him. Abū Bakr al-Ṣiddīq, "the trusting", was the first free man to believe in him, while 'Ali b. Abū Ṭālib was the first youth to do so. The first woman to believe was Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, his wife. Of the freed men, the mawāli, his own mawlā Zayd b. Ḥāritha al-Kalbi was the first. May God be pleased with all the above. We have previously written about the faith of Waraqa b. Nawfal, and how he reacted to the revelation and died during the intermission period. May God be pleased with him.
lessons: Truth attracts people of genuine intelligence, excellence, and good character — the quality of the first believers tells us something about the quality of what they believed in.
Faith crosses every social boundary; the first believers included a woman, a child, a wealthy free man, and a freed slave — God's invitation is universal.
Those who accept truth early, at personal cost and with little social support, carry a special honor; courage in faith is itself a form of worship.
practical_advice: Reflect on who the early, honest believers in your life have been — the ones who stood by goodness before it was popular — and draw strength from their example.
Do not let social status, age, or background make you feel that faith or righteousness is not for you; the earliest Muslims were a cross-section of humanity.
Be among those who respond to truth with openness rather than stubbornness; ask yourself regularly whether habit or ego is preventing you from growth and positive change.

title: The Protection of Revelation from Demonic Eavesdropping
summary: God protected the Qur'an from being contaminated by demonic eavesdropping by expelling devils from heaven with shooting stars, ensuring the revelation reached humanity pure and unaltered.
story: Section: On preventing the demons and evil spirits from eavesdropping when the Qur'ān was revealed, so that none of them would acquire any of it, even so much as a syllable, and deliver it through the speech of his human associates, which would have led to complications and a confusion of the truth. It was God's mercy, grace and kindness to His creation that He excluded them from heaven. God Almighty spoke of them in His words, "and that we tried heaven, but found it filled with a strong guard and shooting stars. We used to sit in seats there to listen, but now any of us who eavesdrops finds a shooting star in wait. And we do not know whether harm is meant for those on earth, or whether their Lord intends guidance for them" (sūrat al-Jinn, LXXII, v.8-10). The Almighty said, "And the devils have not brought it down. This would not be right for them; and they could not. They are kept away from hearing it" (sūrat al-Shu'arā, XXVI, v.210-12). The ḥāfiẓ Abū Nu'aym al-Ṭabarāni stated that Sulaymān b. Aḥmad — he being al-Ṭabarāni — related to him, quoting 'Abd Allāh b. Muḥammad b. Sa'id b. Abū Maryam, quoting Muḥammad b. Yūsuf al-Firyābi, quoting Isrā'il, on the authority of Abū Isḥāq, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbās, who said, "The devils would ascend to heaven to listen to the revelation. Having memorized the statement, they would then make nine additions to it. The statement itself would be true, but their additions thereto would be false. "When the Prophet (SAAS) was given his mission, their seats were denied them. They complained of this to Satan. And prior to that they did not have shooting stars fired at them. Satan told them, 'This is due to some event on earth.' "He therefore sent his forces down and they found the Messenger of God (SAAS) standing between two mountains and praying. They went back to him (Satan) and told him. He then said, 'That is what has occurred on earth.'" Abū Dāwūd gave an account from Abū Bishr, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbās, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) left with others on a trip to the market of 'Ukāẓ. At this time the devils were being denied access to information from heaven and shooting stars had been sent against them. The devils had returned to their own people who asked them what was wrong. They replied, 'We've been denied access to information.'"
lessons: The purity and integrity of divine guidance is actively protected; when we seek authentic religious knowledge, we can trust that the core of revelation has been preserved.
Even forces of corruption cannot contaminate what God has chosen to protect; this is a reminder that divine preservation operates at a level beyond human or spiritual interference.
The moment of the Prophet's mission was a cosmic turning point that reorganized the spiritual order of existence — its significance extended far beyond the human realm.
practical_advice: When you encounter contradictory or confusing religious claims, return to authenticated primary sources — the Qur'an and established hadith — trusting that what is truly preserved is protected.
Guard the integrity of what you pass on to others; just as devils mixed falsehood into truth, we must be careful not to contaminate genuine wisdom with our own biases or additions.
Reflect on the idea that sincere prayer and devotion — as the Prophet was found doing between two mountains — is itself a spiritual force that repels corruption and confusion.

title: The Jinn Encounter the Quran at the Market of Ukaz
summary: A group of jinn, barred from heavenly information by shooting stars, overheard the Prophet reciting the Quran during the dawn prayer near the market of Ukaz and accepted Islam, after which God revealed the opening verses of Surah Al-Jinn.
story: Abū Waqqa gave an account from Abū Bishr, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) left with others on a trip to the market of Ukaz. At this time the devils were being denied access to information from heaven and shooting stars had been sent against them. The devils had returned to their own people who asked them what was wrong. They replied, "We've been denied access to information from heaven, and shooting stars have been fired at us." Their people told them, "This has to be because something has happened. So go and search the ends of the earth!" One group set off towards Tihama and passed over a date plantation where he was, while on their way to Ukaz. He was with his Companions there performing the dawn prayer. When the devils overheard the Qur'an, they listened to it and said, "It is this that has denied us access to information from heaven." So they returned home and told the rest: "O our people, we have heard a wonderful Qur'an that leads to right guidance; we believe in it, and will never associate another deity with our Lord" (surat al-Jinn, LXXII, v.1-2). And so God revealed to His Prophet (SAAS) the words: "Say: It was revealed to me that a group of jinn listened and said, We have heard a wonderful Qur'an" (surat al-Jinn, LXXII, v.1)." This account was included in both Sahih collections.'
lessons: Truth and guidance can reach the most unexpected hearts; the Quran's power transcends human and unseen worlds alike.
When one sincerely encounters divine truth, the proper response is immediate belief and gratitude, as the jinn demonstrated.
God's plan of revelation unfolded in ways that confirmed the Prophet's mission even through cosmic signs, reinforcing that His truth cannot be suppressed.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually disconnected or distant from God, return to the recitation and listening of the Quran — it has the power to transform even the hardest of hearts.
If you are searching for purpose or meaning, approach sacred texts with an open heart rather than preconceptions, just as the jinn listened carefully and were moved.
Trust that guidance will find you at the right moment; remain consistent in your worship and spiritual practices even when times feel uncertain.

title: The Angels and Devils at the Dawn of Revelation
summary: Ibn Abbas narrates how every tribe of jinn used to eavesdrop on heavenly matters before revelation began, and how the coming of the Prophet's mission was accompanied by shooting stars that drove them away, disrupting their long-established practice.
story: Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba stated that Muhammad b. Fudayl related to him, from 'Ata' b. al-Sa'ib, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'Every single tribe of jinn had places where they would sit and listen. When revelation began the angels would hear a sound like that of metal being cast against stone. When the angels heard this, they would fall down prostrate and not raise their heads until revelation had come. When it had, they would ask one another, "What was it your Lord said?" And if it related to matters in heaven, they would say, "He spoke the truth; and He is the All-High, the Almighty." But if it related to matters on earth, dealing with the future, with death or other worldly affairs, they would talk about it and say, "It would be so, and such." The devils would listen to this and transmit it down to their human companions. Now when the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) received his mission, they were chased away with shooting stars. The first to learn of this were the tribe of Thaqif. Those with livestock began making sacrifice. Those with sheep would slaughter one each day, those with camels would slaughter one similarly. They made haste to their flocks to do this. But some said, "No, don't do this. If it is the stars people use to find their way, (then it is right to make sacrifice) but otherwise it is just happening because of some specific event." When they looked, they found that nothing had happened to the stars used for navigation. And so they ceased making sacrifice. And God sent the jinn into motion, and they heard the Qur'an. When they were there present, they said, "Listen carefully!" The devils would go off to Iblis (Satan) and inform him. He told them, "This is some event that has occurred on earth. Therefore bring me some dust from every part of it." And when they brought him some from Tihama, he said, "This is the site of the event."'
lessons: The beginning of every divine mission is accompanied by cosmic shifts; the Prophet's coming was so significant that even the unseen world was reorganized around it.
Hasty reactions based on fear without careful observation lead to unnecessary losses, as the people of Thaqif discovered when they prematurely slaughtered their livestock.
Evil forces that rely on distorting information are rendered powerless when authentic divine revelation enters the world.
practical_advice: When confronted with alarming news or signs, pause and verify carefully before taking drastic action — patience and observation often reveal a clearer picture.
Recognize that moments of great spiritual significance in your life may come with turbulence and confusion before clarity emerges; trust the process.
Reflect on how divine truth naturally repels falsehood in your own life; seek authentic sources of knowledge and be wary of hearsay.

title: The People of Ta'if React to Shooting Stars and Learn of the Prophet's Emergence
summary: As shooting stars intensified upon the Prophet's mission being given, the people of Quraysh and Ta'if panicked, releasing slaves and livestock, until the wise 'Abd Ya'layl counseled them to observe carefully, determining the stars were unknown ones signaling a new prophet's appearance.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that Usama b. Zayd b. Aslam related to him, from 'Umar b. 'Abd al-'Absi, from Ka'b, who said, 'No stars had been cast since the ascension of Jesus until the time when the Messenger of God (SAAS) became a prophet. Quraysh then saw sights they never before witnessed. And so they began releasing their flocks and freeing their slaves, thinking that it meant the end of the world. News of these actions of theirs reached the people of al-Ta'if, and Thaqif did the same (as Quraysh). What Thaqif had done became known to the slave of Ya'layl b. 'Amr, and he asked them, "Why have you done what I see?" They replied, "There have been shooting stars, and we have seen them falling from the sky." He observed, "But it's hard to replace properties once they have gone; don't be in a hurry and observe. If they are stars that are recognized, then it does mean destruction for us. But if they are stars that were unknown stars, then it's all because of some event that has occurred." So they made observations, found they were unknown stars and told him of this. He commented, "Well, there's still some time left then. This is because of the appearance of a prophet." Soon thereafter Abu Sufyan b. Qarb came for his animals and 'Abd Ya'layl went to him and told him about the stars. Abu Sufyan then commented, "Muhammad son of 'Abd Allah has come forth and made a claim that he is a prophet who has been sent." 'Abd Ya'layl then said, "Well, that's why there have been shooting stars."'
lessons: Calm, measured wisdom in times of collective panic is a gift; one thoughtful voice can save a community from rash and harmful decisions.
Cosmic and worldly events often carry deeper spiritual significance that requires reflection rather than reflexive fear.
The emergence of prophethood was a world-altering event recognized even by those outside the faith community through signs in the natural world.
practical_advice: When those around you are swept up in panic or fear, practice grounding yourself through careful observation and rational thinking before acting.
In moments of crisis, seek the counsel of someone with wisdom and experience rather than following the crowd's reactive behavior.
Use moments of uncertainty as an invitation to reflect on deeper spiritual realities rather than focusing solely on material concerns.

title: Satan Searches for the Prophet After the Idols Are Overturned
summary: Upon the Prophet's mission being given, all idols on earth were overturned; Satan and his evil spirits set out to find the source of this upheaval, eventually locating the Prophet in Mecca accompanied by the Angel Gabriel.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that Muhammad b. Salih related to him, from the son of Abu Hakim - by whom he meant Ishaq - from 'Ata' b. Yasar, from Abu Hurayra, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) received his mission, all the idols were overturned. So the evil spirits went and told him (Satan), "Every single idol on earth has been overturned." He responded, "This is due to a Prophet who has been given a mission. Look for him in villages in the countryside." They did so, but reported to him that they could not find him. He then said, "I'll do it." And so he went off and looked for him. A voice called out to him, "Go to Janbat al-Bab" - the "gate quarter" - meaning Mecca. He looked for him there and found him at Qarn al-Tha'alib. Satan then went off to the devils and told them, "I found him, and Gabriel was with him. What's your opinion?" They answered, "We'll make earthly pleasures delightful in people's eyes and so make them covet them." "So no problem then," he commented.'
lessons: The presence of Gabriel with the Prophet signifies that divine protection accompanies those chosen by God; no evil force can ultimately overcome divine support.
Evil forces, upon being unable to suppress truth directly, resort to distraction and temptation — awareness of this strategy is the first step in resisting it.
The overturning of idols at the moment of revelation symbolizes that falsehood is inherently fragile before authentic divine truth.
practical_advice: Be aware that when you commit to a righteous path, opposition and temptation will intensify; prepare yourself spiritually with prayer and remembrance of God.
Identify the 'earthly pleasures' in your own life that distract you from your spiritual goals and take steps to moderate your relationship with them.
Take comfort in the knowledge that when you are on the path of truth, you are not alone — divine support accompanies sincere effort and faith.

title: Satan Discovers the Prophet at Hira and Devises His Strategy
summary: When the Prophet received his mission at the Cave of Hira with Gabriel present, Satan and his devils acknowledged they could not suppress this prophethood and resolved instead to seduce people through worldly temptations.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that Talha b. 'Amr related to him, from Ibn Abu Mulayka, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr, who said, 'When the day came when the Messenger of God (SAAS) became a Prophet, the devils were denied access to heaven and shooting stars were fired at them. So they went to Satan and when they told him this he commented, "Some event has occurred. This is a Prophet who has come forth to them in the Holy Land, the place where the tribe of Israel came from." So the devils went off to Syria, then returned and told him, "There's no one there." Satan responded, "I'll do it." Then he went off to look for him in Mecca. And there was the Messenger of God in Hira', and Gabriel was there with him. Satan returned to his evil spirits and told them, "Ahmad has received his mission and Gabriel is with him. What is your opinion?" They replied, "Earthly delights; we'll make them attractive to people." He replied, "That will do it."'
lessons: The Prophet's mission was divinely guarded from its very first moment; the presence of Gabriel at Hira assured that no opposition could derail God's plan.
Recognizing that worldly temptation is a deliberate strategy of evil helps us approach desires and distractions with greater awareness and intentionality.
When one path of harm is closed, evil seeks another; spiritual vigilance must therefore be constant, not situational.
practical_advice: Whenever you feel pulled toward worldly excess or distraction, pause and ask yourself whether this desire serves your long-term spiritual wellbeing or draws you away from it.
Strengthen your connection to God especially during moments when life feels materially comfortable, as ease can sometimes be a greater test than hardship.
Reflect regularly on what truly matters to you beyond material pleasures, and align your daily choices with those deeper values.

title: The People of Ta'if and 'Abd Ya'layl's Warning About the Shooting Stars
summary: Sa'id b. Mansur narrates how 'Abd Ya'layl counseled the people of Ta'if not to panic over shooting stars, advising careful observation; their findings pointed to a new prophetic event rather than the end of the world.
story: Sa'id b. Mansur stated, from Khalid b. Husayn, from 'Amir al-Sha'bi, who said, 'The stars were not cast until the Messenger of God (SAAS) received his mission. People then released their flocks and set free their slaves. So 'Abd Ya'layl said, "Observe; and if it is stars that are known, it means the end of mankind is near. But if they are not known, then it is happening because of some event that has occurred." And so they did make observations and found them to be unknown. They therefore ceased what they had been doing and soon thereafter news came to them of the emergence of the Prophet (SAAS).'
lessons: Wisdom lies in careful discernment rather than reactive fear; not every alarming sign signals catastrophe.
The emergence of the Prophet was confirmed by multiple converging signs, demonstrating that God's plan for humanity is never random or hidden.
Those who approach uncertainty with patience and inquiry are better positioned to recognize truth when it arrives.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by what seems like signs of doom or catastrophe around you, take a breath and seek knowledge before reacting emotionally.
Practice distinguishing between what you know for certain and what you fear might be true; this habit of mind protects you from unnecessary suffering.
Look for multiple perspectives and sources of confirmation before drawing major conclusions about your life or the world around you.

title: The Jinn of Nasibin Accept Islam After Hearing the Quran in the Haram Mosque
summary: Al-Suddi narrates how Satan, after the shooting stars terrified the people of Ta'if, traced the source of the cosmic disturbance to Mecca and dispatched seven jinn from Nasibin, who heard the Prophet reciting the Quran in the Sacred Mosque and accepted Islam.
story: Al-Suddi stated, 'The heavens were only guarded when there was a prophet on earth, or when a religion of God was appearing. The devils, prior to Muhammad (SAAS), used to take seats in the skies above the world and overhear what was going on in heaven. When God sent Muhammad (SAAS) as a prophet, one night they had stars shot at them. This terrified the people of al-Ta'if, and they told one another that the people of heaven had been destroyed! This was because of the violence of the fires in the sky and the great variety of the shooting stars. So they began setting their slaves free and releasing their livestock. At this 'Abd Ya'layl b. 'Amr b. Umayr told them, "Woe upon you, O people of Ta'if! Secure your property and observe the most prominent stars. If you see them to be still set in their places, then the people of heaven are not destroyed, but it's all because of the son of Abu Kabsha. But if you don't see them there, then the people of heaven have perished." And when they looked, they did still see them and so they secured their property. That night the devils were terrified and went to Satan and he told them, "Bring me a handful of earth from every place." When they did so, he sniffed it and said, "Your man is in Mecca." He then dispatched seven of the spirits of Nasibin and they came to Mecca and found the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the Haram (Holy) Mosque reciting the Qur'an. They approached him so eager to hear it that their bodies almost touched him. They then accepted Islam, and God sent down revelation to his Prophet (SAAS) about them.'
lessons: The Quran carries a power that transcends all barriers — physical, spiritual, and cosmological — drawing sincere hearts toward guidance.
Those who approach divine speech with genuine eagerness and humility, as the jinn of Nasibin did, are rewarded with faith and transformation.
God honors those who sincerely seek truth by sending His revelation and guidance to confirm and dignify their journey.
practical_advice: Make the recitation or listening to the Quran a daily anchor in your life; its effect on the heart is profound even when you are not consciously aware of it.
Approach moments of spiritual seeking with the same eagerness the jinn showed — let go of reservations and open yourself to being moved by truth.
When you experience a transformation of heart or a moment of spiritual breakthrough, know that God's revelation is still active in the world and can reach anyone who is genuinely open.

title: Satan's Response to the Prophet's Mission and Gabriel's Protection
summary: When the Prophet Muhammad received his mission, Satan attempted to harm him but was repelled by Gabriel. The devils lamented their loss of access to heavenly revelation and Satan plotted to distract people with earthly delights.
story:
was the Messenger of God in Hirn', and Gabriel was there with him. Satan returned to his evil spirits and told them, ' A h a d has received his mission and Gabriel is with him. What is your opinion?' They replied, 'Earthly delights; we'll make them attractive to people.' He replied, 'That will do it.'

Al-Wnqidi said that Talha b. 'Amr related to him, from 'At@, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The devils used to listen to the revelation. And when Muhammad (SAAS) was assigned to his mission, they were prevented from doing so. They complained about this to Satan, and he responded, 'Some event has occurred.' He then went on up above Abii Qubays, the very first mountain placed on the face of the earth, and saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) at prayer behind the shrine. He told himself, 'I'll go on down and break his neck!' So he strutted off towards him. But Gabriel was there with him, and charged out at Satan sending him flying in all directions. So the devil took to his heels."

Both al-Waqidi and Abn Ahmad al-Zubayri also related this, from Rabb* b. Abn Ma'mf, from Qays b. Sa'd, from Mujnhid. Their account was similar, with the variation, "Gabriel kicked the devil and threw him down to Aden."
lessons: Divine protection surrounds those who are committed to God's path; no evil can overcome someone who is under God's care.
Satan's primary strategy against humanity is to make worldly distractions seem appealing, which is a reminder to stay grounded in what truly matters.
The beginning of any righteous mission may be met with opposition, but that opposition itself is a sign of the mission's significance and truth.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually attacked or overwhelmed by worldly temptations, remember that seeking closeness to God through prayer is itself a form of divine protection.
Recognize that an increase in difficulty or opposition when you are doing something good may be a sign you are on the right path, not a reason to abandon it.
Counter the pull of materialistic distractions by regularly reflecting on your deeper purpose and spiritual values, especially during moments of temptation.

title: The Manner in Which Revelation Came to the Prophet
summary: Multiple companions and narrators describe the intense physical and spiritual experience the Prophet Muhammad underwent when receiving divine revelation, including sounds like ringing bells, sweating on cold days, and the heavy weight felt by those near him.
story:
Section: On the manner in which the revelation came to the Messenger of God (MAS).

It has previously been related how Gabriel came to him on both the first and second occasions. Mdik stated, from H i s h b b. Wrwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, God bless her, that al-Harith b. Hishzm asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) "0 Messenger of God, how does revelation come to you?" He replied, "Sometimes it comes like the ringing of a bell, it being then most painful. When it leaves me I remain conscious of what he spoke. Sometimes the angel appears before me as a man who addresses me, and I am fully aware of what he is saying." 'A'isha, God bless her, stated, "I saw h i (SAAS) when revelation came to him on the extremely cold day. Then it left him, and his forehead was bathed in perspiration."

Imam Ahmad related it from 'Amir b. Salih, from Hisham b. 'Urwa in a similar manner. 'Ahda b. Sulaymm and Anas b. 'Iyyad related it similarly from Hisham b. 'Urwa. Ayyub al-Sakhtiyani related it from Hisham b., from his father, from al-Harith b. Hisham who said, "I asked the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'How does revelation come to you?'" He related the same hadith as above, but made no reference to 'A'isha.

In the hadith al-'Ifk she stated, "By God, the Messenger of God (SAAS) did not get up, nor did any member of the family leave, until revelation came down to him. He underwent the agony that used to seize him (on such occasions) with sweat emerging like pearls from him, even though it was a winter's day, because of the heavy burden of the revelation that came down upon him."

Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Razzaq related to him, quoting Yunus b. Salim, who said that Yunus b. Yazid dictated to him, from Ibn Shihab, from 'Urwa b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Abd al-Qari, who said, "I heard Umar b. al-Khattab say, 'When revelation came down to the Messenger of God (SAAS), it would be heard like the buzzing of a bee near his face.'"

In the Sahih collection of Muslim and others, it is recounted by al-Hasan, from Hita b. 'Abd Allah al-Raqashi, from 'Ubada b. al-Samit, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) would become in pain when revelation came to him, and his face would look very serious." According to one account, this wording should be "and his eyes would close; we recognized that in him."

In both Sahih collections there is the account of Zayd b. Thabit that relates to the circumstance when the verse 'Those of the believers who sit still are not on equality with . . .' (surat al-Nisa', VI, v.95) was revealed; and when the son of Umm Maktum complained of his disabilities, the next verse came down: "except for those with disabilities". His account states, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was seated with his thigh right next to mine while I was writing. When revelation came, his leg almost crushed mine."

In the Sahih collection of Muslim, there is an account by Hammam b. Yahya, from 'Ata, from Ya'la b. Umayya, who is quoted as saying, "Umar said to me, 'Would you like to look at the Messenger of God (SAAS) while revelation is coming to him?' He raised the edge of his robe from his face while he was receiving revelation at al-Ji'rana, and he was all flushed. And he would moan like a newborn calf."

It was established in the two Sahih collections from a hadith of 'A'isha relating to when revelation came concerning the hijab (the wearing of the veil) that Sawda went out thereafter to the privies, by night. 'Umar spoke to her, saying, "We can recognize you, 0 Sawda!" She then went back to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and questioned him, while he was sitting having supper and holding his milk (mug) in his hand. God then sent down revelation to him, while the milk was in his hand. He then raised his head and said, "You women have been given permission to go out and attend to your needs." This shows that revelation did not fully absent his feelings from him. This is proven by his being seated; and the milk, moreover, did not drop from his hand (SAAS)!

Abu Dawud al-Tayalisi stated that 'Abbad b. Mansur related to him, quoting 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "When revelation came down, the body and face of the Messenger of God would become pale; he would ignore those with him, and none of them would address him."

In the collection of traditions made by Ahmad and others, there is an account of Ibn Lahi'a in which he states, "Yazid b. Abu Habib related to me, from 'Amr b. al-Walid, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr, who said, 'I asked, "0 Messenger of God, can you feel revelation coming?" He replied, "Yes; I hear ringing sounds. Then I recover. And never once did I receive revelation without thinking that my soul was being seized by it."'"

Abu Ya'la al-Mawsili stated that Ibrahim b. al-Hajjaj related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Wahid b. Ziyad, quoting 'Asim b. Kulayb, quoting his father, from his uncle al-'Ilya b. 'Asim, who said, "We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) when revelation came to him. When this happened, he could still see and his eyes remained open, while his hearing and his heart were made empty for what would come to him from God, Almighty and Glorious is He."

Abu Nu'aym related, from a tradition of Qutayba that 'Ali b. Ghurab related to him, from al-Ahwaz b. Hakim, from Abu 'Awna, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyib, from Abu Hurayra, who said, "When revelation came down to the Messenger of God, he would get a headache and would cover his head with the henna plant."

Imam Ahmad stated that Abu al-Nadr related to him, quoting Abu Mu'awiya Shayba, from Layth, from Shahr b. Hawshab, from Asma', daughter of Yazid, who said: "I was holding the reins of al-'Adba', the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS), when the whole text of the surat al-Ma'ida (Qur'an, V) was revealed to him.
