When you find someone you trust, be honest about your purpose and ask for guidance directly — sincerity invites sincere help in return.

title: Abu Dharr Publicly Declares His Faith and Is Beaten
summary: Immediately after accepting Islam, Abu Dharr defied the Prophet's advice to keep quiet and publicly declared the shahada in the mosque. He was beaten twice but was rescued both times by al-Abbas.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) then told him, "Go back to your people and tell them; then wait till you hear from me." But Abu Dharr replied, "No, by Him who sent you with the truth, I'll shout it out in full public!" He then left and went to the mosque, where he shouted at the top of his voice, "I bear witness that there is no God but God, and that Muhammad is the Messenger of God!" At that everyone there set about beating him till they laid him low. Then al-Abbas came and, bending over him, exclaimed, "Woe unto you! Don't you realize that he is from Ghifar, and that your trade route is to Syria?" He then rescued Abu Dharr from them. But the next day Abu Dharr did the same and was again attacked and then saved by al-Abbas.
lessons: The courage to proclaim the truth publicly, even at personal cost, is one of the most powerful expressions of sincere faith.
God often sends unexpected protectors to those who stand boldly for truth, even when their boldness outpaces their wisdom.
There is a place for both bold proclamation and strategic patience in serving a cause — both reflect genuine devotion when rooted in sincerity.
practical_advice: When you feel compelled to speak a truth that others may reject or punish you for, weigh the cost honestly — but know that sincere conviction, when expressed clearly, often moves hearts even among witnesses.
If you have been metaphorically 'beaten down' for standing by your values, take heart: there are often unseen protectors and allies who will come to your side.
Reflect on the difference between courageous honesty and reckless provocation — Abu Dharr's boldness was born of love for truth, and that intention matters profoundly.

title: Abu Dharr Survives 30 Days at the Kaaba on Zamzam Water Alone
summary: Before meeting the Prophet, Abu Dharr spent thirty days and nights near the Kaaba sustained only by zamzam water, hiding from the hostile Meccans, yet growing stronger rather than weak from hunger.
story: "So I left and went to Mecca, where I asked a weak-looking man, 'Where is the one people refer to as the heretic?' But he pointed at me and the people of that valley pelted me with clods of earth and bones until I was injured with wounds and fainted. When I got up later, I was like a red pole. I made my way to zamzam, drank its water and washed the blood off myself. I then positioned myself between the ka'ba and its hangings. And there I stayed, O nephew, for 30 days and nights without sustenance except for water from zamzam. But I gained weight and developed folds of fat on my belly, finding no evidence on my body of the emaciation of starvation. "One night when the moon was very bright and the Meccans were fast asleep, there were only two women circumambulating the ka'ba. As they came past me they were saying prayers to the gods Isaf and Na'ila. I commented, 'Make one of them have intercourse with the other!' This did not deter them, so I then said, 'They're just like bits of wood, but I'd not lean on them!' "At this they scurried away, wailing, 'If only one of our menfolk were here!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abu Bakr, on their way down from the mountain, met these women and asked them what was the matter. 'It's that heretic between the ka'ba and its hangings.' They asked them what I had said, and the women replied, 'He said a real mouthful to us!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companion then came along, kissed the stone, circumambulated the ka'ba, then prayed. I went over to him and was the first person to address him with the greeting of the community of Islam. He replied, 'Peace be upon you, and God's mercy. Who are you?' I told him I was from the Ghifar tribe. He brought his hand to his forehead in a gesture of surprise and I told myself, 'He dislikes my being from Ghifar!' "I made as if to take him by the hand, but his Companion pushed me away, knowing him better than I did. "The Prophet (SAAS) then asked, 'How long have you been here?' 'I've been here for 30 days and nights,' I answered. "'And who has been feeding you?' he asked. "'There's been nothing except zamzam water, but I've gained so much weight that I developed folds of fat on my belly. And I feel no emaciation from hunger.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'It's blessed! Wonder food!'"
lessons: When a person is on a sincere journey toward truth, God provides sustenance — physical, spiritual, or both — in ways that defy ordinary expectation.
Isolation and hardship endured in pursuit of something sacred can be a form of purification that strengthens rather than diminishes the seeker.
The blessed nature of simple, pure things — like water — is easily overlooked, yet they can sustain us through our most difficult trials.
practical_advice: In periods of difficulty where resources feel scarce, focus on what you do have rather than what you lack — gratitude for small provisions can carry you further than you expect.
If you are going through a long, lonely waiting period in pursuit of something meaningful, recognize it as preparation rather than abandonment.
Do not measure God's care for you by the abundance of your provisions; measure it by the strength you still carry despite what you have been through.

title: Abu Dharr's Journey from Ghifar: Family Hardship and Setting Out
summary: Abu Dharr recounts how family envy forced his clan to leave their uncle's home, how his brother Anis won back their camels through a wager, and how Anis first brought word of the Prophet to him.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Yazid b. Harun related to him, quoting Sulayman b. al-Mughira, quoting Humayd b. Hilal, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Samit, who quoted Abu Dharr as saying, "We — that is I, my brother Anis, and our mother — left our tribe of Ghifar because they used to disregard the restrictions of the holy month. "We took up residence with an uncle of ours who was a man of wealth and status; he honoured us and treated us extremely well. But his family envied us and told him, 'If you were to go away from your people, Anis would take your place over them.' "Our uncle then came to us and reported what he had been told. I then said to him, 'You've cancelled out all your prior generosity to us. We'll have no communion with you henceforth.' "So we assembled our herd of camels and set off with them, while our uncle covered himself up in his robe and began to weep. "We rode on until we made camp on the outskirts of Mecca. My brother Anis then made a wager, betting our flock of camels and an additional number equal to our herd. He (and his competitor) then went to a diviner who decided in favour of Anis. Anis returned to us with our herd and a like one." Abu Dharr continued, "Now, my nephew, I had been praying for three years prior to meeting the Messenger of God (SAAS)." I asked, "To whom?" "To God." "In which direction?" "Wherever God directed me. And I would pray each evening till deep into the night, when I find myself under my blanket, until the sun would rise." "Well any way," Anis said, "I have business in Mecca; await me until I return." He left and was away for what seemed a long time, then he came back. I asked him, "What kept you?" He said, "I met a man who claimed that God had sent him with your religion." "And what do people say of him?" I asked. "They say that he is a poet and a soothsayer." Anis was himself a poet. He went on, "Anis commented, 'I've heard soothsayers and he does not speak like them. And I've checked his utterances against the measures of poetry and, by God, it doesn't fit anyone's tongue as poetry. And, I swear, he is truthful, whereas the rest are liars.' "I asked him, 'Could you stand in for me while I leave?' 'Yes,' he replied, 'but watch out for the Meccans. They have pilloried and berated him.'"
lessons: Even before formal religious guidance reaches a person, a sincere heart may already be in worship — Abu Dharr prayed to God for three years before ever hearing of the Prophet.
Envy within families and communities can displace the innocent, but God often uses such painful disruptions to redirect the sincere toward a greater destiny.
The testimony of a trustworthy witness who has personally examined the evidence carries great weight and can light the first lamp of faith in another's heart.
practical_advice: If you have been displaced or hurt by the jealousy of others, resist the urge to define yourself by that rejection — it may be the very push that moves you toward where you truly belong.
If you have been praying or seeking God sincerely, even without a full framework of faith, know that God sees that search and will bring the knowledge to meet it.
When sharing important information with someone, be honest and precise — say what you actually observed and felt, not merely what others are saying.

title: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari's Acceptance of Islam and Survival on Zamzam Water
summary: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari spent 30 days in Mecca surviving solely on Zamzam water, gained weight, and eventually accepted Islam before returning to call his tribe to the faith.
story: been here?' 'I've been here for 30 days and nights,' I answered. '"And who has been feeding you?' he asked. "'There's been nothing except zamzam water, but I've gained so much weight that I developed folds of fat on my belly. And I feel no emaciation from hunger.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'It's blessed! Wonder food!' "Abii Bakr then asked, 'Please, 0 Messenger of God, permit me to give him some food tonight.' And he agreed. "The Prophet (SAAS) then left and I went with them. Abn Bakr opened a gate and began picking some grapes from Ta'if for us. And that was the first food I ate there. I stayed some time in Mecca. "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, 'I have been directed to go to a land of palm trees, none other than Yathrib, I believe. Will you go and inform your people about me? Perhaps God will benefit them through you, and give you reward among them too.' "So I left and rejoined my brother Anis. He asked me, 'What did you do?' 'What I did was, I accepted Islam and believed.' "He commented, 'I've nothing against your religion. I too accept Islam and believe.' "We then went to our mother and she said, 'Well, I've nothing against your religion. I accept Islam and believe.' We then packed up and rejoined our tribe of Ghifar, and some of them accepted Islam before the Messenger of God migrated to Medina. Khufaf b. Ayma' b. Rakhsha the Ghifari led them as he was their chief at that time. The remainder of them said, 'If the Messenger of God (SAAS) comes, we will accept Islam.' "And the Messenger of God (SAAS) did come and the remainder of them did accept Islam. Then the Aslam tribe arrived and said, 'O Messenger of God, we'll accept Islam just as our Ghifar brothers did.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'God has ghafara, "forgiven", Ghafar, and God has salama, "made peace with", Aslam!'"
lessons: Divine provision can come in unexpected forms — sustenance of the soul and body may arrive through means beyond our imagination, as Zamzam water physically nourished Abu Dharr miraculously.
One sincere believer returning to their community can become the means by which an entire tribe receives guidance; personal transformation carries communal responsibility.
The Prophet's compassion was evident in his concern for Abu Dharr's welfare, demonstrating that caring for others' basic needs is an act of worship and leadership.
practical_advice: When facing scarcity or hardship, renew your trust in God's provision — reflect on historical accounts of divine sustenance to strengthen your certainty.
Share your faith journey authentically with family and loved ones; your personal transformation may be the doorway through which they find guidance.
When you experience a spiritual breakthrough, consider how you can channel that energy into serving your wider community rather than keeping it private.

title: Dimad's Acceptance of Islam After Hearing the Prophet's Words
summary: Dimad, a healer from the Azd tribe who came to Mecca believing the Prophet was afflicted, was so struck by the Prophet's words of praise to God that he immediately accepted Islam and pledged allegiance on behalf of his tribe.
story: Muslim and al-Bayhaqi related a hadith of Dawad b. Aba Hind, from 'Amr b. Sa'id, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said that Dimad came to Mecca, he being a man of the Azd tribe from Shanu'a. He used to say incantations to protect people from those winds. He overheard some Meccan fools saying, "Muhammad is crazy." He asked them, "Where is this man? Perhaps God will cure him at my hands." He recounted, "I met Muhammad and said, 'I use incantations against these winds. And God cures at my hands whomever He wishes. Do come along.' "Muhammad (SAAS) replied, 'Praise be to God! We praise Him and seek His help. Whomever God leads aright, no one can lead astray. Whomever God leads astray, no one can lead aright. I bear testimony that there is no God but God alone who has no associates.' He repeated this three times." Dimad said, "I swear, I have heard the speech of soothsayers, sorcerers, and poets, but I never heard the like of these words. Give me your hand and I'll pledge you my allegiance in Islam." The Messenger of God accepted his pledge and asked him, "For your tribe too?" "Yes, for my tribe too," he agreed. The Prophet (SAAS) dispatched an army that passed by the people of Dimad. The army commander asked his troops, "Have you seized anything from these people?" One of the men replied, "I did take one strong-backed camel from them." He ordered the man, "Give it back to them; they are from the tribe of Dimad." In another account Dimad said to the Prophet (SAAS), "Repeat these words of yours to me; they are the epitome of oratory."
lessons: Truth carries its own power — the Prophet's simple words of praise and monotheism moved a skilled healer more than any elaborate argument could have.
God can guide anyone through any means; what appears to be an act of mockery (calling the Prophet 'crazy') was in fact the trigger that brought Dimad to seek him out and find the truth.
The Prophet's immediate protection of Dimad's tribe after his conversion shows that accepting someone into the community of faith comes with real, practical responsibilities of care and respect.
practical_advice: When feeling spiritually lost or searching, expose yourself to authentic Islamic speech and Qur'anic recitation — the truth in those words has a unique ability to move hearts.
Do not underestimate the power of a sincere testimony of faith; sometimes a few deeply felt words can change the course of someone's life.
If someone extends trust to you by joining your community or cause, honor that trust immediately through action, just as the Prophet protected Dimad's people without delay.

title: Early Companions Who Accepted Islam — The First Generation of Believers
summary: Ibn Ishaq records a comprehensive list of the earliest men and women who accepted Islam, illustrating that the faith spread across diverse backgrounds and tribes in Mecca from its earliest days.
story: Ibn Ishaq gave a list of the names of the Companions, God be pleased with them, who accepted Islam early on. He stated, "Then Abu Lubayda accepted Islam, as did Abu Salama, al-Arqam b. Abu al-Arqam, 'Uthman b. Maz'un, 'Ubayda b. al-Harith and Sa'id b. Zayd, along with his wife Fatima, daughter of al-Khattab. Also there were Asma', daughter of Abu Bakr, and 'A'isha, also daughter of Abu Bakr, when she was young. As well there were Qudama b. Maz'un, 'Abd Allah b. Maz'un, Khabbab b. al-Aratt, 'Umayr b. Abu Waqqas, 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud, Mas'ud b. al-Qari, Salir b. 'Amr, 'Ayyash b. Abu Rabi'a, along with his wife Asma', daughter of Salama b. Mukharriba of the Taym tribe, and Khunays b. Hudhafa, and 'Amir b. Rabi'a. Then there were 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh, Abu Ahmad b. Jahsh, Ja'far b. Abi Talib, along with his wife Asma' daughter of 'Umays. Others included Hatib b. al-Harith and his wife Fatima, daughter of al-Mujallil, and also Hattab b. al-Harith and his wife Fukayha, daughter of Yasa, Ma'mar b. al-Harith b. Ma'mar al-Jumahi, and al-Sa'ib b. 'Uthman b. Maz'un, al-Muttalib b. Azhar b. 'Abd 'Awf b. Suhayra b. Sa'id b. Sahm, al-Nahham, whose name was Nu'aym b. 'Abd Allah b. Asid, 'Amir b. Fuhayra, the freed-man of Abu Bakr, Khalid b. Sa'id, Umayma, daughter of Khalaf b. As'ad b. 'Amir b. Bayada, of Khuza'a, and Hatib b. 'Amr b. 'Abd Shams, Abu Hudhayfa b. 'Utba b. Rabi'a, Waqid b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd Yalayl b. Nashib b. Ghira, of the tribe of Sa'd b. Layth. Also there were Khalid b. al-Bukayr, 'Amir b. al-Bukayr, 'Aqil b. al-Bukayr, Iyas b. al-Bukayr b. 'Abd Yalayl b. Nashib b. Ghira, of the tribe of Sa'd b. Layth. The given name of 'Aqil (a word meaning 'wise') had originally been Ghafil (a word meaning 'heedless') and so the Messenger of God (SAAS) had renamed him 'Aqil. These were all allied to the tribe of 'Adi b. Ka'b. Also there were 'Ammar b. Yasir and Suhayb b. Sinan. Thereafter people began accepting Islam in large numbers, men and women both, so that it became a prominent subject of discussion in Mecca."
lessons: The early Muslim community was built by ordinary people from all walks of life — free and enslaved, men and women, young and old — demonstrating that faith transcends every social barrier.
Community and belonging are fundamental to human flourishing; the early believers formed a spiritual family that gave one another strength to withstand persecution.
Each name in this list represents a person who made an individual choice for truth in the face of social pressure, reminding us that personal conviction must precede communal identity.
practical_advice: When feeling isolated in your faith or values, remember that throughout history sincere people have always found each other — seek out or build a supportive community around shared principles.
Read the biographies of the early Companions to draw inspiration; knowing that real people from difficult circumstances held firm to their convictions can strengthen your own resolve.
Celebrate quiet, individual acts of faith and integrity in yourself and others — not every act of courage is visible, but each one contributes to something larger.

title: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas Strikes the First Blow in Defense of Muslim Prayer
summary: While a small group of Muslims prayed in a mountain trail to avoid detection, polytheists attacked them, and Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas struck back with a camel's jawbone, making it the first blood spilled in Islam.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "God then ordered his Messenger (SAAS), after three years of his mission, to announce it openly and to remain firm against the evil of the polytheists. "The Companions of the Prophet (SAAS) would go off along mountain trails when they prayed, to hide this from their people. "While Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas was at prayer, along with a small group of others, on a mountain trail, some polytheists came at them. They upbraided them, reviled them for what they were doing, and attacked them. "Sa'd struck one man with a camel's jawbone and cut him open; this was the first blood spilled in Islam." Al-Umawi related this incident at length in his maghazi study of the early campaigns, through al-Waqidi, from al-Zuhri, from 'Amir b. Sa'd, from his father. His account states that the man wounded was 'Abd Allah b. Khatal, God damn him.
lessons: The right to worship in peace is worth defending; the early Muslims did not provoke conflict but were willing to protect their sacred practice when attacked.
Even in the most constrained circumstances, believers found ways to maintain their worship — mountain trails became their sanctuaries, showing that devotion finds a way.
The first blood in Islam was shed in defense, not aggression, establishing a foundational principle that Muslims only resort to physical defense as a last resort.
practical_advice: When external pressures make it difficult to maintain your spiritual practices, find creative solutions rather than abandoning those practices — even small, quiet spaces can become places of worship.
If you face hostility or mockery for your beliefs, hold your ground with dignity; you do not need to apologize for your faith or your right to practice it.
Channel feelings of anger or frustration at injustice into productive defense of what is right, rather than allowing those emotions to lead to disproportionate or unprovoked reaction.

title: God's Command to the Prophet to Announce Islam Publicly
summary: After three years of secret preaching, God commanded the Prophet to publicly announce his mission, call his nearest kin to accountability, and remain patient against the opposition of the polytheists.
story: This chapter deals with God's command to his Messenger (SAAS) to announce publicly his mission to high and low and how He told him to be patient and stoical, and to oppose those who remain stubborn, mendacious and ignorant even after the evidence had been brought against them and the Messenger had been sent to them. It also mentions the damages they had done to him and to his Companions, may God bless them all. God Almighty stated, "And warn those of your people closest to you and bring down your wing to protect those believers who follow you. And if they disobey you, say, 'I am innocent of what you do'. Put your trust in the Almighty and All-Merciful, who sees you when you arise and when you turn about among those prostrating in prayer; He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" (surat al-Shu'ara', XXVI, v.214-20). And the Almighty stated, "It is surely a message to you and to your people, and you will all be questioned" (surat al-Zukhruf, XLIII, v.44). Also He stated, "He who imposed the Qur'an upon you will bring you back to an after life" (surat al-Qasas, XXVIII, v.85). This means, He who has made a demand upon you and has required you to announce the Qur'an will return you to the hereafter, which is the ma'ad, the afterlife, and He will question you about that. As the Almighty stated, "And, by your Lord, we will question them all for what they have done" (surat al-Hijr, XV, v.92-3).
lessons: Divine commands often require moving from the comfort of the private to the vulnerability of the public; growth in faith frequently demands greater exposure and risk.

God pairs every great command with assurance of His support — the instruction to announce publicly was accompanied by the reminder that God sees and hears all, providing the Prophet with certainty he was not alone.
Patience and steadfastness in the face of stubborn opposition are not passive virtues; they require active choice and ongoing inner resolve, reinforced by trust in God's ultimate authority.
practical_advice: When you feel called to speak a difficult truth or take a stand, prepare yourself spiritually first — ground yourself in prayer and remembrance of God before stepping into challenging conversations.
Recognize that accountability begins at home; the command to warn 'those closest to you' reminds us that our primary responsibility for moral example is within our own families and immediate circles.
When facing overwhelming opposition to doing what is right, focus on the principle that your duty is to convey the truth clearly — outcomes are in God's hands, not yours.

title: The Prophet Calls the Quraysh on Mount Safa and Abu Lahab's Rejection
summary: Acting on divine command, the Prophet climbed Mount Safa and called the tribes of Quraysh together, warning them of divine punishment, only to be publicly cursed by his own uncle Abu Lahab, after which Surah al-Lahab was revealed.
story: On this subject, Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd Allah b. Numayr related, from al-A'mash, from 'Amr b. Murra, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "When God sent down, 'And warn those of your people closest to you', the Prophet (SAAS), went up on Mt. Safa and called out, 'Ya Sabahal' "People gathered there around him; they consisted of men who had come to him themselves and others who had sent messengers. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) addressed them, saying, 'O tribe of 'Abd al-Muttalib, O tribe of Fihr, O tribe of Ka'b! If I were to tell you that there were horsemen on the heights of this mountain who planned to attack you, don't you think you would believe me?' 'Yes,' they replied. He then announced, 'Well, I am come to warn you of a terrible punishment.' "Abu Lahab, God damn him! said, 'Damn you! May you perish all day long! You called us here for this?' "And so God, the Almighty and Glorious, revealed 'Damn the hands of Abu Lahab! Damn him!'" (surat al-Lahab, CXI, v.1).
lessons: The Prophet used a brilliant rhetorical strategy — establishing his credibility as a truthful warner before delivering his message — teaching us that how we communicate truth matters as much as the truth itself.
Rejection by those closest to us, even family, is one of the most painful forms of opposition; the Prophet's experience validates the deep hurt that comes from being dismissed by loved ones.
Divine revelation responding directly to Abu Lahab's insult shows that God defends His messenger — those who stand for truth are not alone, even when they are humiliated publicly.
practical_advice: When sharing an important truth with people you care about, first establish common ground and shared trust before delivering the difficult message.
If you are mocked or rejected when trying to do what is right, do not internalize that rejection as a verdict on your worth — the opposition of others does not determine the validity of your path.
Use the story of Abu Lahab as a reminder that arrogance and dismissiveness toward truth have real consequences; cultivate humility and genuine openness when encountering ideas that challenge you.

title: The Prophet's Warning to His Family: 'Save Yourselves from Hellfire'
summary: The Prophet summoned all branches of the Quraysh by name, including his own daughter Fatima, declaring that family ties would not protect anyone from divine accountability and that each person must take responsibility for their own soul.
story: Ahmad stated that Mu'awiya b. 'Amr related to him, quoting Za'ida, quoting 'Abd al-Malik b. 'Umayr, from Musa b. Talha, from Abu Hurayra, who said that when this verse, "And warn those of your people closest to you" was revealed, the Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned the people of Quraysh, high and low alike, and told them, "O Quraysh, save yourselves from hell-fire! O tribe of Banu Kalab, save yourselves from hell-fire! O tribe of 'Abd al-Muttalib, save yourselves from hell-fire! O Fatima, daughter of Muhammad, save yourself from hell-fire! For I, I swear by God, have nothing to protect you from God, except the fact that you have kinship (to me) for which I shall care." Muslim related this from a hadith of 'Abd al-Malik b. 'Umayr, and it was published in both Sahih collections from an account of al-Zuhri, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyib and Abu Salama, from Abu Hurayra.
lessons: True love for family means caring about their ultimate wellbeing, not just their immediate comfort — the Prophet's warning to Fatima herself was an act of profound paternal love, not abandonment.
No relationship, however close, exempts a person from personal accountability before God; each soul stands alone in its ultimate responsibility.

The Prophet's transparency about the limits of his own intercession — 'I have nothing to protect you from God' — models a crucial form of honesty: not overpromising what we cannot deliver even to those we love.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether the people you love most are also being cared for spiritually — sometimes the most loving thing is to have a difficult, honest conversation about values and ultimate purpose.
Do not rely on the righteousness of others — family, teachers, or community leaders — as a substitute for your own personal relationship with God and your own moral choices.
Practice honest communication in your relationships, even when the truth is uncomfortable; long-term trust is built on honesty, not false reassurances.

title: The Feast of Warning: Prophet Muhammad Calls His Closest Relatives to Islam
summary: The Prophet Muhammad, commanded by God to warn his closest relatives, prepared a miraculous feast for the Banu Abd al-Muttalib twice, only to be interrupted by Abu Lahab before he could address them, and on the third gathering he delivered his message declaring he had brought them the best of this world and the next.
story:
I swear by God, have nothing to protect you from God, except the fact that you have kinship (to me) for which I shall care."

Ahmad also stated that Waki' b. Hish- related to him, from his father, from 'A'isha, God bless her, who said, "When there was revealed the verse, 'And warn those of your people closest to you' the Messenger of God (SAAS) arose and said, 'O Fatima, daughter of Muhammad! O Safiyya, daughter of 'Abd al-Muttalib! O family of 'Abd al-Muttalib! I have nothing to protect you from God; ask whatever you will from my wealth.'"

The hafiz Abu Bakr al-Bayhaqi stated in his work al-Dala'il (The Signs) that Muhammad b. 'Abd al-Hafiz related to him, quoting Abu al-'Abbas Muhammad b. Ya'qub, quoting Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, quoting Yunus b. Bukayr, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, who said that someone who concealed his name from him told him that he heard 'Abd Allah b. al-Harith b. Nawfal relate from Ibn 'Abbas, from 'Ali b. Abi Talib, who said, "When there was revealed to the Messenger of God (SAAS) the verses, 'And warn those of your people closest to you and bring down your wing to protect those believers who follow you', the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'I realized that if I announced it to my people I would see from them what I would hate, and so I kept silent. Then Gabriel, peace be upon him, came to me and said, 'O Muhammad, if you do not act as your Lord has ordered you, He will punish you with hell-fire.'"

'Ali continued, "So he called for me and said, 'O 'Ali, God has commanded me to warn my closest relatives. So prepare for us a sheep on a platter of food, along with a large pot of milk, then assemble Banu 'Abd al-Muttalib for me.' I did so and they assembled on that day. There were 40 men, more or less; among them there were his uncles Abu Talib, Hamza, and al-'Abbas, along with Abu Lahab, the vile polytheist.

"I served the food platter to them and the Messenger of God (SAAS) took from it a piece of meat, split it with his teeth and tossed it in all directions, saying, 'Eat, in the name of God!' Everyone ate until satiated, nothing being seen left but the marks of their fingers. No one ate like that ever before! Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Now give them to drink, 'Ali!' So I brought the large pot of milk, and they drank it up till they were full. I swear, no one ever drank that way before!

"When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was on the point of addressing them, Abu Lahab, God damn him! forestalled him by saying, 'Well, what a feast your host served to you to eat!' At that they dispersed without the Messenger of God (SAAS) addressing them.

"Next day the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Prepare for us the same food and drink as yesterday; that fellow forestalled me by saying what he did before I could address them.' I did so, then gathered them for him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) did as he had the previous day. They ate till they were full; I swear, none of them ate like that ever before! Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Give them to drink, 'Ali!' So I brought them the pot and they drank of it till they were satiated. I swear, none of them drank like that before!

"When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was about to address them, Abu Lahab, God damn him! forestalled him by saying, 'Well, what a feast your host served to you to eat!' So they dispersed without the Messenger of God (SAAS) addressing them.

"Next day the Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, 'Ali, prepare food and drink for us as you did yesterday. This fellow forestalled me by saying what you heard before I could address the people.' I did so and assembled them again. The Messenger of God (SAAS) acted as he had the day before. They ate till they were satiated, and then I served them from the pot until they were full. I swear, I never saw people eat and drink like that!

"Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke, 'O Family of 'Abd al-Muttalib, I know of no Arab man, by God, who brought his people anything better than I have brought you. I have brought you what deals with this world and the afterlife as well.'"
lessons: Fulfilling divine commands may require repeated effort and perseverance even when initial attempts are frustrated or sabotaged.
Opposition from within one's own family is among the most painful trials, yet the Prophet remained composed and simply tried again the next day.
God's command to deliver the message carries a weight that supersedes social comfort or fear of rejection; obedience to God must come before the desire for approval.
practical_advice: When you face repeated interruptions or obstacles in pursuing a righteous goal, do not interpret them as signs to stop — regroup, prepare again, and try once more as the Prophet did.
If family members undermine your efforts publicly, respond not with anger but with renewed resolve; focus on the message rather than the opposition.

When anxiety about delivering difficult news to loved ones arises, remember that God does not burden a soul beyond what it can bear, and that the same God who gave you the responsibility will also grant you the means to fulfil it.

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib Steps Forward as the Prophet's First Supporter
summary: When the Prophet asked who among his assembled relatives would support him in his mission, only the young Ali ibn Abi Talib volunteered, leading to laughter from those present but marking a historic moment of loyalty.
story:
'Ali continued, "But everyone drew back from that. Since I was the youngest, had the most bleary eyes, the largest stomach and the shortest legs, I called out, 'O Prophet of God, I will be your wazir (i.e. your deputy) in this!' "The Prophet (SAAS) took me by the neck and said, 'This is my brother' and so on and so forth, 'so listen to him and obey him!' 'Ali went on, "Everyone began laughing and saying to Abu Talib, 'He has ordered you to listen to your son and to obey him!'"

This account is much the same up to the words, "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) forestalled them in speaking and asked, 'Which of you will settle my debt for me, and be my successor for my family?' They were all silent. Al-'Abbas kept quiet because he feared the issue might involve his money. And I kept quiet because of al-'Abbas's age. The Prophet (SAAS) repeated his question and al-'Abbas kept silent and, witnessing this, I said, 'I will, O Messenger of God!' 'You?' he replied. At that time I was the most homely looking person there. I was bleary eyed, had a large stomach and short legs."

It seems that the Messenger of God (SAAS) feared that if he announced his mission to the Arab polytheists they might kill him. He therefore wanted to establish that whoever came in his place thereafter would be beneficial to his family and fulfil his obligations. But God removed his concern at that with His words, "O Messenger, announce what has been revealed to you from your Lord. If you do not, then you will not have fulfilled your mission. God will protect you from the people" (surat al-Ma'ida, V, v.67).
lessons: True support often comes from unexpected places; worth is not measured by age, appearance, or status but by sincerity of heart.
Stepping forward when everyone else holds back is an act of profound courage, and God honours those who answer His call even when they feel least qualified.
Feeling inadequate or inferior does not disqualify a person from greatness; Ali's self-described limitations did not prevent him from being the one chosen to stand beside the Prophet.
practical_advice: When a righteous cause needs a volunteer and hesitation fills the room, do not wait for someone more qualified — your willingness itself is a form of divine qualification.
If people mock or laugh at your commitment to something meaningful, hold firm; history remembers those who stepped forward, not those who laughed.
When you feel you lack the qualities needed for a task, focus on sincerity of intention rather than self-assessment of capability, and trust that God equips those whom He calls.

title: Abu Talib and Abu Lahab: Two Uncles, Two Opposite Paths
summary: The Prophet's two uncles Abu Talib and Abu Lahab represent contrasting responses to his mission: Abu Talib protected and supported the Prophet out of natural love while remaining a polytheist, while Abu Lahab was among the most hostile opponents and was condemned in the Quran.
story:
Violent and powerful Quraysh polytheists inflicted harm both verbal and physical upon the Messenger of God (SAAS) and upon those powerless individuals who followed him. One of the most violent of those opposed to him was his uncle Abu Lahab; his full name was 'Abd al-'Uzza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib. His wife, Umm Jamil, was named Arwa, daughter of Harb b. Umayya, the sister of Abu Sufyan.

Another uncle, Abu Talib b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, was at variance with Abu Lahab, for he favoured the Messenger of God (SAAS) above all other men for his fine qualities. He treated him with the utmost compassion and kindness and gave him his support and protection. In doing this he was in opposition to his own people, even though he shared their religion and their friendship. God had, however, put his heart to the test by making him love the Prophet (SAAS) by natural disposition rather than for religious reasons. His persisting in the religion of his people was, in fact, an expression of the wisdom of Almighty God and a factor in the protection He was affording to His Messenger. For if Abu Talib had accepted Islam, he would have no longer held any weight or leadership among the Quraysh polytheists; they would have held no respect for or fear of him. They would have penalized him and done him verbal and actual harm. For your Lord determines and establishes whatever He wills! And He has divided up His creation into many different types and kinds.

Both these uncles, Abu Talib and Abu Lahab, were infidels. But whereas the former will be at the very surface of the fire, the latter will be deep down in its lowest depths. About him God made revelation in one of the chapters of the Qur'an to be recited from the pulpit and read in sermons and prayers. It contains information that he will roast in flaming hell-fire, along with his wife, that "firewood hawker."
lessons: God's wisdom operates in ways that human beings cannot always perceive; Abu Talib's remaining a polytheist was itself a divinely arranged protection for the Prophet.
A person's outward religious affiliation does not define the entirety of their role in God's plan; people can serve sacred purposes through natural qualities such as loyalty and love.
The consequences of one's choices in this life extend into the next; the contrasting fates of Abu Talib and Abu Lahab illustrate that how one treats the truth carries eternal weight.
practical_advice: When you cannot understand why God has arranged circumstances in a particular way, trust that divine wisdom encompasses dimensions you cannot see from your vantage point.
Extend compassion and gratitude to those who support you and protect you even when they do not share your beliefs, as the Prophet valued Abu Talib's support deeply.
Reflect regularly on how you treat those who sincerely seek truth and goodness — your response to goodness reveals the state of your own heart.

title: The Prophet Preaches Openly at Fairs and Gatherings While Abu Lahab Follows to Discredit Him
summary: The Prophet traveled to fairs, pilgrimage stations, and public gatherings calling people to say there is no God but God, while his uncle Abu Lahab followed closely behind him calling him a liar and a Sabian to undermine his message.
story:
The outcome was that the Messenger of God (SAAS) continued to call upon people to believe in God Almighty day and night, secretly and openly, without anyone being able to deter, dissuade, or prevent him. He followed people everywhere, into their celebrations, meetings and gatherings, to the fairs and to the pilgrimage stations. Everyone he met, free man or slave, weak or powerful, rich or poor he urged; as far as he was concerned all were equal and alike.

Imam Ahmad stated that Ibrahim b. Abi al-'Abbas related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi al-Zinad, from his father who said that a man named Rabi'a b. 'Abbad of Banu al-Dil, who had been an idol-worshipper and had accepted Islam, recalled, "I saw the Messenger of God, before the coming of Islam, at the fair of Dha al-Majaz, and he was saying, 'O you people, say, "There is no god but God", and you will prosper.' There were people gathered all around him, while behind him stood a mean-looking, squint-eyed man wearing two plaits who was saying, 'He's a lying Sabian!' This man followed him wherever" he went.
lessons: Consistency and persistence in conveying truth, regardless of who mocks or follows to discredit, is a defining characteristic of prophetic character and a model for all believers.
The Prophet made no distinction between free and enslaved, wealthy and poor, powerful and weak — true da'wah (calling to God) is rooted in seeing the equal humanity and worth of every soul.
Public humiliation and deliberate campaigns to discredit do not invalidate the message; the truth stands on its own merit regardless of the opposition arrayed against it.
practical_advice: When you are ridiculed or undermined by someone close to you in public, remember that the Prophet continued walking and speaking — do not allow the noise of detractors to silence your pursuit of what is right.
Treat every person you encounter as equally deserving of respect and the truth, regardless of their social status or background, as the Prophet addressed free and enslaved, rich and poor alike.

If you find yourself in a period where your efforts seem to yield no visible fruit and opposition is constant, take strength from the fact that the Prophet endured exactly this — and the world was ultimately changed by his perseverance.

title: Abu Lahab Follows the Prophet at Dhu al-Majaz Fair
summary: Rabi'a b. Abbad recalls witnessing the Prophet at the Dhu al-Majaz fair calling people to monotheism while his uncle Abu Lahab followed behind him, calling him a liar and urging people to ignore him.
story: 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi al-Zinad, from his father who said that a man named Rabi'a b. 'Abbad of Bana al-Dil, who had been an idol-worshipper and had accepted Islam, recalled, "I saw the Messenger of God, before the coming of Islam, at the fair of Dha al-Maja, and he was saying, 'O you people, say, "There is no God but God", and you will prosper.' There were people gathered all around him, while behind him stood a mean-looking, squint-eyed man wearing two plaits who was saying, 'He's a lying Sabian!' This man followed him wherever he went. I asked who he was and was told it was his uncle, Abu Lahab." Imam Ahmad and al-Bayhaqi both also related this anecdote from a hadith in similar form from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi al-Zinad. Al-Bayhaqi also stated that Abu Tahir al-Faqih related to us, quoting Abu Bakr Muhammad b. al-Hasan al-Qasw, quoting Abu al-Azhar, quoting Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah al-Ansari, quoting Muhammad b. 'Umar, from Muhammad b. al-Munkadar, from Rabi'a al-Dili, who said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Dha al-Maja following people into their dwellings urging them to believe in God, while behind him went a squint-eyed man with scarred cheeks who was saying, 'O people, let not this fellow entice you away from your religion, the religion of your forefathers!' "I asked, 'Who is he?' And I was told, 'That's Abu Lahab.'" He then related this anecdote through Shu'ba, from al-Ash'ath b. Salim, from a man of Kinana, who said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the Dhu al-Majaz fair. He was saying, 'O people, say "There is no God but God" and you will prosper.' There was a man standing there behind him scattering dust on him. It was Abu Jahl, and what he was saying was, 'O people, let not this fellow entice you away from your religion. He just wants you to give up worship of al-Lat and al-'Uzza.'" This anecdote states it was Abu Jahl, but it is obvious that it was Abu Lahab.
lessons: Steadfast dedication to one's mission in the face of public ridicule and family opposition is a mark of true conviction.
Opposition from those closest to us, including family, can be among the most painful trials, yet the Prophet continued without bitterness.
The truth of a message is not diminished by the hostility of its opponents; sincerity speaks for itself over time.
practical_advice: When you are mocked or undermined publicly, focus on the sincerity of your own intentions rather than on silencing your critics.
If a family member opposes or embarrasses you, seek emotional grounding in your principles rather than retaliating or withdrawing completely.
Remember that continuing to do good in the face of sustained opposition builds a kind of inner resilience that external comfort alone cannot provide.

title: Abu Talib Refuses to Abandon the Prophet Despite Quraysh's Pressure
summary: Quraysh complained to Abu Talib about the Prophet disturbing their meetings and mosque, and Abu Talib initially wavered, but after seeing the Prophet's tearful resolve, he pledged never to abandon him.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr stated that he was informed from Talha b. Yahya, from 'Abd Allah b. Musa b. Talha, that 'Uqayl b. Abi Talib related, "Quraysh came to Abu Talib and said, 'This nephew of yours is disturbing our meetings and our mosque; keep him away from us!' He asked me, 'O 'Uqayl, go and bring me Muhammad.' So I went off and brought him out from a kanas. This word means a 'tent'; or he might have used the word khanas, a 'hide-out', or alternatively as some say, a 'small house'. He arrived back at midday in the full heat. When he came in, Abu Talib told him, 'These relatives of yours charge that you are annoying them in their meetings and in their mosque. Stop annoying them!' "At this the Messenger of God (SAAS) gazed upwards to the sky and asked, 'Do you see that sun?' 'Yes,' they replied. He then said, 'I'm no more capable of stopping that than you are of getting a flame from it.' "Abu Talib then said, 'By God, I swear that my nephew never lied. So go home!'" Al-Bukhari related this story in his history, from Muhammad b. al-'Ala', from Yunus b. Bukayr. And al-Bayhaqi related it from al-Hakim, from al-Asamm, from Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar in the same words. Moreover al-Bayhaqi related it through Yunus, from Ibn Ishaq, who stated, "Ya'qub b. 'Utba b. al-Mughira b. al-Akhnas related to me that he was told that when Quraysh made this complaint to Abu Talib, he sent for the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, 'O nephew, your relatives have come to me and told me this and that. Do right by me and by yourself by not burdening me with what neither you nor I could bear. Stop telling your relatives what they dislike hearing!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) thought that Abu Talib's attitude towards himself and his Muslims had changed and that his support for them had weakened. And so he addressed Abu Talib as follows, 'O uncle, if the sun were placed in my right hand and the moon in my left, I would not give up this matter until either God gives me success in it, or I perish in its pursuit.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then broke down into tears. "When he turned to leave, Abu Talib said, having seen how deeply the Messenger of God (SAAS) had been moved, said to him, 'O nephew!' When he turned to him, he said, 'Continue as before; do as you like. By God, I will never abandon you for any reason.'" Ibn Ishaq stated that Abu Talib spoke the following verses on that subject: 'By God, they'll never get their gang to you until I'm laid to rest, buried in the ground. Carry on with your work that is beyond reproach; rejoice, and may you be content in doing so. You invited me, and I knew you were advising well; you spoke the truth, and you were ever trustworthy. You offered a religion I knew to be the best religion of mankind. Were it not for blame and anger, you'd find me giving it my favour openly.' Al-Bayhaqi stated, 'Ibn Ishaq related various verses of Abu Talib on that subject.' All this gives evidence that God Almighty gave the prophet (SAAS) His protection through his uncle, even though he differed in his religion. And God had protected him when his uncle was not there; God's will is final and unchallenged.
lessons: Even those who do not fully share our faith can be moved by genuine sincerity and tears to stand by us in our most vulnerable moments.
When the people closest to us appear to waver in their support, honest and heartfelt communication can restore the bond more powerfully than argument.
Divine protection operates through many channels, including through the hearts of those who may not yet fully believe.
practical_advice: When you fear that a supporter is withdrawing, speak from the heart about your commitment rather than defending yourself logically — genuine emotion can move people more than debate.
Do not interpret a loved one's moment of hesitation as permanent abandonment; give them space to see your sincerity before assuming the worst.
When facing pressure to compromise your values, remind yourself clearly what you stand for — as the Prophet did with the image of the sun and moon — so you can articulate it with conviction.

title: Abu Jahl Attempts to Kill the Prophet During Prayer but Is Repelled by Gabriel
summary: Abu Jahl vowed to smash the Prophet's head with a rock while he prayed, but when he approached, he was overcome with terror by a vision of a great camel — identified as the angel Gabriel — and fled in panic.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr stated that Muhammad b. Ishaq related to him, quoting a man originally from Egypt some 40 years previously, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, a long anecdote relating what transpired between the polytheists of Mecca and the Messenger of God (SAAS). When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arose, Abu Jahl b. Hisham said, "O Quraysh, Muhammad is persisting, as you know, in criticizing our religion, reviling our forefathers, ridiculing our values and insulting our gods. I swear by God that I'll sit and wait for him tomorrow carrying a rock, and if he prostrates in his prayer, I'll smash his head with it! And let 'Abd Manaf's people do whatever they like about it afterwards." Next day Abu Jahl, God curse him! took a rock and sat to wait for the Messenger of God (SAAS). That morning, the Messenger of God (SAAS) came as usual; his direction of prayer at that time was towards Syria. When he prayed he positioned himself between the corner (of the ka'ba) with the "blackstone" and the Yemeni corner, facing so that the ka'ba was between himself and Syria. By the time he came to pray Quraysh were already sitting in their meeting places, waiting. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) prostrated in prayer, Abu Jahl did pick up the rock and approached him. When he got near him, he retreated in fear, his colour pale in terror. His hands lost their grip on the rock and it fell. Quraysh men went up to him and asked, "What's the matter with you, Abu al-Hakam?" He answered, "When I got up to do as I told you yesterday I would and got near him, a stallion camel blocked my way to him. And, by God, I never saw a stallion so big as it, with a neck and fangs like it had! It was about to devour me!" Ibn Ishaq related, "It was reported to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'That was Gabriel. And if he had come near me, he would have taken him.'" Al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu 'Abd Allah, the hafiz, related to him, quoting Abu al-Nadr, the jurist, quoting 'Uthman al-Darami, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Salih, quoting al-Layth b. Sa'd, from Ishaq b. 'Abd Allah b. Abi Farwa, from Abah b. Salih, from 'Ali b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas, from his father, from 'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, who said, "One day I was in the mosque when Abu Jahl, God curse him! arrived. He said, 'I swear by God, that if I see Muhammad prostrating in prayer, I'll stomp on his neck!' "I then left and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him what Abu Jahl had said. He left home in a rage and went to the mosque. He was in a hurry to enter through the door, but he hit the wall instead. I told myself that this was an evil day; I put on my cloak and followed him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) entered then, recited the verse, 'Read in the name of your Lord who created, created man from a clot.' When he reached the passage relating to Abu Jahl: 'No; man is indeed impious, to see himself self-sufficient' (surat al-'Alaq, XCVI, v.1, 2; 6, 7), a man said to Abu Jahl, 'O Abu al-Hakam, that's Muhammad.' Abu Jahl replied, 'Can't you see what I see? By God, the vistas of heaven have been barred to me!' When the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached the end of the surat he prostrated himself in prayer." Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Razzaq related to him, quoting Ma'mar, from 'Abd al-Karim, from 'Ikrima, who said, "Ibn 'Abbas stated, 'What Abu Jahl said was, "If I see Muhammad praying at the ka'ba, I will tread on his neck!"' "This comment reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) and he said, 'If he does any such thing the angels will take him, in full view!'" Al-Bukhari related this in the same way from Yahya, from 'Abd al-Razzaq. Dawud b. Abi Hind stated from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "Abu Jahl passed by the Prophet (SAAS) while he was praying and addressed him with the words, 'Hey, Muhammad, didn't I forbid you to pray? You know there to be no one who can call upon more people in council than I can.' "The Prophet (SAAS) reprimanded him and Gabriel spoke the words, 'Let him call his henchmen; we will summon the angels of punishment!' (surat al-'Alaq, XCVI, v.17-18). By God, if he does summon his people, the angels of justice will inflict punishment on him." Ahmad and al-Tirmidhi related this; and al-Nasa'i authenticated it through Dawud. Imam Ahmad stated that Isma'il b. Yazid Abu Zayd related to him, quoting Furat, from 'Abd al-Karim, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "Abu Jahl stated, 'If I see Muhammad at the ka'ba praying, I'll fight him till I tread on his neck!'" He said that he (the Prophet) also used the words, "If he does so, then the zabaniyya, (the angels of punishment) will take him away in full view." Abu Ja'far b. Jarir stated that Ibn Humayd related to him, quoting Yahya b. Wadih, quoting Yunus b. Abi Ishaq, from al-Walid b. al-'Ayzar, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "Abu Jahl said, 'If Muhammad ever again prays at the shrine, I will kill him!' "And so God Almighty sent down the verses, 'Read in the name of your Lord who created' up to the verse 'We will certainly smite his...'"
lessons: Those who stand firm in worship even when facing mortal threats demonstrate that devotion to God surpasses fear of human power.
Divine protection is real and can manifest in ways that confound even the most determined enemies of truth.
The arrogance of oppressors ultimately undoes them — Abu Jahl's boasts were each met with responses that exposed his powerlessness before God.
practical_advice: When you feel threatened or intimidated for practicing your faith or values openly, remember that sincerity in worship itself carries a kind of protection that hostile onlookers cannot fully understand.
Do not let the loudness of an opponent's threats convince you that they have the final word — continue your practice and trust that integrity outlasts intimidation.
If you witness someone being threatened for doing what is right, follow the example of al-Abbas: inform them, stand nearby, and do not leave them to face it alone.

title: Abu Jahl's Threat to Kill the Prophet During Prayer and the Angels' Protection
summary: Abu Jahl threatened to kill the Prophet if he prayed at the shrine, but when he approached, he was terrified by a vision of angelic squadrons and a trench of fire, and fled. God revealed verses from Surah Al-Alaq in response.
story: Abti Jacfar b. Jarir stated that Ibn Humayd related to him, quoting Yahya b. Wadih, quoting Yiinus b. Abn Ishaq, from al-Walid b. al-'izw, from Ibn 'Abbs, who said, "Aba Jahl said, 'If Muhammad ever again prays at the shrine, I will kill him! ' "And so God Almighty sent down the verses, 'Read in the name of your Lord who created' up to the verse 'We will certainly smite his forehead, the lying, sinful forehead; let him summon his henchmen, and we will summon the zab&nayyal (siirat al-'Alaq, XCVI, v.15-18). "Then the Prophet (SAAS) came and prayed and Aba Jahl was asked, 'Well, what's stopping you?' He replied, '(The space) between myself and him is black with his squadrons.'" Ibn CAbbas stated, "And, by God, if he had so much as moved, the angels would have seized him, while the people looked on." Ibn Jarir stated that Ibn 'Abd al-A'la related to him, quoting al-Muctamir, from his father, from Nucayrn b. Aba Hind, from Abo H e i m , from Aba Hurayra, who said, "Aba Jahl asked, 'Is Muhammad to place his face down among your backs?' 'Yes', they replied." The account continues, "So he said, 'By al-Lat and al-Wzz~,if I see him praying like that, I'll stomp on his neck and 1'11 flatten his face down into the dust!' So he went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) while he prayed in order to tread on his neck, but everyone was surprised to see him retreat and put his hands out as if to protect himself." He went on, "So he was asked what was the matter, and Aba Jahl replied, 'Between h i and myself there is a trench of fire, terrifying things, and wings.'" The account continues, "And the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'If he had come near me the angels would have picked him apart limb by limb.'" He said, "And God Almighty revealed - I don't know whether or not this is in the hadith from Aba Hurayra - 'No, man is indeed impious, to see himself self-sufficient' (sarat at-'Alaq, XCVI, v.&7), and so on to the end of the stirat." Ahmad, Muslim, al-NasiPi, Ibn Aba Hztim and al-Baybaqi related this from a hadith of Mu'tamir b. Sulaymzn b. Tarkhan of Taym.
lessons: Divine protection surrounds those who worship sincerely; no human threat can override what God has decreed.
Arrogance and self-sufficiency blind people to the truth, as God warns in Surah Al-Alaq — 'man is indeed impious, to see himself self-sufficient.'
The courage to continue worship openly in the face of threats is itself a form of trust in God that attracts His support.
practical_advice: When you feel threatened or intimidated for living according to your values, remind yourself that your protection ultimately rests with God, not with circumstances.
If fear of others is causing you to neglect your spiritual practice, start small — consistent worship, even when difficult, builds inner strength over time.
Reflect on the verses of Surah Al-Alaq when facing those who act with arrogance; it is a reminder that no power is self-sufficient, and that humility before God is the truest strength.

title: The Camel Placenta Thrown on the Prophet During Prayer and Fatima's Response
summary: While the Prophet prayed, Quraysh leaders had Uqba bin Abi Mu'ayt throw the filthy placenta of a slaughtered camel onto his back. He remained prostrating until his daughter Fatima came and removed it, after which he supplicated against the chiefs by name — and all of them were later killed at Badr.
story: Imzm A h a d stated that Wahb b. Jarir related to him, quoting Shu'ba, from Abti Ish%q, from 'Amr b. Mayman, from 'Abd All&, who said, "I never saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) say a prayer against Quraysh except on one occasion. He was praying while a group of Quraysh sat there; there was the placenta (salz) of a sacrificed camel near by him. Quraysh asked, 'Who will pick up that placenta and throw it over his back?' Wqba b. Abii Mu* replied, 'I will.' And he picked it up and threw it over his hack. The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained there prostrating until Fatima came and removed it off his back. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'God take this crowd of Quraysh! 0 God, take away Wtba b. Rabi'a! 0 God, take away Shayba b. Rabi'a! 0 God, take away Abii Jahl b. Iiishm! 0 God, take away Wqba b. Abn Mu%yt! 0 God, take away Ubayy b. Khalaf - or Umayya b. Khalaf - they're like a patch of thorns!'" 'Abd All& stated, "And I saw all of them killed at the battle of Badr, and they were carried off to al-Qalih; except for Ubayy, or Umayya b. Khalaf; he was a very large man and so was kept separate." Al-Bukhai related this tradition in several places in his jahih collection, and Muslim related it with lines of authority from Ibn Ishaq. The man who should rightly be referred to in this tradition is Umayya b. Khalaf. It was he who was killed at the battle of Badr. His brother Ubayy, however, was killed at the battle of Uhud, as we will explain hereafter. The word al-salz, "placenta", used above is what emerges in birth with a baby camel, like the placenta of a woman's baby. In some versions told in the jahih collection, when Quraysh did this they laughed so hard they fell right over one another; that is, one would collapse onto the one next to him because of the violence of their laughter. God curse them! Regarding the same incident, when Fatima threw it off him, she drew near Quraysh and insulted them. And also it is said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) finished his prayer he held up his hands to curse them. And when they saw him do this their laughter died down, fearing his imprecation. They say he cursed all the Quraysh chiefs, singling out in his prayer seven of them. In most accounts six of these are named, they being Wtba and his brother Shayba, the two sons of Rabi'a, al-Walid b. Wtba, Abii Jahl b. Hishm, Wqba b. Abii Mu'ayt, and Umayya b. Khalaf. Ibn Ishaq stated, "I forget the seventh." But I state that he was W m a a b. al-Walid; his name is given in the jahih of al-Bukhari.
lessons: Those who humiliate the righteous do not escape the consequences of their actions; the Prophet's supplication at Badr was answered in full.
Steadfastness in worship — remaining in prostration despite humiliation — is one of the highest expressions of reliance on God.
Fatima's act of removing the filth from her father's back illustrates that love expressed through action, even small acts of service, carries profound spiritual dignity.
practical_advice: When you are humiliated or mocked for your faith or values, do not abandon your practice — complete it. The act of finishing your prayer or commitment despite the pain is itself a victory.
Channel righteous anger through supplication rather than retaliation; entrust justice to God, who hears and answers.
Look to the example of Fatima: when someone you love is being wronged, step in with courage and act on their behalf, even when it is difficult.

title: The Story of the Man from Irash: The Prophet Recovers His Payment from Abu Jahl
summary: A foreign traveller cheated by Abu Jahl appealed to the Quraysh council for help, and they mockingly directed him to the Prophet. The Prophet immediately went to Abu Jahl's home, and through divine awe, caused Abu Jahl to pay the man in full without hesitation.
story: Ymus b. Bukayr stated, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, who said that 'Abd al-Malik b. Abii Sufyan al-Thaqafi stated, "A man from Irash brought in to Mecca some camels he owned and Abn Jahl b. H i s h m bought them from hi. But he was slow in paying for them and so the man from I r s h came to the place where Quraysh met; the Messenger of God (SAAS) was also there at the time, sitting near the mosque. The man asked them, '0 Tribe of Quraysh, which man of you will give me help against Abn al-Hakam b. Hishan? I am a stranger, a traveller, and he has cheated me.' "The Quraysh council replied, 'You see that person' - indicating the Messenger of God (SAAS) both to mock him and because of the enmity they knew existed between him and Abn Jahl - 'go to him. He will help you against hi.' "So the man from Irash went and stood before the Messenger of God (SAAS) and related his story to him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) got up and left with him. When Quraysh saw h i do so they told one of their men to follow and see what he would do. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left and went to Abn Jahl's house and knocked on the door. Abii Jahl said, 'Who is there?' He replied, 'It's Muhammad; come outside!' So Abn Jahl came outside, there being not a drop of blood in his face, his colour having turned pale. The Prophet (SAAS) then said to him, 'Give this man his due!' Abn Jahl replied, 'Don't leave before 1give him what I owe.' He then went back inside and returned with the money he then paid to h i . The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left and said to the man from Irash, 'Now, go to your destination.' The man then went again before the Quraysh council and said, 'May God reward him well; I have received what was due to me.' "The man Quraysh had sent to watch came back to them and they asked what he had seen. He replied, 'It was incredible! I swear, when he knocked on Ahn Jahl's door and he came out, his spirit was not accompanying him! He told him to give the man his due, and Abn Jahl replied, "Don't leave till I bring it out for him." He then went hack inside, then came out and gave it to him.' "Soon thereafter Abn Jahl arrived and Quraysh asked, 'Well, what's wrong with you? By God, we never saw the like of what you did.' He replied, 'It was terrible! As soon as he knocked on my door and I heard his voice, I was filled with terror. Then when I went outside to him, there on his head was a stallion camel more fearful than any I ever saw before; its neck and teeth were unlike those of any camel. I swear, if I had refused, it would have eaten me!'"
lessons: The Prophet's commitment to justice extended beyond his own community — he stood up for a foreign stranger with no obligation to do so, demonstrating that upholding rights is a universal moral duty.
Divine support manifests in unexpected ways; even those who openly opposed the Prophet were compelled by unseen forces to submit to justice.
What opponents intend as mockery can become the means of someone's relief — God can turn the schemes of the unjust into causes of good.
practical_advice: When you witness injustice toward someone vulnerable, do not wait to be asked twice — take action as the Prophet did, rising immediately to accompany the man from Irash.
If you are in a position where you are owed something and feel powerless to recover it, seek the help of someone with integrity and a just character rather than trying to fight alone.
Trust that standing firmly for someone's rights, even when it seems hopeless or awkward, carries a divine weight that can move even the most stubborn hearts.

title: Uqba ibn Abi Mu'ayt Chokes the Prophet During Prayer; Abu Bakr Intervenes
summary: While the Prophet prayed at the Hijr of the Kaaba, Uqba ibn Abi Mu'ayt wrapped his robe around the Prophet's neck and choked him violently, until Abu Bakr rushed forward and pulled him away, citing the Quranic verse about a man who declared God as his Lord.
story: Al-Bukhai stated that {Ayyash b. al-Walid related to him, quoting al-Walid b. Muslim, quoting al-Awzaci, from Y a b y b. Aba Kathir, from Muhammad b. Ibrahim al-Taymi (who said) that Wrwa b. al-Zubayr related to him that he asked Ibn 'Amr b. al-'AS, "Tell me what the worst thing was the polytheists did to the Messenger of God (SAAS)." He replied, "While the Prophet (SAAS) was praying at the hyr (the outer enclosure of the kacba) Wqba b. Abii Mu'ayy went up to hi, drew his robe around his neck and began choking him hard. "Aba Bakr, God bless him, approached, took him by the shoulders and pulled him off the Prophet (SAAS) saying, 'Would you murder a man for saying, "God is my Lord; He has brought you clear signs from your Lord" to the end of the verse'" (ssrat Ghajir, XL, v.28). Ihn Ishzq gives the same hadith but uses the words "Y&yx b. Wrwa related to me: I asked 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr . . .". 'Abda gives it in the form: "from Hish%n, from his father, who said, "Amr h. al-'AS was asked . . .'". And Muhammad b. CAmr said, 'from Aba Salama, who said, "Amr b. al-'As related to me . . .'.". Al-Bayhaqi stated it, and Sulaymitn b. Bild related it, from Hish%n b. Wrwa as CAbda gave it. A l - B u u i is the sole authority giving this anecdote; he told it in several places in his ~ahihcollection. In one of these he related it to 'Abd Alhh b. 'Amr b. al-'&. That account is closest to that of Wnva. The fact that it was related from CAmr (rather than from the son of 'Amr) is more likely, since the incident had occurred so early.
lessons: Abu Bakr's intervention shows that standing up against oppression, even at personal risk, is a hallmark of true friendship and faith.
The Prophet endured physical violence in the act of worship itself, illustrating that the path of truth is often met with the harshest resistance.
Speaking a simple truth — 'God is my Lord' — can provoke violent opposition, yet it is precisely this truth that deserves to be defended and preserved.
practical_advice: When you see someone being wronged and have the ability to intervene, do not hesitate out of fear — Abu Bakr acted swiftly and his example has been remembered across centuries.
If you face hostility for your beliefs or values, remember that you are not alone; community and solidarity are among the most powerful shields against oppression.
In moments when your spiritual practice is attacked or interrupted, let the example of the Prophet inspire you to return to it — the act of worship is worth defending.

title: The Prophet Announces 'Slaughter' to the Quraysh Leaders During Tawaf
summary: While circumambulating the Kaaba and being repeatedly insulted by gathered Quraysh leaders, the Prophet turned to them and declared, 'By Him in whose hands is my soul, I'm bringing you slaughter,' causing the previously aggressive chiefs to fall silent and address him with sudden gentleness.
story: Al-Bayhaqi narrated from al-Hxkim, from al-A~amm, from Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, from Yanus, from Muhammad b. Ishxq, who said that Yahyz b. Wrwa related to hi, from his father Wrwa, who said that he asked 'Abd All* b. 'Amr b. al-'A~, "What is the worst thing you saw Quraysh inflict on the Messenger of God (SAAS)?" He replied, "I saw them once when all their chiefs had gathered at the hqr of the kacba and they were discussing the Messenger of God (SAAS). They said, 'We've been extraordinarily patient in putting up with this man; he has ridiculed our values, reviled our forefathers, criticized our religion, made divisions among us and insulted our gods. We've put up with a lot from hi.' They used words such as these." He went on, "While they were thus engaged, the Messenger of God (SAAS) appeared. He approached on foot, kissed the 'black stone', then passed on by them as he circumambulated the Kacba. They, as I recognized from the face of the Messenger of God (SAAS) then said some insulting things to him. He went on his path but when he came round past them on his second circuit they again, as I saw from his face, insulted him. When he came round for the third time, they did the same. "He then spoke, 'Are you listening, 0 Quraysh? By Him in whose hands is my soul, I'm bringing you slaughter!' "His words had such great impact on them that they went totally silent. Then those previously most harsh to him addressed h i with kindness, saying, 'Why
lessons: There is a time for patient endurance and a time for firm, clear warning; the Prophet's declaration after three circuits of sustained insult shows that truth must eventually speak with clarity and authority.
The sudden shift from aggression to fearful gentleness in the Quraysh leaders reveals that those who mock the truth often carry a deep, unacknowledged fear of it.
Continuing one's worship with dignity in the face of repeated taunts — completing each circuit before responding — is a form of moral and spiritual strength that commands genuine respect.
practical_advice: When you are repeatedly provoked or disrespected, do not abandon your purpose or your practice — complete what you set out to do before addressing the source of harm.
If you must confront those who wrong you, choose your moment and your words with care; a single clear and calm statement can silence what hours of argument cannot.
Reflect on how the Prophet's face revealed his feelings even as he continued his worship — acknowledging your emotions internally while maintaining composure outwardly is not weakness, it is mastery.

title: Quraysh Leaders Meet the Prophet with Demands and Objections
summary: Leaders of Quraysh summoned the Prophet hoping to reconcile, but instead presented him with offers of wealth, power, and kingship to abandon his message, and then demanded miraculous signs as conditions for belief.
story: Yiinus and Ziyad related, from Ibn Ishaq, and from a certain scholar, namely a sheikh from Egypt named Muhammad b. Abii Muhammad, from Sacid h. Jubayr and 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Ahbas, who said, "Leaders from Quraysh chiefs met - and be enumerated their names - after sunset at the rear of the ka'ba. Some said, 'Send for Mubmmad and speak with h i m and argue with him so you will find excuse for him.' "So they sent a message to him, saying, 'The chiefs of your people have assembled to speak with you.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) came to them quickly, believing that there had been a change in their attitude to him. He was eager for them to accept the truth for their error, which was painful to him. He sat down with them. "They said, '0 Muhammad, we sent for you to reconcile with ybu. By God, we know of no Arab man who has ever brought his people as much trouble as you have. You have reviled the forebears, criticized the religion, ridiculed the values, cursed the gods, and divided our community. Every unpleasant thing possible you have done to make a rift between you and us. "'If you had come to say these things merely to seek wealth, we would have collected money for you from our own until you were the richest among us. If what you wanted was prestige, we would have placed you in leadership over us. If you had wanted sovereignty, we would have made you king over us. If what you were bringing us was because of a spirit that had possessed you (they used the word ra'i for tabs< meaning spirit) and that may be the case, we would expend our resources seeking a potion to free you from him, or we would excuse you.' "The Messenger of God replied, 'What you have said does not apply to me. I have not brought you my message seeking your money, nor honour among you, nor sovereignty over you. God has sent me to you as a Messenger; He has revealed a document to me and has ordered me to bring you good news and to warn you. I have brought you a message from my Lord and have given you counsel. If you accept what I have brought you, then that is for your good on earth and in the hereafter. If you reject it, I will await God's decision, until He decides between myself and you.' The Messenger of God used some such words. "They responded, '0Muhammad, if you will not accept what we are offering you, then you will realize that there are no people with less land than us, nor any less wealthy, nor any with a more difficult life. So ask for us your Lord who has sent you with whatever he has sent you, to level out for us these mountains that close us in, and spread out our country and give us some rivers here that flow like those in Syria and Iraq. Ask him to resurrect for us some of our dead forefathers, including among them Qusayy b. Kil?ib, since he was a truthtelling sheikh. We will then ask them whether what you say is true or false. "'If you do what we ask and they believe you, we will believe you and recognize your status with God, and that he has sent yqu as a messenger, as you say.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'This is not why I have been sent. I have only hrought you from God what He has sent me with. I have informed you of what I was sent to convey to you. If you accept it, then that is your good fortune on earth and in the hereafter. If you reject it, I must wait patiently for God's decree and for Him to judge between us.' "They responded, 'If you won't do this for us, take something for yourself. Ask your Lord to send us down an angel who will verify what you say and make us leave you alone. Ask him to provide for us some gardens, some castles and treasures of gold and silver, and to take care of those needs we see you have, for we see you engage in business and earn your living like us. That way we will know the favour and prestige you have with your Lord - if, that is, you are a messenger as you claim.' "He replied, 'I'm not going to do so. I'm not one to ask such things of his Lord. That is not why I have been sent to you. God has sent me to announce and to warn. If you accept my message, then that is your good fortune here on earth and in the afterlife. If you reject it, then I must be patient and leave the matter up to God, until He decides between myself and you.' "They replied, LWellthen, make the sky fall down as you have claimed your Lord can do if He wishes. We'll not believe in you unless you do this.' "He answered, 'That is up to God. If He wishes, He will do that to you.' "They commented, '0 Muhammad, did your Lord not know we would sit with you and ask you questions as we have asked and make requests we have made, and so come forward to you and inform you how to respond to us, and tell you what He would then do about us if we did not accept the message from you?' "'We have learned that you just receive this instruction from a man in al-Yamana known as al-R~hrnan;'~we will never, by God, believe in that al-Rahmzn! We've made every excuse for you, M+mmad, but, by God, because of what you've done to us we'll not leave you alone until we destroy you or you destroy us!' "One of them said, 'We worship the angels who are God's daughters.' Another said, 'We'll not believe in you till you bring us God and the angels beforehand.' "When they had said this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) arose to leave them. 'Abd A b h b. Aba Umayya b. al-Mughira b. 'Abd All& b. Wmar b. Makhziim, who was his cousin, being the son of his aunt 'Atika, daughter of 'Abd al-Munalib, got up with him and said to him, '0Muhammad, your people have made certain offers to you that you have not accepted. Then they made requests for themselves so that they could discover your status with God, but you did nothing. Then they asked you to send promptly some punishment that would scare them. By God, I'll never believe in you until you put up a ladder to heaven then climb it, with me watching, until you reach there, bring back with you a copy of a document accompanied by four angels who testify that you are as you claim! And yet, by God, even if you did that, I think I'd not believe you!' "He then left the Messenger of God (SAAS), who went off home sad and regretful at his failure to achieve what he had expected when his people had summoned him, and at the alienation he had seen them express towards him."
